topics.title: Simplified Flow Diagrams of a Typical Refinery - Distillation Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simplified Flow Diagrams of a Typical Refinery - Distillation Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distillation Unit (Crude Distillation Unit - CDU):** A petroleum refinery process unit that separates crude oil into its constituent hydrocarbon fractions based on their **boiling point range** (true boiling point / TBP), utilizing a combination of atmospheric and vacuum distillation columns, feed preheat trains, fired heaters, and overhead/side-cut product recovery systems operating in continuous steady-state mode.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the *Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries* syllabus, a **Typical Refinery Distillation Unit** comprises two primary integrated sections:

1. **Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU / Crude Distillation Unit - CDU):** Operates at near-atmospheric pressure (slightly above **101.325 kPa / 1 atm**) to separate crude oil into lighter fractions without thermal cracking. The maximum feed temperature at the heater outlet is typically limited to approximately **370 °C – 380 °C** to prevent thermal degradation.
2. **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU):** Operates under reduced pressure (typically **10 mmHg to 50 mmHg absolute**) to separate heavier residues at temperatures below their cracking temperatures, producing Vacuum Gas Oils (VGO) and Vacuum Residue.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Boiling Pot of the Refinery"**
> Think of the distillation unit like a **multi-tiered cafeteria hot-pot kitchen**. Imagine crude oil as a *mixed vegetable soup* containing ingredients that cook (vaporize) at different temperatures. The **bottom layer** of the pot has the heaviest, highest-boiling ingredients (residue). As the soup boils, **lighter vapors (gasoline, naphtha)** rise to the topmost tier first and condense. **Medium-weight ingredients (kerosene, diesel)** condense on middle shelves. The kitchen chef uses a **vacuum lid (VDU)** to cook the remaining thick stew at lower temperatures, extracting more flavorful oils (VGO) without burning the bottom.
> - The **fired heater** = The gas stove at the bottom
> - The **fractionating tower** = The multi-shelf cooling rack
> - The **trays/packing** = The shelves that let lighter vapors pass upward and heavier liquid trickle down
> - The **condenser** = The cold lid that collects the lightest vapors
> - The **reflux drum & pumps** = The recycling system that sends some condensate back as a "washing stream" for sharper separation

### 1.3 Salient Operating Metrics (Standard Refinery Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Crude feed rate | **50,000 – 200,000 barrels per stream day (BPSD)** |
| ADU column top pressure | **105 – 170 kPa (gauge)** |
| ADU heater outlet temp | **365 °C – 380 °C** |
| VDU column top pressure | **10 – 50 mmHg (absolute)** |
| VDU heater outlet temp | **400 °C – 425 °C** |
| Number of theoretical trays | **30 – 50** (ADU), **20 – 30** (VDU) |
| Reflux ratio (external) | **1.5 – 3.0** |
| Overhead receiver temperature | **35 °C – 45 °C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The integration of **ADU + VDU** is fundamental to refinery safety analysis because approximately **40–50 % of all refinery fires and explosions historically originate in the distillation section**, particularly in fired heaters, overhead accumulators, and pump rooms (per OSHA / API RP 753 guidelines).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process Logic – Block-by-Block Operating Philosophy

The distillation unit is engineered around a **counter-current mass-transfer operation** between rising hydrocarbon vapors and descending liquid reflux. The fundamental sequence is:

1. **Feed Receipt & Desalting (Electrostatic Coalescer)**
   - Crude is mixed with wash water (**5 – 10 % vol**) and demulsifier chemicals.
   - Salts (NaCl, MgCl₂) and sediments are removed to prevent downstream fouling and corrosion.
   - Salt content reduced from **~ 200 PTB (pounds per thousand barrels)** to **< 5 PTB**.
   - *Safety link:* Hydrochloric acid (HCl) formation in the heater is a major corrosion hazard → desalter efficiency is a **critical safety control point**.

2. **Preheat Train (Shell & Tube Heat Exchangers)**
   - Cold crude is progressively heated by exchange with hot product streams leaving the column.
   - Typical network: **Desalted crude → 1st exchanger (with pumparound) → 2nd exchanger (with kerosene) → 3rd exchanger (with diesel) → 4th exchanger (with VGO) → 5th exchanger (with atmospheric residue)**.
   - Energy recovered: up to **60–70 % of fired-heater duty**.
   - Crude enters heater at **220 – 260 °C** after preheat.

3. **Fired Heater (Furnace)**
   - Direct-fired tubular heater using refinery fuel gas / fuel oil.
   - Preheated crude (vapor + liquid two-phase) is brought to **365 – 380 °C** at the heater outlet (ADU) or **400 – 425 °C** (VDU feed).
   - *Safety link:* Tube rupture → fire box explosion risk; **API RP 556** mandates burner management systems (BMS), flame scanners, and tube skin thermocouples.

4. **Atmospheric Distillation Column (Fractionator)**
   - **Trayed tower** (valve, sieve, or GLitsch V-grid trays) operating at near-atmospheric pressure at the top.
   - **Stripping sections** at the bottom of each side-draw (using steam) to remove light ends and adjust product flash points.
   - **Pumparound circuits** remove heat at intermediate zones, return cooled liquid back to a tray above — providing internal reflux and additional fractionation.
   - Typical side-cut products (top to bottom): **Off-gas → Naphtha → Kerosene (ATK) → Diesel → Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO) → Reduced Crude / Atmospheric Residue**.

5. **Overhead System**
   - Vapors from the column top are condensed in **air-fin / water-cooled condensers**.
   - Condensate collected in the **reflux drum (overhead receiver)**.
   - Light non-condensables (C1–C4) vented through a **flare header** after recovery in a vapor recovery unit.
   - Liquid split: **~ 50 % returned as reflux**, balance drawn as **overhead naphtha / unstabilized gasoline**.

6. **Vacuum Distillation Column**
   - Receives **atmospheric residue** from ADU bottom.
   - Uses **steam ejectors / liquid-ring vacuum pumps** to maintain low absolute pressure.
   - Reduces hydrocarbon partial pressure → allows vaporization at lower temperatures → prevents cracking.
   - Products: **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO – light & heavy)** as side draws, **Vacuum Residue (VR)** as bottoms.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 Exam Valuation** — Master these for full marks on Part A and Part B numerical sub-parts.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Antoine Equation** (vapor pressure) | $\ln P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | Predicts bubble/dew points of fractions |
| 2 | **Raoult's Law** | $P_i = x_i \cdot \gamma_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$ | Vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) for multicomponent mix |
| 3 | **Dalton's Law** | $P = \sum y_i P$ | Vapor-phase partial pressures |
| 4 | **Reflux Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ (internal) $\quad R_{ext} = \dfrac{L_0}{D}$ | Controls separation sharpness |
| 5 | **Fenske Equation** (minimum trays) | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \cdot \left(\dfrac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Theoretical minimum stages at total reflux |
| 6 | **Gilliland Correlation** | $Y = 0.75 \left( 1 - X^{0.5668} \right)$ where $X = \dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N+1}$ | Estimates actual number of stages vs. $N_{min}$ |
| 7 | **Erbar-Maddox Correlation** | Function of $(R - R_{min})$ and feed condition $q$ | Refines stage estimates for ADU/VDU design |
| 8 | **Heat Balance (Overall)** | $Q_{furnace} + Q_{exchangers} = Q_{overhead} + Q_{pumparound} + Q_{products} + Q_{losses}$ | Energy audit of the distillation train |
| 9 | **Steam-to-Oil Ratio (VDU)** | $SOR = \dfrac{W_{steam}}{W_{feed}}$ (typical 0.05 – 0.15) | Stripping efficiency in VDU |
| 10 | **Reduced Pressure Vaporization** | $P_{reduced} \approx \dfrac{P_{atm}}{10}$ → T reduction ≈ **50 – 80 °C** | Justification for VDU operation |

### 2.3 Why This Architecture is Standard in Safety-Critical Design

- **Reduces heater tube metal temperatures** in the VDU by using vacuum → lower thermal stress → fewer tube failures.
- **Staged heat removal via pumparound** (typically 3 pumparounds in ADU) limits vapor velocities, preventing entrainment and column flooding.
- **Feed vaporization at multiple trays** distributes thermal load → more uniform tower metallurgy.
- **Overhead water-wash / caustic injection** neutralizes HCl and removes ammonium salts (NH₄HS) — a major cause of air-fin cooler corrosion and **crude unit overhead exchanger failure** incidents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Number of Theoretical Trays (Fenske Equation)

The Fenske equation is the cornerstone of distillation design and is **frequently tested in KTU exams (Part B – 7 mark sub-questions)**.

**Derivation Logic:**

For a binary mixture of Light Key (LK) and Heavy Key (HK) components at **total reflux** (no feed, no products, D → 0, L → ∞), the operating lines coincide with the **45-degree diagonal** of the McCabe–Thiele diagram. Each ideal stage produces a constant ratio of component relative volatilities.

**Step 1:** Vapor-liquid equilibrium relation for stage *j*:
$$y_j = \dfrac{\alpha_j \, x_j}{1 + (\alpha_j - 1) x_j}$$

**Step 2:** Combining the equilibrium relation with the total-reflux operating line ($y_{j-1} = x_j$):
$$x_{j} = \dfrac{\alpha_j \, x_{j-1}}{1 + (\alpha_j - 1) x_{j-1}}$$

**Step 3:** Taking the ratio of LK to HK across *N* stages, with relative volatility $\alpha = K_{LK}/K_{HK}$ assumed constant:
$$\dfrac{x_{LK,N}}{x_{HK,N}} = \alpha^N \cdot \dfrac{x_{LK,0}}{x_{HK,0}}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the top product (D) and bottom product (B) compositions:
$$\dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{HK,D}} = \alpha^{N_{min}} \cdot \dfrac{x_{LK,B}}{x_{HK,B}}$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $N_{min}$ by taking natural logarithm:
$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \cdot \left(\dfrac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU valuation:** The constant relative volatility assumption is valid only over narrow boiling ranges. For wide-boiling crude cuts, use the **Winn equation** with effective $\alpha$ values per stage.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Balance on the ADU Overhead System

Let $F$ = feed rate (kmol/h), $D$ = distillate, $B$ = bottoms, $L$ = liquid reflux, $V$ = vapor boil-up, $L_0$ = external reflux returned to column.

**Component balance on the top tray (rectifying section):**
$$V \cdot y_{n+1} = L \cdot x_n + D \cdot x_D$$

**Total balance:**
$$V = L + D$$

**External reflux ratio definition:**
$$R = \dfrac{L_0}{D} = \dfrac{L}{D}$$

**Substituting and solving for $y_{n+1}$ (operating line of rectifying section):**
$$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$$

This is the **rectifying section operating line** that must be plotted alongside the equilibrium curve on the McCabe–Thiele diagram to graphically determine the actual number of stages.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Crude Unit Material Balance (Overall)

Let the ADU receive crude feed $F$ and produce products: Off-gas ($G$), Naphtha ($N$), Kerosene ($K$), Diesel ($D_1$), AGO ($A$), Residue ($R_{atm}$).

**Total mass balance:**
$$F = G + N + K + D_1 + A + R_{atm}$$

**Component balance for sulfur (critical for safety & product blending):**
$$F \cdot S_F = G \cdot S_G + N \cdot S_N + K \cdot S_K + D_1 \cdot S_{D1} + A \cdot S_A + R_{atm} \cdot S_{R}$$

**Volume balance (using API gravities):**
$$F \cdot \dfrac{1}{SG_F} = \sum_i \dfrac{P_i}{SG_i}$$

where $P_i$ = product volume in barrels and $SG_i$ = specific gravity.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Exam Style)

> **Problem [14-mark KTU 2024 Style]:**
> A refinery ADU processes **100,000 kg/h of desalted crude** (mean molecular weight = 220, sulfur = 1.8 wt %). The unit produces:
> - Naphtha: 18,000 kg/h, S = 0.05 %
> - Kerosene: 22,000 kg/h, S = 0.20 %
> - Diesel: 28,000 kg/h, S = 0.50 %
> - AGO: 14,000 kg/h, S = 1.20 %
> - Residue: 18,000 kg/h, S = 3.50 %
> - (Assume negligible S in off-gas)
>
> **Tasks:**
> (a) Verify the material balance and compute the off-gas flow rate.
> (b) Calculate the sulfur content in the residue if 2,000 kg/h of residue is recycled to mix with AGO.
> (c) Comment on the safety implications of the sulfur distribution.

**Solution:**

**(a) Off-gas flow rate:**
$$G = F - (N + K + D_1 + A + R_{atm})$$

$$G = 100{,}000 - (18{,}000 + 22{,}000 + 28{,}000 + 14{,}000 + 18{,}000)$$

$$G = 100{,}000 - 100{,}000 = 0 \text{ kg/h}$$

*Result:* Material balance **balances exactly**; off-gas assumed negligible (incorporated in naphtha or vented via flare).

**(b) Sulfur in mixed stream (residue + recycle):**
Total mass = $18{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 20{,}000$ kg/h
Sulfur carried in = $(18{,}000 \times 0.035) + (2{,}000 \times 0.035) = 700$ kg/h
Mixed stream S% = $(700 / 20{,}000) \times 100 = \mathbf{3.50 \%}$

*Result:* Recycle preserves composition — separation integrity maintained.

**(c) Safety implications:**
- High-sulfur residue (**3.5 wt %**) requires **Claus unit sulfur recovery** before storage.
- Sulfur compounds (H₂S, mercaptans) in the hot overhead system → **API RP 571** carbon steel sulfidation attack above **260 °C**.
- Risk of **iron sulfide pyrophoricity** during maintenance → mandate steam-out and inert gas blanketing (N₂) before tank entry.
- **Naphtha's low sulfur (< 0.05 %)** → minimizes SOₓ emissions but increases fire hazard due to high volatility (FP < 0 °C).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Simplified Block Flow Diagram — Atmospheric + Vacuum Distillation Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil Storage Tank] --> B[Desalter Electrostatic Coalescer Wash Water 5 to 10 percent]
    B --> C[Preheat Train 1 Pumparound]
    C --> D[Preheat Train 2 Kerosene Product]
    D --> E[Preheat Train 3 Diesel Product]
    E --> F[Preheat Train 4 VGO Product]
    F --> G[Preheat Train 5 Atmospheric Residue]
    G --> H[ADU Fired Heater Outlet 370 to 380 C]
    H --> I[Atmospheric Distillation Column 30 to 50 trays]
    I --> J[Overhead Condenser and Reflux Drum]
    J --> K[Overhead Naphtha to Stabilizer]
    J --> L[Non Condensables to Flare Header]
    I --> M[Kerosene Side Draw to Steam Stripper]
    I --> N[Diesel Side Draw to Steam Stripper]
    I --> O[AGO Side Draw to Steam Stripper]
    I --> P[Atmospheric Residue Bottom]
    P --> Q[VDU Fired Heater Outlet 400 to 425 C]
    Q --> R[Vacuum Distillation Column 20 to 30 trays]
    R --> S[Vacuum Ejector System Steam Driven]
    R --> T[Light VGO Side Draw]
    R --> U[Heavy VGO Side Draw]
    R --> V[Vacuum Residue Bottom to Coker or Visbreaker]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid block above represents a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the sequential process interactions of a typical two-stage crude distillation train, as required for KTU 2024 schematic-based questions.

### 4.2 Process Flow Architecture — Auxiliary & Safety Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SAFETY_SYSTEMS
        A1[BMS Burner Management System] --- A2[Flame Scanners UV IR]
        A1 --- A3[Tube Skin Thermocouples]
        A1 --- A4[High Pressure Trip 110 percent]
        A1 --- A5[Low Flow Trip on Feed]
    end
    subgraph UTILITY_SYSTEMS
        B1[Steam 3.5 kg per sq cm LP] --- B2[Steam 10 kg per sq cm MP]
        B1 --- B3[Condensate Recovery]
        B4[Cooling Water Tower] --- B5[Air Fin Cooler]
        B6[Instrument Air 6 kg per sq cm] --- B7[Nitrogen Blanketing]
    end
    subgraph ENVIRONMENTAL_CONTROL
        C1[Overhead Caustic Injection NaOH] --- C2[Waste Water Treatment DAF]
        C3[Flare Stack Elevated] --- C4[Knock Out Drum]
    end
    HEATER[Fired Heater] --> SAFETY_SYSTEMS
    TOWER[Distillation Column] --> UTILITY_SYSTEMS
    OVERHEAD[Overhead Receiver] --> ENVIRONMENTAL_CONTROL
```

### 4.3 Material Flow Hierarchy (Text-Schematic)

```
                 ┌───── LIGHT GAS → FLARE
                 │
   CRUDE → DESALTER → PREHEAT TRAIN → FURNACE
                                    │
                                    ▼
                            ATMOSPHERIC TOWER
                            │   │   │    │    │
                            ▼   ▼   ▼    ▼    ▼
                          NAPH KER DSL AGO  ATM RES
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                       VACUUM FURNACE
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                         VACUUM TOWER
                                          │   │   │
                                          ▼   ▼   ▼
                                        LVGO HVGO  VR
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q1. Define the term "Fenske Equation" and state its significance in distillation column design.**

**Model Answer:**
The Fenske equation is a rigorous analytical method to determine the **minimum number of theoretical stages ($N_{min}$)** required for a distillation column operating at **total reflux** (no feed or product withdrawal). Its significance lies in providing the **lower bound on the number of stages** required for a specified separation between a Light Key (LK) and Heavy Key (HK) component. It uses the **geometric mean of relative volatility ($\alpha_{avg}$)** between LK and HK across the operating range. KTU 2024 examiners award full marks only if the student mentions: (i) total reflux assumption, (ii) the logarithmic form, and (iii) the use as a baseline for Gilliland/Underwood design.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Atmospheric Distillation Unit (ADU) and Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU) in terms of operating pressure, feed, and product slate.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ADU | VDU |
|---|---|---|
| Operating pressure (top) | ~ 101 – 170 kPa (gauge) | ~ 10 – 50 mmHg (absolute) |
| Feed | Desalted crude oil | Atmospheric residue (bottoms of ADU) |
| Heater outlet temperature | 365 – 380 °C | 400 – 425 °C |
| Main products | Naphtha, Kerosene, Diesel, AGO, Residue | LVGO, HVGO, Vacuum Residue |
| Purpose | Recover light & middle distillates | Distill heavy fractions without cracking |
| Vacuum source | None | Steam ejectors / liquid-ring pumps |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Atmospheric Distillation Design Analysis**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> 
> **(a)** Draw a **simplified process flow diagram** of a typical crude oil refinery distillation unit showing the ADU and VDU, including all major equipment (desalter, preheat train, heater, columns, condensers, reflux drum, side-cut strippers, vacuum ejector). Clearly label all major product streams and their typical boiling ranges. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A desalter receives 1,00,000 kg/h of crude (0.8 wt % salt, SG = 0.86). Wash water is added at 8 % vol of crude, and the desalter removes 95 % of salts. Compute: (i) salt removed (kg/h), (ii) salt in desalted crude (PTB), (iii) brine effluent flow rate (kg/h), and (iv) % reduction in salt content. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PFD Drawing (7 marks):**
Award breakdown by KTU examiner pattern:
- Desalter with wash water and demulsifier: **1 mark**
- Preheat train (3+ exchangers): **1 mark**
- ADU fired heater with outlet temperature labeled: **1 mark**
- ADU column with 5 side cuts (Naphtha, Kero, Diesel, AGO, Residue): **1.5 marks**
- Overhead condenser and reflux drum: **0.5 mark**
- VDU heater and column with ejector: **1.5 marks**
- VGO side draws and residue bottom: **0.5 mark**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Crude flow $F = 100{,}000$ kg/h
- Salt content = 0.8 wt % → $0.008 \times 100{,}000 = 800$ kg/h salt
- Wash water = 8 % vol of crude volume
- SG of crude = 0.86 → volume flow = $100{,}000 / 0.86 \times 1000 / 1000$ m³/h

Volume of crude = $100{,}000 / (0.86 \times 1000)$ m³/h = 116.28 m³/h

Wash water volume = $0.08 \times 116.28 = 9.30$ m³/h → mass = $9.30 \times 1000 = 9{,}300$ kg/h

**Crude density** = $0.86 \times 1000 = 860$ kg/m³

**Step-by-step (each step 1.5 marks, last step for % reduction):**

**(i) Salt removed:**
$$S_{removed} = 0.95 \times 800 = 760 \text{ kg/h}$$

**(ii) Salt in desalted crude:**
$$S_{out} = 800 - 760 = 40 \text{ kg/h}$$

Convert to PTB (pounds per thousand barrels):
$$\text{Volume of crude} = \frac{100{,}000}{860} \times 6.2898 \text{ (bbl/m³)} = 731.4 \text{ bbl/h}$$

$$PTB = \frac{40 \text{ kg/h} \times 2.2046 \text{ lb/kg} \times 1000}{731.4 \text{ bbl/h} \times 1000} = 0.121 \text{ PTB}$$

**(iii) Brine effluent (mass balance):**
$$M_{brine} = M_{salt,out} + M_{wash,resid} \approx 40 + 9{,}300 = 9{,}340 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Roughly 9,300 kg/h water + 40 kg/h residual salt.**

**(iv) % salt reduction:**
$$\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{760}{800} \times 100 = 95\%$$

**Result:** Desalter reduces salt from **~ 200 PTB (input)** to **~ 0.12 PTB (output)** — well below the industry standard of **< 5 PTB**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Vacuum Distillation & Safety Analysis (Alternative Choice)**

> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **operating principle of a Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)**. Justify why vacuum operation is preferred over atmospheric distillation for processing atmospheric residue. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** The bottom of an ADU is at 380 °C. If this stream is sent to a VDU operating at 30 mmHg absolute, estimate the new equilibrium vaporization temperature. Discuss the implications on heater metallurgy and fire safety. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) VDU Principle (7 marks):**

**Operating principle:** The VDU operates on the principle of **reduced-pressure distillation**, where the absolute pressure at the top of the fractionator is reduced to 10–50 mmHg using **steam-jet ejectors** or **liquid-ring vacuum pumps**. The Clausius–Clapeyron relation states that **boiling point decreases with pressure**. Hence, hydrocarbon molecules that would crack at 380 °C at 1 atm can be vaporized at 250–300 °C under vacuum, preserving molecular integrity.

**Why vacuum for atmospheric residue:**
- Prevents thermal cracking (coking) → longer heater run-length.
- Allows recovery of valuable **Lube Oil Base Stocks (LOBS)** or catalytic cracker feed (VGO).
- Reduces heater tube metal temperature → lower thermal stress and longer tube life.

**Sketch:** Standard VDU diagram with heater, column, ejector, condenser, and three side cuts (LVGO, HVGO, VR). Award 3 marks for sketch, 4 marks for explanation.

**(b) Temperature Estimation (7 marks):**

Using the **rule-of-thumb approximation** (refinery engineering standard, based on NIST vapor pressure data):
$$\Delta T \approx 50 - 80 \text{ °C reduction per 10× pressure drop}$$

Pressure ratio = 760 / 30 = **25.3** → ~ 1.4 decades

Estimated temperature drop = $1.4 \times 65 \approx 91$ °C (interpolated)

New equilibrium vaporization temperature:
$$T_{VDU} \approx 380 - 91 = 289 \text{ °C} \approx 290 \text{ °C}$$

**Safety implications:**
1. **Heater metallurgy:** T = 290 °C is below the **sulphidation attack threshold** of 5Cr-0.5Mo steel (≈ 480 °C), allowing use of **carbon steel tubes** — significant CAPEX savings.
2. **Fire safety:** Lower tube temperature reduces **tube rupture probability** and **fire-box flashback** risk. However, the hot vacuum residue (320–350 °C) is **above its flash point** → require **thermal insulation to API 2510** and **steam-snuffing for pump seals**.
3. **Vacuum system hazards:** Air ingress → **explosive mixture with hydrocarbon vapor**. Mitigations: N₂ padding on hot wells, positive-pressure leak checks every shift.
4. **Ejector steam safety:** Multi-stage ejectors require **continuous steam supply** — loss → column pressure surge → potential **VDU overpressure** and structural damage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Student Mistakes:**
> 1. **Failing to label all major equipment** in PFDs — students often omit stripper reboilers, pumparounds, and the reflux drum. KTU awards zero marks for unlabeled side-streams.
> 2. **Confusing "Reflux Ratio" with "Reflux Flow"** — Reflux ratio is *dimensionless*; reflux flow is in kg/h or m³/h. Many students lose 1–2 marks per question on this.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversions** in numerical problems — particularly PTB ↔ kg/h and bbl ↔ m³. Always show the conversion factor explicitly.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of total reflux** when applying the Fenske equation — this is a mandatory 1-mark deduction if omitted.
> 5. **Mixing up VDU and ADU products** — VDU produces VGO and Vacuum Residue, NOT kerosene or diesel. Examiners flag this as a fundamental conceptual error.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Exam (Module 1)**

- ☐ **ADU** operates at ~ **1 atm**; **VDU** operates at **10 – 50 mmHg absolute**.
- ☐ Crude oil is separated based on **boiling point range**, not carbon number alone.
- ☐ The **desalter** is the first critical equipment — protects downstream heater from salt corrosion (HCl formation).
- ☐ **Preheat train** recovers 60 – 70 % of heat — energy efficiency cornerstone.
- ☐ **Fired heater outlet T** for ADU: 365 – 380 °C (limits coking). VDU: 400 – 425 °C.
- ☐ **Fenske equation** → $N_{min}$ at total reflux.
- ☐ **Gilliland correlation** → estimates actual stages from $N_{min}$ and operating reflux ratio.
- ☐ **Reflux drum** splits condensate into reflux and product; non-condensables → flare.
- ☐ **Pumparound** = internal reflux used for heat removal and additional fractionation.
- ☐ **VDU products**: LVGO, HVGO (cracker feed), Vacuum Residue (to coker, visbreaker, or bitumen).
- ☐ **Salt in desalted crude** must be **< 5 PTB** (refinery standard).
- ☐ **Tube metal temperature** must stay below **sulphidation threshold** for carbon steel ≈ 260 °C, and 5Cr-0.5Mo ≈ 480 °C.
- ☐ **Safety instruments**: BMS, flame scanners, tube skin TC, low-flow trip, high-pressure trip.
- ☐ **Overhead corrosion** control: caustic injection, water wash, NH₄HS salt-point monitoring.
- ☐ **Pyrophoric iron sulfide (FeS)** risk during shutdown → steam-out and N₂ blanket before opening.
- ☐ **Sulfur distribution** in products increases with boiling range — residue has the highest S content.
- ☐ Distillation unit fires account for **40 – 50 %** of all refinery incidents — **safety-in-design** is non-negotiable.
- ☐ The **reflux ratio (R)** must always be **> R_min** for feasible separation.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Distillation Unit**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalytic Cracker
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Catalytic Cracker

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Catalytic Cracker** (most commonly implemented as a **Fluid Catalytic Cracking Unit, FCCU**) is a continuous, high-temperature refinery process unit that converts long-chain, high-molecular-weight hydrocarbon fractions (such as **Vacuum Gas Oil - VGO**, atmospheric residue, or deasphalted oil) into lighter, higher-value products such as **gasoline (petrol), LPG, light olefins (propylene/butylene), and middle distillates**.

The process achieves molecular breakdown (cracking) at temperatures of approximately **480 °C – 540 °C** and near-atmospheric pressures (1 – 3 bar gauge), in the presence of a finely divided, regenerated solid acid catalyst — typically a **zeolite-based catalyst (e.g., Y-zeolite / USY / ZSM-5)** dispersed as a fluidized bed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A *catalytic cracker* is a refinery conversion unit that uses a **circulating solid acid catalyst** in a *reactor–regenerator* configuration, where cracking takes place in the riser-reactor and the deactivated (coked) catalyst is continuously regenerated by controlled combustion of deposited coke in the regenerator. This regeneration loop is the **primary source of safety hazards** in the unit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant industrial **"toaster with a circulating sand-belt"**:

1. **Feedstock (the dough / raw bread)** = heavy oil (long, sluggish, hard-to-sell hydrocarbon chains).
2. **The hot sand (catalyst)** = zeolite powder fluidized by vapor; it acts as a molecular "cheese grater" that snaps long molecules into shorter, more useful pieces.
3. **The oven (riser reactor, ~520 °C)** = where the slicing happens in a few seconds.
4. **The burnt crumbs (coke)** = a carbonaceous deposit that coats the sand and deactivates it.
5. **The toaster (regenerator, ~700 °C)** = where air is blown to burn off the crumbs so the sand can be reused.

After this process, the *bread* is no longer a heavy, slow-selling loaf; it has been converted into **light, high-demand, value-added products** (gasoline, LPG, olefins).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive takeaway:** The catalyst is **not consumed** — it acts as a reusable molecular "tool" that gets *fouled* during cracking and *cleaned* during regeneration. The continuous loop between reactor and regenerator is what makes the process economically (and safety-wise) unique.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Hydrocarbon Safety

| Aspect | Safety Significance |
|---|---|
| **High Temperature** (up to 750 °C in regenerator) | Risk of metallurgical failure, hot-spot burn-through |
| **Hydrocarbon + Air Interface** (regenerator) | Inherent risk of **deflagration / explosion** if hydrocarbons leak into oxidizing zone |
| **Catalyst Handling** (fine powder, < 80 µm) | **Dust explosion** hazard, respiratory exposure |
| **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)** in feed | Toxic gas release risk, sour corrosion |
| **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** in flue gas | Toxic asphyxiation, indicator of incomplete combustion |
| **Cyclone Separators** | Mechanical failure → catalyst entrainment, erosion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping:** This topic maps directly to **CO1: Understand major hydrocarbon processing units and their inherent safety hazards.** Catalytic crackers are the *single most hazardous unit* in a modern refinery and account for a disproportionate share of **Major Accident Hazard (MAH)** scenarios in process safety regulations such as **OSHA PSM** and **India's Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, MSIHC 1989**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

The FCCU operates on the principle of **heterogeneous catalytic cracking** with continuous catalyst regeneration. The key logical steps are:

1. **Feed Pre-heating:** VGO feedstock is preheated to ~200–300 °C using heat exchange with product streams.
2. **Feed Injection & Atomization:** The preheated feed is injected through **feed nozzles** at the base of the *riser reactor*, where it contacts hot regenerated catalyst (~700 °C).
3. **Catalytic Cracking in the Riser:** Cracking reactions proceed as hydrocarbon vapors fluidize the catalyst and travel upward. Residence time is **2 – 5 seconds** (very short to minimize secondary overcracking).
4. **Catalyst–Vapor Separation:** At the top of the riser, **cyclone separators** disengage the spent catalyst from cracked vapors.
5. **Spent Catalyst Stripping:** Steam stripping in the **stripper section** removes entrained hydrocarbons from the coked catalyst.
6. **Catalyst Regeneration:** The coked catalyst flows to the **regenerator**, where air is blown to burn off the coke:
   - **Coke (C) + O₂ → CO₂** (complete combustion, exothermic)
   - **Coke (C) + ½ O₂ → CO** (partial combustion, exothermic)
7. **Flue Gas Treatment:** Hot flue gas (containing CO, CO₂, SOₓ, NOₓ, catalyst fines) passes through **internal cyclones** and **CO boilers / CO incinerators** before atmospheric release.
8. **Product Fractionation:** Cracked vapors are sent to the **main fractionator** for separation into gases, gasoline, LCO, and slurry.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| Short riser residence | Maximizes **selectivity to gasoline**; suppresses secondary coking |
| Continuous catalyst regeneration | Maintains constant **catalyst activity** (~65–75) |
| Steam stripping | Recovers valuable hydrocarbons; reduces regenerator load |
| CO boilers | Mitigates **CO emissions** and recovers waste heat |
| Cyclones | Prevents **catalyst loss** and protects downstream equipment |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM TIP:** All formulas below are **numerical-problem favorites** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the units and limiting assumptions.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Conversion** (mass basis) | $\displaystyle X = \frac{F_{feed} - F_{unconv}}{F_{feed}} \times 100$ | $F$ = mass flow (kg/h); $X$ in **%** |
| 2 | **Product Yield** | $\displaystyle Y_i = \frac{F_{product,i}}{F_{feed}} \times 100$ | $i$ = gasoline, LPG, LCO, etc. |
| 3 | **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O)** | $\displaystyle C/O = \frac{F_{catalyst}}{F_{feed}}$ | Dimensionless, typically **5 – 8** |
| 4 | **Weight Hourly Space Velocity (WHSV)** | $\displaystyle WHSV = \frac{F_{feed}}{W_{catalyst,bed}}$ | Units: **h⁻¹**; typical FCC value 5 – 20 |
| 5 | **Coke on Catalyst (wt %)** | $\displaystyle C_c = \frac{m_{coke}}{m_{catalyst}} \times 100$ | Typical spent catalyst: **0.8 – 1.2 wt%** |
| 6 | **Stoichiometric O₂ for Coke Burn-off** | $\displaystyle m_{O_2} = m_{coke} \times \left( \frac{32}{12} \right) = 2.667 \, m_{coke}$ | For complete combustion: **C + O₂ → CO₂** |
| 7 | **Regenerator Air Requirement** | $\displaystyle m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = 11.49 \, m_{coke}$ | Air is **23.2 wt% O₂** |
| 8 | **Heat of Coke Combustion** | $\displaystyle \Delta H_{coke} = -32{,}800 \text{ kJ/kg coke}$ | (Approx. for graphitic C) |
| 9 | **Carbon Monoxide Conversion in CO Boiler** | $\displaystyle \eta_{CO} = \frac{[CO]_{in} - [CO]_{out}}{[CO]_{in}} \times 100$ | Must be **≥ 99.5 %** for environmental compliance |
| 10 | **Sulfur Distribution in Products** | $\displaystyle S_{dist,i} = \frac{S_{feed} \cdot \alpha_i}{100}$ | $\alpha_i$ = distribution coefficient to product $i$ |
| 11 | **Fluidization Velocity Check** | $\displaystyle U_{op} = (1.5 \text{ to } 3) \times U_{mf}$ | $U_{mf}$ = minimum fluidization velocity |
| 12 | **Lower Flammability Limit of FCC Flue Gas** | $LFL_{CO} \approx 12.5\%$ vol in air | Critical for CO safety interlocks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions (assumed in KTU numericals):**
> - Feed: pure hydrocarbon, no oxygen content
> - Coke is treated as **100% elemental carbon** unless specified
> - Air composition by mass: **N₂ = 76.8%, O₂ = 23.2%**
> - All reactions in regenerator reach **complete combustion** unless partial combustion is specified

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Economics:** The FCCU typically contributes **35 – 50 %** of a refinery's total gasoline pool — it is the **profit centre** of a modern refinery.
- **Process Safety:** A single FCCU represents the largest **inventory of hot hydrocarbon + air** on a refinery site; thus, it is the *focal point* of HAZOP, LOPA, and quantitative risk assessment (QRA) studies.
- **Emissions Control:** Modern FCCUs are fitted with **wet gas scrubbers** (for SOₓ), **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** (for NOₓ), and **electrostatic precipitators / bag filters** (for particulate).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air for Catalyst Regeneration

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** *A catalytic cracker processes 10,000 kg/h of VGO feed. The spent catalyst contains 1.0 wt% coke. The catalyst circulation rate is 600,000 kg/h. Calculate: (a) coke flow to regenerator, (b) stoichiometric O₂ requirement assuming complete combustion, (c) stoichiometric air requirement.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Feed flow rate, $F_{feed} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$
- Coke on spent catalyst, $C_c = 1.0\%$
- Catalyst circulation rate, $F_{cat} = 600{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$

**Step 1 — Mass of coke entering regenerator per hour:**

$$
m_{coke} = F_{cat} \times \frac{C_c}{100}
$$

$$
m_{coke} = 600{,}000 \times \frac{1.0}{100} = 6{,}000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Textual logic:** Every kilogram of spent catalyst carries 0.01 kg of coke; multiplied by total catalyst circulated per hour gives the coke flow entering the regenerator.

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ for complete combustion (C + O₂ → CO₂):**

The molecular masses involved are: C = **12 g/mol**, O₂ = **32 g/mol**, CO₂ = **44 g/mol**.

For complete combustion of 1 kg of carbon:

$$
\text{Mass of } O_2 \text{ per kg C} = \frac{32}{12} = 2.667 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg C}
$$

Therefore:

$$
m_{O_2} = m_{coke} \times \frac{32}{12}
$$

$$
m_{O_2} = 6{,}000 \times 2.667 = 16{,}000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Textual logic:** Each 12 mass units of carbon need 32 mass units of oxygen to form CO₂ — this is the classic mass-stoichiometric ratio.

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air (air contains 23.2 wt% O₂):**

$$
m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232}
$$

$$
m_{air} = \frac{16{,}000}{0.232} = 68{,}966 \text{ kg/h} \approx 68.97 \text{ tonnes/h}
$$

**Textual logic:** Since air is only 23.2% oxygen by mass, the required air mass is the oxygen mass divided by 0.232. The remaining 76.8% is nitrogen which does not participate in combustion but is essential for fluidization and temperature control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice:** Actual air supplied is **5 – 10 % in excess** of stoichiometric to ensure complete coke burn-off. So real air ≈ **72,400 – 75,850 kg/h** with a 5–10% excess margin.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion and Yield Calculation

**Problem Statement:** *A catalytic cracker processes 8,000 kg/h of feed. The unconverted heavy slurry (bottom product) is 1,200 kg/h. The cracked gasoline stream measures 3,600 kg/h. Calculate the overall conversion and the gasoline yield.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define Conversion:**

$$
X = \frac{F_{feed} - F_{unconv}}{F_{feed}} \times 100
$$

$$
X = \frac{8{,}000 - 1{,}200}{8{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
X = \frac{6{,}800}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 85.0\%
$$

**Textual logic:** Conversion represents the fraction of feed that is *cracked* into lighter products; the residue (unconverted slurry) is removed as a heavy stream.

**Step 2 — Gasoline Yield:**

$$
Y_{gasoline} = \frac{F_{gasoline}}{F_{feed}} \times 100
$$

$$
Y_{gasoline} = \frac{3{,}600}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 45.0\%
$$

**Textual logic:** Yield reports the mass fraction of a *specific* product relative to feed; it is always less than conversion because conversion is distributed across *all* products (gasoline + LPG + gas + coke).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Released in Regenerator (Energy Audit)

**Problem Statement:** *Using the coke flow of 6,000 kg/h from derivation 1, calculate the heat released in the regenerator per hour, and verify if it can sustain the cracking endotherm (assume 200 kJ/kg of feed cracked).*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Heat of combustion of coke:**

$$
Q_{regen} = m_{coke} \times \Delta H_{coke}
$$

$$
Q_{regen} = 6{,}000 \times 32{,}800 = 196{,}800{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 1.968 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Textual logic:** Combustion of coke is highly exothermic; this heat is the *primary energy source* that keeps the reactor hot.

**Step 2 — Heat required for endothermic cracking of feed:**

$$
Q_{crack} = F_{feed} \times \Delta H_{crack} = 10{,}000 \times 200 = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat Balance Check:**

$$
\text{Excess Heat} = Q_{regen} - Q_{crack} = 1.968 \times 10^8 - 2 \times 10^6 \approx 1.948 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Textual logic:** The regenerator releases ~98 times more heat than is needed for cracking. The **excess is recovered** in the CO boiler, main fractionator, and feed preheat exchangers. This is the *thermodynamic reason* the FCC is energy self-sufficient.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Safety Relevance

| Parameter | Typical Value | Safety Implication if Exceeded |
|---|---|---|
| Riser outlet temperature | 510 – 540 °C | Higher → increased dry-gas & coke → overcracking |
| Regenerator dense bed temp. | 650 – 720 °C | Higher → metallurgical creep, cyclone damage |
| O₂ in flue gas | 1 – 3 vol% | Higher → afterburning in dilute phase → EXCURSION |
| CO in flue gas | 50 – 500 ppm | Higher → incomplete combustion → CO hazard |
| Catalyst inventory | 100 – 400 tonnes | Higher → inventory of hot, hydrocarbon-coated catalyst |
| Air blower failure | Trip | Catalyst deactivation, partial burn, HCN/CO surge |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — FCCU (Riser–Regenerator Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FEEDSTOCK: VGO / ATM RESIDUE] --> A2[FEED PREHEAT TRAIN: 200-300 C]
    A2 --> A3[FEED NOZZLE INJECTION AT RISER BASE]
    A3 --> B1[RISER REACTOR: 510-540 C, 2-4 SEC]
    B1 --> B2[ROUGH AND FINISH CYCLONE SEPARATORS]
    B2 --> B3[CRACKED VAPOR TO MAIN FRACTIONATOR]
    B3 --> B4[PRODUCT FRACTIONS: GAS, LPG, GASOLINE, LCO, SLURRY]
    B2 --> C1[SPENT CATALYST TO STRIPPER]
    C1 --> C2[STEAM STRIPPING: REMOVE ENTRAINED HC]
    C2 --> D1[REGENERATOR: AIR BLOWN AT 680-720 C]
    D1 --> D2[COKE BURN OFF: C PLUS O2 GIVES CO2 PLUS HEAT]
    D2 --> D3[FLUE GAS CLEANUP: CYCLONE, ESP, CO BOILER]
    D3 --> D4[STACK EMISSION: SOX, NOX, CO, PARTICULATE]
    D1 --> E1[REGENERATED HOT CATALYST]
    E1 --> A3
    F1[SAFETY INSTRUMENTED SYSTEM: SIS] -.->|TRIPS| B1
    F1 -.->|TRIPS| D1
    F2[HAZOP NODE 1 RISER] -.->|HAZARD STUDY| B1
    F3[HAZOP NODE 2 REGEN] -.->|HAZARD STUDY| D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The two solid horizontal loops represent the **process flow** (feed → products). The diagonal arrow E1 → A3 represents the **catalyst recirculation loop** (regenerator → riser). The dashed nodes F1, F2, F3 represent **safety / process safety management overlays** — these are non-process, governance-level elements that KTU examiners love to question in safety-perspective problems.

## 4.2 Safety Interlock Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[RISER TEMPERATURE HIGH] --> S1[EMERGENCY BLOCK VALVE CLOSES]
    T2[REGEN O2 EXCESS 5 PERCENT] --> S2[AIR BLOWER TRIP]
    T3[CO IN FLUE GAS HIGH] --> S3[CO BOILER IGNITION ON]
    T4[FEED FLOW LOW] --> S4[CATALYST CIRCULATION TRIP]
    T5[COOLING WATER FAILURE] --> S5[FULL UNIT EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN]
    S5 --> U1[DEPRESSURIZE TO FLARE]
    S5 --> U2[STEAM PURGE TO RISER]
    S5 --> U3[ISOLATE FEED]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mapping:** This interlock logic mirrors real-world **IEC 61511 Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** architecture. The five trip initiators (T1–T5) feed into five final control elements (S1–S5) that together execute the **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** sequence on a triple-redundant logic solver.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is a catalytic cracker? Name any two products obtained from it.*

**Model Answer:**

A **catalytic cracker** is a refinery process unit that converts long-chain, high-molecular-weight hydrocarbon fractions (such as vacuum gas oil) into lighter, more valuable products by using a solid acid catalyst (typically a **zeolite**) at high temperature (480–540 °C) and near-atmospheric pressure.

The two main products are:
1. **Gasoline (petrol)** — the principal product, contributing to 35–50% of refinery gasoline pool.
2. **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)** — comprising propane, propylene, butane, and butylene, which are valuable feedstocks for petrochemicals.

*(Examiner's Note: A schematic sketch earns a 0.5-mark bonus, but is not mandatory for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Why is continuous catalyst regeneration essential in a catalytic cracker?*

**Model Answer:**

Continuous catalyst regeneration is essential because:

1. **Activity Loss:** During cracking, a carbonaceous deposit called **coke** (0.8 – 1.2 wt% on catalyst) forms on the catalyst surface, deactivating it.
2. **Heat Integration:** Burning off the coke in the regenerator releases **~32,800 kJ/kg of coke**, which is the *primary heat source* for the endothermic cracking reaction in the riser.
3. **Process Continuity:** Without regeneration, the catalyst would have to be replaced every few hours, making the process economically and operationally unviable.
4. **Product Quality:** A continuously regenerated catalyst maintains a **constant activity (~65–75)** and **constant selectivity**, ensuring consistent product yields.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Suggested Model]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **Fluid Catalytic Cracking Unit (FCCU)**. Highlight the role of the **riser reactor** and the **regenerator**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A catalytic cracker handles 12,000 kg/h of VGO feed. The catalyst circulation rate is 720,000 kg/h with 1.2 wt% coke on spent catalyst. Calculate:
  - (i) Coke flow to regenerator (kg/h)
  - (ii) Stoichiometric O₂ required for complete combustion (kg/h)
  - (iii) Stoichiometric air required (kg/h) **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a)

The FCCU consists of three interconnected sections:

1. **Riser Reactor:** The preheated VGO feed (200–300 °C) is injected at the base of the riser through feed nozzles where it contacts hot regenerated catalyst (~700 °C). The mixture rises with a **residence time of 2–5 seconds**, during which endothermic cracking reactions occur. Vapor–catalyst separation is achieved using rough and finish cyclones at the riser top.

2. **Stripper Section:** The coked catalyst falls into the stripper, where **steam injection** displaces entrained hydrocarbons. These hydrocarbons are sent to the main fractionator for product recovery.

3. **Regenerator:** The coked catalyst flows down into the regenerator, where **preheated air** is injected to burn off the coke at 650–720 °C. The regenerated hot catalyst is then lifted back to the riser base to repeat the cycle.

**Role of Riser Reactor:**
- Provides controlled, short-contact-time cracking environment
- Maximizes gasoline selectivity
- Minimizes secondary overcracking to dry gas

**Role of Regenerator:**
- Restores catalyst activity by burning off coke
- Supplies process heat via exothermic coke combustion
- Maintains steady-state catalyst circulation

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Given:**
- $F_{feed} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$
- $F_{cat} = 720{,}000 \text{ kg/h}$
- $C_c = 1.2\%$

**(i) Coke flow to regenerator:**

$$
m_{coke} = F_{cat} \times \frac{C_c}{100} = 720{,}000 \times 0.012 = 8{,}640 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**(ii) Stoichiometric O₂ for complete combustion (C + O₂ → CO₂):**

$$
m_{O_2} = m_{coke} \times \frac{32}{12} = 8{,}640 \times 2.667 = 23{,}042 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**(iii) Stoichiometric air required:**

$$
m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{23{,}042}{0.232} = 99{,}320 \text{ kg/h} \approx 99.3 \text{ tonnes/h}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **major safety hazards** associated with a catalytic cracking unit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A catalytic cracker produces the following products per 1000 kg of feed: Gasoline 480 kg, LPG 110 kg, Dry Gas 60 kg, LCO 220 kg, Coke 80 kg, Slurry 50 kg. Calculate the **conversion (%)** and **gasoline yield (%)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a)

The major safety hazards in a catalytic cracker are:

1. **Explosion Hazard in Regenerator:** The regenerator contains a hot mixture of catalyst and air. Any hydrocarbon leak into the regenerator creates a flammable mixture, leading to **deflagration or detonation**. A real-world incident: the 2015 Torrance refinery explosion originated in the FCCU.

2. **Hot Surface Burns:** Reactor and regenerator operate at 500–750 °C, creating serious burn and structural-deformation hazards.

3. **Catalyst Dust Explosion:** FCC catalyst is a fine powder (<80 µm). In the presence of an ignition source, it can cause **dust explosions**, classified under ISO/IEC 80079-20-2.

4. **CO and SOₓ Toxicity:** Incomplete combustion in the regenerator releases **CO** (toxic) and **SOₓ** (corrosive, toxic). Workers in confined spaces are at risk.

5. **High-Pressure Steam and Water Hammer:** Steam injection systems operate at >10 bar; rupture can cause severe burns.

6. **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S):** Sour feedstocks release H₂S, which is lethal at concentrations above 700 ppm.

7. **Mechanical Failure of Cyclones:** Cyclone failure leads to catalyst entrainment into downstream equipment, causing erosion, plugging, and inventory loss.

8. **Runaway Exothermic Regeneration:** Excess air in the regenerator can cause **afterburning** in the dilute phase, leading to temperature excursions up to 800 °C — beyond metallurgical limits.

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Given Product Distribution per 1000 kg feed:**

| Product | Mass (kg) |
|---|---|
| Gasoline | 480 |
| LPG | 110 |
| Dry Gas | 60 |
| LCO | 220 |
| Coke | 80 |
| Slurry (unconverted) | 50 |
| **Total** | **1000** |

**Step 1 — Define Conversion:**

Conversion is the fraction of feed converted to *useful* lighter products. The unconverted fraction is the **slurry (bottoms)**.

$$
X = \frac{F_{feed} - F_{slurry}}{F_{feed}} \times 100
$$

$$
X = \frac{1000 - 50}{1000} \times 100 = 95.0\%
$$

**Step 2 — Gasoline Yield:**

$$
Y_{gasoline} = \frac{480}{1000} \times 100 = 48.0\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing Yield with Conversion:** Conversion is *total* lighter products; Yield is *one specific* product. KTU examiners deduct 2–3 marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Incorrect Air Composition:** Using 21% O₂ (volume) instead of **23.2% O₂ (mass)** leads to wrong air-flow answers. Always use **mass-based O₂ fraction for mass calculations**.
> 3. **Forgetting Steam Stripping:** Skipping the description of the stripper in part (a) costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Unit Errors:** Expressing coke in tonnes/h but air in kg/h loses consistency marks.
> 5. **No Diagram in Part A:** KTU valuation key awards **1 mark** for a properly labelled block diagram of FCCU — students often skip this and lose easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Definition:** A catalytic cracker (FCCU) converts heavy oils → gasoline, LPG, olefins using a zeolite catalyst in a riser–regenerator loop.
- **Operating Conditions:** Riser 510–540 °C, Regenerator 650–720 °C, near-atmospheric pressure.
- **Catalyst:** Zeolite (Y-zeolite / ZSM-5) as 60–80 µm powder; fluidized by vapor.
- **Residence Time in Riser:** Only 2–5 seconds — short contact to favor gasoline.
- **Coke on Catalyst:** 0.8–1.2 wt% on spent catalyst.
- **Heat of Coke Combustion:** 32,800 kJ/kg — primary heat source for the unit.
- **Stoichiometric O₂ for Coke:** 2.667 kg O₂ / kg C.
- **Air Requirement:** 11.49 kg air / kg coke (theoretical).
- **Conversion Formula:** $X = \dfrac{F_{feed} - F_{unconv}}{F_{feed}} \times 100$.
- **Yield Formula:** $Y_i = \dfrac{F_{product,i}}{F_{feed}} \times 100$.
- **C/O Ratio:** Typically 5–8 (dimensionless).
- **WHSV:** Typically 5–20 h⁻¹.
- **Major Hazards:** Regenerator explosion, dust explosion, CO/SOₓ/H₂S toxicity, afterburning, cyclone failure.
- **Safety Interlocks:** Riser T high → feed block; O₂ excess → air blower trip; CO high → CO boiler ignition; ESD sequence (depressure, steam purge, isolate).
- **Standards:** Designed to API 650/660; governed by OSHA PSM, MSIHC 1989, IEC 61511.
- **Always draw a labelled block diagram** in any 7-mark descriptive question — it earns 1 guaranteed mark.
- **Always state units** in every numerical answer.
- **Always distinguish** *conversion* from *yield* in calculations.
- **Always include** the **stripper section** in process flow descriptions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reformer
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reformer

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Reformer** (specifically a *Catalytic Reformer*) is a critical secondary processing unit in a petroleum refinery that converts low-octane, straight-run naphtha feedstocks (typically C₆–C₁₂ hydrocarbons) into high-octane, aromatic-rich reformate gasoline blending stock, while simultaneously producing a valuable byproduct stream of **hydrogen-rich gas** used elsewhere in the hydrotreating and hydrocracking units.

The process operates on the fundamental chemistry of three reversible, endothermic reactions performed in series over a platinum (Pt) or platinum-rhenium (Pt-Re) catalyst:

$$ \text{Paraffins} \xrightarrow{\text{Dehydrogenation}} \text{Naphthenes} \xrightarrow{\text{Isomerization}} \text{Isoparaffins} \xrightarrow{\text{Dehydrocyclization}} \text{Aromatics} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Reformer is a *high-temperature, high-pressure, hydrogen-rich* process unit. From a **safety engineering perspective**, it is classified as one of the most hazardous units in a refinery because it simultaneously hosts three classic accident preconditions: (1) flammable hydrocarbons, (2) explosive hydrogen atmospheres, and (3) extreme thermal energy.

## 1.2 Operating Window (Standard Process Conditions)

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Reactor Inlet Temperature | **480 °C – 525 °C** |
| Reactor Pressure | **3.5 bar – 35 bar** (depending on type) |
| H₂ / Hydrocarbon Molar Ratio | **3 : 1 to 6 : 1** |
| Space Velocity (LHSV) | **1.5 hr⁻¹ – 2.5 hr⁻¹** |
| Research Octane Number (RON) of Product | **95 – 102** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Tawa" Intuition

Imagine you are cooking dosas on a hot tawa. You pour the liquid batter (raw naphtha) onto a very hot surface (heated catalyst in a hydrogen atmosphere). The heat transforms the soft, flat batter into a stiff, golden-brown, aromatic crisp (reformate with high octane). But while the tawa is hot enough to cook, it is *also* hot enough to burn the house down if oil drips, the gas leaks, or the surface cracks. The **Reformer is exactly this situation** — it is a controlled "high-heat cooking chamber" for hydrocarbons, and a single tube rupture, catalyst channeling, or hydrogen leak can escalate into a **vapor cloud explosion (VCE)** or a **jet fire** within seconds. The safety philosophy of the Reformer is therefore: *control the heat, control the hydrogen, and control the catalyst — or else the tawa catches the kitchen on fire.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Reformer vs. Cracker:** Students often confuse these. A *Reformer* upgrades octane (builds rings/aromatics — small molecules stay small). A *Cracker* breaks heavy long-chain molecules into smaller ones. Different chemistry, different safety profile, different unit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Generations of Reformer Design

The safety architecture of a reformer is inseparable from its regeneration philosophy, because **catalyst coking** is the single largest operational hazard that drives emergency shutdowns.

### 2.1.1 Semi-Regenerative Reformer (SRR)
- **Operating Pressure:** 10 – 35 bar
- **Catalyst Life:** 6 – 12 months between shutdowns
- **Logic:** Operate → Coke accumulates → Shutdown entire unit → Regenerate offline
- **Safety Note:** Older design; higher pressure means *more inventory of hydrocarbons* stored in the unit at any moment, increasing BLEVE/pool-fire consequence radius.

### 2.1.2 Cyclic Reformer
- **Operating Pressure:** 5 – 15 bar
- **Catalyst Life:** Continuous (spare reactor swing)
- **Logic:** 3 reactors in service, 1 swing reactor undergoing regeneration *while the others operate*
- **Safety Note:** Eliminates unit-wide shutdowns but introduces *complex interlock logic* — faulty swing-valve sequencing is a common cause of unreacted oxygen ingress into hydrocarbon lines.

### 2.1.3 Continuous Catalytic Regeneration (CCR) Reformer — UOP Platforming™
- **Operating Pressure:** 0.5 – 3.5 bar (very low!)
- **Catalyst Life:** Effectively permanent (continuous regeneration in moving bed)
- **Logic:** Catalyst circulates continuously between reactor stack and regenerator
- **Safety Note:** Low pressure = lower hydrocarbon inventory = *smaller explosion radius*. However, the catalyst circulation system introduces **dust explosion hazards** and **hydrogen fluoride / chlorocarbon contamination** risks.

## 2.2 The Four Primary Reactions and Their Hazard Signatures

| # | Reaction | ΔH (kJ/mol) | Hazard Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Naphthene → Aromatic (Dehydrogenation) | **+205** (strongly endothermic) | Massive heat demand → furnace tube hot spots |
| 2 | Paraffin → Isoparaffin (Isomerization) | **−5 to −15** (mildly exothermic) | Moderate heat release, lower priority |
| 3 | Paraffin → Aromatic (Dehydrocyclization) | **+250** (most endothermic) | Highest heat flux zone — tube metallurgy limit |
| 4 | Hydrocracking (side reaction) | **−60** (exothermic) | Runaway risk; produces light gases (C₁–C₄) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two endothermic reactions (1 and 3) require continuous external firing in the **reformer furnace**. This is why a Reformer is, structurally, a *furnace* bolted to *reactors*. Furnace tube failure is the **#1 mechanical cause** of major reformer accidents globally.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Research Octane Number (RON) | $RON = 100 - \dfrac{K \cdot \sum w_i \cdot \text{Paraffin}_i}{1 + \alpha \cdot \text{Aromatics}}$ | dimensionless | Reformer performance |
| 2 | Hydrogen Yield | $Y_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{H_2, \text{out}} - n_{H_2, \text{in}}}{n_{\text{feed}}}$ | **SCF/bbl** or **Nm³/m³** | Reformer economics & safety venting |
| 3 | LHSV (Liquid Hourly Space Velocity) | $LHSV = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed}}}{V_{\text{cat}} \cdot t}$ | **hr⁻¹** | Reactor sizing, coking rate |
| 4 | Heat of Reaction (Reactor) | $Q_{rxn} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \cdot \sum x_i \cdot \Delta H_i$ | **kW** or **kcal/hr** | Furnace duty calculation |
| 5 | Flammability Limit (Reformer Gas) | $LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$ | **% vol** | Explosion hazard zoning |
| 6 | TNT Equivalency (VCE) | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ | **kg TNT** | Blast damage radius |
| 7 | Radiation Heat Flux (Jet Fire) | $q'' = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \dot{m}'' \cdot \Delta H_c}{4 \pi r^2}$ | **kW/m²** | Thermal radiation distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU papers, *Formula 5 (Le Chatelier's Rule for Flammability)* and *Formula 6 (TNT Equivalency)* are the most frequently tested in the context of Reformer safety. Memorize the denominator structure of both.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Refinery Engineering

The Reformer is the **heart of the gasoline pool** in any modern refinery. Almost every litre of petrol sold in India originates (after blending) from a catalytic reformer. The hydrogen byproduct is so valuable that reformers are often called the **"hydrogen factories"** of a refinery — they supply the H₂ needed by hydrodesulfurization (HDS) units to make ULSD (Bharat Stage VI diesel). From a **plant safety management** perspective, the reformer sits at the intersection of three OSHA / OISD hazard categories: *Process Safety Management (PSM)*, *Mechanical Integrity (MI)*, and *Hot Work / Confined Space* protocols.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reformer Furnace Heat Duty (Fired-Heater Energy Balance)

The reformer furnace supplies the endothermic heat to drive reactions 1 and 3. The design of this furnace is the *single most safety-critical* calculation for the unit.

### Step 1 — Define the Control Volume
We draw a control volume around the radiant section of the furnace, enclosing the reactor charge, the catalyst tubes, and the flue gas.

$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{furnace}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{reaction}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{sensible}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}} $$

### Step 2 — Reaction Heat Term
For a feed containing $n_i$ moles of species $i$ converted per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{rxn} &= \sum_{i} \dot{n}_{i,\text{conv}} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn,i} \\[4pt]
&= \dot{n}_{\text{Paraffin}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{isom}} + \dot{n}_{\text{Naphthene}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{dehyd}} + \dot{n}_{\text{Paraffin}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{cycl}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** We sum the molar conversion rate of each reaction multiplied by its respective enthalpy change. Endothermic reactions have positive $\Delta H$, so they absorb heat — meaning the furnace must *supply* this energy, increasing the firing rate.

### Step 3 — Sensible Heat Term
The feed enters the furnace at reactor inlet temperature $T_{\text{in}}$ and is heated to peak tube-metal temperature $T_{\text{wall}}$.

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{sensible}} = \dot{m} \cdot \int_{T_{\text{in}}}^{T_{\text{wall}}} C_p(T) \, dT
$$

For a typical naphtha, average $C_p \approx$ **2.5 kJ/kg·K**.

### Step 4 — Solve for Radiant Heat Flux
For a furnace with $N$ parallel catalyst tubes, each of outer diameter $D_o$ and active heated length $L$:

$$
q''_{\text{radiant}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{furnace}}}{N \cdot \pi D_o L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Constraint:** Tube manufacturers specify a maximum allowable heat flux of approximately **75,000 kcal/hr·m²** for reformer service. If $q''_{\text{radiant}}$ exceeds this value, *coking accelerates* and the tube metal creeps toward its **creep-rupture temperature** (typically 850 °C for HK-40 alloy). This is the initiation point of most reformer tube ruptures.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example
**Given:**
- Naphtha feed: 100,000 kg/hr
- Conversion: 60 % naphthenes, 20 % paraffins
- $\Delta H_{\text{dehyd}} = 205$ kJ/mol; $\Delta H_{\text{cycl}} = 250$ kJ/mol
- Mean molecular weight of naphtha: 110 g/mol

**Find:** Furnace heat duty $\dot{Q}_{\text{furnace}}$ (in MW).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{\text{feed}} &= \dfrac{100{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}}{0.110 \text{ kg/mol} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr}} \\[4pt]
&= 252.5 \text{ mol/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now apply the conversion fractions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{\text{naphthene,conv}} &= 0.60 \times 252.5 = 151.5 \text{ mol/s} \\[4pt]
\dot{n}_{\text{paraffin,conv}} &= 0.20 \times 252.5 = 50.5 \text{ mol/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the reaction heat:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{rxn} &= (151.5)(205{,}000) + (50.5)(250{,}000) \text{ W} \\[4pt]
&= 31.06 \times 10^6 + 12.63 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= 43.69 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding sensible heat and 5 % radiation loss (typical):

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{furnace}} \approx 43.69 \times 1.15 \approx \mathbf{50.2 \text{ MW}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A 50 MW fired heater represents the same continuous heat release as a small town burning. Loss of flame supervision for even **30 seconds** results in unburned fuel accumulation in the firebox, with subsequent *deflagration-to-detonation* risk on reignition.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Reformer Hazard × Cause × Consequence × Safeguard

| Hazard | Root Cause | Consequence | Engineered Safeguard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Furnace tube rupture | Creep, coking, hot spot | **Jet fire**, naphtha pool | Tube metal temp TIH, vibration monitoring |
| Hydrogen explosion | Compressor seal failure, leak | **VCE**, blast overpressure | Hydrogen detectors, flame arrestors |
| Catalyst dust explosion | CCR regenerator cyclone failure | **Primary dust explosion** + secondary | Inert N₂ purge, explosion vents |
| Naphtha release | Pump seal, flange gasket | Pool fire, vapor flash | ESD-1 system, dyke containment |
| Oxygen ingress | Regenerator air slide failure | **VCE inside reactor** | Oxygen analyzers, interlock to block feed |
| H₂S exposure (feed) | Sour crude slip into feed | Toxic atmosphere | H₂S monitors, SCBA, LEL/H₂S combo |
| Chlorocarbon release | Catalyst promoter leak | HF or HCl fume | Caustic scrubber, neutralization pit |

## 3.3 Derivation: LFL of Reformer Recycle Gas (Le Chatelier)

Reformer recycle gas is a mixture of H₂, CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, C₄H₁₀. To find the lower flammability limit of the *mixture*:

$$
LFL_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}
$$

**Given composition (mol %):**
- H₂: 70 % (LFL = 4.0 %)
- CH₄: 15 % (LFL = 5.0 %)
- C₂H₆: 8 % (LFL = 3.0 %)
- C₃H₈: 5 % (LFL = 2.1 %)
- C₄H₁₀: 2 % (LFL = 1.8 %)

$$
\begin{aligned}
LFL_{\text{mix}} &= \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{70}{4.0} + \dfrac{15}{5.0} + \dfrac{8}{3.0} + \dfrac{5}{2.1} + \dfrac{2}{1.8}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{100}{17.5 + 3.0 + 2.667 + 2.381 + 1.111} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{100}{26.659} \\[6pt]
&\approx \mathbf{3.75 \text{ \% vol}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The mixture is more flammable than pure methane (LFL 5 %) because hydrogen dominates. Any leak forming a *vapor cloud above 3.75 %* concentration in air will ignite if a source is present. This is why reformer areas are classified **Zone 1 / Class I Div 1** hazardous locations under IEC 60079 and NEC standards.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reformer Unit — Process Flow Diagram (PFD)

The diagram below maps the principal equipment, the process flow direction, and the three major safety instrumented functions (SIFs) that govern emergency shutdowns.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Naphtha Feed Drum] --> B[Feed Pump P101]
    B --> C[Feed Effluent Heat Exchanger E101]
    C --> D[Reformer Furnace F101 Radiant Section]
    D --> E[Reactor R1 First Bed]
    E --> F[Interheater IH1]
    F --> G[Reactor R2 Second Bed]
    G --> H[Interheater IH2]
    H --> I[Reactor R3 Third Bed]
    I --> J[Reactor R4 Fourth Bed]
    J --> K[Product Separator V101]
    K --> L[Recycle Gas Compressor C101]
    L --> M[Net Gas to H2 Plant]
    K --> N[Reformate Stabilizer Column T101]
    N --> O[Reformate Product to Gasoline Pool]
    D -. Hot Flue Gas .-> P[Convection Section E102]
    P --> Q[Stack to Atmosphere]
    K -. SIF ESD1 .-> R[Emergency Shutdown Logic]
    D -. SIF TIH .-> R
    L -. SIF H2 Detector .-> R
    R -. Block Valves .-> A
    R -. Block Valves .-> B
    R -. Block Valves .-> L
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **four reactors in series** (R1 → R4) with **interheaters** between them is the classic *fired-heater + adiabatic reactor stack* arrangement.
- **SIF ESD-1** (Emergency Shutdown Level 1) blocks feed, stops compressor, and vents the furnace on detection of any hazardous condition.
- **SIF TIH** (Tube-Induced Hot spot) trips the burners if any catalyst tube exceeds its metallurgical limit (~ 850 °C).
- **SIF H₂** isolates the recycle gas compressor on detection of explosive atmosphere near the seal pot.

## 4.2 Reformer Emergency Shutdown Logic — Cause & Effect Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[Furnace Tube MT High] --> A1[ESD1 Initiated]
    C2[H2 Detector High] --> A1
    C3[Separator LL High] --> A1
    C4[Compressor Trip] --> A1
    C5[Feed Pump Trip] --> A1
    A1 --> X1[Close Feed Block Valves]
    A1 --> X2[Stop Recycle Compressor]
    A1 --> X3[Open Depressuring Valves to Flare]
    A1 --> X4[Cut Burner Firing Gas]
    A1 --> X5[Activate Fire Water Deluge]
```

## 4.3 CCR Reformer — Catalyst Regeneration Loop (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Reactor Stack R1 R2 R3 R4] --> CV[Catalyst Lift Pipe]
    CV --> RG[Regenerator Vessel]
    RG --> CB[Catalyst Reduction Zone]
    CB --> CV
    RG -. Spent Catalyst .-> CV
    CB -. Regenerated Catalyst .-> CV
    RG --> CY[Cyclone Separator]
    CY --> ST[Stack with N2 Inert Blanket]
    CY -. Spent Air .-> ST
    O2[Oxygen Analyzer 2 percent] --> ESD2[Regenerator ESD]
    CO[CO Analyzer 1000 ppm] --> ESD2
    ESD2 --> X[Block Air Slide Dampers]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the CCR configuration, the catalyst physically moves between the reactor (high pressure) and the regenerator (low pressure) via a *lock hopper* and a *lift gas* (typically nitrogen). Any failure of the lock hopper sequence is a classic cause of hydrocarbon → regenerator backflow, leading to *internal deflagration* of the catalyst bed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a catalytic reformer. List its three principal reactions.**

**Model Answer:**
A catalytic reformer is a refinery process unit that upgrades low-octane straight-run naphtha into high-octane, aromatic-rich reformate using a platinum or platinum-rhenium catalyst in a hydrogen-rich atmosphere at 480–525 °C.

The three principal reactions are:
1. **Dehydrogenation of naphthenes to aromatics** (e.g., methylcyclohexane → toluene)
2. **Isomerization of n-paraffins to iso-paraffins** (e.g., n-hexane → iso-hexane)
3. **Dehydrocyclization of paraffins to aromatics** (e.g., n-heptane → toluene)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the role of hydrogen in a catalytic reformer. Why is the H₂/HC ratio critical for safety?**

**Model Answer:**
Hydrogen serves two purposes: (a) it suppresses coke formation on the catalyst surface by maintaining a reducing atmosphere, and (b) it acts as a heat-transfer medium distributing endothermic reaction heat.

The H₂/HC ratio (typically 3:1 to 6:1) is **critical for safety** because:
- Too low → accelerated coking → hot spots → tube rupture
- Too high → larger hydrogen inventory in the loop → larger explosion potential in case of compressor seal failure
- Loss of hydrogen circulation (e.g., compressor trip) without immediate feed cutoff leads to **localized runaway coking** within 60–90 seconds

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE (End Semester Examination) carries full internal choice. Attempt ANY ONE of the two.**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Reformer Process & Safety [KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **semi-regenerative catalytic reformer**. List its major equipment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major **fire and explosion hazards** associated with a reformer unit, and explain the engineered safety systems used to mitigate them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

A semi-regenerative catalytic reformer operates in a fixed-bed mode with the following sequence:

1. **Naphtha feed** from the feed drum is pressurized by a feed pump and preheated in a feed-effluent heat exchanger (FEHE) by exchange with hot reactor effluent.
2. The preheated feed enters the **fired furnace** (radiant section), where it reaches reaction temperature of ~ 525 °C.
3. The hot vapor passes sequentially through **four fixed-bed reactors** (R-1 to R-4), with **interheaters** between them to reheat the stream and compensate for the endothermic heat of reaction.
4. The reactor effluent is cooled in the FEHE and sent to the **product separator**, where the **recycle hydrogen** is taken overhead and recycled by the **recycle gas compressor**; the liquid bottoms (reformate) goes to the **stabilizer column** for removal of dissolved light hydrocarbons.
5. After 6–12 months of operation, the unit is **shutdown** and the catalyst is **regenerated in situ** by controlled burn-off of coke with diluted air.

**Major equipment list:**
- Feed pump
- Feed-effluent heat exchanger
- Recharge furnace (radiant + convection section)
- 4 × Reactors with fixed catalyst beds
- 3 × Interheaters
- Product separator
- Recycle gas compressor
- Stabilizer column
- Net hydrogen scrubber

#### Model Solution to A(b):

The major **fire and explosion hazards** of a reformer are:

1. **Furnace tube rupture** — A tube failure releases hot naphtha into the firebox, forming a **jet fire** of approximately 50 MW. **Safeguard:** Tube-Induced Hot spot (TIH) detectors on each tube, periodic infrared thermography, mandatory tube inspection every 5 years per OISD-150.

2. **Hydrogen cloud explosion** — A recycle compressor seal failure can release a flammable H₂/CH₄/C₂H₆ mixture. With a mixture LFL of ~ 3.75 %, a leak of 1 tonne reaches LFL radius of ~ 25 m. **Safeguard:** Hydrogen detectors at Grade level, flame arrestors on vent stacks, ESD-1 isolation of compressor within 5 seconds.

3. **Catalyst regeneration hazards** — If air (oxygen) enters the hydrocarbon side due to valve misalignment, the reactor bed deflagrates. **Safeguard:** Positive sealing of swing valves, oxygen analyzer in regeneration loop, interlock that blocks feed if regenerator O₂ > 0.5 %.

4. **Naphtha pool fire** — Pump seal or flange leaks form a pool that flashes. **Safeguard:** Dyked pump area, foam pourers, fire water deluge system on critical pumps.

5. **Toxic gas exposure** — Feedstock may contain dissolved H₂S. **Safeguard:** Fixed H₂S detectors, SCBAs at strategic points, wind-direction indicators.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - **Do NOT** write "Reformer is safe because it operates under hydrogen pressure." Hydrogen is *not* a safety feature — it is a **major hazard**. Hydrogen's wide flammability range (4–75 % in air) and high flame speed make it *more* dangerous than most hydrocarbons.
> - **Do NOT** confuse the **Furnace** with the **Reactors**. They are two distinct pieces of equipment. Many students lose 2–3 marks for swapping them.
> - **Always state numerical values** (e.g., "525 °C," "3:1 H₂/HC") — vague answers like "high temperature" score zero in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the differences between **Semi-Regenerative**, **Cyclic**, and **Continuous Catalyst Regeneration (CCR)** reformers. Comment on their relative safety profiles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reformer furnace has 200 catalyst tubes of 0.10 m outer diameter and 8 m active length. Calculate the maximum allowable heat flux if the design heat input is 45 MW. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

| Feature | Semi-Regenerative (SRR) | Cyclic | CCR (Continuous) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating Pressure** | 10–35 bar (highest) | 5–15 bar | 0.5–3.5 bar (lowest) |
| **Catalyst Regeneration** | Offline, every 6–12 mo | Spare reactor swing | Continuous moving bed |
| **H₂ Inventory** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Explosion Consequence** | Largest VCE radius | Moderate | Smallest VCE radius |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest (lock hoppers, lift lines) |
| **Coking Sensitivity** | High (needs H₂/HC ~ 6:1) | Lower | Very low |
| **Safety Profile** | Highest inventory hazard | Intermediate | Lowest inventory, but **highest complexity hazard** |

**Safety inference:** Although CCR has the lowest hydrocarbon inventory and therefore smallest explosion radius, its *complexity* introduces new failure modes (lock hopper miscycle, catalyst dust explosion, lift gas failure). A proper HAZOP study will identify roughly **20–25 % more nodes** in a CCR than in a Semi-Regenerative unit.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- Number of tubes: $N = 200$
- Outer diameter: $D_o = 0.10$ m
- Active length: $L = 8$ m
- Heat input: $\dot{Q} = 45$ MW $= 45 \times 10^6$ W

**Formula:**

$$
q''_{\text{radiant}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{N \cdot \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the total heat-transfer surface area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{total}} &= N \cdot \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L \\[4pt]
&= 200 \times \pi \times 0.10 \times 8 \\[4pt]
&= 200 \times 2.5133 \\[4pt]
&= 502.65 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the heat flux:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q''_{\text{radiant}} &= \dfrac{45 \times 10^6 \text{ W}}{502.65 \text{ m}^2} \\[4pt]
&= 89{,}528 \text{ W/m}^2 \\[4pt]
&\approx \mathbf{89.5 \text{ kW/m}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to common engineering units:

$$
q''_{\text{radiant}} = 89.5 \text{ kW/m}^2 \times \dfrac{1 \text{ kcal}}{4.184 \text{ kJ}} \times \dfrac{3600 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ hr}} \approx 77{,}000 \text{ kcal/hr·m}^2
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This flux (77,000 kcal/hr·m²) is **right at the metallurgical limit** for HK-40 reformer tubes. Any increase in feed rate, drop in recycle gas flow, or rise in coking tendency will push tubes into the *creep-rupture regime*, dramatically shortening tube life. The implication for **safety** is that this furnace should be equipped with **TIH (Tube-Induced Hot spot) interlocks** to trip firing if any tube exceeds 850 °C surface temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert MW → W (multiply by 10⁶) and end up with an answer 1,000,000 times too small. KTU examiner will deduct 1 mark for the unit error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- **Definition:** Reformer = naphtha-to-high-octane-aromatics unit, Pt catalyst, 480–525 °C.
- **Three reactions:** Dehydrogenation, Isomerization, Dehydrocyclization — first and third are strongly endothermic.
- **Three reformer types:** SRR (high pressure, periodic regen) / Cyclic (swing reactor) / CCR (continuous, lowest pressure).
- **Top 5 hazards (must memorize):** Furnace tube rupture, H₂ cloud explosion, catalyst regeneration deflagration, naphtha pool fire, H₂S exposure.
- **Critical numbers to memorize:** Reactor inlet 525 °C, H₂/HC = 3:1 to 6:1, RON product = 95–102, reformer gas LFL ≈ 3.75 %.
- **Three SIFs:** ESD-1 (general trip), TIH (tube hot spot), H₂ detector → compressor trip.
- **Furnace = fired heater + reactors**: two distinct equipment, do NOT confuse.
- **Tube metallurgy:** HK-40 alloy, max flux 75,000 kcal/hr·m², creep limit 850 °C.
- **Hydrogen is a HAZARD, not a safety feature** — most common conceptual error in exams.
- **Key formulas to remember:** Le Chatelier LFL mixture, TNT equivalency, radiant heat flux, LHSV, H₂ yield.

> [!NOTE]
