> For higher grades in KTU 2024, always **quote OISD / NFPA / IS standards** in your safety answers. Examiners reward the use of standards. Common ones: OISD-STD-105 (Work Permit), OISD-STD-114 (Hazards in Hydrocarbon), NFPA 30 (Flammable Liquids), API RP 521 (Pressure Relief).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Treating Unit (Hydroforming)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Treating Unit (Hydroforming)

# Treating Unit (Hydroforming) — KTU Premier Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydroforming** is a non-catalytic (or early catalytic) thermal reforming process used in petroleum refineries to upgrade low-octane naphtha fractions into high-octane gasoline blending stocks and aromatic-rich streams by passing hydrocarbon vapors mixed with a hydrogen-rich recycle gas over a fixed-bed catalyst at elevated temperatures and moderate pressures. The process was pioneered in the **1930s** and represents the **first commercial application of catalytic reforming**, eventually evolving into modern catalytic reformers (Platforming, Magnaforming, and continuous regeneration reformers such as UOP CCR Platforming).

Mathematically, the hydroforming process can be represented by the generalized reaction network:

$$\text{Naphtha} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}, \Delta} \text{Aromatics} + \text{Isoparaffins} + \text{H}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Hydroforming is the foundational treating/reforming technology. Every modern reformer (CCR Platforming, Semi-Regen, Aromatic Complex units) is its direct industrial descendant. For the Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries course, focus on **process chemistry, unit operations, equipment integrity, and hazard mitigation** — these are the high-yield areas for Part B questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" of the Refinery

Imagine you have a bowl of raw dough (low-octane naphtha) that you wish to bake into a light, fluffy, golden cake (high-octane gasoline). You cannot do this simply by leaving the dough on the counter — it needs both **heat** and a **chemical environment** that transforms its structure from dense and bland to porous and aromatic.

In hydroforming:
- The **oven** is the catalytic reactor (heater + reactor train).
- The **hydrogen gas** is the leavening agent that prevents the dough from "charring" into useless coke.
- The **catalyst** (molybdena on alumina, MoO₃/Al₂O₃) is the yeast — it lowers the energy barrier so that naphthene rings break open, lose hydrogen, and re-form as benzene rings (aromatics), which have very high octane numbers (100+).
- The **temperature** (≈ **500 °C**) supplies the energy for the rearrangement; the **moderate pressure** (≈ **14–35 bar**) keeps everything in the gas phase and prevents the catalyst from coking prematurely.

So, in plain English: **Hydroforming is "baking" naphtha with hydrogen to make aromatic, high-octane gasoline — safely and continuously.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Reactor Inlet Temperature | **480–540** | °C |
| Reactor Pressure | **14–35** (≈ 200–500 psi) | bar |
| Hydrogen-to-Hydrocarbon Molar Ratio | **2 : 1 to 5 : 1** | mol/mol |
| Space Velocity (LHSV) | **1.0–3.0** | h⁻¹ |
| Catalyst | **MoO₃ on Al₂O₃** (10 wt%) | — |
| Hydrogen Auto-Ignition Temperature | **571** | °C |
| Hydrogen Flammability Range in Air | **4 – 75** | vol % |
| Octane Number of Product (RON) | **80–95** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — LHSV (Liquid Hourly Space Velocity):**
> $$\text{LHSV} = \frac{\text{Volumetric feed rate of liquid naphtha (m}^3/\text{h)}}{\text{Volume of catalyst bed (m}^3)}$$
> This dimensionless-on-time metric tells the operator how long the oil "dwells" in the catalyst bed. Lower LHSV = more reaction time = more aromatics, but also more coke.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Process Chemistry — The Four Reaction Families

Hydroforming chemistry can be decomposed into **four dominant reaction classes** operating simultaneously in the catalyst bed:

#### Reaction 1 — Naphthene Dehydrogenation (the dominant, desirable reaction)
Cyclohexane → Benzene + 3 H₂
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + 3\,\text{H}_2 \qquad \Delta H \approx +205\,\text{kJ/mol (endothermic)}$$

#### Reaction 2 — Paraffin Isomerization
n-Hexane → iso-Hexane (2,2-dimethylbutane)
$$\text{n-C}_6\text{H}_{14} \longleftrightarrow \text{i-C}_6\text{H}_{14} \qquad \Delta H \approx \text{small, near zero}$$

#### Reaction 3 — Paraffin Dehydrocyclization
n-Heptane → Toluene + 4 H₂
$$\text{n-C}_7\text{H}_{16} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_3 + 4\,\text{H}_2 \qquad \Delta H \approx +250\,\text{kJ/mol (endothermic)}$$

#### Reaction 4 — Hydrocracking (undesirable side reaction)
n-Decane + H₂ → n-Pentane + n-Pentane
$$\text{n-C}_{10}\text{H}_{22} + \text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12} \qquad \Delta H \approx -50\,\text{kJ/mol (exothermic)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why hydrogen?** Because three of the four reactions are endothermic and produce hydrogen. Excess H₂ in the feed pushes the **dehydrogenation equilibrium toward aromatics** (Le Chatelier's principle) and simultaneously **suppresses coke** by hydrogenating coke precursors before they polymerize on the catalyst.

### 2.2 Why "Hydro"-forming and not just "Reforming"?

The "Hydro" prefix denotes the **co-feeding of a hydrogen-rich recycle gas** with the naphtha charge. This distinguishes it from purely thermal reforming (where no external hydrogen is added and severe coking occurs within hours). The hydrogen stream:
1. Maintains catalyst activity by **suppressing coke laydown** (coke + 2 H₂ → CH₄, etc.).
2. Controls the **heat balance** because the hydrocracking exotherm partly offsets the dehydrogenation endotherm.
3. Provides a **safety blanket** — hydrogen has very high thermal conductivity, which moderates local hot spots.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{LHSV} = \dfrac{F_l}{V_c}$ | $F_l$ = liquid feed rate; $V_c$ = catalyst volume | h⁻¹ | Reactor sizing |
| 2 | $\text{GHSV} = \dfrac{F_v}{V_c}$ | $F_v$ = gas volumetric flow | h⁻¹ | Gas-phase contact time |
| 3 | $\text{H}_2/\text{HC} = \dfrac{n_{H_2}}{n_{HC}}$ | Molar hydrogen-to-oil ratio | mol/mol | Safety & equilibrium |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{arom}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{arom,out}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{arom,in}}}$ | Aromatic yield efficiency | — | Process performance |
| 5 | $\Delta\text{RON} = \text{RON}_{\text{out}} - \text{RON}_{\text{in}}$ | Octane uplift | — | Product quality |
| 6 | $K_p = \dfrac{p_{\text{arom}} \cdot p_{H_2}^3}{p_{\text{naph}}}$ | Equilibrium constant (dehydrogenation) | bar³ | Thermodynamics |
| 7 | $\dfrac{dC_A}{d\tau} = -k\,C_A^n$ | Reaction rate law (n = order) | mol/(L·s) | Kinetics |
| 8 | $\dot{Q} = U\,A\,\Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat duty across reactor | W | Heat integration |
| 9 | $P_{\text{safe,design}} = 1.1 \cdot P_{\text{max,operating}}$ | Design margin (ASME) | bar | Pressure relief |
| 10 | $\text{MAWP} = \text{Min}\,(\sigma_t\,,\sigma_b)$ | Allowable stress basis | MPa | Vessel integrity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Trap Tip (KTU valuation):** When you write $\vert x \vert$ in a markdown table, the vertical pipe **breaks the table syntax** and you lose marks for "improper presentation". Always render absolute value using the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` inside table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The aromatic-rich product stream from hydroforming feeds downstream units such as:
- **Aromatic Extraction Units** (Sulfolane, UDEX) — to recover pure benzene, toluene, xylenes (BTX).
- **Petrochemical steam crackers** — for ethylene/propylene production from naphthenes.
- **High-octane gasoline pool blending** — research octane numbers (RON) of 90+ are routine.

In modern refineries, the safety standards developed for hydroforming (hydrogen handling, hot-wall reactors, catalyst regeneration) became the **template** for hydrocracker, hydrotreater, and CCR platformer safety protocols worldwide.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Aromatic Yield as a Function of Temperature and Pressure

We will derive the working equation used by KTU examiners for "Calculate the equilibrium conversion of methylcyclohexane to toluene at 700 K and 20 bar total pressure."

**Given reaction:**
$$\text{Methylcyclohexane (MCH)} \longleftrightarrow \text{Toluene} + 3\,\text{H}_2$$

**Step 1 — Stoichiometry and Initial Moles**

Let 1 mol of MCH be the basis. Let $\alpha$ = fractional conversion at equilibrium. Then at equilibrium:

| Species | Initial moles | Change | Equilibrium moles |
|---|---|---|---|
| MCH | 1 | $-\alpha$ | $1-\alpha$ |
| Toluene | 0 | $+\alpha$ | $\alpha$ |
| H₂ | $r$ (recycle ratio) | $+3\alpha$ | $r + 3\alpha$ |
| **Total** | $1+r$ | $+3\alpha$ | $1+r+3\alpha$ |

**Step 2 — Mole Fractions and Partial Pressures**

For an ideal gas mixture at total pressure $P$:

$$y_{\text{MCH}} = \frac{1-\alpha}{1+r+3\alpha}, \qquad y_{\text{Tol}} = \frac{\alpha}{1+r+3\alpha}, \qquad y_{H_2} = \frac{r+3\alpha}{1+r+3\alpha}$$

Therefore the partial pressures are:

$$p_{\text{MCH}} = y_{\text{MCH}} \cdot P, \qquad p_{\text{Tol}} = y_{\text{Tol}} \cdot P, \qquad p_{H_2} = y_{H_2} \cdot P$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium Constant Expression**

$$K_p = \frac{p_{\text{Tol}} \cdot p_{H_2}^3}{p_{\text{MCH}}} = \frac{\dfrac{\alpha}{1+r+3\alpha} \cdot \left(\dfrac{r+3\alpha}{1+r+3\alpha}\right)^3 \cdot P^4}{\dfrac{1-\alpha}{1+r+3\alpha}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \frac{\alpha \,(r+3\alpha)^3}{(1-\alpha)(1+r+3\alpha)^3} \cdot P^3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $P^3$ (not $P^4$) arises because **total moles appear both in numerator and denominator** and three of the four moles are H₂, cancelling one power of $P$ from the denominator. This is the classic KTU-equilibrium trap.

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values**

Suppose $T = 700\,\text{K}$, $P = 20$ bar, $r = 4$ (recycle ratio), and $K_p(700\text{K}) = 2.0 \times 10^3\,\text{bar}^3$ (literature value).

$$2.0 \times 10^3 = \frac{\alpha \,(4+3\alpha)^3}{(1-\alpha)(5+3\alpha)^3} \cdot (20)^3$$

Since $20^3 = 8000$:

$$\frac{\alpha \,(4+3\alpha)^3}{(1-\alpha)(5+3\alpha)^3} = \frac{2000}{8000} = 0.25$$

**Step 5 — Solve Iteratively**

Assume $\alpha \approx 1$ (high conversion is expected). Let $\alpha = 0.95$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.95 \times (4 + 2.85)^3 = 0.95 \times (6.85)^3 = 0.95 \times 321.5 \approx 305.4 \\
\text{Denominator} &= (1 - 0.95) \times (5 + 2.85)^3 = 0.05 \times (7.85)^3 = 0.05 \times 484.0 \approx 24.2 \\
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{305.4}{24.2} \approx 12.6
\end{aligned}$$

This is much greater than 0.25, so $\alpha$ must be **lower**. Try $\alpha = 0.55$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.55 \times (4 + 1.65)^3 = 0.55 \times (5.65)^3 = 0.55 \times 180.4 \approx 99.2 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 0.45 \times (5 + 1.65)^3 = 0.45 \times (6.65)^3 = 0.45 \times 294.1 \approx 132.3 \\
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{99.2}{132.3} \approx 0.75
\end{aligned}$$

Still too high. Try $\alpha = 0.35$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.35 \times (4 + 1.05)^3 = 0.35 \times (5.05)^3 = 0.35 \times 128.8 \approx 45.1 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 0.65 \times (5 + 1.05)^3 = 0.65 \times (6.05)^3 = 0.65 \times 221.5 \approx 144.0 \\
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{45.1}{144.0} \approx 0.313
\end{aligned}$$

Try $\alpha = 0.30$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.30 \times (4 + 0.90)^3 = 0.30 \times (4.90)^3 = 0.30 \times 117.6 \approx 35.3 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 0.70 \times (5 + 0.90)^3 = 0.70 \times (5.90)^3 = 0.70 \times 205.4 \approx 143.8 \\
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{35.3}{143.8} \approx 0.245
\end{aligned}$$

This is very close to **0.25**. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\alpha_{\text{equilibrium}} \approx 0.30 \;\; (30\% \text{ conversion of MCH at 700 K, 20 bar, } r = 4)}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables on Yield

| Variable | Direction of Change | Effect on Aromatic Yield | Effect on Coke | Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature | ↑ | Strongly ↑ (endothermic favored) | ↑↑ (coking accelerates) | Risk of hot-spot, run-away |
| ↑ Pressure | ↑ | ↓ (Le Chatelier — fewer gas moles) | ↓ (less coke) | Higher relief valve set pressure |
| ↑ H₂/HC ratio | ↑ | ↓ slightly (dilution) | ↓↓ (coke suppression) | Larger recycle compressor load |
| ↑ LHSV | ↑ | ↓ (less contact time) | ↓ | Throughput up, severity down |
| ↑ Catalyst Acidity | ↑ | ↑ (more isomerization) | ↑ | Risk of hydrocracking exotherm |
| ↑ Feed Sulfur | ↑ | ↓↓ (catalyst poisoning) | ↓ | Poisoning is irreversible on Mo |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Flow Diagram — Hydroforming Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Feed1["NAPHTHA FEED"] --> H1["FEED HEATER 1"]
    H1 --> M1["MIXER TEE"]
    Gas1["RECYCLE HYDROGEN GAS"] --> COMP1["RECYCLE COMPRESSOR"]
    COMP1 --> M1
    M1 --> H2["CHARGE HEATER 480-540C"]
    H2 --> R1["REACTOR R1"]
    R1 --> R2["REACTOR R2 LAST BED"]

### 4.3 Material Balance — Sankey-Style Flow

    end

```

### 4.4 Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) Architecture

```mermaid

    TT["TT HIGH TEMPERATURE TRANSMITTER 950C"] --> SIS["SIS LOGIC SOLVER SIL 2"]
    PT["PT HIGH PRESSURE TRANSMITTER"] --> SIS

    SIS --> XV["EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN VALVE XV-101"]
    SIS --> SDV["BLOCK VALVE SDV-202"]

```

### 4.5 Hydrogen Handling Safety Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENVELOPE["H2 SAFETY ENVELOPE AT 540C 25 BAR"]
        LEL["LEL 4 percent IN AIR"]
        UEL["UEL 75 percent IN AIR"]
        AIT["AUTO IGNITION 571C"]
        DET["DETECTOR RANGE 0-100 percent LEL"]
    end
    LEL --> RISK["FLAMMABLE MIXTURE ZONE"]
    UEL --> RISK
    AIT --> RISK
    DET --> MIT["MITIGATION VENT INERT PURGE BLOCK VALVE"]
    RISK --> MIT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define hydroforming. List the four major reactions occurring in a hydroforming reactor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Hydroforming is a catalytic reforming process in which naphtha hydrocarbons are converted to high-octane gasoline blending stocks and aromatics by reaction with hydrogen over a fixed-bed catalyst (typically MoO₃/Al₂O₃) at 480–540 °C and 14–35 bar pressure.

**Four major reactions (2 marks):**
1. **Naphthene dehydrogenation:** cyclohexane → benzene + 3 H₂ (dominant, endothermic).
2. **Paraffin isomerization:** n-hexane ↔ iso-hexane (mild exothermic).
3. **Paraffin dehydrocyclization:** n-heptane → toluene + 4 H₂ (highly endothermic).
4. **Hydrocracking:** C₁₀ + H₂ → lighter paraffins (mild exothermic, undesirable).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Why is hydrogen co-fed with naphtha in a hydroforming unit? Mention any two safety implications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Why hydrogen is co-fed (2 marks):**
1. Hydrogen suppresses catalyst coking by hydrogenating coke precursors (polyaromatics) before they polymerize on active sites.
2. Hydrogen partial pressure controls the equilibrium of dehydrogenation reactions, favoring higher aromatic yields (Le Chatelier).
3. The hydrocracking exotherm (consuming H₂) partly offsets the strong dehydrogenation endotherm, improving reactor temperature control.

**Two safety implications (1 mark):**
1. Hydrogen has a very wide flammability range (4–75 vol% in air) and very low minimum ignition energy (0.02 mJ) — leaks must be detected instantly.
2. Hydrogen embrittlement of carbon steel at high temperature demands use of low-alloy Cr-Mo steels for reactor shells and piping.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (Module-internal choice).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Process Design Focus**

**Q.A(a) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the process flow scheme of a hydroforming unit. Identify the major equipment and state the function of each.

**Model Solution:**

**Process Flow Description (4 marks):**

1. **Naphtha feed** (C₆–C₁₀ fraction, 80–200 °C boiling range) is pumped from storage, mixed with **recycle hydrogen**, and passed through a **feed pre-heater** (waste-heat recovery from reactor effluent).
2. The combined feed enters the **charge heater**, a fired heater raising the stream to **510–540 °C** before entering the first reactor.
3. The reactor train typically consists of **3 adiabatic fixed-bed reactors in series** with **inter-stage heaters** to reheat the endothermically cooled stream between beds.
4. Reactor effluent is cooled in a **product cooler**, then routed to an **HP separator** (high-pressure) where **unreacted hydrogen** is recovered as overhead.
5. The liquid from the HP separator goes to a **LP separator** for further gas-liquid separation at lower pressure.
6. The recovered hydrogen is recompressed by the **recycle compressor** and mixed with fresh naphtha feed.
7. The liquid product is sent to a **stabilizer column** to remove dissolved light gases (C₁–C₄), yielding the final **stabilized reformate** product.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. The student must show:
Feed → Feed heater → Charge heater → Reactor 1 → Inter-heater → Reactor 2 → Inter-heater → Reactor 3 → Cooler → HP separator → LP separator → Stabilizer → Product.

**Equipment functions (1 mark):**
- Reactor: catalytic conversion.
- Inter-heater: reheat between beds.
- Compressor: recycle hydrogen.
- Stabilizer: remove light ends.

---

**Q.A(b) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
A hydroforming reactor processes **40,000 kg/h** of naphtha (mean molecular weight = 110) mixed with **8,000 kg/h** of hydrogen (MW = 2). The reactor operates at **500 °C and 25 bar**. Calculate:
(i) The H₂/HC molar ratio.
(ii) The total molar flow rate entering the reactor.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) H₂/HC Molar Ratio (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Convert naphtha mass flow to molar flow:
$$\dot{n}_{HC} = \frac{40{,}000 \text{ kg/h}}{110 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 363.64 \text{ kmol/h}$$

Step 2 — Convert hydrogen mass flow to molar flow:
$$\dot{n}_{H_2} = \frac{8{,}000 \text{ kg/h}}{2 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 4{,}000 \text{ kmol/h}$$

Step 3 — Compute the ratio:
$$\frac{H_2}{HC} = \frac{4{,}000}{363.64} = 11.0 \;\; \text{mol/mol}$$

**(ii) Total Molar Flow Rate at Reactor Inlet (4 marks):**

Step 4 — Sum the molar flows:
$$\dot{n}_{total} = 363.64 + 4{,}000 = 4{,}363.64 \text{ kmol/h}$$

Step 5 — Convert to standard volumetric flow at STP (0 °C, 1 atm):
$$\dot{V}_{STP} = \dot{n}_{total} \times 22.414 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol} = 4{,}363.64 \times 22.414 = 97{,}805 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

Step 6 — Convert to actual volumetric flow at 500 °C = 773 K and 25 bar using ideal gas law:
$$\dot{V}_{actual} = \dot{V}_{STP} \times \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{STP}} \times \frac{P_{STP}}{P_{actual}}$$

$$\dot{V}_{actual} = 97{,}805 \times \frac{773}{273} \times \frac{1}{25} = 97{,}805 \times 2.831 \times 0.04 = 11{,}076 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Final Answer:** H₂/HC = **11.0 mol/mol**; Total molar flow = **4,363.64 kmol/h** (actual volumetric flow at reactor conditions ≈ **11,076 m³/h**).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Safety & Hazard Focus**

**Q.B(a) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Discuss the major safety hazards associated with a hydroforming unit. For each hazard, propose at least one engineering control.

**Model Solution:**

| # | Hazard | Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hydrogen leakage & fire** (wide flammability 4–75%) | Continuous H₂ detectors with IR/electrochemical sensors; emergency block valves; nitrogen purge on shutdown |
| 2 | **High-temperature run-away** (exothermic hydrocracking) | Multi-point thermocouples in catalyst bed; Hi-Hi trip at 545 °C; automatic quench with fresh feed |
| 3 | **High-pressure rupture** (25 bar, cyclic fatigue) | ASME-coded vessels; pressure relief valves sized for blocked-outlet fire case; regular NDT inspection |
| 4 | **Hydrogen embrittlement** of reactor shell | Use Cr-Mo steel (e.g., 1.25 Cr-0.5 Mo); post-weld heat treatment; hardness testing |
| 5 | **Catalyst dust explosion** during regeneration | Oxygen concentration limit < 1 vol% in regeneration loop; inert regeneration gas; dust collection |
| 6 | **Toxic exposure** to naphtha vapors / CO | Closed sampling systems; LEV; gas masks for confined space entry |
| 7 | **Hot oil / naphtha pool fire** from flange leak | Fireproofing of structural steel; foam pourers; remote-operated emergency isolation valves |

**Allocation of 7 marks:** 1 mark per major hazard identified + control (with 0.5 mark bonus for the most complete answer).

---

**Q.B(b) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
A hydroforming reactor is fitted with the following Safety Instrumented Function (SIF):
- Temperature transmitter range: 400–600 °C
- High-trip set point: **540 °C**
- Pressure transmitter range: 0–50 bar
- High-high trip set point: **30 bar**
- Flow transmitter (feed): 0–100 m³/h
- Low-low trip set point: **10 m³/h**

**(i)** Identify the SIL level typically required for this SIF. Justify.
**(ii)** Draw the SIF logic diagram. What is the safety action on trip?

**Model Solution:**

**(i) SIL Level Identification (4 marks):**

According to **IEC 61511**, the SIL is determined by:
- Severity of harm: Catastrophic (loss of life, major equipment loss) → **Severity = High**.
- Frequency of exposure: Continuous (operator in field during operation) → **Frequency = High**.
- Possibility of avoidance: Low (reaction is fast, no time for manual intervention) → **Avoidance = Low**.

With **High Severity + High Frequency + Low Avoidance**, the required SIL is **SIL 2**.

A SIL 2 SIF has:
- Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) between **10⁻² and 10⁻³**.
- Hardware Fault Tolerance = **1** (i.e., redundant transmitters — use 1oo2 or 2oo3 voting).

**Justification (1 mark):** Because the consequences of failure (vessel rupture, hydrocarbon fire, hydrogen explosion) can cause multiple fatalities, SIL 2 is mandatory under both IEC 61511 and OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 PSM regulations.

**(ii) SIF Logic Diagram & Action (2 marks):**

Refer to the SIF architecture in Section 4.4 of these notes. The trip logic is:

```
TT-HI  ≥ 540 °C  ─┐
PT-HIH ≥ 30 bar   ─┤ AND ─→ SIS Solver ─→ Close XV-101 (Reactor Inlet)
FT-LO  ≤ 10 m³/h  ─┘                              Open SDV-202 (Reactor Vent)
                                          ─→ Sound DCS Alarm (Operator)
```

**Safety Action on Trip:**
1. **Close** emergency block valve XV-101 on the reactor inlet to stop hydrocarbon feed.
2. **Open** SDV-202 on the vent line to depressurize the reactor.
3. **Trigger** nitrogen purge to inert the catalyst bed.
4. **Sound** DCS alarm for operator notification.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Hydroforming):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** KTU strictly deducts 0.5 mark for missing or wrong units.
> 2. **Confusing hydroforming with catalytic reforming.** Hydroforming uses MoO₃/Al₂O₃ and is the **first-generation** process; modern platforming uses Pt/Al₂O₃ or Pt-Re. Examiners expect this distinction.
> 3. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ inside markdown tables** — use `\vert` or `\mid` only.
> 4. **Omitting the hydrogen safety angle** in Part B — this is a **Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries** course, not a generic process design paper. Every numerical answer must include a brief **safety comment** (e.g., "H₂ flammability = 4–75%, detectors required").
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram** in descriptive answers — at least 2 marks are reserved for diagrams.
> 6. **Wrong equilibrium constant units.** For $A \longleftrightarrow B + 3H_2$, $K_p$ has units of **bar³**, not dimensionless — losing 1 mark.
> 7. **Confusing LHSV and GHSV.** LHSV uses **liquid** feed volume; GHSV uses **gas** feed volume. Examiners will test this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hydroforming** = first-generation catalytic reforming using MoO₃/Al₂O₃ catalyst, naphtha + H₂ feed, 480–540 °C, 14–35 bar.
- **Four reactions:** naphthene dehydrogenation (main), paraffin isomerization, paraffin dehydrocyclization, hydrocracking.
- **Hydrogen role:** suppresses coke, controls equilibrium, moderates hot spots.
- **Catalyst:** 10 wt% MoO₃ on alumina; deactivated by sulfur.
- **Typical yields:** 80–85 wt% C₅⁺ reformate with RON 90+.
- **Safety hot-spots:** hydrogen flammability 4–75% in air; AIT 571 °C; embrittlement of C-steel.
- **Key formulas:** LHSV, GHSV, H₂/HC ratio, $K_p = p_{arom} \cdot p_{H_2}^3 / p_{naph}$.
- **SIF requirement:** SIL 2 minimum for reactor protection; redundant transmitters with 1oo2 or 2oo3 voting.
- **Block diagram mandatory** for any descriptive answer of ≥ 5 marks.
- **Numerical answers must include:** given data, formula, substitution, final numerical result with **units**, and a one-line safety comment.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Purification
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gas Purification

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Gas Purification** in the hydrocarbon processing industry refers to the systematic removal of unwanted contaminants from raw natural gas, refinery off-gases, and synthesis gas streams to produce a product that meets pipeline specifications, environmental emission norms, and downstream process safety requirements. The contaminants targeted are classified as:

1. **Acid Gases** — Hydrogen Sulfide ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$) and Carbon Dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$)
2. **Free and Dissolved Water** — causing hydrate formation
3. **Heavy Hydrocarbons** — C3+ condensate drop-out
4. **Mercury** — trace toxic metal
5. **Particulates and Entrained Liquids**
6. **Nitrogen ($\text{N}_2$), Helium ($\text{He}$)**, and other inerts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Gas purification is treated primarily as a **Safety Engineering** topic. The emphasis is on the *consequences of failing to purify* — toxicity, corrosion, explosion, and environmental damage — rather than purely on chemical engineering design.

### 1.2 Why Gas Purification is a SAFETY Issue, Not Just a Process Issue

| Impurity | Primary Safety Hazard | Threshold of Concern |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ | Acute toxic gas (odorless at lethal concentration) | **>10 ppm**: OSHA PEL (Permissible Exposure Limit) |
| $\text{CO}_2$ | Asphyxiant, corrosive when combined with water | **>3%** in confined spaces |
| $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Hydrate plug formation → pipeline rupture | Dew point **< -10°C** at line pressure |
| $\text{Hg}$ | Severe neurotoxin, attacks aluminum heat exchangers | **>0.01 $\mu$g/Nm³** |
| Particulates | Erosion, valve seat damage, instrument fouling | **<1 mg/Nm³** for turbine meters |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Exhaust" Model

Think of a gas purification train exactly like the **chimney/exhaust system of a commercial kitchen**:

- **Raw Natural Gas (Inlet)** is the *dirty, smoky cooking vapor* — full of grease droplets (water/heavy hydrocarbons), pungent smell ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$), and carbon dioxide from incomplete burning.
- **The Grease Filter (Slug Catcher / Inlet Separator)** catches the *big visible drops* (free liquids and particulates) — quick and cheap.
- **The Activated Charcoal Layer (Amine Contactor)** absorbs the *odorous molecules* ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$, $\text{CO}_2$) at the molecular level.
- **The Final Fine Filter (Molecular Sieve / Dryer Bed)** removes the *invisible moisture* that would otherwise condense and cause damage downstream.
- **The Polished Exhaust (Sales Gas)** is what comes out — clean, dry, sweet, and safe to pipe into homes.

Just as you would **never serve food prepared in a kitchen without an exhaust system**, you would **never** transport unpurified hydrocarbon gas — the consequences are immediate and catastrophic (Bhopal-style toxic release, pipeline explosion, hydrate blockage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sweet Gas vs. Sour Gas:** A gas containing less than **1 grain of $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ per 100 standard cubic feet** (≈ **0.000016 mol/mol** or **16 ppmv**) is termed *sweet*. Anything above this threshold is *sour* and **must** be treated before sale.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Units Used in Gas Purification

- **ppmv** — parts per million by volume
- **ppmw** — parts per million by weight
- **mg/Nm³** — milligrams per Normal cubic meter (at **0°C, 1 atm**)
- **grains/100 scf** — 1 grain = **64.79891 mg**; 100 scf = **2.832 Nm³**
- **GPA Standard** — Gas Processors Association reference temperature **15.56°C (60°F)** and pressure **101.325 kPa**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Gas Purification (Operational Logic)

### Pillar 1: Acid Gas Removal (AGR) — "Sweetening the Gas"

**Objective:** Reduce $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ to **<4 ppmv** and $\text{CO}_2$ to **<2 mol%** for pipeline spec, or **<50 ppmv $\text{H}_2\text{S}$** for LNG feed.

**Dominant Process:** **Amine Treating** (chemical absorption).

**Reaction with MDEA (Methyldiethanolamine) — selective for $\text{H}_2\text{S}$:**
$$\text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{R}_3\text{N} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_3\text{NH}^+ + \text{HS}^-$$

**Reaction with MEA (Monoethanolamine) — removes both $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ and $\text{CO}_2$:**
$$\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{RNH}_2 \rightarrow \text{RNHCOO}^- + \text{RNH}_3^+$$

The amine is regenerated in a **reboiled stripper** at **115–125°C**, reversing the reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Implication:** Amine degradation products — **Heat Stable Salts (HSS)** and **carbamates** — are corrosive. Lean amine must be filtered, and foaming must be controlled with anti-foam agents to prevent column flooding.

### Pillar 2: Dehydration — "Water is the Enemy"

**Objective:** Suppress hydrate formation by reducing water content to achieve a **hydrate-free dew point** (typically **< -10°C at 70 bar** for offshore pipelines).

**Hydrate Formation Condition (Thermodynamic Criterion):**
$$T_{\text{system}} < T_{\text{hydrate}}(P, \text{composition})$$

where $T_{\text{hydrate}}$ depends on pressure, gas composition, and water content.

**Primary Methods:**
| Method | Dew Point Achieved | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Glycol Dehydration (TEG)** | -10°C to -30°C | Onshore gas plants, wellheads |
| **Molecular Sieves (3Å/4Å zeolites)** | -50°C to -100°C | LNG pretreatment, cryogenic plants |
| **Membrane Separation** | -20°C to -40°C | Offshore platforms (compact footprint) |
| **Supersonic Separators (Twister)** | -15°C to -25°C | Compact, no chemicals |

### Pillar 3: Mercury Removal — "The Invisible Destroyer"

**Objective:** Reduce Hg to **<0.01 $\mu$g/Nm³** to protect cryogenic aluminum brazed heat exchangers in LNG plants (Hg causes liquid metal embrittlement).

**Primary Method:** **Fixed-bed adsorption** using **sulfur-impregnated activated carbon** or **metal sulfide (Ag, Cu) on support**.

**Adsorption Mechanism (chemisorption):**
$$\text{Hg}^{0} + \text{S}_{\text{ads}} \rightarrow \text{HgS}_{\text{ads}}$$

### Pillar 4: Particulate & Entrained Liquid Removal

- **Inlet Separators** (vane-type, mesh-type) — remove 99% of droplets **>10 $\mu$m**
- **Filter Coalescers** — reduce to **<0.1 ppmw** liquid in gas
- **Cyclones** — for solid particle removal down to **5 $\mu$m**

## 2.2 The Claus Process — Converting Toxic $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ to Elemental Sulfur

Once $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ is absorbed by amine, the concentrated acid gas stream is sent to a **Claus plant** for safe disposal as elemental sulfur (non-hazardous solid).

**Thermal Stage (1100–1300°C):**
$$\text{H}_2\text{S} + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -518\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Catalytic Stage (Modified Claus — fixed bed):**
$$2\,\text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{SO}_2 \rightleftharpoons 3\,\text{S} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -96\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Overall:**
$$\text{H}_2\text{S} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{S} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Conversion efficiency:** Modern Claus with **Tail Gas Treatment Unit (TGTU)** achieves **>99.8% recovery**, leaving $<50\ \text{ppm}\ \text{SO}_2$ in stack — meeting **MOEF&CC India emission norms**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary Conditions | Safety Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acid gas loading of amine | $\alpha = \dfrac{\text{mol acid gas}}{\text{mol amine}}$ | $\alpha_{\text{lean}} < 0.05$, $\alpha_{\text{rich}} \approx 0.4$–$0.5$ | Higher $\alpha$ → corrosion, foaming |
| 2 | Hydrate suppression — Inhibitor required (Hammerschmidt equation) | $\text{wt\% MeOH} = \dfrac{32.04 \cdot \Delta T}{1297 \cdot M + 32.04 \cdot \Delta T}$ | $M$ = molecular weight of inhibitor; $\Delta T$ in °F | Methanol dosing for hydrate prevention |
| 3 | Claus air demand ratio | $\text{Air} = 3 \cdot V_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} + V_{\text{HC}} - V_{\text{O}_2,\text{feed}}$ | Stoichiometric; ±2% control accuracy required | Critical to avoid $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ breakthrough or excess $\text{SO}_2$ |
| 4 | Particle Stokes settling velocity | $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18\mu}$ | Laminar, $Re_p < 0.2$ | Inlet separator droplet design |
| 5 | Molecular sieve water capacity | $C = C_{\text{sat}} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{P_{\text{sat}}(T)}\right)^n\right]$ | $n \approx 4$–$8$ for 4Å zeolite | Cycle time calculation for adsorption bed |
| 6 | TEG circulation rate | $\text{GPM}_{\text{TEG}} = \dfrac{\text{lb H}_2\text{O/hr}}{\text{TEG conc} \times 8.33 \times \text{gal/100 lb}}$ | TEG 99.6 wt% typical | Dehydration column design |
| 7 | Henry's Law — $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ solubility | $P_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} = H \cdot x_{\text{H}_2\text{S}}$ | $H_{298K} \approx 0.1$ MPa/mol-fraction in water | Lowers $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ partial pressure in amine solution |
| 8 | $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ IDLH concentration | — | **IDLH = 100 ppm** (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) | Defines PPE selection for confined space |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The gas purification train is the **"immune system"** of a hydrocarbon facility. Just as a human body cannot function with a failing liver or kidney, a gas plant cannot safely produce LNG, petrochemicals, or pipeline-grade gas without a robust purification chain. **A single missed step** — failing to remove mercury before cryogenic heat exchange — caused the **Skikda LNG disaster (Algeria, 2004)** where aluminum exchangers failed catastrophically.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hammerschmidt Equation for Hydrate Inhibitor Dosage

### Problem Context
A wet natural gas leaves an offshore platform at **70 bar** with a water dew point problem. Inhibitor (methanol, $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$, $M = 32.04$ g/mol) is injected at the wellhead. Determine the mass percent of methanol required to suppress the hydrate formation temperature by **$\Delta T = 15°C$**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Hammerschmidt's empirical correlation expresses the mass percent of inhibitor in the **free water phase** as a function of the hydrate temperature depression $\Delta T$:

$$W = \frac{100 \cdot M_{\text{inhibitor}} \cdot \Delta T}{K_H + M_{\text{inhibitor}} \cdot \Delta T}$$

where:
- $W$ = mass percent of inhibitor in the aqueous phase (%)
- $M_{\text{inhibitor}}$ = molecular weight of inhibitor (g/mol)
- $\Delta T$ = depression of hydrate formation temperature (°F)
- $K_H$ = Hammerschmidt constant — depends on inhibitor type

**Step 1: Identify the constants for methanol.**
For methanol, $K_H = 1297$ (dimensionless, when $\Delta T$ is in °F and $M$ in g/mol).

**Step 2: Convert $\Delta T$ from °C to °F.**
$$\Delta T\ (°F) = \Delta T\ (°C) \times \frac{9}{5}$$
$$\Delta T = 15 \times 1.8 = 27\ °F$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the equation.**
$$W = \frac{100 \times 32.04 \times 27}{1297 + 32.04 \times 27}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the numerator.**
$$100 \times 32.04 \times 27 = 86{,}508$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the denominator.**
$$32.04 \times 27 = 865.08$$
$$1297 + 865.08 = 2162.08$$

**Step 6: Final division.**
$$W = \frac{86{,}508}{2162.08} \approx 40.0\ \text{wt\% methanol in aqueous phase}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation:** A **40 wt% methanol** solution in the free water phase depresses the hydrate point by 15°C. Injected methanol concentration at the wellhead is typically **20–30 wt%** because it gets diluted by condensed water. This methanol-rich water must be **recovered, regenerated, and recycled** — never discharged to sea due to marine toxicity.

## 3.2 Derivation: Acid Gas Mass Balance Through Amine Contactor

### Problem Context
A sour natural gas stream enters an amine contactor with the following composition:

| Component | Flow (kgmol/hr) | Inlet | Outlet (Target) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ | 5.0 | $x_{\text{in}}$ | $< 4$ ppmv |
| $\text{CO}_2$ | 25.0 | 5 mol% | $< 2$ mol% |
| $\text{CH}_4$ | 470.0 | balance | balance |

Lean amine (MDEA, 50 wt% in water) enters with loading $\alpha_{\text{lean}} = 0.02$ and exits rich with $\alpha_{\text{rich}} = 0.45$ mol acid gas/mol amine. Determine the required **amine circulation rate** and confirm $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ slip.

**Step 1: Total molar flow of gas.**
$$n_{\text{gas, in}} = 5 + 25 + 470 = 500\ \text{kgmol/hr}$$

**Step 2: $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ to be removed.**
$$\Delta n_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} = 5.0 - \left(500 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}\right) = 5.0 - 0.002 = 4.998\ \text{kgmol/hr}$$

**Step 3: $\text{CO}_2$ to be removed.**
$$\Delta n_{\text{CO}_2} = 25.0 - 500 \times 0.02 = 25.0 - 10.0 = 15.0\ \text{kgmol/hr}$$

**Step 4: Total acid gas absorbed.**
$$\Delta n_{\text{acid}} = 4.998 + 15.0 = 19.998 \approx 20.0\ \text{kgmol/hr}$$

**Step 5: Amine loading difference.**
$$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{\text{rich}} - \alpha_{\text{lean}} = 0.45 - 0.02 = 0.43\ \text{mol acid gas / mol amine}$$

**Step 6: Required amine molar flow.**
$$n_{\text{amine}} = \frac{\Delta n_{\text{acid}}}{\Delta \alpha} = \frac{20.0}{0.43} = 46.51\ \text{kgmol/hr}$$

**Step 7: Convert to mass flow.** Molecular weight of MDEA = 119.17 g/mol.
$$\dot{m}_{\text{amine}} = 46.51 \times 119.17 = 5541\ \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 8: Mass of total MDEA solution (50 wt%).**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{solution}} = \frac{5541}{0.50} = 11{,}082\ \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 9: Convert to volumetric flow.**
$$\dot{V}_{\text{solution}} = \frac{11{,}082}{1042}\ (\text{density of 50\% MDEA}) \approx 10.6\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr} \approx 46.8\ \text{GPM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Cross-Check:** If the rich amine becomes over-loaded ($\alpha > 0.5$), the system **slips $\text{H}_2\text{S}$** — meaning toxic gas breaks through to the sales line. This is a **MAJOR process safety incident** with regulatory consequences. The above design keeps margin at $\alpha_{\text{rich}} = 0.45$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Claus Plant Air Demand

### Problem Context
A modified Claus unit treats acid gas with **90 mol% $\text{H}_2\text{S}$** and **10 mol% $\text{CO}_2$**, with no hydrocarbons. Total acid gas flow = **100 kmol/hr**. Determine the stoichiometric air requirement.

**Step 1: Moles of $\text{H}_2\text{S}$.**
$$n_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} = 100 \times 0.90 = 90\ \text{kmol/hr}$$

**Step 2: Moles of $\text{O}_2$ required** (from Section 2.2 thermal reaction: 1.5 mol $\text{O}_2$ per mol $\text{H}_2\text{S}$):
$$n_{\text{O}_2} = 1.5 \times 90 = 135\ \text{kmol/hr}$$

**Step 3: Air contains 21 mol% $\text{O}_2$**.
$$n_{\text{air}} = \frac{135}{0.21} = 642.86\ \text{kmol/hr}$$

**Step 4: Confirm with overall Claus stoichiometry** (1 mol $\text{O}_2$ per mol $\text{H}_2\text{S}$):
$$n_{\text{O}_2,\text{overall}} = 1 \times 90 = 90\ \text{kmol/hr}$$
$$n_{\text{air},\text{stoich}} = \frac{90}{0.21} = 428.57\ \text{kmol/hr}$$

The thermal stage requires **3×** more air than overall because **$\tfrac{2}{3}$ of the $\text{O}_2$** is used to form $\text{SO}_2$ that is then reduced back. This is a **common board question** — students frequently confuse the two ratios.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:** Never use the ratio **1.5 × $\text{H}_2\text{S}$** as the *overall* air demand — that is only for the thermal stage. The overall stoichiometry is **1 mol $\text{O}_2$ per mol $\text{H}_2\text{S}$**. Mismatched ratio means either **excess $\text{H}_2\text{S}$** in tail gas (toxic release) or **excess $\text{SO}_2$** (acid rain).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Integrated Gas Purification Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Wellhead Gas: Sour, Wet, with Hg] --> B[Inlet Slug Catcher]
    B --> C[Inlet Coalescer Filter]
    C --> D[Mercury Removal Bed: S-impregnated Activated Carbon]
    D --> E[Amine Contactor: H2S and CO2 Absorption]
    E --> F[Water Wash Section: Remove Amine Fines]
    F --> G[Knockout Drum: Entrainment Separation]
    G --> H[TEG Dehydration Column]
    H --> I[Molecular Sieve Polisher: Deep Dew Point -100C]
    I --> J[Sales Gas Compressor and Pipeline]
    
    E --> K[Rich Amine Flash Drum]
    K --> L[Amine Regenerator Stripper: 120C]
    L --> M[Lean Amine Cooler]
    M --> E
    
    L --> N[Acid Gas to Claus Unit]
    N --> O[Claus Thermal Reactor]
    O --> P[Claus Catalytic Reactors: 3 Stages]
    P --> Q[Tail Gas Treatment Unit TGTU]
    Q --> R[Incinerator Stack: SO2 less than 50 ppm]
    P --> S[Liquid Sulfur Pit and Pelletizer]
    
    style A fill:#ffe5e5
    style J fill:#e5ffe5
    style S fill:#fff5e5
    style R fill:#f0e5ff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Acid Gas Removal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INLET["INLET STREAM"]
        A1[Sour Gas: H2S + CO2 + HC + H2O]
    end
    
    subgraph ABSORPTION["ABSORPTION SECTION"]
        B1[Amine Contactor Tower: 20-30 trays]
        B2[Lean Amine In: 50C, alpha 0.02]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTLET["SWEET GAS OUTLET"]
        C1[Sweet Gas: less than 4 ppmv H2S]
        C2[Saturated with H2O: needs dehydration]
    end
    
    subgraph REGEN["REGENERATION LOOP"]
        D1[Rich Amine: alpha 0.45, 80C]
        D2[Reboiler Stripper: 120C, 1.8 bar]
        D3[Lean Amine Cooler and Pump]
    end
    
    subgraph SAFETY["SAFETY INSTRUMENTED FUNCTIONS"]
        E1[SIF-1: High H2S in Sales Gas, ESD]
        E2[SIF-2: Low Lean Amine Flow, Trip]
        E3[SIF-3: High Level in Contactor]
    end
    
    A1 --> B1
    B2 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> B2
    
    B1 -.-> E1
    B1 -.-> E2
    B1 -.-> E3
    
    style B1 fill:#ffcccc
    style D2 fill:#ffffcc
    style E1 fill:#ffaaaa
    style E2 fill:#ffaaaa
    style E3 fill:#ffaaaa
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Selecting the Right Purification Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Raw Gas Analysis Received] --> Q1{Is H2S more than 4 ppm?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> AGR[Use Amine Treating: MEA or MDEA]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is CO2 more than 2 mol percent?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> AGR
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is water dew point higher than -10C at operating pressure?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> DEH[Use TEG + Molecular Sieve]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is mercury more than 0.01 microgram per Nm3?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> HG[Sulfur-Impregnated Activated Carbon Bed]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Particulate more than 1 mg per Nm3?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> F[Install Coalescer Filter]
    Q5 -- No --> OK[Sales Quality Gas: Approved for Export]
    
    AGR --> DEH
    DEH --> HG
    HG --> F
    F --> OK
    
    style AGR fill:#ffdddd
    style DEH fill:#ddffdd
    style HG fill:#ddddff
    style F fill:#ffffdd
    style OK fill:#ddffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define "sweet gas" and state the maximum permissible $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ content for pipeline-quality natural gas as per GAIL/Indian standards. **[3 marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Sweet gas is a natural gas containing negligible or no hydrogen sulfide. According to GAIL India's pipeline specification, **sweet gas must contain $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ less than 4 ppmv (mg/Nm³)** and total sulfur less than **150 mg/Nm³**. The $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ must be removed via amine treating because it is toxic (IDLH = 100 ppm), corrosive to carbon steel, and produces $\text{SO}_2$ on combustion contributing to acid rain.

### Question A2: Why is mercury removal essential before entering an LNG heat exchanger? **[3 marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Mercury in natural gas (typically 0.1–10 $\mu$g/Nm³) causes **liquid metal embrittlement (LME)** of aluminum heat exchangers used in LNG cryogenic units. Mercury attacks aluminum grain boundaries, causing catastrophic brittle fracture and LNG release. This was the root cause of the **Skikda LNG accident (2004, Algeria)**. Removal to <0.01 $\mu$g/Nm³ is achieved using **sulfur-impregnated activated carbon** or **silver/copper sulfide beds** that form stable HgS.

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Treatment of Sour Gas

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Amine Gas Treating Process** for the removal of $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ and $\text{CO}_2$ from natural gas. Discuss the role of MEA, DEA, and MDEA as selective solvents. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Modified Claus Process** with all key reactions. A Claus plant receives **200 kmol/hr** of acid gas containing **92 mol% $\text{H}_2\text{S}$** and **8 mol% $\text{CO}_2$** with no hydrocarbons. Calculate: (i) the stoichiometric air requirement in the thermal stage, and (ii) the mass of elemental sulfur produced per hour assuming **96% overall conversion**. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The amine treating process consists of two main sections:

**1. Absorption Section (Contactor Tower):**
- Operating at **30–50°C** and **5–70 bar**.
- Sour gas enters the bottom of a trayed/packed column (typically 20–30 trays or structured packing).
- Lean amine enters the top with loading $\alpha < 0.05$.
- Acid gases ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$, $\text{CO}_2$) are absorbed by chemical reaction.
- Sweet gas leaves the top with $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ < 4 ppmv.

**2. Regeneration Section (Stripper Column):**
- Rich amine (loaded $\alpha \approx 0.4$–$0.5$) is preheated and sent to the stripper.
- Operating at **115–125°C** and **1.5–2.0 bar**.
- Steam from reboiler reverses the chemical absorption reaction, releasing concentrated acid gas overhead.
- Lean amine is cooled by lean/rich exchanger and recycled.

**Comparison of Amines:**

| Solvent | Type | Selectivity | Acid Gas | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MEA** (Monoethanolamine) | Primary | Non-selective | Removes both $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ and $\text{CO}_2$ | High reboiler duty, corrosion issues |
| **DEA** (Diethanolamine) | Secondary | Non-selective | Both gases | Lower vapor pressure than MEA |
| **MDEA** (Methyldiethanolamine) | Tertiary | **Selective for $\text{H}_2\text{S}$** | Rejects $\text{CO}_2$ | Lower energy, used in "selective" treating |
| **Formulated MDEA** | Tertiary + activator | Tunable | Field-adjustable ratio | Modern plants |

**Safety Features:** Lean amine filter, activated carbon adsorber (for degradation products), anti-foam injection, corrosion coupon monitoring, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ continuous analyzer on sweet gas with **SIF (Safety Integrity Function Level 1)** trip to ESD.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Thermal Stage Air Requirement:**

From the thermal reaction:
$$\text{H}_2\text{S} + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$\text{H}_2\text{S}$ flow = $200 \times 0.92 = 184$ kmol/hr.

$\text{O}_2$ required = $1.5 \times 184 = 276$ kmol/hr.

Air flow (at 21 mol% $\text{O}_2$): 
$$\dot{n}_{\text{air}} = \frac{276}{0.21} = 1314.3\ \text{kmol/hr}$$

**(ii) Mass of Sulfur Produced:**

Overall reaction: $\text{H}_2\text{S} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{S} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

Stoichiometric sulfur = $184$ kmol/hr (one mol S per mol $\text{H}_2\text{S}$).

At 96% conversion: $\text{S}_{\text{produced}} = 184 \times 0.96 = 176.64$ kmol/hr.

Molecular weight of S = 32.06 g/mol:
$$\dot{m}_S = 176.64 \times 32.06 = 5663\ \text{kg/hr} = 5.66\ \text{tonnes/hr}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B / 14 marks):** Students frequently **(i) confuse the thermal stage ratio (1.5) with the overall ratio (1.0)**, **(ii) forget to multiply by conversion efficiency**, and **(iii) confuse mol% with mass%** when calculating air demand. Each error costs **2–3 marks**. Always **show all three conversions** explicitly: gas → component → $\text{O}_2$ → air.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): Dehydration and Mercury Removal

**(a)** Discuss the phenomenon of **gas hydrate formation** in hydrocarbon pipelines. Derive the **Hammerschmidt equation** for methanol inhibitor dosage. A gas at **60 bar** requires hydrate temperature depression of **20°C**. Calculate methanol concentration required. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the working principle and safety significance of **mercury removal units** in natural gas processing. Describe the construction of a typical sulfur-impregnated activated carbon bed with reference to breakthrough curve analysis. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Hydrate Formation:** Gas hydrates are clathrate crystalline compounds where water molecules form a cage-like lattice trapping small hydrocarbon molecules ($\text{CH}_4$, $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$) or non-hydrocarbons ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{N}_2$). They form at high pressure and low temperature when free water is present, plugging pipelines, instruments, and safety valves — a major cause of **unplanned shutdowns and overpressure events**.

**Inhibitor Methods:**
1. **Thermodynamic inhibitors** (methanol, MEG, DEG) — shift hydrate equilibrium curve.
2. **Low Dosage Hydrate Inhibitors (LDHI)** — kinetic and anti-agglomerant types.

**Hammerschmidt Equation Derivation:**

Hammerschmidt (1939) empirically correlated hydrate temperature depression $\Delta T$ with the mass fraction $W$ of inhibitor in the aqueous phase:

$$W = \frac{100 \cdot M \cdot \Delta T}{K_H + M \cdot \Delta T}$$

where:
- $W$ = wt% inhibitor in free water
- $M$ = molecular weight of inhibitor (g/mol)
- $\Delta T$ = hydrate temperature depression in °F
- $K_H$ = Hammerschmidt constant (for methanol = **1297**)

**Numerical Calculation:**

Convert $\Delta T$: $20°C \times 1.8 = 36 °F$.

$$W = \frac{100 \times 32.04 \times 36}{1297 + 32.04 \times 36}$$

Numerator: $100 \times 32.04 \times 36 = 115{,}344$.

Denominator: $1297 + 1153.44 = 2450.44$.

$$W = \frac{115{,}344}{2450.44} \approx 47.1\ \text{wt\% methanol}$$

> **Interpretation:** The aqueous phase must contain **47.1 wt% methanol** to depress hydrate formation by 20°C. Because this methanol gets diluted by condensed water in the line, the **injection concentration** at the wellhead typically ranges **30–40 wt%** in offshore operations.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Why Mercury Removal?**

Mercury exists in natural gas as elemental vapor ($\text{Hg}^0$). At cryogenic LNG temperatures (-162°C), it condenses and amalgamates with aluminum in brazed heat exchangers, causing **liquid metal embrittlement (LME)**. This is a known root cause of multiple LNG plant incidents including the 2004 Skikda disaster. Mercury is also a potent neurotoxin (OSHA PEL = **0.05 mg/m³** for Hg vapor).

**Sulfur-Impregnated Activated Carbon (SIAC) Bed:**

1. **Construction:**
   - Two parallel vessels (one online, one standby/regenerating).
   - Carbon substrate with **10–15 wt% sulfur** impregnated in micropores.
   - Operating temperature: **20–60°C** (no heating required).
   - Pressure drop target: **< 0.5 bar** across the bed.

2. **Chemisorption Mechanism:**
   $$\text{Hg}^{0}_{(g)} + \text{S}_{\text{ads}} \rightarrow \text{HgS}_{\text{ads}}$$

3. **Breakthrough Curve:**
   - $C/C_0$ (outlet concentration / inlet concentration) plotted against time.
   - **MTZ (Mass Transfer Zone)** moves down the bed as saturation progresses.
   - **Breakthrough point:** defined as $C/C_0 = 0.05$ (5%) or as per spec (<0.01 $\mu$g/Nm³).
   - Bed is taken offline for replacement when breakthrough occurs.
   - **Bed life** typically 1–3 years depending on Hg inlet loading.

**Safety Instrumentation:**
- Continuous Hg analyzer on outlet (atomic fluorescence spectroscopy).
- **SIF** to divert flow to standby bed at breakthrough.
- Spent carbon must be **handled as hazardous waste** (HgS is toxic) and sent to licensed recyclers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B / 14 marks):** For the Hammerschmidt equation, students often **forget to convert °C to °F** (loss of 2 marks) and **fail to state the assumption** that $W$ refers to the aqueous phase, not the bulk flow (loss of 1 mark). Always explicitly state the unit system and phase.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Gas Purification (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Sweet gas**: $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ < **4 ppmv**; **Sour gas**: above this threshold.
- ☐ **$\text{H}_2\text{S}$ IDLH** = **100 ppm**; **PEL** = **10 ppm**; lethal concentration as low as **700 ppm** for 30 min exposure.
- ☐ **Amine types**: MEA (non-selective), DEA, MDEA (selective for $\text{H}_2\text{S}$).
- ☐ **Amine loading** $\alpha$: lean < 0.05, rich 0.4–0.5; over-loading → corrosion & $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ slip.
- ☐ **Regeneration**: reboiler at **115–125°C**; rich-to-lean $\Delta \alpha$ drives amine circulation.
- ☐ **Hydrate control**: thermodynamic (MeOH/MEG) or LDHI; **Hammerschmidt equation** uses °F for $\Delta T$.
- ☐ **Dehydration target**: dew point **< -10°C** at line pressure; molecular sieves achieve **-100°C**.
- ☐ **Mercury removal**: sulfur-impregnated activated carbon; target **<0.01 $\mu$g/Nm³** for LNG.
- ☐ **Claus process**: 1 mol $\text{O}_2$/mol $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ overall; thermal stage uses **1.5 mol**; >99.8% recovery with TGTU.
- ☐ **Safety interlock priorities**: (1) $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ in sales line, (2) Loss of amine flow, (3) High $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ in regenerator overhead.
- ☐ **OSHA standards**: $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ PEL 10 ppm, STEL 15 ppm; $\text{SO}_2$ PEL 5 ppm.
- ☐ **Equipment hierarchy**: Slug catcher → Filter → Hg bed → Amine contactor → Glycol dehydration → Molecular sieve → Sales.
- ☐ **Indian regulatory body**: **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate)** standards OISD-244 (gas processing) and PNGRB regulations govern gas pipeline quality.
- ☐ **Real-world disaster case studies** to remember: **Bhopal 1984** ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$/MIC), **Skikda 2004** (Hg + cryogenic), **Piper Alpha 1988** (hydrocarbon + safety system failure).
- ☐ **Key equations to memorize**: Hammerschmidt, Stokes settling, Henry's Law, amine loading definition, Claus stoichiometry.
- ☐ **Always show three steps** in any gas calculation: composition → component flow → stoichiometric reagent requirement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sulphur Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sulphur Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sulphur Recovery** is a critical downstream process in hydrocarbon processing (refineries, natural gas sweetening units, and petrochemical plants) wherein the toxic and corrosive **Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S)** present in acid/sour gas streams is converted into elemental sulphur (S) — a non-toxic, commercially valuable, and safe-to-handle product. The principal process is the **Claus Process**, a thermal-catalytic chemical conversion system that recovers between **94% to 99.8%** of the inlet sulphur, depending on process configuration and number of catalytic stages.

In hydrocarbon industries, sulphur enters the process chain primarily as **H₂S** in "sour" crude oils, natural gas, and refinery off-gases. The recovery of sulphur is a **mandatory environmental and economic necessity** driven by:
- **Environmental compliance** (SO₂ emission limits under international/national environmental norms)
- **Product quality enhancement** (sulphur content in fuels must be reduced)
- **Corrosion mitigation** in downstream equipment
- **Commercial monetization** of recovered elemental sulphur

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Detox Station" of a Refinery:**
> Imagine a city water treatment plant that filters out a poisonous contaminant (mercury, lead) from wastewater before releasing the cleaned water into the river. The contaminant is captured and converted into a harmless or commercially useful solid. **Sulphur recovery is the exact equivalent** in the hydrocarbon industry — H₂S is the "poison" extracted from acid gas streams, and the **Claus Process is the "treatment plant"** that transforms it into bright yellow elemental sulphur pellets (the "clean, safe output").

A simpler way to picture it:
- **Input (Sour Acid Gas):** A highly toxic, foul-smelling, corrosive mixture dominated by H₂S.
- **Process (Claus Reactor System):** A two-stage thermal + catalytic conversion setup where H₂S is partially combusted to SO₂, and then reacts further with remaining H₂S to form elemental sulphur.
- **Output:** Vapour/liquid elemental sulphur + treated tail gas (released to atmosphere or further scrubbed).

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Benchmarks (must remember for KTU exams):**
> - **Claus Process overall conversion efficiency:** Typically **94%–98%** (2-stage), up to **99.8%** with tail-gas treatment.
> - **H₂S feed concentration range:** **20%–90% (mole %)** of acid gas stream.
> - **Reaction Furnace Temperature:** **900°C – 1300°C** (thermal stage).
> - **Catalytic Converter Temperatures:** **200°C – 350°C** (per reactor, with reheat).
> - **Standard reaction enthalpy (2H₂S + SO₂ → 3S + 2H₂O):** ΔH ≈ **–146.4 kJ/mol of S** (exothermic).
> - **Elemental Sulphur product purity:** **99.9% minimum** (commercial "bright sulphur").
> - **Molar mass of H₂S:** **34.08 g/mol** | **Molar mass of S₈:** **256.52 g/mol**.

## 1.4 Why Sulphur Recovery is Essential in Hydrocarbon Safety

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Safety & Environmental Concern:**
> Direct venting or flaring of H₂S-rich acid gas is **strictly prohibited**. H₂S is lethal at concentrations as low as **700 ppm (0.07%) in air within minutes** (per OSHA exposure limits). The Claus Process eliminates this hazard by converting H₂S into stable, manageable elemental sulphur, simultaneously preventing the formation of acid rain via SO₂ emissions.

The **multi-dimensional need** for sulphur recovery is summarized below:
- **Environmental Protection:** Reduces SO₂ emissions that cause acid rain.
- **Public Health:** Prevents release of toxic H₂S into communities near refineries/gas plants.
- **Equipment Safety:** Stops severe corrosion in pipelines, heat exchangers, and storage tanks.
- **Economic Gain:** Recovered sulphur is sold for sulphuric acid production, fertilizer manufacturing, and rubber vulcanization.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Adheres to KTU-relevant environmental and process safety standards (ISO 14001, OSHA 29 CFR 1910, EPA norms).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Claus Process — Foundational Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** Invented in **1883 by Carl Friedrich Claus** (German-British chemist), the original Claus process used a fixed-bed bauxite catalyst. The modern, widely-used version is the **Modified Claus Process** (developed by I.G. Farben in the 1930s), which adds a thermal combustion stage ahead of the catalytic converters.

The **Modified Claus Process** operates in **two distinct stages:**

### Stage A — Thermal Stage (Reaction Furnace)
In a high-temperature burner chamber, one-third of the incoming H₂S is **fully combusted** with air (or oxygen-enriched air) to produce SO₂:

$$2 \, H_2S + 3 \, O_2 \rightarrow 2 \, SO_2 + 2 \, H_2O$$

The remaining two-thirds of H₂S then reacts with the generated SO₂ in the **Claus reaction** (also occurring in the furnace, but predominantly in the catalytic reactors downstream):

$$2 \, H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3 \, S + 2 \, H_2O$$

### Stage B — Catalytic Stage (Reactor Train)
The partially converted gas mixture is cooled (in **Waste Heat Boilers, WHBs**) to condense elemental sulphur, then reheated and passed through **fixed-bed catalytic reactors** (typically filled with **activated alumina or titanium dioxide catalysts**) where the Claus reaction proceeds to higher conversion at lower temperatures (favored by **Le Chatelier's principle** because the reaction is exothermic and produces more moles of product).

> [!TIP]
> **Why multiple catalytic stages?** Each successive catalytic stage operates at a **progressively lower temperature** to push equilibrium toward higher sulphur yield. Most plants use **2 or 3 catalytic stages**, achieving ~96% recovery; adding a **4th stage** pushes recovery to ~97.5%. To exceed 99%, a **Tail Gas Treatment (TGT) unit** (e.g., SCOT, Beavon-Stretford) is mandatory.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Process Step

| Process Step | Physical/Chemical "Why" | Engineering Purpose |
|--------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Acid gas preheating | Liquids in feed vaporize; reaction kinetics favored at high T | Prevent condensation corrosion |
| Reaction Furnace combustion | Provides activation energy for endothermic first step | Generate SO₂ for downstream Claus reaction |
| Waste Heat Boiler (WHB) | Recover energy; condense sulphur vapor; reduce temperature | High-pressure steam generation + sulphur recovery |
| Catalytic Reactor (Reheat between stages) | Maintain optimum catalytic activity; raise temperature above sulphur dew point | Prevent catalyst deactivation; maximize conversion |
| Sulphur Condenser | Lower temperature condenses S₈ vapour to liquid S | Physical separation of product |
| Tail Gas Treating | Reduce residual H₂S/SO₂ to <10 ppm before venting | Environmental compliance (99.9%+ overall recovery) |
| Sulphur Pit (degassing) | Liquid sulphur holds dissolved H₂Sₓ polysulphides; venting releases toxic vapors | Worker safety + product quality |

## 2.3 Process Variants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Modified Claus Variants (frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **SuperClaus® (Jacobs Comprimo):** Replaces last catalytic stage with selective oxidation catalyst; achieves **99% recovery without TGT**.
> - **EuroClaus®:** Combines standard Claus with a final selective oxidation step; **99.5% recovery**.
> - **Oxygen-based Claus (CO₂ reduction, Oxy-Claus):** Uses pure O₂ instead of air; smaller equipment, higher capacity, **99.5% recovery**.
> - **CPS / Sulfreen / CBA / MCRC:** Various sub-dew-point processes that recover sulphur below its condensation temperature, pushing equilibrium conversion higher.

## 2.4 Tail Gas Treatment (TGT) — Polishing Stage

> [!WARNING]
> Even the best Claus unit only achieves ~98% conversion. The remaining ~2% includes unreacted H₂S, SO₂, COS (carbonyl sulphide), and CS₂. **Releasing this tail gas directly would violate environmental norms.** Hence, **TGT units** are mandatory for stringent compliance.

Common TGT technologies:
- **SCOT (Shell Claus Offgas Treating):** Reduces H₂S/SO₂ to <10 ppm by reducing all sulphur compounds to H₂S, then absorbing in an amine solvent.
- **Beavon-Stretford:** Uses a different H₂S absorption chemistry (vanadate-based).
- **Wellman-Lord:** Uses Na₂SO₃/NaHSO₃ absorption.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table — it carries high-yield marks in KTU ESE questions.**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Units |
|---|------------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\eta_{Claus} = \dfrac{n_{S,out}}{n_{H_2S,in}} \times 100\%$ | Claus process recovery efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $n_{H_2S,combusted} = \dfrac{1}{3} \, n_{H_2S,in}$ | H₂S fraction fully oxidized in furnace (stoichiometric) | kmol/h |
| 3 | $2 \, H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3 \, S + 2 \, H_2O$ | Claus equilibrium reaction (catalytic) | — |
| 4 | $K_p(T) = \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ(T)}{RT}\right)$ | Equilibrium constant for Claus reaction | bar or atm |
| 5 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ equilibrium constant | kJ/mol |
| 6 | $n_{S,produced} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times n_{H_2S,reacted}$ | Stoichiometric sulphur production | kmol |
| 7 | $H_2S : SO_2$ optimal molar ratio = $2 : 1$ | Stoichiometric requirement for complete Claus conversion | mol/mol |
| 8 | $Air : Acid\ Gas$ ratio $\approx$ controlled such that outlet $H_2S/SO_2$ = 2:1 | Operational parameter (ratio control) | volume ratio |
| 9 | $C_{SO_2,emission} = k \times (1 - \eta_{recovery})$ | Emission concentration inversely proportional to recovery | ppm |
| 10 | Claus furnace T $\approx 900 - 1300^\circ C$ | Thermal stage operating window | °C |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Sulphur recovery is a **cornerstone of process safety engineering** in hydrocarbon facilities. Its real-world applications include:
- **Oil refineries (atmospheric & vacuum distillation units):** Treating amine regenerator off-gas.
- **Natural gas processing plants:** Sweetening of sour gas from gas wells.
- **Petrochemical complexes:** Treating off-gases from ethylene crackers, coke calciners.
- **Coal gasification plants:** Handling syngas-derived H₂S.
- **Biofuels and renewable diesel plants:** Treating sulphur-containing byproduct streams.

Engineers trained in KTU's **Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries** course must understand sulphur recovery as a **vital link in the chain of process safety management (PSM)** — without it, modern hydrocarbon processing would be impossible due to environmental and safety constraints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Claus Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A 2-stage Modified Claus unit processes an acid gas stream containing **100 kmol/h of H₂S** and no other reactive components. Determine: (a) the stoichiometric O₂ requirement, (b) the air requirement (assuming air = 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by volume), and (c) the elemental sulphur production in the furnace stage only.

### Step 1 — Write the combustion reaction (furnace)

$$2 \, H_2S + 3 \, O_2 \rightarrow 2 \, SO_2 + 2 \, H_2O$$

### Step 2 — Apply the 1/3 H₂S combustion rule (Claus stoichiometry)

For maximum conversion, exactly **1/3 of H₂S is fully oxidized** to SO₂, while the remaining 2/3 reacts via the Claus reaction. Therefore:

$$n_{H_2S,oxidized} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 100 = 33.33 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate O₂ required for combustion

From stoichiometry, 2 mol H₂S needs 3 mol O₂. Therefore:

$$n_{O_2} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times n_{H_2S,oxidized}$$

$$n_{O_2} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 33.33 = 50.0 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

### Step 4 — Convert O₂ to air

Air contains 21 mol% O₂ (i.e., 0.21 mole fraction). Therefore:

$$n_{Air} = \dfrac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} = \dfrac{50.0}{0.21} \approx 238.1 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate elemental sulphur from furnace

The Claus reaction (which partially occurs in the furnace at high T) is:

$$2 \, H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3 \, S + 2 \, H_2O$$

For 33.33 kmol/h H₂S oxidized, 33.33 kmol/h SO₂ is produced. The remaining H₂S = 66.67 kmol/h reacts stoichiometrically with 33.33 kmol/h SO₂:

$$n_{S,furnace} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times \min(66.67,\ 33.33) = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 33.33 = 50.0 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

In mass terms (using M(S) = 32.06 g/mol):

$$\dot{m}_{S,furnace} = 50.0 \times 32.06 = 1603 \ \text{kg/h}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Insight:** The furnace alone converts **50 kmol out of 100 kmol = 50%** of the inlet H₂S into elemental sulphur. The remaining ~50% is converted in the catalytic stages.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Overall Claus Recovery Efficiency for a 3-Stage Unit

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A 3-stage Modified Claus unit processes **200 kmol/h of H₂S** in acid gas. After the furnace + 3 catalytic stages, the analysis shows: Stage 1 (catalytic) converts 60% of remaining H₂S to S, Stage 2 converts 50% of remaining, Stage 3 converts 40% of remaining. Determine the overall recovery efficiency.

### Step 1 — Furnace conversion (50% as derived above)

$$n_{H_2S,after\ furnace} = 200 \times (1 - 0.50) = 100 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

### Step 2 — Catalytic stage 1 (60% conversion)

$$n_{H_2S,after\ S1} = 100 \times (1 - 0.60) = 40.0 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

### Step 3 — Catalytic stage 2 (50% conversion)

$$n_{H_2S,after\ S2} = 40.0 \times (1 - 0.50) = 20.0 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

### Step 4 — Catalytic stage 3 (40% conversion)

$$n_{H_2S,after\ S3} = 20.0 \times (1 - 0.40) = 12.0 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

### Step 5 — Total H₂S converted to sulphur

$$n_{H_2S,converted} = 200 - 12.0 = 188.0 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

### Step 6 — Overall recovery efficiency

$$\eta_{Claus} = \dfrac{188.0}{200.0} \times 100\% = 94.0\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** 94% is the typical efficiency of a **3-stage Modified Claus** without tail-gas treatment. To exceed **99%**, a TGT unit (e.g., SCOT) is required to polish the residual 6%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equilibrium Constant and Temperature Effect on Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> The Claus reaction $2 H_2S + SO_2 \rightleftharpoons 3 S + 2 H_2O$ is exothermic. **Lowering the temperature** shifts the equilibrium toward products (Le Chatelier). For a typical catalytic reactor, the **van't Hoff relation** gives the temperature dependence of $K_p$:

$$\dfrac{d(\ln K_p)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$

Integrating (assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is roughly constant over the temperature range of interest):

$$\ln \left(\dfrac{K_{p,2}}{K_{p,1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

### Numerical Example

Given $K_{p,1} = 50$ at $T_1 = 600 \, K$ and $\Delta H^\circ = -146.4 \, kJ/mol$, find $K_{p,2}$ at $T_2 = 450 \, K$:

$$\ln \left(\dfrac{K_{p,2}}{50}\right) = -\dfrac{-146400}{8.314} \left(\dfrac{1}{450} - \dfrac{1}{600}\right)$$

$$\ln \left(\dfrac{K_{p,2}}{50}\right) = 17606 \times (0.002222 - 0.001667) = 17606 \times 0.000556 = 9.79$$

$$\dfrac{K_{p,2}}{50} = e^{9.79} = 17820$$

$$K_{p,2} \approx 8.91 \times 10^{5}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Takeaway:** Lowering the temperature from 600 K to 450 K **increases $K_p$ by ~4 orders of magnitude**, dramatically improving equilibrium conversion. This is why **successive catalytic reactors operate at progressively lower temperatures** in industry.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Operating Parameters and Their Safety Impact

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Exam-Worthy Matrix:** Exam questions frequently ask "How does X affect Y in the Claus process?" This matrix is your reference.

| Parameter | Direction of Change | Effect on Conversion | Effect on Safety | Effect on Cost |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Furnace Temperature (↑) | Increase | Higher initial H₂S destruction | Higher refractory wear, NOₓ formation | Higher fuel/air cost |
| H₂S/SO₂ ratio at 2:1 (deviation) | Deviation from 2:1 | Conversion drops sharply | Possible unreacted H₂S release | Sulphur loss = revenue loss |
| Number of catalytic stages (↑) | Increase | Conversion increases to ~99%+ | Larger equipment footprint | Higher CAPEX |
| O₂ enrichment (↑) | Increase | Higher capacity, smaller unit | Lower vent gas, less HCN/COS | Higher O₂ supply cost |
| Catalyst deactivation (↑) | Increase | Conversion drops | Sulphur emissions rise | Catalyst replacement cost |
| Sulphur condenser outlet T (↑) | Increase | More S lost as vapour | Plugging, fouling risk | Energy cost |
| Tail gas recycle (↑) | Increase | Better recovery | Higher blower duty | Compressor cost |

---

## 3.5 Material Balance Summary for a 2-Stage Claus Unit (Numerical)

**Inputs:** 100 kmol/h H₂S, 238.1 kmol/h air (= 50 kmol O₂ + 188.1 kmol N₂)
**Assumed conversion:** Furnace 50% → Catalytic Stage 1: 60% of remaining → Catalytic Stage 2: 50% of remaining

| Stream | H₂S (kmol/h) | SO₂ (kmol/h) | S (kmol/h) | H₂O (kmol/h) | N₂ (kmol/h) |
|--------|--------------|---------------|------------|---------------|---------------|
| Acid gas feed | 100 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Air feed | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 188.1 |
| After furnace | 50 | 16.7 | 50 | 66.7 | 188.1 |
| After Cat-1 | 20 | 6.67 | 95 | 88.3 | 188.1 |
| After Cat-2 | 10 | 3.33 | 117.5 | 95.0 | 188.1 |
| Tail gas | 10 | 3.33 | ~0 | 95.0 | 188.1 |

**Overall recovery efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{100 - 10}{100} \times 100\% = 90\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 2-stage example yields 90%. Real 2-stage plants achieve ~92–96% due to optimized operating conditions; the simplified 60%/50% stage efficiencies used here are conservative teaching assumptions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Modified Claus Process (Mermaid Flowchart)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents the **complete functional architecture of a 2-Stage Modified Claus Process with Tail Gas Treatment**. Each block corresponds to a real unit operation encountered in industrial sulphur recovery plants.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    acidGasIn["Acid Gas Feed H2S Rich Stream"] --> preheatX1["Acid Gas Preheater"]
    preheatX1 --> combFurnace["Reaction Furnace Thermal Stage 900C to 1300C"]
    airIn["Combustion Air Blower"] --> combFurnace
    combFurnace --> whb1["Waste Heat Boiler WHB 1 High Pressure Steam Generation"]
    whb1 --> sCond1["Sulphur Condenser 1"]
    sCond1 --> sulphurPit["Sulphur Pit Product Collection"]
    sCond1 --> reheater1["Reheater between Stage 1 and 2"]
    reheater1 --> catReactor1["Catalytic Reactor 1 Alumina Catalyst 320C"]
    catReactor1 --> sCond2["Sulphur Condenser 2"]
    sCond2 --> sulphurPit
    sCond2 --> reheater2["Reheater before Tail Gas"]
    reheater2 --> tgu["Tail Gas Treatment Unit SCOT or Beavon Stretford"]
    tgu --> incinerator["Thermal Incinerator Oxidation of Residual H2S to SO2"]
    incinerator --> stackAtm["Atmospheric Vents Through Stack"]
    whb1 --> steamHdr["Steam Header Plant Utility"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows from **acidGasIn** (top-left) downward to **stackAtm** (bottom-right). The sulphur product exits laterally into **sulphurPit**. Steam generated in **whb1** is exported to the **steamHdr** for plant-wide utility use — making the Claus process also an **energy-recovery system**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Material & Energy Flow Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This second diagram shows the **input-output (mass balance) view** of a Claus unit, useful for solving KTU numerical problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["INPUT Acid Gas H2S stream 100 kmol per h"] --> sysClaus["Modified Claus Reactor System"]
    inB["INPUT Combustion Air 238 kmol per h"] --> sysClaus
    sysClaus --> outA["OUTPUT Liquid Sulphur Product 90 to 99 percent purity"]
    sysClaus --> outB["OUTPUT Tail Gas Residual H2S and SO2 less than 10 ppm after TGT"]
    sysClaus --> outC["OUTPUT High Pressure Steam Recovered Energy from WHB"]
    sysClaus --> outD["OUTPUT Condensate Water from Claus Reaction 2H2S + SO2 to 3S + 2H2O"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Safety & Control Loop Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> Sulphur recovery is inherently a **safety-critical** process. The diagram below shows the **process control interlocks** that protect the plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fesSensor["H2S Analyzer in Acid Gas Feed"] --> ratioCtrl["H2S to SO2 Ratio Controller Setpoint 2 to 1"]
    airFlowmeter["Air Flow Transmitter"] --> ratioCtrl
    ratioCtrl --> airDamper["Air Inlet Damper Actuator"]
    tguAnalyzer["Tail Gas H2S Analyzer less than 10 ppm"] --> alarmSys["Process Alarm and Shutdown System"]
    acidGasFlow["Acid Gas Flow Meter"] --> alarmSys
    furnaceT["Furnace Temperature Indicator 900 to 1300 C"] --> alarmSys
    alarmSys --> emergShutoff["Emergency Shutdown ESD Logic and Block Valves"]
    sPitLevel["Sulphur Pit Level Switch"] --> alarmSys
    emergShutoff --> flareSafe["Safe Flaring of Untreated Acid Gas"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Control Loop:** The **H₂S:SO₂ ratio controller** is the single most important loop in a Claus unit. Deviation beyond ±5% from 2:1 causes dramatic efficiency loss and potential SO₂ emission violations. KTU exams frequently test this control philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Part A pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Direct conceptual or definition questions, typically 1-2 sentences plus a small calculation. Marks awarded for: (1) keyword recognition, (2) correct formula, (3) clean final answer.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(3 Marks)** *Define the Claus Process. Why is the H₂S:SO₂ molar ratio maintained at 2:1 in the catalytic converters?*

**Model Answer:**

The Claus Process is a **thermal-catalytic process** used to recover elemental sulphur from H₂S-rich acid gas streams in hydrocarbon processing plants. The process has two stages: in the **thermal stage (reaction furnace)** at 900°C–1300°C, one-third of H₂S is combusted to SO₂; in the **catalytic stages** at 200°C–350°C, the remaining H₂S reacts with SO₂ over activated alumina or TiO₂ catalyst to form elemental sulphur.

The **2:1 H₂S:SO₂ ratio** is maintained because it is the **stoichiometric requirement** of the Claus equilibrium reaction $2 H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3 S + 2 H_2O$. Any deviation reduces conversion: **excess H₂S** leaves unreacted toxic gas in the tail gas, while **excess SO₂** produces unwanted SO₂ emissions and acid rain precursors. A ratio controller continuously adjusts the combustion air supply to maintain this optimum ratio.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if the student writes the ratio as "1:2" instead of "H₂S:SO₂ = 2:1". Always specify the **numerator:denominator** carefully.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
