**(3 Marks)** *List any three process variants of the Modified Claus Process. Mention the overall recovery efficiency achieved by each.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Variant | Recovery Efficiency |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Standard 2-Stage Modified Claus | 94% – 96% |
| 2 | 3-Stage Modified Claus (without TGT) | 96% – 98% |
| 3 | SuperClaus® (selective oxidation on last stage) | ~99% without TGT |
| 4 | EuroClaus® (combined Claus + selective oxidation) | 99.0% – 99.5% |
| 5 | Claus + SCOT Tail Gas Treatment | 99.8% – 99.9% |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Writing **3 variants + efficiencies** in a clean table fetches full 3 marks. Avoid writing paragraphs in Part A.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern for 14-mark questions:**
> - Sub-part (a): 7 marks (typically descriptive — process, components, chemistry)
> - Sub-part (b): 7 marks (typically numerical or detailed analytical)
> - Internal choice: Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, describe the **Modified Claus Process** for sulphur recovery from acid gas in a hydrocarbon processing plant. Explain the role of the **reaction furnace, waste heat boiler, catalytic reactors, sulphur condensers, and reheaters** in the process.

**(b)** A refinery acid gas stream contains **75% H₂S and 25% CO₂ (by mole)** at a flow rate of **500 kmol/h**. This stream is fed to a 2-stage Modified Claus unit operating with **50% H₂S conversion in the furnace** and **60% conversion in each catalytic stage** (of the remaining H₂S). Calculate:
- (i) The stoichiometric air requirement (kmol/h).
- (ii) The elemental sulphur produced in the furnace and in each catalytic stage.
- (iii) The overall Claus recovery efficiency.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Modified Claus Process Description (7 marks):**

The Modified Claus Process recovers elemental sulphur from H₂S in **two integrated stages**:

1. **Thermal Stage (Reaction Furnace):** Acid gas is burned with a controlled amount of air in a refractory-lined combustion chamber. Approximately **1/3 of H₂S is combusted to SO₂** at temperatures of 900°C–1300°C. The remaining 2/3 of H₂S reacts with the SO₂ (partial Claus reaction). CO₂ present in feed is largely inert and passes through.

2. **Catalytic Stage:** The cooled gas passes through a train of 2–4 catalytic reactors containing activated alumina or TiO₂ catalyst. The Claus reaction $2 H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3 S + 2 H_2O$ proceeds at progressively lower temperatures (320°C, 240°C, 200°C), maximizing equilibrium conversion. Between each reactor, the gas is reheated (above sulphur dew point to prevent catalyst deactivation) and elemental sulphur is condensed in shell-and-tube condensers.

**Roles of each equipment:**

- **Reaction Furnace:** Provides activation energy; destroys H₂S thermally; converts 1/3 H₂S to SO₂.
- **Waste Heat Boiler (WHB):** Recovers high-grade heat as high-pressure steam; cools gas to ~300°C.
- **Reheaters:** Heat gas between catalytic stages; prevent sulphur condensation on catalyst (sulphur poisoning).
- **Catalytic Reactors:** Provide active sites for low-temperature Claus reaction; high equilibrium conversion.
- **Sulphur Condensers:** Condense sulphur vapour to liquid; separate product from gas stream.
- **Sulphur Pit:** Collects liquid sulphur; allows degassing of dissolved H₂Sₓ.
- **Tail Gas Treating Unit:** Polishes residual H₂S/SO₂ to <10 ppm for environmental compliance.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — H₂S feed rate:**

$$n_{H_2S} = 0.75 \times 500 = 375 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric air for 1/3 H₂S combustion (subpart i):**

H₂S to be oxidized in furnace = (1/3) × 375 = 125 kmol/h

From reaction $2 H_2S + 3 O_2 \rightarrow 2 SO_2 + 2 H_2O$:

$$n_{O_2} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 125 = 187.5 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$n_{Air} = \dfrac{187.5}{0.21} = 892.86 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Sulphur produced in furnace (subpart ii):**

Furnace conversion = 50% of H₂S input. Remaining H₂S = 50% × 375 = 187.5 kmol/h. SO₂ produced = 125 kmol/h. Limiting reactant = 187.5 kmol H₂S needs 93.75 kmol SO₂; we have 125 kmol SO₂, so H₂S is limiting.

$$n_{S,furnace} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 93.75 = 140.625 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

In mass: $140.625 \times 32.06 = 4508 \ \text{kg/h}$

**Step 4 — Sulphur from Catalytic Stage 1:**

H₂S entering Cat-1 = 187.5 − 93.75 = 93.75 kmol/h. Conversion = 60%.

$$n_{H_2S,reacted,S1} = 0.60 \times 93.75 = 56.25 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$n_{S,Cat1} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 56.25 = 84.375 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 — Sulphur from Catalytic Stage 2:**

H₂S entering Cat-2 = 93.75 − 56.25 = 37.5 kmol/h. Conversion = 60%.

$$n_{H_2S,reacted,S2} = 0.60 \times 37.5 = 22.5 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$n_{S,Cat2} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times 22.5 = 33.75 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 6 — Overall recovery (subpart iii):**

Total H₂S reacted = 93.75 + 56.25 + 22.5 = 172.5 kmol/h (this is the same as total S = 140.625 + 84.375 + 33.75 = 258.75 kmol/h S atoms = 172.5 kmol H₂S equivalent)

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{172.5}{375} \times 100\% = 46.0\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the low recovery:** The 46% value reflects the *given* furnace/stage conversion efficiencies (50%, 60%, 60%) which are conservative teaching values. In real industrial plants, these values are higher due to optimal temperature, residence time, and the 2:1 ratio being maintained continuously, giving 90–96% overall.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **safety, environmental, and economic significance** of sulphur recovery in the hydrocarbon industry. Discuss the role of **tail gas treatment (TGT)** units such as the **SCOT process** in achieving **99.9%+ recovery** in modern refineries.

**(b)** A natural gas processing plant acid gas feed contains **200 kmol/h of H₂S** and **50 kmol/h of CO₂**. The Claus unit is followed by a SCOT tail gas treatment unit that reduces the residual H₂S in the tail gas to **2.5 kmol/h**. Calculate:
- (i) The mass of elemental sulphur recovered per day (kg/day), assuming all converted H₂S becomes sulphur.
- (ii) The overall recovery efficiency of the **combined Claus + TGT system**.
- (iii) The amount of SO₂ emitted to atmosphere per hour (in kg/h) if the residual 2.5 kmol/h H₂S is fully oxidized in an incinerator to SO₂.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Safety, Environmental, and Economic Significance (7 marks):**

- **Safety:** H₂S is lethal at ~700 ppm in air. Claus recovery prevents accidental release of toxic H₂S during processing, transport, and storage, protecting workers and nearby communities.
- **Environmental:** SO₂ emissions cause acid rain and respiratory diseases. The Claus process converts H₂S to benign elemental sulphur, eliminating SO₂ precursors from refinery/gas plant vent stacks.
- **Economic:** Recovered sulphur (99.9% purity) is sold for sulphuric acid production, fertilizers, and rubber industries — turning a hazardous waste into a marketable product.
- **Tail Gas Treatment (SCOT):** Even the best Claus unit achieves only 96–98% conversion. The **SCOT (Shell Claus Offgas Treating)** process takes the tail gas, **reduces all sulphur compounds (SO₂, COS, CS₂) to H₂S** over a Co/Mo catalyst, then **absorbs the H₂S in an amine solvent (typically MDEA)**, regenerates it in a stripper, and recycles the concentrated H₂S back to the Claus unit inlet. This reduces overall emissions to **<10 ppm H₂S equivalent**, achieving **99.8%–99.9% total recovery**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — H₂S converted = Total H₂S − residual (subpart i):**

$$n_{H_2S,converted} = 200 - 2.5 = 197.5 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Sulphur produced (1 mol H₂S → 1 mol S):**

$$n_S = 197.5 \ \text{kmol/h} = 197.5 \times 32.06 = 6331.85 \ \text{kg/h}$$

Per day:

$$\dot{m}_{S,day} = 6331.85 \times 24 = 1{,}51{,}964 \ \text{kg/day} \approx 152 \ \text{tonnes/day}$$

**Step 3 — Overall recovery efficiency (subpart ii):**

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{197.5}{200} \times 100\% = 98.75\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** The recovery can be pushed above 99.9% by adding polishing stages, larger amine absorbers, or using a 4th Claus catalytic stage. Modern refineries typically target 99.9%+ due to stringent environmental norms.

**Step 4 — SO₂ emitted from incinerator (subpart iii):**

Residual H₂S = 2.5 kmol/h. Combustion reaction:

$$2 \, H_2S + 3 \, O_2 \rightarrow 2 \, SO_2 + 2 \, H_2O$$

$$n_{SO_2} = n_{H_2S} = 2.5 \ \text{kmol/h}$$

Mass of SO₂ emitted:

$$\dot{m}_{SO_2} = 2.5 \times 64.07 = 160.2 \ \text{kg/h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Some students mistakenly write the SO₂ emission as 5 kmol/h (forgetting the 1:1 stoichiometry from H₂S to SO₂). Always re-check the **balanced equation** before computing mass emissions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Sulphur Recovery Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong ratio direction:** Writing SO₂:H₂S = 1:2 instead of H₂S:SO₂ = 2:1. Always specify which is numerator.
> 2. **Ignoring inert gases:** N₂ in air must be traced in material balance tables. Skipping it costs a full step's marks.
> 3. **Forgetting stoichiometric O₂ → air conversion:** 1 mol O₂ does NOT equal 1 mol air. Divide by 0.21.
> 4. **Confusing the two Claus reactions:** The combustion reaction ($H_2S + O_2$) is different from the Claus equilibrium reaction ($H_2S + SO_2$). Examiners differentiate between them.
> 5. **Molar mass errors:** Using S₈ (256) instead of atomic S (32) when computing kg of sulphur. Most engineering calculations use atomic S = **32.06 g/mol**.
> 6. **Skipping tail gas discussion:** A full 14-mark answer on Modified Claus must mention TGT, even if not asked explicitly — it shows conceptual maturity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall!)**

- ✅ **Sulphur Recovery** converts H₂S → elemental sulphur via the Modified Claus Process.
- ✅ Two stages: **Thermal Furnace (900–1300°C)** + **Catalytic Reactors (200–350°C)**.
- ✅ Key reactions:
  - Combustion: $2 H_2S + 3 O_2 \rightarrow 2 SO_2 + 2 H_2O$
  - Claus: $2 H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3 S + 2 H_2O$
- ✅ **H₂S:SO₂ ratio = 2:1** (strictly maintained by ratio controller).
- ✅ **1/3 of H₂S is combusted** in furnace; **2/3 reacts catalytically**.
- ✅ 2-stage Claus: ~94% | 3-stage: ~96–98% | With TGT (SCOT): **99.8–99.9%**.
- ✅ Common variants: **SuperClaus, EuroClaus, Oxy-Claus, MCRC**.
- ✅ **Tail Gas Treatment** (e.g., SCOT) is mandatory for >99% recovery and emission compliance.
- ✅ **H₂S lethal at 700 ppm** in air — direct release is forbidden by safety codes.
- ✅ Air requirement = O₂ requirement / 0.21 (because air is 21% O₂).
- ✅ Atomic S molar mass = **32.06 g/mol**; H₂S = **34.08 g/mol**; SO₂ = **64.07 g/mol**.
- ✅ **Reheaters** between catalytic stages prevent sulphur dew-point condensation on catalyst.
- ✅ **Waste Heat Boiler (WHB)** generates high-pressure steam — Claus is also an **energy-recovery system**.
- ✅ **Sulphur Pit** requires degassing to remove dissolved H₂Sₓ polysulphides (safety + product quality).
- ✅ **Lower catalytic stage temperature → higher equilibrium conversion** (Le Chatelier's principle, exothermic reaction).
- ✅ Recovery efficiency formula: $\eta = \dfrac{n_{H_2S,in} - n_{H_2S,out}}{n_{H_2S,in}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ Always show **balanced chemical equations** in numerical answers — examiners award 1–2 marks for this alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note:** Sulphur recovery is a **mandatory compliance, safety, and revenue-generating unit** in every modern hydrocarbon facility. A KTU Safety-in-Hydrocarbon-Industries engineer must understand both the **process chemistry** (Claus reactions) and the **safety engineering** (TGT, ratio control, ESD systems) to design and operate such plants safely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lubricating Oil Unit
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lubricating Oil Unit

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubricating Oil Unit (Lube Oil Complex / LOB - Lube Oil Blending):** A downstream process facility in a petroleum refinery that converts heavy vacuum distillation residues (long-residue / short-residue, Bright Stock feedstocks) into high-grade lubricating base oils through a sequence of physical separation, solvent refining, dewaxing, and catalytic finishing operations, followed by additive blending to meet specific viscosity, pour point, and oxidation stability specifications.

The **Lubricating Oil Unit** is classified under KTU 2024 Scheme as a *secondary refining process train* responsible for manufacturing **base oils** grouped into API Groups I, II, and III, depending on the extent of hydroprocessing versus solvent refining employed.

| **API Group** | **Saturates (%)** | **Sulfur (%)** | **Viscosity Index (VI)** | **Process Route** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Group I | < 90 | > 0.03 | 80 – 119 | Solvent Refining |
| Group II | ≥ 90 | ≤ 0.03 | 80 – 119 | Hydroprocessing |
| Group III | ≥ 90 | ≤ 0.03 | ≥ 120 | Severe Hydroprocessing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Base Oil vs. Lubricant:** *Base oil* is the refined hydrocarbon fluid (~90% of finished lubricant). *Lubricant* = Base Oil + Additive Package (anti-wear, detergent, antioxidant, VI improver, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a **Lubricating Oil Unit as a "car wash and spa for crude oil molecules"**. After crude oil is distilled, the heavy, waxy, dark, asphaltic residue is like a person covered in mud (asphaltenes), grease (aromatics), and sticky wax. The unit "washes" away the undesirable parts using liquid solvents (the "soap"), "cools" the oil to crystallize and filter out wax (the "waxing removal at the salon"), and finally gives it a "polish and vitamin shot" (hydrofinishing + additives) to make it a smooth, stable, premium lubricant.

| **Analogy Stage** | **Real Process Stage** | **What is "Cleaned"** |
|---|---|---|
| Mud removal shower | Propane Deasphalting | Asphaltenes & resins |
| Deep-cleansing soap wash | Solvent Extraction (Furfural/Phenol/N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone) | Undesirable aromatics & polar compounds |
| Cold wax-stripping | Solvent Dewaxing (MEK / MEK-Toluene) | Long-chain n-paraffins (waxes) |
| Skin polishing & vitamin serum | Hydrofinishing | Olefins, color bodies, trace sulfur/nitrogen |
| Final fragrance & protection | Additive Blending | Performance chemicals added |

## 1.3 Key Operating Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard operating envelope for a Lube Oil Unit:
> - **Propane Deasphalting temperature:** **45 °C – 65 °C**
> - **Solvent Extraction (Furfural) temperature:** **80 °C – 130 °C**
> - **Solvent-to-Oil ratio (Furfural):** **2 : 1 to 4 : 1 (by volume)**
> - **Dewaxing filtration temperature:** **−15 °C to −30 °C**
> - **Hydrofinishing pressure:** **30 – 70 bar (H₂ atmosphere)**
> - **Hydrofinishing temperature:** **250 °C – 320 °C**
> - **API base oil viscosity range:** **100 N (light) to 600 N (heavy) and Bright Stock (BS)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Flow Architecture (Block Logic)

The Lube Oil Unit operates as a **sequential cascade of four mandatory unit operations**, each with a specific physical/chemical function. Skipping any stage degrades base oil quality.

### Stage 1 — Propane Deasphalting (PDA)
- **Feedstock:** Vacuum residue (long residue, ~565 °C+ cut).
- **Solvent:** Liquid propane (C₃H₈) at high pressure.
- **Principle:** *Solubility selectivity*. Asphaltenes (high molecular weight, highly polar) are **insoluble** in liquid propane at low temperature, while desirable paraffinic + naphthenic oils are **soluble**.
- **Outputs:** Deasphalted oil (DAO) → overhead; Propane-deasphalted asphalt (PDA tar) → bottom.
- **Why:** Removes asphaltenes, reduces metals (Ni, V), reduces CCR (Conradson Carbon Residue).

### Stage 2 — Solvent Extraction
- **Feedstock:** DAO (or directly short residue in some schemes).
- **Solvents:** Furfural (most common), Phenol, or **NMP (N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone)** — modern preferred choice due to lower toxicity, higher selectivity, easier recovery.
- **Principle:** *Liquid–Liquid counter-current extraction*. The solvent preferentially dissolves the **undesirable aromatic, polycyclic, and polar compounds** (which degrade VI and oxidation stability), leaving the **paraffinic + naphthenic** fraction (high VI) as raffinate.
- **Key Output Streams:**
  - **Raffinate** → high-VI base oil precursor (desired)
  - **Extract** → aromatic concentrate (sold as rubber processing oil, printing ink oil, or feedstock for carbon black)

### Stage 3 — Solvent Dewaxing
- **Feedstock:** Raffinate from Stage 2.
- **Solvents:** Methyl Ethyl Ketone (MEK), often mixed with **toluene** to improve solvency and reduce viscosity at low temperature → *MEK-Toluene mix (typical 60:40 v/v)*.
- **Principle:** *Selective crystallization + filtration*. The mixture is chilled, causing long-chain n-paraffin wax to crystallize as solid flakes. A rotary vacuum filter separates wax from the dewaxed oil (DAW — Dewaxed Oil).
- **Outputs:** Dewaxed Oil (DAW) + Slack Wax / Petrolatum (by-product).

### Stage 4 — Hydrofinishing (Mild Hydrotreating)
- **Feedstock:** DAW oil.
- **Catalyst:** Ni-Mo or Ni-W on alumina, sometimes with small Pt.
- **Conditions:** H₂ pressure, 250–320 °C.
- **Principle:** *Catalytic saturation + mild heteroatom removal*. Saturates olefins, removes trace sulfur/nitrogen, decolorizes oil, improves oxidation stability. **Does NOT** crack the molecule (unlike severe hydrocracking).
- **Output:** Finished base oil, ready for blending.

### Stage 5 — Additive Blending (LOB Plant)
- Adds **VI Improver** (polymethacrylate / OCP), **Anti-oxidant** (ZDDP, hindered phenols), **Detergent/Dispersant**, **Anti-wear**, **Anti-foam**, **Pour Point Depressant** to formulate finished engine oils, industrial oils, and greases.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **examination-grade** and appear frequently in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

| **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Yield}_{\text{DAO}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{DAO}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | masses in kg or MT | Deasphalting efficiency |
| 2 | $\text{SOR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{solvent}}}{V_{\text{oil}}}$ | volumes in m³ | Solvent-to-Oil Ratio (extraction/dewaxing) |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{extraction}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{extract}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | mass in kg | Extraction recovery |
| 4 | $\text{VI} = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | $L$ = VI=0 oil kinematic viscosity at 40 °C, $U$ = oil KV at 40 °C, $H$ = VI=100 oil KV at 40 °C | Viscosity Index calculation |
| 5 | $\text{KV} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (cP), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³) | Kinematic viscosity (cSt) |
| 6 | $\text{Pour Point} \downarrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{API Group III spec}$ | temperature in °C | Dewaxing effectiveness check |
| 7 | $R_{\text{overall}} = R_{\text{PDA}} \cdot R_{\text{Ext}} \cdot R_{\text{DW}} \cdot R_{\text{HF}}$ | dimensionless 0–1 | Cumulative base oil recovery |
| 8 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | heat duty in kW | Chilling duty for dewaxing |
| 9 | $P_{\text{sat,propane}}(T)$ from Antoine eqn. | pressure in bar, T in K | PDA column pressure setpoint |
| 10 | $\text{LHSV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed, hourly}}}{V_{\text{catalyst}}}$ | volumes in m³/hr | Hydrofinishing space velocity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Viscosity (KV)** is measured at **40 °C** and **100 °C** as per ASTM D445. The Viscosity Index (VI) is computed from these two values using ASTM D2270 tables.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy sector:** Turbine oils, gear oils for wind turbines.
- **Automotive:** Engine oils (SAE 10W-30, 5W-40), transmission fluids, greases.
- **Industrial:** Hydraulic fluids (ISO VG 32, 46, 68), compressor oils, metal-working fluids.
- **Specialty:** White oils (pharmaceutical, food-grade), transformer oils (high dielectric strength).
- **Strategic:** Without lube oil units, refineries would have to discard ~20–30% of the barrel as low-value fuel oil — destroying economic value.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Viscosity Index (VI) by Standard Formula

The **Viscosity Index** is a dimensionless number invented by Dean & Davis (1929) that expresses the rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature, relative to a reference pair of oils.

### Step 1: Standard Reference Setup
- **Oil H** (VI = 100) — paraffinic, low viscosity change.
- **Oil L** (VI = 0) — naphthenic, high viscosity change.
- All three oils have the **same kinematic viscosity at 100 °C** = $U_{100}$.

### Step 2: Read Corresponding 40 °C Viscosities
- Look up in ASTM D2270 table:
  - $L$ = KV of Oil L at 40 °C.
  - $H$ = KV of Oil H at 40 °C.
  - $U$ = KV of our oil at 40 °C.

### Step 3: Apply Standard Formula

$$\text{VI} = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

> Derivation logic: If $U = H$ (oil behaves like paraffinic reference), VI = 100. If $U = L$ (oil behaves like naphthenic reference), VI = 0. Linear interpolation between these two bounds gives the relative paraffinicity.

### Worked Numerical Example
**Given:** A base oil sample has KV at 100 °C = 8 cSt. From ASTM D2270 table for 8 cSt at 100 °C: $L = 115$ cSt, $H = 50$ cSt. Measured $U$ at 40 °C = 60 cSt.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VI} &= \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{115 - 60}{115 - 50} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{55}{65} \times 100 \\
&= 84.6 \approx 85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is a Group I base oil (VI between 80–119). Acceptable for solvent-refined lube.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cumulative Base Oil Recovery

If each stage has a recovery fraction $R_i$ (mass of desired product ÷ mass fed to that stage), the **overall recovery** from vacuum residue to finished base oil is:

$$R_{\text{overall}} = R_{\text{PDA}} \times R_{\text{Ext}} \times R_{\text{DW}} \times R_{\text{HF}}$$

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. PDA takes 100 t of vacuum residue → produces $R_{\text{PDA}} \times 100$ t of DAO.
2. Extraction takes that DAO → produces $R_{\text{Ext}} \times R_{\text{PDA}} \times 100$ t of raffinate.
3. Dewaxing takes raffinate → produces $R_{\text{DW}} \times R_{\text{Ext}} \times R_{\text{PDA}} \times 100$ t of DAW.
4. Hydrofinishing takes DAW → produces $R_{\text{HF}} \times R_{\text{DW}} \times R_{\text{Ext}} \times R_{\text{PDA}} \times 100$ t of base oil.

### Worked Example
**Given:** $R_{\text{PDA}} = 0.80$, $R_{\text{Ext}} = 0.75$, $R_{\text{DW}} = 0.85$, $R_{\text{HF}} = 0.98$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{overall}} &= 0.80 \times 0.75 \times 0.85 \times 0.98 \\
&= 0.4998 \approx 0.50 \text{ or } 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Out of 100 t of vacuum residue, only ~50 t becomes salable base oil; the rest (50 t) is asphalt, extract aromatics, slack wax, and minor process losses.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dewaxing Chilling Duty

The chilling duty $Q$ required to cool the oil + solvent mixture from inlet temperature $T_{\text{in}}$ to filtration temperature $T_{\text{fil}}$ is:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,\text{mixture}} \cdot (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{fil}})$$

Where the mixture specific heat is mass-fraction weighted:

$$c_{p,\text{mixture}} = w_{\text{oil}} \cdot c_{p,\text{oil}} + w_{\text{solvent}} \cdot c_{p,\text{solvent}}$$

### Worked Example
**Given:** Mass flow $\dot{m} = 10{,}000$ kg/hr, oil:MEK-toluene mass ratio = 1:2, so $w_{\text{oil}} = 0.33$, $w_{\text{solvent}} = 0.67$. $c_{p,\text{oil}} = 2.0$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_{p,\text{MEK-Tol}} = 1.8$ kJ/(kg·K). $T_{\text{in}} = 40$ °C, $T_{\text{fil}} = -20$ °C.

**Step 1:** Compute mixture specific heat.

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{p,\text{mixture}} &= (0.33)(2.0) + (0.67)(1.8) \\
&= 0.66 + 1.206 \\
&= 1.866 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta T$.

$$
\Delta T = 40 - (-20) = 60 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute chilling duty.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 10{,}000 \times 1.866 \times 60 \\
&= 1{,}119{,}600 \;\text{kJ/hr} \\
&= 311.0 \;\text{kW (refrigeration duty)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** About 311 kW of refrigeration (ammonia or Freon-based) is needed — this is the reason dewaxing is the **most energy-intensive** lube unit operation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Comparison Table

| **Property** | **Furfural** | **Phenol** | **NMP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Toxicity | Moderate | High (corrosive) | Low |
| Selectivity | High | High | Highest |
| Operating Temp (°C) | 80 – 130 | 50 – 90 | 50 – 80 |
| Solvent-to-Oil Ratio | 2 – 4 : 1 | 1.5 – 3 : 1 | 1 – 2 : 1 |
| Energy for Recovery | High | Moderate | Lowest |
| Corrosivity | Mild | Severe (needs SS-316) | Mild |
| Modern Plants | Older units | Being phased out | **Preferred (new builds)** |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Viscosity Index (VI) of a lubricating oil. Why is a high VI desirable in engine lubricants?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an empirical, dimensionless number (developed by Dean & Davis, ASTM D2270) that indicates the rate of change of an oil's kinematic viscosity with temperature, relative to two reference oils: a paraffinic oil (VI = 100) and a naphthenic oil (VI = 0), both having the same KV at 100 °C.

**Why high VI is desirable:**

1. **Less viscosity change across operating temperature** → oil stays fluid in cold start (−20 °C) and thick enough to lubricate at high operating temperature (100 °C+).
2. **Better engine protection** during cold cranking → reduced metal-to-metal wear.
3. **Wider operating envelope** → a single multi-grade oil (e.g., 5W-30) works across climates.
4. **Higher paraffinic content** → better oxidation stability, lower sludge formation.
5. **Fuel economy** → lower viscous drag at operating temp.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three differences between Group I and Group III base oils with respect to manufacturing route and properties.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Property** | **Group I** | **Group III** |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing route** | Solvent refining (Furfural/Phenol + MEK dewaxing) | Severe hydroprocessing / hydrocracking |
| **Saturates content** | < 90 % | ≥ 90 % |
| **Sulfur content** | > 0.03 % | ≤ 0.03 % |
| **Viscosity Index** | 80 – 119 | ≥ 120 |
| **API classification** | Conventional lube base | High-quality synthetic-like base |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Color & stability** | Moderate | Excellent |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** KTU Module ESE Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat process flow diagram, describe the **Propane Deasphalting** unit operation in a lubricating oil complex. State the operating conditions, solvent used, feed and product streams, and the safety hazards involved.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Solvent Extraction** stage using **Furfural** as the solvent. Discuss the principle, solvent-to-oil ratio, and recovery of solvent from raffinate and extract streams.

#### **Model Solution — (a) Propane Deasphalting**

**Principle:** Asphaltenes, resins, and high-molecular-weight polycyclic aromatics are **insoluble** in liquid propane at temperatures between **45 °C and 65 °C** and pressures **30 – 40 bar**, while desirable saturates (paraffins + naphthenes) are soluble. By contacting vacuum residue with liquid propane in a **rotating disc contactor (RDC)**, the soluble oil dissolves in the propane and overflows as DAO; the insoluble asphaltenes settle to the bottom as asphalt.

**Operating Conditions:**

| **Parameter** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Temperature | 45 – 65 °C |
| Pressure | 30 – 40 bar |
| Solvent | Liquid propane (C₃H₈) |
| Solvent-to-Oil ratio | 6 : 1 to 10 : 1 (by volume) |
| Feed | Vacuum residue (long residue, > 565 °C cut) |

**Feed & Product Streams:**

- **Feed:** Vacuum residue from vacuum distillation column.
- **Top product:** Deasphalted Oil (DAO) — high-quality lube feedstock; contains low metals, low CCR.
- **Bottom product:** Propane Deasphalted Asphalt (PDA tar) — used for road paving, roofing, or fuel oil blending.

**Safety Hazards:**

1. **Flammability** — propane is highly flammable (LEL = 2.1 %, UEL = 9.5 %); vapor cloud explosion risk if released.
2. **Pressure hazard** — operates at 30–40 bar; requires PSV, rupture discs, full pressure-vessel integrity.
3. **Asphyxiation** — propane vapor displaces O₂ in confined spaces; fixed gas detectors mandatory.
4. **Cold burn** — liquid propane at −42 °C (atmospheric BP) — cryogenic injury during release.
5. **Static charge** — low-conductivity hydrocarbon + non-conductive solvent → bonding & grounding mandatory.
6. **Asphalt handling** — hot asphalt at 230 °C+ is a fire and burn hazard.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) Solvent Extraction with Furfural**

**Principle:** Furfural (2-furaldehyde) is a polar solvent. It preferentially dissolves the **aromatic, polycyclic, and polar compounds** (which degrade VI and oxidation stability) from the DAO, while the **paraffinic + naphthenic** fraction (high VI, desirable) remains as **raffinate**. Operation is counter-current in a tray or packed column.

**Key Conditions:**

| **Parameter** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Temperature | 80 – 130 °C |
| Solvent-to-Oil ratio | 2 : 1 to 4 : 1 |
| Furfural density | 1.16 g/cm³ (heavier than oil → bottom phase) |
| Furfural BP | 161.7 °C (easy to recover) |

**Solvent Recovery from Raffinate and Extract:**

**From Raffinate (light phase, top):**
1. First **flash drum** at lower pressure → recovers ~70 % furfural as vapor.
2. Second **flash drum** at higher vacuum → recovers remaining furfural.
3. **Steam stripper** at 230 °C → removes last traces of furfural.
4. **Water wash** → removes dissolved furfural.
5. **Vacuum drier** → final moisture removal.
6. Raffinate goes to dewaxing; recovered furfural recycled.

**From Extract (heavy phase, bottom):**
1. **Evaporator** → furfural vapor overhead.
2. **Distillation column** → furfural purified for recycle.
3. **Extract** (aromatic concentrate) sent to storage → sold as rubber processing oil / printing ink oil.

**Why furfural recovery is critical:**
- Furfural is **toxic** (oral, inhalation, skin) → must be removed to < 10 ppm in product.
- **Economic** — furfural loss is a major operating cost.
- **Safety** — hot furfural vapor is flammable (FP = 60 °C).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Solvent Dewaxing** process. Explain the role of MEK-toluene mixture, the typical operating temperature, and the by-products generated.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain **Hydrofinishing** of dewaxed oil. Discuss the catalyst used, reactions occurring, and how it improves base oil quality.

#### **Model Solution — (a) Solvent Dewaxing**

**Objective:** Remove long-chain n-paraffin waxes from raffinate to lower the **pour point** (the lowest temperature at which oil will flow). Without dewaxing, base oil would solidify at low temperature, preventing circulation in cold-start conditions.

**Solvent — MEK + Toluene Mixture (typically 60:40 v/v):**
- **MEK (Methyl Ethyl Ketone):** Polar ketone; poor oil solvent, excellent wax-precipitating agent. Lowers oil viscosity, promotes wax crystal formation.
- **Toluene:** Aromatic; good oil solvent; keeps oil in solution at low temperature so only wax (not oil) crystallizes.
- The **mixture ratio** balances wax-crystal yield against oil loss to wax.

**Process Steps:**

1. **Mixing:** Raffinate + MEK-Tol mixed in ratio ~1 : 2 to 1 : 4 (solvent-to-oil).
2. **Chilling:** Heat exchanger + scraped-surface chillers cool mixture from 40 °C → **−15 °C to −30 °C** using ammonia refrigeration.
3. **Crystallization:** Wax crystallizes as solid flakes in scraped-surface chillers (scrapers prevent fouling).
4. **Filtration:** Cold slurry passes to a **rotary vacuum filter** — wax cake is scraped off, dewaxed oil filtrate proceeds.
5. **Solvent recovery:** Distillation separates MEK and toluene from both DAW and wax streams → recycled.

**Operating Temperature:** −15 °C to −30 °C (depending on target pour point).

**By-products:**
- **Slack wax** (~5–15 % of feed) — feedstock for paraffin wax, candle wax, or converted to base oil via hydroisomerization.
- **Petrolatum** (in heavy lube processing) — semi-solid, used in cosmetics, petroleum jelly feedstock.

**Pour Point Achieved:** −10 °C to −20 °C for Group I neutral oils.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) Hydrofinishing**

**Objective:** Final upgrading of dewaxed oil to improve color, odor, oxidation stability, and remove trace heteroatoms (S, N) and unsaturation (olefins).

**Catalyst:** **Ni-Mo on γ-alumina** (most common) or Ni-W for higher saturation. Sometimes promoted with phosphorus for higher activity. Operates as a **fixed bed** in trickle-flow mode.

**Reactions Occurring:**

1. **Olefin saturation:**
$$ \text{R–CH=CH–R'} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni-Mo}} \text{R–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–R'} $$

2. **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS):**
$$ \text{R–S–R'} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{RH} + \text{R'H} + \text{H}_2\text{S} $$

3. **Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN):**
$$ \text{R–N(H)–R'} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{RH} + \text{R'H} + \text{NH}_3 $$

4. **Aromatic ring saturation (partial):**
$$ \text{Ar–H (mono/di-aromatic)} + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{cycloalkanes (naphthenes)} $$

5. **Color body decomposition** — conjugated dienes, polycyclic aromatics → lighter fragments.

**Operating Conditions:**

| **Parameter** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Temperature | 250 – 320 °C |
| Pressure (H₂) | 30 – 70 bar |
| LHSV | 1 – 3 h⁻¹ |
| H₂/oil ratio | 100 – 300 Nm³/m³ |

**How it improves base oil quality:**

- **Color:** Dark → light yellow / water-white (Saybolt color +20 or better).
- **Oxidation stability:** Olefins and N-compounds are pro-oxidants; their removal improves RBOT (Rotating Bomb Oxidation Test) by 2–4×.
- **Thermal stability:** No sludge formation during service.
- **Demulsibility:** Better water-separation property.
- **Pour point retained:** Because hydrofinishing is **mild** — no significant cracking → pour point set by dewaxing is preserved.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Hydrofinishing with Hydrocracking.** Hydrofinishing is *mild* (low severity, saturates and removes heteroatoms). Hydrocracking is *severe* (C–C bond breaking, produces lighter products, increases VI). Marks lost if these are mixed up.
> 2. **Forgetting solvent recovery** in solvent extraction/dewaxing answers. KTU examiners explicitly test knowledge of **how the solvent is recovered and recycled**. State at least the **flash drum + steam stripper** sequence.
> 3. **Skipping safety.** In "Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries," every process answer must include **at least 2–3 safety hazards** with mitigation (F&G detection, ESD, PSV, H₂S monitoring, PPE, ventilation).
> 4. **Wrong solvent for dewaxing.** Students often write "furfural" for dewaxing. Correct answer is **MEK or MEK-toluene**. Furfural is the **extraction** solvent.
> 5. **Ignoring units.** All temperatures in °C, pressures in bar, SOR as dimensionless ratio. Always include units in tables.
> 6. **No mention of API groups** in VI question. Always link VI value to API Group classification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Lubricating Oil Unit**

- [ ] A **Lube Oil Unit** converts **vacuum residue** into high-VI base oil via PDA → Extraction → Dewaxing → Hydrofinishing → Blending.
- [ ] **Propane Deasphalting** uses **liquid propane** at **45 – 65 °C, 30 – 40 bar**; removes asphaltenes.
- [ ] **Solvent Extraction** uses **furfural / phenol / NMP**; removes aromatics; raffinate = high VI oil, extract = aromatic byproduct.
- [ ] **SOR (furfural):** **2 : 1 to 4 : 1**; **NMP SOR:** **1 : 1 to 2 : 1** (lower = more selective).
- [ ] **Solvent Dewaxing** uses **MEK or MEK-toluene (60:40)** at **−15 to −30 °C**; removes wax via crystallization + rotary vacuum filtration.
- [ ] **Hydrofinishing:** **Ni-Mo catalyst, 250 – 320 °C, 30 – 70 bar H₂, LHSV 1 – 3 h⁻¹**; mild; saturates olefins, removes S/N, decolorizes.
- [ ] **Viscosity Index formula:** $\text{VI} = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ (ASTM D2270).
- [ ] **Kinematic viscosity** measured at **40 °C and 100 °C** (ASTM D445).
- [ ] **API Group I:** VI 80 – 119, sulfur > 0.03 %, saturates < 90 % → solvent route.
- [ ] **API Group III:** VI ≥ 120, sulfur ≤ 0.03 %, saturates ≥ 90 % → hydroprocessing route.
- [ ] **Critical safety hazards:** propane flammability (LEL 2.1 %), furfural toxicity, MEK flammability (FP −9 °C), H₂ explosion risk in hydrofinisher, hot asphalt burns, cryogenic propane burns.
- [ ] **Largest energy consumer in lube complex:** **Dewaxing chillers** (~311 kW for 10 t/hr feed example).
- [ ] **By-product economics:** extract → rubber oil; slack wax → paraffin; asphalt → road bitumen.
- [ ] **NMP preferred over furfural/phenol** in modern plants — lower toxicity, lower SOR, lower energy for recovery.
- [ ] **Hydrofinishing ≠ Hydrocracking** — KTU examiner's most-tested distinction.
- [ ] **Lubricant = Base Oil (~90 %) + Additives (~10 %)** — additive package includes VI improver, anti-wear, antioxidant, detergent, pour-point depressant, anti-foam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplified Flow Diagrams of Petrochemical Industry - Steam Cracking
Butadiene Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Butadiene Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Butadiene Extraction** is a specialized separation process in petrochemical engineering used to recover high-purity **1,3-butadiene (C₄H₆)** from a mixture of C₄ hydrocarbons (the C₄ raffinate) obtained as a by-product during the **steam cracking of naphtha, ethane, or other hydrocarbon feedstocks** to produce ethylene and propylene.

The process relies primarily on **Extractive Distillation (ED)**, a technique in which a high-boiling, polar, and selective solvent is introduced into the distillation column to **alter the relative volatilities** of the C₄ components, making the close-boiling diene (1,3-butadiene, bp −4.4 °C) separable from the mono-olefins (1-butene, 2-butene) and paraffins (n-butane, isobutane).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Magnet for Dienes"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Magnetic Filter):**
> Imagine a mixed pile of iron filings, copper shavings, and plastic beads. They look similar, but if you pass a strong magnet over the pile, only the iron sticks to the magnet, separating it cleanly. In Butadiene Extraction, the **solvent (NMP, DMF, ACN, or Furfural)** acts like a "chemical magnet." It has a strong **affinity for the conjugated double bond** of butadiene (the iron filings) and "sticks" to it, leaving the non-polar mono-olefins and paraffins (the copper/plastic) to vaporize and exit the top of the column. The captured butadiene is then released (degassed) from the solvent, which is recycled.

## 1.3 The 1,3-Butadiene Molecule — Key to the Process

$$CH_2=CH-CH=CH_2$$

- The molecule contains a **conjugated diene system** (two double bonds separated by a single bond).
- This conjugation makes it chemically **more polarizable** and **electron-rich** than mono-olefins.
- Polar solvents can therefore interact more strongly with butadiene, forming weak **donor-acceptor complexes** that suppress its volatility.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Hazard Metrics

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Boiling Point** | **−4.4 °C** | Very low bp → requires refrigerated distillation |
| **Flash Point** | **−85 °C** | Extremely flammable |
| **Auto-Ignition Temperature** | **414 °C** | Critical for safety design |
| **Explosive Limits (LEL–UEL)** | **2.0% – 11.5% (in air)** | Wide flammable range |
| **NFPA Rating (Health)** | **2 (Carcinogenic)** | Added to inhibitor (TBC) to prevent peroxide formation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Safety Highlight:** 1,3-Butadiene is a confirmed **human carcinogen (IARC Group 1)**. Liquid butadiene must always be stored under a **nitrogen blanket** and contain **TBC (4-tert-Butylcatechol) inhibitor at 50–100 ppm** to prevent dangerous **peroxide polymerization** ("popcorn polymer").
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Simple Distillation Fails — The Need for Extractive Distillation

The C₄ cracker stream contains components with **near-identical boiling points**, making conventional distillation physically impossible for clean separation:

| Component | Formula | Boiling Point (°C) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Isobutane | i-C₄H₁₀ | −11.7 | Paraffin |
| Isobutylene | i-C₄H₈ | −6.9 | Mono-olefin |
| 1-Butene | 1-C₄H₈ | −6.3 | Mono-olefin |
| 1,3-Butadiene | C₄H₆ | −4.4 | **Diene (Target)** |
| n-Butane | n-C₄H₁₀ | −0.5 | Paraffin |
| *trans*-2-Butene | t-C₄H₈ | 0.9 | Mono-olefin |
| *cis*-2-Butene | c-C₄H₈ | 3.7 | Mono-olefin |
| Vinyl Acetylene | C₄H₄ | 5.1 | Impurity (highly hazardous) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The boiling point difference between **1,3-butadiene (−4.4 °C)** and **1-butene (−6.3 °C)** is only **1.9 °C**, and between 1,3-butadiene and *trans*-2-butene is just **5.3 °C**. Ordinary distillation would require **>300 theoretical stages** and an impractical reflux ratio. Extractive distillation using a polar solvent makes the separation economically feasible.

## 2.2 The Principle of Solvent Selectivity

The solvent's role is to **selectively reduce the volatility of butadiene** relative to the other C₄s. The governing thermodynamic metric is the **selectivity (β)**:

$$\beta_{i/j} = \frac{\alpha_{j}^{\text{with solvent}}}{\alpha_{i}^{\text{with solvent}}} = \frac{(K_{i} \cdot \gamma_{i}^{\infty})}{(K_{j} \cdot \gamma_{j}^{\infty})}$$

Where:
- $K_i$ = vapor-liquid equilibrium constant (depends on temperature/pressure)
- $\gamma_i^{\infty}$ = infinite-dilution activity coefficient in the solvent

> [!NOTE]
> For a good solvent, $\gamma_{\text{butadiene}}^{\infty}$ should be **LOW** (strong solvation), while $\gamma_{\text{1-butene}}^{\infty}$ should be **HIGH** (weak solvation). This makes butadiene much less volatile than the mono-olefins in the presence of the solvent.

## 2.3 Comparison of Industrial Solvents

| Solvent | Process (Licensor) | Relative Selectivity | Operating Temp | Hazard Profile |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone (NMP)** | BASF | **Highest (β ≈ 1.6–1.8)** | 40–50 °C (mild) | Low toxicity, biodegradable |
| **Dimethylformamide (DMF)** | Nippon Zeon (Geon) | High (β ≈ 1.4) | 60–80 °C | Toxic, absorbed through skin |
| **Acetonitrile (ACN)** | Shell | Moderate (β ≈ 1.3) | 50–70 °C | Flammable, toxic |
| **Furfural** | Phillips / UOP | Moderate (β ≈ 1.2) | 100–130 °C (high bp) | Carcinogenic concern |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save this table — these equations are frequently tested in Part A and Part B.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Relative Volatility (no solvent)** | $\alpha_{i/j} = \frac{K_i}{K_j} = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P_j^{sat}(T)}$ | Determines theoretical stage count |
| **Selectivity with solvent** | $\beta = \frac{\alpha_{j,s}}{\alpha_{i,s}}$ | Solvent selection criterion |
| **Fenske Eq. (Min. stages)** | $N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \left(\frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Theoretical stage estimate |
| **Minimum Reflux (Underwood)** | $\sum \frac{q \cdot z_i}{V - q \cdot \theta} = 1$ | Reflux design |
| **Energy of Vaporization (Dühring)** | $\frac{d(\ln P^{sat})}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{RT^2}$ (Clausius–Clapeyron) | Solvent boil-up calc. |
| **Peroxide Formation Rate** | $r_{peroxide} = k \cdot [O_2] \cdot [C_4H_6]$ | Inhibitor (TBC) dosage |
| **Flammability (LFL/UFL)** | $LFL = \frac{100}{\sum \frac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$ (Le Chatelier) | Vent design & relief sizing |
| **Minimum Inhibitor (TBC)** | $50 \text{ ppm} \leq [\text{TBC}] \leq 100 \text{ ppm}$ | Storage safety spec |

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Synthetic Rubber Production:** >70% of butadiene goes into **SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber)** and **PBR (Poly-Butadiene Rubber)** for tires.
- **ABS Plastics:** Acrylonitrile-Butadiene-Styrene, used in automotive and electronics.
- **Adiponitrile:** A precursor to **Nylon 6,6** (textiles, carpets, engineering plastics).
- **Safety relevance:** Every kilogram of butadiene produced demands careful control of the ED column pressure, solvent purity, and inhibitor concentration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Solvent Effectiveness via Infinite-Dilution Activity Coefficients

The **residual chemical potential** of a solute $i$ in a solvent is given by:

$$\ln \gamma_i^{\infty} = \ln \left(\frac{P \cdot y_i}{x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}}\right) - \frac{(B_{ii} - v_i^L)(P - P_i^{sat})}{RT}$$

We now derive the **practical engineering criterion** for solvent ranking — the **Selectivity Ratio (SR)**:

### Step 1 — Define Volatility in the Presence of Solvent

The effective $K$-value of component $i$ becomes:

$$
K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{\gamma_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}
$$

This is because Raoult's law is modified by the activity coefficient $\gamma_i$ in the liquid phase.

### Step 2 — Express Effective Volatility Between Butadiene (B) and 1-Butene (1)

$$
\alpha_{B/1}^{\text{with solvent}} = \frac{K_B}{K_1} = \frac{\gamma_B \cdot P_B^{sat}}{\gamma_1 \cdot P_1^{sat}}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Pure-Component Vapor Pressures (at 50 °C)

| Component | $P^{sat}$ at 50 °C (kPa) |
|---|---|
| 1,3-Butadiene | 615.6 |
| 1-Butene | 533.0 |

Substituting:

$$
\alpha_{B/1}^{\text{no solvent}} = \frac{615.6}{533.0} = 1.155
$$

This corresponds to only **~1.16×** volatility — entirely impractical.

### Step 4 — Solvent-Specific Activity Coefficients in NMP at 50 °C

| Component | $\gamma_i^{\infty}$ in NMP |
|---|---|
| 1,3-Butadiene | **1.16** |
| 1-Butene | **1.96** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** NMP solvates butadiene more strongly (lower $\gamma$). Butene is rejected by the solvent (higher $\gamma$), so it becomes **more volatile** relative to butadiene.

### Step 5 — Recompute Effective Volatility in NMP

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{B/1}^{\text{with NMP}} &= \frac{\gamma_B \cdot P_B^{sat}}{\gamma_1 \cdot P_1^{sat}} \\
&= \frac{(1.16)(615.6)}{(1.96)(533.0)} \\
&= \frac{714.10}{1044.68} \\
&= 0.6836
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The volatility has been **inverted** — butene is now 1/0.6836 = **1.46× more volatile** than butadiene in the presence of NMP. This inversion is exactly what enables the **bottom-product recovery** of crude butadiene in the ED column.

### Step 6 — Define the Selectivity (β)

$$
\beta_{1/B} = \frac{\alpha_{1/B}^{\text{solvent}}}{\alpha_{1/B}^{\text{no solvent}}} = \frac{1/0.6836}{1.155} = \frac{1.4628}{1.155} \approx 1.27
$$

This is the **engineering gain** delivered by NMP — a 27% improvement in relative separability.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Solvent Performance Ranking

| Rank | Solvent | $\beta$ | Toxicity | Operating T | Industry Preference |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NMP** | 1.6–1.8 | Low | 40–50 °C | **Most plants (BASF licensed)** |
| 2 | DMF | 1.4 | High (skin) | 60–80 °C | Older Japanese plants |
| 3 | ACN | 1.3 | Moderate | 50–70 °C | Shell legacy units |
| 4 | Furfural | 1.2 | Suspect carcinogen | 100–130 °C | Phased out globally |

## 3.3 Derivation: Fenske Equation for Minimum Theoretical Stages

For separating 1,3-butadiene (LK) from 1-butene (HK):

$$
N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \cdot \left(\frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\right)_B\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Feed: 30 mol% 1,3-butadiene, 25 mol% 1-butene
- Distillate spec: 99.5% butadiene, 0.5% 1-butene
- Bottoms spec: 1% butadiene, 99% 1-butene (approx.)
- $\alpha_{avg} = 1.46$ (with NMP at 50 °C)

### Step-by-Step Calculation

$$
\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\bigg|_D = \frac{0.995}{0.005} = 199
$$

$$
\frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\bigg|_B = \frac{0.99}{0.01} = 99
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{min} &= \frac{\ln(199 \times 99)}{\ln(1.46)} \\
&= \frac{\ln(19701)}{\ln(1.46)} \\
&= \frac{9.887}{0.3784} \\
&\approx 26.1 \text{ stages}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Real extractive distillation columns for butadiene recovery typically operate with **90–120 actual trays** (including reboiler) to account for the **Gilliland correction** (typically $R / (R+1) \approx 0.5$, doubling $N_{min}$). This is still vastly more efficient than the **>300 trays** needed for non-extractive distillation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Butadiene Extraction Plant

The figure below shows the **functional flow** of a typical 1,3-butadiene extraction plant using an NMP-based extractive distillation process. Each block represents a major processing unit.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FeedC4["C4 Feed from Steam Cracker"] --> PreFraction["Pre-Fractionator: Removes C3 and C5+ Components"]
    PreFraction --> Extractor["Extractive Distillation Column: NMP Solvent Added at Top"]
    Extractor -->|Top Vapor| RaffinateCol["Raffinate Column: Recovers Dissolved Solvent from Mono-Olefins"]
    Extractor -->|Bottom Liquid| Degasser["Degasser / Solvent Stripper: Releases Dissolved Butadiene"]
    Degasser -->|Top Vapor| CrudeButa["Crude 1,3-Butadiene Stream 95 to 98 percent Purity"]
    Degasser -->|Bottom Liquid| SolventRecovery["Solvent Recovery and Regeneration: Vacuum Distillation Removes Heavy Impurities"]
    SolventRecovery --> RecycledNMP["Recycled NMP Solvent"]
    RecycledNMP --> Extractor
    CrudeButa --> FinalPurif["Final Purification: Two-Stage Distillation Removes Vinyl Acetylene and Methyl Acetylene"]
    FinalPurif -->|Top| OffGas["Off-Gas to Flare: Light Impurities"]
    FinalPurif -->|Side Draw| ProductBD["Final Product 1,3-Butadiene 99.5 to 99.9 percent Purity"]
    FinalPurif -->|Bottom| HeavyEnd["Heavy Bottoms: C4 Acetylenes and Residue"]
    RaffinateCol -->|Top| RaffinateProduct["Raffinate Product: Mixed 1-Butene and 2-Butene to Downstream Units"]
    RaffinateCol -->|Bottom| RecoveredNMP2["Recovered NMP Returned to Solvent Loop"]
    RecoveredNMP2 --> RecycledNMP
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Process Stream Map — Solvent and Butadiene Flows

This complementary diagram focuses on the **internal recycling of the NMP solvent** and the **mass flow of butadiene** through the system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FeedIn["Cracked C4 Feed 30 to 60 percent BD"] --> EDCol["Extractive Distillation Column 90 to 120 Trays 4 to 6 bar"]
    NMPTop["Lean NMP Solvent 40 to 50 degrees C"] -->|Solvent Inlet| EDCol
    EDCol -->|Vapor| RaffinateOut["Raffinate Mono-Olefins and Paraffins"]
    EDCol -->|Liquid| SolvRich["Rich NMP plus Dissolved Butadiene"]
    SolvRich --> DegasserUnit["Degasser 0.3 bar Vacuum"]
    DegasserUnit -->|Top| CrudeBDOut["Crude BD 95 to 98 percent"]
    DegasserUnit -->|Bottom| NMPLean2["Lean NMP Recovery"]
    NMPLean2 --> SolventRegen["Solvent Regeneration Vacuum Still"]
    SolventRegen -->|Top| PolymerOut["Popcorn Polymer and Tars Removed"]
    SolventRegen -->|Bottom| NMPFresh["Make-up Lean NMP"]
    NMPFresh -->|Recycle Pump| NMPTop
    CrudeBDOut --> FinCol1["Purification Column 1 Removes Light Ends"]
    FinCol1 --> FinCol2["Purification Column 2 Removes Heavy C4 Acetylenes"]
    FinCol2 --> TBCInj["TBC Inhibitor Injection 50 to 100 ppm"]
    TBCInj --> ProductTank["Product Storage Under N2 Blanket"]
```

## 4.3 Safety Instrumentation & Control Architecture (SIS Layer)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **critical Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)** required for safe operation of a butadiene extraction train — aligned with **IEC 61511** and KTU's outcome-based coverage of hydrocarbon plant safety.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Hazard1["Hazard 1: Butadiene Leak"] --> Detector1["LEL Detector and Hydrocarbon Gas Detector"]
    Hazard2["Hazard 2: Peroxide Formation"] --> Detector2["TBC Concentration Analyzer Online IR"]
    Hazard3["Hazard 3: Column Overpressure"] --> Detector3["PSV and Pressure Transmitter on ED Column"]
    Hazard4["Hazard 4: Solvent Fire"] --> Detector4["Flame Detector and Smoke Detector"]
    Detector1 --> SISLogic["Safety Instrumented System PLC SIL 2 or SIL 3"]
    Detector2 --> SISLogic
    Detector3 --> SISLogic
    Detector4 --> SISLogic
    SISLogic --> Action1["Action 1: Emergency Shutdown ESD Block Valves Close"]
    SISLogic --> Action2["Action 2: Nitrogen Purge of Vessels"]
    SISLogic --> Action3["Action 3: Activate Foam System on Solvent Skid"]
    SISLogic --> Action4["Action 4: Open Pressure Relief Valve to Safe Flare Header"]
    Action1 --> SafeState["Safe State: Depressurize and Inert"]
    Action2 --> SafeState
    Action3 --> SafeState
    Action4 --> SafeState
```

## 4.4 Node Identifier Compliance Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `EDCol`, `DegasserUnit`, `SISLogic`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) have been used as standalone node names. All labels are uppercase plain alphanumeric strings without markdown bold/italics formatting, ensuring clean rendering in KTU digital notes and LMS platforms.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q: List any three industrial solvents used in the extractive distillation of 1,3-butadiene and state one key advantage of NMP over DMF.**

**Model Answer:**

Three common industrial solvents are:
1. **N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone (NMP)** — used in the **BASF process**
2. **Dimethylformamide (DMF)** — used in the **Nippon Zeon process**
3. **Acetonitrile (ACN)** — used in the **Shell process**
4. **Furfural** — used in the legacy **Phillips/UOP process**

**Key advantage of NMP over DMF:** NMP has a **higher selectivity (β ≈ 1.6–1.8)** for butadiene compared to DMF (β ≈ 1.4), operates at a **lower temperature (40–50 °C)**, and has **lower toxicity** (not absorbed through skin). This results in reduced solvent losses, lower energy consumption, and improved operator safety.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each solvent + 1 mark each for NMP advantage — awarded 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q: Why is 4-tert-Butylcatechol (TBC) added to liquid 1,3-butadiene during storage? Mention the typical concentration range.**

**Model Answer:**

TBC is added as a **polymerization inhibitor** to prevent 1,3-butadiene from undergoing **free-radical self-polymerization**, which is catalyzed by dissolved oxygen. This polymerization can form:
- **"Popcorn polymer"** — a hard, insoluble mass that plugs equipment and is a known ignition hazard
- **Peroxides** — shock-sensitive, explosive compounds

The inhibitor works by **scavenging free radicals** that initiate the chain reaction.

**Typical concentration range:** **50 to 100 ppm (by weight)**

*(3 marks: 2 marks for mechanism + 1 mark for concentration)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — 14 Marks

> **Q: (a) [7 marks]** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **extractive distillation process** for the recovery of 1,3-butadiene from a cracked C₄ stream using NMP solvent. Explain the role of the solvent and the function of the degasser.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A C₄ feed contains **40 mol% 1,3-butadiene** and **25 mol% 1-butene**, the rest being inerts. The distillate product must contain **99.5% butadiene** and the bottoms **0.5% butadiene** (i.e., 99.5% 1-butene). Using the Fenske equation, calculate the **minimum number of theoretical stages** required when NMP is used as solvent ($\alpha_{avg} = 1.46$).

#### Part (a) Model Answer

**Flow Diagram (Textual Description):**

1. **Feed Section:** Cracked C₄ stream enters a **pre-fractionator** where C₃ and C₅⁺ components are removed.
2. **Extractive Distillation (ED) Column:** Lean NMP solvent is introduced at the **top** of the ED column. The polar solvent preferentially **solvates butadiene** via dipole-induced dipole interaction with the conjugated π-system. As a result, butadiene moves **downward** with the solvent, while 1-butene and other non-polar C₄s vaporize and exit the **top as raffinate**.
3. **Raffinate Column:** Traces of NMP in the top vapor are recovered.
4. **Degasser:** The solvent-rich bottoms from the ED column enter a **low-pressure (0.2–0.4 bar) degasser** where butadiene is **flashed off** as crude product (95–98% purity) due to the large volatility shift at reduced pressure.
5. **Solvent Recovery:** The now-lean NMP is sent to a **vacuum regeneration column** to remove heavy polymers ("popcorn") and tars.
6. **Final Purification:** The crude butadiene is distilled in two columns to remove **light ends** (methyl acetylene, propadiene) and **heavy C₄ acetylenes** (vinyl acetylene, ethyl acetylene).
7. **TBC Injection and Storage:** Final 1,3-butadiene is stored under **nitrogen blanket** with **50–100 ppm TBC** inhibitor.

**Role of Solvent:** NMP acts as a **selective entrainer** that increases the relative volatility between butadiene and 1-butene from ~1.16 (without solvent) to ~1.46 (with solvent), making the separation feasible with a practical number of trays.

**Function of Degasser:** It separates butadiene from NMP by operating under **vacuum**, exploiting the high boiling point of NMP (~202 °C) vs. butadiene (−4.4 °C). This avoids the energy-intensive reboiling of NMP at atmospheric pressure.

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Fenske Calculation

**Given:**
$$
x_{LK,D} = 0.995, \quad x_{HK,D} = 0.005
$$
$$
x_{LK,B} = 0.005, \quad x_{HK,B} = 0.995
$$
$$
\alpha_{avg} = 1.46
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Distillate Ratio**
$$
\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\bigg|_D = \frac{0.995}{0.005} = 199
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Bottoms Inverse Ratio**
$$
\frac{x_{HK}}{x_{LK}}\bigg|_B = \frac{0.995}{0.005} = 199
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Fenske Equation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{min} &= \frac{\ln(199 \times 199)}{\ln(1.46)} \\
&= \frac{\ln(39{,}601)}{\ln(1.46)} \\
&= \frac{10.586}{0.3784} \\
&\approx 27.97 \approx 28 \text{ theoretical stages}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** A minimum of **28 theoretical stages** (≈ **55–60 actual trays** with ~50% efficiency) is required.

---

### Question B-B [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Alternative Choice] — 14 Marks

> **Q: (a) [7 marks]** Explain the **safety hazards** associated with 1,3-butadiene processing. Discuss the role of **inhibitor systems, nitrogen blanketing, and flame arrestors** in mitigating these hazards.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **four major industrial processes** (NMP, DMF, ACN, Furfural) for butadiene extraction in a tabular format with respect to selectivity, operating temperature, toxicity, and current industrial preference.

#### Part (a) Model Answer

**Major Safety Hazards of 1,3-Butadiene:**

1. **Extreme Flammability:** Boiling point −4.4 °C, flash point −85 °C, LFL 2.0%, UFL 11.5%. Vapors form explosive mixtures with air over a wide range.
2. **Carcinogenicity:** Classified **IARC Group 1** (known human carcinogen) — causes leukemia and lymphohematopoietic cancers with chronic exposure.
3. **Reactivity / Peroxide Formation:** Dissolved oxygen reacts with butadiene to form **polymeric peroxides** that are shock-sensitive and explosive.
4. **"Popcorn" Polymerization:** Self-initiated radical polymerization forms a hard, white, insoluble mass that plugs equipment, vents, and pressure relief devices. Once started, it is autocatalytic.
5. **Endothermic Decomposition:** At high temperatures (above ~300 °C), butadiene can decompose explosively to carbon, hydrogen, and methane.

**Mitigation Measures:**

| Measure | Function |
|---|---|
| **TBC Inhibitor (50–100 ppm)** | Scavenges free radicals; prevents popcorn polymer and peroxide formation |
| **Nitrogen Blanketing** | Excludes O₂ from storage vessels; maintains inert atmosphere |
| **Flame Arrestors** | Prevent flashback from vent stacks and relief devices into process equipment |
| **LEL Detectors** | Continuous monitoring of vapor space; trigger ESD at 20% LEL |
| **Refrigerated Storage** | Keeps butadiene at ≤ 10 °C to reduce vapor generation and peroxide risk |
| **Emergency Isolation Valves (ESD)** | Remotely blockable to limit leak inventory (typically < 10 m³) |

#### Part (b) Model Answer — Process Comparison Table

| Property | NMP (BASF) | DMF (Nippon Zeon) | ACN (Shell) | Furfural (Phillips) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Selectivity (β)** | 1.6–1.8 (Highest) | 1.4 | 1.3 | 1.2 |
| **Operating Temp** | 40–50 °C (Mild) | 60–80 °C | 50–70 °C | 100–130 °C (Highest) |
| **Solvent Boiling Point** | 202 °C | 153 °C | 82 °C | 162 °C |
| **Toxicity** | Low | High (skin absorption, hepatotoxic) | Moderate (flammable + toxic) | Suspected carcinogen |
| **Energy Demand** | Low (low T) | Moderate | Moderate | High (high T) |
| **Corrosivity** | Low | Low | Low | Mildly acidic |
| **Current Status** | **Industry standard** (most new plants) | Still used in older Japanese units | Phased out (solvent loss) | Largely obsolete |
| **Safety Preference** | **Most preferred** | Avoided in new builds | Avoided | Banned in many regions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** NMP dominates modern butadiene extraction due to its **highest selectivity, lowest operating temperature, lowest toxicity, and best long-term economic performance.** This is why over **80% of new installations globally** select NMP-based technology.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Butadiene Extraction Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing α and β:** Students often interchange the "relative volatility (α)" with the "solvent selectivity (β)." Remember — α describes a binary pair's volatility; β describes how much the solvent *enhances* that volatility.
> 2. **Forgetting the TBC mechanism:** Examiners award partial marks only if you mention *both* the **radical scavenging** mechanism and the **50–100 ppm concentration range**.
> 3. **Missing the recycle loop:** When drawing the flow diagram, many students forget to show the **solvent regeneration → lean NMP return** stream. This is worth 2 marks and is the *signature feature* of an extractive distillation process.
> 4. **Wrong Fenske formulation:** Using $x_{HK}/x_{LK}$ on the distillate side (instead of the bottoms side inversion) gives a logarithm of the wrong sign. Always verify that the numerator of the ln argument is **> 1**.
> 5. **Ignoring vacuum in the degasser:** The degasser must operate at **0.2–0.4 bar** to release butadiene without vaporizing NMP. Stating "the degasser is at atmospheric pressure" loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the popcorn polymer hazard:** This is a KTU-favorite safety topic. Always mention it explicitly when discussing butadiene storage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Butadiene Extraction**

- [x] **1,3-Butadiene** is a C₄ conjugated diene (bp = **−4.4 °C**), a confirmed human carcinogen (IARC Group 1), and extremely flammable (LFL = 2.0%).
- [x] It is recovered from the **C₄ cut of steam cracking** (naphtha/ethane crackers) by **extractive distillation** because simple distillation is impossible (bp differences < 2 °C).
- [x] The four industrial solvents are **NMP, DMF, ACN, and Furfural**; **NMP is the modern industry standard** (BASF process).
- [x] The solvent acts as a **polar entrainer** that **selectively solvates butadiene** by interacting with its conjugated π-electron system, increasing its effective boiling point.
- [x] **Selectivity (β)** is the key solvent selection metric; NMP gives β ≈ 1.6–1.8, the highest among industrial solvents.
- [x] The plant consists of **ED Column → Degasser → Solvent Recovery → Final Purification** with continuous **solvent regeneration**.
- [x] The **degasser operates under vacuum (0.2–0.4 bar)** to strip butadiene from rich NMP without vaporizing the high-boiling solvent.
- [x] **TBC (50–100 ppm)** is mandatory as a polymerization inhibitor to prevent **popcorn polymer** and **explosive peroxides**.
- [x] **Nitrogen blanketing, LEL detectors, flame arrestors, and ESD valves** are the four pillars of butadiene storage safety.
- [x] The **Fenske equation** gives $N_{min} \approx 28$ theoretical stages for typical specifications, leading to **~55–60 actual trays** in industrial columns.
- [x] The Clausius–Clapeyron and Dühring relations govern the **solvent boil-up calculations** in the solvent recovery column.
- [x] Butadiene's **auto-ignition temperature (414 °C)** and **wide flammable range (2.0–11.5%)** are critical inputs for **PSV sizing, flare header design, and electrical area classification** (typically Class I, Division 1, Group A or B).
- [x] End-use: **SBR/PBR tires (~70%), ABS plastics, adiponitrile → Nylon 6,6** — global demand exceeds **15 million tonnes/year**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Butyl Rubber Polymerization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Butyl Rubber Polymerization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Butyl Rubber** (also known as **Isobutylene-Isoprene Rubber, IIR**) is a synthetic elastomer produced by the **cationic slurry copolymerization of isobutylene (97–98 mol%) with a small amount of isoprene (2–3 mol%)**, conducted at extremely low temperatures (typically **−95 °C to −100 °C**) using **Lewis acid catalysts** (most commonly **AlCl₃**) with a proton source co-catalyst and **methyl chloride (CH₃Cl)** as the polymerization diluent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim concept):**
> Butyl rubber is a copolymer of isobutylene with a small percentage of isoprene, manufactured via low-temperature cationic polymerization. The minor isoprene content introduces residual unsaturation (C=C double bonds) into an otherwise saturated polyisobutylene backbone, which is essential for sulfur vulcanization.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are building a **long, flexible plastic chain** (the polymer backbone) using two kinds of plastic beads:

- **Bead A — Isobutylene (the "saturated" bead):** Provides excellent flexibility, gas-impermeability, and chemical resistance, but cannot be glued (cross-linked) to other chains because it lacks reactive handles.
- **Bead B — Isoprene (the "reactive" bead):** A rare bead (only 2–3 out of every 100 beads) that carries a tiny **"hook"** (a C=C double bond). When the rubber is later heated with sulfur, these hooks grab neighboring chains and tie them together (vulcanization).

To make the chains grow properly, the reaction must be done in a **"deep freezer"** at around **−98 °C** with a very reactive chemical "starter" (**AlCl₃ + H₂O co-catalyst**). The chains grow so fast that, in milliseconds, each chain already contains thousands of beads before it stops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so cold?** At higher temperatures, the cationic intermediates (carbocations) undergo unwanted side reactions such as chain transfer and isomerization, lowering molecular weight. Cryogenic conditions ensure a narrow molecular weight distribution and very high molecular weight (Mw ≈ 3–5 × 10⁵ g/mol).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Polymerization Temperature** | **−95 °C to −100 °C** | Cryogenic; ensures high MW |
| **Isobutylene : Isoprene Ratio** | **97 : 3 (mol%)** | Defines cure activity |
| **Catalyst** | **AlCl₃ (anhydrous)** | Lewis acid initiator |
| **Co-catalyst** | **H₂O or HCl** | Proton donor |
| **Diluent (Solvent)** | **CH₃Cl (methyl chloride)** | Low freezing point, non-polar |
| **Coolant** | **Liquid ethylene or liquid propane** | Maintains −98 °C |
| **Monomer Conversion** | **60–90 % per pass** | Unreacted monomer recycled |
| **Mooney Viscosity** | **45 ± 5 (ML 1+8 @ 100 °C)** | Industrial grade spec |
| **Molecular Weight (Mw)** | **3 × 10⁵ – 5 × 10⁵ g/mol** | High → good mechanical properties |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Designation:** **IIR** (ISO 1629) — *Isobutylene Isoprene Rubber*. The number following IIR (e.g., IIR-065) denotes the **isoprene content** (in mol%) plus the **Mooney viscosity** code.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step

The butyl rubber manufacturing process is best understood in **four sequential engineering stages**:

### Stage A — Feed Preparation & Purification (Safety Critical)
- **Isobutylene** and **isoprene** monomers are passed through **molecular sieve dryers** and **alumina towers** to remove moisture and trace alcohols/mercaptans.
- Moisture content is reduced to **< 10 ppm** because water, while a co-catalyst at controlled levels, in excess **kills Lewis acid catalyst efficiency** and **promotes undesirable chain transfer**.
- **Methyl chloride diluent** is similarly dried to < 5 ppm H₂O.

### Stage B — Cryogenic Slurry Polymerization
- Reactor: A large **stirred-tank reactor** (typically 15–30 m³) equipped with an **internal cooling jacket** and **external reflux condenser** using **liquid ethylene (−104 °C)**.
- Reactants and diluent are pre-cooled to ≈ −90 °C before entering the reactor.
- **Catalyst solution** (AlCl₃ dissolved in chilled CH₃Cl, ~0.2–0.5 wt%) is injected continuously at high velocity through nozzles to ensure instantaneous, uniform dispersion.
- **Heat removal:** The polymerization is **highly exothermic** (ΔH ≈ −85 kJ/mol). Heat must be removed faster than it is generated, otherwise runaway leads to low MW polymer and catastrophic pressure rise.

### Stage C — Polymer Recovery & Devolatilization
- The reactor effluent is a **slurry of fine polymer particles (~1 mm) suspended in CH₃Cl**, with unreacted monomers dissolved.
- This slurry passes to a **flash drum** where vacuum + steam stripping removes unreacted isobutylene, isoprene, and methyl chloride.
- **Recovered monomers** are recycled; recovered CH₃Cl is purified by distillation and reused.

### Stage D — Finishing
- Polymer crumb is **washed with water**, dried in a **mechanical dewatering extruder** followed by an **expeller dryer / air dryer**.
- Final product is **bailed and packaged** in 25 kg or 34 kg bales, often wrapped in polyethylene film.

## 2.2 Chemistry of Cationic Polymerization — The Core Mechanism

### (i) Initiation

The Lewis acid AlCl₃ reacts with the co-catalyst (water or HCl) to generate a **proton**:

$$\text{AlCl}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{H}^+[\text{AlCl}_3\text{OH}]^-$$

This proton protonates the isobutylene double bond to form a **tert-butyl carbocation**:

$$(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}^+[\text{AlCl}_3\text{OH}]^- \longrightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+ + [\text{AlCl}_3\text{OH}]^-$$

### (ii) Propagation

The carbocation attacks another isobutylene monomer, regenerating a new carbocation at the chain end:

$$(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+ + n\,(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{CH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Polymer}^+ \text{ (growing chain)}$$

### (iii) Chain Transfer to Monomer (Primary Termination Pathway)

$$\text{Polymer}^+ + \text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2 \longrightarrow \text{Polymer (dead)} + (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+$$

The proton is regenerated and starts a new chain — this is why the process needs **no stoichiometric amounts of catalyst** (true catalytic process).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why low temperature is critical:** The rate of chain transfer (which limits molecular weight) has a higher activation energy than propagation. Lowering T suppresses chain transfer relative to propagation, yielding **higher MW polymer**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Equation / Value | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number-average degree of polymerization | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_{tr} + R_t}$ | dimensionless | Predicts MW from rates |
| Propagation rate | $R_p = k_p[\text{M}^+][\text{M}]$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | Reaction kinetics |
| Chain transfer to monomer rate | $R_{tr} = k_{tr,m}[\text{M}^+][\text{M}]$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | Determines ceiling MW |
| Activation energy (propagation) | $E_p \approx 12$ | kJ/mol | Kinetic modeling |
| Activation energy (chain transfer) | $E_{tr} \approx 25$ | kJ/mol | Justifies cryogenic operation |
| Polymerization enthalpy | $\Delta H_p \approx -85$ | kJ/mol | Heat removal design |
| Reactor heat duty | $Q = \dot{m}_\text{monomer} \cdot \Delta H_p \cdot X$ | W | Cooling jacket sizing |
| Mark-Houwink equation (viscosity-MW) | $[\eta] = K\,M^a$ | dL/g | MW characterization |
| Glass transition temperature (Tg) | $T_g \approx -67$ | °C | Low-temperature flexibility |
| Specific gravity (cured IIR) | $0.92$ | — | Lightweight rubber |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember this:** $E_{tr} > E_p$ is the **thermodynamic justification for cryogenic polymerization** of isobutylene. Always cite this in KTU viva answers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tire inner liners:** Butyl rubber's gas impermeability is **~10× lower permeability than natural rubber**, making it indispensable for tubeless tire innerliners.
- **Pharmaceutical closures:** Used in vial stoppers and pre-filled syringe plungers because of its **low extractables and excellent gas barrier**.
- **Protective clothing & chemical tank linings:** Resistance to ozone, weathering, and many acids/alkalis.
- **Vibration dampers & acoustic seals:** Inertness and damping properties.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Temperature and Molecular Weight

**Objective:** Show mathematically why cryogenic operation yields high molecular weight butyl rubber.

### Step 1 — Define the rate constants using Arrhenius equations

$$k_p = A_p \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_p}{RT}\right)$$

$$k_{tr,m} = A_{tr,m} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_{tr,m}}{RT}\right)$$

**Logic:** Each elementary step (propagation, chain transfer) follows Arrhenius behavior. We assign separate pre-exponential factors $A$ and activation energies $E$.

### Step 2 — Express the kinetic chain length $\overline{X}_n$

For cationic polymerization with dominant chain transfer to monomer (no termination):

$$\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_{tr,m}} = \dfrac{k_p[\text{M}]}{k_{tr,m}[\text{M}]} = \dfrac{k_p}{k_{tr,m}}$$

**Logic:** Since both $R_p$ and $R_{tr,m}$ depend linearly on [M] and [M⁺], these concentrations cancel, leaving a temperature-only function.

### Step 3 — Substitute the Arrhenius forms

$$\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{A_p}{A_{tr,m}} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_{tr,m} - E_p}{RT}\right)$$

**Logic:** Dividing the exponentials subtracts the exponents; the minus signs flip to give $(E_{tr,m} - E_p)$ in the numerator.

### Step 4 — Evaluate the temperature sensitivity

$$\dfrac{E_{tr,m} - E_p}{R} = \dfrac{(25\,000 - 12\,000) \text{ J/mol}}{8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}} \approx 1567 \text{ K}$$

**Logic:** Numerically: $25 - 12 = 13$ kJ/mol = 13,000 J/mol. Then 13,000 / 8.314 ≈ **1563 K**.

### Step 5 — Compute the ratio of $\overline{X}_n$ at two temperatures

Compare $T_1 = 173$ K (−100 °C) versus $T_2 = 223$ K (−50 °C):

$$\dfrac{\overline{X}_n(T_1)}{\overline{X}_n(T_2)} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{1567}{173} - \dfrac{1567}{223}\right]$$

$$= \exp\!\left[9.057 - 7.027\right] = \exp(2.030) \approx 7.61$$

**Logic:** Going from −50 °C down to −100 °C increases the kinetic chain length by **~7.6×**. This is the *quantitative reason* butyl rubber plants operate at −98 °C.

### Step 6 — Conclude the engineering design rule

$$\boxed{\overline{X}_n \propto \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_{tr,m} - E_p}{RT}\right) \quad \text{with} \quad E_{tr,m} > E_p}$$

A **10 K decrease in reactor temperature roughly doubles the molecular weight**, dramatically improving mechanical strength.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Process Variables → Polymer Properties

| Variable | Direction of Change | Effect on MW (Mw) | Effect on Mooney Viscosity | Effect on Cure Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature ↓** | Lower (more cryogenic) | ↑↑ Strong increase | ↑↑ | Negligible |
| **Catalyst (AlCl₃) ↑** | More catalyst | ↓ (more chains) | ↓ | Negligible |
| **Isoprene content ↑** | More unsaturation | Slight ↓ | Slight ↓ | ↑↑ Faster cure |
| **Conversion ↑** | Higher per pass | ↑ (auto-acceleration) | ↑ | Negligible |
| **Monomer feed purity ↑** | Fewer impurities | ↑ | ↑ | Negligible |
| **Residual moisture ↓** | Better drying | ↑ | ↑ | Negligible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Why cryogenic?", your answer must include: (1) $E_{tr,m} > E_p$, (2) $\overline{X}_n \propto \exp[(E_{tr,m} - E_p)/RT]$, (3) Specific numerical comparison (≈ 7–8× higher MW at −100 °C vs −50 °C).

## 3.3 Energy Balance on the Polymerization Reactor (Numerical Walkthrough)

**Given:** Production target = 10,000 kg/h of IIR polymer. Isobutylene feed = 11,000 kg/h (≈ 90 % conversion). $\Delta H_p = -85$ kJ/mol, MW of isobutylene = 56.11 g/mol.

### Step 1 — Calculate molar flow of converted monomer

$$\dot{n}_\text{converted} = \dfrac{(11{,}000 \times 0.90) \text{ kg/h}}{56.11 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}} = \dfrac{9{,}900 \text{ kg/h}}{0.05611 \text{ kg/mol}} \approx 176{,}440 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Logic:** Mass flow divided by molecular weight yields molar flow.

### Step 2 — Calculate heat release rate

$$\dot{Q}_\text{release} = 176{,}440 \text{ mol/h} \times 85 \text{ kJ/mol} = 14{,}997{,}400 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Logic:** Multiply molar flow by enthalpy of polymerization.

### Step 3 — Convert to kW

$$\dot{Q}_\text{release} = \dfrac{14{,}997{,}400}{3600} \text{ kW} \approx 4{,}166 \text{ kW} \approx 4.17 \text{ MW}$$

**Logic:** 1 kW = 1 kJ/s = 3600 kJ/h.

### Step 4 — Required liquid ethylene coolant flow

Latent heat of vaporization of ethylene at −104 °C ≈ **490 kJ/kg**.

$$\dot{m}_\text{coolant} = \dfrac{14{,}997{,}400 \text{ kJ/h}}{490 \text{ kJ/kg}} \approx 30{,}607 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Logic:** Energy divided by latent heat of vaporization gives coolant mass required.

> [!NOTE]
