> **Design takeaway:** A 10,000 kg/h IIR plant requires the *continuous vaporization of ~30 tonnes of liquid ethylene per hour*. This is why loss of coolant flow is the single largest **inherent safety hazard** in a butyl rubber plant.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Block Diagram — Butyl Rubber Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED[Feed Preparation Section]
        A1[Liquid Isobutylene Storage]
        A2[Liquid Isoprene Storage]
        A3[Methyl Chloride Storage]
        A4[Molecular Sieve Dryer Train]
        A5[Chilled Brine Pre-cooler]
    end

    subgraph REACT[Cryogenic Polymerization]
        B1[Slurry Reactor -98 C]
        B2[Liquid Ethylene Cooling Jacket]
        B3[AlCl3 Catalyst Dissolver]
        B4[High Speed Stirrer]
    end

    subgraph RECOV[Polymer Recovery]
        C1[Flash Drum Primary]
        C2[Steam Stripper Secondary]
        C3[Vacuum Devolatilizer]
        C4[Monomer Recovery Compressor]
        C5[Methyl Chloride Distillation]
    end

    subgraph FINISH[Finishing Section]
        D1[Water Wash Tank]
        D2[Mechanical Dewatering Extruder]
        D3[Expeller Dryer]
        D4[Bale Press]
        D5[Packaging and Storage]
    end

    A1 --> A4
    A2 --> A4
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> B1
    B3 --> B1
    B2 --> B1
    B1 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> D5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Feed (FEED) is purified and pre-cooled, then enters the cryogenic reactor (REACT). The reactor slurry goes to RECOV where unreacted monomer and diluent are flashed, recovered, and recycled. Solid polymer is washed, dried, and baled in FINISH.

## 4.2 Cationic Polymerization Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: AlCl3 plus H2O] --> P2[Proton H plus AlCl3OH minus]
    P2 --> P3[Step 2: H plus attacks Isobutylene]
    P3 --> P4[tert butyl carbocation formed]
    P4 --> P5[Step 3: Propagation - add 1000s of IB units]
    P5 --> P6[Growing polymer carbocation]
    P6 --> P7{Step 4a: Chain transfer to monomer}
    P6 --> P8{Step 4b: Proton loss termination}
    P7 --> P9[Dead polymer chain plus new tert butyl cation]
    P8 --> P10[Dead polymer chain with terminal C=C]
    P9 --> P4
    P10 --> P11[Final polymer chain with occasional isoprene units]
```

## 4.3 Process Control & Safety Interlock Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR[Process Sensors]
        S1[Reactor Temperature TC]
        S2[Reactor Pressure PT]
        S3[Catalyst Flow FT]
        S4[Monomer Flow FT]
        S5[Coolant Flow FT]
        S6[Catalyst Dissolver Level LT]
    end

    subgraph LOGIC[Distributed Control System DCS]
        L1[SIL3 Safety Logic Solver]
        L2[Alarm Management Module]
        L3[Reactor Control Loop]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATOR[Final Control Elements]
        AC1[Emergency Catalyst Shutoff Valve ESDV]
        AC2[Emergency Monomer Block Valve ESDV]
        AC3[Reactor Dump Valve to Quench Tank]
        AC4[Cryogenic Vent Valve]
    end

    S1 --> L1
    S2 --> L1
    S5 --> L1
    L1 --> AC1
    L1 --> AC2
    L1 --> AC3
    L1 --> AC4
    S3 --> L3
    S4 --> L3
    S6 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The figure represents a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** with Safety Integrity Level 3 (SIL 3). Any deviation in **temperature, pressure, or coolant flow** triggers simultaneous **catalyst shutdown, monomer block, and reactor dump** — the *triple-redundancy* principle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]

**Define butyl rubber. State its typical monomer composition and the role of isoprene in the polymer structure.**

**Model Answer:**

Butyl rubber is a synthetic elastomer produced by the **cationic copolymerization of isobutylene (97–98 mol%) and isoprene (2–3 mol%)** at cryogenic temperatures (−95 °C to −100 °C) using AlCl₃ as catalyst.

**Role of isoprene:**
1. Introduces **unsaturation (C=C double bonds)** into the otherwise saturated polyisobutylene backbone.
2. Provides **reactive sites for sulfur vulcanization**, without which the rubber cannot be cross-linked.
3. Controls the **cure rate and cross-link density** of the final product.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students often answer "isoprene provides flexibility." This is **wrong**. Flexibility comes from isobutylene. The correct role is **enabling vulcanization via unsaturation**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**Why is butyl rubber polymerized at very low temperatures (−100 °C)? Mention two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Suppression of chain transfer reactions:** The activation energy for chain transfer to monomer ($E_{tr} \approx 25$ kJ/mol) is greater than that for propagation ($E_p \approx 12$ kJ/mol). Lowering T suppresses chain transfer relative to propagation, yielding **high molecular weight** polymer.

2. **Prevention of cationic side reactions:** At higher temperatures, carbocations undergo **rearrangement, isomerization, and proton loss**, producing low-MW oligomers and unwanted unsaturation patterns.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students sometimes write "low temperature increases reaction rate." This is **incorrect** for chain transfer-dominated polymerization. Always cite the **Arrhenius relationship between $E_{tr}$ and $E_p$**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Regular]

**(a) Describe with a neat flow diagram the manufacturing process of butyl rubber by the slurry process. List the major feed stocks, catalyst, diluent, and operating conditions. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the cationic polymerization mechanism of isobutylene with AlCl₃ catalyst. Derive the relationship between reactor temperature and molecular weight of the polymer. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Q.A (a):

**Feedstocks:**
- **Isobutylene** (97–98 mol%) — main monomer.
- **Isoprene** (2–3 mol%) — co-monomer for unsaturation.
- **Methyl chloride (CH₃Cl)** — diluent/solvent.
- **Anhydrous AlCl₃** — Lewis acid catalyst.
- **Trace H₂O or HCl** — co-catalyst.
- **Liquid ethylene** — coolant.

**Operating conditions:**
- Temperature: **−95 °C to −100 °C**
- Pressure: near atmospheric (1–2 bar)
- Catalyst concentration: 0.2–0.5 wt% of monomer
- Monomer conversion: 60–90 %

**Process steps (refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram):**
1. **Feed purification:** Monomers and diluent are dried through molecular sieves and alumina beds to < 10 ppm H₂O.
2. **Pre-cooling:** Cooled to ~ −90 °C using chilled brine/ethylene.
3. **Polymerization:** Fed into a large stirred-tank reactor fitted with cooling jacket. AlCl₃ catalyst solution injected at high velocity. Polymer precipitates as fine slurry.
4. **Devolatilization:** Slurry sent to flash drum and steam stripper. Unreacted monomers and CH₃Cl recovered and recycled.
5. **Finishing:** Polymer is water-washed, mechanically dewatered, dried in expeller dryer, baled, and packaged.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A (b):

**Mechanism:**

**Step 1 — Initiation:** AlCl₃ reacts with co-catalyst water:

$$\text{AlCl}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{H}^+[\text{AlCl}_3\text{OH}]^-$$

The proton attacks isobutylene to form tert-butyl cation:

$$(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+$$

**Step 2 — Propagation:** Sequential addition of isobutylene:

$$(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+ + n(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{CH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Polymer}^+$$

**Step 3 — Termination (chain transfer to monomer):**

$$\text{Polymer}^+ + \text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)_2 \longrightarrow \text{Polymer} + (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}^+$$

**Derivation of MW-temperature relationship:**

Using Arrhenius expressions for $k_p$ and $k_{tr,m}$:

$$\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p}{k_{tr,m}} = \dfrac{A_p}{A_{tr,m}} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_{tr,m} - E_p}{RT}\right)$$

Substituting $E_{tr,m} - E_p \approx 13$ kJ/mol and operating at $T = 173$ K versus 223 K:

$$\dfrac{\overline{X}_n(173)}{\overline{X}_n(223)} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{13{,}000}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{173} - \dfrac{1}{223}\right)\right] \approx 7.6$$

**Conclusion:** Operating at −100 °C yields **~7.6× higher molecular weight** than at −50 °C, justifying cryogenic operation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Alternative]

**(a) Discuss the major safety hazards involved in butyl rubber manufacturing. What are the inherent safety features in the cryogenic slurry reactor? (7 marks)**

**(b) A butyl rubber plant produces 8,000 kg/h of polymer at 85 % conversion of isobutylene. The polymerization enthalpy is −85 kJ/mol. Calculate (i) the heat released per hour, and (ii) the liquid ethylene coolant required per hour if its latent heat of vaporization is 490 kJ/kg. (7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Q.B (a):

**Major Safety Hazards:**
1. **Cryogenic burn and embrittlement** from liquid ethylene (−104 °C) and reactor surfaces.
2. **Flammability and explosion** of isobutylene (LEL = 1.8 %, UEL = 8.8 %).
3. **Toxicity** of methyl chloride (TLV-TWA = 50 ppm; narcotic at high exposure).
4. **Corrosive nature** of HCl co-catalyst.
5. **Runaway reaction** if cooling fails — exothermic polymerization at ~85 kJ/mol.
6. **Catalyst hazard:** AlCl₃ reacts violently with water, releasing HCl fumes.

**Inherent Safety Features:**
1. **Inventory minimization** — small continuous feed rather than large batch reactors.
2. **Intensive cooling** with refrigerant at lower temperature than reaction → no possible temperature rise without external heating.
3. **Quench tank dump** — emergency reactor contents can be discharged into a water/quench tank.
4. **Catalyst injection interlocks** — catalyst flow stops on loss of monomer, diluent, or coolant.
5. **Double-walled piping** for cryogenic lines to prevent frosting and embrittlement.
6. **Inert gas blanketing** on all monomer and diluent storage tanks.

---

### Model Solution to Q.B (b):

**Given:**
- Polymer output = 8,000 kg/h
- Conversion = 85 %
- $\Delta H_p = -85$ kJ/mol
- MW of isobutylene = 56.11 g/mol
- Latent heat of liquid ethylene, $\lambda = 490$ kJ/kg

**Step 1 — Isobutylene feed required**

$$\text{Feed} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{0.85} = 9{,}412 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Molar flow of reacted isobutylene**

$$\dot{n} = \dfrac{8{,}000 \text{ kg/h}}{56.11 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}} = 142{,}578 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Heat released per hour (i)**

$$\dot{Q} = 142{,}578 \text{ mol/h} \times 85 \text{ kJ/mol} = 12{,}119{,}130 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 1.21 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kW (optional check)**

$$\dot{Q} = \dfrac{12{,}119{,}130}{3600} \approx 3{,}367 \text{ kW} \approx 3.37 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5 — Liquid ethylene coolant required (ii)**

$$\dot{m}_\text{coolant} = \dfrac{12{,}119{,}130 \text{ kJ/h}}{490 \text{ kJ/kg}} \approx 24{,}733 \text{ kg/h} \approx 24.7 \text{ tonnes/h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not** use the *feed* mass (9,412 kg/h) — use the *reacted* mass (8,000 kg/h) for enthalpy calculation.
> 2. **Do not** forget to convert kg → mol using MW = 56.11 g/mol.
> 3. **Do not** confuse latent heat with sensible heat — the coolant warms by **boiling**, so use latent heat only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Butyl Rubber Polymerization**

- ☐ Butyl rubber = **copolymer of isobutylene (97–98 %) + isoprene (2–3 %)**.
- ☐ Standard designation: **IIR (ISO 1629)**.
- ☐ Polymerization type: **cationic slurry polymerization**.
- ☐ Temperature: **−95 °C to −100 °C** (cryogenic, liquid ethylene coolant).
- ☐ Catalyst: **AlCl₃ (Lewis acid) + H₂O / HCl co-catalyst**.
- ☐ Diluent: **Methyl chloride (CH₃Cl)** — chosen for low freezing point and non-reactivity.
- ☐ Isoprene role: introduces **C=C unsaturation** for **sulfur vulcanization**.
- ☐ $E_{tr,m} > E_p$ → cryogenic operation **suppresses chain transfer** → **high MW**.
- ☐ $\overline{X}_n \propto \exp[(E_{tr,m} - E_p)/RT]$; **~7.6× MW increase from −50 °C → −100 °C**.
- ☐ Reaction is highly **exothermic**: $\Delta H_p \approx -85$ kJ/mol.
- ☐ Termination by **chain transfer to monomer** — catalyst is **regenerated** (true catalysis).
- ☐ Major hazards: **cryogenic burns, flammability of isobutylene, toxicity of CH₃Cl, runaway**.
- ☐ Key inherent safety feature: **refrigerant temperature < reaction temperature** — heat always flows out.
- ☐ Applications: **tire inner liners, pharma stoppers, gas masks, tank linings**.
- ☐ Properties to remember: **very low gas permeability (~10× better than NR), Tg ≈ −67 °C, Mooney ≈ 45**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Fire Hazards in Petroleum and Petrochemical Industries (Ignition by Local Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Potential Fire Hazards in Petroleum and Petrochemical Industries (Ignition by Local Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Potential Fire Hazards in Petroleum & Petrochemical Industries?

A **Potential Fire Hazard** in petroleum and petrochemical industries is defined as any physical, chemical, electrical, thermal, or mechanical condition that has the inherent capacity to initiate combustion by providing sufficient **activation energy** to a flammable hydrocarbon–air mixture. These hazards are classified as **"local"** ignition sources when they are confined to a specific equipment, zone, or operating point rather than being ambient (e.g., general plant temperature).

As per **KTU 2024 syllabus terminology**, local ignition sources are the localized points or items capable of releasing energy above the **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** of the surrounding hydrocarbon vapours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> A *local ignition source* in hydrocarbon processing is any discrete point of energy release — flame, spark, hot surface, or static discharge — situated within or adjacent to a classified hazardous area (Zone 0, Zone 1, Zone 2), whose surface temperature or instantaneous energy output exceeds the Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) or Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) of the in-situ hydrocarbon medium.

---

## 1.2 The Fire Triangle — Foundation Concept

Every fire in a hydrocarbon facility requires the simultaneous presence of three elements, called the **Fire Triangle**:

| Element | Hydrocarbon Industry Example |
|---|---|
| **Fuel** | Crude oil, LPG, naphtha, gasoline vapours, methane, H₂S |
| **Oxidiser** | Atmospheric oxygen (O₂ > 21%), or pure O₂ in process lines |
| **Ignition Source (Local)** | Spark, hot surface, static discharge, hot work ember |

> [!NOTE]
> The "Fire Triangle" is the fundamental model taught in **NFPA / OISD / IS 14435** and is the bedrock of all **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** studies in KTU-aligned Process Safety modules.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy — "The Gas Stove in Your Kitchen":**

Imagine a domestic LPG cylinder leak. The invisible LPG vapour (fuel) and kitchen air (oxygen) are always present, but nothing happens — until you press the igniter knob. That tiny **piezoelectric spark** is a *local ignition source*. Now scale this up to a refinery: the same spark, but in a cloud of gasoline vapour above a storage tank, becomes catastrophic.

In a petroleum refinery, the analogue of the kitchen igniter is any of the following:
- A **welding spark** during maintenance ("hot work")
- **Static electricity** discharge when a tanker unloads
- An **overheated pump bearing** reaching $250°C$ (above AIT of diesel)
- A **loose electrical connection** arcing inside a junction box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industrial Constants (must memorise):**
> - **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) of methane** ≈ **0.28 mJ**
> - **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) of hydrogen** ≈ **0.017 mJ** (most dangerous)
> - **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) of gasoline** ≈ **280 °C**
> - **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) of diesel** ≈ **210 °C**
> - **Flash Point of gasoline** ≈ **–43 °C**
> - **Flash Point of kerosene** ≈ **38–72 °C**

---

## 1.4 Why "Local" Sources Matter in KTU Module 1

In Module 1, the focus on **local** sources is deliberate because:
1. **Ambient (non-local) sources** like ambient temperature are easier to control (just keep below AIT).
2. **Local sources** are transient, often hidden, and appear only during specific operations (loading, sampling, hot work).
3. The **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework for PESFT745 expects students to identify, classify, and propose control measures for *local* ignition sources — this maps to **CO1: Identify and assess fire and explosion hazards in hydrocarbon industries**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Local Ignition Sources

Local ignition sources in petroleum and petrochemical industries are classified into **six major families** as per **OISD-STD-105** and **NFPA 30** standards:

### A. Open Flames and Hot Work
- Welding, cutting, brazing sparks
- Smoking, matches, lighters
- Furnace doors, flare pilot flames

### B. Hot Surfaces
- Overheated bearings, pump housings
- Steam pipes above AIT
- Lagged surfaces that have lost insulation
- Process heater tubes

### C. Sparks from Friction, Impact, and Electrical Origin
- **Friction sparks** — grinding wheels, metal-on-metal contact
- **Impact sparks** — hammer on rusty steel, dropped tools
- **Electrical sparks** — relay contacts, switchgear, loose terminals

### D. Static Electricity
- Flow of hydrocarbons through pipes (charge generation)
- Splash filling of tanks
- Steam/moisture喷射
- Walking on non-conductive floors

### E. Electrical Equipment (Non-Sparking vs. Sparking)
- Motors, lighting, junction boxes
- Classified by **IS 5571 / IEC 60079** into Ex d, Ex e, Ex i, Ex p

### F. Hot Particles and Embers
- Smouldering cotton waste (the "Houston Ship Channel" disaster, 2005)
- Hot slag from cutting operations
- Coke dust in FCC units

---

## 2.2 Why and How Each Source Ignites — The Physics

### 2.2.1 Hot Surface Ignition

A hot surface ignites a vapour when its **surface temperature** exceeds the **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of the vapour. The relevant heat transfer is:

$$Q_{\text{crit}} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_{AIT})$$

Where:
- $Q_{\text{crit}}$ = critical heat flux required for ignition (W)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = contact area (m²)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature (K)
- $T_{AIT}$ = Auto-Ignition Temperature of vapour (K)

> **Engineering Insight:** In a refinery, an overheated **pump bearing** running at $250°C$ near a gasoline vent line is an extreme hazard because gasoline AIT is only $\mathbf{280°C}$ — the margin is razor-thin.

---

### 2.2.2 Spark Ignition — Energy Criterion

A spark ignites a flammable mixture when the **discharge energy** exceeds the **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)**:

$$E_{\text{spark}} \geq E_{\text{MIE}}$$

For capacitive discharge sparks:

$$E_{\text{spark}} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

Where:
- $C$ = capacitance (Farads)
- $V$ = breakdown voltage (Volts)
- $E_{\text{spark}}$ = stored electrical energy (Joules)

---

### 2.2.3 Static Electricity — Charge Accumulation

In hydrocarbon flow, charge is generated by **charge separation** at the pipe wall. The resulting **streaming current** is:

$$I_s = q_v \cdot \rho \cdot u \cdot A$$

Where:
- $I_s$ = streaming current (Amperes)
- $q_v$ = volume charge density (C/m³)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $u$ = flow velocity (m/s)
- $A$ = pipe cross-section area (m²)

The **relaxation time** $\tau$ for charge decay is:

$$\tau = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \rho_e$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the liquid
- $\rho_e$ = resistivity (Ω·m)

> **Critical Industrial Rule:** If $\tau > 0.5$ s, the liquid is considered a **static accumulator** (e.g., kerosene, diesel, gasoline), and bonding/grounding is mandatory.

---

### 2.2.4 Auto-Ignition Delay Time

The time between vapour exposure to a hot surface and actual ignition follows the **Semenov thermal explosion theory**:

$$\tau_{\text{ignition}} = \frac{C_p \cdot \rho \cdot V \cdot (T_{AIT} - T_0)}{Q_{\text{gen}} - Q_{\text{loss}}}$$

Where $Q_{\text{gen}}$ is the heat generated by exothermic oxidation and $Q_{\text{loss}}$ is the heat lost to surroundings.

> **Why this matters:** Hot surfaces do **not** ignite instantly. A short exposure (e.g., flying spark) may be insufficient even if $T_s > T_{AIT}$. This is why **tank entry hot work permits** mandate cool-down periods of 30–60 minutes minimum.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spark energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Joules | Verify MIE compliance |
| 2 | Streaming current | $I_s = q_v \rho u A$ | Amperes | Static charge in pipelines |
| 3 | Charge relaxation | $\tau = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \rho_e$ | seconds | Identify static accumulators |
| 4 | Critical heat flux | $q'' = h (T_s - T_{AIT})$ | W/m² | Hot surface ignition |
| 5 | Flash Point rule | $T_{FP} < T_{amb} < T_{AIT}$ | °C | Defines "flammable liquid" |
| 6 | Flammable range (LFL/UFL) | $C_{LFL} \leq C \leq C_{UFL}$ | vol% | Explosive atmosphere test |
| 7 | Energy vs. MIE | $E_{\text{source}} > E_{\text{MIE}}$ | J | Spark source classification |
| 8 | Auto-ignition delay | Semenov eqn above | s | Hot work permit timing |
| 9 | Reynolds number (flow regime) | $Re = \rho u D / \mu$ | — | Determines turbulence-induced static |
| 10 | Electrostatic discharge field | $E_{\text{field}} = V/d$ | V/m | Determines breakdown threshold |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Most 3-mark Part A questions on this module test either *MIE values* or *fire triangle identification*. Most 14-mark Part B questions test *static electricity* or *classification of local ignition sources*.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding local ignition sources enables engineers to:
- **Design hazardous area classification maps** (Zone 0/1/2 for gases; Zone 20/21/22 for dusts)
- **Select Ex-proof equipment** (Ex d flameproof, Ex e increased safety, Ex i intrinsically safe)
- **Issue hot work permits** correctly
- **Justify bonding/grounding systems** in truck loading racks
- **Conduct HAZOP & HAZID** studies for new projects

> **Industrial Case Study:** The **2005 Buncefield disaster (UK)** — a gasoline storage terminal — was caused by a *local* overfill leading to vapour cloud formation, ignited by an unidentified local source (likely a diesel engine or electrical spark). Loss: $£ 1.5$ billion. This case is taught in every KTU PESFT745 module as a benchmark.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Flow Velocity to Avoid Static Ignition

The **API RP 2003** standard prescribes a maximum flow velocity for hydrocarbon transfer to limit static charge accumulation.

**Given:**
- Pipe diameter $D = 0.20$ m
- Fluid density $\rho = 750$ kg/m³ (gasoline)
- Volume charge density $q_v = 10^{-5}$ C/m³
- Minimum Ignition Energy of gasoline vapour, $E_{\text{MIE}} = 0.25$ mJ $= 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ J
- Effective capacitance of the receiving tank, $C = 10^{-9}$ F (1 nF)

**Find:** Maximum safe flow velocity $u_{\max}$

**Step 1 — Relate spark energy to voltage on the tank:**

The tank acts as a capacitor. The energy stored is:

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

**Step 2 — Set the stored energy equal to the MIE (worst case):**

$$\frac{1}{2} C V^2 = E_{\text{MIE}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum allowable voltage:**

$$V_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{\text{MIE}}}{C}}$$

$$V_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-9}}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the square root:**

$$\frac{2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.0 \times 10^{5}$$

$$V_{\max} = \sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{5}} = 707.1 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Relate streaming current to flow velocity:**

The streaming current charges the tank capacitance:

$$I_s = C \frac{dV}{dt} \Rightarrow V = \frac{I_s \cdot t}{C}$$

For steady flow during filling time $t_f$:

$$V = \frac{q_v \cdot \rho \cdot u \cdot A \cdot t_f}{C}$$

**Step 6 — Cross-sectional area of pipe:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^2 = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Restrict voltage and solve for u:**

For filling time $t_f = 600$ s (10 min):

$$u_{\max} = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot C}{q_v \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot t_f}$$

$$u_{\max} = \frac{707.1 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-5} \times 750 \times 0.0314 \times 600}$$

**Step 8 — Numerator:**

$$707.1 \times 10^{-9} = 7.071 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 9 — Denominator:**

$$10^{-5} \times 750 \times 0.0314 \times 600 = 1.413 \times 10^{-1}$$

**Step 10 — Final division:**

$$u_{\max} = \frac{7.071 \times 10^{-7}}{1.413 \times 10^{-1}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The calculated velocity is unrealistically low because the model assumed **no charge relaxation** during filling. In reality, the relaxation time $\tau$ limits accumulation. Including $\tau$:

$$u_{\max} = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot C}{q_v \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot \tau} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t_f/\tau}\right)^{-1}$$

For $\tau = 5$ s and $t_f = 600$ s, the bracketed term ≈ 1, so:

$$u_{\max} \approx 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s} \approx 2.2 \text{ m/hr}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **API RP 2003 limit:** Maximum $u = 7$ m/s for gasoline splash loading, $u = 1$ m/s initially for new tanks ("1 m/s rule") to avoid charge accumulation. The rigorous calculation confirms the industry rule.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hot Surface Minimum Area for Ignition

A flat hot plate at $T_s = 500°C$ is exposed to hexane vapour (AIT = $225°C$).

**Given:**
- $T_s = 773$ K
- $T_{AIT} = 498$ K
- Convective heat transfer coefficient $h = 50$ W/m²·K
- Critical heat flux for ignition $q''_{\text{crit}} = 5000$ W/m² (from literature)

**Find:** Minimum ignition area $A_{\min}$

**Step 1 — Energy balance at ignition threshold:**

The hot surface must supply heat at rate $q''_{\text{crit}}$:

$$Q = h A (T_s - T_{AIT}) = q''_{\text{crit}} \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Solve for A:**

$$A = \frac{q''_{\text{crit}} \cdot A}{h (T_s - T_{AIT})}$$

This is consistent, so use absolute form:

$$A_{\min} = \frac{q''_{\text{crit}}}{h (T_s - T_{AIT})}$$

**Step 3 — Plug values:**

$$A_{\min} = \frac{5000}{50 \times (773 - 498)} = \frac{5000}{50 \times 275} = \frac{5000}{13750}$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$A_{\min} = 0.364 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Interpretation:** A hot surface area as small as **0.36 m²** (roughly a small tabletop) at 500°C is enough to ignite hexane vapour. This is why **OISD-STD-105** mandates temperature monitoring of all surfaces within 3 m of hydrocarbon vents.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Local Ignition Sources vs. Hydrocarbon Class

| Local Source | Energy Range | Effective Against | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Welding spark | $\sim 10$ J (Far > MIE) | All hydrocarbons | Hot work permit, gas testing |
| Electrostatic spark | $\sim 1$–$100$ mJ | Vapours MIE < 0.5 mJ (H₂, C₂H₂) | Bonding, grounding, $u \leq 7$ m/s |
| Hot bearing | $T_s$ up to $400°C$ | Gasoline (AIT 280°C) | Vibration monitoring, $T$ cutout |
| Light switch arc | $\sim 5$–$20$ mJ | LPG (MIE 0.25 mJ) | Ex-proof enclosures |
| Cigarette ember | $\sim 800°C$ | All classes | No-smoking policy, vapour detection |
| Mobile phone | $\sim 1$ W RF | Hydrogen only (MIE 0.017 mJ) | Ex-intrinsically-safe devices only |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example — Capacitor-Discharge Spark Hazard

A maintenance worker uses a **capacitive discharge tool** (used to ignite pilot burners) with $C = 1 \mu F$ and $V = 30 V$ near a methane leak.

**Step 1 — Compute spark energy:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-6} \times (30)^2$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 900 = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.45 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 2 — Compare to MIE of methane (0.28 mJ):**

$$E_{\text{spark}} = 0.45 \text{ mJ} > E_{\text{MIE, CH}_4} = 0.28 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\text{IGNITION HAZARD — Spark energy exceeds MIE by 1.6x}}$$

> **Mitigation:** Use **intrinsically safe (Ex i) tools** where stored energy $E < 0.28$ mJ. For methane, this requires $C V^2 < 5.6 \times 10^{-4}$ J.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Classification of Local Ignition Sources in Hydrocarbon Industry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Local Ignition Sources in Petroleum and Petrochemical Industries]:::root
    A --> B[Thermal Sources]
    A --> C[Electrical Sources]
    A --> D[Mechanical Sources]
    A --> E[Chemical Sources]

    B --> B1[Open Flames and Hot Work]
    B --> B2[Hot Surfaces above AIT]
    B --> B3[Hot Particles and Embers]
    B --> B4[Steam Pipes and Lagged Surfaces]

    C --> C1[Static Electricity Discharges]
    C --> C2[Electrical Sparks from Switchgear]
    C --> C3[Lightning Induced Surges]
    C --> C4[RF Emissions from Mobile Devices]

    D --> D1[Friction Sparks from Grinding]
    D --> D2[Impact Sparks from Tools]
    D --> D3[Bearing Failure Sparks]

    E --> E1[Spontaneous Combustion of Pyrophoric Iron Sulphide]
    E --> E2[Reactive Chemical Spillage]
    E --> E3[Exothermic Decomposition]

    B1 --> X[Control Hot Work Permit]
    B2 --> Y[Surface Temperature Monitoring]
    C1 --> Z[Bonding and Grounding]
    D1 --> W[Non Sparking Tools]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef src fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ctrl fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fire Triangle and Local Ignition Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Fuel: Hydrocarbon Vapour]:::fuel
    O[Oxidiser: Atmospheric Oxygen]:::ox
    I[Local Ignition Source: Spark or Hot Surface]:::ign
    FIRE[Combustion and Fire or Explosion]:::fire

    F --> FIRE
    O --> FIRE
    I --> FIRE

    I --> I1[Energy greater than MIE]
    I1 --> I2[Temperature greater than AIT]
    I2 --> FIRE

    F --> F1[Concentration within Flammable Range LFL to UFL]
    O --> O1[Oxygen greater than 10 percent]
    F1 --> FIRE
    O1 --> FIRE

    classDef fuel fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ox fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ign fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef fire fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Hot Work Permit and Local Ignition Prevention

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Eng as Maintenance Engineer
    participant Sup as Supervisor
    participant Saf as Safety Officer
    participant Ops as Operations Team
    participant Fac as Facility

    Eng->>Sup: Request Hot Work Permit
    Sup->>Saf: Forward for Review
    Saf->>Ops: Isolate Equipment and Purge Lines
    Ops->>Saf: Equipment Gas Freed and Vented
    Saf->>Fac: Conduct LEL Gas Test Below 1 percent
    Fac->>Saf: LEL Reading 0.2 percent Safe
    Saf->>Eng: Issue Permit with Validity 4 Hours
    Eng->>Fac: Perform Welding or Cutting
    Fac->>Saf: Continuous Gas Monitoring Every 30 Minutes
    Eng->>Sup: Complete Work and Request Permit Closure
    Sup->>Saf: Close Permit and Restore Equipment
    Saf->>Ops: Hand Back Equipment for Commissioning
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Static Electricity Generation in Hydrocarbon Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Hydrocarbon Flow in Pipe]:::flow
    P1 --> Q1[Charge Separation at Pipe Wall]
    Q1 --> Q2[Streaming Current Generation]
    Q2 --> Q3[Charge Accumulation on Tank Wall]
    Q3 --> Q4{Potential Exceeds Breakdown Voltage}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Electrostatic Discharge Spark]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[Charge Decays via Relaxation]
    Q5 --> Q7[Ignition if Energy Exceeds MIE]
    Q7 --> Q8[Fire or Explosion]
    Q6 --> Q9[Safe Discharge via Grounding]

    B1[Bonding and Grounding at Fill Point]:::ctrl
    B2[Flow Velocity Restriction below 7 m/s]:::ctrl
    B3[Relaxation Time Monitoring]:::ctrl
    B1 --> Q5
    B2 --> Q3
    B3 --> Q6

    classDef flow fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#008000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE). Why is hydrogen considered the most hazardous hydrocarbon in terms of ignition?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** is defined as the smallest amount of electrical energy stored in a capacitor that, when discharged as a spark across a spark gap, is just sufficient to ignite the most easily ignitable mixture of a gas or vapour with air under specified test conditions.

- **Mathematical Definition:** MIE is the energy at which ignition probability becomes 50%, expressed in millijoules (mJ).
- **Hydrogen MIE:** $E_{\text{MIE, H}_2} = 0.017$ mJ, which is the **lowest** of all common hydrocarbons.
- **Comparative values:** Methane 0.28 mJ, propane 0.25 mJ, gasoline vapour 0.25 mJ, hydrogen sulphide 0.077 mJ.
- Hydrogen's low molecular weight and high flame velocity (3.06 m/s) make it the most easily ignited gas.

**Conclusion:** Hydrogen requires only 1/16th the energy of methane to ignite, making any local spark a critical hazard in hydrogen handling units. (3 marks)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six local ignition sources commonly encountered in petroleum refineries.**

**Model Answer:**

The six local ignition sources are:
1. **Open flames and hot work sparks** — welding, cutting, smoking
2. **Hot surfaces** — overheated pump bearings, lagged steam pipes
3. **Static electricity** — flow-induced charge in hydrocarbon transfer
4. **Electrical sparks** — switchgear, relays, loose terminals
5. **Friction and impact sparks** — grinding, dropped tools on rusty steel
6. **Pyrophoric iron sulphide** — spontaneous ignition in process equipment

Each source must be addressed with **engineering control** as per **OISD-STD-105**. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the phenomenon of **static electricity generation** during petroleum transfer operations. Discuss the conditions under which static discharge can ignite hydrocarbon vapours, and outline **six control measures** to prevent static-induced fires.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A refinery pump has a bearing housing running at $T_s = 350°C$. The pump handles **n-hexane** (AIT = $225°C$) under a vapour-rich environment. With the heat transfer coefficient $h = 75$ W/m²·K and the bearing housing area $A = 0.12$ m², calculate:
1. The temperature difference driving heat to the vapour.
2. The total heat flux supplied to the vapour cloud.
3. Whether the pump constitutes an ignition hazard, and if so, recommend **two engineering controls**.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) — Static Electricity**

**1. Mechanism of charge generation:**

When hydrocarbons flow through pipes, filters, valves, and during splash filling of tanks, **charge separation** occurs at the liquid–solid interface. The streaming current $I_s = q_v \rho u A$ is generated. Because hydrocarbon liquids are **electrical insulators** (resistivity $\rho_e > 10^9$ Ω·m), the charges do not leak away quickly; they accumulate on the surface of the receiving tank.

**2. Ignition conditions:**

Ignition occurs when:
- The **tank potential** $V$ exceeds the **breakdown voltage** of the vapour space (typically 25–35 kV for a non-conductive vapour).
- The **stored energy** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ exceeds the **MIE** of the vapour.
- The vapour concentration lies within the **flammable range** (LFL ≤ C ≤ UFL).

**3. Six control measures:**

1. **Bonding and grounding** of all transfer equipment (resistance < 10 Ω).
2. **Flow velocity restriction**: $u \leq 7$ m/s for gasoline splash loading; $u \leq 1$ m/s initial for new tanks.
3. **Use of anti-static additives** (e.g., Stadis 450) to increase conductivity.
4. **Bottom loading** instead of splash filling (no charge generation by falling stream).
5. **Relaxation time** wait of at least 30 seconds after flow stop before sampling.
6. **Elimination of free-fall splash** and use of **submerged fill** pipes.

(7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) — Numerical**

**Step 1 — Temperature difference:**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_{AIT} = 350 - 225 = 125°C = 125 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Heat flux supplied:**

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 75 \times 0.12 \times 125$$

$$Q = 75 \times 0.12 \times 125 = 1125 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Heat flux density:**

$$q'' = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1125}{0.12} = 9375 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Hazard assessment:**

Since $T_s = 350°C > T_{AIT} = 225°C$ AND $q'' = 9375 \text{ W/m}^2 > q''_{\text{crit}} \approx 5000 \text{ W/m}^2$ for n-hexane, the bearing is an **active ignition hazard**.

**Step 5 — Two engineering controls:**

1. **Install vibration/temperature monitoring** with automatic shutdown at $T_s = 200°C$ (below AIT margin of 25°C).
2. **Replace bearing with high-temperature rated grease** (e.g., silicone-based) and **shield the housing** with a thermal barrier.

(7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of the **fire triangle**, classify all major **local ignition sources** in petrochemical industries. Discuss in detail why **hot work** is the single most common cause of refinery fires, with reference to **OISD-STD-105** and **NFPA 51B** standards.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **storage tank** contains gasoline with a vapour space of $C = 50$ vol% relative to the LFL. The tank has effective capacitance $C_t = 2$ nF, and is being filled at flow velocity $u = 5$ m/s through a 6-inch pipe ($\rho_{\text{gasoline}} = 740$ kg/m³, $q_v = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ C/m³). Determine:
1. The streaming current.
2. The voltage developed in 60 seconds (assume steady filling).
3. Whether the system is safe given $E_{\text{MIE, gasoline}} = 0.25$ mJ.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a) — Fire Triangle and Hot Work**

**1. Fire Triangle Recap:**
- **Fuel** (hydrocarbon vapour/liquid)
- **Oxidiser** (air/O₂)
- **Local Ignition Source** (energy > MIE)

**2. Classification of local sources** (briefly list the 6 families from Section 2.1): thermal, electrical, mechanical, chemical, static, RF.

**3. Why hot work dominates:**

According to **API and OISD fire statistics**, hot work accounts for ~25–30% of all refinery fires globally.

- Hot work introduces an **active, high-energy ignition source** (welding arc ~ 10 J, cutting spark ~ 5 J) — both vastly exceeding MIE of any hydrocarbon (0.02–0.5 mJ).
- The work is often performed during **turnarounds** when equipment is opened, increasing vapour release.
- **NFPA 51B** mandates a 35-foot (11 m) clear zone, gas testing, fire watch, and **permit validity ≤ 4 hours**.

**4. Hot Work Permit — Critical Elements** (as per OISD-STD-105):
- LEL < 1% at point of work
- Continuous fire watch with extinguisher
- Equipment isolation, blinding, and gas freeing
- Permit closure with area monitoring for 30 minutes post-work

(7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to (b) — Numerical**

**Step 1 — Pipe diameter in metres:**

$$D = 6 \text{ in} = 6 \times 0.0254 = 0.1524 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.1524^2 = 0.01824 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Streaming current:**

$$I_s = q_v \cdot \rho \cdot u \cdot A$$

$$I_s = (5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 740 \times 5 \times 0.01824$$

$$I_s = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 740 \times 5 \times 0.01824$$

**Step 4 — Numerator multiplication:**

$$740 \times 5 = 3700$$

$$3700 \times 0.01824 = 67.49$$

$$I_s = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 67.49 = 3.37 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} = 0.337 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 5 — Voltage after $t = 60$ s:**

$$V = \frac{I_s \cdot t}{C_t} = \frac{3.37 \times 10^{-4} \times 60}{2 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute:**

$$3.37 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 = 2.022 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$V = \frac{2.022 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.011 \times 10^{7} \text{ V} = 10.1 \text{ MV}$$

**Step 7 — Stored energy check:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C_t V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-9} \times (1.011 \times 10^{7})^2$$

$$(1.011 \times 10^{7})^2 = 1.022 \times 10^{14}$$

$$E = 10^{-9} \times 1.022 \times 10^{14} = 1.022 \times 10^{5} \text{ J} = 102.2 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 8 — Hazard assessment:**

$$E = 102.2 \text{ kJ} \gg E_{\text{MIE, gasoline}} = 0.25 \text{ mJ} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J}$$

**Step 9 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\text{CRITICAL HAZARD — System is grossly unsafe. Reduce velocity to } u \leq 0.05 \text{ m/s or add anti-static additive.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT forget to convert pipe diameter** from inches to metres (1 in = 0.0254 m). Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> - **Watch the units** of $E_{\text{MIE}}$: must convert mJ to J before comparing.
> - **Streaming current formula** is dimensionally $C/m^3 \times kg/m^3 \times m/s \times m^2 = A/m$? No — it's Ampere (the $\rho$ here is mass density; the equation comes from charge per unit mass × mass flow rate = current). Use correct notation.
> - **Common mistake:** Using $D$ as radius. Always cross-check: $A = \pi r^2 = \pi D^2/4$.

(7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fire Triangle** is the foundation: Fuel + Oxidiser + Local Ignition Source = Fire.
- **MIE** of hydrogen is **0.017 mJ** — lowest of all hydrocarbons.
- **AIT** of gasoline is **280°C** — pump bearings must stay below 200°C.
- **Static electricity** is governed by the streaming current $I_s = q_v \rho u A$ and relaxation time $\tau = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \rho_e$.
- **API RP 2003 velocity limit** for gasoline splash loading = **7 m/s**; initial fill = **1 m/s**.
- **Hot work** is the leading cause of refinery fires (~25–30%); governed by **OISD-STD-105** and **NFPA 51B**.
- **Bonding and grounding** resistance must be < **10 Ω** for hydrocarbon transfer.
- **LEL gas test** must be < **1%** before hot work begins.
- **Flammable range** (LFL to UFL) defines the explosive envelope; below LFL = too lean, above UFL = too rich.
- **Pyrophoric iron sulphide** ignites spontaneously in air — common in H₂S service equipment.
- **Spark energy formula** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ must be compared to MIE for intrinsically safe design.
- **Six families of local ignition sources**: open flames, hot surfaces, sparks, static, electrical, pyrophoric.
- **Hot surface minimum area** for ignition: $A_{\min} = q''_{\text{crit}} / [h (T_s - T_{AIT})]$.
- **Hazardous area classification** zones (Zone 0/1/2 for gases) must be respected when selecting Ex-proof equipment.
- **Memorise key constants**: $T_{\text{FP, gasoline}} = -43°C$, $T_{\text{FP, diesel}} = 55°C$, $T_{\text{FP, kerosene}} = 38–72°C$.
- **Semenov thermal explosion theory** explains ignition delay time at hot surfaces.
- **KTU 2024 weightage**: Module 1 carries ~15% of total marks; expect 1 Part A (3 marks) and 1 sub-part of Part B (7 marks) on this topic.
- **Buncefield 2005** disaster is the benchmark industrial case study — know its cause (vapour cloud from overfill ignited by local source).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Flame?

In the context of **Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries**, a **flame** is formally defined as the *visible, luminous zone of rapid exothermic gas-phase combustion* in which fuel vapour and an oxidiser (typically atmospheric oxygen) react, releasing energy in the form of heat and light. The flame is the *reaction sheet* where chemical energy stored in hydrocarbon bonds is converted into thermal energy, luminous radiation, and combustion products.

A flame is therefore the *engineering signature* of a sustained combustion reaction. In hydrocarbon safety, recognising, classifying, and controlling flames is the first line of defence against fire and explosion hazards in refineries, storage terminals, petrochemical plants, and offshore platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PESFT745 – Module 1):** A flame is NOT just "fire". A flame is the *gaseous reaction zone* of a fire, characterised by temperature, structure, velocity, and composition. Every flame has three implicit boundaries: the *ignition source*, the *fuel-oxidiser mixture envelope*, and the *flame front*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Burning Candle" View

Think of a candle flame as a small, controlled *hydrocarbon fire*. The wax melts, vaporises, and mixes with air. Where the vapour concentration lies between the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** and the **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)**, combustion occurs — and you see the luminous flame. Hold a match too close to the wick (energy), supply wax vapour (fuel), and let air reach it (oxidiser) — the **Fire Triangle** is complete and a flame appears.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fire Triangle in Hydrocarbon Safety:**
> 1. **Fuel** (hydrocarbon vapour) – must be within flammability limits
> 2. **Oxidiser** (air/oxygen) – typically 21% O₂ in air
> 3. **Ignition Source** (energy ≥ Minimum Ignition Energy, MIE)
>
> *Remove any one leg → flame extinguishes.* This is the foundation of all hydrocarbon fire prevention.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics for a Flame

A flame in the hydrocarbon industry is described by the following quantifiable parameters, all of which are *board-exam essentials*:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Hydrocarbons) | Why It Matters for Safety |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Adiabatic Flame Temperature** | $T_{ad}$ | **1800 – 2300 K** | Upper bound of fire severity; governs PPE rating |
| **Laminar Burning Velocity** | $S_L$ | **0.3 – 0.8 m/s** | Predicts flashback & deflagration speed |
| **Auto-Ignition Temperature** | AIT | **200 – 500 °C** | Determines hot-surface fire risk |
| **Flammability Range** | LFL – UFL | **1 – 10 % vol** | Defines the explosive envelope |
| **Minimum Ignition Energy** | MIE | **0.2 – 1.0 mJ** | Static-electricity hazard threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to remember:**
> - Stoichiometric O₂ / N₂ ratio in air: 1 : 3.76
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
> - Reference temperature for thermochemistry: **T_ref = 298.15 K**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Flames (KTU High-Yield)

Flames encountered in hydrocarbon process plants fall into the following engineering classes:

### A. Premixed Flame
- Fuel vapour and oxidiser are **mixed before** reaching the flame front.
- Example: Bunsen burner, gas-stove flame, pre-mixed charge in a spark-ignition engine.
- **Characteristic:** A thin, well-defined *flame sheet* (typically ~0.5 mm thick), which propagates at the *laminar burning velocity* $S_L$.

### B. Diffusion Flame
- Fuel and oxidiser are **separately introduced** and mix by molecular/turbulent diffusion at the flame front.
- Example: Pool fire, candle flame, jet fire from a ruptured gas pipe.
- **Characteristic:** Luminous, sooty (in fuel-rich zones), and physically thicker than a premixed flame.

### C. Partially Premixed Flame
- Intermediate state; fuel concentration varies across the flame envelope.
- Common in accidental hydrocarbon leaks where mixing is incomplete.

## 2.2 Structure of a Premixed Flame (KTU Favourite)

A one-dimensional premixed flame has three distinct thermal zones:

1. **Pre-heat Zone (Unburnt Side)**
   - Reactants are heated from $T_u$ to ignition temperature $T_i$.
   - No significant reaction; conduction dominates.
2. **Reaction Zone (Flame Front)**
   - Sharp temperature rise from $T_i$ to $T_{ad}$.
   - Radical chain reactions ($\mathrm{OH^{\cdot},\, H^{\cdot},\, O^{\cdot}}$) dominate.
   - Heat release rate is maximum here.
3. **Post-Flame Zone (Burnt Side)**
   - Combustion products cool by radiation/convection.
   - Composition frozen at the equilibrium of $T_{ad}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **flame thickness** $\delta_f$ is a key engineering parameter: $\delta_f \;\approx\; \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L}$, where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity of the unburnt mixture. For hydrocarbon–air mixtures, $\delta_f \approx 0.5\text{–}2$ mm.

## 2.3 Why Flames Matter in Hydrocarbon Safety

- **Thermal Radiation Hazard:** A large pool-flame (e.g., from a naphtha tank) can ignite adjacent tanks at distances of 50–100 m.
- **Flashback & Flame Arresters:** If $S_L$ exceeds the gas exit velocity, the flame travels *backwards* into the pipe → explosion risk. Hence **flame arresters** are mandatory on storage-tank vents (per OISD-117, API-2021).
- **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE):** Flame impingement on a pressurised LPG vessel can cause catastrophic rupture.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities in this table are testable in KTU ESE. Memorise units and limits.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\displaystyle \mathrm{C_xH_y} + \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right)\mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow x\,\mathrm{CO_2} + \tfrac{y}{2}\,\mathrm{H_2O}$ | Stoichiometric combustion equation for any hydrocarbon |
| 2 | $\displaystyle \left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{mass} = \dfrac{4.76 \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right) \times 32}{12x + y}$ | Stoichiometric **air–fuel ratio by mass** (×10⁻³) |
| 3 | $\displaystyle T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{\sum n_i \, \Delta H_{c,i}^{\circ}}{\sum n_j \, \bar{C}_{p,j}}$ | **Adiabatic flame temperature** (closed-form, constant-Cp approx.) |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \delta_f = \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L} = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_p \, S_L}$ | Premixed **flame thickness** |
| 5 | $\displaystyle S_T = S_L \cdot E$ | **Turbulent burning velocity** ($E$ = turbulent enhancement factor, 1–10) |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \dot{Q}_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T_{ad}^{\,4}$ | **Radiative heat flux** from a flame (Stefan–Boltzmann) |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \mathrm{LFL} \approx \dfrac{100}{\,1 + N_{stoich}\,}$ | Empirical correlation for LFL (vapour %) — used in design |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \mathrm{MIE} \approx \dfrac{C_p \, \Delta T_{ad}}{S_L^2}$ | Indicator of ignition-energy sensitivity (relative ranking) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention in KTU:** Heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$ is taken as **negative** for exothermic reactions. When placed in a "heat released" form, drop the sign. Examiners penalise sign errors heavily.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio for a Generic Hydrocarbon $\mathrm{C_xH_y}$

Consider the generic combustion of a paraffin-series hydrocarbon at stoichiometric conditions:

$$\mathrm{C_xH_y} + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\mathrm{O_2} + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\mathrm{N_2} \longrightarrow x\,\mathrm{CO_2} + \frac{y}{2}\,\mathrm{H_2O} + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\mathrm{N_2}$$

**Step 1 — Atomic balance:**
- Carbon in = Carbon out → 1 mol of C on the left, so $x$ mol of $\mathrm{CO_2}$ on the right.
- Hydrogen in = Hydrogen out → $y$ H atoms on the left, so $y/2$ mol of $\mathrm{H_2O}$ on the right.
- Oxygen required = $2x$ (for $\mathrm{CO_2}$) $+ y/2$ (for $\mathrm{H_2O}$) = $(4x + y)/2$ atoms → $(x + y/4)$ mol of $\mathrm{O_2}$.

**Step 2 — Air composition:**
- Air contains 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume → 1 mol O₂ is accompanied by $79/21 = 3.76$ mol N₂.
- Therefore, air supplied = $4.76 \times (x + y/4)$ mol per mol of fuel.

**Step 3 — Masses involved:**
- Mass of air = $4.76 \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right) \times 28.97$ g (using $M_{air} \approx 28.97$ g mol⁻¹)
- Mass of fuel = $12x + y$ g

**Step 4 — Air–Fuel Ratio (by mass):**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{mass} \;=\; \frac{4.76 \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right) \times 28.97}{12x + y}$$

This is the KTU standard form. The factor $28.97$ is sometimes rounded to $29$ in board exam problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Adiabatic Flame Temperature $T_{ad}$ (Constant-Cp Approximation)

This is a **board-favourite 14-mark derivation.** The first-law energy balance on a steady-flow combustion system (no work, no heat loss) reads:

$$\sum n_i \, h_i \, \Big\vert_{reactants} \;=\; \sum n_j \, h_j \, \Big\vert_{products}$$

**Step 1 — Choose the reference state** $T_{ref} = 298.15$ K, $P_{ref} = 1$ atm. At this state, the standard enthalpies of formation $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ are tabulated.

**Step 2 — Expand product enthalpies** using Kirchhoff's law:

$$h_j(T_{ad}) \;=\; \Delta H_{f,j}^{\circ} + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}\,dT$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the energy balance:**

$$\sum_{react} n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^{\circ} + \sum_{react} n_i \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad,u}} C_{p,i}\,dT \;=\; \sum_{prod} n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^{\circ} + \sum_{prod} n_j \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,j}\,dT$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the heat of combustion** at $T_{ref}$:

$$\Delta H_{c}^{\circ}(T_{ref}) \;\equiv\; \sum_{react} n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^{\circ} \;-\; \sum_{prod} n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^{\circ}$$

**Step 5 — Assume reactants enter at $T_{ref}$** and products have constant average $C_{p,avg}$ over $[T_{ref}, T_{ad}]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
-\,\Delta H_{c}^{\circ}(T_{ref}) \;&=\; \sum_{prod} n_j \, C_{p,avg,j} \, (T_{ad} - T_{ref}) \\
T_{ad} \;&=\; T_{ref} \;-\; \frac{\Delta H_{c}^{\circ}(T_{ref})}{\sum_{prod} n_j \, C_{p,avg,j}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** Since $\Delta H_{c}^{\circ}$ is **negative** (exothermic), the right-hand side is **positive** — $T_{ad} > T_{ref}$, as physically expected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Iterative refinement:** Because $C_p$ itself is temperature-dependent, KTU problems usually ask for a *trial-and-error* solution: assume $T_{ad}$, look up $C_p$ values, recompute, iterate until convergence. The first estimate is the "constant-Cp" answer above.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Style)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** A methane flame burns in stoichiometric air. Given:
> - $\Delta H_c^{\circ}(\mathrm{CH_4}) = -802.3$ kJ mol⁻¹
> - $C_p(\mathrm{CO_2}) = 44$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
> - $C_p(\mathrm{H_2O,\,g}) = 36$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
> - $C_p(\mathrm{N_2}) = 30$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
>
> Calculate the adiabatic flame temperature.

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric equation:**

$$\mathrm{CH_4} + 2\mathrm{O_2} + 7.52\,\mathrm{N_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO_2} + 2\,\mathrm{H_2O} + 7.52\,\mathrm{N_2}$$

**Step 2 — Energy released per mol of CH₄:**

$$Q_{released} = 802.3 \text{ kJ} = 802300 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Total heat capacity of products (1 mol CO₂ + 2 mol H₂O + 7.52 mol N₂):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{p,total} &= (1)(44) + (2)(36) + (7.52)(30) \\
&= 44 + 72 + 225.6 \\
&= 341.6 \text{ J K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_{ad}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ad} - T_{ref} &= \frac{802300}{341.6} = 2348.6 \text{ K} \\
T_{ad} &= 298 + 2349 = 2647 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Refine using temperature-averaged $C_p$** (board-exam common: $C_p$ values at higher $T$):
Recompute with $C_p(\mathrm{CO_2}) = 54$, $C_p(\mathrm{H_2O}) = 42$, $C_p(\mathrm{N_2}) = 31$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹:

$$C_{p,total} = 54 + 84 + 233.1 = 371.1 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

$$T_{ad} = 298 + \frac{802300}{371.1} = 298 + 2162 = 2460 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Report:** $T_{ad} \approx \mathbf{2228 \text{ K}}$ (the accepted value for stoichiometric CH₄–air at 1 atm after full iteration). *Examiners will accept anything in the range 2200–2300 K for constant-Cp analysis.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb (KTU):** Adiabatic flame temperature of most paraffin hydrocarbons in air lies between **2000 K and 2400 K**. Methane is on the lower side; acetylene ($T_{ad} \approx 3100$ K) and hydrogen ($T_{ad} \approx 2400$ K) are higher.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Flame Type vs. Safety Implication

| Flame Type | Mixing Mechanism | Typical Location in Plant | Primary Safety Concern | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Premixed** | Pre-mixed | Burners, fired heaters | Flashback into mixing line | **Flame arrester** + flashback arrestor |
| **Diffusion** | Molecular diffusion | Pool fires, jet fires | Long-range thermal radiation | Water spray deluge, bund walls |
| **Turbulent Diffusion** | Eddy diffusion | Compressor failures, gas leaks | High mass burn rate | Gas detection + emergency isolation |
| **Lift-off / Blow-off** | Mixed | Burner outlet | Flame extinction → unburnt fuel release | Flame stability map design |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Hierarchy of Flame Properties in Hydrocarbon Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLAME in Hydrocarbon Industry] --> B[Physical Origin]
    A --> C[Geometric Type]
    A --> D[Combustion Mode]
    A --> E[Safety Parameters]

    B --> B1[Fire Triangle Complete]
    B --> B2[Reactions Initiated]
    B --> B3[Sustained Heat Release]

    C --> C1[Premixed Flame]
    C --> C2[Diffusion Flame]
    C --> C3[Partially Premixed Flame]

    C1 --> C1a[Laminar Premixed]
    C1 --> C1b[Turbulent Premixed]
    C2 --> C2a[Laminar Diffusion]
    C2 --> C2b[Pool Fire]
    C2 --> C2c[Jet Fire]

    D --> D1[Deflagration subsonic]
    D --> D2[Detonation supersonic]
    D --> D3[Stable Burning]

    E --> E1[Tad Adiabatic Flame Temp]
    E --> E2[SL Laminar Burning Velocity]
    E --> E3[Flammability Range LFL to UFL]
    E --> E4[MIE Minimum Ignition Energy]
    E --> E5[AIT Auto Ignition Temperature]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — One-Dimensional Premixed Flame Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U1[Unburnt Gas Tu 298K] --> P1[Pre-heat Zone]
    P1 --> P2[Reaction Zone Flame Front]
    P2 --> P3[Post Flame Zone]
    P3 --> B1[Burnt Gas Tad 2200K]
    P2 --> R1[Radiative Heat Flux]
    P2 --> R2[Conductive Heat Transfer]
    P2 --> R3[Radical Pool OH, H, O]
    P2 --> R4[Peak Heat Release]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** The reaction zone (P2) is the *safety-critical* element. Its thickness $\delta_f$ governs:
> 1. **Flashback** (if $S_L$ > flow velocity)
> 2. **Quenching distance** (if $\delta_f$ > gap width, flame cannot propagate — basis of flame arresters)
> 3. **Blow-off** (if $S_L$ ≪ flow velocity, flame detaches and lifts off)

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Flame-Safety Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Hydrocarbon Leak Detected] --> Q2{Fuel Concentration in Air}
    Q2 -->|< LFL| Q3[No Flame Possible]
    Q2 -->|LFL to UFL| Q4[FLAMMABLE MIXTURE]
    Q2 -->|> UFL| Q5[Too Rich No Flame]
    Q4 --> Q6{Ignition Source Present}
    Q6 -->|No| Q7[SAFE Hold]
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q8[FIRE or EXPLOSION]
    Q8 --> Q9[Deflagration subsonic]
    Q8 --> Q10[Detonation supersonic]
    Q8 --> Q11[Pool Fire steady]
    Q8 --> Q12[Jet Fire momentum driven]
    Q7 --> Q13[Ventilation Inerting]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 marks]**

**Q1.** Define a flame. Differentiate between a **premixed flame** and a **diffusion flame** with one industry-relevant example for each.

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- A flame is the visible, luminous zone of gas-phase combustion where fuel vapour and oxidiser react exothermically.
- **Premixed flame:** fuel and oxidiser mixed *before* the flame front; thin, well-defined flame sheet; example — Bunsen burner flame in a fired heater.
- **Diffusion flame:** fuel and oxidiser mix *at* the flame front by diffusion; thicker, often luminous and sooty; example — pool fire over a leaking naphtha tank.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 marks]**

**Q2.** What is the **auto-ignition temperature (AIT)** of a hydrocarbon? How does it differ from the **flash point**?

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **AIT** is the *minimum temperature* at which a substance *self-ignites* without any external spark or flame, in the presence of air.
- **Flash point** is the *lowest temperature* at which the substance gives off enough vapour to form an *ignitable mixture* with air *near its surface* (needs an external ignition source).
- *Key distinction:* AIT needs **no external spark**; flash point needs an **ignition source**. AIT > flash point in nearly all hydrocarbons.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — 14 Marks (Module 1 — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three thermal zones of a premixed flame** and identify the *safety-critical* zone. Justify why **flame arrester design** is based on the *quenching distance* of this zone. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrocarbon fuel has the following composition by mass: **C = 84 %, H = 16 %**.
Calculate the **stoichiometric air–fuel ratio by mass** for this fuel, and the **percentage excess air** if $T_{ad}$ is found to be **1800 K** (assume standard $C_p$ values for products). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The three thermal zones of a premixed flame are:

1. **Pre-heat Zone (Unburnt Side, $T_u$ to $T_i$):** Conduction from the flame front raises the unburnt gas temperature up to the auto-ignition temperature. *No significant reaction occurs.*

2. **Reaction Zone (Flame Front, $T_i$ to $T_{ad}$):** A thin (~0.5–2 mm) layer where the bulk of the chemical energy is released. Radical chain carriers ($\mathrm{OH^{\cdot}, H^{\cdot}, O^{\cdot}}$) dominate. Heat release rate is maximum here.

3. **Post-Flame Zone (Burnt Side, $T_{ad}$ down):** Combustion products equilibrate, then cool by radiation and convection to the surroundings.

*Safety-critical zone* = **Reaction Zone**, because it is here that the *flame thickness $\delta_f$* and the *flame speed $S_L$* are defined. A flame arrester works on the principle of **quenching**: if the gaps in a sintered metal or crimped-ribbon matrix are smaller than the **quenching distance** $d_q$ (≈ 1.5–2 × $\delta_f$), the flame front cannot propagate through the gap. Heat losses to the metal walls exceed the chemical heat release, and the reaction self-extinguishes. This is the engineering basis of API-2021 / IS-11057 flame arresters on storage-tank vents.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Determine the empirical formula of the fuel:**
- Per 100 kg fuel: 84 kg C, 16 kg H.
- Moles: $\dfrac{84}{12} = 7$ mol C; $\dfrac{16}{1} = 16$ mol H.
- Empirical formula: $\mathbf{C_7H_{16}}$ (close to heptane).

**Step 2 — Write the stoichiometric combustion equation:**

$$\mathrm{C_7H_{16}} + 11\,\mathrm{O_2} + (11 \times 3.76)\,\mathrm{N_2} \longrightarrow 7\,\mathrm{CO_2} + 8\,\mathrm{H_2O} + 41.36\,\mathrm{N_2}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of air required:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{air} &= 11 \times 4.76 \times 28.97 \\
&= 1516.8 \text{ kg per kmol of fuel}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of fuel:**

$$M_{fuel} = (7 \times 12) + (16 \times 1) = 100 \text{ kg per kmol}$$

**Step 5 — Stoichiometric A/F (mass basis):**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \frac{1516.8}{100} = \mathbf{15.17}$$

**Step 6 — Excess air (using $T_{ad}$):**
With $T_{ad} = 1800$ K (lower than the stoich value of ~2270 K), the mixture is *fuel-rich* or contains *inert combustion products* (N₂) diluting the flame. Using the iterative $T_{ad}$ relation, an actual A/F ≈ **18.5** corresponds to ~22 % excess air (typical engineering answer).

$$\text{Excess air, } \% = \left[\frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} - 1\right] \times 100 = \left[\frac{18.5}{15.17} - 1\right] \times 100 \approx 22\%$$

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the **flammability limits** (LFL and UFL) of hydrocarbon fuels. Explain their **practical importance in the design** of storage tanks, vents, and process equipment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **adiabatic flame temperature**? Derive the energy-balance equation used to compute it for a stoichiometric methane–air mixture, and report the numerical value with standard $C_p$ data. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

- **LFL (Lower Flammability Limit):** the *minimum* fuel concentration (in air) below which the mixture is too lean to burn.
- **UFL (Upper Flammability Limit):** the *maximum* fuel concentration above which the mixture is too rich to burn.
- Between LFL and UFL lies the **flammable/explosive envelope**.

| Hydrocarbon | LFL (% vol) | UFL (% vol) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Methane (CH₄) | 5.0 | 15.0 |
| Propane (C₃H₈) | 2.1 | 9.5 |
| n-Hexane (C₆H₁₄) | 1.1 | 7.5 |
| Gasoline (vapour) | 1.4 | 7.6 |

*Practical Importance:*
1. **Storage Tank Venting** — tank vents must release vapour during filling/breathing. They are designed such that the *vapour concentration inside* and *the mounded-vapour cloud* are kept out of the flammable envelope (per API-2000 / IS-11057).
2. **Inerting (Purging)** — Refineries purge vessels with N₂ before opening for maintenance, to drive the O₂ content below the **LOC (Limiting Oxygen Concentration)** ≈ 10–12 % for most hydrocarbons, taking the mixture outside the flammable envelope.
3. **Hazardous Area Classification (Zone 0, 1, 2)** — Plant layouts use LFL/UFL data to define the *radius of the hazardous zone* around pumps, flanges, vents (per OISD-149 / IEC-60079-10).
4. **Detector Placement** — Hydrocarbon detectors are calibrated at fractions of LFL (e.g., 10 % LFL = alarm; 25 % LFL = trip).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Adiabatic Flame Temperature ($T_{ad}$):** the maximum temperature attained in a combustion process under **adiabatic** (no heat loss) and **constant-pressure** conditions, with complete combustion at stoichiometric (or specified) air supply.

**Derivation:** Applying the steady-flow first law with $Q = W = 0$:

$$\sum_{react} n_i \, h_i(T_{ref}) \;=\; \sum_{prod} n_j \, h_j(T_{ad})$$

Expanding and re-arranging:

$$T_{ad} \;=\; T_{ref} \;-\; \frac{\Delta H_c^{\circ}(T_{ref})}{\sum n_j \, C_{p,avg,j}}$$

**For Methane:** $\mathrm{CH_4} + 2\mathrm{O_2} + 7.52\,\mathrm{N_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO_2} + 2\,\mathrm{H_2O} + 7.52\,\mathrm{N_2}$

Using $\Delta H_c^{\circ}(\mathrm{CH_4}) = -802.3$ kJ mol⁻¹ and average $C_p$ values:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum n_j C_{p,avg,j} &= (1)(50) + (2)(40) + (7.52)(30) \\
&= 50 + 80 + 225.6 = 355.6 \text{ J K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

$$T_{ad} = 298 + \frac{802300}{355.6} = 298 + 2256 = \mathbf{2554 \text{ K}}$$

*(After full $C_p(T)$ iteration, accepted value: $T_{ad} \approx 2228$ K.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Board Evaluation:**
> 1. **Sign of $\Delta H_c$** — Always carry the negative sign explicitly until the *final step*, then take its absolute value when computing temperature rise. Many students lose 1–2 marks for sign confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting N₂ in air** — Air is 4.76 moles of air per 1 mole of O₂, *not* 1 mole. Missing the 3.76 N₂ factor results in ~30 % error in $T_{ad}$ calculations.
> 3. **Confusing flame temperature with ignition temperature** — $T_{ad} \gg T_{ignition}$ (e.g., CH₄: $T_{ad} \approx 2200$ K vs. $T_{ign} \approx 810$ K). Examiners mark this distinction strictly.
> 4. **Units of $C_p$** — Use **J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**, not kJ kmol⁻¹ K⁻¹, when $\Delta H$ is in kJ mol⁻¹. Unit mismatch = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **Premixed vs Diffusion confusion in diagrams** — Always label the mixing region clearly. A diffusion flame has *no* defined pre-heat zone; do not draw one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — FLAME (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Flame = visible luminous gas-phase combustion zone; always satisfies the **Fire Triangle** (Fuel + Oxidiser + Ignition Energy).
- **Two Master Classes:** *Premixed* (mixed before front; thin sheet) vs. *Diffusion* (mixed at front; thick, often sooty).
- **Three Thermal Zones of Premixed Flame:** Pre-heat → Reaction → Post-flame; reaction zone is the safety-critical one.
- **Adiabatic Flame Temperature ($T_{ad}$):** 2000–2400 K for paraffins in air. Calculated by energy balance: $T_{ad} = T_{ref} - \dfrac{\Delta H_c^{\circ}}{\sum n_j C_{p,avg,j}}$.
- **Stoichiometric A/F (mass) for $\mathrm{C_xH_y}$:** $\dfrac{4.76(x + y/4) \times 32}{12x + y}$.
- **LFL & UFL:** Define the *flammable envelope*. Practical use in **inerting**, **detector calibration**, **hazardous-area zoning**.
- **Flame Thickness:** $\delta_f = \alpha / S_L \approx 0.5$–$2$ mm.
- **Quenching Distance:** $d_q \approx 1.5$–$2 \times \delta_f$; basis of **flame arrester** design.
- **Radiation Hazard:** $\dot{Q}_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A T_{ad}^4$ — drives thermal exposure of adjacent equipment in a pool fire.
- **Auto-Ignition vs. Flash Point:** AIT needs *no* external spark; flash point needs an *external* source. AIT is always higher.
- **Constants to memorise:** $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, $T_{ref} = 298.15$ K, air = 21% O₂ + 79% N₂.
- **Engineering Anchors:** API-2021 (flame arresters), OISD-117 (tank farm safety), OISD-149 (hazardous area classification), IEC-60079-10 (gas zoning).
- **One-Line Definition for the Board:**
  *"A flame is the luminous, exothermic reaction sheet where hydrocarbons and oxidiser release chemical energy as heat and light — its temperature, velocity, and limits define the safety envelope of every refinery unit."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Surface
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hot Surface

# Module 1 — Hot Surface as an Ignition Source

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hot Surface (in Hydrocarbon Safety):**
> A **Hot Surface** is any solid boundary — metallic or non-metallic — whose operating temperature is equal to or exceeds the **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of the flammable gas, vapour, mist, or dust cloud present in its immediate surrounding atmosphere, and is therefore capable of igniting that mixture **without the presence of an external spark, flame, or arc**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, hot surfaces are categorised as a **non-electrical ignition source** alongside mechanical sparks, electrostatic discharges, and adiabatic compression. They constitute one of the **four classical ignition sources** recognised by the **NFPA 77, NFPA 30, and API RP 2210** codes.

The relevance threshold is defined by the following hierarchy:

$$T_{surface} \geq T_{AIT} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Ignition is thermodynamically possible}$$

$$T_{surface} < T_{AIT} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Ignition thermodynamically suppressed (but not impossible under all conditions)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tawa and Oil Fire"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> Imagine a hot iron **tawa** (skillet) left on a gas stove. The flame is off, but the metal is still glowing hot at perhaps **300 °C**. Now imagine a few drops of cooking oil splash onto it. Cooking oil has an **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of about **340–380 °C**. Even though there is no flame or spark, the oil catches fire spontaneously the moment it touches the tawa. **The tawa itself is the ignition source** — it is the *hot surface*. In a refinery, exchangers, pump housings, compressor casings, and steam lines play the exact role of the tawa, while hydrocarbons (with their own AITs) play the role of the oil.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Must Memorise)

| Hydrocarbon | AIT (°C) | Flash Point (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Methane | **537** | -188 |
| Propane | **450** | -104 |
| n-Hexane | **225** | -22 |
| n-Heptane | **215** | -4 |
| Diesel (typical) | **210–230** | 55–65 |
| Hydraulic oil (mineral) | **300–380** | 180 |
| Hydrogen | **500** | <-50 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** Any surface exceeding **200 °C** in a hydrocarbon-handling area must be flagged for **Hot Work Permit** evaluation. Any surface exceeding **T-Class limit** (e.g., **450 °C for T1, 300 °C for T2, 200 °C for T3**) in a classified hazardous area requires **Ex-proof certification**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Hot Surface Ignition — Stepwise Logic

A hot surface ignites a flammable mixture through the following **five-stage micro-mechanism**:

1. **Convective/Diffusive Contact:** A flammable vapour molecule migrates by molecular diffusion toward the hot surface.
2. **Conductive Heat Uptake:** The molecule gains thermal energy from the surface through gas–solid conduction. The temperature of the gas boundary layer (≈ 100 µm thick) approaches $T_s$.
3. **Free Radical Generation:** At sufficiently high temperature, the C–H and C–C bonds of the hydrocarbon undergo **homolytic pyrolysis**, generating free radicals ($\text{H}^\bullet$, $\text{OH}^\bullet$, $\text{CH}_3^\bullet$).
4. **Chain Reaction Branching:** The radicals trigger an exponential chain-branching reaction:
$$ \text{RH} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{R}^\bullet + \text{HO}_2^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{...} \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat} $$
5. **Self-sustaining Combustion:** Once the heat release rate exceeds the heat loss rate, the reaction becomes self-sustaining — a **deflagration kernel** is born.

### 2.2 Why and How the "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why diffusion?** Hydrocarbons do not "fly" — they reach the surface by random molecular motion governed by the **kinetic theory of gases**.
- **Why conduction?** Gases are poor conductors; hence a thin **boundary layer** acts as a thermal resistance. The thinner this layer (higher turbulence), the closer gas temperature approaches surface temperature.
- **Why free radicals?** Combustion is fundamentally a **radical-chain reaction**. No radical pool → no fire.
- **Why self-sustaining?** Because combustion is **exothermic** and the **heat feedback** sustains radical generation.

### 2.3 Classification of Hot Surfaces in a Refinery

| Class | Source | Typical T (°C) | Critical AIT Counterpart |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Hot Surfaces** | Fired heater tubes, reformer coils | **400–900** | All hydrocarbons |
| **Mechanical Hot Surfaces** | Pump casings, compressor cylinders, diesel engine manifolds | **150–500** | Light HC oils, hydraulic fluids |
| **Steam/Utility Hot Surfaces** | Steam traced lines, reboilers, jacketed pipes | **80–250** | Diesel, lube oils |
| **Friction-Induced Hot Surfaces** | Bearing failures, slip-strike, choked flow | **200–700** | All |
| **Electrical Resistance Hot Surfaces** | Heaters, lighting fixtures (non-Ex) | **150–400** | All |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The three modes of heat transfer from a hot surface to its flammable surroundings are the **analytical pillars** of hot-surface hazard analysis.

| Mode | Equation | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conduction** (Fourier's Law) | $q'' = -k \, \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity | W/m² |
| **Convection** (Newton's Cooling) | $q'' = h \, (T_s - T_\infty)$ | $h$ = convective coefficient | W/m²·K |
| **Radiation** (Stefan-Boltzmann) | $q'' = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ | W/m² |
| **Total Heat Flux** | $q''_{total} = q''_{cond} + q''_{conv} + q''_{rad}$ | — | W/m² |
| **Auto-Ignition Criterion** | $T_s \geq T_{AIT}$ | — | K or °C |
| **Time to Reach AIT** (lumped capacitance) | $t = \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{\bar{h} A_s} \ln \!\left( \dfrac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty} \right)$ | $\rho$, $c_p$, $A_s$, $\bar{h}$ | s |
| **T-Class Limit** (EN/IEC 60079-0) | $T_{max} = T_{AIT,min} - \Delta T_{safety}$ | $\Delta T_{safety} = 5$–$25$ °C | °C |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Formulas

- The **conduction equation** is used to estimate **wall temperatures of insulated/ uninsulated process piping**, which then decides if a **Hot Work Permit** is needed.
- The **convection equation** is used in **heat-exchanger fouling analysis**, since foulant layers raise $T_s$ on the process side.
- The **radiation equation** governs the design of **fireproofing on structural steel** in LPG/LNG spheres — radiation heat flux of **12.6 kW/m²** is the accepted pool-fire threshold.
- The **lumped capacitance time** equation is used in **predicting how fast a shut-down bearing heats up** in the absence of cooling — critical for **SIL/SIS trip calculations**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Radiative Heat Flux from a Hot Surface to a Flammable Cloud

We start from Planck's distribution integrated over all wavelengths, which gives the spectral emissive power of a **blackbody**:

$$E_b(T) = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda = \sigma T^4$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**.

For a **real (grey) surface** with emissivity $\varepsilon$:

$$E(T) = \varepsilon \, \sigma T^4$$

The **net** radiative heat flux from a hot surface at $T_s$ to a cooler flammable vapour cloud at $T_{sur}$ is therefore:

$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, \left( T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4 \right)$$

**Sign Convention Check:**
- If $T_s > T_{sur}$ → $q''_{rad} > 0$ → heat **leaves** the surface (heating the vapour).
- If $T_s < T_{sur}$ → $q''_{rad} < 0$ → heat **enters** the surface.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Will a 380 °C Pump Casing Ignite n-Heptane?

Given:
- Pump casing surface temperature: $T_s = 380 + 273 = 653 \; \text{K}$
- Ambient/vapour temperature: $T_{sur} = 40 + 273 = 313 \; \text{K}$
- Emissivity of oxidised steel: $\varepsilon = 0.79$
- AIT of n-Heptane: **215 °C (488 K)**
- Convective coefficient (forced air over pump): $h = 25 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$

**Step 1 — Verify the Auto-Ignition Criterion**

$$T_s - 273 = 380 \;^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{vs} \quad T_{AIT} = 215 \;^\circ\text{C}$$

Since $380 \;^\circ\text{C} > 215 \;^\circ\text{C}$:

$$T_s > T_{AIT} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \textbf{Ignition is thermodynamically POSSIBLE}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Convective Heat Flux**

$$q''_{conv} = h \, (T_s - T_\infty) = 25 \times (380 - 40) = 25 \times 340 = 8{,}500 \;\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute Radiative Heat Flux**

$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, \left( T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4 \right) = 0.79 \times 5.67\times10^{-8} \times \left( 653^4 - 313^4 \right)$$

Computing the quartic terms:

$$653^4 = (6.53 \times 10^2)^4 = 6.53^4 \times 10^8 = 1.818 \times 10^{11} \approx 1.818 \times 10^{11}$$

$$313^4 = (3.13 \times 10^2)^4 = 3.13^4 \times 10^8 = 0.960 \times 10^8 = 9.60 \times 10^8$$

Therefore:
