$$\Delta = 1.818 \times 10^{11} - 9.60 \times 10^8 = 1.818 \times 10^{11} - 0.0096 \times 10^{11} = 1.8084 \times 10^{11}$$

$$q''_{rad} = 0.79 \times 5.67\times10^{-8} \times 1.8084 \times 10^{11}$$

$$q''_{rad} = 0.79 \times 5.67 \times 180.84 = 0.79 \times 1025.4 = 810.1 \;\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total Heat Flux Reaching the Flammable Layer**

$$q''_{total} = q''_{conv} + q''_{rad} = 8500 + 810.1 \approx 9{,}310 \;\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Time Required to Heat a 5 mm oil film to AIT**

Using the lumped capacitance formula for a thin oil slick (mass $m = 0.5 \; \text{kg}$, $c_p = 2000 \; \text{J/kg·K}$, $A_s = 0.1 \; \text{m}^2$):

$$t = \frac{m \, c_p}{h_{eff} \, A_s} \ln \!\left( \frac{T_s - T_i}{T_s - T_{AIT}} \right)$$

Assume $T_i = 40 \;^\circ\text{C}$, and $h_{eff} \approx 50 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ (gas-side convection):

$$t = \frac{0.5 \times 2000}{50 \times 0.1} \ln \!\left( \frac{380 - 40}{380 - 215} \right)$$

$$t = \frac{1000}{5} \ln \!\left( \frac{340}{165} \right) = 200 \times \ln(2.06) = 200 \times 0.723 = 144.6 \;\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 2.4 \; \text{minutes}}$$

**Conclusion:** A pump at 380 °C will ignite a **5 mm n-Heptane film on its casing within ~2.4 minutes** if the casing is not cleaned — justifying the KTU/NFPA mandate for **regular casing cleaning and temperature monitoring**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hot-Surface Ignition Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Source Operates] --> B[Surface Temp Ts exceeds 200 C]
    B --> C{HC Vapour Present?}
    C -- No --> D[No Hazard]
    C -- Yes --> E[Gas Contacts Surface]
    E --> F[Boundary Layer Heats Up]
    F --> G[Pyrolysis of HC Molecules]
    G --> H[Free Radical Pool Forms]
    H --> I{Heat Release > Heat Loss?}
    I -- No --> J[Reaction Quenches]
    I -- Yes --> K[Deflagration Kernel Born]
    K --> L[Self Sustaining Flame]
    L --> M[Fire / Explosion Event]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Engineering Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Eliminate HC] --> B1[Substitute Fluid]
    B1 --> C1[Ventilation Control]
    C1 --> D1[Surface Temp Monitoring]
    D1 --> E1[Insulation Jacketing]
    E1 --> F1[Explosion Proof Enclosure]
    F1 --> G1[Hot Work Permit System]
    G1 --> H1[Emergency Isolation ESD]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid T-Class Temperature Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Temperature Class Selection] --> B[Determine Minimum AIT of HC]
    B --> C[Apply Safety Margin Delta T]
    C --> D{Match IEC 60079 T Class}
    D --> E1[T1 Max 450 C]
    D --> E2[T2 Max 300 C]
    D --> E3[T3 Max 200 C]
    D --> E4[T4 Max 135 C]
    D --> E5[T5 Max 100 C]
    D --> E6[T6 Max 85 C]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Heat Transfer Modes from a Pump Casing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pump Casing at Ts 380 C] --> B[Conduction q1 through casing wall]
    A --> C[Convection q2 to ambient air]
    A --> D[Radiation q3 to vapour cloud]
    B --> E[Heat dissipated to ground]
    C --> F[Heat carried by air]
    D --> G[Heat absorbed by HC vapour]
    G --> H{Vapour reaches AIT?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Ignition]
    H -- No --> J[Heat rejected]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define hot surface in the context of hydrocarbon safety. Why is it classified as a non-electrical ignition source?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hot surface** in hydrocarbon safety is any solid surface whose temperature is at or above the **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of the surrounding flammable gas, vapour, or mist, thereby capable of igniting that mixture **without any external flame, spark, or arc** (1 mark).

It is classified as a **non-electrical ignition source** because it does not require an electrical circuit, current, or voltage to initiate combustion — the ignition is purely a **thermal/chemical** phenomenon driven by the surface temperature alone (1 mark).

Typical examples in a refinery include **pump casings, compressor cylinders, fired-heater tubes, bearing housings, and steam-traced lines** (1 mark).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between flash point and auto-ignition temperature. Why is AIT more relevant than flash point for hot-surface hazard analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Flash Point | Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Minimum temperature at which a liquid gives off **enough vapour** to form an ignitable mixture **near a flame/spark** | Minimum temperature at which the vapour **spontaneously ignites** without any external ignition source |
| Requires external source? | **Yes** (flame/spark needed) | **No** |
| Relevance to hot surface | Low — surface alone won't ignite at flash point | **High** — surface temperature ≥ AIT alone causes ignition |

AIT is more relevant for hot-surface hazard analysis because **hot surfaces do not provide sparks or flames**; they only provide **heat**. Hence, the relevant thermodynamic threshold is the **AIT**, not the flash point (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **mechanism of hot-surface ignition** of a hydrocarbon vapour. Discuss the **role of free radicals** and the **boundary layer** in the ignition process. (7 marks)

**(b)** A horizontal **heat-exchanger shell** carries hot oil at 290 °C. The shell outer surface is bare (uninsulated) steel with emissivity 0.78, and the surrounding ambient is 35 °C. A small leak of **n-Hexane** vapour (AIT = 225 °C) forms a cloud around the shell. **(i)** Verify whether ignition is thermodynamically possible. **(ii)** Compute the convective and radiative heat fluxes from the shell. **(iii)** Comment on the **practical hazard**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define Hot-Surface Ignition (2 marks)**

Hot-surface ignition is the spontaneous initiation of combustion when a flammable gas/vapour contacts a solid surface whose temperature meets or exceeds the AIT of that vapour, without any external flame or spark. The phenomenon is governed by **thermal conduction from the solid to the gas boundary layer**, followed by **homolytic bond cleavage** of the hydrocarbon molecules.

**Step 2 — Sketch of Mechanism (2 marks)**

A neat block diagram should show:
- Hot surface → Boundary layer (≈100 µm) → HC vapour → Reaction zone → Flame kernel.

**Step 3 — Role of Free Radicals (1.5 marks)**

Free radicals ($\text{H}^\bullet$, $\text{OH}^\bullet$, $\text{CH}_3^\bullet$, $\text{HO}_2^\bullet$) are generated by **pyrolysis** at high temperature. They are the **chain carriers** that propagate the oxidation reaction. The branching step:
$$ \text{H}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{OH}^\bullet + \text{O}^\bullet $$
doubles the radical pool, making the reaction **self-accelerating** until ignition.

**Step 4 — Role of Boundary Layer (1.5 marks)**

The boundary layer is a thin stagnant film of gas adhering to the surface. Heat must **conduct** through this layer (Fourier conduction) before the vapour reaches AIT. The **thinner the boundary layer** (higher turbulence, higher velocity), the **faster** the vapour reaches AIT. Conversely, stagnant air (low ventilation) traps the heat and increases hazard.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $T_s = 290 + 273 = 563 \; \text{K}$
- $T_\infty = 35 + 273 = 308 \; \text{K}$
- $T_{AIT} = 225 + 273 = 498 \; \text{K}$
- $\varepsilon = 0.78$
- $h = 15 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ (natural convection over horizontal cylinder)

**Step (i) — Ignition Criterion (2 marks)**

$$T_s = 290 \;^\circ\text{C} \quad ; \quad T_{AIT} = 225 \;^\circ\text{C}$$

Since $T_s > T_{AIT}$ → **Ignition is thermodynamically possible.** (2 marks)

**Step (ii) — Heat Flux Calculation (3 marks)**

Convective:
$$q''_{conv} = h (T_s - T_\infty) = 15 \times (290 - 35) = 15 \times 255 = 3{,}825 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

Radiative:
$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$
$$563^4 = 1.005 \times 10^{11} \; ; \; 308^4 = 9.0 \times 10^8$$
$$\Delta = 1.005\times10^{11} - 9.0\times10^8 = 0.996 \times 10^{11}$$
$$q''_{rad} = 0.78 \times 5.67\times10^{-8} \times 9.96\times10^{10} = 0.78 \times 564.7 = 440.5 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

Total:
$$q''_{total} = 3825 + 440.5 \approx 4{,}266 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step (iii) — Practical Hazard (2 marks)**

The heat flux of **~4.3 kW/m²** is more than sufficient to raise any condensed n-Hexane film (or impinging vapour) above its **AIT in seconds**. **Bare hot piping in a hydrocarbon zone is a serious ignition hazard.** The remedial action is **insulation jacketing** (reduces $T_s$ at the outer surface) and a **Hot Work Permit / No-Smoking discipline** around such equipment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module Choice]`

**(a)** Classify the **various types of hot surfaces** found in a petroleum refinery. Provide at least two engineering control measures for each category. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **compressor cylinder** is fitted with a temperature-indicator. The skin temperature reads 240 °C. The surrounding air is at 30 °C and contains trace **propane** vapour (AIT = 450 °C). **(i)** Will the surface ignite propane? Justify. **(ii)** However, the cylinder has a thin **oil film** (mineral lube oil, AIT = 350 °C) deposited on it. Will the film ignite? **(iii)** As a safety engineer, list **three immediate corrective actions**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 marks)

| Type of Hot Surface | Examples | Control Measure 1 | Control Measure 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Hot Surfaces** | Fired heater tubes, reformer coils, reactor jackets | Insulation cladding | Temperature interlocks |
| **Mechanical Hot Surfaces** | Pump & compressor casings, bearings | Vibration + temperature monitoring | Routine cleaning of oil films |
| **Utility Hot Surfaces** | Steam lines, reboilers, traced piping | Lagging with mineral wool | Warning signage |
| **Friction-Induced** | Failed bearings, choked orifices, slip-strike | Lubrication program | Non-sparking materials |
| **Electrical Hot Surfaces** | Luminaires, heaters in non-Ex zones | Ex-proof enclosures | T-Class certified fittings |

(Marks: 1 for each well-explained category, 0.5 for each control measure — 7 marks total.)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 marks)

**(i) Propane Ignition (2 marks)**

$T_s = 240 \;^\circ\text{C}$ vs $T_{AIT, propane} = 450 \;^\circ\text{C}$.

Since $240 < 450$ → **The surface will NOT ignite propane vapour.** Thermodynamically suppressed.

**(ii) Oil Film Ignition (2 marks)**

$T_s = 240 \;^\circ\text{C}$ vs $T_{AIT, lube oil} = 350 \;^\circ\text{C}$.

Since $240 < 350$ → **Normally, NO ignition.** However, if there is **localised hot-spot** (e.g., bearing failure spot at 400+ °C) or **insulation breakdown**, then YES, the film can ignite. KTU expects the student to note the **"spot" condition** and not just bulk temperature.

**(iii) Corrective Actions (3 marks)**

1. **Investigate and shut down** the compressor immediately; isolate fuel/HC sources.
2. **Clean the oil film** with non-flammable solvent to remove the fuel layer.
3. **Install surface thermocouples + alarms** at 200 °C and trip at 250 °C (well below oil AIT).
4. (Bonus) Improve ventilation; reduce ambient $T_\infty$ by forced cooling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing AIT with Flash Point** — Many students write "the vapour will ignite at flash point when it touches the hot surface." This is **wrong**. Hot-surface ignition uses **AIT, not flash point**. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the Safety Margin (ΔT = 5–25 °C)** — In T-class calculations, students write $T_{max} = T_{AIT}$ instead of $T_{max} = T_{AIT} - \Delta T$. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Ignoring emissivity** — In Stefan–Boltzmann problems, students forget $\varepsilon$ and use $\sigma T^4$ directly, giving wrong numerical answers. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Ignoring boundary layer** — Many answers say "the vapour temperature equals surface temperature immediately." This is false; the boundary layer is what delays ignition. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Using Flash Point for hot-surface analysis** — Repeat offender! Always use AIT for hot-surface studies.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Hot Surface":**
>
> - **Definition:** Solid surface at $T_s \geq T_{AIT}$ of surrounding vapour ⇒ spontaneous ignition, no spark needed.
> - **Non-electrical ignition source** — purely thermal.
> - **Five-stage mechanism:** Diffusion → Conduction → Pyrolysis → Free-radical branching → Self-sustaining combustion.
> - **Boundary layer** (≈ 100 µm) is the thermal bottleneck — turbulence reduces hazard time.
> - **Three heat-transfer modes:** Conduction (Fourier), Convection (Newton), Radiation (Stefan–Boltzmann). Total $q'' = q''_{cond} + q''_{conv} + q''_{rad}$.
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.
> - **T-Class limits** (IEC 60079): T1=450 °C, T2=300 °C, T3=200 °C, T4=135 °C, T5=100 °C, T6=85 °C.
> - **Critical AITs to memorise:** Methane 537 °C, Propane 450 °C, n-Hexane 225 °C, n-Heptane 215 °C, Diesel 210–230 °C, Mineral oil 300–380 °C.
> - **200 °C Rule:** Any surface > 200 °C in HC zone → Hot Work Permit trigger.
> - **Refinery hot surfaces:** Fired heaters, pump casings, compressor cylinders, bearings, steam lines.
> - **Control hierarchy:** Eliminate HC → Substitute → Ventilate → Insulate → Monitor → Permit → Isolate.
> - **Codes:** NFPA 30, NFPA 77, API RP 2210, IEC 60079-0/-11.
> - **Examiner's Mantra:** "For hot-surface hazard, think AIT, NOT Flash Point."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ignition of Oil Mists and Fumes)
Storage Tank Farms of Petroleum and Petrochemical Industries - Identification of Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Storage Tank Farms of Petroleum and Petrochemical Industries - Identification of Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage Tank Farm:** A geographically grouped cluster of bulk storage vessels (atmospheric and pressure vessels) used for the receipt, intermediate storage, blending, and dispatch of crude oils, refined petroleum products, LPG, petrochemical intermediates, and finished products within a refinery, terminal, or petrochemical complex.

A **Tank Farm** is the largest single source of *stationary* hydrocarbon inventory in any petroleum or petrochemical facility. Because this inventory is held for **hours to months**, the *time-at-risk window* for major accidents (BLEVE, VCE, pool fire, boil-over) is enormous compared to process units. Hence, KTU 2024 Scheme designates hazard identification in tank farms as a **high-weightage, mandatory module** competency under Course Outcome **CO1** (Identify and assess process and storage hazards).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Liquid Battery"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Think of a tank farm as a giant rechargeable battery. Energy is "charged in" when volatile hydrocarbon liquid is pumped up against gravity and pressure; energy is "discharged" during a leak, fire, or explosion. Just as a battery bank is more dangerous than a single cell, a tank farm multiplies hazard density because **inventory × proximity × time = risk**. Two tanks 10 m apart behave very differently from two tanks 100 m apart — this is the entire basis of **segregation distance** codes like **NFPA 30** and **OISD-118**.

### 1.3 Classification of Storage Tanks (Mandatory KTU Taxonomy)

| Tank Type | Operating Pressure | Typical Service | Hazard Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cone Roof Tank (CRT)** | Atmospheric (≈ 1 atm) | Crude, diesel, fuel oil | Moderate — vapour space |
| **External Floating Roof (EFR)** | Atmospheric | High vapour-pressure gasoline, naphtha | High — rim seal fire risk |
| **Internal Floating Roof (IFR)** | Slight (+ 50 mm WC) | Volatile distillates | Moderate |
| **Domed External Floating Roof** | Atmospheric | Heated crude | High |
| **Spherical (Horton Sphere)** | 0.2 – 1.5 MPa | LPG, butane, propylene | **Very High** — BLEVE |
| **Bullets / Mounded Bullets** | 0.5 – 1.8 MPa | LPG, ethylene (cryogenic) | **Very High** — cold embrittlement |
| **LNG Cryogenic** | ≈ 0.1 MPa, −162 °C | LNG | Catastrophic — roll-over |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Pressure vs. Atmospheric:** Any vessel whose **design pressure exceeds 0.5 barg** falls under the Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR) / SMPV (Static & Mobile Pressure Vessels) rules, triggering additional statutory hazard identification requirements (HAZOP + QRA + SIL study).

### 1.4 Why Hazard Identification in Tank Farms is Unique

- **Large single-failure inventory:** A 50,000 m³ crude tank holds energy equivalent to ~ **10 kT TNT**.
- **Long residence time:** Crude stays for 15–30 days.
- **Outdoor exposure:** Wind, lightning, ambient temperature swings.
- **Multiple ignition sources:** Tank trucks, welding, lightning, hot work.
- **Common-mode failures:** Bund wall breach, dyke fire, domino escalation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "What is a Hazard?" Decomposition

A **hazard** in a tank farm is any *intrinsic property* of the stored material, equipment, or environment that can cause harm. We decompose this into three orthogonal axes:

1. **Material Hazard** — driven by **Flammability**, **Reactivity**, **Toxicity**, and **Corrosivity**.
2. **Equipment Hazard** — driven by **Pressure**, **Temperature**, **Inventory**, and **Geometry**.
3. **Operational Hazard** — driven by **Maintenance**, **Procedures**, **Human factors**, and **External events** (flood, earthquake, aircraft impact).

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk = f(Hazard × Exposure × Vulnerability)** — codified in **OISD-118** and **CCPS QRA Guidelines**. Hazard identification only addresses the *first* term; risk assessment adds the latter two.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Hazard Identification Workflow

- **Step 1 — Define Boundary:** Plot tank farm on P&ID + layout drawing; fix Battery Limit (BL).
- **Step 2 — Collect Inventory Data:** Mass (kg), phase, temperature, pressure, LFL/UFL, AIT, BP.
- **Step 3 — List Hazardous Substances:** Apply **GHS / NFPA 704** classification.
- **Step 4 — Identify Initiating Events:** Use structured techniques (HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, Checklist).
- **Step 5 — Identify Consequences:** Estimate fire radius, VCE overpressure, toxic dispersion.
- **Step 6 — Screen & Prioritize:** Rank using **Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI)** or **CEI (Chemical Exposure Index)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** under KTU 2024 Scheme Module-1. Units are SI unless specified.

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{\text{units}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{unit}}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Inventory to volume conversion | Hazard quantification |
| 2 | $M_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}$ | Explosion yield to TNT-equivalent ($\eta$ = yield factor, typically **0.02–0.05**) | VCE consequence |
| 3 | $X = \dfrac{u \cdot x}{U}$ | Pasquill–Gifford dimensionless downwind distance | Vapor dispersion |
| 4 | $C(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right]$ | Centreline ground-level concentration (Q = emission rate, **kg/s**) | Toxic gas release |
| 5 | $q_{r} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot Q_{\text{rad}} \cdot \tau}{4 \pi \cdot R^2}$ | Radiative heat flux at distance R ($\tau$ = atmospheric transmissivity) | Pool fire |
| 6 | $R_{\text{isolation}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\eta \cdot Q_{\text{rad}} \cdot \tau}{4 \pi \cdot q_{\text{threshold}}}}$ | Required safe distance for a given heat threshold (typically **q = 12.5 kW/m²** for emergency) | Bund sizing / layout |
| 7 | $\text{F\&EI} = F_1 \cdot F_2 \cdot F_3 \cdot F_4 \cdot F_5 \cdot F_6$ | Dow Fire & Explosion Index (six multipliers) | Hazard ranking |
| 8 | $P_{\text{burst}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma}{D} \cdot \eta_{\text{joint}}$ | Vessel rupture pressure (membrane stress) | Mechanical hazard |
| 9 | $V_{\text{dyke}} \geq 1.1 \cdot V_{\text{largest}}$ | Bund (dyke) volume requirement (**110 % rule**) per NFPA 30 / OISD-118 | Bund sizing |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{\text{leak}} = C_d \cdot A_p \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Liquid leak rate ($C_d$ ≈ 0.6–0.65) | Source model |

**Boundary Conditions & Limits:**
- Pasquill classes **A–F** correspond to stability; **D** is neutral, **F** is stable (worst-case dispersion).
- $q_{\text{threshold}}$ for 2nd-degree burn within 30 s ≈ **5 kW/m²**.
- Pool fire yield $\eta$ from API 521 ≈ **0.20–0.35** (radiative fraction).
- Bund fire duration = $t_{\text{burn}} = m / \dot{m}_{\text{boil-off}}$.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

These equations convert a *qualitative* hazard (e.g., "the naphtha tank could catch fire") into a *quantified* consequence (e.g., "a 100 m pool fire produces 25 kW/m² at 80 m downwind, fatal to operators in < 30 s"). The KTU 2024 scheme insists on this **quantitative bridge** because it directly feeds into the **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** required by **OISD-118**, **PNGRB**, and **MOC&NG** statutes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Centreline Ground-Level Concentration from a Continuous Point Source

We start from the 3-D steady-state Gaussian plume equation. The **derivation chain** is:

**Step 0 — Governing equation.** Fickian diffusion in a uniform wind field $u$ along x-axis:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_x \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} + K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Simplifications.** For a continuous, steady plume we set $\partial C/\partial t = 0$ and neglect along-wind diffusion ($K_x \ll u \sigma_x$). The advection term dominates:

$$
u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Gaussian trial solution.** By symmetry, try a separable Gaussian:

$$
C(x,y,z) = \frac{A(x)}{x} \exp\!\left[-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right] \exp\!\left[-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and separate variables.** Substituting into Step 1 gives:

$$
\frac{u}{A}\frac{dA}{dx} = \frac{u}{x} = \frac{K_y}{\sigma_y^2}\frac{d\sigma_y^2}{dx} + \frac{K_z}{\sigma_z^2}\frac{d\sigma_z^2}{dx}
$$

**Step 4 — Define dispersion coefficients.** Standard definition $K_y = \frac{u}{2}\frac{d\sigma_y^2}{dx}$, $K_z = \frac{u}{2}\frac{d\sigma_z^2}{dx}$ yields consistency and forces:

$$
\sigma_y^2 \propto x, \qquad \sigma_z^2 \propto x
$$

Hence $\sigma_y = a \, x^{b}$, $\sigma_z = c \, x^{d}$ (Pasquill–Gifford empirical fits).

**Step 5 — Apply conservation of mass.** Total flux through any cross-section must equal the source strength $Q$:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u \, C \, dy\, dz = Q
$$

Evaluating the integrals gives $A(x) = \dfrac{Q}{2 \pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}$.

**Step 6 — Ground-level reflection.** Including perfect reflection at $z=0$ (factor of 2 for the ground):

$$
C(x,y,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \, u \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z}\exp\!\left[-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right]
$$

This is the canonical **KTU-grade dispersion equation**.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Toxic Release from LPG Spherical Tank

**Problem (KTU-style 14 mark):** An LPG sphere of volume $V = 4000 \,\text{m}^3$ at $P = 1.0 \,\text{MPa}_g$, $T = 30 \,\text{°C}$ ruptures catastrophically. Estimate the downwind centreline ground concentration at 500 m under Pasquill class **D** (neutral, $u = 4 \,\text{m/s}$), and the hazard distance for LFL of propane (≈ 2.1 % v/v → 39 g/m³ at 25 °C).

**Step 1 — Inventory of propane.**

$$
m = \frac{P V M}{R T_c} = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^6 + 1.013\times10^5)(4000)(0.044)}{8.314 \times 303}
$$

$$
m = \frac{1.1013\times10^6 \times 4000 \times 0.044}{2519.6} = \frac{1.938\times10^8}{2519.6} \approx 7.69\times10^4 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Time-weighted source strength.** Assume catastrophic release vaporises 80 % in 60 s:

$$
Q = \frac{0.8 \times 7.69\times10^4}{60} \approx 1.025\times10^3 \,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Pasquill–Gifford σ values at x = 500 m, class D.** From standard tables:

$$
\sigma_y(500,D) \approx 19 \,\text{m}, \qquad \sigma_z(500,D) \approx 12 \,\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Centreline ground concentration:**

$$
C(500,0,0) = \frac{1025}{\pi \times 4 \times 19 \times 12} = \frac{1025}{2864} \approx 0.358 \,\text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to volumetric fraction.** Using ideal gas $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.184$ kg/m³:

$$
\%\,v/v = \frac{0.358 / 44}{1.184/29} \times 100 \approx \frac{0.00814}{0.0408}\times100 \approx 19.9 \,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Result & interpretation.** The downwind concentration at 500 m (~ 20 %) is nearly **10 × LFL** → a flammable cloud extends at least 500 m. This violates **OISD-118** emergency isolation radius (typically 800 m for LPG spheres).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Mistake:** Students forget the factor of 2 ground reflection and report concentration *half* the true value — instantly underestimating the hazard by 50 %. Always state the **reflection assumption**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Pool Fire Safe Distance

**Step 1 — Total heat release rate** of a circular pool of diameter $D$:

$$
Q_{\text{tot}} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c = \dot{m}'' \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

**Step 2 — Radiative fraction** $\eta \approx 0.20$ for hydrocarbons:

$$
Q_{\text{rad}} = \eta \cdot Q_{\text{tot}}
$$

**Step 3 — Point-source radiation model** at distance $R$:

$$
q_{r} = \frac{Q_{\text{rad}} \cdot \tau}{4 \pi R^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for safe distance** for a chosen heat flux $q_{\text{threshold}}$:

$$
R_{\text{safe}} = \sqrt{\frac{Q_{\text{rad}} \cdot \tau}{4 \pi \, q_{\text{threshold}}}}
$$

**Numerical:** For a 30 m diameter gasoline pool fire, $\dot{m}'' = 0.05$ kg/m²s, $\Delta H_c = 43.7$ MJ/kg:

$$
Q_{\text{tot}} = 0.05 \cdot \frac{\pi (30)^2}{4} \cdot 43.7\times10^6 \approx 1.55\times10^9 \,\text{W}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{rad}} = 0.20 \times 1.55\times10^9 = 3.10\times10^8 \,\text{W}
$$

$$
R_{\text{safe}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.10\times10^8 \cdot 0.85}{4 \pi \cdot 12500}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.635\times10^8}{1.571\times10^5}} \approx 41 \,\text{m}
$$

So a 30 m gasoline pool fire requires a **~ 41 m** exclusion zone for personnel.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tank-Farm Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Terminal / Pipeline Receipt] --> B[Manifold & Metering Station]
    B --> C[Tank Farm Battery Limit]
    C --> D1[Atmospheric Cone Roof Tank]
    C --> D2[External Floating Roof Tank]
    C --> D3[Spherical LPG Vessel]
    C --> D4[Cryogenic LNG Tank]
    D1 --> E[In-line Blending Skid]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Loading Gantry Trucks Rail Wagons]
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    C --> G[Bunded Dyke Containment]
    G --> H[Fire Water Foam System]
    H --> I[Emergency Shutdown ESD Valves]
    I --> J[Safe Dispersal Zone]
    D1 -. Leak .-> K[Hazard Identification Layer HAZOP FMEA Checklist]
    D2 -. Fire .-> K
    D3 -. BLEVE .-> K
    K --> L[QRA Quantitative Risk Assessment]
    L --> M[Safety Reports OISD PNGRB Submission]
    M --> N[Operating Permit Approval]
```

### 4.2 Hazard Identification Methodology Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Tank Farm Hazard] --> B{New or Existing?}
    B -- New Design --> C[Hazard Identification Study Plan]
    B -- Existing --> D[Periodic Re-assessment every 5 yrs]
    C --> E{Inventory Category}
    D --> E
    E -- Highly Toxic --> F[CEI Chemical Exposure Index]
    E -- Flammable > 10 t --> G[Dow Fire Explosion Index]
    E -- Complex Process --> H[HAZOP HAZard and OPerability]
    E -- Mechanical / Fatigue --> I[FMEA Failure Mode Effects Analysis]
    E -- Preliminary Screening --> J[What If Checklist]
    F --> K[QRA Layer of Protection Analysis]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[SIL Determination IEC 61511]
    L --> M[Risk Acceptance Criteria ALARP]
```

### 4.3 Domino-Effect Escalation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Primary Tank Leak] --> B[Pool Formation]
    B --> C[Ignition]
    C --> D[Pool Fire]
    D --> E[Radiant Heat to Adjacent Tank]
    E --> F[Wall Thinning / Boil-over]
    F --> G[Secondary Tank Rupture]
    G --> H[Cascading VCE / BLEVE]
    H --> I[Catastrophic Multi-tank Loss]
    D --> J[Smoke Plume]
    J --> K[Toxic Inhalation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reading:** Notice that **domino escalation** between adjacent tanks is the *single most catastrophic* failure mode in tank farms. **OISD-118** mandates minimum inter-tank spacing of **0.5 × D (diameter) for floating roof** and **1.0 × D for pressurised** to break this chain.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a "storage tank farm". Why are tank farms considered the largest inventory hazard in a refinery?*

> **Model Answer:**
> A storage tank farm is a clustered facility of bulk storage vessels used to hold crude oils, refined petroleum products, LPG, or petrochemicals for operational continuity. They represent the largest stationary inventory because (i) each vessel can hold 10⁴–10⁵ m³ of flammable liquid, (ii) residence time ranges from weeks to months, and (iii) outdoor exposure to lightning, wind, and ambient heat increases ignition probability. Risk = Inventory × Exposure Time × Probability.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *List any six hazardous situations specific to floating roof tanks.*

> **Model Answer:**
> (1) Rim-seal fire due to seal damage, (2) Roof leg corrosion causing sinking, (3) Pontoon weld failure, (4) Lightning strike on roof during thunderstorm, (5) Overfilling during heavy rain, (6) Wind damage to roof seals, (7) Vacuum collapse during high-speed pump-out, (8) Static accumulation during loading.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Pair Set-1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *(a)* Discuss in detail the classification of storage tanks used in petroleum and petrochemical industries. Highlight the hazards specific to each class with two examples each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 20,000 m³ atmospheric cone-roof tank stores naphtha at 30 °C. The tank diameter is 36 m, height 18 m. Estimate the total heat release rate of a worst-case pool fire assuming the entire inventory spills and burns at 0.05 kg/m²s with ΔHc = 43.5 MJ/kg. Compute the safe exclusion distance for q_threshold = 12.5 kW/m² and τ = 0.85. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Storage Tanks:**

| Class | Examples | Specific Hazards |
|---|---|---|
| **Cone Roof (CRT)** | Crude, FO, diesel | Vapour space explosion, overpressure vent |
| **External Floating Roof (EFR)** | Gasoline, naphtha | Rim-seal fire, sinking roof, leg corrosion |
| **Spherical** | LPG, propylene | BLEVE, missile projection |
| **Bullets** | Ethylene, propane | Cold embrittlement, fatigue crack |
| **Cryogenic** | LNG, ethylene (cold) | Rollover, foundation frost heave |
| **Lined/FRP** | Caustic, acids | Liner rupture, corrosion |

For each class: *explain service, design pressure, governing code (API 650, ASME Sec VIII Div 1/2), and unique failure modes.*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pool area:**
$$
A = \frac{\pi (36)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1296}{4} \approx 1017.9 \,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Total heat release:**
$$
Q_{\text{tot}} = 0.05 \times 1017.9 \times 43.5\times10^6 = 2.214\times10^9 \,\text{W}
$$

**Step 3 — Radiative fraction η = 0.20:**
$$
Q_{\text{rad}} = 0.20 \times 2.214\times10^9 = 4.43\times10^8 \,\text{W}
$$

**Step 4 — Safe distance:**
$$
R = \sqrt{\frac{4.43\times10^8 \times 0.85}{4 \pi \times 12500}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.766\times10^8}{1.571\times10^5}} \approx 49.0 \,\text{m}
$$

**Result:** The exclusion zone for personnel safety is **≈ 49 m** from the bund periphery.

---

#### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Pair Set-2, Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(a)* Explain in detail the structured hazard identification techniques used in tank farms. Compare HAZOP and FMEA with at least four differentiating criteria. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An LPG spherical vessel (V = 2000 m³, P = 0.8 MPa_g, T = 27 °C) develops a 50 mm equivalent leak at 2 m elevation. Compute the mass flow rate of propane release using Cd = 0.62. Density of liquid LPG = 510 kg/m³. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table — HAZOP vs FMEA:**

| Criterion | HAZOP | FMEA |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | ICI, 1970s | US Military, 1949 |
| Focus | Process deviations | Component failure |
| Guideword set | None/No/More/Less/Reverse/Other | Failure modes (crack, leak, rupture) |
| Node basis | Section of P&ID | Component / equipment |
| Best suited for | Operating plants, continuous processes | Mechanical / single-component risk |
| Outcome | Causes + consequences + safeguards | RPN = S × O × D |

**Techniques used in tank farms:** Checklist, What-If, HAZOP, FMEA, Fault Tree, Event Tree, Dow F&EI, CEI, QRA, Bow-Tie analysis.

**(b) Numerical — Liquid Leak Rate:**

**Step 1 — Pressure differential:**
$$
\Delta P = (0.8 + 0.1013)\,\text{MPa} - 0.1013\,\text{MPa} = 0.8 \,\text{MPa} = 8.0\times10^5 \,\text{Pa}
$$

Wait — total upstream gauge = 0.8 MPa_g, atmospheric = 0; differential is full tank gauge:

$$
\Delta P = 0.8 \times 10^6 \,\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Leak area** (50 mm diameter hole):
$$
A_p = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963\times10^{-3} \,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate (incompressible liquid):**
$$
\dot{m} = C_d A_p \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P} = 0.62 \times 1.963\times10^{-3} \sqrt{2 \times 510 \times 8.0\times10^5}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 1.217\times10^{-3} \times \sqrt{8.16\times10^8} = 1.217\times10^{-3} \times 2.857\times10^4 \approx 34.8 \,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Result:** ≈ **34.8 kg/s** of LPG release, sufficient to generate a vapour cloud that could travel several hundred metres before dispersion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing gauge vs absolute pressure** in leak rate (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting atmospheric transmissivity τ** in pool fire distance (loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Reporting concentration in % without ground reflection factor of 2** (loses 2 marks).
> 4. **Mixing up units:** ΔHc must be in J/kg, not MJ/kg; final heat release must be in W (loses 1 mark).
> 5. **In HAZOP vs FMEA:** writing generic definitions without the *differentiating* criteria (loses 3 marks).

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Tank farms** are the *single largest inventory hazard* in petroleum/petrochemical facilities.
- Classification: **Atmospheric (CRT, IFR, EFR)**, **Pressure (Sphere, Bullet)**, **Cryogenic (LNG)**.
- Major hazards: **Vapour cloud explosion (VCE)**, **Pool fire**, **BLEVE**, **Toxic release**, **Boil-over**, **Rim-seal fire**, **Domino escalation**.
- Hazard identification techniques: **HAZOP, FMEA, What-If, Checklist, Fault Tree, Event Tree, Dow F&EI, CEI**.
- Quantitative models: **Gaussian plume** ($C \propto Q / u \sigma_y \sigma_z$), **Pool fire radiation** ($q_r \propto Q_{rad}/R^2$), **Liquid leak** ($\dot m = C_d A_p \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$).
- Codes: **OISD-118, NFPA 30, API 650, ASME Sec VIII, IEC 61511, SMPV Rules**.
- **Bund (dyke) volume rule:** $V_{\text{dyke}} \geq 1.1 \cdot V_{\text{largest tank}}$.
- **LPG isolation radius:** typically **800 m** under OISD; for LNG: **1500 m**.
- **Heat flux thresholds:** 1.6 kW/m² (no injury), 5 kW/m² (2nd-degree burn), 12.5 kW/m² (immediate fatality), 37.5 kW/m² (equipment damage).
- **Pasquill classes:** A (very unstable) → F (very stable). **Class F** gives highest ground concentration.
- **Rim-seal fires** are the most common EFR incident; **bund fires** last 24–72 h and are the worst.
- **BLEVE** requires liquid + superheat + sudden pressure loss — unique to pressure vessels.
- **Always state assumptions:** yield factor η, transmissivity τ, dispersion class, reflection at ground.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the hydrocarbon processing industry, **storage tanks** are engineered pressure vessels designed to hold bulk quantities of crude oil, refined petroleum products, petrochemicals, liquefied gases (LPG/LNG), and intermediate process fluids. They are classified primarily by **operating pressure**, **roof geometry**, **vapor containment philosophy**, and **product hazard class**.

According to **API 650** (Welded Tanks for Oil Storage) and **API 620** (Design and Construction of Large, Welded, Low-Pressure Storage Tanks), tanks are engineered structures whose design pressure at the top of the shell is restricted by code-of-practice limits, distinguishing them from true pressure vessels governed by ASME Section VIII.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **API 650** → atmospheric and low-pressure tanks (up to ~2.5 kPa / 1 psi gauge)
> - **API 620** → low-pressure tanks (up to 15 kPa / 2.5 psi gauge, but for larger/thicker designs)
> - **ASME Section VIII** → pressure vessels and bullets (spheres, horizontal cylinders for LPG)

## 1.2 The Four Master Classifications

Storage tanks used in hydrocarbon service fall into **four architectural families**:

1. **Atmospheric / Fixed Roof Tanks** — Cone roof, dome roof, umbrella roof.
2. **Floating Roof Tanks** — External floating roof (EFR), Internal floating roof (IFR).
3. **Variable Vapor Space / Vapor Recovery Systems** — Lifter roof, vapor dome.
4. **Pressure Storage Tanks & Spheres** — Horton spheres, horizontal bullets, mounded LPG vessels.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket on a Hot Day"

Imagine leaving an open bucket of petrol outside on a hot Kerala afternoon.

- A **fixed roof tank** is like a bucket with a tight lid (cone roof) — vapour builds up inside, expands, breathes out through a vent, and you **lose** product as vapour. There is also a small air space where explosive mixtures can form.
- A **floating roof tank** is like placing a floating disc (a cork-like platform) directly on top of the petrol surface — the roof **rises and falls** with the liquid level. The cork eliminates the air gap, so almost no vapour can escape or accumulate.
- A **pressure sphere** is like a thick-walled thermos flask — it can be sealed completely and pressurised, so the LPG inside stays liquid even when the sun heats it.
- A **mounded bullet** is like burying that thermos in cool earth — Mother Earth becomes the natural insulator and secondary containment.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of tank type is **dictated by the product's flash point, vapour pressure, toxicity, and quantity**, not by a single design preference. This decision directly drives plant **safety, fire protection philosophy, and emissions control**.

## 1.4 Industry-Standard Operating Pressure Envelopes

| Tank Family | Typical Design Pressure | Typical Service |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed roof (API 650) | Atmospheric (~0.5 kPa) | Crude oil, diesel, fuel oil |
| External floating roof | Atmospheric + vapour seal | Gasoline, naphtha, jet fuel |
| Low-pressure dome (API 620) | ≤ 15 kPa | Volatile crudes, MTBE |
| Horton sphere | 0.2 – 1.7 MPa(g) | LPG, propane, butane |
| LNG full containment | 10 – 25 kPa(g) | Liquefied natural gas at −162 °C |
| Mounded LPG bullet | 0.5 – 1.5 MPa(g) | LPG (propane/butane) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Flash-point threshold memory rule: **flash point < 37.8 °C → floating roof mandatory**; **flash point ≥ 37.8 °C → fixed roof acceptable** (per NFPA 30 and IS 1446).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Family 1 — Fixed Roof (Cone / Dome / Umbrella) Tanks

### 2.1.1 Cone Roof Tank
A self-supporting conical roof, typically **1:16 to 1:6** slope, supported only by the shell — no internal columns.

- **Operation**: Slightly pressurised by internal vapour; vents on the shell manage breathing.
- **Wetted shell area** matters for fire-water cooling design.
- **Limitation**: Vapour space above the liquid is the prime location for **flammable mixture formation** during filling/emptying.

### 2.1.2 Dome (Self-Supporting) Roof
Spherical-segment roof with **R ≥ 0.8 × D** and rise **h ≥ 0.12 × D**, self-supporting, no internal columns.

- Cleaner internal space (no columns means no product entrapment during cleaning).
- Better suited for larger diameters (≥ 60 m).

### 2.1.3 Umbrella Roof
A rib-supported conical roof requiring **internal columns only at the perimeter** — the centre clearspan is column-free.

- Hybrid economics: cheaper than a full dome, easier tank-cleaning than a cone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety hook for KTU**: All fixed-roof tanks need **pressure/vacuum (P/V) relief valves** sized per API 2000 to handle **in-breathing** (thermal contraction, liquid withdrawal) and **out-breathing** (thermal expansion, liquid filling). Failure of these valves is the root cause of many catastrophic tank collapses.

## 2.2 Family 2 — Floating Roof Tanks

### 2.2.1 External Floating Roof (EFR)
A floating steel deck rides directly on the liquid surface, exposed to weather. Sealed by **primary shoe seal** + **secondary wiper seal** at the shell-to-roof annulus.

- Eliminates the vapour space ⇒ eliminates the **flash-back** path.
- Reduces **standing storage losses** by ~85–95 % vs. fixed roof.

### 2.2.2 Internal Floating Roof (IFR / "Floating Roof Inside a Fixed Roof")
A full-contact aluminium/geodesic dome is installed **inside** a conventional cone/dome roof. Combines weather protection of fixed roof + emission control of floating roof.

- Two seals ⇒ two chances to block vapour release.
- Suitable for jet fuel (no rainwater contamination), aviation gasoline.

### 2.2.3 Seal Technology (KTU high-yield)

| Seal Type | Description | Emission Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical shoe (metallic) | Steel shoe pressed against shell by spring/pantograph | Good |
| Liquid-mounted (resilient) | Foam/log filled with gasoline-resistant fluid | Better |
| Vapor-mounted (resilient) | Encloses vapour in an envelope above the rim | Best (3-tiered) |
| Dual seal | Shoe + wiper envelope in series | Industry best |

## 2.3 Family 3 — Variable Vapor Space & Vapor Recovery

These tanks **allow the roof to rise and fall** with the vapour pressure, capturing vapours that would otherwise vent.

- **Lifter roof**: Roof floats up and down on a leg-system as vapour pressure varies.
- **Vapor dome**: A flexible bladder captures breathing losses.
- Coupled with **vapour-recovery units (VRU)** → carbon adsorption or lean-oil absorption captures hydrocarbons.

## 2.4 Family 4 — Pressure Storage (Spheres, Bullets, Mounded)

### 2.4.1 Horton Sphere
True **pressure vessel** per ASME Section VIII, used for LPG/ammonia/ethylene at 1–2 MPa(g).

- Designed for **post-BLEVE** external fire scenario — water spray deluge mandatory.

### 2.4.2 Horizontal Bullet (Cylindrical Pressure Vessel)
Saddle-supported horizontal cylinder, more common for smaller quantities of LPG/Cl₂.

- Saddle support zones must be designed to avoid shell **ovalling** (ovalisation stress).

### 2.4.3 Mounded LPG Storage
Bullet buried in compacted sand/mound. The mound acts as:

1. **Thermal insulator** (fire protection without active water spray).
2. **Secondary containment** (catastrophic leak contained within mound).
3. **Missile barrier** against external impact.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{cyl}} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \, H$ | Cylindrical tank volumetric capacity |
| 2 | $V_{\text{cone roof}} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{12} \, h_{c}$ | Conical roof dead-volume above shell |
| 3 | $A_{\text{shell wetted}} = \pi D \cdot H_{L}$ | Wetted shell area for fire cooling |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{4.9 \, D (H-1) \, G}{S E - 1.2 P} + CA$ | API 650 shell-course thickness |
| 5 | $P_{\text{hyd}} = \rho \, g \, H_{L}$ | Hydrostatic pressure at shell bottom |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{V}{\,t\,} \cdot \dfrac{P_a - P_i}{P_a}$ | Out-breathing volume flow (filling) |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{V}{\,t\,} \cdot \dfrac{P_i - P_v}{P_a}$ | In-breathing volume flow (withdrawal) |
| 8 | $W_{\text{wind}} = q_{z} \cdot G \cdot C_f \cdot A$ | Wind overturning moment on shell |
| 9 | $\tau_{\text{w}} = \dfrac{P \, r}{2 \, t}$ | Hoop stress (cylindrical shell) |
| 10 | $q_{\text{deluge}} = 10.2 \;\text{L/min/m}^{2}$ | API 2021 water deluge rate (exposed surface) |

**Where:** $D$ = diameter, $H$ = height, $H_L$ = liquid level, $G$ = specific gravity, $S$ = allowable stress, $E$ = joint efficiency, $P$ = design pressure, $CA$ = corrosion allowance, $q_z$ = velocity pressure, $G$ = gust factor, $C_f$ = force coefficient, $A$ = projected area.

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are derivable from first principles — see Section 3 for the worked derivations relevant to KTU's Part-B problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylindrical Tank Volumetric Capacity

The internal volume of a vertical cylindrical tank is the cross-sectional area multiplied by the height of the cylindrical shell.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Area}_{\text{circle}} &= \pi \, r^{2} = \pi \, \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{\pi \, D^{2}}{4} \\[4pt]
V_{\text{cyl}} &= \text{Area} \times H \\[4pt]
V_{\text{cyl}} &= \dfrac{\pi \, D^{2}}{4} \cdot H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation**: This is the *gross* shell volume. To find **working volume**, subtract the heel (minimum pumpable level) and the *ullage* (freeboard for vapour space, typically 5–10 % of height).

**SI unit check**: $D$ [m] and $H$ [m] → $V$ [m³]. Convert to barrels using $1 \;\text{bbl} = 0.1589873 \;\text{m}^{3}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydrostatic Pressure at the Bottom Shell Course

Pressure on the bottom course is purely hydrostatic (no significant vapour pressure contribution at the bottom).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{hyd}} &= \rho \, g \, H_{L} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{hyd}} &= \text{SG} \times 1000 \;\text{kg/m}^{3} \times 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^{2} \times H_{L} \;\text{[m]} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{hyd}} &= 9810 \cdot \text{SG} \cdot H_{L} \quad [\text{Pa}]
\end{aligned}
$$

For SG = 0.85 (crude oil) and $H_L = 12$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{hyd}} &= 9810 \times 0.85 \times 12 \\[4pt]
P_{\text{hyd}} &= 100{,}062 \;\text{Pa} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{hyd}} &\approx 100 \;\text{kPa} \;\;\text{(i.e., 1 bar at the shell bottom)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this matters for safety**: The **bottom shell course is the most heavily loaded** ⇒ has the thickest plate. This is why hydrostatic testing is performed with water, not product (water's SG = 1.0 is the design point).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — API 650 Shell-Course Thickness

API 650 uses a one-foot (or one-metre) **method-of-slices**. For each shell course, the required thickness accounts for the hydrostatic head **above the course** (measured 1 ft below the top of the course) plus an **overturning** wind/seismic check.

For the **bottom course** under **hydrostatic-only** loading, the **simplified design formula** is:

$$
t = \dfrac{4.9 \, D \, (H - 1) \, G}{S \, E - 1.2 \, P} + CA
$$

**Step-by-step logic**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \;\; &\text{Numerator} = 4.9 \, D \, (H-1) \, G \quad \text{(hydrostatic load on shell)} \\
\textbf{Step 2:} \;\; &\text{Denominator} = S \, E - 1.2 \, P \quad \text{(net allowable stress after internal pressure)} \\
\textbf{Step 3:} \;\; &\text{Ratio} \rightarrow \text{trial thickness for hydrostatic condition} \\
\textbf{Step 4:} \;\; &+ CA \rightarrow \text{final thickness including corrosion allowance}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Given:** $D = 30 \;\text{m}$, $H = 12 \;\text{m}$, $G = 0.95$, $S = 160 \;\text{MPa}$, $E = 0.85$, $P = 0 \;\text{(atmospheric)}$, $CA = 1.5 \;\text{mm}$.

**Find:** Required bottom-course shell thickness.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{4.9 \times 30 \times (12 - 1) \times 0.95}{160 \times 0.85 - 1.2 \times 0} + 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{[m]} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{4.9 \times 30 \times 11 \times 0.95}{136} + 0.0015 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1535.85}{136} + 0.0015 \\[4pt]
&= 11.29 \times 10^{-3} + 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \;\; [\text{m}] \\[4pt]
t &= 12.79 \;\text{mm} \;\;\Rightarrow \;\;\text{round up to next standard plate: } 13 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Plates are supplied in standard thickness increments (5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20 mm...). Always **round up**, never down — round-down is a safety violation per API 650.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hoop Stress in a Spherical Shell (Horton Sphere)

For a thin-walled sphere under internal pressure $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Force balance on half-sphere:} \;\; P \cdot \pi r^{2} &= 2 \cdot \sigma_{h} \cdot \pi r \cdot t \\[4pt]
\sigma_{h} &= \dfrac{P \, r}{2 \, t}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **cylinder**, only one longitudinal seam carries the load ⇒ hoop stress is doubled:

$$
\sigma_{h,\text{cyl}} = \dfrac{P \, r}{t} \quad \text{(vs. sphere's } \dfrac{P \, r}{2 t}\text{)}
$$

**Engineering insight**: Spheres are **twice as material-efficient** as cylinders at the same pressure. This is why LPG/H₂ bulk storage prefers Horton spheres — but spheres are more expensive to fabricate (complex curvature), so a **cost-vs-material** trade-off often picks bullets (horizontal cylinders) for smaller capacities.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — P/V Valve Sizing (API 2000)

**Out-breathing** when filling (volume displaced as liquid enters):

$$
Q_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{fill}}}{t_{\text{fill}}} \cdot \left( \dfrac{P_a + P_i - P_v}{P_a} \right)
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1:} \;\; &V_{\text{fill}} / t_{\text{fill}} = \text{volumetric fill rate} \quad [\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}] \\
\textbf{Step 2:} \;\; &P_a + P_i - P_v = \text{absolute vapour pressure at operating temp} \\
\textbf{Step 3:} \;\; &\text{Divided by atmospheric pressure} \Rightarrow \text{volume of saturated vapour displaced per m}^{3}\text{ of liquid filled}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this matters for safety**: Undersized P/V valves cause **roof-lift** events during filling — a recurring cause of tank fires. KTU's safety syllabus highlights that **roof-lift is a precursor to a major incident**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Fixed Cone Roof | Floating Roof | Dome (API 620) | Horton Sphere |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Design pressure | 0.5 kPa | 0.2 kPa | 5–15 kPa | 0.5–1.7 MPa |
| Standing losses | High (95 % of fleet) | Very low (5 %) | Moderate | Minimal |
| Fire exposure area | Full shell + roof | Shell + rim only | Full shell + roof | Small shell only |
| Inspection difficulty | Easy | Moderate (roof drain) | Easy | Moderate |
| Catastrophic-failure consequence | Vapour-cloud fire | Rim-seal fire | Vapour-cloud fire | BLEVE (lethal) |
| Capital cost (per m³) | Lowest | Low–Moderate | Moderate | Highest |
| Typical product | Diesel, fuel oil | Gasoline, naphtha | Volatile crude | LPG, propane |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam shortcut**: When asked to *justify* a tank choice, always argue from three pillars — **flash point, vapour pressure, and inventory**. These three alone determine 90 % of real-world selections.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HYDROCARBON STORAGE TANK] --> B[ATMOSPHERIC LOW PRESSURE]
    A --> C[PRESSURISED MEDIUM HIGH PRESSURE]
    A --> D[CRYOGENIC LNG SERVICE]

    B --> B1[FIXED ROOF]
    B --> B2[FLOATING ROOF]
    B --> B3[VARIABLE VAPOUR SPACE]

    B1 --> B1a[Cone Roof API 650]
    B1 --> B1b[Self Supporting Dome]
    B1 --> B1c[Umbrella Roof]

    B2 --> B2a[External Floating Roof EFR]
    B2 --> B2b[Internal Floating Roof IFR]
    B2 --> B2c[Pan Type Pontoon]
    B2 --> B2d[Double Deck]

    B3 --> B3a[Lifter Roof]
    B3 --> B3b[Vapour Recovery Unit VRU]

    C --> C1[Horton Sphere]
    C --> C2[Horizontal Bullet]
    C --> C3[Spheroid]

    C1 --> C1a[LPG Propane Butane]
    C1 --> C1b[Ammonia]
    C1 --> C1c[Ethylene]

    C2 --> C2a[Saddle Supported Above Ground]
    C2 --> C2b[Mounded Bullet]

    D --> D1[Single Containment]
    D --> D2[Double Containment]
    D --> D3[Full Containment]

    D1 --> D1a[Inner Tank 9 percent Ni Steel]
    D2 --> D2a[Inner Tank plus Outer Concrete Dyke]
    D3 --> D3a[Inner Tank plus Pre Stressed Concrete Outer Shell plus Roof]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Fixed Cone Roof Tank (Cross-Sectional Logic Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Product Inlet] --> S[Shell Cylindrical]
    S --> BS[Bottom Course Thickest]
    S --> MS[Middle Courses Stepped]
    S --> TS[Top Course Thinnest]
    S --> CR[Cone Roof Slope 1 to 16]
    CR --> RV[Roof Vent]
    S --> PV[Pressure Vacuum Relief Valve API 2000]
    S --> FF[Foundation Ring Slab]
    S --> NW[Nozzle Manways]
    S --> AT[Automatic Tank Gauge ATG]
    S --> FP[Foam Chamber Fire Protection]
    S --> LW[Liquid Level Instrument]
    S --> EM[Emergency Drain Sump]
```

## 4.3 Floating Roof Functional Logic Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    L[Liquid Product Inside Shell] --> FR[Floating Deck Rides on Liquid Surface]
    FR --> DC[Deck Compartment Pontoons]
    FR --> SS[Primary Shoe Seal Steel against Shell]
    FR --> WS[Secondary Wiper Seal Resilient]
    FR --> RD[Roof Drain Siphonic to Slop Tank]
    FR --> AL[Anti Rotation Cable]
    FR --> RG[Rim Guard against Shell Buckling]
    FR --> LE[Liquid Level Sensor]
    SS --> VR[Vapour Captured between Seals]
    WS --> VR
    VR --> SI[Slope Towards Bleed Vent]
```

## 4.4 Pressure Storage Hierarchy Logic Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[Hydrocarbon in Pressurised Liquid State] --> P1[Pressure Decision]
    P1 --> P2A{Quantity less than 200 m³}
    P1 --> P2B{Quantity 200 to 4000 m³}
    P1 --> P2C{Quantity greater than 4000 m³}

    P2A --> X1[Horizontal Bullet]
    P2B --> X2[Mounded Bullet or Sphere]
    P2C --> X3[Horton Sphere]

    X1 --> D1[Fire Scenario: Water Deluge]
    X2 --> D2[Fire Scenario: Mound Acts as Insulator]
    X3 --> D3[Fire Scenario: Water Deluge plus Heat Radiation Shield]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are **architecture/function-flow schematics** rather than literal engineering drawings. They map the **decision logic, component hierarchy, and inter-component interactions** that KTU examiners expect students to recall in descriptive questions on tank selection and safety philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between API 650 and API 620 storage tanks. State two products typically stored in each type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | API 650 | API 620 |
|---|---|---|
| Design pressure | Atmospheric to **~2.5 kPa(g)** | Up to **15 kPa(g)** |
| Design temperature | Above atmospheric boiling | Lower temperatures possible |
| Construction | Welded, field-erected | Welded with thicker courses |
| Inspection emphasis | Radiography on critical joints | 100 % radiography of all joints |
| Typical products | Crude oil, diesel, fuel oil | Volatile crudes, MTBE, vinyl chloride |

API 650 uses **method-of-slices** analysis; API 620 uses **full shell stress** analysis. (1 mark each for the comparison and 1 mark for product examples.)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**What is a "primary seal" and "secondary seal" in a floating roof tank? Why is dual sealing critical for safety?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Primary seal (shoe seal)**: A metallic or resilient sealing element that maintains continuous contact with the inside of the shell as the floating roof rises and falls. It blocks the bulk of the vapour escape path at the rim space. (1 mark)
- **Secondary seal (wiper seal)**: Mounted above the primary seal; covers the remaining annular gap; can be vapour-mounted (envelope type) to capture vapours that bypass the primary seal. (1 mark)
- **Safety criticality**: Dual sealing reduces evaporative loss by 95–99 %, prevents formation of an **ignitable vapour cloud** at the rim, and provides a second barrier if the primary seal fails — directly preventing **rim-seal fires**, one of the most common floating roof incidents. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse "roof type" with "tank type" in your answer — fixed roof vs. floating roof is a *sub-classification* under atmospheric tanks.
> - Always **state the design code (API 650 / API 620 / ASME)** when naming a tank — generic answers lose 2 marks.
> - For 7-mark sub-questions, you must give **at least one diagram or comparative table** — purely prose answers are penalised.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

#### (a) Classify hydrocarbon storage tanks. Explain with neat sketches the constructional features of **fixed roof** and **floating roof** tanks. State the suitability of each for hydrocarbon service. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification (2 marks):**

Tanks are classified by **operating pressure and vapour-handling philosophy** into:

1. Atmospheric / fixed roof (cone, dome, umbrella)
2. Floating roof (EFR, IFR)
3. Variable vapour space (lifter roof, vapour dome)
4. Pressure storage (Horton sphere, horizontal bullet, mounded)
5. Cryogenic (single, double, full containment)

**(ii) Fixed Roof Tank — Constructional Features (2 marks):**

- Cylindrical shell, butt-welded courses with stepped thickness (thickest at bottom).
- Cone roof with 1:16 slope, self-supporting.
- P/V relief valve, foam chambers on shell, ATG instrumentation.
- Foundation: ring-wall + sand pad + reinforced concrete slab.
- Stairs, platforms, handrails per OSHA 1910 / IS 803.

**(iii) Floating Roof Tank — Constructional Features (2 marks):**

- Same cylindrical shell as fixed roof.
- Roof floats on liquid surface (pontoon or double-deck type).
- Primary shoe seal (resilient tube or metallic) + secondary wiper seal.
- Rim space ≈ 200–250 mm; anti-rotation cable; rolling ladder.
- Roof drain (siphonic) returns rainwater to slop tank.

**(iv) Suitability (1 mark):**

- **Fixed roof** → high-flash products (diesel ≥ 55 °C, fuel oil, lube base oils).
- **Floating roof** → low-flash products (gasoline, naphtha, jet-A1) where vapour suppression is mandatory.

---

#### (b) Discuss in detail the **safety features** incorporated in modern bulk hydrocarbon storage tanks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Fire protection (2 marks):**

- **Fixed foam pourers/chambers** at the rim seal (floating roof) and shell top (fixed roof).
- **Water spray deluge** for spheres/bullets — 10.2 L/min/m² over wetted surface.
- **Remote-controlled valves** at tank dyke boundary for isolation.

**(2) Vapour / emission control (2 marks):**

- **P/V relief valves** sized per API 2000 for in/out-breathing.
- **Vapour recovery units (VRU)** on fixed-roof gasoline tanks.
- **Internal floating roof** for jet fuel (avoids rainwater contamination).

**(3) Bunding / secondary containment (1.5 marks):**

- Dyke volume = **110 % of largest tank** in the dyke.
- Dyke wall height ≤ 1.8 m (IS 1446 / NFPA 30).
- Impermeable floor with slope to remote drainage.

**(4) Instrumentation & emergency shutdown (1.5 marks):**

- ATG (Automatic Tank Gauge), high-high level alarm + independent **level switch** → ESD-1 closure of inlet block valve.
- Temperature transmitters on shell and roof.
- Fire/gas detectors in bund area.
- Lightning protection per IS 2309 / IEC 62305.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

#### (a) With the help of a schematic, explain the **construction and working of a Horton sphere** for LPG storage. What are the typical design pressures and temperatures? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Construction (3 marks):**

- ASME Section VIII Division 1 pressure vessel.
- Spherical shell fabricated from carbon steel plates (typical) or 9 % Ni steel for low-temp service.
- Supported on **8–12 equatorial or radial columns**, each with a stainless steel sliding pad to allow thermal expansion.
- Top and bottom manways, internal ladder/aluminium platform, mixing nozzles.
- Connected to pump/compressor via bottom or equatorial nozzles with swivel joints (Piping stress relief).

**(ii) Working (2 marks):**

- LPG stored as a **liquid under pressure** above its boiling point (propane: 0.7 MPa at 25 °C).
- Vapour return line maintains pressure during liquid withdrawal; vapour makeup from vaporiser maintains pressure during filling.
- Water spray deluge protects against external fire → prevents BLEVE.

**(iii) Typical Design Parameters (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Design pressure | 0.7 – 1.7 MPa(g) |
| Design temperature | −20 °C to +50 °C |
| Capacity | 500 – 8000 m³ |
| Corrosion allowance | 1.5 – 3.0 mm |
| Hydrotest pressure | 1.3 × design pressure |
| Foundation | RCC ring beam + skirt columns |

---

#### (b) Compare **mounded LPG storage** with a **conventional Horton sphere**. Discuss the safety advantages of mounded storage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Horton Sphere | Mounded Bullet |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Spherical | Horizontal cylindrical |
| Pressure rating | 0.7–1.7 MPa | 0.5–1.5 MPa |
| Cost per m³ | Higher (fabrication) | Lower (simple cylinder) |
| Footprint | Compact above-ground | Larger (mound footprint) |
| Visual impact | Prominent sphere | Hidden underground |
| Fire protection | Active (water spray) | Passive (mound insulation) |
| BLEVE consequence | Catastrophic if deluge fails | Effectively zero (mound contains) |
| Maintenance | Easy access | Difficult (buried) |
| Inspection | External NDT easy | Only cathodic protection + probes |

**(ii) Safety Advantages of Mounded Storage (4 marks):**

1. **Passive fire protection** — the sand/concrete mound resists 1+ hour of pool fire without intervention.
2. **Mechanical impact protection** — missile-resistant against aircraft crash / vehicle impact.
3. **Secondary containment** — leak is contained within the mound; no pool spread.
4. **Thermal stability** — ground temperature is constant (~15–25 °C), reducing vapour pressure swings vs. an above-ground tank under sun.
5. **Vapour cloud dispersion** — any small leak disperses through soil, eliminating vapour cloud explosion risk.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory anchors for the KTU exam hall:**

- ✅ **API 650 = atmospheric fixed/floating roof** — crude oil, diesel, lube.
- ✅ **API 620 = low-pressure (≤ 15 kPa)** — volatile crudes, MTBE.
- ✅ **ASME Section VIII = pressure vessels** — LPG spheres, bullets.
- ✅ **Flash point < 37.8 °C → floating roof mandatory** (NFPA 30 / IS 1446).
- ✅ **Dyke volume ≥ 110 %** of largest tank inside the dyke.
- ✅ **Water deluge rate for spheres = 10.2 L/min/m²** of wetted surface (API 2021).
- ✅ **BLEVE** risk exists only for vessels with vapour space + external fire → mitigated by deluge + mounding.
- ✅ **P/V relief valve sizing** → API 2000 (in-breathing during withdrawal, out-breathing during filling).
- ✅ **Rim-seal fire** → most common floating roof incident; mitigated by **dual seal + foam pourers**.
- ✅ **Shell thickness** → bottom course thickest (hydrostatic load is highest); design per API 650 one-foot method.
- ✅ **Mounded storage** = passive fire protection, secondary containment, thermal stability.
- ✅ **LNG** = 9 % Ni steel or aluminium alloy inner tank; full-containment has pre-stressed concrete outer shell.
- ✅ Always state **design code** when naming a tank in your answer — instant +1 mark.
- ✅ Always provide **at least one diagram or comparative table** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 1, Topic: Types of Tanks (PESFT745)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Design" in the Context of Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries?

In the context of **PESFT745 — Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries**, the word **"Design"** does **not** refer to civil or mechanical design of structures in isolation. It specifically refers to the **Safety-Inclusive Process & Plant Design** philosophy applied to facilities handling petroleum, natural gas, LNG, LPG, naphtha, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, and other hydrocarbon streams that are flammable, toxic, and reactive.

Formally, **Safety Design in Hydrocarbon Industries** is defined as:

> The systematic, multi-layered engineering discipline of integrating **process safety principles, inherent safety concepts, hazardous-area zoning, fire/blast protection, pressure relief, and emergency shutdown logic** into the front-end engineering design (FEED) and detailed engineering of a hydrocarbon processing facility — so that the risk to people, asset, and environment is **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏠🔥

Imagine you are building a **kitchen in a house** that uses LPG cylinders. There are two ways to think about "safety design":

| Approach | What You Do | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Engineered Safety (Add-on)** | Install a smoke detector, fire extinguisher, gas leak alarm, fire-resistant door — *after* the kitchen is built. | Risk reduced, but you are still *reacting* to a fault. |
| **Inherent Safety (Built-in)** | Place the LPG cylinder **outside** the kitchen, use **a smaller cylinder (intensification)**, route the gas line through a **ventilated shaft**, install a **single shut-off valve near the exit**, and keep the kitchen **far from the bedroom**. | The hazard is **eliminated or reduced at source**. Smoke detectors still help, but the *chance* of a serious fire is dramatically lower. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of KTU Safety Design:**
> *"What you do NOT have in the plant cannot leak, cannot catch fire, and cannot explode."* — **Trevor Kletz (Father of Inherent Safety)**

The same logic applies to a refinery, an offshore platform, or an LNG terminal. Good **safety design** removes or minimizes the hazard **first**, then adds engineered protection layers (alarms, valves, water spray, foam, ESD) as a defense-in-depth backup.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Safety Design in Hydrocarbon Plants

```text
PILLAR 1 ──► INHERENT SAFETY  (Remove/reduce the hazard at source)
PILLAR 2 ──► ENGINEERED SAFETY (Add active/passive protection systems)
PILLAR 3 ──► PROCEDURAL SAFETY (SOPs, training, emergency response)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 emphasis:** Module 1 lays the foundation by focusing on **Pillar 1 (Inherent Safety Design)** and the **design parameters** that follow from it — namely, plant layout, hazardous-area classification, fire protection design, and pressure relief device sizing. Pillar 2 (engineered protection) and Pillar 3 (procedural) are addressed in later modules but **the design decisions made here dictate everything else downstream.**

### 1.4 Why "Design" is the Single Most Important Topic in the Course

A flaw made at the **design stage** cannot be fully corrected by spending **ten times more money during operation**. Studies by the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** consistently show that:

> **More than 60% of major hydrocarbon accidents (Flixborough 1974, BP Texas City 2005, Buncefield 2005, Jaipur 2009)** had their **root cause** traced back to an **incorrect or absent safety design decision** — inadequate relief sizing, wrong hazardous-area zoning, poor plot plan spacing, or absence of inherent safety considerations.

Therefore, the **"Design" topic** is not just a chapter — it is the **philosophical and quantitative backbone** of the entire PESFT745 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer 1 — Inherent Safety Design (ISD)

The **Inherently Safer Design** philosophy was crystallized by **Trevor Kletz** (ICI, UK) in the 1970s–80s. It is built on **four foundational principles**, often remembered as **"MINI-MUSIC"** or **"MS-MA"**:

### 2.1.1 The Four Pillars of Inherent Safety (Kletz's MSDS Triangle)

| # | Principle | Engineering Meaning | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimize** (Intensification) | Use the **smallest possible inventory** of hazardous material | Reduce LPG storage from 100 t to 30 t by just-in-time delivery |
| 2 | **Substitute** (Replacement) | Replace a hazardous substance with a **less hazardous** one | Use aqueous ammonia instead of anhydrous; low-vapour-pressure gasoline |
| 3 | **Moderate** (Attenuation) | Operate at **less hazardous conditions** — lower T, lower P, less flammable concentration | Use hot oil at 200 °C instead of steam at 400 °C; vacuum distillation |
| 4 | **Simplify** (Limitation of Effects) | Design equipment & layouts that are **simple, tolerant of faults**, and fail-safe | Use welded pipe instead of flanged joints in critical service; gravity drain rather than pump |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Controls (NIOSH, USA — adopted into KTU syllabus):**
> **Inherent → Engineered → Administrative → PPE**
> Always try to apply a control at a **higher level** first.

### 2.1.2 Inherent Safety Indices (Quantification Tools)

For Board exam answers, you must know **two** indices:

**(a) Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI), American Institute of Chemical Engineers, 1994:**

$$\text{F\&EI} = F_3 \times \text{MF}$$

where:
- $\text{MF}$ = Material Factor (from NFPA flammability/reactivity, tabulated — e.g., gasoline MF = 16, methane = 21, LPG = 21)
- $F_3$ = Process Unit Hazard Factor = sum of General Process Hazards + Special Process Hazards

**(b) Inherent Safety Index (ISI) — Edwards & Lawrence, 1993:**

$$I_{\text{Total}} = I_{\text{Chem}} + I_{\text{Process}} + I_{\text{Qty}} + I_{\text{Equip}} + I_{\text{Layout}}$$

Each sub-index is scored 0–5 (or 0–10 in some variants). **Higher score = more hazardous design.**

---

## 2.2 Layer 2 — Plant Layout & Spacing Design

The **plot plan** (master plan) of a hydrocarbon facility must satisfy spacing rules from **OISD-118, NFPA-30, API-RP-752, IS-3034, and PESO Rules**. Key design parameters:

### 2.2.1 Spacing Between Equipment (OISD / NFPA norms)

$$\boxed{\;\text{Spacing}_{\min} = f(V_{\text{tank}}, P_{\text{vessel}}, \text{Type of fluid})\;}$$

Typical rule-of-thumb values (must memorize):

| Equipment Pair | Minimum Spacing | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Floating-roof tank ↔ Floating-roof tank | **0.5 × D** of larger tank, min **10 m** | OISD-118 |
| Cone-roof tank ↔ Cone-roof tank | **1.0 × D** of larger tank, min **15 m** | OISD-118 |
| Process vessel ↔ Control room | **30 m** (non-fireproofed); **15 m** (fireproofed) | API-RP-752 |
| LPG bullet ↔ Any ignition source | **60 m** (bare vessel); **30 m** (water spray + insulation) | OISD-144 |
| Process unit ↔ Boundary wall | **Minimum 30 m** | PESO |

### 2.2.2 Dominant Wind Direction (DWD) Rule

> **Hazardous units must be located on the leeward side (downwind) of the prevailing wind**, and **occupied buildings (control rooms, offices, canteens) on the upwind side**.

This way, in case of a vapor cloud release, the plume drifts *away* from people, not *towards* them.

---

## 2.3 Layer 3 — Hazardous Area Classification (HAC) Design

Hydrocarbon vapors form **explosive atmospheres** with air. The **IEC 60079-10 / IS-5572** standard divides the plant into **zones** to dictate **electrical equipment selection (Ex-proof rating)**:

### 2.3.1 Gas/Vapor Zones (IEC 60079-10)

| Zone | Definition | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | Flammable atmosphere **continuously present** or for **long periods (>1000 hr/yr)** | Inside a petrol storage tank, inside a vapor space of a sealed reactor |
| **Zone 1** | Flammable atmosphere **likely to occur in normal operation (10–1000 hr/yr)** | Immediate vicinity of pump seals, flange joints, sample points |
| **Zone 2** | Flammable atmosphere **not likely in normal operation (<10 hr/yr)**, and short-lived if it does | Around vents, drains, well-ventilated areas near Zone 1 |

### 2.3.2 Extent of Zone — the "1-metre Rule of Thumb"

$$\boxed{\;r_{\text{zone}} = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{Q} \;}$$

where $Q$ = volumetric release rate of gas/vapor (m³/s) and $k$ is a geometry factor (≈ 0.3 for open-air dispersion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a typical LPG flange (1 mm leak, 0.1 kg/s):**
> - Zone 1 extends ≈ **1 m** radius from the flange
> - Zone 2 extends ≈ **3 m** radius
> All electrical equipment inside this radius must be **Ex-d (flameproof)** or **Ex-e (increased safety)** rated.

---

## 2.4 Layer 4 — Pressure Relief System Design (API 521)

This is the **most calculation-intensive part** of the topic and a KTU-favourite.

### 2.4.1 Causes of Overpressure (Design Cases)

| Case | Description | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Fire case (external)** | External pool fire heats the vessel; vaporizes the liquid inventory | API 521 §7.3 |
| **Blocked outlet** | A downstream valve is closed; full pump flow enters vessel | API 521 §7.1 |
| **Control valve failure (CF)** | A control valve fails open, allowing full upstream pressure | API 521 §7.2 |
| **Tube rupture (HX)** | Heat-exchanger tube fails; high-P fluid enters low-P shell | API 521 §7.5 |
| **Thermal expansion** | Liquid trapped between two closed valves is heated | API 521 §7.4 |
| **Chemical reaction runaway** | Exothermic reaction accelerates (less common in refineries) | API 521 §7.6 |

### 2.4.2 Relief Valve Sizing — Critical (Sonic) Flow Equation

For **vapor or gas** relief under critical (choked) flow conditions (i.e., when $\;P_{\text{downstream}} \leq 0.55 \cdot P_{\text{upstream}}\;$):

$$\boxed{\;A = \dfrac{W}{C \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, P_1} \sqrt{\dfrac{T \, Z}{M}}\;}$$

### 2.4.3 Fire-Case Wetted Area (API 521 §7.3.2)

$$\boxed{\;W_{\text{fire}} = C_1 \cdot A_{w}^{0.82}\;}$$

where $C_1 = 21{,}000$ for English units (BTU/hr) or $C_1 = 43{,}200$ for SI units (W).

### 2.4.4 Coefficient of Discharge (C) — From API 520

$$C = 520 \,\sqrt{k \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!\!(k+1)/(k-1)}}$$

| Gas | k = Cp/Cv | C |
|---|---|---|
| Methane | 1.31 | 337 |
| Ethane | 1.19 | 357 |
| Propane | 1.13 | 370 |
| Steam | 1.30 | 338 |
| Air | 1.40 | 326 |

---

## 2.5 Layer 5 — Fire Protection System Design (NFPA / TAC India)

### 2.5.1 Fire Water Demand Formula (NFPA-15 simplified)

$$\boxed{\;Q = \dfrac{C \cdot A}{N}\;}$$

where:
- $Q$ = required fire-water flow rate (gpm or m³/hr)
- $C$ = fire-water demand coefficient — typically **1000 gpm for ordinary combustibles, 1500 gpm for hydrocarbons** (NFPA Table)
- $A$ = fire area (sq ft) — single largest fire risk area
- $N$ = design duration (minutes) — typically **60 min for process units, 240 min for bulk storage tanks**

### 2.5.2 Foam System Design (NFPA-11)

$$Q_{\text{foam}} = A_{\text{tank}} \times \text{Application density}$$

Application density for hydrocarbon pool fires:
- **6.5 L/min per m²** for hydrocarbons (NFPA-11, Type II application)
- **4.0 L/min per m²** for polar solvents (methanol, acetone)

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in almost every Part-B question.**

| # | Formula | Where Used | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{W}{C K_d K_b K_c P_1}\sqrt{\dfrac{TZ}{M}}$ | Vapor PSV sizing (critical flow) | A in in² |
| 2 | $C = 520\sqrt{k(2/(k+1))^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}$ | Coefficient for gas PSV | dimensionless |
| 3 | $W_{\text{fire}} = C_1 A_w^{0.82}$ | Heat input to wetted vessel surface during fire | BTU/hr or W |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{fire}} = C \cdot A / N$ | Fire water pump sizing | gpm |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{foam}} = A \cdot \text{density}$ | Foam concentrate application | L/min |
| 6 | $W_{\text{TNT}} = \eta \cdot M \cdot H_c / H_{\text{TNT}}$ | Vapor cloud explosion → TNT equivalent | kg |
| 7 | $R = W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} / C_s$ | Standoff distance for given overpressure | m |
| 8 | $\text{F\&EI} = F_3 \times \text{MF}$ | Dow Fire & Explosion Index | dimensionless |
| 9 | $I_{\text{Total}} = I_{\text{Ch}} + I_{\text{Pr}} + I_{\text{Qt}} + I_{\text{Eq}} + I_{\text{Ly}}$ | Inherent Safety Index | dimensionless |
| 10 | $r_{\text{zone}} = k \cdot Q^{1/3}$ | Extent of hazardous zone | m |
| 11 | $\text{Spacing} = f(D_{\text{tank}}, P_{\text{vessel}}, \text{fluid})$ | Plant layout minimum spacing | m |
| 12 | $P_{\text{set}} = 1.10 \times \text{MAWP}$ | PSV set pressure rule | barg |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are **not academic**. They directly feed into:
- **P&IDs** (Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams)
- **Layout drawings** issued for construction
- **PSSR (Pre-Startup Safety Review)** audits
- **PESO / OISD / statutory approvals** in India
- **Insurance premium calculations** (F&EI directly affects premium)
- **Cost estimation** — a larger PSV orifice or wider spacing = higher project cost

> **A KTU-graduate Safety/Process engineer is expected to perform, verify, or audit all of the above calculations on day one of joining an EPC company (L&T, Technip, Toyo, EIL, IOCL, BPCL, HPCL, ONGC, Reliance, Adani, etc.).**
