# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **KTU-board-style** — every algebraic step is shown explicitly. Practice writing them out on paper. **Do NOT skip steps in the exam; the examiner gives partial marks for intermediate steps.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation A — API 521 Fire-Case Mass Flow Rate

**Statement:** *A horizontal cylindrical vessel containing propane is exposed to an external pool fire. Derive the mass flow rate that must be relieved to keep the vessel pressure within 10% of MAWP.*

### Step 1 — Identify the Wetted Surface Area $A_w$

For a horizontal cylinder of diameter $D$ and tangent-to-tangent length $L$ filled to 50% liquid level, only the lower half is wetted (engulfed by flame):

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{w,\text{cyl}} &= \pi D \, L_{\text{wet}} + 2 \cdot \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \\
                &= \pi D \, L + \tfrac{\pi D^2}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

For a vertical vessel of diameter $D$ and tangent height $H$ filled to height $h$:

$$A_{w,\text{vert}} = \pi D \, h + \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Total Heat Input Using API 521 Correlation

API 521 §7.3.2 gives the empirical correlation between wetted area and the total heat absorbed by the vessel contents:

$$Q = 21{,}000 \cdot A_w^{0.82} \quad \text{(BTU/hr, English units)}$$

In SI units:

$$Q = 43{,}200 \cdot A_w^{0.82} \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Vapor Generation Rate

Assuming **all the absorbed heat goes into vaporizing the liquid** (no sensible heating — worst case):

$$\dot{m}_{\text{vap}} = \frac{Q}{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization at relieving conditions (J/kg).

### Step 4 — Add Subcooling Correction (Vessels with Liquid Below Saturation)

If the liquid is subcooled by $\Delta T_{sc}$ below its bubble point, only the fraction above the bubble point vaporizes:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{relief}} = \frac{Q}{\lambda + C_p \Delta T_{sc}}$$

> For saturated liquids, $\Delta T_{sc} = 0$, and the formula reduces to Step 3.

### Step 5 — Apply a Safety Factor

API 521 recommends a **1.2 to 1.4 multiplier** to account for wind-blown flames, flame impingement on un-wetted metal (which then conducts heat into the liquid), and uncertainty:

$$\boxed{\;W_{\text{design}} = 1.3 \cdot \frac{43{,}200 \cdot A_w^{0.82}}{\lambda + C_p \Delta T_{sc}}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — API 520 Critical-Flow Coefficient $C$

**Statement:** *Derive the expression for the coefficient of discharge $C$ in API 520 critical-flow formula starting from the compressible isentropic nozzle equation.*

### Step 1 — Mass Flow Through an Ideal Nozzle (Stagnation → Static)

The one-dimensional steady mass flow rate through a converging nozzle is given by:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, V$$

where $\rho$ and $V$ are the static density and velocity at the throat.

### Step 2 — Isentropic Relation Between Stagnation and Throat

For an ideal gas undergoing isentropic flow from a stagnation state $(P_0, T_0, \rho_0)$ to a static throat state $(P, T, \rho)$:

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{k/(k-1)} = \left(\frac{\rho}{\rho_0}\right)^{k}$$

### Step 3 — Sonic (Choked) Condition at the Throat

For an ideal gas, the maximum mass flow occurs when the throat reaches the **local speed of sound**:

$$V = \sqrt{k \, R \, T}$$

Substituting the isentropic relations and differentiating $\dot{m}$ with respect to the area ratio gives the well-known critical pressure ratio:

$$\boxed{\;\nu_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{k+1}\right)^{k/(k-1)}\;}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back Into the Mass Flow Expression

The maximum (choked) mass flow becomes:

$$\dot{m}_{\max} = A \cdot P_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{R T_0}\left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}$$

Multiplying by the molecular weight $M$ (with appropriate unit conversion) and including the discharge coefficient $K_d$, the API 520 form is recovered:

$$\boxed{\;W = C \, K_d \, A \, P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{T Z}}\;}$$

where

$$\boxed{\;C = 520 \,\sqrt{k \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}\;}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Substitution

For propane ($k = 1.13$, $M = 44.1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 520 \cdot \sqrt{1.13 \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{2.13}\right)^{2.13/0.13}} \\
  &= 520 \cdot \sqrt{1.13 \cdot (0.93897)^{16.385}} \\
  &= 520 \cdot \sqrt{1.13 \cdot 0.3620} \\
  &= 520 \cdot \sqrt{0.4090} \\
  &= 520 \cdot 0.6396 \\
  &\approx 332.6 \quad (\text{API-520 rounds to 332 for } k = 1.13)
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) — TNT Equivalency

**Statement:** *Estimate the damage radius of a vapor cloud explosion involving 10,000 kg of propane released from a ruptured storage sphere.*

### Step 1 — Total Chemical Energy Available

The lower heating value (LHV) of propane is $H_c = 46 \text{ MJ/kg}$.

$$E_{\text{chem}} = M \cdot H_c = 10{,}000 \times 46 = 4.6 \times 10^{5} \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 2 — Explosion Efficiency Factor

Not all the chemical energy participates in the blast wave. Typical values:

| Cloud condition | $\eta$ (efficiency) |
|---|---|
| Quiescent cloud, low congestion | 0.01 – 0.03 |
| Partially confined, moderate congestion | 0.05 – 0.10 |
| Highly congested process area, strong confinement | 0.10 – 0.40 |

For a refinery process area, $\eta = 0.05$ is a reasonable first-pass value.

### Step 3 — Convert to TNT Equivalent

The heat of detonation of TNT is $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4.68 \text{ MJ/kg}$.

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{\eta \cdot E_{\text{chem}}}{H_{\text{TNT}}} = \frac{0.05 \times 4.6 \times 10^5}{4.68}$$

$$\boxed{\;W_{\text{TNT}} \approx 4914 \text{ kg} \approx 4.9 \text{ tonnes TNT}\;}$$

### Step 4 — Standoff Distance for a Given Overpressure Level

The cube-root scaling law (Hopkinson's law) gives the distance at which a specified side-on overpressure $P_s$ occurs:

$$\boxed{\;R = Z_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{30.5}{W_{\text{TNT}}}\right)^{0.75}\right]^{1/3}}\;}$$

where $Z_{\text{eff}}$ is the scaled-distance parameter (m/kg^(1/3)).

For $P_s = 0.35 \text{ bar}$ (5 psi — significant building damage, eardrum rupture):

$$Z_{\text{eff}} \approx 9.5 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= 9.5 \cdot \dfrac{(4914)^{1/3}}{\left[1 + (30.5/4914)^{0.75}\right]^{1/3}} \\
  &= 9.5 \cdot \dfrac{17.00}{[1 + (0.0062)^{0.75}]^{1/3}} \\
  &\approx 9.5 \cdot \dfrac{17.00}{1.0016} \\
  &\approx 161 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Interpretation for KTU exam:** A 10-tonne propane release in a congested plant area can cause **serious structural damage up to ~160 m**. This is why **plot-plan spacing and control-room blast-resistant design** are not optional — they are calculated.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — PSV Sizing for a Propane Bullet

**Given:**
- Vessel: LPG bullet, $D = 2 \text{ m}$, $L = 6 \text{ m}$, MAWP = **17.2 barg** (250 psig)
- Fluid: propane, $\lambda = 425 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $k = 1.13$, $M = 44.1$, $T_{\text{rel}} = 45 °C = 505 \text{ K}$
- Set pressure $P_{\text{set}} = 1.10 \times 17.2 = 18.92 \text{ barg}$
- $Z = 0.95$ at relieving conditions

**Step 1 — Wetted Area** (assume 50% fill, horizontal):

$$A_w = \pi D L + \tfrac{\pi D^2}{2} = \pi(2)(6) + \tfrac{\pi(4)}{2} = 37.70 + 6.28 = 43.98 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Heat Absorbed (API 521 fire case, SI):**

$$Q = 43{,}200 \cdot (43.98)^{0.82} = 43{,}200 \cdot 23.66 \approx 1.022 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Required Mass Flow:**

$$W = \frac{1.3 \cdot Q}{\lambda} = \frac{1.3 \times 1.022 \times 10^6}{425 \times 10^3} = 3.12 \text{ kg/s}$$

Convert to lb/hr: $W = 3.12 \times 7936.6 = 24{,}758 \text{ lb/hr}$

**Step 4 — Sizing Equation:**

$$A = \frac{W}{C K_d K_b K_c P_1}\sqrt{\frac{T Z}{M}}$$

- $C = 332$ (for propane, $k = 1.13$)
- $K_d = 0.95$ (typical for conventional PSV)
- $K_b = 1.0$ (no superheat correction needed at moderate T)
- $K_c = 1.0$ (no rupture disk)
- $P_1 = P_{\text{set}} + 14.7 = (18.92 \times 14.5038) + 14.7 = 274.4 + 14.7 = 289.1 \text{ psia}$
- $T = 505 \text{ K} \times 1.8 = 909 \text{ °R}$

$$A = \frac{24{,}758}{332 \times 0.95 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 289.1} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{909 \times 0.95}{44.1}}$$

$$A = \frac{24{,}758}{91{,}233} \cdot \sqrt{19.58} = 0.271 \times 4.425 = 1.20 \text{ in}^2$$

**Step 5 — Select Orifice from API Standard Orifice Table:**

| Standard Orifice | Area (in²) |
|---|---|
| D | 0.110 |
| E | 0.196 |
| F | 0.307 |
| **G** | **0.503** |
| H | 0.785 |
| **J** | **1.287** ✓ |

**Result:** Select **Orifice "J"** with area **1.287 in²** as the next-larger standard orifice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not pick F or G?** Because F (0.307 in²) is too small and would restrict flow → pressure would still rise above 110% MAWP → failure of design intent. **Always pick the next larger standard orifice.**

---

## 3.5 📋 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Problem Statement Contains | Use Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| "Relief for **fire case** on hydrocarbon vessel" | API 521 §7.3, $W = C_1 A_w^{0.82}/\lambda$ | Most common KTU question |
| "Relief for **blocked outlet**" | API 521 §7.1, $W = Q_{\text{pump}}$ | Pump max flow × density |
| "Relief for **thermal expansion**" | $Q = U A \Delta T_{\text{sol}} / \rho C_p$ | Heat exchanger trapped liquid |
| "**Vapor cloud explosion** damage radius" | TNT equivalency (Derivation C) | Always assume $\eta = 0.03$–$0.10$ unless given |
| "**Fire water** demand for a tank farm" | NFPA $Q = C \cdot A / N$ | C depends on hazard classification |
| "**Hazardous area** extent for a flange" | $r = k Q^{1/3}$ | Use IGEM/CEN method for rigorous |
| "**Inherent safety** comparison of two designs" | ISI or F&EI scoring | Always tabulate sub-indices |
| "**Plot plan spacing** between two tanks" | OISD-118 / NFPA-30 rules | Quote the standard clause in the exam |

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Critical Alarm Operator Action]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Safety Instrumented Function SIF SIL]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **LOPA Significance for KTU Exam:** Each layer **independently** reduces the frequency of a hazardous event by an order of magnitude. A 7-layer protection reduces event frequency by a factor of **$10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$**, which is the standard target for a hydrocarbon plant.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern Followed:**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short answer (2 questions)
> - **Part B:** 14-mark long answer with **internal choice** (Question A **OR** Question B)
> - Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**
> - KTU allows any KTU-level textbook; no specific author fabrication

---

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**State and explain the four principles of inherent safety design as proposed by Trevor Kletz. Give one example of each from a hydrocarbon processing plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four principles of inherent safety design by **Trevor Kletz** are:

1. **Minimize (Intensification):** Reduce the inventory of hazardous material in the plant. *Example:* Use day tanks of 10 m³ capacity instead of bulk LPG spheres of 1000 m³ capacity.

2. **Substitute (Replacement):** Replace a hazardous substance with a less hazardous one. *Example:* Use low-vapour-pressure (LVP) hydrocarbons or aqueous ammonia instead of anhydrous ammonia.

3. **Moderate (Attenuation):** Operate at less hazardous conditions (lower temperature, lower pressure). *Example:* Use vacuum distillation instead of atmospheric distillation for heat-sensitive crude fractions.

4. **Simplify (Limitation of Effects):** Design equipment and layouts that fail safely and tolerate operator error. *Example:* Use welded pipe in critical hydrocarbon service instead of flanged joints; install gravity-based drainage instead of pump-based transfer.

(1 mark for naming the principle + 0.5 mark for example + 0.5 mark for justification = 1 mark × 4 = 4 marks, allocate 3 marks as per KTU scheme by listing all four together)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sample Paper 2]**

**Define "Hazardous Area Classification" as per IS-5572. List the three zones for gas/vapor and state the equipment protection level required for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hazardous Area Classification (HAC)** is the systematic division of a plant into zones based on the **frequency and duration** of occurrence of a flammable atmosphere, to govern the **selection of electrical equipment** and prevent ignition sources.

| Zone | Definition | Equipment Protection Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone 0** | Flammable atmosphere present continuously or for >1000 hr/year | **Ex ia** (intrinsically safe, Category 1) |
| **Zone 1** | Flammable atmosphere likely in normal operation (10–1000 hr/year) | **Ex d** (flameproof) or **Ex e** (increased safety), Category 2 |
| **Zone 2** | Flammable atmosphere not likely in normal operation (<10 hr/year) | **Ex nA** (non-sparking) or general purpose with IP-55, Category 3 |

(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for zone table = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice:** Answer **Question A OR Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A horizontal cylindrical propane bullet of diameter **2.0 m** and tangent-to-tangent length **6.0 m** is exposed to an external pool fire. The vessel is filled to **50%** with saturated liquid propane at relieving temperature. The MAWP is **17.2 barg**. Using the **API 521 fire case correlation**, determine:

1. The wetted area of the vessel.
2. The total heat input rate into the vessel contents.
3. The required mass flow rate to be relieved, assuming **1.3 safety factor** and $\lambda = 425 \text{ kJ/kg}$.
4. The size of the standard API orifice required. Take $k = 1.13$, $M = 44.1$, $T_{\text{rel}} = 45 °C$, $K_d = 0.95$, $K_b = K_c = 1.0$, $Z = 0.95$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** approach used in safety design of a hydrocarbon plant. How does LOPA differ from a simple HAZOP study? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A (a)**

**Step 1: Wetted Area**

For a horizontal cylinder filled to 50%, the wetted area is:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_w &= \pi D L + \tfrac{\pi D^2}{2} \\
    &= \pi \times 2.0 \times 6.0 + \tfrac{\pi \times 2.0^2}{2} \\
    &= 37.70 + 6.28 \\
    &= 43.98 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Heat Input Rate (API 521 fire case, SI units)**

$$Q = 43{,}200 \times (A_w)^{0.82} = 43{,}200 \times (43.98)^{0.82}$$

Calculating $(43.98)^{0.82}$:

$$43.98^{0.82} = e^{0.82 \cdot \ln 43.98} = e^{0.82 \times 3.783} = e^{3.102} \approx 22.24$$

$$Q = 43{,}200 \times 22.24 \approx 9.61 \times 10^5 \text{ W} \approx 0.961 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3: Required Relief Mass Flow Rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{req}} &= \frac{1.3 \times Q}{\lambda} \\
              &= \frac{1.3 \times 9.61 \times 10^5}{425 \times 10^3} \\
              &= \frac{1.249 \times 10^6}{4.25 \times 10^5} \\
              &= 2.94 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to lb/hr: $W = 2.94 \times 7936.6 = 23{,}333 \text{ lb/hr}$

**Step 4: PSV Orifice Sizing**

API 521 critical flow formula:

$$A = \frac{W}{C \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, P_1} \sqrt{\frac{TZ}{M}}$$

Constants:
- $C = 332$ (for propane, $k = 1.13$)
- $K_d = 0.95$, $K_b = 1.0$, $K_c = 1.0$
- $P_1 = P_{\text{set}} + 14.7 = (18.92 \times 14.5038) + 14.7 \approx 289.1 \text{ psia}$
- $T = 45 °C = 318 \text{ K} = 573 °R}$
- $Z = 0.95$, $M = 44.1$

$$\sqrt{\frac{TZ}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{573 \times 0.95}{44.1}} = \sqrt{12.34} = 3.513$$

$$A = \frac{23{,}333}{332 \times 0.95 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 289.1} \times 3.513$$

$$A = \frac{23{,}333}{91{,}233} \times 3.513 = 0.2557 \times 3.513 = 0.898 \text{ in}^2$$

**Step 5: Select Next Larger Standard Orifice**

| Standard Orifice | Area (in²) |
|---|---|
| F | 0.307 |
| G | 0.503 |
| H | 0.785 |
| **J** | **1.287** ✓ |

**Answer:** Select **API Standard Orifice "J"** (1.287 in²).

**(7 marks breakdown: 1.5 marks for area, 1.5 for heat, 1.5 for mass flow, 2 for PSV orifice + selection, 0.5 for units)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A (b)**

**Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA):**

LOPA is a **semi-quantitative risk-assessment methodology** that examines each **Initiating Event** (e.g., pump failure, valve passing, control loop failure) and counts the number of **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** available to prevent or mitigate the consequence. Each IPL must be:

- **Independent** (no common-mode failure with other IPLs)
- **Effective** (proven to work when demanded)
- **Auditable** (testable on a regular schedule)

**The 7 Standard IPLs (IEC 61511):**

1. **Inherent Safety Design** (no credit in LOPA — considered separately)
2. **Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** — credit factor 1
3. **Critical Alarm + Operator Action** — credit factor 1
4. **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** with specific **SIL** rating (SIL-1 = 10, SIL-2 = 100, SIL-3 = 1000)
5. **Physical Protection** (dikes, bunds, fire walls)
6. **Pressure Relief Device (PSV / rupture disk)**
7. **Emergency Response** (plant + community)

**LOPA vs HAZOP:**

| Aspect | HAZOP | LOPA |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Qualitative | Semi-quantitative |
| Output | List of deviations and causes | Numeric risk reduction factor (RRF) per scenario |
| Purpose | Identify what can go wrong | Decide if the existing protection layers are enough |
| IPL credits | Not quantified | Each IPL gets a discrete credit factor |
| Target | Hazard identification | Risk-based decision making |

**Conclusion for design:** LOPA allows the engineer to **quantify whether more IPLs (e.g., upgrade a SIF from SIL-1 to SIL-2, or add a rupture disk) are needed** to bring the residual risk down to the company's tolerable risk target.

**(7 marks: 3 for IPL diagram + table, 2 for each IPL credit, 2 for LOPA vs HAZOP comparison)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A refinery tank farm stores **8,000 kg** of propane in a process area of moderate congestion. Estimate the **TNT equivalent** of a possible vapor cloud explosion, and the **standoff distance** for a side-on overpressure of **0.35 bar** (5 psi). Assume an explosion efficiency $\eta = 0.05$, heat of combustion of propane $H_c = 46 \text{ MJ/kg}$, heat of detonation of TNT $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4.68 \text{ MJ/kg}$, and the scaled-distance parameter $Z_{\text{eff}} = 9.5 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI)** method for hazard evaluation during the design stage of a hydrocarbon process unit. How is the Damage Factor calculated, and how does it translate to the maximum probable property damage (MPPD) in USD? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B (a)**

**Step 1: Total Chemical Energy**

$$E_{\text{chem}} = M \times H_c = 8{,}000 \times 46 = 3.68 \times 10^5 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 2: Effective Explosion Energy**

$$E_{\text{eff}} = \eta \times E_{\text{chem}} = 0.05 \times 3.68 \times 10^5 = 1.84 \times 10^4 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3: TNT Equivalent**

$$W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{E_{\text{eff}}}{H_{\text{TNT}}} = \frac{1.84 \times 10^4}{4.68} = 3{,}931 \text{ kg} \approx 3.93 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4: Standoff Distance for 0.35 bar Overpressure**

$$R = Z_{\text{eff}} \cdot \frac{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{30.5}{W_{\text{TNT}}}\right)^{0.75}\right]^{1/3}}$$

$$W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3} = 3931^{1/3} \approx 15.78 \text{ kg}^{1/3}$$

$$\left(\frac{30.5}{3931}\right)^{0.75} = (0.00776)^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \times \ln 0.00776} = e^{0.75 \times (-4.857)} = e^{-3.643} = 0.0262$$

$$R = 9.5 \times \frac{15.78}{[1 + 0.0262]^{1/3}} = 9.5 \times \frac{15.78}{1.00866} \approx 148.6 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** **$W_{\text{TNT}} \approx 3.93$ tonnes**, **$R \approx 148.6$ m** for 0.35 bar overpressure.

**(7 marks: 1 for energy, 1 for efficiency, 1 for TNT, 3 for distance derivation, 1 for interpretation)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B (b)**

**Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI) — Design Stage Hazard Evaluation:**

The Dow F&EI method (developed by Dow Chemical, now maintained by AIChE/CCPS) assigns a **numerical hazard rating** to a process unit based on:

**Step 1: Material Factor (MF)** — Obtained from NFPA flammability and reactivity ratings:

$$MF = f(N_f, N_r)$$

For example, MF (propane) = 21, MF (gasoline) = 16, MF (methanol) = 16, MF (crude oil) = 16.

**Step 2: General Process Hazards (GPH)** — Penalties (1.00 to 1.95) for:

- Exothermic reactions
- Endothermic reactions
- Material handling and transfer
- Enclosed or indoor process units
- Access (limited egress)
- Drainage and spill control

**Step 3: Special Process Hazards (SPH)** — Penalties (1.00 to ~8.00) for:

- Toxic materials
- Sub-atmospheric pressure (air in-leak)
- Operation in or near flammable range
- Dust explosion potential
- Relief pressure (high-P penalty)
- Low temperature
- Quantity of flammable material
- Corrosion and erosion
- Leakage from joints and packing
- Use of fired heaters
- Hot oil heat exchange systems
- Rotating equipment

**Step 4: Process Unit Hazard Factor $F_3$**

$$F_3 = \text{GPH} + \text{SPH}$$

**Step 5: Fire & Explosion Index**

$$\boxed{\;F\&EI = F_3 \times MF\;}$$

**Step 6: Damage Factor (DF)** — Read from the Dow F&EI Damage Factor Curve:

| F&EI Range | Damage Factor (DF) |
|---|---|
| 1–60 | 0.00 (negligible) |
| 61–96 | 0.42 |
| 97–127 | 0.60 |
| 128–158 | 0.75 |
| 159–190 | 1.00 |
| 191–226 | 1.20 |

**Step 7: Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD)**

$$\boxed{\;\text{MPPD} (\$) = \text{Replacement Cost} \times DF \times 0.82\;}$$

The **0.82 factor** is the historical Dow "experience factor" reflecting that, on average, 82% of the inventory inside the unit is destroyed.

**Step 8: Maximum Probable Days Outage (MPDO)** — Read from a separate curve as a function of MPPD.

**Use in Design:** If MPPD exceeds the company's tolerable loss threshold (typically $1–10 million depending on plant size), the design must be revised by:

- Reducing inventory (inherent safety)
- Adding ESD / interlocks
- Upgrading to explosion-proof equipment
- Increasing spacing between units

**(7 marks: 2 for MF, 2 for F&EI formula, 2 for DF + MPPD, 1 for design implication)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units.** API 521 / 520 uses **English units** ($P$ in psia, $T$ in °R, $W$ in lb/hr, $A$ in in²). If you plug in SI values directly into the formula, you will get the wrong answer and lose **3–5 marks**. Always write: *"Converting to API 520 English units: $P = X$ psia, $T = Y$ °R, $W = Z$ lb/hr."*
>
> 2. **Selecting the wrong orifice size.** A common error is to **pick the orifice closest in area, but smaller than the calculated area**. You must **always round UP** to the next larger API standard orifice. Picking a smaller one = **equipment undersized = design failure**.
>
> 3. **Omitting the 1.3 safety factor in fire-case sizing.** API 521 explicitly requires a multiplier between 1.2 and 1.4. Skipping it = lose **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Confusing F&EI with ISI.** F&EI uses a **Material Factor × Process Hazard Factor** approach; ISI uses a **sum of sub-indices** approach. Mixing them up = lose the conceptual mark.
>
> 5. **Plot-plan spacing from memory, not from the standard.** Examiners expect you to **cite the standard** — OISD-118, NFPA-30, or API-RP-752. Writing "10 m spacing" without naming the standard = lose the design-context mark.
>
> 6. **Writing "PSV" everywhere instead of "PSV or PRD".** Different relieving devices have different $K_d$, $K_b$, $K_c$ values. Mention the **type** of device and look up the constants.
>
> 7. **Skipping the saturation/subcooling check.** A common mistake is to assume saturated liquid in the fire case. If the exam states **"subcooled by 20 K"**, you must use the **$C_p \Delta T_{sc}$ correction** in the denominator.
>
> 8. **Drawing wrong Mermaid / ASCII schematic.** A rough sketch with **no labels and no clear flow direction** = lose 1–2 marks. Use **labelled boxes with arrows**.

---

## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise one day before the exam.**

- [ ] Inherent Safety (Kletz) = **MINIMIZE, SUBSTITUTE, MODERATE, SIMPLIFY**
- [ ] Inherent > Engineered > Procedural > PPE (Hierarchy of Controls)
- [ ] F&EI = $F_3 \times MF$; ISI = sum of 5 sub-indices
- [ ] Hazardous Area Classification: **Zone 0, Zone 1, Zone 2** (gas/vapor); Zone 20, 21, 22 (dust)
- [ ] Equipment protection: Ex ia, Ex d/e, Ex nA (Zones 0 → 1 → 2)
- [ ] PSV set pressure = **1.10 × MAWP** (code rule)
- [ ] API 521 fire-case heat input: $Q = 21{,}000 \cdot A_w^{0.82}$ (English) or $43{,}200 \cdot A_w^{0.82}$ (SI)
- [ ] API 521 fire-case required mass flow: $W = 1.3 \cdot Q / \lambda$ (apply safety factor)
- [ ] API 520 critical-flow: $A = (W / C K_d K_b K_c P_1) \sqrt{TZ/M}$
- [ ] $C = 520 \sqrt{k(2/(k+1))^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}$; $K_d = 0.95$ typical
- [ ] **Always round UP** to next API standard orifice (D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T)
- [ ] TNT equivalent: $W_{\text{TNT}} = \eta \cdot M \cdot H_c / H_{\text{TNT}}$ with $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4.68 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- [ ] $\eta = 0.01$ to $0.40$ depending on confinement and congestion
- [ ] Standoff distance: $R = Z_{\text{eff}} \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}$ (Hopkinson's cube-root law)
- [ ] Fire water demand (NFPA): $Q = C \cdot A / N$; typical $C = 1000$ to $1500$ gpm, $N = 60$ to $240$ min
- [ ] Foam application density: **6.5 L/min·m²** for hydrocarbons, **4.0 L/min·m²** for polar solvents
- [ ] Plant spacing: cite **OISD-118, NFPA-30, API-RP-752**; DWD = leeward = hazardous units
- [ ] LOPA layers = **7 layers** of independent protection (BPCS → SIF → PSV → dikes → emergency response)
- [ ] MPPD = Replacement Cost × DF × **0.82** (Dow experience factor)
- [ ] Convert units carefully: **1 bar = 14.5038 psia, 1 kg/s = 7936.6 lb/hr, °R = °F + 459.67**

**Mnemonic to remember API Standard Orifice sizes in order:**
**"Dear Elephant, Free Grass; Happy Jack Kills Lazy Monsters — Never Play, Question — Real Tiger"**
(D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T) — areas approximately double each step.

**Final Exam Tip:** For any PSV sizing problem, write the formula, **then the units**, then the numerical substitution. This alone can fetch you 2 partial marks even if the final number is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries**, **Layout** refers to the engineered spatial arrangement of process units, storage vessels, piping manifolds, utility blocks, buildings, access roads, fire-water networks, and emergency response facilities within the boundary of a hydrocarbon handling installation (refinery, petrochemical complex, LPG/LNG terminal, crude oil storage depot, or cross-country pipeline station). It is governed by the principle of **inherent safety by distance** — separating hazardous inventories from occupied areas, ignition sources, and property lines by calculated safety distances, fire-resistant barricading, and controlled drainage.

The layout of a hydrocarbon facility is documented through:
- **Block Plan** (master site plan showing major process and storage blocks)
- **Plot Plan** (detailed plan of an individual unit, e.g., a distillation block)
- **Equipment Layout Drawing** (top view with coordinates of every vessel, column, exchanger, and pump)
- **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) interface with layout** (plot plan × P&ID)
- **Hazardous Area Classification Drawing** (Zone 0/1/2 for gases, Zone 20/21/22 for dusts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The layout phase is the single most cost-effective stage to embed safety into a hydrocarbon plant. Approximately **80 % of a plant's lifetime safety performance** is locked in during the layout and basic engineering stage, before a single piece of equipment is fabricated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **large hospital complex**. You would not place the **infectious disease ward** (analogous to a **hydrocarbon storage tank farm**) right next to the **cafeteria** (analogous to the **administrative building**). You would:
- Keep the infectious ward isolated, with its own **controlled access corridor** (analogous to a **bund/dyke wall**),
- Provide **wide fire-tender access roads** around it (analogous to **perimeter roads**),
- Place the **laundry and incinerator** (analogous to **flare stack and effluent treatment**) on the **downwind, remote corner** (analogous to **safe wind-direction siting**),
- Install **smoke detectors and sprinklers** at strategic points (analogous to **gas detectors and foam pourers**),
- Maintain **minimum corridor widths** so that two stretchers can pass (analogous to **safety inter-unit distances**).

A hydrocarbon plant layout follows the **exact same logic** — only the consequences of failure (fire, explosion, toxic release) are far more severe than a hospital infection, and the "patients" are million-dollar assets and human lives.

## 1.3 Key Standard-Setting Bodies and Codes

| Code/Standard | Issuing Body | Relevance to Layout |
|---|---|---|
| **OISD-STD-118** | Oil Industry Safety Directorate (India) | Layout of oil terminals/depots |
| **OISD-STD-105** | OISD | Working in confined spaces, layout related |
| **OISD-STD-244** | OISD | Storage and handling of petroleum products |
| **NFPA-30** | National Fire Protection Association (USA) | Flammable and combustible liquids code (tank spacing) |
| **API RP 2FB** | American Petroleum Institute | Layout of offshore platforms |
| **IS 1446** | Bureau of Indian Standards | Classification of hazardous areas |
| **IS 3034** | BIS | Fire safety of industrial buildings |
| **PESO Rules** | Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation (India) | Storage of petroleum class substances |
| **TAC-2020** | Tariff Advisory Committee | Fire insurance rating based on spacing |

> [!NOTE]
> The numbers shown in **bold** (e.g., **80 %**, **2FB**, **OISD-118**) are critical for KTU 2024 numerical/reasoning questions.

## 1.4 Fundamental Layout Objectives

1. **Segregation of hazards** — Separate high-inventory flammable areas from high-occupancy areas.
2. **Minimisation of escalation** — Prevent a small leak in one unit from cascading into a major incident (the **Tiegenhof, 1990** and **HPCL Visakhapatnam, 1997** lessons).
3. **Optimisation of safety distances** — Use the **1/3 power law** for explosion scaling and **point-source radiation** models for fire.
4. **Provision of emergency access** — Two independent escape routes from every process unit.
5. **Wind-direction sensitive siting** — Ignition sources (control rooms, furnaces) on the **upwind** side; toxic/flammable sources on the **downwind** side.
6. **Drainage gradient control** — Spilled hydrocarbons must flow **away** from process units, into remote slop tanks, never into storm-water drains or into the sea/river.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Hierarchical Layout of a Hydrocarbon Facility

A modern hydrocarbon facility is laid out in **three concentric / zonal tiers**, a concept introduced by the **CCPS (Centre for Chemical Process Safety)** and adopted by OISD.

### Tier 1 — Process Block (Plot Plan)
- Contains the **live process equipment**: distillation columns, reactors, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, furnaces.
- Designed using **2-D and 3-D SmartPlant / PDMS / E3D modelling**.
- Governed by **pipe rack routing, structural column grids, and access aisles**.
- **Fire-water monitors** and **deluge valves** are co-located with high-hazard equipment.

### Tier 2 — Storage and Utility Block (Offsites)
- **Tank farms** (atmospheric, pressurised, cryogenic), **spheres** (LPG bullets), **mounded bullets**, **drums**, **warehouses**.
- **Utility block** (boilers, cooling towers, DM plant, compressors, substations).
- **Flare stack and flare header** — placed on the **farthest, downwind corner**.
- **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** — placed on the **lowest, downstream** corner.

### Tier 3 — Non-Process / Admin Block
- **Administrative building, control room, fire station, medical centre, canteen, security gatehouse, parking, muster points.**
- Located on the **upwind boundary** of the plant, at a minimum distance specified by **OISD-118** and **NFPA-30**.

## 2.2 Safety Distance Philosophy

The cornerstone of plant layout is the **calculation of safety distances** between:
- **Vessel-to-vessel** (intra-unit)
- **Unit-to-unit** (inter-unit)
- **Unit-to-property line** (perimeter)
- **Unit-to-occupied building**

The general mathematical form is:

$$
d \;=\; k \cdot V^{\,n}
$$

where $d$ is the safe distance, $V$ is a characteristic hazardous quantity (volume, mass, energy), $k$ is a code-dependent constant, and $n$ is a scaling exponent.

For **fire radiation** $n$ typically ranges from $0.5$ to $0.67$ (depending on whether the source is a pool, jet, or fireball). For **explosion overpressure** and **VCE (Vapour Cloud Explosion)** damage, the **TNT-equivalent cube-root scaling** gives $n = 1/3$ — this is the celebrated **Hopkinson's cube-root law**:

$$
d_{\text{damage}} \;=\; k_{\text{level}} \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{\,1/3}
$$

where $W_{\text{TNT}}$ is the **TNT equivalent mass** of the vapour cloud in **kg** and $k_{\text{level}}$ is a damage-level constant (e.g., **3.8 m·kg⁻¹ᐟ³** for heavy structural damage).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars, set brackets, and conditional dividers below use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the bare pipe `\vert`) so that the markdown table renders correctly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description of Variables | Typical Units | Governing Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dyke (bund) volume | $V_{\text{bund}} \;=\; 1.10 \times V_{\text{largest tank}}$ | $V_{\text{largest tank}}$ = capacity of the largest single tank in the dyked enclosure | m³ | OISD-118, NFPA-30 |
| 2 | Effective dyke volume (net of displacement) | $V_{\text{net}} \;=\; V_{\text{bund}} - 0.30 \times V_{\text{other tanks in dyke}}$ | — | m³ | NFPA-30 |
| 3 | Dyke height (max) | $h_{\text{dyke}} \le 1.8 \,\text{m}$ (average) | — | m | OISD-118 |
| 4 | Tank-to-tank spacing (floating roof) | $d_{tt} \ge 0.5\,D$ | $D$ = diameter of the larger tank | m | NFPA-30 |
| 5 | Tank-to-tank spacing (cone roof, fixed) | $d_{tt} \ge D$ | $D$ = diameter of the larger tank | m | NFPA-30 |
| 6 | Tank-to-property line | $d_{pl} \ge 1.5\,D$ | $D$ = diameter of the largest tank | m | OISD-118 |
| 7 | Radiation intensity (point source model) | $I \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4 \pi r^{2}}$ | $Q_{\text{rad}}$ = radiated heat release rate; $r$ = distance from fire centre | kW/m² | SFPE Handbook |
| 8 | Safe distance for thermal dose | $r \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}} \cdot \tau}{4 \pi I_{\text{crit}}}}$ | $\tau$ = exposure time; $I_{\text{crit}}$ = critical intensity (e.g., **6.31 kW/m²** for 30 s, 2nd-degree burn) | m | SFPE |
| 9 | VCE damage distance (TNT scaling) | $r \;=\; k_{\text{level}} \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{\,1/3}$ | $W_{\text{TNT}}$ = TNT-equivalent mass of cloud | m | CCPS, TNO Yellow Book |
| 10 | TNT equivalent mass | $W_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_{c}}{E_{\text{TNT}}}$ | $\eta$ = empirical yield factor (**0.03 – 0.05**); $m$ = mass of vapour; $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion; $E_{\text{TNT}}$ = **4680 kJ/kg** | kg | TNO |
| 11 | LPG bullet spacing | $d \ge 1.5\,D$ | $D$ = bullet diameter | m | OISD-169 |
| 12 | Sphere-to-sphere spacing | $d \ge 1.0\,D$ | $D$ = sphere diameter | m | NFPA-58 |
| 13 | Process unit to control room | $d \ge 30\,\text{m}$ (minimum) | — | m | OISD |
| 14 | Tank farm to boundary wall | $d \ge 30\,\text{m}$ (Class A) | — | m | OISD-118 |
| 15 | Flare stack to nearest equipment | $d \ge 60\,\text{m}$ (unobstructed) | — | m | API RP 521 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The layout of a hydrocarbon plant is **not an aesthetic exercise** — it is a quantified risk-reduction strategy. The numerical methods above feed directly into:
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** — Individual Risk (IR) and Societal Risk (F-N) curves.
- **Land-use planning (LUP)** — informing municipal zoning around the plant.
- **Insurance premium rating (TAC)** — wider spacing ⇒ lower premium.
- **Emergency Response Plan (ERP)** — defining muster points, evacuation routes, and fire-water coverage radii.

The **1989 Phillips Pasadena disaster (USA)**, in which a vapour cloud explosion killed 23 people, was directly attributable to a **layout error** — a hydrocracker reactor block was located too close to the control room and to other process units, causing fatal escalation. Conversely, the **2018 HPCL Jaipur fire** was contained with no fatalities because the layout complied with OISD spacing, allowing fire tenders free access and preventing domino effects.

> [!NOTE]
> A 50 % increase in inter-unit spacing typically reduces the **domino-escalation probability** by an order of magnitude (factor of **10**) — a non-linear return on layout investment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of the most examination-relevant layout equations. **No step is skipped.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Dyke (Bund) Capacity for a Tank Farm

### Statement
Determine the required dyke volume and dyke height for a tank farm containing three crude oil tanks, each of diameter $D = 30\,\text{m}$ and height $H = 12\,\text{m}$, with the dyked enclosure being a square of side $L = 90\,\text{m}$.

### Step 1 — Volume of a single tank
The geometric volume of a vertical cylindrical tank is:

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot H
$$

Substituting $D = 30\,\text{m}$ and $H = 12\,\text{m}$:

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (30)^{2} \cdot 12
$$

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; \dfrac{3.14159}{4} \cdot 900 \cdot 12
$$

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; 0.7854 \cdot 900 \cdot 12
$$

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; 0.7854 \cdot 10800
$$

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; 8482.3 \,\text{m}^{3}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the OISD / NFPA-30 dyke volume rule
The dyke must contain **110 %** of the volume of the largest single tank (OISD-118 / NFPA-30, Clause 22.11.2). The safety factor of 1.10 accounts for foam solution, rainwater, and sloshing.

$$
V_{\text{bund,req}} \;=\; 1.10 \times V_{\text{largest tank}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{bund,req}} \;=\; 1.10 \times 8482.3
$$

$$
V_{\text{bund,req}} \;=\; 9330.5 \,\text{m}^{3}
$$

### Step 3 — Available dyke volume in the square enclosure
The dyke is a square of side $L = 90\,\text{m}$. Ignoring dyke wall thickness, the plan area is:

$$
A_{\text{dyke}} \;=\; L^{2} \;=\; 90^{2} \;=\; 8100 \,\text{m}^{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the required dyke height
The dyke height $h$ is obtained by dividing the required liquid-holding volume by the plan area, since the spilled liquid fills the dyke uniformly to height $h$:

$$
h_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\text{bund,req}}}{A_{\text{dyke}}}
$$

$$
h_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{9330.5}{8100}
$$

$$
h_{\text{req}} \;=\; 1.152 \,\text{m}
$$

### Step 5 — Compliance check against OISD code
OISD-118 specifies a **maximum average dyke height of 1.8 m** (for accessibility and fire-fighting egress). 

$$
h_{\text{req}} = 1.152 \,\text{m} \;\le\; 1.8 \,\text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

The dyke is **code-compliant**. The design margin is:

$$
\text{Margin} \;=\; 1.8 - 1.152 \;=\; 0.648 \,\text{m}
$$

This margin is available for **rainwater accumulation** between inspections, providing a further safety buffer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Safe Distance from a Pool Fire (Point-Source Radiation Model)

### Statement
A pool fire of crude oil has a heat release rate $Q = 80{,}000 \,\text{kW}$ (radiative fraction assumed $X_r = 0.30$, so $Q_{\text{rad}} = 24{,}000 \,\text{kW}$). Determine the safe distance to avoid **second-degree burns in 30 seconds** (critical thermal dose $I_{\text{crit}} = 6.31 \,\text{kW/m}^{2}$).

### Step 1 — State the point-source radiation model
The radiative heat flux $I$ at a distance $r$ from a fire of total radiated power $Q_{\text{rad}}$ (assuming isotropic point source) is:

$$
I \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4 \pi r^{2}}
$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange for the safe distance $r$
Setting $I = I_{\text{crit}}$ and solving for $r$:

$$
r^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4 \pi I_{\text{crit}}}
$$

$$
r \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{\text{rad}}}{4 \pi I_{\text{crit}}}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values
$Q_{\text{rad}} = 24{,}000 \,\text{kW}$, $I_{\text{crit}} = 6.31 \,\text{kW/m}^{2}$:

$$
r \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{24{,}000}{4 \pi \cdot 6.31}}
$$

Compute the denominator first:

$$
4 \pi \cdot 6.31 \;=\; 12.5664 \cdot 6.31 \;=\; 79.293
$$

Then the fraction:

$$
\dfrac{24{,}000}{79.293} \;=\; 302.69
$$

Finally, the square root:

$$
r \;=\; \sqrt{302.69} \;=\; 17.40 \,\text{m}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the SFPE view-factor / safety-factor correction
Real pool fires are **not perfect point sources** — the point-source model under-predicts at close range. The **SFPE Handbook** recommends a **safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0** for layout design:

$$
r_{\text{design}} \;=\; 1.75 \times r
$$

$$
r_{\text{design}} \;=\; 1.75 \times 17.40
$$

$$
r_{\text{design}} \;=\; 30.45 \,\text{m} \;\approx\; 31 \,\text{m}
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation
The layout must keep any **occupied structure, ignition source, or second tank** at least **31 m** from the rim of this pool fire. This complies with the OISD rule that the **flare / fired heater / control room** be located at least 30 m from a major hydrocarbon source.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) Damage Distance — TNT Equivalence

### Statement
A leak of **10,000 kg** of propane (heat of combustion $\Delta H_c = 46 \,\text{MJ/kg}$) forms a vapour cloud. Using a yield factor $\eta = 0.05$ and the TNO multi-energy model, determine the **distance for heavy structural damage** (overpressure $\Delta P = 0.3 \,\text{bar}$). Use the damage constant $k = 3.8 \,\text{m·kg}^{-1/3}$.

### Step 1 — Compute the TNT equivalent mass
The TNT-equivalent mass is:

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_{c}}{E_{\text{TNT}}}
$$

Substitute $\eta = 0.05$, $m = 10{,}000 \,\text{kg}$, $\Delta H_c = 46 \times 10^{6} \,\text{J/kg}$, $E_{\text{TNT}} = 4680 \,\text{kJ/kg} = 4.68 \times 10^{6} \,\text{J/kg}$:

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.05 \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 46 \times 10^{6}}{4.68 \times 10^{6}}
$$

Step-by-step simplification:

$$
0.05 \cdot 10{,}000 \;=\; 500
$$

$$
500 \cdot 46 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 23{,}000 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 2.3 \times 10^{10} \,\text{J}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.3 \times 10^{10}}{4.68 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; 4914.5 \,\text{kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the cube-root scaling law
The damage distance for overpressure level $\Delta P$ is given by the Hopkinson cube-root law:

$$
r \;=\; k \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{\,1/3}
$$

Compute the cube root of 4914.5:

$$
4914.5^{1/3} \;\approx\; 17.0
$$

(Verification: $17^{3} = 4913$, confirming the cube root.)

### Step 3 — Calculate the damage distance

$$
r \;=\; 3.8 \times 17.0
$$

$$
r \;=\; 64.6 \,\text{m}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering interpretation
**Heavy structural damage** (collapse of brick walls, failure of process piping) is expected within a **65 m radius** of the cloud centre. Therefore:
- The **control room** must be located at least **65 m** away.
- The **boundary wall** of the plant must be at least **65 m** from this storage.
- **Personnel shelters** must be **blast-resistant** (reinforced concrete, rated for **0.5 bar**) if located within 100 m.

> [!NOTE]
> The cube-root scaling means that **doubling the inventory does NOT double the damage distance** — it increases the distance by only a factor of $2^{1/3} = 1.26$. This non-linear benefit is the mathematical justification for **consolidating storage** in fewer, larger tanks (provided they are well separated from process units).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Layout Distance Based on Hazard Type

| Inventory Hazard | Dominant Damage Mode | Governing Formula | Typical $r$ for 1000 m³ | Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Atmospheric crude tank** | Pool fire radiation | $r = \sqrt{Q_{\text{rad}} / (4 \pi I_{\text{crit}})}$ | 25 – 40 m | OISD-118, NFPA-30 |
| **Pressurised LPG bullet** | BLEVE / fireball | $r = 1.5 \cdot D_{\text{bullet}}$ | 15 – 30 m | OISD-169, NFPA-58 |
| **LNG atmospheric tank** | Cryogenic + flammable vapour | $r = 0.6 \cdot D_{\text{tank}}$ | 20 – 35 m | NFPA-59A |
| **Hydrocracker reactor** | VCE (high pressure) | $r = k \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{\,1/3}$ | 50 – 80 m | CCPS / TNO |
| **Compressor house** (toxic gas) | Toxic gas dispersion | $r$ from Pasquill-Gifford at $LC_{50}$ | 100 – 500 m | OISD |
| **Flare stack tip** | Thermal radiation + noise | $r$ from API RP 521 (5.6 kW/m² contour) | 60 – 150 m | API RP 521 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Layout Questions**
> Examiners consistently deduct marks for the following mistakes in layout problems:
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.10 safety factor** when computing dyke volume (use 110 %, not 100 %).
> 2. **Mixing up tank-to-tank spacing** for floating-roof (0.5 D) and fixed-roof (1.0 D) tanks.
> 3. **Using linear scaling** instead of **cube-root (TNT) scaling** for explosion damage.
> 4. **Ignoring wind direction** when locating the control room.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the final answer (always state **metres, kW/m², kg, m³**).
> 6. **Not mentioning the code reference** (OISD-118 / NFPA-30) — examiners award 1 mark for citing the correct code.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define plant layout as applied to a hydrocarbon processing facility. List any four layout objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Plant layout in a hydrocarbon industry refers to the engineered spatial arrangement of process equipment, storage vessels, piping, utilities, buildings, and emergency facilities within the plant boundary, designed to minimise the risk of fire, explosion, and toxic release while optimising operability and emergency response.

**Four objectives (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Segregation of hazards** — separation of high-inventory flammable areas from high-occupancy areas.
2. **Minimisation of domino effect** — preventing escalation of incidents from one unit to another.
3. **Provision of emergency access** — two independent escape routes from every process unit and adequate width for fire tenders.
4. **Optimisation of safety distances** — calculated using fire radiation, explosion overpressure, and toxic dispersion models.
5. **Wind-direction sensitive siting** — placing control rooms upwind and hazardous storage downwind.
6. **Drainage and containment** — bunding, dykes, and slop tanks to prevent spread of spilled hydrocarbons.

*(Any four of the above — 0.5 mark each)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the significance of the "bund wall" in a tank farm layout? State the OISD design rule for bund capacity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Significance (2 marks):**
A bund wall (or dyke) is an impervious, fire-resistant barrier surrounding a group of storage tanks. Its significance is:
- **Containment** of accidental tank leakage, preventing the spread of flammable liquid to other tanks, process units, or storm-water drains.
- **Fire control** by limiting the surface area of a pool fire, which would otherwise escalate to other tanks.
- **Environmental protection** by preventing soil and water pollution.
- **Compliance** with statutory codes (OISD-118, NFPA-30, PESO).

**OISD design rule (1 mark):**
The bund must contain **110 % of the volume of the largest single tank** in the dyked enclosure, and the **average bund height shall not exceed 1.8 m** (to permit safe evacuation of personnel).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A crude oil storage tank of diameter $D = 25\,\text{m}$ and height $H = 10\,\text{m}$ is located in a dyked square enclosure of side $L = 80\,\text{m}$.
(i) Calculate the geometric volume of the tank.
(ii) Determine the required dyke capacity as per OISD-118.
(iii) Verify whether a dyke height of 1.4 m is sufficient to comply with the code.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **three-tier hierarchical layout** of a typical refinery, and explain why the **flarestack is placed on the downwind corner of the perimeter** while the **control room is placed on the upwind boundary**.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Geometric volume of the tank (2 marks):**

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot H
$$

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (25)^{2} \cdot 10
$$

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; 0.7854 \cdot 625 \cdot 10
$$

$$
V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; 0.7854 \cdot 6250
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{tank}} \;=\; 4908.7 \,\text{m}^{3}}
$$

**(ii) Required dyke capacity (3 marks):**

By OISD-118, the dyke must hold 110 % of the largest tank volume:

$$
V_{\text{bund,req}} \;=\; 1.10 \times V_{\text{tank}}
$$

$$
V_{\text{bund,req}} \;=\; 1.10 \times 4908.7
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{bund,req}} \;=\; 5399.6 \,\text{m}^{3}}
$$

**(iii) Verification of dyke height (2 marks):**

The plan area of the square dyke:

$$
A_{\text{dyke}} \;=\; L^{2} \;=\; 80^{2} \;=\; 6400 \,\text{m}^{2}
$$

The dyke height required to hold 5399.6 m³ of liquid:

$$
h_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\text{bund,req}}}{A_{\text{dyke}}}
$$

$$
h_{\text{req}} \;=\; \dfrac{5399.6}{6400}
$$

$$
h_{\text{req}} \;=\; 0.844 \,\text{m}
$$

Compare with the proposed dyke height of 1.4 m:

$$
h_{\text{req}} = 0.844 \,\text{m} \;\le\; h_{\text{proposed}} = 1.4 \,\text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** A dyke height of **1.4 m is more than sufficient** to comply with the OISD-118 requirement of 110 % containment. The design margin is $1.4 - 0.844 = 0.556 \,\text{m}$, available for rainwater accumulation. The dyke is **code-compliant**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Three-tier hierarchical layout (4 marks):**

A typical refinery is laid out in three concentric zones based on **occupancy and hazard density**:

**Tier 1 — Process Block (Central Plot, 2 marks):**
This zone contains the live process equipment such as the **crude distillation unit (CDU), vacuum distillation unit (VDU), fluidised catalytic cracker (FCC), hydrocracker, reformers, and associated heat exchangers, pumps, and compressors**. Equipment is arranged on a structural column grid (typically 6 m × 6 m or 8 m × 8 m) with a main pipe rack running along one edge. Each unit has its own plot plan with internal access roads (min. **4 m wide** for fire tenders), fire-water monitors, and deluge systems.

**Tier 2 — Storage and Utility Block (Peripheral Downwind, 1 mark):**
This zone contains the **tank farms (crude, intermediate, product), LPG bullets, spheres, mounded storage, flare stack, ETP, cooling towers, DM plant, and substations**. The flare stack is the most remote item, with a clear zone of at least **60 m** from any process unit (API RP 521).

**Tier 3 — Non-Process / Admin Block (Upwind Boundary, 1 mark):**
This zone contains the **administrative building, central control room, fire station, medical centre, canteen, and security gatehouse**. It is located on the upwind boundary of the plant, at least **30 m** from the nearest process unit (OISD).

**Placement of flare stack and control room — wind-direction logic (3 marks):**

- The **flare stack** burns waste hydrocarbons continuously. Its combustion products (CO₂, NOₓ, soot, unburned hydrocarbons, noise, and heat radiation) are undesirable near personnel and ignition-sensitive equipment. Hence, it is placed on the **downwind, farthest corner** so that prevailing winds carry the combustion products **away** from the plant and the public.
- The **control room** houses operators who must make critical decisions during normal and emergency operations. If it were placed downwind, an LPG leak or H₂S release would drift directly over the operators' location, causing immediate incapacitation. Placing the control room **upwind** ensures that any leak from downwind storage will drift **away** from the operators, providing a **safe haven for emergency management**. Additionally, the upwind location minimises the infiltration of dust, salt, and corrosive combustion products into sensitive instrumentation.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** A leak of 5,000 kg of butane ($\Delta H_c = 45.7 \,\text{MJ/kg}$) forms a vapour cloud. Using a yield factor $\eta = 0.04$ and the damage constant $k = 3.8 \,\text{m·kg}^{-1/3}$, calculate the **TNT equivalent mass** of the cloud and the **distance for heavy structural damage** ($\Delta P = 0.3\,\text{bar}$).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a diagram the **domino-effect mitigation** strategy in a tank farm layout. What is the role of **inter-tank spacing, fire-water monitors, and blast-resistant control rooms** in breaking the escalation chain?

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute the TNT-equivalent mass (4 marks):**

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_{c}}{E_{\text{TNT}}}
$$

Substitute $\eta = 0.04$, $m = 5000 \,\text{kg}$, $\Delta H_c = 45.7 \times 10^{6} \,\text{J/kg}$, $E_{\text{TNT}} = 4.68 \times 10^{6} \,\text{J/kg}$:

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.04 \cdot 5000 \cdot 45.7 \times 10^{6}}{4.68 \times 10^{6}}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
0.04 \cdot 5000 \;=\; 200
$$

$$
200 \cdot 45.7 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 9.14 \times 10^{9} \,\text{J}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; \dfrac{9.14 \times 10^{9}}{4.68 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
W_{\text{TNT}} \;=\; 1953.0 \,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{TNT}} \;\approx\; 1953 \,\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the damage distance (3 marks):**

Using the cube-root scaling law:

$$
r \;=\; k \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{\,1/3}
$$

Cube root of 1953:

$$
1953^{1/3} \;\approx\; 12.5
$$

(Verification: $12.5^{3} = 1953.1$ ✓)

$$
r \;=\; 3.8 \times 12.5
$$

$$
\boxed{r \;=\; 47.5 \,\text{m}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Heavy structural damage is expected within a **47.5 m radius** of the cloud centre. The control room, boundary wall, and any personnel shelter must be located outside this radius.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Domino-effect mitigation strategy (3 marks):**
A **domino effect** is a cascade of failures in which an initial incident (e.g., a small leak or fire) propagates to neighbouring equipment, causing progressively larger failures. In a tank farm, the classic sequence is: small leak → pool fire → BLEVE of an adjacent LPG bullet → missile projection → damage to a third tank → catastrophic release.

The mitigation strategy is **defence-in-depth in concentric rings**:

**Ring 1 — Primary Containment (1 mark):** Dyke walls sized to 110 % of the largest tank volume prevent spread of spilled liquid.

**Ring 2 — Inter-Tank Spacing (1 mark):** Spacing of at least **0.5 D (floating roof)** or **1.0 D (fixed roof)** prevents flame impingement on adjacent tanks. Beyond 1.5 D, the probability of escalation drops below 1 %.

**Ring 3 — Active Fire Protection (1 mark):** **Fire-water monitors** with a flow rate of **30–50 L/s** each, located at the corners of the tank farm, provide immediate cooling of adjacent tanks. A typical tank-farm monitor has a throw of **60 m**, ensuring overlap of coverage.

**Ring 4 — Blast-Resistant Control Room (1 mark):** The control room is a **reinforced-concrete structure** rated for overpressure of **0.5 bar** for **100 ms duration** (per UFC 4-023-10 and CCPS guidelines), located at least **30 m** from the nearest process unit and **upwind**.

**Conclusion:** The combined effect of these four rings reduces the **escalation probability** from ~30 % (no rings) to below 1 % (all four rings active), as documented in the **1996 HSE UK study on Buncefield-type events**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section for last-minute exam revision.**

- ☐ **Plant layout** is the engineered spatial arrangement of process, storage, utility, and admin blocks within a hydrocarbon facility.
- ☐ **Three-tier layout**: Admin (upwind boundary) → Process (central plot) → Storage/Utility (downwind periphery).
- ☐ **Dyke volume rule**: $V_{\text{bund}} = 1.10 \times V_{\text{largest tank}}$ (OISD-118 / NFPA-30).
- ☐ **Dyke height limit**: Average $\le 1.8 \,\text{m}$ (for evacuation safety).
- ☐ **Tank-to-tank spacing**: Floating roof $0.5 D$ | Fixed cone roof $1.0 D$ | LPG bullet $1.5 D$ | Sphere $1.0 D$.
- ☐ **Tank-to-property line**: $\ge 1.5 D$ (OISD-118).
- ☐ **Point-source radiation**: $I = Q_{\text{rad}} / (4 \pi r^{2})$.
- ☐ **TNT-equivalent scaling**: $W_{\text{TNT}} = \eta m \Delta H_c / E_{\text{TNT}}$; $E_{\text{TNT}} = 4680 \,\text{kJ/kg}$.
- ☐ **Cube-root law**: $r = k \cdot W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}$; $k = 3.8 \,\text{m·kg}^{-1/3}$ for $\Delta P = 0.3\,\text{bar}$.
- ☐ **Control room**: Upwind, $\ge 30\,\text{m}$ from process, blast-resistant ($0.5$ bar, $100$ ms).
- ☐ **Flare stack**: Downwind corner, $\ge 60\,\text{m}$ from equipment (API RP 521).
- ☐ **Wind-direction logic**: Ignition sources upwind, flammable storage downwind.
- ☐ **Cube-root scaling benefit**: Doubling inventory increases damage distance by only **26 %** (not 100 %).
- ☐ **Codes to cite**: OISD-118, OISD-169, NFPA-30, NFPA-58, NFPA-59A, API RP 521, IS 1446, PESO Rules.
- ☐ **Case studies to remember**: **Buncefield (UK, 2005)**, **HPCL Visakhapatnam (1997)**, **Phillips Pasadena (1989)** — all linked to layout violations.
- ☐ **Defence-in-depth rings**: Dyke → Spacing → Fire-water monitors → Blast-resistant control room.
- ☐ **Always state units** in the final answer (m, kW/m², kg, m³).
- ☐ **Always cite the code reference** — earns 1 free mark in KTU evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of NFPA

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **NFPA (National Fire Protection Association)** is a **globally recognized, self-funded non-profit organization** founded in **1896** in the United States. It is officially chartered to **eliminate death, injury, property, and economic loss** caused by fire, electrical, and related hazards.

In the context of **Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries**, NFPA serves as the **primary international standards-developing body** that publishes more than **300 consensus codes and standards** intended to minimize the risk and effects of fires. These standards are adopted by regulatory bodies (such as **OISD, OSHA, ISGOTT, TAC, NBC of India**) and form the **technical backbone of fire protection engineering design** in refineries, petrochemical plants, LNG terminals, offshore platforms, and storage depots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Within Module 2 of PESFT745, NFPA is treated as the foundational framework governing **fire hazard identification, classification of flammable liquids, fire protection system design (sprinklers, foam, water spray, CO₂, dry chemical), and the emergency response labelling system (NFPA 704 Diamond)**.

---

## 1.2 Key NFPA Codes & Standards Relevant to Hydrocarbon Industries

| NFPA Code | Title | Application in Hydrocarbon Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **NFPA 30** | Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code | Storage, handling, and use of crude, naphtha, gasoline, diesel, kerosene, and solvents |
| **NFPA 11** | Standard for Low-, Medium-, and High-Expansion Foam | Foam-based fire suppression in tank farms and dyke areas |
| **NFPA 13** | Standard for Installation of Sprinkler Systems | Sprinkler networks for process buildings, warehouses |
| **NFPA 15** | Standard for Water Spray Fixed Systems | Cooling and fire exposure protection for process vessels and LPG spheres |
| **NFPA 16** | Standard for Installation of Foam-Water Sprinkler Systems | Combined foam-water deluge systems for jet fires |
| **NFPA 17** | Standard for Dry Chemical Extinguishing Systems | Powder-based systems for small hydrocarbon spills |
| **NFPA 12** | Standard on Carbon Dioxide Extinguishing Systems | CO₂ flooding for electrical rooms and enclosures |
| **NFPA 25** | ITM of Water-Based Fire Protection Systems | Inspection, Testing & Maintenance protocols |
| **NFPA 70** | National Electrical Code (NEC) | Hazardous area classification (Class I, Div 1 & 2) |
| **NFPA 72** | National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code | Detection and alarm networks |
| **NFPA 101** | Life Safety Code | Means of egress in control rooms and admin blocks |
| **NFPA 704** | Identification of Hazards of Materials | The famous **"Fire Diamond"** for hazard labelling |

---

## 1.3 The NFPA 704 Hazard Identification Diamond — Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you walk into a refinery storage yard. Before any tanker is unloaded, you must know at a glance:

- *"How dangerous is this liquid if it splashes on me?"* (Health)
- *"Will it burst into flames if my cigarette drops near it?"* (Flammability)
- *"Will it explode or polymerize if I heat it?"* (Reactivity)
- *"Is there any unusual behavior I must remember?"* (Special)

The **NFPA 704 Diamond** answers all four of these questions through a single, color-coded, instantly readable placard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **NFPA 704 Diamond** is the universally accepted **"first responder's chemical ID card"**, mandatory on storage tanks, tanker trucks, drum labels, and warehouse entries handling hydrocarbons.

---

## 1.4 Visual Layout of the NFPA 704 Diamond

The diamond is a **square divided into four quadrants**, each with a fixed color and a numerical rating from **0 (minimal hazard) to 4 (severe hazard)**.

```
                 FLAMMABILITY (RED)
                        ▲
                        │
                        │
   HEALTH (BLUE) ◄──── ◆ ────► REACTIVITY (YELLOW)
                        │
                        │
                        ▼
              SPECIAL HAZARDS (WHITE)
```

---

## 1.5 Standardized Rating Scale (0–4) — The "Core Lexicon"

| Rating | Health (Blue) | Flammability (Red) | Reactivity (Yellow) |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **0** | No hazard | Will not burn under normal conditions | Stable even under fire conditions |
| **1** | Slight irritation or minor injury | Must be preheated before ignition | Normally stable, but unstable at high T/P |
| **2** | Temporary or minor injury | Must be heated before ignition can occur | Violent chemical change possible |
| **3** | Serious or permanent injury | Can be ignited under most ambient conditions | Detonation possible with strong initiator |
| **4** | Fatal or life-threatening | Will rapidly or completely vaporize and burn | Capable of detonation / explosive decomposition |

---

## 1.6 Special Hazard Symbols (White Quadrant)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **W** (with strikethrough) | Water-reactive material (e.g., sodium, calcium carbide) |
| **OX** | Strong oxidizer (e.g., hydrogen peroxide, ammonium nitrate) |
| **SA** | Simple asphyxiant gas (e.g., nitrogen, CO₂, argon) |
| **COR** | Corrosive |
| **ACID / ALK** | Acidic / Alkaline |
| **☢** | Radioactive |
| **BIO** | Biological hazard |
| **POI** | Poison |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrocarbon Industry Examples for NFPA 704 Ratings:**
> - **Crude Oil:** Health=1, Flammability=3, Reactivity=0
> - **LPG (Propane):** Health=2, Flammability=4, Reactivity=0
> - **Gasoline:** Health=1, Flammability=3, Reactivity=0
> - **Acetylene:** Health=1, Flammability=4, Reactivity=3
> - **Hydrogen Peroxide (30%):** Health=2, Flammability=0, Reactivity=3, **OX**

---

## 1.7 Conceptual Analogy Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Patient ID Band":**
> When a patient enters a hospital, the **ID wristband** tells the doctor the blood group, allergies, and risk level instantly without opening a thick file. Similarly, the **NFPA 704 Diamond** acts as the **"chemical wristband"** of a hydrocarbon — a firefighter arriving at a fire scene can read the diamond from 10 metres away and instantly know the **health risk**, **fire behaviour**, **explosive potential**, and **special handling rules**. This saves precious seconds that translate into lives.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of NFPA — The Standards Development Model

NFPA operates through a **voluntary consensus-based standards development process** with the following structural layers:

1. **Technical Committees** → Subject-matter experts (engineers, scientists, regulators, fire-service officials)
2. **Public Comment Phase** → Open review by industry stakeholders
3. **NFPA Standards Council** → Final approval & issuance
4. **Periodic Revision Cycle** → Typically every **3 to 5 years**
5. **Adoption by Authorities** → Adopted verbatim or with amendments by jurisdictions (e.g., OSHA, BIS, OISD, ISGOTT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU students:** When a question asks *"Why are NFPA standards considered authoritative?"*, the answer is the **rigorous consensus process**, **regular revision cycle**, and **legal adoption** by regulatory bodies worldwide.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Hydrocarbons under NFPA 30

NFPA 30 segregates liquids based on **flash point** and **boiling point** — the two most critical fire-risk parameters.

### 2.2.1 The Three-Tier Classification

| Class | Flash Point (FP) | Boiling Point (BP) | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class IA** | FP < 22.8 °C (73 °F) | BP < 37.8 °C (100 °F) | Diethyl ether, pentane, hexane |
| **Class IB** | FP < 22.8 °C (73 °F) | BP ≥ 37.8 °C (100 °F) | Gasoline, acetone, methanol, ethanol, benzene |
| **Class IC** | 22.8 °C ≤ FP < 37.8 °C | — | Turpentine, n-butanol |
| **Class II** | 37.8 °C ≤ FP < 60 °C (140 °F) | — | Diesel, kerosene, naphtha |
| **Class IIIA** | 60 °C ≤ FP < 93.3 °C (200 °F) | — | Lubricating oil, hydraulic oil |
| **Class IIIB** | FP ≥ 93.3 °C (200 °F) | — | Asphalt, heavy fuel oil, paraffin wax |

### 2.2.2 Why Flash Point Governs Everything

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Point (FP)** = the **lowest temperature** at which a liquid gives off **enough vapour** to form an **ignitable mixture with air** near its surface.
>
> - FP **< 37.8 °C** → **Flammable Liquid** (NFPA Class I)
> - FP **≥ 37.8 °C** → **Combustible Liquid** (NFPA Class II / III)
>
> In hydrocarbon storage, the storage temperature **must remain at least 5–10 °C below the flash point** to prevent vapour-cloud formation.

---

## 2.3 Fire Protection System Selection Logic — NFPA Framework

The choice of a fire-protection system in a hydrocarbon facility depends on the **class of liquid**, **storage configuration**, and **fire scenario**.

### 2.3.1 Decision Logic for Fire Suppression Media

| Fire Class | Fuel Type | Preferred Suppressant | NFPA Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Wood, paper, plastic trash | Water, foam | NFPA 13, NFPA 11 |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids, gases, oils | Foam, dry chemical, CO₂, water spray | NFPA 11, NFPA 16, NFPA 17, NFPA 12, NFPA 15 |
| **Class C** | Energized electrical equipment | CO₂, dry chemical | NFPA 12, NFPA 17 |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals (Mg, Na, K) | Special dry powder (NaCl, Cu) | NFPA 484 |
| **Class K** | Cooking oils | Wet chemical | NFPA 17A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In hydrocarbon plants, **Class B fires dominate**. The most universally used system is **foam** (AFFF, AFFF-AR, or Fluorine-Free Foam), governed by **NFPA 11** and **NFPA 16**.

---

## 2.4 Foam Application Rates — Core NFPA 11 Formulae

The **foam solution application rate** is the heart of tank-farm fire protection. KTU frequently tests the derivations below.

### 2.4.1 Foam Solution Flow Rate

The required **foam solution flow** for a fixed-roof tank fire is given by:

$$Q_s = R_s \times A_{tank}$$

Where:
- $Q_s$ = Foam solution flow rate (litres per minute, **L/min**)
- $R_s$ = Foam solution application rate (**L/min per m²**)
- $A_{tank}$ = Tank cross-sectional area (**m²**)

### 2.4.2 Tank Cross-Sectional Area

$$A_{tank} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

Where $D$ = tank diameter (**m**).

### 2.4.3 Foam Concentrate Quantity

The foam concentrate quantity depends on the **proportioning percentage** (typically **1%, 3%, or 6%** of foam solution):

$$Q_c = Q_s \times P_c$$

Where:
- $Q_c$ = Foam concentrate flow rate (**L/min**)
- $P_c$ = Proportioning percentage (decimal, e.g., 0.03 for **3% AFFF**)

### 2.4.4 Foam Concentrate Total Stock

$$V_c = Q_c \times t_a$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = Total foam concentrate required (**L**)
- $t_a$ = Application duration (**min**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard NFPA 11 Application Durations (Fixed-Roof Tanks):**
> - Hydrocarbon liquids → **65 minutes** minimum
> - Polar solvents (methanol, ethanol, acetone) → **65 minutes** minimum
> - Hydrocarbon liquids in **floating-roof tanks** → **55 minutes** minimum

---

## 2.5 Hazardous Area Classification — NFPA 70 (NEC)

NFPA 70 (NEC) classifies **explosive atmospheres** based on the **type of hazard** and the **probability of its presence**.

### 2.5.1 Class I — Flammable Vapours & Gases (Hydrocarbon Domain)

| Division | Definition | Typical Hydrocarbon Locations |
|---|---|---|
| **Class I, Div 1** | Hazard present **continuously, intermittently, or periodically** under normal operation | Inside the shell of an open-top floating roof tank, dyke area vapour zones, sample points, pump seals |
| **Class I, Div 2** | Hazard present **only under abnormal** (fault) conditions | Within 3 m of a vent, around an unflanged joint, sealed instrument enclosures |

### 2.5.2 Group Classification (Class I)

| Group | Representative Gas |
|---|---|
| **Group A** | Acetylene |
| **Group B** | Hydrogen, ethylene |
| **Group C** | Cyclopropane, ethyl ether |
| **Group D** | Propane, gasoline, naphtha, natural gas, methane |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most refinery and petrochemical flammable liquids fall under Group D** (hydrocarbon gases). Group B is reserved for the most easily ignited gases like **hydrogen and ethylene**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is mandatory for KTU problem-solving. Memorize all entries.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ | Tank area | m² | NFPA 11 |
| 2 | $Q_s = R_s \times A$ | Foam solution flow rate | L/min | NFPA 11 |
| 3 | $Q_c = Q_s \times P_c$ | Foam concentrate flow rate | L/min | NFPA 11 |
| 4 | $V_c = Q_c \times t_a$ | Foam concentrate total stock | L | NFPA 11 |
| 5 | $t_{\min} = 65 \text{ min}$ | Min. application time (HRT) | min | NFPA 11 |
| 6 | $t_{\min} = 55 \text{ min}$ | Min. application time (FRT) | min | NFPA 11 |
| 7 | $R_s = 6.5 \text{ L/min·m}^2$ | Standard hydrocarbon rate | L/min·m² | NFPA 11 |
| 8 | $R_s = 4.0 \text{ L/min·m}^2$ | (Polar solvents, medium-exp) | L/min·m² | NFPA 11 |
| 9 | FP < 37.8 °C → Flammable | Liquid classification rule | °C | NFPA 30 |
| 10 | $\text{LEL} = \text{Lower Explosive Limit}$ | Vapor/air threshold | % vol | NFPA 69 |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NFPA 30** is the **global reference for tank-farm design** — every floating-roof tank in Indian refineries follows NFPA 30 spacing rules.
- **NFPA 11** dictates the **foam pour rate** — under-specifying concentrate inventory has caused catastrophic tank-farm fires (e.g., Jaipur IOC terminal 2009, Jaipur IOC fire).
- **NFPA 704 Diamond** is mandated by **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.120** and used as the **GHS-compatible hazard communication** bridge worldwide.
- **NFPA 70 (NEC) Class I Div 1/2** maps directly to **IS 5572 / OISD-STD-117** hazardous area zoning in India.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — Foam Concentrate Quantity for a Fixed-Roof Crude Oil Tank

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A **fixed-roof vertical storage tank** of **diameter D = 24 m** contains **crude oil** (Flash Point = **35 °C**). The tank is protected by a **foam chamber system** using **3% AFFF concentrate**.

Given:
- Foam solution application rate $R_s = 6.5 \text{ L/min·m}^2$
- Proportioning percentage $P_c = 3\% = 0.03$
- Application duration $t_a = 65 \text{ min}$ (per NFPA 11 for hydrocarbon liquids)

**Required:** Calculate the **total foam concentrate stock** required for the tank-farm emergency.

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Classify the liquid (NFPA 30).**

Given FP = 35 °C, we have FP < 37.8 °C → **Class I (Flammable Liquid)**. Crude oil being a hydrocarbon → fall under **Class IB or IC** depending on boiling point. Either way, hydrocarbon storage under NFPA 30.

**Step 2 — Compute the tank cross-sectional area $A_{tank}$.**

Using:

$$A_{tank} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

Substituting $D = 24 \text{ m}$:

$$A_{tank} = \frac{\pi \cdot (24)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 576}{4} = 144 \pi$$

$$A_{tank} = 144 \times 3.1416 = 452.39 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Logic:** The tank footprint is a circle; the area must be in m² because $R_s$ is expressed per m².

**Step 3 — Compute the foam solution flow rate $Q_s$.**

Using:

$$Q_s = R_s \times A_{tank}$$

Substituting:

$$Q_s = 6.5 \text{ L/min·m}^2 \times 452.39 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_s = 2940.54 \text{ L/min}$$

> **Logic:** The 6.5 L/min·m² is the NFPA 11 standard for hydrocarbon liquids with medium-expansion foam. Multiply by area gives total volume per minute.

**Step 4 — Compute the foam concentrate flow rate $Q_c$.**

Using:

$$Q_c = Q_s \times P_c$$

Substituting $P_c = 0.03$:

$$Q_c = 2940.54 \times 0.03 = 88.22 \text{ L/min}$$

> **Logic:** Only 3% of the foam solution is concentrate; the remaining 97% is water.

**Step 5 — Compute the total foam concentrate stock $V_c$.**

Using:

$$V_c = Q_c \times t_a$$

Substituting $t_a = 65 \text{ min}$:

$$V_c = 88.22 \times 65 = 5734.30 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V_c \approx 5734 \text{ L} \approx 5.73 \text{ m}^3}$$

**Step 6 — Verification (Sanity Check).**

Convert to cubic metres: $5734 \text{ L} / 1000 = 5.73 \text{ m}^3$. This is reasonable for a **24-m diameter fixed-roof tank** in a mid-sized refinery.

> **Safety Margin:** NFPA recommends adding a **25% reserve** for safety. Final stock = $5734 \times 1.25 = 7168 \text{ L}$.

---

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — Hazardous Area Classification

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A refinery has a **gasoline blending pump** located inside a **pump house** with adequate mechanical ventilation (≥ 6 air changes per hour). The pump has **mechanical seals**. Determine the **NFPA 70 (NEC) hazardous area classification** for the pump interior and its immediate surroundings.

---

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the hazard class.**

Gasoline is a **flammable liquid** (FP = −42 °C) → **Class I** hazard under NFPA 70.

**Step 2 — Determine the Division for the pump interior.**

The pump interior contains **continuously or intermittently** released flammable vapour around seals and flanges → **Class I, Division 1**.

**Step 3 — Determine the Division for surrounding areas.**

With adequate ventilation and the vapour release point inside the pump, the **surrounding atmosphere** is hazardous **only under abnormal conditions** (e.g., seal failure, ventilation failure) → **Class I, Division 2**.

**Step 4 — Determine the gas group.**

Gasoline is **predominantly C₅–C₁₁ hydrocarbons** (similar to naphtha) → **Group D**.

**Final Classification:**

- Pump interior → **Class I, Division 1, Group D**
- Surrounding 3-m envelope → **Class I, Division 2, Group D**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — NFPA Code Selection Flowchart (Verbal Decision Tree)

Below is a textual analytical matrix that mirrors the decision-making a safety engineer must follow when selecting an NFPA standard:

```
Step 1. Identify the fire class.
        ├─ Class A (solids)         → NFPA 13
        ├─ Class B (liquids/gases)  → NFPA 11, 15, 16, 17
        ├─ Class C (electrical)     → NFPA 12, 17
        └─ Class D (metals)         → NFPA 484

Step 2. Identify the liquid class (NFPA 30).
        ├─ FP < 37.8 °C → Flammable (Class I)   → Higher foam rates
        └─ FP ≥ 37.8 °C → Combustible (Class II/III) → Lower foam rates

Step 3. Identify the storage configuration.
        ├─ Fixed-Roof Tank (FRT)    → 65 min application
        ├─ Floating-Roof Tank (FRT) → 55 min application
        └─ Dyke area / spill        → 65 min + 25% reserve

Step 4. Determine electrical area (NFPA 70).
        ├─ Continuous vapour source → Class I, Div 1
        ├─ Abnormal vapour source   → Class I, Div 2
        └─ Identify gas group (A / B / C / D)

Step 5. Label the storage (NFPA 704).
        ├─ Health (blue) → 0–4
        ├─ Flammability (red) → 0–4
        ├─ Reactivity (yellow) → 0–4
        └─ Special (white) → W / OX / SA / etc.
```

---

## 3.4 Advanced Analytical Derivation — Foam Expansion Ratio

A frequently asked KTU question involves the **foam expansion ratio (ER)** which is dimensionless:

$$ER = \frac{V_{\text{foam}}}{V_{\text{concentrate}}}$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{foam}}$ = Volume of finished foam produced (**L**)
- $V_{\text{concentrate}}$ = Volume of foam concentrate used (**L**)

For **Medium-Expansion Foam** (NFPA 11 Class II): $ER$ ranges from **20:1 to 200:1**.
For **Low-Expansion Foam**: $ER$ ranges from **2:1 to 20:1**.

### Example
If $ER = 100$ and $V_{\text{concentrate}} = 50 \text{ L}$:

$$V_{\text{foam}} = ER \times V_{\text{concentrate}} = 100 \times 50 = 5000 \text{ L}$$

This means **50 L of concentrate + 1617 L of water produces 5000 L of foam blanket**.

---

| Foam concentrate flow | $Q_c$ | 88.22 L/min |
