# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> List the **four quadrants of the NFPA 704 hazard identification diamond** along with their **associated colors** and **meaning**.

#### Model Answer

The NFPA 704 Diamond is a placard used to identify the hazards of materials for emergency response. Its four quadrants are:

| Quadrant | Color | Hazard Type | Rating Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top | **Red** | Flammability | 0 (Will not burn) to 4 (Will rapidly vaporize and burn) |
| Left | **Blue** | Health | 0 (No hazard) to 4 (Fatal or life-threatening) |
| Right | **Yellow** | Reactivity / Instability | 0 (Stable) to 4 (Capable of detonation) |
| Bottom | **White** | Special Hazards | Symbols W, OX, SA, COR, POI, etc. |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> Differentiate between **Class I (Flammable)** and **Class II/III (Combustible)** liquids as per **NFPA 30**, using the **flash point criterion**.

#### Model Answer

| Property | Class I (Flammable) | Class II/III (Combustible) |
|---|---|---|
| **Flash Point** | **< 37.8 °C (100 °F)** | **≥ 37.8 °C (100 °F)** |
| NFPA 30 Sub-Classes | IA, IB, IC | II, IIIA, IIIB |
| Examples | Gasoline, acetone, methanol, naphtha | Diesel, kerosene, lubricating oil, asphalt |
| Vapour generation | Continuous at ambient | Significant only on heating |
| Storage risk | High — requires flame arrestors, foam systems | Lower — general fire precautions suffice |

## 5.2 PART B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

> **(a) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the **NFPA 704 Hazard Identification Diamond** with a **neat labelled sketch**, the **rating scale (0–4)** for each quadrant, and the meaning of **three special hazard symbols**.

> **(b) (7 Marks)** A **fixed-roof vertical storage tank** of **diameter 20 m** stores **crude oil** (FP = 40 °C). It is protected by a **3% AFFF foam chamber system** as per **NFPA 11**. Given the application rate $R_s = 6.5 \text{ L/min·m}^2$ and minimum application time of **65 minutes**, calculate the **total foam concentrate stock** required, including a **25% safety reserve**.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
The **NFPA 704 Diamond** is a standardized placard developed by the **National Fire Protection Association (NFPA)** in the USA. It is the **emergency response hazard communication tool** used at refineries, terminals, and warehouses handling chemicals and hydrocarbons. It enables firefighters to identify risks from a distance of up to **15 metres**.

**2. Layout and Colours (2 Marks):**

The diamond is a **square rotated by 45°**, divided into four equal quadrants:

- **RED (Top)** — Flammability
- **BLUE (Left)** — Health
- **YELLOW (Right)** — Reactivity / Instability
- **WHITE (Bottom)** — Special Hazards

**3. Rating Scale (3 Marks):**

| Rating | Health (Blue) | Flammability (Red) | Reactivity (Yellow) |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 0 | No hazard | Will not burn | Stable |
| 1 | Slight injury | Must be preheated to burn | Unstable at high T/P |
| 2 | Temporary injury | Must be heated for ignition | Violent chemical change possible |
| 3 | Serious injury | Ignites under most ambient conditions | Detonation possible with strong initiator |
| 4 | Life threatening | Rapid vaporization & burning | Explosive decomposition |

**4. Special Hazard Symbols (1 Mark):**

- **W (with strikethrough)** — Water-reactive material (e.g., sodium, calcium carbide)
- **OX** — Strong oxidizer (e.g., hydrogen peroxide)
- **SA** — Simple asphyxiant gas (e.g., nitrogen, CO₂)

---

#### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Classify the liquid (NFPA 30).**
FP = 40 °C → FP ≥ 37.8 °C → **Class II Combustible Liquid** under NFPA 30.

**Step 2 — Tank cross-sectional area $A_{tank}$.**

$$A_{tank} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (20)^2}{4} = \frac{400\pi}{4} = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Foam solution flow rate $Q_s$.**

$$Q_s = R_s \times A_{tank} = 6.5 \times 314.16 = 2042.04 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 4 — Foam concentrate flow rate $Q_c$.**

$$Q_c = Q_s \times P_c = 2042.04 \times 0.03 = 61.26 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 5 — Total concentrate $V_c$.**

$$V_c = Q_c \times t_a = 61.26 \times 65 = 3982 \text{ L}$$

**Step 6 — Apply 25% safety reserve.**

$$V_{c,\text{net}} = 3982 \times 1.25 = 4978 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V_{c,\text{net}} \approx 4978 \text{ L} \approx 4.98 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

## 5.3 PART B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss the **NFPA 30 classification of liquids** based on **flash point and boiling point**. Tabulate the classes with examples of hydrocarbons.

> **(b) (7 Marks)** Describe the **hazardous area classification** as per **NFPA 70 (NEC)** for **Class I locations** in a hydrocarbon processing plant. Explain **Class I Division 1 and Division 2** with suitable examples, and state the **gas groups** applicable to hydrocarbon streams.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
NFPA 30 segregates liquids into **six classes** based on **Flash Point (FP)** and **Boiling Point (BP)**. This classification drives every downstream decision — from tank-farm spacing to foam selection and electrical area zoning.

**2. Classification Table (3 Marks):**

| Class | FP Range (°C) | BP Range | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| IA | FP < 22.8 | BP < 37.8 | Diethyl ether, pentane |
| IB | FP < 22.8 | BP ≥ 37.8 | Gasoline, acetone, methanol, benzene |
| IC | 22.8 ≤ FP < 37.8 | — | Turpentine, n-butanol |
| II | 37.8 ≤ FP < 60 | — | Diesel, kerosene, naphtha |
| IIIA | 60 ≤ FP < 93.3 | — | Lubricating oil, hydraulic oil |
| IIIB | FP ≥ 93.3 | — | Asphalt, paraffin wax, heavy fuel oil |

**3. Flammable vs Combustible (1 Mark):**
- **Class I (IA, IB, IC)** → **Flammable liquids** (FP < 37.8 °C)
- **Class II + III (II, IIIA, IIIB)** → **Combustible liquids** (FP ≥ 37.8 °C)

**4. Hydrocarbon Examples (2 Marks):**
- **Crude oil** (FP 30–70 °C) → Class IC or II
- **Gasoline** (FP = −42 °C) → Class IB
- **Diesel** (FP = 52–96 °C) → Class II or IIIA
- **LPG** (FP < −50 °C) → Class IA special category
- **Asphalt** (FP > 200 °C) → Class IIIB

---

#### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
NFPA 70 (National Electrical Code / NEC) classifies areas where **flammable vapours or gases** may be present into **Class I, Division 1 and Division 2** based on the **probability of the hazardous atmosphere being present** under normal or abnormal conditions.

**2. Class I, Division 1 (3 Marks):**
Hazardous atmosphere is **present continuously, intermittently, or periodically** under **normal operating conditions**.

**Examples in hydrocarbon plants:**
- Interior of open-top floating roof tank vapour space
- Pump interiors with mechanical seal leakage
- Sample collection points during product transfer
- Inside the shell of an operating fixed-roof tank during filling

**3. Class I, Division 2 (2 Marks):**
Hazardous atmosphere is present **only under abnormal conditions** such as equipment failure, seal rupture, or accidental release.

**Examples:**
- Within 3 metres of pressure-relief vents
- Around closed but not gas-tight instrument enclosures
- Adjacent (outside) of Class I Div 1 zones

**4. Gas Groups (1 Mark):**

| Group | Representative Gas |
|---|---|
| A | Acetylene |
| B | Hydrogen, ethylene |
| C | Cyclopropane |
| **D** | **Propane, gasoline, naphtha, natural gas** (most hydrocarbons) |

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Points in NFPA-based Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in foam calculations — always carry **L/min, m², minutes** explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing NFPA 30 vs NFPA 11** — NFPA 30 is the **liquid classification**; NFPA 11 is the **foam application**. Students mix them up and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong diamond colors** — the diamond is **NOT** rotated in the same way as the **GHS/ADR placards**. Mark off if student puts "Reactivity on top".
> 4. **Mixing up Class I Div 1 vs Div 2** — remember the mnemonic **"1 = Normal, 2 = Abnormal"**.
> 5. **Missing the 25% safety reserve** in foam-concentrate calculations — examiners **specifically test** this as a final step.
> 6. **Wrong application duration** — students often quote 30 or 45 minutes; the **NFPA 11 standard is 65 min for hydrocarbons (FRT)** and **55 min (floating-roof)**.
> 7. **Group D** is the most common gas group in refineries. Writing "Group B" for gasoline **costs 1 mark minimum**.
> 8. **Special hazard symbols** are placed in the **white (bottom) quadrant**, not bottom-right or anywhere else.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — NFPA for KTU PESFT745 Module 2:**

- ⭐ NFPA is a **US-based non-profit** (founded 1896), publishes **300+ consensus codes**.
- ⭐ **NFPA 30** classifies liquids using **Flash Point & Boiling Point** into **six classes (IA, IB, IC, II, IIIA, IIIB)**.
- ⭐ **Flammable** = FP < 37.8 °C; **Combustible** = FP ≥ 37.8 °C.
- ⭐ **NFPA 704 Diamond** uses **4 quadrants — Red (Flammability), Blue (Health), Yellow (Reactivity), White (Special)**.
- ⭐ Each quadrant uses a **0–4 rating scale**.
- ⭐ Special hazard symbols: **W (water-reactive), OX (oxidizer), SA (asphyxiant), COR, POI**.
- ⭐ **NFPA 11** governs **foam systems**; key rate = **6.5 L/min·m²** for hydrocarbons.
- ⭐ Application duration: **65 min** (FRT), **55 min** (Floating-roof).
- ⭐ Foam calculations: $Q_s = R_s \times A$, $Q_c = Q_s \times P_c$, $V_c = Q_c \times t_a$.
- ⭐ **NFPA 70 (NEC)** — Class I Division 1 = normal ops; Division 2 = abnormal.
- ⭐ **Group D** is the **default hydrocarbon group** in refineries.
- ⭐ Foam concentrate stock must always include a **25% safety reserve** in KTU problems.
- ⭐ Common pitfall: confusing **NFPA 30** (liquid class) with **NFPA 11** (foam application).

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic — NFPA**
> This concludes Module 2's notes on NFPA. Students should now be able to (a) identify any NFPA standard, (b) classify a hydrocarbon liquid under NFPA 30, (c) compute foam concentrate requirements for a storage tank, (d) classify hazardous areas under NFPA 70, and (e) read & construct an NFPA 704 diamond placard.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OISD and SHELL
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** OISD and SHELL

# OISD and SHELL: Safety Standards & Management Systems in Hydrocarbon Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** This topic covers two of the most influential safety governance bodies in the global hydrocarbon sector — the **Oil Industry Safety Directorate (OISD)** of India and the **Shell Group Standards & HSE Management System** framework adopted internationally. Both provide the regulatory and procedural backbone that governs design, operation, inspection, and emergency response of hydrocarbon facilities.

---

## 1.1 What is OISD?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Oil Industry Safety Directorate (OISD)** is the apex *technical and statutory body* under the **Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG), Government of India**, constituted in **1986**, responsible for formulating, implementing, monitoring, and periodically reviewing safety standards for the **petroleum, natural gas, and petrochemical industry** across the entire hydrocarbon value chain in India.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of OISD as the **"Rule Book Authority"** for India's fuel and gas industry. Just as the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** decides what an electrical plug must look like for safety, **OISD decides how a refinery, an LPG bottling plant, or a cross-country pipeline must be designed, operated, maintained, and audited** so that no explosion, fire, or toxic release puts workers or the public in danger.

> **Key Identifiers (Bold Highlights):**
> - **Founded:** 1986 (post-Bhopal disaster era, response to industrial safety needs)
> - **Parent Ministry:** Ministry of Petroleum & Natural Gas (MoPNG)
> - **Head Office:** New Delhi, India
> - **Function:** Standardization, Auditing, Investigation, Advisory
> - **Authority Type:** *Statutory-cum-Technical Directorat*

---

## 1.2 What is SHELL (in Safety Context)?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> In the context of hydrocarbon safety, **SHELL** refers to **Royal Dutch Shell's Group Standards and HSE (Health, Safety & Environment) Management System** — a globally benchmarked corporate safety framework developed by Shell that defines mandatory requirements, guidelines, and Life-Saving Rules for all Shell-operated and Shell-contracted assets worldwide.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

If **OISD** is the *country's law*, then **Shell's HSE framework** is the *private company's constitution*. Where OISD mandates minimum rules for all Indian operators (IOCL, BPCL, HPCL, Reliance, etc.), Shell imposes its own often stricter *internal* rules across its global operations — drilling rigs in the North Sea, refineries in Singapore, LNG plants in Australia, and pipelines in Nigeria.

> **Key Identifiers (Bold Highlights):**
> - **Origin:** Royal Dutch Shell plc, headquartered in **The Hague, Netherlands / London, UK**
> - **Focus:** Process Safety, Asset Integrity, Contractor Safety, Personal Safety, Environmental Protection
> - **Key Tools:** **Life-Saving Rules**, **HSSE-MS**, **Heart** & **Care** frameworks
> - **Authority Type:** *Corporate / Self-Regulatory*

---

## 1.3 Why OISD and SHELL Together?

In KTU Module 2, both are studied together because they represent the **two pillars of hydrocarbon safety governance**:

| Perspective | Body | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **National / Regulatory** | **OISD** | Mandatory for all oil & gas operators in India |
| **International / Corporate** | **SHELL** | Benchmark for global best practices in hydrocarbon safety |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Insight:** KTU examiners frequently frame questions as *"Compare OISD standards with Shell's HSE Management System"* or *"Discuss the role of OISD in ensuring safety of hydrocarbon installations."* Understanding **both** is essential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis: Frameworks, Standards & Reference Sheets

## 2.1 Organizational Architecture of OISD

OISD operates through a structured hierarchy that connects policy formulation with on-ground implementation:

1. **Governing Council** — Chaired by the Secretary, MoPNG; includes CEOs of all major oil PSUs.
2. **Functional Divisions** — Process Safety, Operations, Design, Electrical, Inspection, Environment, Fire Protection, Training.
3. **Standing Committees** — Sector-wise (Refineries, Pipelines, Marketing, LPG, Drilling, E&P).
4. **Safety Audits** — Conducted at PSU sites on a defined frequency (typically every 2-3 years for major installations).
5. **Accident Investigation Cell** — Analyzes incidents and recommends corrective standards.

### Core Functions (The "Why" and "How" of OISD)

- **Standard Formulation** → *Why?* To ensure uniform safety across the fragmented Indian hydrocarbon sector.
- **Safety Audits** → *How?* Through multidisciplinary teams visiting installations against published checklists.
- **Accident Investigation** → *Why?* To convert failures into learning and update standards.
- **Capacity Building** → *How?* Through training programs and competency certifications.
- **Advisory Role to MoPNG** → On policy framing, licensing, and regulatory reform.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield OISD Standards Reference Table

The following table lists the most frequently tested OISD standards:

> [!NOTE]
> The numbers below (e.g., **OISD-STD-117**) are the *official OISD document codes*. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper for this module.

| **Standard Code** | **Title / Coverage Area** | **Application Sector** |
|---|---|---|
| OISD-STD-117 | Fire Protection Facilities for Petroleum Refineries | Refineries |
| OISD-STD-118 | Layout for Oil and Gas Installations | Refineries, Terminals |
| OISD-STD-130 | Cross-Country Hydrocarbon Pipelines | Pipelines |
| OISD-STD-138 | Process Design and Operational Safety | Process Plants |
| OISD-STD-144 | LPG Installations | LPG Bottling, Bulk Storage |
| OISD-STD-150 | Marketing Locations (Retail Outlets) | Petrol Pumps |
| OISD-STD-153 | Fire Fighting Equipment | General |
| OISD-STD-154 | Oil Terminals and Depots | Terminals |
| OISD-STD-156 | Fire Protection System for Airports | Aviation Fuel |
| OISD-STD-160 | Fire Protection for Onshore Tank Farms | Storage |
| OISD-STD-189 | Process Design and Operations | Process Safety |
| OISD-STD-191 | Safety Audits | All Sectors |
| OISD-STD-192 | Safety Management System (SMS) | All Sectors |
| OISD-STD-194 | Inspection of Cross-Country Pipelines | Pipelines |
| OISD-STD-203 | Hydrocarbon Leak Detection | All |
| OISD-STD-226 | Inspection of Static Equipment | Refineries |
| OISD-GDN-206 | Process Safety Management (PSM) | Process Plants |
| OISD-RP-110 | Safe Handling of LNG | LNG Sector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic Aid for Top 5 Exam Favourites:**
> **"117 Fire, 118 Layout, 138 Process, 144 LPG, 191 Audit"** — these five appear in over 80% of past KTU questions on OISD.

---

## 2.3 Shell HSE Management System (HSSE-MS)

Shell's framework is built on **three concentric pillars**:

1. **Goals** — "Goal Zero" (no harm to people, no leaks, no incidents).
2. **HSSE Management System (HSSE-MS)** — 9 core elements.
3. **Life-Saving Rules** — 9 absolute non-negotiable rules.

### The 9 Elements of Shell's HSSE-MS

1. **Leadership & Accountability** — Visible management commitment.
2. **Risk Management** — Hazard identification and control.
3. **People, Competency & Training** — Skilled workforce.
4. **HSE Design** — Inherent safer design in projects.
5. **Operations & Maintenance Integrity** — Asset integrity management.
6. **Management of Change (MOC)** — Controlled modifications.
7. **Information & Documentation** — Knowledge management.
8. **Communication, Consultation & Engagement** — Stakeholder dialogue.
9. **Incident Management** — Reporting, investigation, learning.

### Shell's 12 Life-Saving Rules

> [!WARNING]
> **Life-Saving Rules** are *absolute* — violation means immediate stand-down. KTU examiners love to ask "List any 5 Life-Saving Rules."

| # | Rule |
|---|---|
| 1 | Obtain authorization before entering a confined space |
| 2 | Obtain authorization before overriding or disabling equipment |
| 3 | Protect yourself against a fall when working at height |
| 4 | Do not walk under a suspended load |
| 5 | Do not smoke or use electronic devices near flammable substances |
| 6 | Do not work under the influence of alcohol or drugs |
| 7 | Obtain authorization before hot work in hazardous areas |
| 8 | Wear your seat belt when driving |
| 9 | Comply with journey management plans |
| 10 | Follow prescribed gas testing requirements |
| 11 | Follow safe lifting procedures |
| 12 | Obtain authorization before working on energized systems |

### Shell's HEART Framework (Hazard Identification)

**HEART** = **H**azard identification, **E**nergy isolation, **A**sset integrity, **R**isk assessment, **T**ask management.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Engineering Analysis: OISD vs. SHELL

| **Parameter** | **OISD (India)** | **Shell HSSE-MS (Global)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Statutory (Govt. of India, 1986) | Corporate (Royal Dutch Shell) |
| **Applicability** | All oil/gas operators in India | All Shell-operated assets globally |
| **Legal Force** | **Mandatory** under MoPNG directive | **Mandatory** for Shell & contractors |
| **Standardization** | Numerical standards (OISD-STD-XXX) | Group Standards + Life-Saving Rules |
| **Audit Mechanism** | Periodic external audits by OISD teams | Internal + 3rd-party audits |
| **Coverage** | Refineries, Pipelines, Marketing, LPG, LNG, E&P | All upstream, midstream, downstream |
| **Cultural Approach** | Compliance-driven | Performance + culture-driven ("Goal Zero") |
| **Worker Empowerment** | Through safety committees | Through **Cardinal Rules** & stop-work authority |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Indian PSU refineries (IOCL, BPCL, HPCL) follow **OISD standards as a legal floor**, but benchmark against **Shell's framework** for global best practices — adopting Shell's *Life-Saving Rules* and *HSSE-MS* elements to strengthen their safety culture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Operational Workflows

> [!NOTE]
> This section breaks down the *operational logic* of both systems into structured analytical sequences — exactly how KTU examiners expect you to structure long-answer (14-mark) responses.

---

## 3.1 OISD's Standard Development Workflow

**Step 1 → Identify Hazard / Industry Need**
- A new technology, a major accident, or a regulatory gap triggers review.
- *Example:* After the **HPCL Visakh Refinery fire (1997)**, OISD accelerated fire protection standard revision.

**Step 2 → Constitute a Working Group**
- Industry experts, PSU engineers, academic researchers, and inspectors draft the standard.

**Step 3 → Draft the Standard**
- Covering design, operation, maintenance, inspection, and emergency provisions.

**Step 4 → Peer Review & Industry Consultation**
- Circulated to all oil PSUs, private operators, and professional bodies.

**Step 5 → Approval by Governing Council**
- Final sign-off by Secretary-MoPNG-chaired council.

**Step 6 → Publication & Notification**
- Standard issued as **OISD-STD-XXX** and notified to all operators.

**Step 7 → Periodic Review (typically every 5 years)**
- Standards updated to incorporate technological advances and lessons learned.

**Step 8 → Audit for Compliance**
- OISD teams audit sites against the published standard; non-conformities flagged.

**Step 9 → Corrective Action & Closure**
- Operator submits action plan; closure verified in follow-up audit.

---

## 3.2 Shell's HSSE-MS Implementation Logic

Shell's system follows the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle applied across all 9 elements:

$$
\boxed{
\text{Plan (Identify Risks)} \rightarrow \text{Do (Implement Controls)} \rightarrow \text{Check (Audit/Review)} \rightarrow \text{Act (Improve)}
}
$$

### Step-by-Step Mapping to Shell's Framework

**Step 1 → Set HSSE Policy & Objectives**
- "Goal Zero" vision cascaded from Board to shop-floor.

**Step 2 → Identify Hazards & Assess Risks**
- Tools used: **HAZOP, HAZID, What-If, Bow-Tie Analysis, LOPA**.

**Step 3 → Define Critical Controls**
- For each major accident scenario, define the controls that *must never fail*.

**Step 4 → Implement Controls via Engineering, Procedures, Training**
- Hardware (relief valves, gas detectors), Software (procedures), People (training).

**Step 5 → Verify Performance**
- Field inspections, audits, leading indicators (e.g., Near-Miss reporting rate).

**Step 6 → Investigate Incidents**
- Root-cause analysis: **Why-Tree, Tripod-Beta, 5-Why**.

**Step 7 → Share Learnings Globally**
- **Shell Global HSE Network** disseminates lessons to all assets.

**Step 8 → Continuous Improvement**
- Annual HSSE review by senior leadership.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: OISD vs. SHELL on Five Critical Parameters

The following analytical matrix is structured exactly as a KTU 14-mark question should be answered:

### Parameter 1: **Standardization Approach**

- **OISD:** Uses a *prescriptive* approach — specifies exact distances, wall thicknesses, firewater capacities.
- **Shell:** Uses a *performance-based* approach — defines objectives and lets operators choose the method to achieve them.

### Parameter 2: **Hazard Identification**

- **OISD:** Mandates periodic **HAZOP** for process units.
- **Shell:** Combines HAZOP, **HAZID**, **LOPA**, **Bow-Tie**, and **Human Factors Analysis**.

### Parameter 3: **Audit Mechanism**

- **OISD:** External audits every **2-3 years** by OISD multidisciplinary teams.
- **Shell:** Layered audits — internal (asset), corporate (Shell Group), and external (3rd party).

### Parameter 4: **Incident Reporting**

- **OISD:** Mandates reporting of major accidents to MoPNG.
- **Shell:** All incidents (including near-misses) reported globally; **5-Why** root cause mandated.

### Parameter 5: **Cultural Engagement**

- **OISD:** Relies on statutory compliance and safety committees under Factories Act.
- **Shell:** Uses **Cardinal Rules, Life-Saving Rules, Visible Felt Leadership (VFL)**, and contractor engagement programs.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked to "compare", always present the comparison in a **tabular matrix form** — this saves writing time and makes your answer visually appealing, which improves valuation scores.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OISD Organizational Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Governing Council Chaired by Secretary MoPNG"] --> A1["OISD Directorate New Delhi HQ"]
    A1 --> A2["Process Safety Division"]
    A1 --> A3["Design Engineering Division"]
    A1 --> A4["Inspection Division"]
    A1 --> A5["Fire Protection Division"]
    A1 --> A6["Training Division"]
    A1 --> A7["Environment Division"]
    A2 --> B1["Refineries Sector Committee"]
    A2 --> B2["Pipeline Sector Committee"]
    A2 --> B3["Marketing Sector Committee"]
    A2 --> B4["LPG Sector Committee"]
    A2 --> B5["E and P Sector Committee"]
    B1 --> C1["Issue OISD Standards e.g. STD 117 118 138"]
    B2 --> C2["Issue Pipeline Standards e.g. STD 130 194"]
    B3 --> C3["Issue Marketing Standards e.g. STD 150"]
    B4 --> C4["Issue LPG Standards e.g. STD 144"]
    B5 --> C5["Issue E and P Standards"]
    C1 --> D1["Periodic Audits at PSU Sites"]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    D1 --> E1["Compliance Reports Submitted to MoPNG"]
    D1 --> E2["Accident Investigation Findings"]
```

## 4.2 Shell HSSE Management System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Shell Group HSSE Policy Goal Zero"] --> S1["Nine HSSE Management System Elements"]
    S1 --> S2["1 Leadership and Accountability"]
    S1 --> S3["2 Risk Management"]
    S1 --> S4["3 People Competency and Training"]
    S1 --> S5["4 HSE Design Inherent Safety"]
    S1 --> S6["5 Operations and Maintenance Integrity"]
    S1 --> S7["6 Management of Change MOC"]
    S1 --> S8["7 Information and Documentation"]
    S1 --> S9["8 Communication and Engagement"]
    S1 --> S10["9 Incident Management"]
    S1 --> S11["Twelve Life Saving Rules"]
    S11 --> L1["Confined Space Authorization"]
    S11 --> L2["Override Equipment Authorization"]
    S11 --> L3["Fall Protection at Height"]
    S11 --> L4["No Smoking Near Flammables"]
    S11 --> L5["Hot Work Permit Required"]
    S11 --> L6["Seat Belt Mandatory"]
    S11 --> L7["Gas Testing Before Entry"]
    S11 --> L8["Lifting Procedure Compliance"]
    S11 --> L9["Energy Isolation Lockout Tagout"]
    S11 --> S10_2["Journey Management Compliance"]
    S11 --> S11_2["Drug and Alcohol Prohibition"]
    S11 --> S12["Suspended Load Avoidance"]
    S0 --> S13["HEART Framework"]
    S13 --> H1["Hazard Identification"]
    S13 --> H2["Energy Isolation"]
    S13 --> H3["Asset Integrity"]
    S13 --> H4["Risk Assessment"]
    S13 --> H5["Task Management"]
```

## 4.3 OISD vs. SHELL — Comparative Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REG["REGULATORY DOMIN OISD"]
        O1["Statutory Body MoPNG India"] --> O2["OISD Standards Series STD XXX"]
        O2 --> O3["Mandatory Compliance"]
        O3 --> O4["External Audits"]
        O4 --> O5["Accident Investigation"]
        O5 --> O6["Standard Revision Feedback Loop"]
    end
    subgraph CORP["CORPORATE DOMIN SHELL"]
        C1["Shell Group HSE Policy"] --> C2["HSSE MS Nine Elements"]
        C2 --> C3["Life Saving Rules"]
        C3 --> C4["Goal Zero Performance"]
        C4 --> C5["Global Incident Learning"]
        C5 --> C6["Continuous Improvement PDCA"]
    end
    REG -. BENCHMARKS WITH .-> CORP
    CORP -. COMPLIANCE WITH .-> REG
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define OISD. State any four of its major standards.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The **Oil Industry Safety Directorate (OISD)** is the apex statutory-technical body under the **Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG), Government of India**, established in **1986**, responsible for formulating, implementing, and monitoring safety standards for the hydrocarbon industry.
>
> Four major OISD standards:
> 1. **OISD-STD-117** – Fire Protection Facilities for Petroleum Refineries.
> 2. **OISD-STD-118** – Layout for Oil and Gas Installations.
> 3. **OISD-STD-138** – Process Design and Operational Safety.
> 4. **OISD-STD-191** – Safety Audits.
>
> *(Any four standards with correct numbering carry full marks.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Q2. What is Shell's HSSE Management System? List any three of its elements.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Shell's **HSSE Management System (HSSE-MS)** is a structured corporate framework that defines how health, safety, security, and environment risks are managed across all Shell-operated assets globally, built on the **Goal Zero** philosophy.
>
> Three elements:
> 1. **Risk Management** – Identification and control of process and occupational hazards.
> 2. **HSE Design** – Incorporation of inherent safer design principles in projects.
> 3. **Incident Management** – Reporting, investigation, and learning from all incidents.
>
> *(Mention of Goal Zero + 3 correct elements = full 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — OISD Focused

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the organizational structure, functions, and significance of **OISD** in the Indian hydrocarbon industry. Discuss any **five major OISD standards** and their application areas. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Organizational Structure of OISD:**
> - Apex body under the **Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG)**, formed in **1986**.
> - **Governing Council** chaired by Secretary-MoPNG with CEOs of all major oil PSUs.
> - **Directorate** headquartered in **New Delhi**, with functional divisions: Process Safety, Design, Inspection, Fire Protection, Environment, and Training.
> - **Sector-specific standing committees** for Refineries, Pipelines, Marketing, LPG, E&P.
>
> **Functions:**
> 1. Formulation and publication of safety standards (**OISD-STD-XXX**).
> 2. Conducting periodic **safety audits** at oil & gas installations.
> 3. **Investigation of major accidents** and recommending corrective measures.
> 4. **Advisory role** to MoPNG on safety policy and regulatory matters.
> 5. **Training and capacity building** of industry personnel.
>
> **Significance:**
> - Provides a **uniform national safety framework** across the fragmented Indian hydrocarbon sector.
> - Reduces accidents through **mandatory compliance** with prescribed standards.
> - Acts as the **knowledge backbone** for all Indian oil PSUs and private operators.
>
> **Five Major OISD Standards:**
> | Standard | Application |
> |---|---|
> | OISD-STD-117 | Fire protection in refineries |
> | OISD-STD-118 | Layout of oil & gas installations |
> | OISD-STD-130 | Cross-country hydrocarbon pipelines |
> | OISD-STD-138 | Process design safety |
> | OISD-STD-144 | LPG installations |
>
> ---
>
> **Q. (b)** Compare the **safety management approach of OISD** with that of **Shell's HSSE Management System** under five parameters. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> | **Parameter** | **OISD** | **Shell HSSE-MS** |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Authority** | Statutory (Govt. of India) | Corporate (Royal Dutch Shell) |
> | **Approach** | Prescriptive (specifies exact requirements) | Performance-based (defines goals, allows flexibility) |
> | **Scope** | Mandatory for all Indian operators | Mandatory for Shell + contractors globally |
> | **Audits** | External audits every 2–3 years | Layered (internal, corporate, 3rd party) |
> | **Cultural Driver** | Compliance + statutory committees | Goal Zero + Life-Saving Rules + Visible Felt Leadership |
>
> **Concluding Statement:** While OISD sets the *national legal floor* for hydrocarbon safety in India, Shell's framework represents *global best practices* and is often used by Indian PSUs as a benchmark above OISD compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Do **NOT** list standards without explaining their application area — examiners award marks for *both* the code and the *correct sector mapping*.
> - In comparison questions, **always use a tabular format** — handwritten tables get faster valuation and better scoring than long paragraphs.
> - Avoid writing only OISD for comparison questions — examiners specifically look for *Shell's distinct features* (Goal Zero, Life-Saving Rules).

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Shell Focused

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Model Question]**
> **Q. (a)** Describe in detail **Shell's HSSE Management System (HSSE-MS)**. Explain the **nine core elements** with examples of their practical implementation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Shell's HSSE-MS** is a comprehensive corporate framework built on the philosophy of **Goal Zero** — zero harm to people, zero leaks, zero incidents. It is built on **nine core elements**:
>
> 1. **Leadership & Accountability** — Visible senior leadership engagement; *example:* CEO-led safety walks at refineries.
> 2. **Risk Management** — Systematic hazard identification using **HAZOP, LOPA, Bow-Tie**; *example:* HAZOP conducted during design of LNG trains.
> 3. **People, Competency & Training** — Mandatory safety induction and competency certification for all workers, including contractors.
> 4. **HSE Design** — Inherent safer design principles applied during EPC phase (e.g., minimization of hydrocarbon inventory).
> 5. **Operations & Maintenance Integrity** — Asset Integrity Management System (AIMS) covering critical equipment like pressure vessels and relief valves.
> 6. **Management of Change (MOC)** — Any technical, procedural, or organizational change must go through formal risk assessment before implementation.
> 7. **Information & Documentation** — Centralized **HSSE Case** repository maintained for each asset.
> 8. **Communication, Consultation & Engagement** — Worker participation in safety committees; toolbox talks; safety suggestion schemes.
> 9. **Incident Management** — Reporting of *all* incidents including near-misses; **5-Why** root cause analysis; global learning shared via Shell's HSE network.
>
> ---
>
> **Q. (b)** Discuss Shell's **12 Life-Saving Rules**. Why are these considered "absolute" in Shell's safety culture? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> Shell's **12 Life-Saving Rules** are a set of non-negotiable behaviors that, if violated, could directly result in a fatality. They were developed from analysis of historical high-severity incidents and represent the most critical controls.
>
> **The 12 Rules:**
> 1. Obtain authorization before entering a **confined space**.
> 2. Obtain authorization before **overriding or disabling** safety equipment.
> 3. **Protect yourself** against a fall when working at height.
> 4. Do not **walk under a suspended load**.
> 5. Do not **smoke** or use electronic devices near flammable substances.
> 6. Do not work under the **influence of alcohol or drugs**.
> 7. Obtain authorization before **hot work** in hazardous areas.
> 8. **Wear seat belt** when driving.
> 9. Comply with **journey management** plans.
> 10. Follow prescribed **gas testing** requirements.
> 11. Follow **safe lifting** procedures.
> 12. Obtain authorization before working on **energized systems**.
>
> **Why are they "absolute"?**
> - Violation of any rule triggers **immediate work stoppage**.
> - They are **few in number** (only 12), making them easy to remember and apply.
> - They focus on **high-fatality scenarios** identified from incident data.
> - They empower **every worker (including contractors)** with stop-work authority.
> - They are a **cultural cornerstone** of Shell's "Goal Zero" vision — not just compliance, but a deep belief that every incident is preventable.
>
> **Implementation:** Posted at all work sites, included in every induction, and rigorously enforced through audits and visible felt leadership.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often write only **5–6** of the 12 Life-Saving Rules. Examiners expect **at least 8–10** for full marks.
> - Do **NOT** merely list the rules — the 7-mark question specifically asks *why they are absolute*. Dedicate **at least 2 paragraphs** to the cultural justification.
> - Avoid confusing Shell's **Life-Saving Rules** with Shell's **HSSE-MS elements** — they are *two different* (but related) constructs.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **OISD = Oil Industry Safety Directorate** — statutory body under MoPNG, formed in **1986**, HQ in **New Delhi**.
> ✅ **OISD Functions:** Standard Formulation, Safety Audits, Accident Investigation, Training, Advisory.
> ✅ **Top OISD Standards to memorize:** **117 (Fire), 118 (Layout), 130 (Pipeline), 138 (Process), 144 (LPG), 150 (Marketing), 191 (Audit), 192 (SMS)**.
> ✅ **Shell HSSE-MS** = Goal Zero vision + 9 elements + 12 Life-Saving Rules.
> ✅ **9 HSSE-MS Elements:** Leadership, Risk, People, HSE Design, O&M Integrity, MOC, Information, Communication, Incident Mgmt.
> ✅ **12 Life-Saving Rules** are *absolute* — violation = immediate stand-down.
> ✅ **Shell's HEART Framework:** Hazard ID, Energy Isolation, Asset Integrity, Risk Assessment, Task Management.
> ✅ **Comparison Mantra:** OISD = *prescriptive + statutory + national*; Shell = *performance-based + corporate + global*.
> ✅ **Cross-Connection:** Indian PSUs use OISD as *legal floor* and Shell framework as *best-practice ceiling*.
> ✅ **Exam Favourite Questions:** "Compare OISD & Shell", "List OISD standards", "Explain Life-Saving Rules", "OISD organizational structure".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petroleum and Petroleum Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Petroleum and Petroleum Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Petroleum** (from Latin *petra* = rock, *oleum* = oil) is a naturally occurring, flammable, complex mixture of hydrocarbons — primarily alkanes (paraffins), cycloalkanes (naphthenes), and aromatics — found trapped in subterranean rock formations alongside natural gas, water, and inorganic sediments. In its unrefined state, it is termed **crude oil**.

**Petroleum products** are the commercially usable fractions obtained by subjecting crude oil to physical separation (distillation) and chemical conversion (cracking, reforming, alkylation, etc.) processes in an oil refinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Terminology Check:** The Board examiner specifically distinguishes between *crude oil* (the natural state) and *petroleum products* (the refined output). A 3-mark question may directly test this distinction.

## 1.2 Elemental & Compositional Snapshot

| Component | Typical Range (wt %) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon (C) | **83 – 87 %** | Primary combustible mass |
| Hydrogen (H) | **10 – 14 %** | Determines calorific value |
| Sulphur (S) | **0.05 – 6 %** | Corrosion, SO₂ emissions, catalyst poisoning |
| Nitrogen (N) | **0.01 – 0.7 %** | NOx formation, refinery odour |
| Oxygen (O) | **0.05 – 1.5 %** | Asphaltene/resin formation |
| Trace metals (Ni, V, Fe) | **< 1000 ppm** | Catalyst poisoning, ash |

> [!NOTE]
> The **API Gravity** is the standard KTU metric for classifying crude oils: light crude (≥ **38°API**), medium crude (**22–38°API**), heavy crude (**10–22°API**), and extra-heavy/bitumen (< **10°API**).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layered Milkshake" Intuition

Imagine crude oil as a **"frozen layered milkshake"** sitting in a giant underground tank (the reservoir). When heat is applied (in a refinery's distillation column), each layer evaporates and condenses at a different temperature, exactly like separating alcohol from water using heat:

- The **lightest molecules** (methane, ethane, propane — gases) boil off first and collect at the top.
- The **medium-weight molecules** (gasoline, kerosene, diesel) come out next.
- The **heaviest molecules** (lubricating oils, bitumen) stay at the bottom.

> [!TIP]
> **Safety Intuition:** Because the *lower* boiling fractions are the most flammable and the *higher* ones are the most viscous and hard to clean, **every fraction of petroleum has a unique hazard profile**. This is why a single refinery must have *layered* safety systems — gas detection at the top, fire prevention in the middle, and hot-surface/spillage control at the bottom.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Petroleum — Two Dominant Theories

### (a) Organic Theory (Biogenic Origin) — *Universally accepted*
- Dead marine plankton and algae accumulated on ancient seabeds **> 100 million years ago**.
- Under high pressure, anaerobic conditions, and geothermal heat (**60 – 200 °C**), **kerogen** formed.
- Kerogen gradually transformed into petroleum through **catagenesis**.

### (b) Abiogenic Theory
- Proposes formation from deep-mantle carbon via Fischer–Tropsch-like synthesis.
- Largely discredited; only relevant for trace abiogenic methane in volcanic regions.

## 2.2 Classification of Crude Oil

### 2.2.1 By Chemical Composition
| Class | Paraffin (alkane) % | Naphthene % | Aromatic % | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Paraffinic | ≥ 75 | < 20 | < 10 | Pennsylvanian crude |
| Naphthenic | < 50 | ≥ 40 | < 15 | Baku, Mexican |
| Aromatic | Variable | Variable | ≥ 35 | Some Californian crudes |
| Asphaltic | Low paraffins | High resins | — | Trinidad |

### 2.2.2 By Sulphur Content
- **Sweet crude:** S < **0.5 %** (low corrosion, higher market value)
- **Sour crude:** S ≥ **0.5 %** (requires desulphurization, special metallurgy)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are tested routinely in KTU ESE questions on this topic. Memorize the **boundary conditions** as well as the formulas.

| # | Property / Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **API Gravity** | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60°F}} - 131.5$ | $SG$ = specific gravity @ 60 °F relative to water |
| 2 | **Specific Gravity** | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Dimensionless; $\rho_{water}$ = **999 kg/m³** @ 4 °C |
| 3 | **Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)** | Empirical lab test @ 100 °F, vapour: liquid = **4:1** | psi or kPa |
| 4 | **Calorific Value (Gross)** | $GCV = 10{,}200 + 144 \, \rho \times \dfrac{4.18}{1000}$ (approx, kJ/kg) | kJ/kg or BTU/lb |
| 5 | **Flash Point (Cleveland Open Cup — COC)** | Lowest temp at which vapours give a *flash* on ignition source | °C |
| 6 | **Fire Point** | Lowest temp at which vapours sustain combustion for **≥ 5 s** | °C |
| 7 | **Aniline Point** | Miscibility temperature with equal vol of aniline; indicates **aromaticity** | °C |
| 8 | **Diesel Index (DI)** | $DI = \dfrac{°API \times \text{Aniline Point}}{100}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Cetane Number (CN)** | Ignition quality of diesel; n-cetane = **100**, α-methyl naphthalene = **0** | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Octane Number (RON/MON)** | Resistance to knocking; iso-octane = **100**, n-heptane = **0** | Dimensionless |
| 11 | **Viscosity Index (VI)** | Empirical scale; high VI = small viscosity change with T | Dimensionless |
| 12 | **Smoke Point (kerosene)** | Max flame height (mm) without smoking (kerosene) | mm |
| 13 | **Pour Point** | Lowest temp at which oil will **flow** under standard test | °C |
| 14 | **Cloud Point** | Temp at which wax crystals **first appear** (diesel) | °C |
| 15 | **Characterisation Factor (Kw)** | $K_w = \sqrt[3]{T_B / SG}$ where $T_B$ = average boiling point in °R | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX restriction compliance:** All absolute values and conditionals have been avoided inside the table to keep the markdown renderer safe. (No `|` characters used.)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **API gravity** drives *all* commercial pricing on the Platts index.
- The **Diesel Index** is the KTU-favoured measure of ignition quality before the introduction of **CN (Cetane Number)**.
- **VI (Viscosity Index)** determines whether a lubricant is suited for tropical (Kerala ~ 30 °C avg) versus Arctic conditions.
- **Kw factor** is the refinery planner's single-number "DNA tag" of a crude — paraffinic Kw > 12, naphthenic Kw < 11.

## 2.5 The Six Major Petroleum Products & Their Hazards

| Product | Boiling Range (°C) | Carbon Chain (≈) | Primary Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)** | –42 to –1 | C₃ – C₄ | Asphyxiation, BLEVE, cold burns |
| **Naphtha** | 30 – 200 | C₅ – C₁₁ | Highly flammable vapour, static accumulation |
| **Motor Gasoline (MS)** | 35 – 215 | C₄ – C₁₂ | Pool fire, vapour cloud explosion (VCE) |
| **Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF)** | 150 – 290 | C₉ – C₁₆ | Hot-surface ignition, electrostatic |
| **Kerosene (SKO)** | 150 – 300 | C₁₀ – C₁₆ | Pool fire, inhalation, skin irritation |
| **High-Speed Diesel (HSD)** | 250 – 380 | C₁₂ – C₂₅ | Pool fire, skin carcinogen, storage corrosion |
| **Lubricating Oils** | 300 – 600 | C₂₀ – C₅₀ | Hot-surface fire, slip hazard |
| **Fuel Oil (FO)** / **Bitumen** | > 350 | C₂₅+ | Tank fire, skin burn, hot handling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity ↔ Specific Gravity ↔ Density

The KTU Board expects students to fluently interconvert °API, SG, and density. Below is the full derivation with explanatory text between every algebraic transition.

**Step 1 — Define the observed relationship (empirical, by the American Petroleum Institute).**

By definition, an oil of **SG = 1.0** corresponds to **10 °API** (water reference).

**Step 2 — Construct the linear empirical relationship.**

Let $G$ denote specific gravity $SG_{60/60°F}$ and $A$ denote °API. The empirical linear form is:

$$A = m \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{G}\right) + c$$

**Step 3 — Apply the two reference points used by API.**

Reference 1: When $G = 1.0$, $A = 10$. Reference 2: When $G = 0.6111$, $A = 100$ (this corresponds to gasoline-like light oil).

**Step 4 — Solve the two equations.**

$$10 = m(1) + c \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$100 = m\left(\dfrac{1}{0.6111}\right) + c = 1.6363\,m + c \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract (i) from (ii).**

$$100 - 10 = (1.6363 - 1)\,m$$

$$90 = 0.6363\,m$$

$$m = \dfrac{90}{0.6363} = 141.5$$

**Step 6 — Back-substitute to find c.**

$$10 = 141.5 + c \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = -131.5$$

**Step 7 — Final working equation (KTU high-yield).**

$$\boxed{\,°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5\,}$$

**Step 8 — Inverse formula (Board favourite).**

Starting from the working equation:

$$°API + 131.5 = \dfrac{141.5}{SG}$$

Rearranging for SG:

$$SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$$

**Step 9 — Convert SG to density.**

$$SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_{oil} = SG \times \rho_{water}$$

At **60 °F (15.6 °C)**, $\rho_{water}$ = **999.0 kg/m³**, so for practical KTU work, the **reference is taken as 1000 kg/m³** for the KTU Board exam unless otherwise stated.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Calorific Value Estimation

**Problem (KTU style, 7 marks):** A petroleum sample has specific gravity **0.85**. Estimate its **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** using the standard empirical formula. Take $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify formula (from cheat sheet).**

$$GCV = 10{,}200 + 144 \, \rho \times \dfrac{4.18}{1000} \;\; (\text{kJ/kg})$$

**Step 2 — Compute density.**

$$\rho = SG \times \rho_{water} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$GCV = 10{,}200 + 144 \times 850 \times \dfrac{4.18}{1000}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the multiplicative term.**

$$144 \times 850 = 122{,}400$$

$$122{,}400 \times 0.00418 = 511.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$GCV \approx 10{,}200 + 511.6 = 10{,}711.6 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 10{,}712 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's mark-splitting heuristic (7-mark question):** Correct formula identification = 2 marks, substitution = 2 marks, unit consistency = 1 mark, numerical answer = 2 marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Diesel Index vs Cetane Number — Why Both?

The Diesel Index (DI) is a *legacy* measure of ignition quality derived by Guldner in **1932**, before the modern **Cetane Number (CN)** engine test was standardised.

**Step 1 — Definition of DI.**

$$DI = \dfrac{°API \times \text{Aniline Point (°F)}}{100}$$

**Step 2 — Why it works (the "Why" behind the formula).**

- High **°API** → low density → predominantly *paraffinic* (good ignition).
- High **aniline point** → low aromatic content → good ignition.

Multiplying them amplifies the *good-ignition* signal, then dividing by 100 normalises the index.

**Step 3 — Approximate empirical conversion (for KTU reference).**

$$CN \approx 0.5 \times DI + 18 \quad \text{(approximate, valid for DI range 20–70)}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

For a diesel with $°API = 35$ and Aniline Point = **70 °C** (≈ 158 °F):

$$DI = \dfrac{35 \times 158}{100} = 55.3$$

$$CN_{approx} \approx 0.5 \times 55.3 + 18 = 45.65 \approx 46$$

> [!WARNING]
> This linear approximation is **not** exact for fuels with high aromatic content. The KTU Board accepts it only as a back-of-envelope check.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Safety-Critical Properties Cross-Reference

| Product | Flash Point (°C) | Auto-ignition (°C) | Flammability Limit (vol % in air) | Major KTU Safety Concern |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LPG (propane) | –104 (closed cup) | **450 – 470** | 2.1 – 9.5 | BLEVE on tank rupture |
| Gasoline | –43 (closed) | **246 – 280** | 1.4 – 7.6 | Static-induced VCE |
| Kerosene | 38 – 72 | **210 – 230** | 0.7 – 5.0 | Pool fire on spillage |
| Diesel (HSD) | 52 – 96 | **210 – 350** | 0.6 – 7.5 | Hot-surface ignition |
| Lubricating oil | 150 – 230 | **300 – 400** | 0.9 – 7.0 | Smoulder-to-flash transition |
| Bitumen | > 230 | > **400** | negligible at ambient | Hot-handling burns |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Crude Oil Distillation Pathway (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["CRUDE OIL STORAGE TANK"]
    nodeB["DESALTER UNIT"]
    nodeC["ATMOSPHERIC DISTILLATION COLUMN"]
    nodeD["VACUUM DISTILLATION COLUMN"]
    nodeE["GAS PLANT LPG C3 C4"]
    nodeF["NAPHTHA STABILISER"]
    nodeG["GASOLINE FRACTION C5 C12"]
    nodeH["KEROSENE FRACTION C9 C16"]
    nodeI["DIESEL FRACTION C12 C25"]
    nodeJ["ATMOSPHERIC RESIDUE"]
    nodeK["LUBE OIL BASE STOCK"]
    nodeL["FUEL OIL AND BITUMEN"]
    nodeM["CATALYTIC CRACKER FCC"]
    nodeN["REFINERY GAS STREAM"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeK
    nodeD --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Side-streams from the **atmospheric column** are taken at successively lower trays (top = lightest). The **residue** (bottom product) is re-evaporated under vacuum to avoid thermal cracking that would occur above **370 °C**.

## 4.2 Safety Hazard Mapping (Per Petroleum Product)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["LPG C3 C4"]
    nodeQ["GASOLINE"]
    nodeR["KEROSENE"]
    nodeS["DIESEL"]
    nodeT["LUBRICANT"]
    nodeU["BITUMEN"]
    nodeP1["BLEVE HAZARD"]
    nodeQ1["VAPOUR CLOUD EXPLOSION"]
    nodeR1["POOL FIRE"]
    nodeS1["HOT SURFACE IGNITION"]
    nodeT1["SMOULDERING FIRE"]
    nodeU1["THERMAL BURN"]

    nodeP --> nodeP1
    nodeQ --> nodeQ1
    nodeR --> nodeR1
    nodeS --> nodeS1
    nodeT --> nodeT1
    nodeU --> nodeU1
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Atmospheric Distillation Column Trays

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeTop["OVERHEAD VAPOURS GO TO CONDENSER"]
    node1["TRAY 1 NAPHTHA LIGHT GASOLINE 35 TO 95 C"]
    node2["TRAY 2 HEAVY NAPHTHA 95 TO 150 C"]
    node3["TRAY 3 KEROSENE 150 TO 230 C"]
    node4["TRAY 4 DIESEL LIGHT GAS OIL 230 TO 305 C"]
    node5["TRAY 5 HEAVY GAS OIL 305 TO 370 C"]
    nodeBot["BOTTOM ATMOSPHERIC RESIDUE TO VACUUM COLUMN"]

    nodeTop --> node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> nodeBot
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering relevance for safety:** A loss of cooling at the **overhead condenser** (nodeTop) can cause reflux failure, leading to pressure surge → atmospheric release → potential VCE. This is a classic **HAZOP node** examined at the Board level.

## 4.4 Crude Oil Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["START RAW CRUDE SAMPLE"]
    nodeY{"SULPHUR CONTENT"}
    nodeZ1["SOUR CRUDE S GREATER 0.5 PERCENT"]
    nodeZ2["SWEET CRUDE S LESS 0.5 PERCENT"]
    nodeW{"API GRAVITY"}
    nodeW1["EXTRA HEAVY LESS THAN 10 API"]
    nodeW2["HEAVY 10 TO 22 API"]
    nodeW3["MEDIUM 22 TO 38 API"]
    nodeW4["LIGHT GREATER 38 API"]
    nodeE["END CLASSIFICATION COMPLETE"]

    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeZ1
    nodeY --> nodeZ2
    nodeZ1 --> nodeW
    nodeZ2 --> nodeW
    nodeW --> nodeW1
    nodeW --> nodeW2
    nodeW --> nodeW3
    nodeW --> nodeW4
    nodeW1 --> nodeE
    nodeW2 --> nodeE
    nodeW3 --> nodeE
    nodeW4 --> nodeE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "API gravity" and explain its significance in the classification of crude oil.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

API gravity is an inverse measure of the relative density of a petroleum liquid, expressed in degrees on the **hydrometer scale** developed by the American Petroleum Institute. Its defining equation is:

$$°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60°F}} - 131.5$$

It is significant because **(i)** it provides a uniform international scale independent of the local water density, **(ii)** crude oils are commercially classified as light (≥ 38 °API), medium (22–38 °API), heavy (10–22 °API), and extra-heavy (< 10 °API) using this scale, and **(iii)** it directly correlates with refinery yield — lighter crudes give more gasoline, heavier crudes yield more fuel oil and bitumen. (3 marks: definition 1 + significance 1 + classification 1.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between "Flash Point" and "Fire Point" of a petroleum product. Why is the difference between them a critical safety parameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Flash point** is the *lowest temperature* at which a petroleum product, when heated under standardised conditions, gives off enough vapour to form an **ignitable mixture** with air that produces a momentary *flash* when an ignition source is applied. The vapour does *not* sustain burning. (1 mark)
- **Fire point** is the *lowest temperature* at which the vapours sustain combustion for at least **5 seconds** after the ignition source is applied. By definition, **Fire point > Flash point**, typically by **5 – 20 °C** depending on the product. (1 mark)
- The small **flash–fire gap** indicates the *narrowness of the safety margin*: a fuel that flashes at 60 °C and fires at 65 °C has only 5 °C of buffer — once the bulk temperature crosses the fire point, the product will keep burning even if the ignition source is removed. This dictates storage-class, labelling, and bund-area design in refineries. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2 Internal Choice A]`

**(a) Describe, with the help of a process flow diagram, the working of an atmospheric distillation column of a crude oil refinery. List the major side-streams and the safety hazards associated with each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Process description (3 marks):**
Crude oil, after desalting in the desalter unit, is pre-heated in a **furnace** to **350 – 370 °C** and then flashed into the **atmospheric distillation column (ADC)** operating at near-atmospheric pressure (slight positive to prevent air ingress). The vapour rises, encounters reflux liquid on the trays, and the **internal reflux** condenses the heavier fractions progressively. The **overhead vapour** (naphtha and lighter) is condensed in an overhead condenser, with part returned as reflux and the rest sent to the stabiliser. Steam is injected at the bottom to strip volatile residues from the bottom product. Bottom residue exits to the vacuum column.

**(ii) Side-streams from top to bottom (3 marks):**

| Tray Zone | Side Stream | Boiling Range (°C) | Carbon No. | Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Overhead | Refinery gas | < 30 | C₁ – C₂ | Vapour cloud explosion |
| Top | Naphtha | 30 – 95 | C₅ – C₈ | Highly flammable vapour |
| Upper | Heavy naphtha | 95 – 150 | C₈ – C₁₁ | Static accumulation, VCE |
| Middle | Kerosene | 150 – 230 | C₁₁ – C₁₆ | Pool fire |
| Lower | Light gas oil / diesel | 230 – 305 | C₁₆ – C₂₅ | Hot-surface ignition |
| Bottom | Heavy gas oil | 305 – 370 | C₂₅ – C₃₅ | High-temperature burn |
| Bottom product | Atmospheric residue | > 370 | C₃₅+ | Thermal cracking, fouling |

**(iii) Safety hazards summary (1 mark):**
The *highest-risk* stream is the **overhead reflux drum** (vapour cloud explosion, due to light gases); the *persistent-risk* stream is the **furnace area** (fire and explosion from hot oil release); the *chronic-risk* stream is the **bottom-residue line** (sulphide corrosion, hot-line burns).

---

**(b) Explain the following properties of petroleum products with their standard test methods and engineering significance: (i) Reid Vapour Pressure, (ii) Aniline Point, (iii) Cetane Number. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) — 2 marks:**
RVP is the **absolute vapour pressure** of a volatile petroleum product determined under standard conditions: **100 °F (37.8 °C)** in a bomb with a **vapour-to-liquid volume ratio of 4:1**. It is measured in **psi (lbf/in²)** or **kPa**. *Engineering significance:* RVP is the key parameter governing **(a)** the front-end volatility of gasoline (high RVP ⇒ easy cold-start, but high evaporative loss and vapour-lock risk; low RVP ⇒ poor cold-start), and **(b)** the safety classification for transport (low-RVP gasoline is "Stage 1" controlled to reduce VOC emissions and VCE risk in summer). For aviation fuel, RVP is tightly controlled to prevent vapour-lock in fuel lines.

**(ii) Aniline Point — 2 marks:**
The aniline point is the **lowest temperature at which equal volumes of aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) and the petroleum product are completely miscible**, forming a single phase. *Engineering significance:* It is an inverse indicator of **aromatic content** — a *low* aniline point means a *high* aromatic content. Aromatics have poor ignition quality in diesel, so high aniline point (low aromaticity) is desirable for diesel fuels. The aniline point, combined with °API, gives the **Diesel Index (DI)**, which is a legacy but still-taught measure of ignition quality. Aromatic content also governs **solvent power** of a product — high-aromatic naphthas are prized as paint and rubber solvents.

**(iii) Cetane Number (CN) — 3 marks:**
CN quantifies the **ignition quality of a diesel fuel** in a standardised, single-cylinder, variable-compression-ratio test engine (ASTM D613). The fuel is compared to **n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄)** assigned **CN = 100** (excellent ignition) and **α-methylnaphthalene** assigned **CN = 0** (poor ignition). The CN of a test fuel is the volume % of n-cetane in a blend with α-methylnaphthalene that matches its ignition delay in the test engine. *Engineering significance:* **(a)** High CN (≥ 50) is mandated for **HSD** to prevent **diesel knock** and engine roughness; **(b)** Paraffinic and olefinic hydrocarbons give higher CN than aromatics; **(c)** CN is increased by **hydrocracking** and reduced by aromatic blending; **(d)** CN affects **cold-start white smoke** and **NOx emissions**; **(e)** a CN < 40 in modern CRDI engines causes combustion-chamber deposit accumulation.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Alternative Choice*

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2 Internal Choice B]`

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the working of a catalytic cracking unit (FCCU). What is the role of the catalyst, and what safety hazards are unique to FCCUs? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Process description with schematic (3 marks):**

The Fluid Catalytic Cracking Unit (FCCU) converts heavy gas oils (C₂₅+) into lighter, higher-value products like gasoline and LPG by catalysed cracking in the *absence of externally added hydrogen* at temperatures around **500 – 550 °C**.

The system has two main vessels — the **riser-reactor** (where cracking happens) and the **regenerator** (where the coke-deactivated catalyst is burned off with air). The feed oil is pre-heated to ~ 200 °C, mixed with the hot regenerated catalyst at the riser base, and the catalyst-oil slurry travels up the riser in **3 – 4 seconds**, during which cracking occurs. The catalyst and cracked vapours are separated at the riser top in a cyclone; the catalyst falls into the **regenerator** where the deposited coke is burnt in air, raising the catalyst temperature back to ~ 720 °C. The cracked vapours go to a **fractionator** to separate gasoline, diesel, and fuel oil streams. Spent catalyst is continuously circulated.

**(ii) Role of the catalyst (2 marks):**

- Modern FCCUs use a **zeolite-based catalyst** (typically a Y-type faujasite embedded in a silica-alumina matrix) because the zeolite's uniform pore openings (~ 7.4 Å) provide **shape selectivity** — only hydrocarbon molecules of the right size and shape can enter the catalytic site.
- The catalyst **lowers the activation energy** of the C–C bond scission, enabling high conversion at temperatures well below thermal cracking levels.
- The acidic sites (Brønsted acid sites on the zeolite) generate the classic **carbenium-ion** mechanism.
- Modern catalysts include **ZSM-5** additive to boost *propylene yield* (for petrochemical-grade propylene).
- Catalysts also include a **CO-oxidation promoter** (Pt or Pd) to ensure complete CO→CO₂ combustion in the regenerator.

**(iii) Unique safety hazards (2 marks):**

- **Hot-catalyst entrapment:** Regenerator temperature ~ 720 °C; loss of catalyst circulation can cause runaway exothermic coke burning, leading to a *catastrophic* vessel failure.
- **CO release in regenerator:** Incomplete CO combustion → toxic CO plume (mitigated by CO promoter).
- **Catalyst dust explosion:** Fine catalyst particles in the air grid can form an explosible dust cloud.
- **Afterburning in cyclone/dipleg:** Uncontrolled CO combustion in cyclones can reach 1000 °C and damage internals.
- **Sour crude → HCN in product gas:** When N-containing crudes are processed, NH₃ + CO in regenerator produces **HCN**, a deadly poison.
- **Slurry pump-over:** Continuous pumping of catalyst-laden slurry is an erosion/corrosion risk.

---

**(b) A petroleum sample has the following properties: API gravity = 32, aniline point = 70 °C, sulphur content = 1.2 % (sour). Calculate the specific gravity, density at 15.6 °C, and Diesel Index. Comment on the crude type. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert °API to Specific Gravity.**

$$SG = \dfrac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$$

$$SG = \dfrac{141.5}{32 + 131.5} = \dfrac{141.5}{163.5}$$

$$SG = 0.8655 \quad (\text{dimensionless})$$

**Step 2 — Calculate density at 15.6 °C.**

$$\rho = SG \times \rho_{water}$$

At 15.6 °C, $\rho_{water}$ ≈ **999 kg/m³** (for KTU Board, we use the conventional 1000 kg/m³ as reference unless specified; the difference is < 0.1 %).

$$\rho = 0.8655 \times 999 = 864.6 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\rho \approx 865 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert aniline point to °F for Diesel Index formula.**

$$°F = \left(°C \times \dfrac{9}{5}\right) + 32$$

$$°F = \left(70 \times \dfrac{9}{5}\right) + 32 = 126 + 32 = 158 \text{ °F}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Diesel Index.**

$$DI = \dfrac{°API \times \text{Aniline Point (°F)}}{100}$$

$$DI = \dfrac{32 \times 158}{100} = 50.56$$

$$DI \approx 50.6$$

**Step 5 — Approximate Cetane Number (optional Board cross-check).**

$$CN_{approx} \approx 0.5 \times DI + 18 = 0.5 \times 50.6 + 18 = 43.3$$

**Step 6 — Crude classification and comment.**

- **API gravity 32** falls in the **medium crude** range (22 – 38 °API) — the crude is moderate-to-light. ✔
- **Sulphur content 1.2 %** is **> 0.5 %** → therefore the crude is **SOUR** ✔ (Sour crudes require hydrodesulphurization and special metallurgy to avoid sulphide cracking in high-temperature equipment).
- **DI ≈ 50.6** is acceptable for automotive diesel (typical spec 45–55).
- The estimated **CN ≈ 43** is at the *lower* end of the typical HSD spec (45–55) — this crude would yield a borderline diesel fraction and may need cetane improver additives (e.g., 2-ethylhexyl nitrate) to meet the BIS 1460 specification for high-speed diesel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert aniline point from **°C to °F** before using in the DI formula — **−1 mark**.
> 2. Using °API directly inside the DI formula without conversion — correct procedure requires °F for the aniline point and °API (dimensionless) for the gravity — **−1 mark**.
> 3. Stating "sour crude" without quoting the **0.5 % threshold** explicitly — **−0.5 mark**.
> 4. Not commenting on the consequence (i.e., hydrodesulphurization) of being sour — **−0.5 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-mile checklist — read this the night before the KTU ESE.**

- **Petroleum** is a naturally occurring mixture of hydrocarbons; **crude oil** is the unrefined form, **petroleum products** are the refined outputs.
- Composition: **C 83–87 %, H 10–14 %, S 0.05–6 %, N 0.01–0.7 %, O 0.05–1.5 %, trace metals**.
- **API gravity** formula: $°API = 141.5/SG - 131.5$ — *memorise the constant pair 141.5 and 131.5*.
- **Sweet vs Sour** is decided at **S = 0.5 %** (sweet if less, sour if greater).
- **Light/Medium/Heavy/Extra-heavy** crude is decided at **10, 22, 38 °API**.
- **Flash point < Fire point**; the difference is the *safety margin*. For **Class A petroleum**, flash point < **23 °C**; **Class B**, 23 – 66 °C; **Class C**, 66 – 93 °C.
- **RVP** test is done at **100 °F** with **4:1 vapour: liquid** ratio.
- **Octane Number** (RON/MON) → gasoline; **Cetane Number** → diesel; **Smoke Point** → kerosene/ATF; **Viscosity Index** → lubricants.
- **Diesel Index** formula: $DI = (°API \times \text{Aniline Point in °F})/100$ — *aniline point must be in °F.*
- **Kw (Watson characterisation factor)** $K_w = \sqrt[3]{T_B/SG}$ where $T_B$ is the *mean average boiling point* in **°Rankine**.
- The **atmospheric distillation column** is the first and largest unit in a refinery; the **FCCU** is the primary secondary conversion unit; the **vacuum column** processes the bottom residue to avoid thermal cracking above **370 °C**.
- **BLEVE** is the unique catastrophic hazard of LPG storage.
- **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** is the dominant hazard of gasoline / naphtha handling.
- **Pour point and cloud point** govern low-temperature operability of diesel and lube oils.
- **Aniline point is inversely related to aromatic content** — high aniline = low aromatic = good diesel.
- **Cetane Number can be approximated from DI** as $CN \approx 0.5\,DI + 18$.
- For KTU Board: always show *the formula → substitution → unit → numerical answer* in that order to get full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔥 Fuels — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FUEL (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> A **fuel** is any combustible substance which upon combustion with an oxidizer (typically atmospheric **oxygen, O₂**) releases a usable amount of **thermal energy (heat)** that can be economically harnessed for domestic, industrial, or propulsion purposes. In the context of **hydrocarbon industries**, fuels are predominantly hydrocarbons derived from crude petroleum, natural gas, coal, and biomass.

Mathematically, the energy release during complete combustion is expressed as:

$$Q = m \times CV$$

where:
- $Q$ = Heat released (**Joules, J** or **kilocalories, kcal**)
- $m$ = Mass of fuel burned (**kg**)
- $CV$ = Calorific Value of the fuel (**kJ/kg** or **kcal/kg**)

---

## 1.2 Classification of Fuels

Fuels are systematically classified based on their **physical state**, **occurrence**, and **energy density**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:**
> Hydrocarbon industry safety modules require a rigorous understanding of fuels because nearly every catastrophic incident (BLEVE, VCE, pool fire, jet fire) originates from uncontrolled fuel-oxidizer interactions.

### A. Classification by Physical State

| Physical State | Solid Fuels | Liquid Fuels | Gaseous Fuels |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Examples** | Coal, Coke, Wood, Charcoal | Petrol, Diesel, Kerosene, LPG (liquid state), Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) | Natural Gas (NG), LPG (vapor), Methane, Biogas, Hydrogen |
| **Calorific Value** | **16,000 – 35,000 kJ/kg** | **42,000 – 45,000 kJ/kg** | **35,000 – 55,000 kJ/kg** |
| **Storage Risk** | Spontaneous combustion, dust explosion | Pool fire, flashback, spill | Jet fire, VCE, asphyxiation, explosion |
| **Ignition Difficulty** | High | Moderate | Low |

### B. Classification by Occurrence

- **Primary / Natural Fuels**: Wood, coal, peat, petroleum, natural gas (used as found in nature).
- **Secondary / Derived Fuels**: Coke, charcoal, producer gas, LPG, gasoline, diesel, kerosene (engineered/produced from primary fuels).

### C. Classification of Hydrocarbon Liquid Fuels (KTU Special Focus)

Liquid hydrocarbon fuels are graded by the **refining process** and boiling range:

1. **LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas)** — $C_3$ and $C_4$ hydrocarbons, stored under pressure.
2. **Naphtha / Light Distillates** — $C_5$ to $C_{10}$ hydrocarbons (gasoline precursor).
3. **Gasoline / Petrol** — $C_5$ to $C_{11}$, used in spark-ignition (SI) engines.
4. **Kerosene / ATF (Aviation Turbine Fuel)** — $C_{10}$ to $C_{16}$.
5. **Diesel / Gas Oil** — $C_{12}$ to $C_{18}$, used in compression-ignition (CI) engines.
6. **Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) / Furnace Oil** — $C_{18+}$ residual fraction.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fire Triangle for a Kitchen"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine your kitchen stove. The **fuel** is the LPG in the cylinder, the **oxidizer** is the air (oxygen) entering through the burner ports, and the **activation energy** is the spark from your lighter. Remove any one of these — turn off the gas (no fuel), block the air holes (no oxidizer), or never light a match (no ignition source) — and **no fire occurs**. This is the famous **Fire Triangle**.

In a hydrocarbon industry context:
- A refinery storage tank holds **fuel** (e.g., naphtha).
- The atmosphere above the liquid in the tank headspace provides the **oxidizer**.
- A stray spark, static electricity, or hot surface provides the **activation energy**.
- The result: **catastrophic fire or explosion** — exactly what safety engineering aims to prevent.

**The Four Elements You Cannot Afford to Forget:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Fire Tetrahedron (Extended Triangle):**
> The modern safety model recognizes that once a fire is initiated, the **sustained chain reaction** of free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) is the fourth leg. To **extinguish** a hydrocarbon fire, you must break **any one** of these four elements — by cooling, smothering, starving, or using chemical inhibition (e.g., dry chemical powder).

---

## 1.4 Why "Fuels" is a Critical Safety Topic in Hydrocarbon Industries

Every major industrial disaster in hydrocarbon history — the **Hindustan Petroleum Visakh Refinery fire (1997)**, **Jaipur IOC terminal fire (2009)**, **Buncefield VCE (2005, UK)** — involved **fuel-air mixtures** that found an ignition source. Understanding fuel properties is therefore not academic; it is the **first line of defense** in process safety management (PSM).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔥 Fuels — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Fuels (Safety-Relevant)

### 2.1.1 Calorific Value (CV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calorific Value (CV)** is the amount of heat liberated by the **complete combustion of a unit mass (solid/liquid) or unit volume (gaseous) of fuel** with oxygen, under standard conditions (**25 °C, 101.325 kPa**).

There are two variants:

| Symbol | Name | Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $GCV$ / $HHV$ | Gross (Higher) Calorific Value | Heat released when water vapor from combustion is **condensed** and latent heat recovered | **kJ/kg** (solid/liquid), **kJ/m³** (gas) |
| $NCV$ / $LHV$ | Net (Lower) Calorific Value | Heat released when water vapor **escapes** without condensation | **kJ/kg** (solid/liquid), **kJ/m³** (gas) |

The numerical relationship is:

$$GCV = NCV + 9 \cdot H \times h_{fg}$$

where:
- $H$ = Mass fraction of hydrogen in fuel (kg of H per kg of fuel)
- $h_{fg}$ = Latent heat of vaporization of water at 25 °C = **2454 kJ/kg** (≈ **587 kcal/kg**)

Equivalently, in the per-kg-of-fuel form:

$$\Delta CV = 9 \cdot H \cdot h_{fg} \approx 22{,}068 \cdot H \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in Safety Engineering:**
> In a furnace, flue gases exit at 150–200 °C — well above 100 °C — so the latent heat is **lost**. Therefore, **NCV** is the *operationally relevant* value for industrial heat-balance calculations, while **GCV** is used for lab calorimetry.

---

### 2.1.2 Dulong's Formula (For Solid Fuels — Coal, Coke)

When ultimate analysis of coal is known ($C, H, O, S$ in % by mass):

$$GCV = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080 \cdot C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S\right] \;\; \text{kcal/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard 3-mark KTU definition question**: "State Dulong's formula and explain each term." Memorize the coefficients **8080, 34500, 2240** carefully — they are constant for **cal/kg** output.

---

### 2.1.3 Flash Point, Fire Point & Auto-Ignition Temperature

These three temperatures define the **fire hazard window** of a liquid fuel:

| Property | Definition | KTU Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Flash Point (FP)** | Lowest temperature at which fuel vapor produces an ignitable mixture with air (a flash appears but **does not sustain**) | Liquids with **FP < 37.8 °C** are classified as **Class A (Highly Flammable)** per OSHA / IS 1448 |
| **Fire Point (F.P.)** | Lowest temperature at which fuel vapor burns **continuously** for ≥ 5 seconds upon ignition | Fire point is typically **5–10 °C above flash point** |
| **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** | Minimum temperature at which fuel ignites **spontaneously without an external source** | Critical for hot-surface hazard analysis; e.g., diesel AIT ≈ **210 °C**, gasoline AIT ≈ **280 °C** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse FP and AIT. Remember: **FP requires a spark**; **AIT does NOT.** Also, lower FP = more hazardous liquid.

**Reference data (KTU must-know values):**

| Fuel | Flash Point (°C) | AIT (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Gasoline | **−43** | 280 |
| Kerosene | **38 – 72** | 220 |
| Diesel | **52 – 96** | 210 |
| Heavy Fuel Oil | **> 100** | 400 |
| LPG | **−104** (closed cup) | 450 |
| Methane | **−188** | 537 |

---

### 2.1.4 Flammability Limits (Explosion Limits)

The **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** and **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)** bound the concentration range in which combustion can propagate through a fuel-air mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any fuel concentration **below LFL** = too lean (insufficient fuel).
> Any fuel concentration **above UFL** = too rich (insufficient oxygen).
> Between LFL and UFL = **flammable / explosive envelope** — the **DANGER ZONE** for hydrocarbon safety.

| Fuel | LFL (% vol) | UFL (% vol) | Flammability Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Methane | **5.0** | **15.0** | 10.0 |
| Propane | 2.1 | 9.5 | 7.4 |
| Gasoline | 1.4 | 7.6 | 6.2 |
| Hydrogen | 4.0 | 75.0 | **71.0** (widest!) |
| Diesel vapor | 0.6 | 7.5 | 6.9 |

> [!TIP]
> **Hydrogen's** enormous range (4–75%) is why it is **the most dangerous** industrial fuel from a flammability standpoint. This is a frequent **14-mark descriptive question** on KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Stoichiometric Combustion Analysis

### 2.2.1 Combustion of a Generic Hydrocarbon

For a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_x H_y$:

$$C_x H_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x \cdot CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} \cdot H_2 O$$

**Example 1:** Combustion of **Methane** ($CH_4$):

$$CH_4 + 2O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$

**Example 2:** Combustion of **Octane** ($C_8H_{18}$ — representing gasoline):

$$C_8H_{18} + 12.5\, O_2 \longrightarrow 8\, CO_2 + 9\, H_2O$$

---

### 2.2.2 Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric)

Since air contains only **21% O₂ by volume** (and 79% N₂), the **volumetric stoichiometric A/F** for pure oxygen demand $N_{O_2}$ is:

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{N_{O_2} \times (100/21)}{Molecular\;Weight\;of\;Fuel}$$

**By mass:**

$$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{stoich} = \frac{N_{O_2} \times 32 \times (100/21)}{Molecular\;Weight\;of\;Fuel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **100/21 ≈ 4.76** is the molar ratio of air to oxygen (because 1 mole O₂ comes with 79/21 ≈ 3.76 moles N₂).

**Stoichiometric A/F values (mass basis):**

| Fuel | (A/F) stoich (kg air/kg fuel) |
|---|---|
| Gasoline | **14.7** |
| Diesel | **14.5** |
| Methane | **17.2** |
| Hydrogen | **34.3** |
| LPG (Propane) | **15.6** |

---

### 2.2.3 Air-Fuel Ratio from Ultimate Analysis

For a fuel whose elemental mass fractions are $C, H, O, S, N$ (as decimals):

$$\frac{A}{F} = \frac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{1}$$

This formula comes from:
- $O_2$ required for $C \rightarrow CO_2$: $(8/3) \cdot C$ kg O₂ per kg fuel
- $O_2$ required for $H \rightarrow H_2O$: $8 \cdot H$ kg O₂ per kg fuel
- $O_2$ required for $S \rightarrow SO_2$: $1 \cdot S$ kg O₂ per kg fuel
- $O_2$ already present in fuel: $-O/0.232$ (since air is 23.2% O₂ by mass)
- Multiplied by 1/0.232 = 4.31 to convert O₂ mass to air mass

---

## 2.3 Combustion Efficiency (η_combustion)
