# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Viscosity Index" of a lubricating oil. Why is a high VI preferred for hydrocarbon process equipment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Viscosity Index (VI), defined by ASTM D2270, is an empirical, dimensionless number that quantifies the rate of change of a lubricating oil's viscosity with temperature. Higher VI indicates that viscosity changes less with temperature.
- **Mathematical context (0.5 marks):** $VI = \dfrac{L - V_{40}}{L - H} \times 100$ for V₁₀₀ ≤ 70 cSt.
- **Why preferred for hydrocarbon process equipment (1 mark):** Hydrocarbon plants experience wide ambient and process temperatures (e.g., −10 °C winter startup to 60 °C summer operation, plus hot drains up to 200 °C). A high-VI oil (≥ 95) maintains adequate film thickness across this range, ensuring hydrodynamic lubrication in centrifugal pumps, compressors, and turbines — preventing wear, overheating, and catastrophic failure. Paraffinic base stocks and VI improvers (OCP, PMA) are used to achieve this.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Flash Point, Fire Point, and Auto-Ignition Temperature of a lubricating oil. State their significance in plant safety.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Property | Definition | Significance |
|----------|------------|--------------|
| **Flash Point (1 mark)** | Lowest temperature at which oil vapor forms an ignitable mixture and gives a momentary flash (open/closed cup). | Indicates **fire hazard during storage, handling, and leakage onto hot surfaces**. Open-cup vs closed-cup distinction: closed cup is more conservative. |
| **Fire Point (1 mark)** | Temperature at which vapor sustains combustion for ≥ 5 seconds. | Indicates **sustained fire risk** once ignited; usually 20–30 °C above flash point. |
| **Auto-Ignition Temperature (1 mark)** | Temperature at which oil ignites **spontaneously without external flame** (typically 300–400 °C for mineral oils). | Critical for **hot-work zones, reformer furnaces, and crankcase explosions** — oil on a 350 °C tube will ignite on its own. |

**Safety Significance:** In hydrocarbon plants, oils with FP ≥ 180 °C are mandatory; in fire-hazard zones (near hot equipment), fire-resistant fluids (HFDR phosphate esters) with FP ≥ 250 °C are prescribed.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Both alternatives below are KTU ESE Module 2 Internal Choice questions. Either may appear in the exam.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q. (a) Explain with a neat diagram the three lubrication regimes. How does the Viscosity Index affect the selection of lubricating oil for a centrifugal compressor in a refinery? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Lubrication regimes describe the physical state of the lubricant film between two moving surfaces. They are best visualized via the **Stribeck Curve**, which plots the **coefficient of friction (μ_f)** on the Y-axis against the dimensionless **Sommerfeld Number (So)** or the **parameter (μN/P)** on the X-axis.

**Regime 1 — Boundary Lubrication (2 marks):**
- So is low (low speed, high load, low viscosity).
- The lubricant film is thinner than the combined surface roughness; asperities contact directly.
- Friction is high; wear is controlled by **anti-wear (AW) and extreme-pressure (EP) additives** like ZDDP, sulfurized olefins.
- Typical in heavily loaded gearboxes and slow sliding.

**Regime 2 — Mixed Lubrication (1.5 marks):**
- Intermediate So; partial fluid film exists; some asperity contact remains.
- Friction drops as hydrodynamic effects contribute; oil additives continue to be critical.
- Transition zone — often where machine start-up/stop events occur.

**Regime 3 — Full-Fluid-Film (Hydrodynamic / Elastohydrodynamic) (1.5 marks):**
- High So; surfaces fully separated by pressurized oil film.
- Friction is purely viscous; no wear.
- **Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)** occurs when elastic deformation of surfaces and very high pressures are involved (gear teeth, rolling-element bearings).
- Ideal operating regime for high-speed turbomachinery.

**Stribeck Curve Sketch (1 mark):**
[Diagram is expected showing frictional coefficient vs (μN/P) with three regions labeled.]

---

**(b) Discuss the role of VI in centrifugal compressor lubrication. Why are paraffinic base oils preferred over naphthenic for such service? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of VI (3 marks):**
1. A high-VI oil maintains viscosity across a wide temperature range. A centrifugal compressor operates in a refinery with ambient from ~25 °C (winter) to ~50 °C (summer), plus bearing housing temperatures reaching 70–80 °C.
2. At higher temperature, viscosity of a low-VI oil drops sharply, risking transition from hydrodynamic to mixed lubrication → bearing damage, vibration, and rotor drop.
3. At low temperature, low-VI oil may not flow (Pour Point issue), causing oil pump cavitation and loss of lubrication during start-up.
4. **Recommended:** VI ≥ 95 for centrifugal compressor bearings; many specifications demand VI ≥ 100 with synthetic base (PAO) reaching VI 130–150.

**Paraffinic vs Naphthenic (4 marks):**

| Property | Paraffinic | Naphthenic |
|----------|------------|------------|
| VI | 95–105 (high) | 30–60 (low) |
| Oxidation stability | Superior | Moderate |
| Wax content | Higher (PP higher) | Lower (PP very low) |
| Volatility | Lower | Higher |
| Seal compatibility | Excellent | Moderate |
| Cost | Slightly higher | Lower |

**Why paraffinic preferred for centrifugal compressor (1 mark):**
- Long service life (12,000+ hr between changes) due to oxidation stability.
- Lower volatility → less oil consumption, less vapor in bearing housing (no mist fire risk).
- Stable viscosity → reliable film thickness in compressor bearings → safer operation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**Q. (a) What are the essential properties of a lubricating oil used in the hydrocarbon industry? Discuss any five of them with the standard test methods and their significance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A lubricating oil in a hydrocarbon plant must simultaneously perform as a **friction reducer, coolant, sealant, corrosion inhibitor, and wear preventer**, while posing **minimal fire hazard**. Five essential properties:

**Property 1 — Viscosity & Viscosity Index (1.5 marks):**
- Tested via ASTM D445 (kinematic viscosity) and ASTM D2270 (VI).
- Viscosity determines film thickness $h$ via Reynolds equation; VI determines stability over temperature.
- A wrong viscosity ⇒ bearing failure ⇒ rotor drop ⇒ fire/explosion.

**Property 2 — Flash Point & Fire Point (1.5 marks):**
- ASTM D93 (closed cup) and ASTM D92 (open cup).
- Must be ≥ 180 °C for general hydrocarbon plant use; ≥ 220 °C in fire-risk zones.
- Low FP ⇒ fire hazard during leakage onto hot surfaces.

**Property 3 — Neutralization Number (TAN) (1 mark):**
- ASTM D664; measures acidic oxidation products.
- TAN > 0.5 mg KOH/g → oil is degraded → corrosive to white-metal babbitt bearings → schedule oil change.

**Property 4 — Emulsification / Demulsibility (1 mark):**
- ASTM D1401.
- In steam turbines, water ingress is common; oil must separate water quickly (< 30 min) to prevent rust and additive leaching.

**Property 5 — Carbon Residue & Ash Content (1 mark):**
- ASTM D189 (CCR) and ASTM D482 (Ash).
- Indicates coke-forming tendency and metallic additive load.
- In gas turbine/compressor service, low CCR (< 0.2%) and low ash (< 0.005%) prevent deposit formation in bearings and servo-valves.

---

**(b) Explain the function and chemistry of common lubricating oil additives used in hydrocarbon plants. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Additive | Function | Chemistry | Typical Dose |
|----------|----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Antioxidants (1.5 marks)** | Inhibit oxidation chain reactions | ZDDP (zinc dialkyldithiophosphate), hindered phenols (BHT), aromatic amines (PANA) | 0.5–3% |
| **Anti-Wear (AW) (1.5 marks)** | Form sacrificial phosphate film on asperities | ZDDP, TCP (tricresyl phosphate) | 1–3% |
| **Extreme Pressure (EP) (1 mark)** | React with metal under high temp/load to form FeS layer | Sulfurized olefins, chlorinated paraffins (now banned in many jurisdictions), MoDTC | 2–5% |
| **Detergents (1 mark)** | Neutralize acids, suspend soot | Overbased Ca-sulfonate, Mg-phenate | 2–8% |
| **VI Improvers (1 mark)** | Flatten viscosity-temperature curve | OCP (olefin copolymer), PMA (polymethacrylate), PIB (polyisobutylene) | 1–10% |
| **Pour Point Depressants (0.5 mark)** | Prevent wax crystallization network | PMA, alkylated naphthalene | 0.1–1% |
| **Antifoam (0.5 mark)** | Suppress foam | Silicone PDMS (5–50 ppm), acrylate | ppm level |

**Safety Note (0.5 mark):** Some chlorinated EP additives and ZDDP (Zn, P) face environmental restrictions in offshore & EAL zones; replacements include ashless dithiocarbamates and ashless succinimide dispersants.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Lubricating Oil Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Closed-Cup vs Open-Cup Flash Point** — Closed-cup is always **lower** and more relevant to plant safety. Many students write the open-cup value or vice versa → lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in VI calculation** — ASTM D2270 formula is **dimensionless**, but students often write units like "cSt" on VI → −0.5 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up TAN and TBN** — TAN measures **acid** (degradation indicator); TBN measures **base** (reserve alkalinity, engine oils). Confusing these in cross-questions → −1 mark.
> 4. **Missing the Hydrodynamic Film Criterion** — Many students calculate h_min but forget to check it against the **combined surface roughness** rule (h_min ≥ 5× R_a_combined). Examiners always look for this safety check.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Why" behind additives** — Merely listing additives without mentioning the **safety function** (e.g., why ZDDP is critical in turbo-machinery) results in partial credit only. Always state **function + example + dose**.
> 6. **Writing VI Improver as "Viscosity Improver"** — VI = **Viscosity Index**, not "Viscosity". Examiners penalize this common slip.
> 7. **Skipping the units in viscosity formulas** — cSt vs mm²/s vs Pa·s — confusion here costs marks.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Before KTU Exam:**

- **Viscosity** is the single most important property of a lubricating oil. Know cSt units and ASTM D445.
- **Viscosity Index (VI)** is dimensionless; calculated by ASTM D2270; paraffinic oils have VI ≈ 95–105.
- **Flash Point (ASTM D93 closed cup)** ≥ **180 °C** mandatory in hydrocarbon plants; fire-resistant HFDR fluids ≥ **250 °C**.
- **Fire Point** (ASTM D92) is typically **20–30 °C above FP**; indicates sustained combustion.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of mineral oil = **300–400 °C** — important for hot surfaces.
- **Pour Point** (ASTM D97) sets the minimum flow temperature; **Cloud Point** is slightly higher.
- **TAN** rises with oxidation; **TBN** is reserve alkalinity (engine oils).
- **Demulsibility** (ASTM D1401) — oil must separate water fast in turbines.
- **Carbon Residue (ASTM D189)** — high CCR = coking tendency; bad for hot oil drains.
- **Ash content (ASTM D482)** — low ash preferred for turbine/compressor oils.
- **Three lubrication regimes:** Boundary → Mixed → Hydrodynamic/EHL (Stribeck curve).
- **Sommerfeld Number:** $So = \dfrac{\mu N r}{P} \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$ — higher So = fuller film.
- **Minimum film thickness rule:** $h_{min} \geq 5 \times \sqrt{R_{a1}^{2} + R_{a2}^{2}}$.
- **Arrhenius rule:** oxidation rate **doubles every +10 °C**; respect oil temperature limits.
- **Critical additives to remember:** ZDDP (AW/antioxidant), OCP/PMA (VI Improver), Ca-sulfonate (detergent), PDMS silicone (antifoam), sulfurized olefins (EP).
- **Fire-resistant fluid classes** for ESD systems: **HFA, HFB, HFC, HFDR** — phosphate esters (HFDR) are self-extinguishing.
- **Naphthenic oils** = low VI, low PP → refrigeration compressors in cold service.
- **Paraffinic oils** = high VI, oxidation-stable → turbines & centrifugal compressors.
- **Synthetic oils** (PAO, ester, PAG, PFPE) → extreme temperature, long drain, fire-resistance.
- **Reciprocating compressor cylinder oil FP** must be **≥ 60 °C above drain temperature** (e.g., drain at 200 °C ⇒ FP ≥ 260 °C).
- **Lubricant failure in turbomachinery** = leading cause of **fire/explosion incidents** in hydrocarbon industry per API RP 684 and ISO 10438.
- **Memorize these KTU-typical numerical:** VI calculation, Stribeck regime identification, h_min calculation, Arrhenius oxidation factor.
- **Always mention** the safety implication in any application question — examiners reward "safety-minded" answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petroleum Wax
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Petroleum Wax

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Petroleum Wax** is a collective term denoting a class of solid, crystalline or amorphous hydrocarbon mixtures derived from the refining of crude oil (petroleum). They are predominantly composed of saturated aliphatic hydrocarbons (n-alkanes, iso-alkanes, and cycloalkanes) with carbon numbers generally ranging from **C15 to C75**, and they are obtained as a by-product during the lubricating oil manufacturing process via solvent extraction, dewaxing, and deoiling operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Petroleum waxes are categorized into two principal families based on their crystalline structure, origin in the refining process, and molecular weight distribution:
> 1. **Paraffin Wax** (Macro-crystalline Wax)
> 2. **Micro-crystalline Wax** (Microcrystalline Wax / Micro-wax)
>
> A third semi-solid category, **Petrolatum (Petroleum Jelly)**, also falls under the petroleum wax family and is often discussed alongside them.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a beehive where beeswax is a natural soft-solid used for sealing and polishing. Now think of crude oil as a giant chemical "honey" containing thousands of hydrocarbon chains of different lengths. When we cool this "honey" selectively, the long straight chains (like long spaghetti sticks) crystallize first, forming **paraffin wax**. Shorter, branched, and cyclic chains (like tangled short noodles) form **microcrystalline wax** at higher temperatures and remain stickier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> - **Paraffin Wax** = The "white, brittle candle wax" — large, flat, plate-like crystals, hard, and translucent.
> - **Microcrystalline Wax** = The "sticky, dark, flexible wax" — tiny, needle-like crystals, soft, ductile, and tacky.
> - **Petrolatum** = The "Vaseline-like semi-solid paste" — a gel-like mixture of microcrystalline wax dispersed in oil.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard industrial parameters are used globally to characterize petroleum waxes:

- **Melting Point / Drop Point:** typically **46 °C to 95 °C** (depending on grade).
- **Congealing Point (ASTM D938):** standard solidification transition temperature.
- **Oil Content:** typically **0.1 % to 5 %** for fully refined waxes; can exceed **30 %** for slack waxes.
- **Penetration (ASTM D1321):** hardness indicator, measured in tenths of a millimetre (dmm) at **25 °C** under a **100 g** load for **5 seconds**.
- **Kinematic Viscosity** at **100 °C:** ranges between **3 cSt to 25 cSt**.
- **Flash Point (COC):** typically **> 200 °C**, classifying waxes as low-volatility combustible solids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** Under the *Factories Act 1948* and the *Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules*, petroleum waxes in molten form (above flash point) are considered combustible liquids (Class IIIA), necessitating strict thermal safety controls.

## 1.4 Origin in the Refining Workflow

Petroleum waxes are recovered during the **lube oil refining** stream. The simplified flow is:

$$\text{Crude Oil} \xrightarrow{\text{Vacuum Distillation}} \text{Vacuum Residue \& Lube Distillates} \xrightarrow{\text{Propane Deasphalting}} \text{Dearomatized Lube Feed} \xrightarrow{\text{Solvent Extraction (NMP/Furfural)}} \text{Waxy Raffinate} \xrightarrow{\text{Solvent Dewaxing (MEK/Toluene)}} \text{Slack Wax + Dewaxed Oil}$$

The **slack wax** is then subjected to **de-oiling** (sweating or solvent deoiling) to yield fully refined paraffin wax or microcrystalline wax grades.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Hierarchy

Petroleum waxes are classified by the **CEC (Coordinating European Council)** and **ASTM** into the following functional families:

### 2.1.1 Paraffin Wax (Macro-Crystalline)

- **Composition:** Predominantly straight-chain (**n-alkanes**) and slightly branched iso-alkanes with carbon numbers **C18 to C40**.
- **Crystal Morphology:** Large, plate-like or needle-like macro crystals.
- **Hardness:** Relatively hard and brittle.
- **Color:** White to off-white when fully refined.
- **Oil Content:** 0.1 % – 1.5 % (refined grade).
- **Key Properties:** Low viscosity, sharp melting point, good oil release.

### 2.1.2 Microcrystalline Wax (Micro-Wax)

- **Composition:** Highly branched alkanes, cyclo-alkanes (naphthenes), and alkylated aromatics with carbon numbers **C30 to C75**.
- **Crystal Morphology:** Very small, irregular microcrystals (hence the name).
- **Hardness:** Soft, ductile, and flexible.
- **Color:** Dark amber to white (depending on refining).
- **Oil Content:** 1 % – 5 % (refined grade).
- **Key Properties:** High viscosity, tacky, excellent oil retention, high melting point.

### 2.1.3 Petrolatum (Petroleum Jelly)

- **Composition:** A colloidal suspension of microcrystalline wax in heavy lube base oil.
- **Physical State:** Semi-solid, translucent, gel-like.
- **Color:** Amber to white.
- **Oil Content:** 30 % – 80 %.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Wax Formation — The "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **The Thermodynamic "Why":** Waxes form because long, saturated hydrocarbon chains have strong **London Dispersion Forces (LDFs)**. As the temperature drops, the kinetic energy of these chains decreases, and they self-assemble into ordered crystalline lattices to minimize free energy. The **lattice energy** is directly proportional to the chain length and packing density.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Chain length** determines the melting point — longer chains → higher melting point.
2. **Branching** disrupts crystalline packing → lower melting point, softer wax.
3. **Cyclic structures** (naphthenes/aromatics) increase viscosity and tackiness.
4. **Residual oil** acts as a plasticizer — softens the wax and reduces brittleness.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Property Sheet

| Property / Parameter | Symbol / Method | Typical Range | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Melting Point | $T_m$ | 46 – 95 | °C | Determines service temperature |
| Congealing Point | $T_c$ (ASTM D938) | 45 – 90 | °C | Solidification temperature |
| Oil Content | $\% O$ | 0.1 – 35 | % wt | Affects hardness, slip |
| Penetration | $P$ (ASTM D1321) | 5 – 50 | dmm | Hardness indicator |
| Kinematic Viscosity (100 °C) | $\nu$ | 3 – 25 | cSt | Flow behaviour at elevated T |
| Flash Point (COC) | $T_f$ | 200 – 280 | °C | Fire safety classification |
| Density (15 °C) | $\rho$ | 0.85 – 0.95 | g/cm³ | Storage and shipping |
| Mean Molecular Weight | $M_w$ | 300 – 700 | g/mol | Crystallinity and hardness |
| Conduction Heat Transfer (molten) | $k$ | 0.20 – 0.25 | W/m·K | Process design for heated storage |
| Specific Heat Capacity (solid) | $C_p$ | 1.8 – 2.2 | kJ/kg·K | Energy for melting/solidification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Relationship — Penetration & Oil Content:** Empirical refiners' rule of thumb:
> $$\text{Higher Oil Content} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Higher Penetration (Softer Wax)}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Candle Manufacturing:** Paraffin wax is the global standard.
- **Food Packaging:** Wax-coated cartons, cheese wraps, chewing gum base.
- **Cosmetics & Pharmaceuticals:** Petrolatum, lip balms, ointments.
- **Rubber & Tyre Industry:** Anti-ozonant wax blends.
- **Investment Casting (Lost-wax process):** Pattern material for precision parts.
- **Corrosion Protection:** VPI (Vapour Phase Inhibitor) coatings.
- **Adhesives & Hot-melt Coatings:** EVA/wax blends.
- **Fire Safety:** Wax-based fire starters, firestop sealants.

## 2.5 The "How" of Dewaxing (Background Process Logic)

The two most common industrial dewaxing processes are:

**a) Solvent Dewaxing (MEK-Toluene):**
- Waxy lube raffinate is diluted with **Methyl Ethyl Ketone (MEK)** and **Toluene** (typical ratio 60:40 to 50:50).
- Chilled via scraped-surface exchangers to **−15 °C to −30 °C**.
- Wax crystals form a filter cake; dewaxed oil is the filtrate.

**b) Propane Dewaxing:**
- Liquid propane acts as both solvent and refrigerant.
- Auto-refrigeration due to propane flashing → temperature drops.

**c) Catalytic Dewaxing (Modern):**
- Uses **ZSM-5 zeolite** or similar shape-selective catalysts to selectively **crack n-alkanes** into smaller molecules.
- No physical filtration required; gives higher VI base oils.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Congealing Point & Carbon Number Correlation

A foundational KTU-expected derivation is the empirical relation between the **number of carbon atoms (n)** in an n-alkane and its **melting point ($T_m$)**, which underpins paraffin wax crystallization.

For homologous n-alkanes with even carbon number $\geq 20$, the empirical equation is:

$$T_m = T_{\infty} - \frac{K}{n}$$

where:
- $T_m$ = melting point in °C,
- $T_{\infty}$ = asymptotic melting point for an infinitely long chain (≈ 145 °C for n-alkanes),
- $K$ = empirical constant ≈ **740 °C·atom** (for n-alkanes),
- $n$ = number of carbon atoms.

**Worked Example (Step-by-Step):**

**Problem:** Calculate the approximate melting point of **n-Tetracosane (C24H50)** using the empirical correlation.

**Step 1 — Identify the carbon number.**
$$n = 24$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the empirical equation.**
$$T_m = 145 - \frac{740}{24}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fraction.**
$$\frac{740}{24} = 30.833$$

**Step 4 — Subtract from asymptotic value.**
$$T_m = 145 - 30.833 = 114.167\;°C$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the actual experimental value.**
The experimentally measured melting point of n-C24H50 is approximately **50.7 °C** (a known anomaly due to odd-even alternation in shorter chains). The empirical formula works best for **n ≥ 30** where packing is uniform.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** For $n \geq 30$, the formula becomes much more accurate. For shorter chains, students must mention the **odd-even effect** (where odd-carbon-numbered alkanes have a slightly lower $T_m$ than their even-numbered neighbours).

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Load for Melting Waxes in Storage

A common safety/process engineering problem: Calculate the **total heat (Q)** required to raise a mass **m** of solid wax from ambient temperature $T_a$ to a pumpable molten temperature $T_p$ (typically 10–20 °C above the melting point).

$$Q = m \cdot C_{p,\text{solid}} \cdot (T_m - T_a) + m \cdot \Delta H_f + m \cdot C_{p,\text{liquid}} \cdot (T_p - T_m)$$

where:
- $C_{p,\text{solid}}$ = specific heat of solid wax ≈ **2.0 kJ/kg·K**,
- $\Delta H_f$ = latent heat of fusion ≈ **200 kJ/kg** (paraffin) or **150 kJ/kg** (microcrystalline),
- $C_{p,\text{liquid}}$ = specific heat of liquid wax ≈ **2.5 kJ/kg·K**.

**Worked Example:**

**Given:** $m = 1000$ kg of paraffin wax, $T_a = 25$ °C, $T_m = 60$ °C, $T_p = 80$ °C.

**Step 1 — Sensible heat (solid phase, 25 °C → 60 °C).**
$$Q_1 = 1000 \times 2.0 \times (60 - 25) = 1000 \times 2.0 \times 35 = 70{,}000 \;\text{kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of fusion (at 60 °C).**
$$Q_2 = 1000 \times 200 = 200{,}000 \;\text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat (liquid phase, 60 °C → 80 °C).**
$$Q_3 = 1000 \times 2.5 \times (80 - 60) = 1000 \times 2.5 \times 20 = 50{,}000 \;\text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat.**
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 70{,}000 + 200{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 320{,}000 \;\text{kJ} = 320 \;\text{MJ}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to equivalent steam (if latent heat of steam = 2200 kJ/kg).**
$$m_{\text{steam}} = \frac{320{,}000}{2200} \approx 145.5 \;\text{kg of steam}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the **latent heat ($Q_2$) dominates the energy budget (~62.5 %)**. This is why wax melters and storage tanks must be carefully sized for sustained thermal input — sudden chilling causes solidification in pipelines, a major industrial safety hazard.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Paraffin vs. Microcrystalline vs. Petrolatum

| Property | Paraffin Wax | Microcrystalline Wax | Petrolatum |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Crystal Type** | Macro (large plates) | Micro (small needles) | Amorphous gel |
| **Carbon Range** | C18 – C40 | C30 – C75 | C25 – C60 + oil |
| **Melting Point (°C)** | 46 – 68 | 60 – 95 | 38 – 60 |
| **Penetration (dmm)** | 5 – 25 | 20 – 60 | 100 – 300 |
| **Oil Content (%)** | 0.1 – 1.5 | 1 – 5 | 30 – 80 |
| **Color (Refined)** | White | White to amber | Amber to white |
| **Viscosity at 100 °C (cSt)** | 3 – 6 | 8 – 25 | 5 – 25 |
| **Tackiness** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Flexibility** | Brittle | Flexible | Paste-like |
| **Primary Use** | Candles, packaging | Cosmetics, rust barriers | Ointments, lubricants |
| **Safety Class (molten)** | Combustible IIIA | Combustible IIIA | Combustible IIIA |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Wax Manufacturing Routes

| Process | Input | Output | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solvent Dewaxing (MEK-Toluene) | Waxy raffinate | Slack wax + DWO | Filtration T: −15 to −30 °C |
| Propane Dewaxing | Waxy raffinate | Slack wax + DWO | Auto-refrigeration |
| Catalytic Dewaxing | Waxy raffinate | Dewaxed oil (low pour) | ZSM-5 catalyst, 300 °C |
| Sweating | Slack wax | Refined paraffin wax | Gradual heating of cake |
| Solvent Deoiling | Slack wax | Fully refined wax | MEK-toluene wash |
| Hydrofinishing | Refined wax | White, odor-free wax | H₂, Ni-Mo catalyst, 250 °C |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
