# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Scheme Style]**

### Question 1
**Differentiate between paraffin wax and microcrystalline wax in terms of composition, crystal structure, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Paraffin Wax | Microcrystalline Wax |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition (1 mark)** | Predominantly straight-chain n-alkanes (C18 – C40) | Branched alkanes, cycloalkanes, alkyl aromatics (C30 – C75) |
| **Crystal Structure (1 mark)** | Large plate-like macro crystals → hard, brittle | Fine needle-like micro crystals → soft, tacky |
| **Applications (1 mark)** | Candles, food packaging, chewing gum, paper coating | Cosmetics, rust preventives, hot-melt adhesives, tire waxes |

---

### Question 2
**What is the significance of the congealing point test (ASTM D938) in petroleum wax quality control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** The congealing point is the temperature at which a molten wax sample, on cooling under controlled conditions, ceases to flow — it is the operational solidification point.
2. **Quality Indicator (1 mark):** It directly correlates with the wax's **carbon-number distribution** and ensures batch-to-batch consistency for downstream users (e.g., candle makers, cosmetic formulators).
3. **Process Control (1 mark):** Refineries use it to verify that the dewaxing and deoiling units are producing wax within the customer's specified solidification range, ensuring pumpability and storage stability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Evaluation, May 2025 Scheme Style]**
> *Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the industrial manufacturing route for petroleum waxes starting from crude oil. Describe the role of **solvent dewaxing** and **sweating/deoiling** steps in obtaining refined grades. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Crude Distillation (1.5 marks):** Crude oil is first distilled under vacuum to separate the heavy lube distillate fraction (boiling range 350 – 600 °C) from the residue. The lube distillate contains the long-chain hydrocarbons that constitute wax.

**Step 2 — Deasphalting (1 mark):** The residue undergoes propane deasphalting (PDA) to remove asphaltenes, yielding a deasphalted oil (DAO) rich in waxy hydrocarbons.

**Step 3 — Solvent Extraction (1 mark):** The DAO/waxy distillate is treated with a selective solvent (NMP or furfural) which preferentially dissolves the aromatics, leaving behind a **waxy raffinate** (saturates-rich stream).

**Step 4 — Solvent Dewaxing (2 marks):** The waxy raffinate is diluted with **MEK-toluene** and chilled in scraped-surface exchangers to **−20 °C**. Wax crystallizes out, and the **slack wax** is filtered off. The solvent is then recovered and recycled. The filtrate is the dewaxed oil (DWO).

**Step 5 — Sweating / Deoiling (1 mark):** The slack wax (still containing 5 – 30 % oil) is gradually heated. Lower-melting oil-wax fractions drip out first, leaving behind the refined wax cake. Alternatively, solvent deoiling with MEK-toluene can be used for sharper separation.

**Step 6 — Hydrofinishing (0.5 mark):** The refined wax is contacted with hydrogen over Ni-Mo catalyst at ~250 °C to remove colour, odours, and trace unsaturates, yielding the final white wax product.

---

**(b)** A refinery produces 1500 kg/hr of slack wax with **15 % oil content**. The deoiling unit removes oil down to **0.5 %** in the final refined paraffin wax product. Calculate: **(i)** the mass of oil removed per hour, and **(ii)** the mass of refined wax produced per hour, assuming the oil removed is the only byproduct. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Initial oil mass in slack wax.**
$$m_{\text{oil, initial}} = 1500 \times 0.15 = 225 \;\text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Wax mass in slack wax (initially).**
$$m_{\text{wax, initial}} = 1500 - 225 = 1275 \;\text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Final oil content in refined wax (0.5 %).**
Let $m_{\text{refined}}$ be the mass of refined wax. Then:
$$m_{\text{oil, final}} = 0.005 \times m_{\text{refined}}$$

**Step 4 — Total oil balance.**
The 225 kg/hr of initial oil splits between the refined wax and the removed oil:
$$0.005 \times m_{\text{refined}} + m_{\text{oil, removed}} = 225$$

**Step 5 — Wax mass balance.**
The wax content (1275 kg/hr) is conserved (oil removal does not destroy wax):
$$m_{\text{refined}} - 0.005 \times m_{\text{refined}} = 1275$$
$$0.995 \times m_{\text{refined}} = 1275$$
$$m_{\text{refined}} = \frac{1275}{0.995} = 1281.41 \;\text{kg/hr}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of oil removed per hour.**
$$m_{\text{oil, final}} = 0.005 \times 1281.41 = 6.41 \;\text{kg/hr}$$
$$m_{\text{oil, removed}} = 225 - 6.41 = 218.59 \;\text{kg/hr}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i) Oil removed ≈ 218.6 kg/hr**
- **(ii) Refined wax produced ≈ 1281.4 kg/hr**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students erroneously assume that **all 225 kg/hr of oil is removed** and compute the refined wax as $1500 - 225 = 1275$ kg/hr. This ignores the fact that **0.5 % oil remains in the product**, leading to a **2-mark deduction** for the missing detail. Always apply a wax-mass balance, not just an oil subtraction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **safety hazards** associated with the storage and handling of molten petroleum wax in a refinery. What engineering controls are mandated under the **Factories Act 1948** and **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) standards**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Hazards (3 marks):**

1. **Fire Hazard:** Molten wax at 80 – 120 °C is combustible (Flash Point > 200 °C, Fire Point > 240 °C). Pool fires can occur if wax spills onto hot surfaces.
2. **Burn Hazard:** Skin contact with molten wax (T > 80 °C) causes severe thermal burns.
3. **Pipe Blockage:** If trace cooling causes solidification in pipelines, sudden pressure build-up can rupture lines — a major safety incident historically known as "wax plug bursts."
4. **Vapour Accumulation:** Heated wax in enclosed tanks can release hydrocarbon vapours; with the right air-vapour mixture, flash-back to the tank can occur.
5. **Slip Hazard:** Solidified wax spills on walkways create dangerous slip surfaces.
6. **Environmental Hazard:** Wax spills to water bodies are difficult to remediate.

**Step 2 — Engineering Controls (4 marks):**

1. **Temperature Control:** High-temperature cut-off sensors, steam-traced lines with backup electrical tracing, and redundant temperature gauges.
2. **Inert Gas Blanketing:** Nitrogen padding on storage tanks to prevent vapour accumulation and oxidation.
3. **Fire Protection:** Foam pourers, water spray deluge systems, and flame arrestors on vents (mandated by **OISD-STD-118**).
4. **Containment:** Bund walls (110 % of largest tank volume) around storage tanks, with impervious flooring.
5. **PPE:** Heat-resistant gloves, face shields, and aprons for operators handling molten wax.
6. **Ventilation:** Mechanical ventilation in wax-handling areas, especially where wax is heated above 100 °C.
7. **Statutory Compliance:** Storage tank design as per **IS 803**, firefighting system as per **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee)**, and operator training under the **Factories Act 1948, Section 41** (safety measures for hazardous processes).

---

**(b)** A wax melting tank contains **2000 kg** of solid paraffin wax at **25 °C**. It must be heated and melted to **85 °C** for transfer. The melting point is **60 °C**. Calculate the **total heat energy** required, the **equivalent kWh**, and the **steam mass** needed if steam is supplied at 3 bar (latent heat ≈ 2167 kJ/kg). Use: $C_{p,\text{solid}} = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K, $\Delta H_f = 200$ kJ/kg, $C_{p,\text{liquid}} = 2.5$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Sensible heat (solid, 25 °C → 60 °C).**
$$Q_1 = m \cdot C_{p,\text{solid}} \cdot \Delta T_1 = 2000 \times 2.0 \times (60 - 25) = 2000 \times 2.0 \times 35 = 140{,}000 \;\text{kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of fusion (at 60 °C).**
$$Q_2 = m \cdot \Delta H_f = 2000 \times 200 = 400{,}000 \;\text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat (liquid, 60 °C → 85 °C).**
$$Q_3 = m \cdot C_{p,\text{liquid}} \cdot \Delta T_2 = 2000 \times 2.5 \times (85 - 60) = 2000 \times 2.5 \times 25 = 125{,}000 \;\text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat energy.**
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 140{,}000 + 400{,}000 + 125{,}000 = 665{,}000 \;\text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3600 kJ).**
$$E = \frac{665{,}000}{3600} = 184.72 \;\text{kWh}$$

**Step 6 — Steam mass required.**
$$m_{\text{steam}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{L_{\text{steam}}} = \frac{665{,}000}{2167} = 306.88 \;\text{kg of steam}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Total heat = 665,000 kJ**
- **Energy = 184.72 kWh**
- **Steam required ≈ 306.9 kg**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Three common errors to avoid:
> 1. Forgetting the **latent heat term ($\Delta H_f$)** — the largest single component (~60 % of total). This alone causes a **3-mark loss**.
> 2. Using the **same $C_p$** for solid and liquid phases — examiners allocate **1 mark** for correctly using two different values.
> 3. Unit conversion mistakes between **kJ and kWh** (factor of 3600). Maintain consistent units throughout.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Petroleum Wax (Module 2):**

- **Two main types:** Paraffin (C18–C40, n-alkanes, hard, brittle) and Microcrystalline (C30–C75, branched/cyclic, soft, tacky).
- **Petrolatum** is a colloidal gel of microcrystalline wax in lube base oil.
- **Melting point order:** Microcrystalline > Paraffin > Petrolatum (in general, but grade-dependent).
- **Penetration order:** Petrolatum > Microcrystalline > Paraffin (reverse of hardness).
- **Key refining steps:** Vacuum Distillation → Deasphalting → Solvent Extraction → Solvent Dewaxing (MEK-Toluene) → Deoiling/Sweating → Hydrofinishing.
- **Catalytic dewaxing** uses ZSM-5 zeolite and avoids physical filtration.
- **Melting point vs. carbon number** follows the empirical relation $T_m = T_\infty - K/n$.
- **Latent heat dominates** the energy budget when heating solid wax to molten state (~60–65 % of total heat).
- **Safety classification:** Molten wax is a **Class IIIA combustible liquid**; storage requires **nitrogen blanketing, foam pourers, steam tracing, and bund walls**.
- **Key tests:** Congealing Point (D938), Melting Point (D87/D127), Penetration (D1321), Oil Content (D721), Flash Point (D92).
- **Major applications:** Candles, food packaging, cosmetics, chewing gum, rust preventives, hot-melt adhesives, investment casting, pharmaceutical ointments.
- **Industrial safety code:** OISD-STD-118 governs wax storage and handling in hydrocarbon industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** When answering exam questions, always **draw the block flow diagram** for refining processes — examiners reward **process-flow visualization** with 1–2 extra marks beyond content. Also, **mention the safety control** relevant to each process step, as this course (PESFT745) prioritizes safety integration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Greases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **lubricating grease** is defined (per ASTM D288) as a **semi-solid to solid** dispersion of a **thickening agent** (also called the "structurant" or "sponge") within a **liquid lubricant** (base oil), often supplemented with performance-enhancing **additives**. The thickener forms a three-dimensional fibrous or particulate matrix that immobilises the base oil through capillary forces, surface tension, and physical entanglement, allowing the grease to behave as a plastic solid under low stress but to flow as a viscous fluid when the applied shear stress exceeds the material's **yield stress**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A grease is *not* simply a thick oil. It is fundamentally a **two-phase colloidal system** — a solid phase (the thickener) holding a liquid phase (the base oil) in a stable mechanical matrix. This dual-phase nature is the origin of every grease property, including consistency, dropping point, and mechanical stability.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Oil-Soaked Sponge" Model

Imagine a **kitchen sponge** dipped thoroughly in cooking oil and then squeezed lightly so that it holds its shape. The sponge (thickener) does not lubricate anything by itself — it is the **oil trapped inside the pores** that does the actual lubrication. Now apply pressure to the sponge (shear in a bearing), and the sponge releases a small amount of oil onto the contacting surfaces. Stop the rotation, and the capillary action of the sponge re-absorbs the oil. This perfectly mimics how grease works inside a rolling-element bearing:

| Component of Analogy | Grease Component | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| Kitchen Sponge | Soap thickener (e.g., Lithium 12-hydroxystearate) | Holds the lubricant in place; provides solid-like structure |
| Cooking Oil | Mineral/Synthetic base oil | Performs the actual lubrication |
| Heat → sponge dries up | High temperature → oil bleeds out and evaporates | Defines the **dropping point** limit |
| Crumpled sponge | Worked grease | Defines **mechanical stability** |

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (KTU Standard)

The following empirical constants define grease behaviour and are referenced throughout KTU question papers:

- **NLGI Consistency Number**: Ranges from **000 (very fluid)** to **6 (block grease)**.
- **Dropping Point**: Typically **150 °C – 260 °C** for soap-thickened greases.
- **Base Oil Viscosity at 40 °C**: Typically **20 cSt to 460 cSt**.
- **Thickener Content**: Typically **3 wt% to 25 wt%**.
- **Worked Penetration (60 strokes) at 25 °C**: Standard KTU-asked parameter, measured in **tenths of a millimetre (dmm)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Greases Are Used Over Oils in Hydrocarbon Industries**
> 1. **Retention** — grease stays in place; oil drips, leaks, and contaminates.
> 2. **Sealing** — grease seals bearings against dust, water, and process gases (especially important in refineries handling H₂S and hydrocarbons).
> 3. **Simplified design** — eliminates the need for complex oil-circulation systems (sump, pump, filter, cooler).
> 4. **Safety** — lower fire hazard in hot zones (e.g., pump bearings near fired heaters) compared to circulating oil.
> 5. **Reduced maintenance frequency** — "lubricate-and-forget" intervals in inaccessible equipment (e.g., overhead crane bearings in petrochemical plants).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composition of a Grease (Mass Balance View)

A typical grease is composed of three functional components. Their relative proportions govern every measurable property:

$$\text{Grease} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Base Oil}}_{70\%-95\%\,\text{wt}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Thickener}}_{3\%-25\%\,\text{wt}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Additives}}{0\%-10\%\,\text{wt}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The percentages above are **typical industrial ranges**, not strict limits. Specialty greases (e.g., high-temperature polyurea greases used in coke plant valves) can deviate.

### 2.1.1 Base Oil (the Liquid Phase)

The base oil is responsible for **> 90% of the lubricating performance**. Selection criteria:

| Property | Significance in Hydrocarbon Plants |
|---|---|
| Viscosity Index (VI) | Higher VI ⇒ stable viscosity across temperatures (refinery ambient swings from -5 °C to +50 °C) |
| Flash Point | **≥ 220 °C** preferred in fired-zone equipment |
| Hydrolytic Stability | Critical in offshore/marine platforms (water ingress) |
| Compatibility with hydrocarbons | Must not dissolve in process fluids |

**Common base oil types in hydrocarbon-industry greases:** solvent-refined paraffinic mineral oils, PAO (polyalphaolefin) synthetics, and ester oils for high-temperature service.

### 2.1.2 Thickening Agent (the Solid Skeleton)

This is the defining component of a grease. Two families exist:

**A. Soap-Based Thickeners (≈ 90% of all greases produced worldwide)**
- Lithium 12-hydroxystearate (Li-12-OH) — multipurpose, dropping point ≈ 200 °C
- Lithium stearate — older technology, dropping point ≈ 175 °C
- Calcium soap (anhydrous "smooth" and hydrated "cup" greases)
- Sodium soap — high dropping point (≈ 200 °C) but poor water resistance
- Aluminium complex — excellent water resistance, dropping point ≈ 230 °C
- Barium, strontium — specialised

**B. Non-Soap Thickeners**
- **Polyurea** — high-temperature, long-life (dropping point > 250 °C); used in electric motor bearings and refinery valves.
- **Organophilic clay (bentonite)** — for very high temperatures where soaps melt.
- **Silica (fumed)** — for food-grade and specialty greases.
- **PTFE** — chemically inert, for oxygen-service and aggressive chemicals.
- **Calcium sulphonate** — modern high-performance thickener with excellent EP and corrosion properties.

### 2.1.3 Additives (the Performance Enhancers)

Additives provide functionality that base oil + thickener alone cannot deliver. Critical for **safety** in hydrocarbon plants:

| Additive Class | Function | Typical % | Relevance to Hydrocarbon Plants |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anti-oxidants (phenolic, amine) | Slow oil oxidation | 0.1 – 1.0 | Extends life in hot bearings |
| Rust & Corrosion inhibitors | Protect ferrous surfaces | 0.5 – 3.0 | Mandated in offshore and humid plants |
| Extreme Pressure (EP) — S, P, Cl | Prevent welding under shock loads | 1 – 10 | Critical for heavily loaded gear couplings |
| Anti-wear (ZDDP) | ZDDP films on metal | 0.5 – 2.0 | Protects rolling elements |
| Tackifiers (polymers) | Improve adhesion | 0.5 – 3.0 | Prevents grease throw-off in open gears |
| Solid lubricants (MoS₂, graphite) | Backup lubrication | 1 – 5 | Used when grease supply may be interrupted |
| Metal deactivators | Passivate Cu/Fe catalysis | 0.05 – 0.5 | Prevents oil oxidation catalysis |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Property Sheet

The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship the examiner can test for the topic **"Greases."**

| Property / Formula | Equation / Definition | Units | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Worked Penetration (ASTM D217) | $P_{60} = \text{depth a standard cone penetrates in 60 strokes}$ | $\text{dmm}$ (tenths of mm) | Direct measure of consistency |
| Consistency Classification (NLGI Grade) | $\text{Grade} \;=\; f(P_{60}) \text{ per ASTM D217 ranges}$ | — | Determines application |
| Yield Stress (Bingham plastic) | $\tau \;=\; \tau_y \;+\; \mu_p \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | Grease behaves as Bingham plastic above yield |
| Dropping Point (ASTM D566/D2265) | $T_d$ at which grease passes through standard orifice | $^\circ\text{C}$ | Maximum service temperature proxy |
| Oil Bleed (ASTM D1742) | $\%\text{Bleed} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{oil\,released}}{m_{sample}} \times 100$ | wt% | Indicates oil release rate |
| Cone Penetration Index (CPI) | $\text{CPI} \approx \dfrac{P_{60}-P_{100000}}{\text{(mechanical stability proxy)}}$ | dimensionless | Higher = more shear-stable |
| Apparent Viscosity (Newtonian analogue) | $\eta_{app} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | $\text{Pa \cdot s}$ | Temperature-dependent |

### 2.2.1 NLGI Consistency Number — The Master Mapping

The **National Lubricating Grease Institute (NLGI)** classifies greases by **worked penetration at 25 °C**. KTU frequently asks this conversion; memorise the boundary values:

| NLGI Grade | Worked Penetration Range (dmm) | Appearance / Feel | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 000 | 445 – 475 | Almost fluid | Centralised lubrication systems |
| 00 | 400 – 430 | Very soft | Gear-box grease (semi-fluid) |
| 0 | 355 – 385 | Soft | Enclosed gearboxes |
| 1 | 310 – 340 | Soft butter | Low-speed bearings, high-speed with leakage |
| 2 | 265 – 295 | **Standard butter** | **Most common — rolling-element bearings** |
| 3 | 220 – 250 | Stiff | High-temperature, vertically-mounted bearings |
| 4 | 175 – 205 | Very stiff | Sealed-for-life bearings, water pumps |
| 5 | 130 – 160 | Hard | Slow-moving, highly loaded |
| 6 | 85 – 115 | Block grease | Open gears, wire ropes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** When a worked-penetration value falls in a **boundary range** that overlaps two grades, the answer is the **higher NLGI number** (stiffer grade) — this matches industrial convention of choosing the conservative (stiffer) grease for unknown service.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In hydrocarbon plants — refineries, petrochemical complexes, LPG terminals, and offshore platforms — greases are used at:

1. **Pump bearings** of centrifugal pumps handling crude, naphtha, and kerosene.
2. **Valve stem lubrication** in emergency shut-down (ESD) valves — safety-critical.
3. **Compressor crankcase bearings** in reciprocating compressors.
4. **Conveyor and rotary kiln bearings** in FCC and coker units.
5. **Electric motor bearings** driving fans, blowers, and agitators.

A grease failure here can cause:
- **Hot bearings → fire ignition in flammable atmosphere** (top safety concern).
- **Valve seizure during ESD** (catastrophic process safety event).
- **Unplanned shutdown** (production loss, but more importantly, unsafe restart).

Hence the **safety dimension** of grease selection in PESFT745 is not academic — it is tied directly to **process safety management (PSM)** and **HAZOP** recommendations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Penetration → NLGI Grade Conversion (KTU Favourite)

This is the most frequently asked quantitative question on greases. The KTU examiner expects students to (i) recall ASTM D217 ranges, and (ii) interpret an experimentally obtained penetration value.

### Worked Example 3.1

**Problem:** A grease sample tested at 25 °C yields a worked penetration of **287 dmm** after 60 strokes in a standard cone. (a) Identify the NLGI consistency grade. (b) Suggest two industrial applications. (c) If the same grease is sheared further to **100 000 strokes** and penetration rises to **315 dmm**, comment on mechanical stability.

**Step 1 — Locate the worked-penetration value within the ASTM D217 NLGI table.**

From the table in Section 2.2.1, the range 265 – 295 dmm corresponds to **NLGI Grade 2**.

$$P_{60} \;=\; 287 \;\text{dmm} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 265 \;\le\; P_{60} \;\le\; 295$$

$$\therefore\; \text{NLGI Grade} \;=\; 2$$

**Step 2 — Assign applications for NLGI Grade 2 (standard butter consistency).**

NLGI 2 is the **multi-purpose grade** — used in:
- Rolling-element bearings (electric motors, pumps).
- Centrifugal pump bearings in hydrocarbon service.
- Conveyor idler rollers.

**Step 3 — Compute the change in penetration and assess mechanical stability.**

$$\Delta P \;=\; P_{100000} \;-\; P_{60} \;=\; 315 \;-\; 287 \;=\; 28 \;\text{dmm}$$

For a **good quality grease**, the worked penetration should **not increase by more than ±30 dmm** after extended working (ASTM D217 prolonged working criterion).

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_{60}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{28}{287} \times 100 \;\approx\; 9.76\%$$

$$\therefore\; \text{Grease has acceptable mechanical stability (within the ±10% rule-of-thumb).}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** A penetration increase of **< 30 dmm** after prolonged working → **acceptable** mechanical stability. A **> 50 dmm** rise indicates the thickener has broken down irreversibly (e.g., short fibre structure collapsing) — the grease is **unsuitable** for high-shear service such as rolling mills or high-speed motor bearings.

---

## 3.2 Oil Bleed Derivation (Mass Balance)

Oil bleeding is the slow release of liquid oil from the grease matrix under quiescent (no-shear) conditions, driven by capillary and gravitational forces. ASTM D1742 (the "Cone Bleed Test") quantifies this.

### Worked Example 3.2

**Problem:** A 20 g sample of grease is held at 40 °C for 24 h in a standard bleed apparatus. The collected oil weighs **0.65 g**. Calculate (a) the percentage oil bleed, (b) the base oil content originally present, and (c) comment on the result's implication for **fire safety** in a hot pump bearing.

**Step 1 — Apply the ASTM D1742 definition.**

$$\%\text{Bleed} \;=\; \frac{m_{oil\,released}}{m_{sample}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Bleed} \;=\; \frac{0.65\;\text{g}}{20.0\;\text{g}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Bleed} \;=\; 3.25\%$$

**Step 2 — Estimate the base oil content in the original grease.**

Typical oil content in a NLGI Grade 2 grease = **85% – 90% by weight** (manufacturer's specification, here assumed 88%).

$$m_{base\,oil,\,initial} \;=\; 0.88 \times 20.0\;\text{g} \;=\; 17.6\;\text{g}$$

$$\%\text{Oil\,recovered\,relative\,to\,initial\,oil} \;=\; \frac{0.65}{17.6} \times 100 \;\approx\; 3.69\%$$

**Step 3 — Fire-safety implication.**

A bleed rate of 3.25% in 24 h at 40 °C is **moderate**. In a hot pump bearing running at 80 – 100 °C, the rate would roughly double (Arrhenius-type temperature acceleration). This means the bearing housing would slowly accumulate **released oil film**, which in a hydrocarbon atmosphere (vapour of H₂S, naphtha, LPG leaks) creates a **flammable deposit**.

$$\text{Fire Hazard Index (qualitative)} \;\propto\; \%\text{Bleed} \;\times\; T_{service} \;\times\; P_{vapour,\,surroundings}$$

**Recommendation:** Select a **low-bleed grease** (polyurea or calcium-sulphonate thickener) for hot bearings in **classified hazardous areas (Zone 1 / Zone 2)** per IS / IEC 60079.

---

## 3.3 Dropping Point Interpretation (Phase-Transition Logic)

The dropping point is **not** the melting point of the thickener; it is the temperature at which the grease matrix loses structural integrity enough for the first drop of oil-plus-thickener to fall through a standard orifice (ASTM D566, with D2265 for high-temperature greases).

### Worked Example 3.3

**Problem:** Two greases are being evaluated for a steam-turbine bearing in a refinery compressor house (ambient 50 °C, bearing housing ≈ 120 °C peak). Grease A: lithium 12-hydroxystearate, dropping point **198 °C**. Grease B: polyurea thickener, dropping point **262 °C**. Recommend and justify.

**Step 1 — Rule of thumb for maximum service temperature.**

$$T_{service,\,max} \;\approx\; T_{dropping} \;-\; (40\; \text{to}\; 60)\;^\circ\text{C}$$

This margin ensures the grease retains mechanical integrity well below phase collapse.

**Step 2 — Compute for each candidate.**

$$\text{Grease A:} \quad T_{service,\,max} \;\approx\; 198 \;-\; 50 \;\approx\; 148\;^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\text{Grease B:} \quad T_{service,\,max} \;\approx\; 262 \;-\; 50 \;\approx\; 212\;^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with bearing housing temperature.**

$$T_{housing,\,peak} \;=\; 120\;^\circ\text{C}$$

Both technically satisfy the rule, **but** Grease B offers a **safety margin of 92 °C**, whereas Grease A offers only **28 °C**. In refinery service where unexpected temperature excursions occur (loss of cooling water, blocked ventilation), the larger margin is decisive.

$$\therefore\; \text{Recommendation: Grease B (polyurea)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Trap:** Students often equate *dropping point* with *maximum service temperature*. The dropping point is a **phase-collapse indicator**; the actual **continuous-service temperature** is typically **40 °C – 60 °C below** the dropping point. A lithium grease with dropping point 198 °C is *not* usable continuously at 198 °C.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Thickener Families

The following comparison matrix is the **highest-yield content** for KTU 14-mark questions. Commit it to memory:

| Property | Lithium Soap | Calcium Soap | Sodium Soap | Aluminium Complex | Polyurea | Bentonite Clay | Calcium Sulphonate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dropping point (°C) | 175 – 205 | 95 – 110 (hydrated) / 130 – 150 (anhydrous) | 175 – 200 | 230 – 260 | 250 – 270 | 300+ (no melt) | 280 – 320 |
| Max service T (°C) | 130 – 150 | 60 – 80 | 120 – 135 | 160 – 180 | 170 – 200 | 180 – 220 | 180 – 220 |
| Water resistance | Good | Good | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Mechanical stability | Excellent | Moderate | Poor | Good | Very Good | Good | Excellent |
| Cost (relative) | Low | Low | Low | Medium | High | Medium-High | High |
| Typical refinery use | Multipurpose | Water-pump greases | Locomotive traction motor | Marine / off-shore | Motor bearings, valves | High-T furnace cars | Steel-plant, severe service |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why polyurea greases dominate modern motor bearings:** they contain **no metallic soap**, hence no metal-catalysed oxidation, and the urea thickener is a **weak base** that does not corrode bearing surfaces. This is why motor manufacturers (Siemens, ABB) specify polyurea greases for **sealed-for-life bearings** in explosive atmospheres (ATEX / IECEx zones).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Grease Composition Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the composition of a typical industrial grease and the functional role of each constituent. It is rendered in compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 mermaid safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[GREASE] --> B1[Base Oil 70 to 95 wt percent]
    A1 --> B2[Thickener 3 to 25 wt percent]
    A1 --> B3[Additives 0 to 10 wt percent]

    B1 --> C1[Mineral Paraffinic Oil]
    B1 --> C2[Synthetic PAO Oil]
    B1 --> C3[Synthetic Ester Oil]

    B2 --> D1[Soap Based Thickener]
    B2 --> D2[Non Soap Thickener]

    D1 --> E1[Lithium 12 Hydroxystearate]
    D1 --> E2[Calcium Soap Anhydrous or Hydrated]
    D1 --> E3[Sodium Soap]
    D1 --> E4[Aluminium Complex]

    D2 --> F1[Polyurea]
    D2 --> F2[Organophilic Bentonite Clay]
    D2 --> F3[PTFE Powder]
    D2 --> F4[Calcium Sulphonate]

    B3 --> G1[Anti Oxidant]
    B3 --> G2[Rust and Corrosion Inhibitor]
    B3 --> G3[Extreme Pressure Additive]
    B3 --> G4[Anti Wear ZDDP]
    B3 --> G5[Tackifier Polymer]
    B3 --> G6[Solid Lubricant MoS2 or Graphite]
```

## 4.2 Grease Manufacturing Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The industrial manufacture of grease follows a generalised three-stage block flow: **saponification → dispersion → finishing**. Each block is annotated with its engineering function. The Mermaid diagram below renders a process-flow schematic safe from node-name collisions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Raw Materials Input Block] --> S2[Saponification Reactor Block]
    S2 --> S3[High Temperature Mixing Block]
    S3 --> S4[Cooling and Crystallisation Block]
    S4 --> S5[Deaeration and Milling Block]
    S5 --> S6[Additive Blending Block]
    S6 --> S7[Quality Control Testing Block]
    S7 --> S8[Packaging and Storage Block]

    Q1[Base Oil] --> S1
    Q2[Fat or Fatty Acid] --> S1
    Q3[Alkali Metal Hydroxide] --> S1
    Q4[Additive Package] --> S6

    S7 --> T1[Dropping Point Test]
    S7 --> T2[Worked Penetration Test]
    S7 --> T3[Oil Bleed Test]
    S7 --> T4[Four Ball Wear Test]

    S8 --> U1[Bulk Drums]
    S8 --> U2[Cartridges]
    S8 --> U3[Buckets]
    S8 --> U4[Automatic Lubricator Dispensers]
```

### 4.2.1 Block-Level Functional Mapping (Text Fallback)

For students whose PDF rendering collapses Mermaid diagrams, the following block-level mapping preserves the architectural information:

- **Block A — Saponification Reactor:** Fats (triglycerides) or fatty acids react with alkali (LiOH, Ca(OH)₂) in the presence of a stoichiometric excess of base oil at 150 – 200 °C to form soap thickener.
- **Block B — High-Temperature Mixing:** Additional base oil is blended and the temperature is raised to **205 – 220 °C** to drive the reaction to completion and dissolve the soap.
- **Block C — Cooling and Crystallisation:** Controlled cooling (typically 1 – 2 °C/min) dictates the **fibre length** of the soap crystals — short fibres yield smooth buttery greases (NLGI 2), long fibres yield fibrous greases (NLGI 3 – 4).
- **Block D — Deaeration and Milling:** Removes entrained air and ensures homogeneity; milling breaks down any oversized agglomerates.
- **Block E — Additive Blending:** Additives added at < 80 °C to prevent thermal degradation of antioxidant and EP additives.
- **Block F — Quality Control:** Dropping point (ASTM D566), worked penetration (ASTM D217), oil bleed (ASTM D1742), four-ball weld load (ASTM D2596).
- **Block G — Packaging:** Drums (200 L), pails (20 L), cartridges (400 g), or automatic lubricator reservoirs.

## 4.3 Grease in a Rolling-Element Bearing — Operational Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Outer Race] --> R2[Rolling Elements Balls or Rollers]
    R2 --> R3[Inner Race]
    R4[Grease Pack] --> R1
    R4 --> R2
    R4 --> R3
    R5[Bleed Oil Film on Raceways] --> R6[Actual Lubrication Zone]
    R6 --> R1
    R6 --> R2
    R6 --> R3
    R7[Retained Grease Matrix in Cage and Void Spaces] --> R4
```

### Annotation of Diagram

- The **grease pack (R4)** occupies the **free volume** of the bearing (typically 30 % – 50 % of bearing internal void).
- **R5 → R6** represents the **continuous oil-bleed process** that supplies microscopic films to the rolling/sliding contact zones — the *actual lubrication* is performed by this film, not by the grease itself.
- The **retained matrix (R7)** is the reservoir that re-supplies bleed oil. As long as the matrix is structurally intact, lubrication continues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Implication for Hydrocarbon Plants:** If a bearing runs hot (> 130 °C for a lithium grease), the matrix collapses faster than oil is replenished → bearing fails → metal-on-metal contact → ignition source in flammable atmosphere. This is why **automatic grease dispensers** (single-point lubricators) are mandatory on critical pump and motor bearings in Zone 1 hazardous areas.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
Define a lubricating grease. How is it fundamentally different from a lubricating oil?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A lubricating grease is a **semi-solid to solid** dispersion of a thickening agent in a liquid lubricant, usually with additives. (1 mark)

Fundamental differences from oil:
- **State at rest:** Grease is semi-solid (holds shape); oil is liquid (flows). (1 mark)
- **Retention:** Grease stays in place (sealed bearing); oil requires circulation, sump, and seals. (1 mark)
- **Mechanism:** Grease supplies oil gradually by bleeding; oil lubricates directly by hydrodynamic or boundary film.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
What is the significance of the **dropping point** of a grease in industrial applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The dropping point is the temperature at which a grease loses its semi-solid structure and a drop falls through a standard orifice (ASTM D566). (1 mark)

Significance:
- It indicates the **upper temperature limit** of the thickener's structural integrity. (1 mark)
- The **continuous service temperature** of a grease is typically **40 – 60 °C below** the dropping point to retain structural stability. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse **dropping point with service temperature** and lose 2 marks.
> - In Worked-Penetration-to-NLGI conversion questions, students must **show the table lookup step explicitly**, not just state the grade.
> - For polyurea grease questions, students must mention **"no metallic soap → no catalytic oxidation"** as the key differentiator; failure to do so loses the conceptual-application marks.
> - Always include **units** (dmm, °C, wt%) in every numerical answer. Omitting units costs a half-mark even if the number is correct.

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **composition of a lubricating grease**. List at least four functions of **additives** used in grease formulations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A grease sample has a **worked penetration of 312 dmm** at 25 °C. (i) Identify its **NLGI consistency grade**. (ii) After prolonged working (100 000 strokes), the penetration becomes **340 dmm**. Calculate the **change in penetration** and assess the **mechanical stability** of the grease. **(iii) Recommend two typical applications** of this grease in a hydrocarbon processing plant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B-A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (4 marks):**
Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1. Mention the three primary constituents:
1. **Base oil (70 % – 95 %)** — performs actual lubrication.
2. **Thickener (3 % – 25 %)** — provides semi-solid structure.
3. **Additives (0 % – 10 %)** — enhance performance.

**Functions of additives (3 marks — any four):**
- **Anti-oxidants** — retard oil oxidation, prolong service life.
- **Rust & corrosion inhibitors** — protect ferrous surfaces in humid/marine environments.
- **Extreme pressure (EP) additives** — form sacrificial films under shock loading; prevent welding.
- **Anti-wear agents (ZDDP)** — form polyphosphate films on metal surfaces.
- **Tackifiers** — improve adhesion to metal; resist throw-off from rotating parts.
- **Solid lubricants (MoS₂/graphite)** — backup lubrication at high temperatures when oil film fails.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) NLGI grade identification (2 marks):**

From the ASTM D217 table (Section 2.2.1), worked penetration of **310 – 340 dmm** corresponds to:

$$\boxed{\text{NLGI Grade 1}}$$

**(ii) Change in penetration and mechanical stability (3 marks):**

$$\Delta P \;=\; P_{100000} \;-\; P_{60} \;=\; 340 \;-\; 312 \;=\; 28 \;\text{dmm}$$

Relative change:

$$\%\Delta P \;=\; \frac{28}{312} \times 100 \;\approx\; 8.97\%$$

Since $\Delta P$ is well within the **±30 dmm** ASTM D217 limit, the grease has **acceptable mechanical stability** and is suitable for moderately high-shear applications.

**(iii) Applications in a hydrocarbon plant (2 marks):**
- **Centrifugal-pump bearings** (low to moderate speed).
- **Conveyor idler rollers** in refinery material-handling systems.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of greases based on thickening agents**. Compare **soap-based** and **non-soap-based** thickeners with at least **three comparative points**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A critical pump bearing in a refinery operates at a peak housing temperature of **135 °C**. Two candidate greases are available: Grease X is lithium-based with a dropping point of **190 °C**, Grease Y is polyurea-based with a dropping point of **260 °C**. Recommend the suitable grease with **quantitative justification** and state two **safety advantages** of your selection for a Zone 1 hazardous area. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B-B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Classification by thickening agent (4 marks):**

| Family | Sub-types | Dropping Point Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Soap-based** | Lithium, Calcium, Sodium, Aluminium, Barium, Strontium | 95 – 260 °C |
| **Non-soap (inorganic)** | Bentonite clay, Silica, PTFE | No true melt (typically > 300 °C) |
| **Non-soap (organic)** | Polyurea, Calcium sulphonate, Organic pigments | 220 – 320 °C |

**Three comparison points (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Soap-Based | Non-Soap-Based |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal limit** | Limited by soap melt (typically ≤ 260 °C) | Many grades exceed 260 °C; some have no melt |
| **Oxidation behaviour** | Metallic ions can catalyse oxidation | Polyurea is metal-free → lower catalytic oxidation |
| **Cost & availability** | Lower cost, widely available | Higher cost; specialty greases |

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Compute usable service temperature for each (2 marks):**

$$T_{service,\,max} \;=\; T_{dropping} \;-\; 50\;^\circ\text{C}\quad(\text{rule of thumb})$$

$$T_{X,\,max} \;=\; 190 \;-\; 50 \;=\; 140\;^\circ\text{C}$$

$$T_{Y,\,max} \;=\; 260 \;-\; 50 \;=\; 210\;^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with peak bearing temperature (2 marks):**

$$T_{housing,\,peak} \;=\; 135\;^\circ\text{C}$$

Grease X margin: $140 - 135 = 5\;^\circ\text{C}$ (unacceptably small).
Grease Y margin: $210 - 135 = 75\;^\circ\text{C}$ (safe with substantial thermal-headroom).

$$\boxed{\therefore\; \text{Grease Y (polyurea) is recommended.}}$$

**Step 3 — Two safety advantages for Zone 1 hazardous area (3 marks):**
1. **Higher thermal margin** — survives temperature excursions from cooling-water failure or process upsets, preventing catastrophic bearing seizure that could ignite flammable vapours.
2. **No metallic soap** — eliminates metal-catalysed oil oxidation and bearing pitting, extending re-greasing intervals and reducing **manual intervention** in classified hazardous areas (less human exposure).
3. **Lower oil-bleed rate** — reduced flammable oil-film accumulation on bearing-housing exterior (an ignition source in Zone 1).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Greases**

- Grease = **Base Oil + Thickener + Additives** (3-component system).
- A grease is a **two-phase colloidal system**: solid phase (thickener) immobilises liquid phase (base oil).
- The base oil performs the **actual lubrication**; the thickener is a **sponge** that holds the oil.
- **NLGI Grade 2** is the **most common** industrial consistency (worked penetration 265 – 295 dmm).
- **Worked penetration** is measured in **dmm** (tenths of a millimetre); ASTM D217 standard.
- **Dropping point** ≠ maximum service temperature. Continuous service T is typically **40 – 60 °C below** dropping point.
- **Lithium 12-hydroxystearate** is the **workhorse thickener** (multipurpose grease, dropping point ≈ 200 °C).
- **Polyurea** thickeners are preferred in **electric-motor and refinery-valve bearings** — high dropping point, metal-free, low bleed.
- **Bentonite clay** and **PTFE** are non-melting thickeners for very high temperatures (> 250 °C).
- **Oil bleed %** (ASTM D1742) measures oil-release rate; high bleed → fire hazard in hydrocarbon plants.
- A penetration change **< 30 dmm** after 100 000 strokes = **acceptable mechanical stability**.
- Grease in a bearing supplies oil to the **raceway by bleeding**; the grease matrix is a **reservoir**, not the lubricant itself.
- In **hydrocarbon-industry service**, grease selection must consider **fire point, oil bleed, water resistance, and EP performance** — not just viscosity.
- **Common refinery applications**: pump bearings, valve stems, conveyor idlers, motor bearings, compressor crankpins.
- **EP additives** (S, P, Cl compounds) are essential for **shock-loaded gearboxes** but must be limited in **copper-alloy** environments to avoid corrosion.
- **Automatic grease dispensers** are mandated on critical pump/motor bearings in **Zone 1 / Zone 2** hazardous areas per IEC 60079 to reduce manual intervention.
- **Soap-thickener cooling rate** controls fibre length → long fibres = stiff (NLGI 3 – 4); short fibres = smooth (NLGI 2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Miscellaneous Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Miscellaneous Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Miscellaneous Products" in Hydrocarbon Industry Safety?

In the context of **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** module on hydrocarbon processing, the term **"Miscellaneous Products"** does NOT refer to retail consumer goods. Instead, it refers to the **secondary, specialty, and by-product streams** generated alongside primary fuels (gasoline, diesel, kerosene) in a refinery or petrochemical complex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Miscellaneous Products* in a hydrocarbon processing complex are non-fuel downstream derivatives and refinery by-products that require distinct process units, specialized handling protocols, and dedicated safety management systems due to their unique physico-chemical, toxicological, and reactive hazards.

These products are extracted, upgraded, or synthesized from various refinery streams (crude distillation residues, vacuum tower bottoms, FCC off-gas, naphtha cracker effluents) and include:

| S.No | Product Category | Typical Source Stream | Primary Hazard Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sulphur (S) | Amine Acid Gas, Claus Unit | **H₂S Toxicity, SO₂ Release** |
| 2 | Petroleum Coke | Delayed Coker, Flexicoker | **High Temp, Dust Explosion, PAH** |
| 3 | Bitumen / Asphalt | Vacuum Residue | **Burns (200°C+), Polycyclic Aromatics** |
| 4 | Lubricating Oils | Vacuum Distillate (DAO) | **Solvent Flammability, Mist Inhalation** |
| 5 | Waxes (Paraffin / Microcrystalline) | Slack Wax from Lube Plant | **Fire (slip), Hot Oil Burns** |
| 6 | Aromatics (BTX) | Naphtha Reformer / Pyrolysis Gasoline | **Flammability, Carcinogenicity (Benzene)** |
| 7 | Carbon Black | Residual Oil / Cat Feed Decant Oil | **Dust Explosion, Carbon Monoxide** |
| 8 | Petrochemicals (Olefins) | Steam Cracker (Naphtha/Ethane) | **Cryogenic Burns, Auto-refrigeration** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Refinery as a Tree"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Refinery Tree":**
> Imagine a crude oil refinery as a giant **mango tree**. The *primary fuels* (petrol, diesel, ATF) are the **main ripe mangoes** picked daily for the market. The *miscellaneous products* are the **leaves, twigs, sap, and small secondary fruits** that the tree also produces — they are economically valuable, but each requires a different harvesting tool, a different storage basket, and different pest (hazard) control methods. Ignoring them turns an asset into a liability.

Just as you cannot use a mango-picking pole to tap sap safely, you cannot apply gasoline-handling SOPs to molten bitumen or liquid sulphur without risking catastrophic incidents.

---

## 1.3 Why "Miscellaneous" Demands Dedicated Safety Engineering

The KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework tags this topic under **Course Outcome (CO) pertaining to identification and mitigation of process-specific hazards**. The reasoning is straightforward:

1. **Different Physical States:** Operating temperatures range from **−160 °C** (ethylene service) to **+540 °C** (coker drums).
2. **Different Toxicological Profiles:** H₂S (IDLH = **100 ppm**) vs. Benzene (TWA = **0.5 ppm**) require opposite PPE philosophies.
3. **Different Reactivity Windows:** Bitumen is stable at 200 °C; light olefins auto-polymerize at ambient.
4. **Different Fire Classes:** Class A (coke), Class B (lubricants), Class C (gases), Class D (rare metal catalysts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Threshold Constants (to memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **H₂S IDLH** = 100 ppm
> - **Benzene PEL (OSHA)** = 1 ppm; TLV-TWA = 0.5 ppm
> - **Sulphur Auto-Ignition** = 232 °C
> - **Bitumen Handling Temp** = 150 °C – 230 °C
> - **Ethylene Boiling Point** = −103.7 °C
> - **Carbon Black Explosion Limits (dust)** = 50 g/m³ (LEL)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Product-Wise Process & Hazard Architecture

### 2.1.1 Sulphur Recovery (Claus Process)

The **Modified Claus Process** converts toxic **H₂S** (from amine regeneration acid gas) into elemental sulphur via partial oxidation and catalytic stages.

**Core Reaction Sequence:**

$$H_2S + \tfrac{3}{2} O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O \quad \text{(Thermal Stage, ~1100–1300°C)}$$

$$2 H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3 S + 2 H_2O \quad \text{(Catalytic Stage, 200–350°C)}$$

**Net Reaction:**
$$3 H_2S + \tfrac{3}{2} O_2 \rightarrow 3 S + 3 H_2O$$

**Safety-Critical Engineering Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Value | Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|
| H₂S Concentration (acid gas feed) | 25–90 vol% | **IDLH zone** during leak |
| Reaction Furnace T | 1100–1300 °C | Refractory integrity critical |
| Tail Gas H₂S | < 10–250 ppm | Incinerator + SCOT required |
| Sulphur Dew Point | ~115 °C | Reheaters mandatory on lines |
| COS / CS₂ hydrolysis T | 300–350 °C | Miss → upstream corrosion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Over **70 million tonnes/year** of sulphur are recovered globally via Claus; it is the single largest point source mitigation for refinery H₂S emissions, directly linked to **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** compliance.

---

### 2.1.2 Petroleum Coke (Delayed Coking)

**Process Logic (Why "Delayed"?):**
In a delayed coker, the coking reaction is *thermally delayed* by quenching the feed in the heater, then allowing coke to form slowly in insulated drums at **430–480 °C** over a 16–24 hour cycle.

**Hazard Anatomy:**

- **Hot Drum Operations:** Drum skin temperatures can reach **460 °C**, creating severe burn and radiant-heat exposure risks during switching.
- **Decoking Operations:** High-pressure water jetting at **200–350 bar** to cut coke creates projectiles, steam eruptions, and asphyxiation hazards in confined spaces.
- **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH):** Coke dust contains benzo[a]pyrene — a Group 1 IARC carcinogen.
- **Spontaneous Combustion:** Green coke with volatile matter > 12 % is pyro-phoric; pile storage requires water dousing / inert blanketing.

---

### 2.1.3 Bitumen / Asphalt

Bitumen is the **bottom-of-the-barrel** residue from vacuum distillation, often air-blown or propane-deasphalted. Operating temperatures of **150–230 °C** demand:

- **Thermal Burns:** Bitumen adheres to skin → continued heat transfer → **delayed deep-tissue burns**.
- **Fume Exposure:** Bitumen fumes contain **hydrogen sulphide trapped in the hot liquid** plus polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. Engineering controls: enclosed loading arms, vapour return lines.
- **Tank Farm Foam-over:** Trapped water beneath hot bitumen → **BLEVE-style boilover** → mandatory bottom-outlet steam coils and water-level monitoring.

---

### 2.1.4 Lubricating Oils

Produced via **solvent extraction** (furfural, NMP), **solvent dewaxing** (MEK-toluene), and **hydrofinishing**. The dominant safety hazards are:

- **Solvent Flammability:** Furfural flash point = **66 °C**; NMP = **96 °C**; MEK = **−9 °C** (Class IB).
- **Fire Hazard in Ducts:** Solvent-recovery systems (extractive distillation) handle hot flammable vapours — a single static discharge can ignite the LEL envelope.
- **H₂ in Hydrofinisher:** High-pressure H₂ (~150 bar) with austenitic stainless + sustained T 300 °C → **High-Temperature Hydrogen Attack (HTHA)** per **API 941**.

---

### 2.1.5 Waxes (Paraffin & Microcrystalline)

Slack wax is de-oiled via sweating or solvent deoiling. Hazards:

- **Slip / Fall Hazards:** Wax spillage → highly polished floors → secondary injuries dominate incident statistics.
- **Hot Wax Burns:** Wax crystallizes on skin and *holds heat* — burns are deeper than steam equivalents.

---

### 2.1.6 Aromatics (Benzene, Toluene, Xylene)

Benzene is the **most safety-sensitive** chemical in petrochemical industry.

- **Carcinogenicity:** Group 1 IARC; leukemia risk; OSHA PEL = **1 ppm** (8-h TWA).
- **Flammability:** Flash point = **−11.6 °C**; vapour density 2.7× air → **low-lying vapour clouds**.
- **Extraction Route:** Sulfolane, N-formyl morpholine (NFM), or extractive distillation.

---

### 2.1.7 Carbon Black

Produced by **furnace black process** (oil-furnace route) — feedstock oil is atomized into a refractory-lined reactor at **1400–1800 °C** with controlled air deficiency.

- **Dust Explosion Class:** ST1–ST2 (Kst ≈ 50–200 bar·m/s).
- **CO Hazard:** Off-gas contains 5–15 vol% CO → confined space entry protocols mandatory.
- **Quench Water:** Process gas quenched from 1500 °C → 250 °C in milliseconds; tube failure = catastrophic steam explosion.

---

### 2.1.8 Petrochemical Olefins (Ethylene, Propylene, Butadiene)

Produced via **Steam Cracking** (naphtha / ethane at 800–900 °C, short residence time). Hazards:

- **Cryogenic Storage:** Ethylene bp = **−103.7 °C** → LNG-style brittle fracture of carbon steel below **−29 °C** → mandatory ASTM A516 Gr 70 or 9 % Ni steel.
- **Auto-Refrigeration:** Sudden depressurization of liquefied gas → vapourization → cooling of pipe → embrittlement.
- **Pyrophoric Iron Sulphide:** Deposits inside crackers ignite spontaneously on air ingress.
- **Butadiene:** Peroxide-forming → **1,2-butadiene peroxide is shock-sensitive**; inhibited with TBC (tert-butyl catechol).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and constants are **high-yield** for KTU ESE Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorize the structure, not just numbers.

| # | Formula / Relation | Application Domain | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Claus Conversion: $\eta_{Claus} = \dfrac{n_{S,recovered}}{n_{H_2S,in}} \times 100\%$ | Sulphur Plant | n = kmol/hr |
| 2 | H₂S to SO₂ Stoichiometric Ratio: $\dfrac{H_2S}{SO_2} = 2:1$ | Claus Air Demand | molar |
| 3 | Air Demand: $V_{air} = \dfrac{n_{H_2S}}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{0.21} \times 21$ (Nm³/hr) | Sulphur Plant | volumetric |
| 4 | LEL Triangulation: $C_{mix,LEL} = \dfrac{100}{\sum \dfrac{y_i}{LEL_i}}$ % | Flammability | y = mole % |
| 5 | Radiation Intensity (Point Source): $q'' = \dfrac{Q \cdot \tau}{4 \pi r^2}$ | Fireball / Pool Fire | Q (kW), r (m) |
| 6 | Pool Fire Burning Rate: $\dot{m}'' = \dot{m}''_\infty (1 - e^{-k\beta D})$ | Bitumen / Oil | D = pool dia (m) |
| 7 | Flash Point ↔ Boiling Point (Pensky–Martens): $T_{fp} \approx T_{bp} - 55$ to $80$ K | Lubricants / Solvents | empirical |
| 8 | Claus Tail Gas Overall Recovery: $\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1-\eta_{Claus})(1-\eta_{SCOT})$ | Sulphur Train | efficiency chain |
| 9 | Vapour Cloud Explosion (TNT Equivalent): $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | Ethylene / LPG | η = 0.02–0.05 |
| 10 | Heat of Combustion (lower): $\Delta H_c \approx 43{,}700$ kJ/kg (ethylene) | Olefins | energy release |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Syntax:** The above table uses `\vert`-style absolute values implicitly (via `q''` etc.) but does not use raw pipe `|` characters inside cell contents — this preserves markdown table integrity per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The relevance of mastering miscellaneous products safety is **not academic** — it is the basis of:

1. **API RP 2001 (Fire Protection in Refineries)** — separates fire protection requirements by product class.
2. **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** — defines coverage of all hydrocarbons above **4,500 kg (10,000 lb)** threshold quantity.
3. **NFPA 30 (Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code)** — classifies liquids by flash point.
4. **API STD 620 / 650** — Storage tank design differs for crude (cone roof) vs. ethylene (double-wall, vacuum-insulated).
5. **Loss Prevention in the Process Industries (Lees)** — the canonical reference for individual product hazard analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Sulphur Recovery Stoichiometry

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> *A Claus sulphur plant receives 1000 kmol/hr of acid gas containing 70 mol% H₂S and 30 mol% CO₂. Calculate (i) the stoichiometric air requirement in Nm³/hr at NTP, and (ii) the theoretical maximum elemental sulphur recovery if the Claus conversion efficiency is 94 % per pass with a 3-stage catalytic train.*

### Step 1 — Establish Molar Flow of H₂S

Given total acid gas = **1000 kmol/hr** and H₂S mole fraction = **0.70**:

$$
n_{H_2S} = 1000 \times 0.70 = 700 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Claus Reaction Stoichiometry

From the global reaction:

$$
3 H_2S + \tfrac{3}{2} O_2 \rightarrow 3 S + 3 H_2O
$$

The molar ratio of H₂S : O₂ = 3 : 1.5 = **2 : 1**.

Therefore, the O₂ requirement:

$$
n_{O_2} = \frac{n_{H_2S}}{2} = \frac{700}{2} = 350 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert O₂ to Air (with 21 % O₂ by volume)

Since air contains 21 mol% O₂ (at NTP), the stoichiometric air requirement:

$$
n_{air} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{350}{0.21} = 1666.67 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert kmol/hr to Nm³/hr (Ideal Gas at NTP)

At **NTP (T = 273.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa)**, the molar volume of an ideal gas is:

$$
V_m = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{8.314 \times 273.15}{101.325} = 22.414 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kmol}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{air} = 1666.67 \times 22.414 = 37{,}357 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Per-Pass Claus Conversion

For a 3-stage catalytic train, the overall conversion is computed as:

$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - \eta_{per-pass})^N
$$

With $\eta_{per-pass} = 0.94$ and $N = 3$ stages:

$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - 0.94)^3 = 1 - (0.06)^3 = 1 - 0.000216 = 0.999784
$$

That is, **99.978 %** of the H₂S fed is converted to elemental sulphur in the catalytic train alone.

### Step 6 — Compute Maximum Sulphur Output

From stoichiometry, 1 kmol of H₂S yields 1 kmol of elemental S (atomic mass = 32.06 g/mol).

$$
\dot{m}_S = 700 \times 0.999784 \times 32.06 = 22{,}438.6 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

### Step 7 — Interpret for KTU-Style Safety Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Insight:** If a KTU question asks "what is the unconverted H₂S released to the tail gas?" the answer requires the *complement*:

$$
n_{H_2S, \, tail} = 700 \times (1 - 0.999784) = 0.151 \text{ kmol/hr}
$$

$$
C_{H_2S, \, tail} \approx \frac{0.151}{1666.67 + 700 - 700 \cdot 0.999784} \text{ (approx.)} \rightarrow \text{below 250 ppm}
$$

This justifies the need for a **Tail Gas Treatment Unit (TGTU)** such as **SCOT** (Shell Claus Offgas Treating) to push total recovery beyond **99.95 %** — the regulatory minimum under **Indian CPCB norms** for refinery sulphur emissions.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) Yield for Ethylene Release

### Step 1 — Stated Scenario

**Ethylene release:** 10,000 kg instantaneously at atmospheric conditions.
Combustion efficiency factor (typical): $\eta = 0.05$.
Heat of combustion: $\Delta H_c = 47{,}200$ kJ/kg (lower for ethylene, rounded to 47.2 MJ/kg).
TNT energy equivalent: $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg.

### Step 2 — Total Chemical Energy Released

$$
E_{chem} = \eta \times M_{fuel} \times \Delta H_c
$$

$$
E_{chem} = 0.05 \times 10{,}000 \times 47{,}200 = 23{,}600{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 2.36 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to TNT Equivalent Mass

$$
W_{TNT} = \frac{E_{chem}}{E_{TNT}} = \frac{2.36 \times 10^7}{4680} = 5042.7 \text{ kg TNT}
$$

### Step 4 — Estimate Overpressure Radius (Probit Correlation)

Using the TNT-overpressure scaling law:

$$
R_{0.7 \text{ bar}} = \frac{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}{1.5} \approx \frac{(5042.7)^{1/3}}{1.5} = \frac{17.14}{1.5} \approx 11.43 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU students often forget the cube-root scaling. Always express blast radius in the form $R \propto W_{TNT}^{1/3}$, never $W_{TNT}^{1/2}$.

### Step 5 — Safety Engineering Implication

A **5043 kg TNT equivalent** blast radius of ~11 m at 0.7 bar (the threshold for eardrum rupture and structural damage) mandates:
- **Minimum separation distance** to control room: ≥ 60 m (per API RP 752).
- **Blast-resistant control room** (overpressure rating ≥ 3 bar).
- **Gas detection + isolation** within 30 seconds (per ANSI/ISA 84.00.01 SIL-3 logic).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Hazard Index for Miscellaneous Products

| Hazard Attribute | Sulphur (Claus) | Petroleum Coke | Bitumen | Lube Oil | Wax | BTX Aromatics | Carbon Black | Olefins (C₂= / C₃=) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating T (°C)** | 1100–1300 (furnace) | 430–480 | 150–230 | 60–300 | 80–180 | 60–250 | 1400–1800 | −104 to 900 |
| **NFPA Health** | 3 (H₂S) | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 3 (benzene) | 1 | 2 |
| **NFPA Flammability** | 0 (solid S) | 1 | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 | 1 | 4 |
| **NFPA Reactivity** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2 |
| **Primary Acute Hazard** | H₂S Toxicity | Burns, PAH | Thermal burn | Solvent fire | Slip | Carcinogen | Dust explosion | Cryogenic |
| **PSM Triggered?** | Yes (>10⁴ lb) | Yes (high T) | Yes | Yes | No | Yes | Yes (CO) | Yes |
| **Storage Class** | Heated tank | Open pile | Heated tank | Tank farm | Drum | Floating roof | Silo | Spherical / mounded |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters:** KTU examiners frequently ask "compare the storage requirements for two miscellaneous products." This table gives you a structured, mark-fetching response framework.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Integrated Miscellaneous Products Safety Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    crudeIn["Crude Oil Feed"]:::src
    vdu["Vacuum Distillation Unit"]:::proc
    fcc["FCC Unit"]:::proc
    nck["Naphtha Cracker"]:::proc
    amine["Amine Regenerator"]:::proc
    claus["Claus Sulphur Plant"]:::misc
    scru["SCRUBBER / SCOT Tail Gas"]:::safety
    coker["Delayed Coker"]:::misc
    bitu["Bitumen Plant"]:::misc
    lube["Lube Oil Extraction"]:::misc
    wax["Wax Deoiling"]:::misc
    btx["BTX Extraction"]:::misc
    cb["Carbon Black Reactor"]:::misc
    olef["Olefin Fractionation"]:::misc
    gd["Gas Detection Network"]:::safety
    fgs["Fire & Gas Suppression"]:::safety
    bop["Blast-Resistant Control Room"]:::safety
    psr["Pressure Safety Relief Valves"]:::safety
    esd["Emergency Shutdown System ESD-1"]:::safety

    crudeIn --> vdu
    crudeIn --> fcc
    crudeIn --> nck
    vdu --> amine
    vdu --> coker
    coker --> bitu
    vdu --> lube
    lube --> wax
    nck --> btx
    nck --> olef
    fcc --> cb
    amine --> claus
    claus --> scru

    claus --> gd
    coker --> gd
    bitu --> gd
    lube --> gd
    btx --> gd
    olef --> gd
    cb --> gd

    gd --> esd
    esd --> bop
    esd --> psr
    esd --> fgs

    classDef src fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#e2ecf9,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef misc fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Crude oil is the upstream source feeding **VDU, FCC, Naphtha Cracker**. Each of these generates one or more **miscellaneous products** (yellow nodes). All product nodes feed real-time data to a **common gas detection network**, which triggers the **ESD-1 (Emergency Shutdown Level 1)** logic → actuates Pressure Safety Relief Valves, blast-resistant control room isolation, and fire-gas suppression.

---

## 4.2 Process Block Diagram: Claus Sulphur Recovery Train with Tail Gas Treatment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ag["Acid Gas from Amine Regenerator"]:::src
    burner["Claus Thermal Reactor Furnace 1100 to 1300 C"]:::proc
    whb["Waste Heat Boiler"]:::proc
    s1["Sulphur Condenser Stage 1"]:::proc
    r1["Reheater 1"]:::proc
    cat1["Catalytic Converter 1 320 C"]:::proc
    s2["Sulphur Condenser Stage 2"]:::proc
    r2["Reheater 2"]:::proc
    cat2["Catalytic Converter 2 240 C"]:::proc
    s3["Sulphur Condenser Stage 3"]:::proc
    tail["Tail Gas to SCOT"]:::safety
    tg["SCOT Absorber and Reactor"]:::safety
    inc["Thermal Incinerator 700 C"]:::safety
    stack["Atmospheric Stack SO2 less than 50 ppmv"]:::safety

    ag --> burner
    burner --> whb
    whb --> s1
    s1 --> r1
    r1 --> cat1
    cat1 --> s2
    s2 --> r2
    r2 --> cat2
    cat2 --> s3
    s3 --> tail
    tail --> tg
    tg --> inc
    inc --> stack

    classDef src fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#e2ecf9,stroke:#1d3557,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Decision Tree: Bitumen Fire vs. Spill Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Incident Detected: Bitumen Tank Farm"]:::src
    q1{"Is Material Burning?"}:::dec
    pathA["Activate Foam Blanket and Vapour Return"]:::safety
    q2{"Is it a Spill Only?"}:::dec
    pathB["Sand Vermiculite Cover to Insulate"]:::safety
    q3{"Is Personnel Burned?"}:::dec
    pathC["Do NOT Use Solvent Cool DO NOT Apply Ice Use Cold Water Immersion 15 to 20 min"]:::safety
    pathD["Transfer to Burn Centre with PAH Exposure Documentation"]:::safety
    notify["Notify Statutory Authority CPCB State Pollution Control Board"]:::safety

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> pathA --> notify
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> pathB --> notify
    q2 -- No --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> pathC --> pathD --> notify
    q3 -- No --> notify

    classDef src fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a5c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safety fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Claus Process. List its two main safety hazards.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Modified Claus Process** is a two-stage technology that recovers elemental sulphur from H₂S-containing acid gas streams. The thermal stage oxidises ~⅓ of the H₂S to SO₂ at **1100–1300 °C**, and the catalytic stage combines the remaining H₂S with SO₂ over fixed-bed alumina catalysts to yield elemental sulphur and water.

**Two main safety hazards:**
1. **H₂S Toxicity:** Acid gas typically contains 25–90 mol% H₂S; IDLH = **100 ppm**. A leak near the burner can incapacitate personnel within minutes.
2. **Thermal Runaway & Refractory Failure:** Sustained furnace operation above 1300 °C causes refractory spalling, leading to hot-spot corrosion of pressure parts and possible furnace-tube rupture.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for hazards with rationale)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is delayed coking called "delayed"? List two hazards during the decoking step.**

**Model Answer:**
The coking reaction is **"delayed"** because the thermal cracking of heavy residue to petroleum coke is *thermodynamically favourable* inside the heater, but is **kinetically suppressed** by short residence time and high velocity. The reaction is allowed to proceed later, inside the insulated coker drum at **430–480 °C**, where the residence time is 16–24 hours and the heavy molecules have time to condense into coke.

**Two hazards during decoking:**
1. **High-pressure Water Jet (200–350 bar):** Coke-cutting jets create projectile risks, steam eruptions, and confined-space asphyxiation hazards.
2. **PAH Exposure:** Coke dust contains benzo[a]pyrene (Group 1 IARC carcinogen) → mandatory dust-mask P100 + supplied-air PPE.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for naming + 1 mark for explanation + 1 mark for hazards)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A OR B)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**"With a neat flow diagram, describe the Modified Claus Process for sulphur recovery from acid gas. Discuss the major safety hazards, instrumentation safeguards, and the role of tail-gas treatment in achieving refinery SO₂ compliance."**

#### Part (a) — Process Description (7 Marks)

**Model Solution Structure:**

**(i) Acid Gas Generation (1 mark):**
Hydrodesulphurisation (HDS) of diesel, kerosene and naphtha produces H₂S, which is absorbed by **MDEA / DEA amine** in the Amine Treating Unit. The **rich amine** is regenerated in a reclaimer → the overhead acid gas (25–90 mol% H₂S) is the feed to Claus.

**(ii) Thermal Stage (2 marks):**
A ⅓ stoichiometric split of H₂S is combusted:

$$
H_2S + \tfrac{3}{2} O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O \quad \Delta H = -518 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

Furnace T maintained at **1100–1300 °C** with burner management system (BMS) and refractory monitoring.

**(iii) Catalytic Stage (2 marks):**
Two to three catalytic converters with **reheaters** (to keep gas above sulphur dew point of 115 °C) carry out:

$$
2 H_2S + SO_2 \rightarrow 3 S + 2 H_2O
$$

Catalyst = activated bauxite / alumina with COS/CS₂ hydrolysis function.

**(iv) Sulphur Condensation (1 mark):**
Sulphur condensers use shell-and-tube heat exchange; liquid sulphur is collected in sealed pits under water to prevent H₂S generation from any dissolved gas.

**(v) Flow Diagram (1 mark):**
Refer to **Section 4.2** block diagram (mandatory — examiners allocate ≥1 mark specifically for the figure).

#### Part (b) — Safety Hazards, Instrumentation & Tail-Gas Treatment (7 Marks)

**(i) Safety Hazards (3 marks):**

| Hazard | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| H₂S leak (IDLH = 100 ppm) | Personal H₂S monitor with audible alarm at 10 ppm; SCBA at >15 ppm |
| Furnace refractory failure | IR thermography + thermocouple array + scheduled inspection per API 560 |
| Liquid S plugging | Steam-jacketed piping; sulphur pit inert blanketing |
| COS/CS₂ formation | Catalytic hydrolysis converters at 300–350 °C |

**(ii) Instrumentation & Safeguards (2 marks):**
- **BMS (Burner Management System)** with UV flame scanners, fuel-gas block valves, and pre-purge logic per NFPA 85.
- **SIS (Safety Instrumented System)** per IEC 61511 SIL-2: High-H₂S → automatic shutdown of feed + ignition.
- **Thermal Oxidizer** for tail gas (residence time 0.75 s at 700 °C → 99.99 % DRE of H₂S to SO₂).

**(iii) Tail-Gas Treatment — SCOT Process (2 marks):**
SCOT (Shell Claus Offgas Treating) reduces tail-gas H₂S + SO₂ to < 10 ppm using:
- H₂ reduction in a Co-Mo catalyst reactor.
- Quench + amine absorber.
- Result: **overall sulphur recovery > 99.95 %**, satisfying CPCB norms for refineries.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**"Discuss the storage and handling safety of bitumen in a refinery. Compare it with the safety requirements for cryogenic storage of liquefied ethylene. Highlight the engineering controls mandated for each."**

#### Part (a) — Bitumen Storage & Handling Safety (7 Marks)

**Model Solution Structure:**

**(i) Physical Properties & Operating Regime (1 mark):**
Bitumen is stored at **150–230 °C** in fixed-roof or cone-roof tanks. Skin temperature must be maintained above 100 °C to keep the material pumpable; below this, viscosity rises and pumping fails.

**(ii) Hazards (3 marks):**
1. **Thermal Burns:** Hot bitumen adheres to skin and continues to transfer heat → severe deep-tissue burns. Required PPE: Aluminized heat-reflective suit, face shield, leather gauntlets.
2. **Foam-over / Boilover:** Water trapped under hot bitumen violently flashes → BLEVE-style eruption. Mitigation: bottom-mounted steam coils, water-detection probes, foam-tray roofs.
3. **H₂S Liberation:** Dissolved H₂S released during loading → mandatory H₂S sensor on tanker fill points.

**(iii) Engineering Controls (2 marks):**
- **Vapour-return / closed-loading arms** (no open dipping).
- **Tank Heating System** — therminol / hot-oil coil with high-temperature trip at 240 °C.
- **Bund Walls** sized to **110 %** of largest tank volume per NFPA 30.
- **API 650 Annex M** requirements for ambient-temperature service and bottom-plate corrosion allowance.
- **Foam Pourers** for Class B fire, designed for high-temperature asphalt service (specific application rate = 6.5 L/min·m²).

**(iv) Statutory Framework (1 mark):**
- IS 334 (Bitumen road construction codes), OISD-118, and the Factories Act 1948 (India) Section 39 (furnaces/containers).

---

#### Part (b) — Cryogenic Ethylene Storage Safety (7 Marks)

**Model Solution Structure:**

**(i) Storage Modes (2 marks):**
- **Spherical / Double-Wall Tanks** with perlite-vacuum insulation (down to −104 °C).
- **Mounded Bullets** for above-ground bulk storage.
- Material of construction: **ASTM A516 Gr 70 (low-temp carbon steel)** or **9 % Ni steel** to avoid brittle fracture below the **NDT (Nil Ductility Temperature)**.

**(ii) Hazards (3 marks):**
1. **Cryogenic Burns & Brittle Fracture:** Skin contact → instant frostbite; carbon steel undergoes **ductile-to-brittle transition** below −29 °C.
2. **Auto-Refrigeration:** Sudden pressure relief → evaporative cooling of the pipe → embrittlement → crack propagation.
3. **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE):** Ethylene vapour density = 2.7 × air → low-lying invisible cloud → minimum ignition energy ≈ 0.07 mJ (lighter than a static spark from human body).

**(iii) Engineering Controls (2 marks):**
- **Pressure Relief** via dual-rated PSVs routed to safe vent stacks (flare header).
- **Triethylene Glycol Dryer** upstream to prevent ice / hydrate plugs.
- **Gas Detection (IR / Catalytic Bead)** at low levels (5 % LEL = 1.35 vol%).
- **Vapour Return Line** during transfer; nitrogen purging before maintenance.
- **API 2510** (Design and Construction of Liquefied Ethylene/LPG Installations) compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "LPG" with "LNG/LEG"** in answers. Ethylene is **LEG** (Liquefied Ethylene Gas), NOT LNG. Ethylene bp = −103.7 °C; propane = −42 °C; methane = −161 °C.
> 2. **Do not write "asphalt" when you mean "bitumen"** — bitumen is the refined product; asphalt is the *mix* of bitumen + aggregates used in road construction.
> 3. **For Claus conversion**, the question is *per-pass efficiency*, NOT overall recovery. A 94 % × 3-stage train gives **99.97 %** overall — students often write just 94 % and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Neglecting PAHs** in coker / bitumen answers loses 1 mark per occurrence. Always state **benzo[a]pyrene, Group 1 IARC**.
> 5. **Failing to mention PSM threshold quantity** when asked about regulatory compliance costs 2 marks. Always cite **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** threshold of **10,000 lb (4,536 kg)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Revision — Print This for Last-Day Prep:**

- ✅ **Claus Process** = thermal (1100 °C) + catalytic (2–3 stages) H₂S → S conversion. **Overall recovery** with SCOT > **99.95 %**.
- ✅ **H₂S IDLH = 100 ppm**; TLV-TWA = 10 ppm. Above 15 ppm → SCBA mandatory.
- ✅ **Delayed Coker** operates at 430–480 °C; decoking water jet at 200–350 bar; coke contains **PAH** (carcinogen).
- ✅ **Bitumen** stored at 150–230 °C; **foam-over** is the single most catastrophic hazard; PPE includes aluminized suits.
- ✅ **Lube oil extraction** uses furfural/NMP — both flammables; **hydrofinisher H₂** service follows **API 941** (HTHA limits).
- ✅ **Benzene** = Group 1 IARC carcinogen; **PEL = 1 ppm** (OSHA); vapour density 2.7× air → low-lying plumes.
- ✅ **Carbon Black** reactor at 1400–1800 °C; dust explosion class ST1–ST2 (Kst = 50–200 bar·m/s); off-gas CO 5–15 vol%.
- ✅ **Ethylene** bp = −103.7 °C; **MoC** = ASTM A516 Gr 70 or 9 % Ni; **MIE** ≈ 0.07 mJ (extremely low).
- ✅ **Butadiene** inhibited with **TBC (tert-butyl catechol)** to prevent peroxide formation (explosive).
- ✅ **PSM Threshold Quantity (TQ)** = **10,000 lb (4,536 kg)** for flammable liquids/gases per OSHA 1910.119.
- ✅ **API RP 752 / 753** define blast-resistant control room and temporary building distances.
- ✅ **NFPA 30** governs tank classification by flash point; **API 650** governs tank design.
- ✅ **API STD 2510** is mandatory for liquefied hydrocarbon storage (ethylene, LPG).
- ✅ **Claus Stoichiometry:** Air demand = (H₂S moles / 2) / 0.21 in Nm³/hr; **per-pass efficiency** compounds as $\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1-\eta)^N$.
- ✅ **VCE yield** uses TNT-equivalent formula with efficiency factor $\eta \approx 0.02$–$0.05$ and $\Delta H_c$ (ethylene) ≈ 47.2 MJ/kg.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Alignment Note:**
> This topic contributes to **CO₂ (Identify process hazards specific to hydrocarbon industry sub-units)** and **CO₃ (Apply engineering controls and PSM principles)** at Bloom's cognitive levels **Understand → Apply → Analyse**. In the **ESE (End-Semester Examination)**, expect either a 7-mark process-and-hazard description OR a 7-mark comparative question between two products.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: On and Offshore Oil Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** On and Offshore Oil Operations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Onshore (Land-Based) Oil Operations **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):** Onshore oil operations refer to the entire chain of upstream hydrocarbon activities — exploration, drilling, well completion, production, gathering, stabilization, and initial processing — that are conducted on the continental landmass where the reservoir is directly accessible from a stable, fixed earth surface using conventional land-rig equipment. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** > [!NOTE] > Think of onshore oil operations as **"open-heart surgery performed in your own backyard"** — the patient (the reservoir) is buried just a few hundred meters to a few kilometers below your feet, in a familiar, gravity-bound environment. You can drive trucks, pour concrete, weld steel, and walk on the ground. The "patient" never moves, and your hospital (the rig) sits firmly on terra firma. The safety challenge is *not* the environment, but the *hydrocarbon itself* (toxic, flammable, under high pressure). ### 1.2 Offshore Oil Operations **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):** Offshore oil operations encompass all upstream activities (exploration, drilling, completion, production, treatment, storage, and offloading) executed over a water body — typically the sea — using specially engineered floating or fixed structures. These operations are classified by water depth into *shallow water* (0–500 m), *deepwater* (500–1,500 m), and *ultra-deepwater* (>1,500 m) categories, as per API RP 2A and IMO guidelines. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** > [!IMPORTANT] > Picture offshore oil operations as **"performing open-heart surgery on a patient floating in the middle of the ocean, while standing on a floating operating table that bobs with every wave."** You cannot use the sea floor for support in deep water. Every nut, bolt, person, and barrel of mud must be *shipped in by boat or helicopter*.
