# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Explain the significance of hydrostatic testing in pipeline construction. State the test pressure multiplier for liquid and gas pipelines.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Hydrostatic testing is the **final integrity verification** performed after pipeline construction and before commissioning. It serves three purposes:
1. **Strength test:** Confirms the pipeline can withstand pressures above normal operating levels.
2. **Leak test:** Detects any small leaks in welds, fittings, or pipe body.
3. **Stress-strain conditioning:** Confirms the system holds at test pressure for the required duration without pressure decay exceeding allowable limits.

Per ASME B31.4 / B31.8:
- **Liquid pipelines:** Test pressure = **1.25 × MAOP**
- **Gas pipelines:** Test pressure = **1.5 × MAOP**

The test is held for a minimum of **8 hours**, with continuous pressure monitoring. Any pressure drop greater than 1% of test pressure indicates a leak requiring location and repair. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** List any **six (6) phases of pipeline construction** in their correct sequence.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The six phases in correct sequence are:

1. Route Survey and Engineering
2. Right-of-Way (RoW) Acquisition and Clearing
3. Pipe Stringing, Bending and Welding
4. Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)
5. Trenching, Lowering-In and Backfilling
6. Hydrostatic Testing, Pigging and Commissioning

Each phase has a defined deliverable and acceptance criteria before progressing to the next. Skipping any phase (e.g., bypassing NDT) is a major safety violation and can lead to in-service failure. **(3 marks — ½ mark per correct phase)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive Barlow's formula for the wall thickness of a thin-walled cylindrical pipe subjected to internal pressure. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cross-country liquid hydrocarbon pipeline is to be designed for the following parameters:
- Design pressure $P = 9.0$ MPa
- Outside diameter $D = 508$ mm (20 in)
- Pipe grade API 5L X60 (SMYS) = **415 MPa**
- Design factor $F = 0.72$ (Class 1 location, liquid line)
- Longitudinal joint factor $E = 1.0$ (seamless pipe)
- Corrosion allowance $C = 1.5$ mm

Calculate the required nominal pipe wall thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Pipe wall is **thin** ($t \leq D/20$) so stress is uniform across thickness.
2. Material is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
3. Internal pressure is the only loading.
4. End-cap effects are neglected.

**Derivation:**

Consider a longitudinal cut through a pipe of length $L$, diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, subjected to internal pressure $P$.

Projected area on which pressure acts:

$$
A_p = D \cdot L
$$

Bursting force:

$$
F_b = P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

Resisting metal area (two walls on either side of the cut):

$$
A_m = 2 \cdot t \cdot L
$$

Equilibrium: $\sigma_h \cdot A_m = F_b$

$$
\sigma_h \cdot (2 t L) = P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

Solving for hoop stress:

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 t}
$$

Rearranging for wall thickness with design factor $F$, joint efficiency $E$, and allowable stress $S$:

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot F \cdot E}
$$

This is Barlow's formula. **(7 marks — 2 marks for assumptions, 5 marks for derivation)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1:** Apply Barlow's formula:

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot F \cdot E}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
t = \dfrac{9.0 \times 508}{2 \times 415 \times 0.72 \times 1.0}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerator:

$$
9.0 \times 508 = 4572 \text{ MPa·mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Denominator:

$$
2 \times 415 \times 0.72 \times 1.0 = 597.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate:

$$
t = \dfrac{4572}{597.6} = 7.65 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Add corrosion allowance:

$$
t_{design} = 7.65 + 1.5 = 9.15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7:** Round up to next standard API 5L wall thickness:

$$
t_{selected} = 9.53 \text{ mm (equivalent to 0.375 inch)}
$$

**Verification of hoop stress:**

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{9.0 \times 508}{2 \times 9.53} = 240 \text{ MPa}
$$

Compare with allowable $F \cdot S = 0.72 \times 415 = 298.8$ MPa.

Since $240 < 298.8$, **design is safe.** **(7 marks — 1 mark each for steps 1–5, 1 mark for selection, 1 mark for verification)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **field cold bending process** for line pipe. State the maximum allowable bend angle per pipe length and the minimum bend radius. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A submarine pipeline has the following data:
- Outside diameter $D = 0.406$ m (16 in)
- Submerged weight of bare pipe $W_p^{sub} = -800$ N/m
- Seawater density $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³
- Concrete density $\rho_c = 3050$ kg/m³
- Required safety factor against floatation $k = 1.3$

Calculate the required concrete coating thickness for negative buoyancy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Cold Field Bending Process:**

Cold field bending is performed using a **hydraulic pipe bender** (e.g., CRC-Evans or similar) to align the pipe with the route profile without requiring welded elbows.

**Process Steps:**
1. Lift pipe onto bender using side-boom tractors.
2. Mark bend start and end points per alignment sheet.
3. Apply incremental bending using hydraulic ram.
4. Verify bend angle using **inclinometer** and **template**.
5. Mark pipe number on extrados for traceability.

**Key Limits (per ASME B31.4 and API 1104):**
- Maximum bend angle per single pipe joint: **1.5° per pipe diameter-length**, i.e., for a 12 m joint, max angle ≈ 1.5° × (12 / D_in_m) — practically limited to about **12° per standard 12 m joint**.
- Minimum bend radius: **40 D** (standard practice) → for 24 in (610 mm) pipe → **R_min = 40 × 0.610 = 24.4 m**.
- Maximum fiber strain: **2.5%** (intrinsically safe limit to avoid wrinkles).

**Diagram (described):** A pipe is shown supported on three-point bending shoes; a hydraulic ram pushes the pipe center against the bending die; the bend is measured with a digital protractor.

**(7 marks — 3 marks process, 2 marks limits, 2 marks diagram)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1:** Compute the buoyant force per unit length for the pipe:

$$
F_{buoy} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g
$$

$$
F_{buoy} = 0.7854 \times (0.406)^2 \times 1025 \times 9.81
$$

$$
F_{buoy} = 0.7854 \times 0.1648 \times 1025 \times 9.81 = 1330 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 2:** Required net downward force with safety factor:

$$
F_{req} = k \cdot F_{buoy} = 1.3 \times 1330 = 1729 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 3:** Net downward force contributed by concrete coating must equal $F_{req} - W_p^{sub}$:

$$
\Delta F = F_{req} - W_p^{sub} = 1729 - (-800) = 2529 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 4:** Concrete coating provides downward force per unit length:

$$
\Delta F = \pi \cdot t_c \cdot (D + t_c) \cdot (\rho_c - \rho_w) \cdot g
$$

For $t_c \ll D$, simplify to:

$$
\Delta F \approx \pi \cdot t_c \cdot D \cdot (\rho_c - \rho_w) \cdot g
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $t_c$:

$$
t_c = \dfrac{\Delta F}{\pi \cdot D \cdot (\rho_c - \rho_w) \cdot g}
$$

$$
t_c = \dfrac{2529}{3.1416 \times 0.406 \times (3050 - 1025) \times 9.81}
$$

$$
t_c = \dfrac{2529}{3.1416 \times 0.406 \times 2025 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
t_c = \dfrac{2529}{25{,}287} \approx 0.100 \text{ m} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Round up to standard coating thickness:

$$
t_c = 100 \text{ mm (commonly available standard)}
$$

**Verification:** Effective negative buoyancy per unit length:

$$
W_{net} = -800 + \pi \times 0.100 \times 0.406 \times (3050 - 1025) \times 9.81
$$

$$
W_{net} = -800 + 2529 = 1729 \text{ N/m}
$$

This matches $F_{req}$ exactly. **Design is safe.** **(7 marks — distributed across all 6 steps)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance** in wall thickness calculations → loses **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing liquid vs. gas test pressure multiplier** (1.25× vs. 1.5× MAOP) → loses **1 mark** in Part A.
> 3. **Wrong units** (mixing MPa with psi, or mm with inches without conversion) → loses **2 marks** in Part B numerical problems.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** before Barlow's derivation → loses up to **2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the safety factor** in buoyancy calculation (always use k = 1.2 to 1.5) → loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Writing "PD/2t" without explaining where D comes from** in derivation → loses clarity marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Recall Points for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Barlow's formula:** $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot F \cdot E}$ — memorize verbatim with all symbols.

- ✅ **Design Factor (F):** 0.80 for offshore, 0.72 for cross-country Class 1, 0.60 for Class 2, 0.50 for Class 3, 0.40 for Class 4 (populated).

- ✅ **Hydrostatic Test Pressure:** **1.25 × MAOP (liquid)**, **1.5 × MAOP (gas)**, hold ≥ 8 hours.

- ✅ **Minimum cold bend radius:** **40 × D** (standard practice); max fiber strain 2.5%.

- ✅ **Pipeline construction phases (in order):** Survey → RoW → Stringing → Bending → Welding → NDT → Coating → Trenching → Lowering → Backfilling → Hydrotest → Pigging → Commissioning.

- ✅ **Concrete coating purpose:** Provides negative buoyancy for submarine pipelines to prevent floatation.

- ✅ **Codes:** ASME B31.4 (liquid), ASME B31.8 (gas), API 5L (pipe material), API 1104 (welding), API 1110 (hydrostatic testing).

- ✅ **Standard pipe diameters:** 16, 20, 24, 30, 36, 42, 48, 56 inches for transmission.

- ✅ **Common corrosion allowance:** 1.0 mm to 3.0 mm depending on service life and fluid corrosivity.

- ✅ **Right-of-Way width:** 30 m (cross-country) — 15 m each side of centerline.

- ✅ **Always include safety factor** in buoyancy calculations (k = 1.3 typical).

- ✅ **NDT methods:** RT (radiographic), UT (ultrasonic), MT (magnetic particle), PT (dye penetrant), AUT (automated UT for girth welds).

- ✅ **Trench depth:** ≥ 1.2 m cover for cross-country; ≥ 1.5 m under roads/railways with casing pipe.

- ✅ **Commissioning steps:** Dewatering → Drying (gas lines) → Pig run → Gas-in / Oil-in → Pressure stabilization → Handover.

> **Final Study Tip:** Practice at least **3 numerical problems** on Barlow's formula and **2 numerical problems** on concrete coating thickness before the exam. These are the two highest-weightage calculation types in the KTU board exam for this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance and Repair Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maintenance and Repair Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maintenance and Repair Activities** in hydrocarbon industries refer to the systematic, planned, and safety-governed set of operations performed on process equipment, piping systems, storage tanks, pressure vessels, rotating machinery, and instrumentation to ensure continued operational integrity, reliability, and compliance with statutory safety standards throughout the equipment life cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Maintenance in hydrocarbon facilities is not merely a mechanical task; it is a **Safety-Critical Function (SCF)** governed by Permit-to-Work (PTW) systems, Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) procedures, Hazard Identification (HAZID), and Risk Assessment matrices under the framework of OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate), API (American Petroleum Institute), and OSHA 29 CFR 1910 standards.

**Repair Activities** are corrective interventions carried out to restore equipment to its design operating condition following wear, corrosion, fatigue, mechanical damage, or process deviation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of an offshore oil platform as the **human body**:
- **Routine Maintenance (Preventive)** = Annual health check-ups, oil changes in a car, brushing teeth.
- **Breakdown Maintenance (Reactive)** = Emergency room visit after a heart attack or accident.
- **Predictive Maintenance** = Smart watches that detect irregular heartbeat *before* a heart attack.

Just as you would not perform open-heart surgery in a kitchen without sterile conditions, you cannot weld a live hydrocarbon pipeline without a **Hot Work Permit, Gas Testing, and Isolation Certificate**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined)

- **Permit to Work (PTW)**: A formal written authorization document controlling high-risk maintenance activities.
- **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)**: A safety procedure ensuring machinery is de-energized and cannot be re-energized during maintenance.
- **Hot Work**: Any work involving sparks, flames, or heat (welding, grinding, cutting) in areas where flammable vapors may be present.
- **Cold Work**: Maintenance activities that do not introduce ignition sources.
- **Confined Space Entry (CSE)**: Entry into vessels, tanks, pits, or ducts with limited entry/exit and hazardous atmosphere potential.
- **MOC (Management of Change)**: Formal process for evaluating changes to equipment, procedures, or materials.
- **LOTO Standard**: **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** and **OISD-STD-105**.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, **Permit-to-Work systems carry a guaranteed 7–14 mark weightage** in any Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries module question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Maintenance Strategies

Maintenance in hydrocarbon industries is classified into **six fundamental strategies**, each with distinct risk and cost profiles:

| S.No | Maintenance Type | Trigger | Cost Profile | Risk Profile |
|------|-----------------|---------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Reactive (Breakdown)** | Equipment failure | Very Low upfront, Very High after failure | Very High |
| 2 | **Preventive (Scheduled)** | Calendar/hours | Moderate | Low |
| 3 | **Predictive (Condition-Based)** | Vibration, thermography, NDT | High upfront | Very Low |
| 4 | **Prescriptive (AI-Driven)** | Real-time analytics | Very High upfront | Minimal |
| 5 | **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** | Failure mode analysis | High | Very Low |
| 6 | **Risk-Based Maintenance (RBM)** | Consequence × Probability | Optimized | Tolerable (ALARP) |

### 2.2 Why Maintenance is a SAFETY Function in Hydrocarbon Industries

**The 'Why' Behind Each Maintenance Step:**

- **Step 1 — Identification:** Equipment in hydrocarbon service handles flammable, toxic, and high-pressure fluids. A flange leak can escalate to **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** within seconds.
- **Step 2 — Isolation:** Before any maintenance, the equipment must be physically separated from live hydrocarbon sources using **Double Block and Bleed (DBB)** valves.
- **Step 3 — Depressurization & Purging:** Residual hydrocarbons are removed using nitrogen purging or steam cleaning to achieve **LEL < 1%**.
- **Step 4 — Gas Testing:** A competent person tests the atmosphere using a calibrated 4-gas detector (O₂, LEL, H₂S, CO).
- **Step 5 — Permit Issuance:** Only after gas test clearance is the appropriate permit issued.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|-------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\sum \text{Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Equipment reliability |
| **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum \text{Downtime for Repairs}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | hours | Maintainability index |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | System uptime |
| **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | failures/hour | Reliability metric |
| **Risk Score (R)** | $R = P \times C$ | dimensionless | RBM prioritization |
| **Risk Reduction Factor** | $\text{RRF} = \frac{P_{\text{without}}}{\ P_{\text{with}}}$ | ratio | Safety barrier efficacy |
| **SIL Verification (PFD)** | $\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i$ | dimensionless (per year) | IEC 61508/61511 |
| **LEL Safety Threshold** | $\text{Hydrocarbon conc.} < 1\% \text{ LEL}$ | % LEL | Hot work criterion |
| **Oxygen Safe Range** | $19.5\% \le O_2 \le 23.5\%$ | % volume | Confined space entry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Questions on **MTBF, MTTR, and Availability** are almost guaranteed in numerical sections of the KTU end-semester exam. Memorize the relationship $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Maintenance engineering in hydrocarbon industries directly supports:
- **Asset Integrity Management (AIM)** per **API RP 580 / 581**.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** determination for Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) per **IEC 61511**.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** calculations.
- **Compliance auditing** for **OISD, PESO, and OSHA** inspections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Maintenance Availability Equation

The **steady-state availability** of a repairable system is derived as follows:

$$
A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}
$$

For a system with multiple failure and repair cycles:

$$
A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Time Between Failure}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Time Between Failure}_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Time To Repair}_i}
$$

$$
A = \frac{n \cdot \text{MTBF}}{n \cdot \text{MTBF} + n \cdot \text{MTTR}}
$$

Simplifying by dividing numerator and denominator by $n$:

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump in a crude oil pipeline has been operated for **8760 hours** in one year. During this period, it failed **4 times** and the cumulative repair time was **60 hours**. Calculate the MTBF, MTTR, and Availability.

#### Step 1: Calculate Operating Time and Downtime

$$
\text{Operating Time} = 8760 - 60 = 8700 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\text{Total Downtime} = 60 \text{ hours}
$$

#### Step 2: Calculate MTBF

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{8700}{4}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF} = 2175 \text{ hours/failure}
$$

#### Step 3: Calculate MTTR

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}} = \frac{60}{4}
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = 15 \text{ hours/repair}
$$

#### Step 4: Calculate Availability

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{2175}{2175 + 15}
$$

$$
A = \frac{2175}{2190} = 0.9932 \text{ or } 99.32\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In hydrocarbon industries, **Availability > 99%** is a benchmark for critical rotating equipment. Below 95% triggers reliability review per API 682.

### 3.3 Risk-Based Maintenance (RBM) Matrix Derivation

The **Risk Score** for prioritizing maintenance is:

$$
R = P \times C
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Probability of Failure (rated 1 to 5)
- $C$ = Consequence of Failure (rated 1 to 5)

**Risk Classification Matrix:**

| Risk Score (R) | Classification | Action |
|----------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 – 4 | **Low** | Routine monitoring |
| 5 – 9 | **Medium** | Planned preventive maintenance |
| 10 – 15 | **High** | Immediate corrective action |
| 16 – 25 | **Very High** | Emergency shutdown + replacement |

### 3.4 Permit-to-Work (PTW) Procedural Derivation

The **Hierarchy of Permit Issuance** for hydrocarbon maintenance:

$$
\text{Safe Maintenance} = \text{Isolation} + \text{Depressurization} + \text{Purging} + \text{Testing} + \text{Permit}
$$

This can be expressed as a Boolean safety gate:

$$
S_{\text{PTW}} = I \cap D \cap P \cap T \cap M
$$

Where each variable is binary (1 = satisfied, 0 = not satisfied). The work proceeds **only if** $S_{\text{PTW}} = 1$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Permit-to-Work (PTW) Workflow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Maintenance Request Raised] --> B{Hazard Classification}
    B -->|Low Risk| C[Cold Work Permit]
    B -->|Medium Risk| D[Hot Work Permit]
    B -->|High Risk| E[Confined Space Permit]
    B -->|Critical| F[Shutdown + Isolation Permit]
    C --> G[Equipment Isolation LOTO]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Depressurization Purging]
    H --> I[Gas Testing LEL Verification]
    I --> J{Gas Safe Condition}
    J -->|No| K[Stop Work Reassess]
    J -->|Yes| L[Issue Permit by Area Authority]
    L --> M[Execute Work Under Supervisor]
    M --> N[Periodic Gas ReTesting]
    N --> O[Work Completion Sign Off]
    O --> P[Remove Isolation Restore Service]
    P --> Q[PTW Closure and Documentation]
```

### 4.2 Maintenance Strategy Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Equipment Criticality Assessment] --> nodeB{Risk Score R = P x C}
    nodeB -->|R less than 4| nodeC[Reactive Maintenance]
    nodeB -->|R 5 to 9| nodeD[Preventive Maintenance]
    nodeB -->|R 10 to 15| nodeE[Predictive CBM Maintenance]
    nodeB -->|R greater than 15| nodeF[Prescriptive AI Driven]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Cost Optimization]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Asset Integrity Compliance]
```

### 4.3 Hot Work Permit Issuance Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req1[Work Site Survey] --> req2[Identify Flammable Sources]
    req2 --> req3[Establish 11m Safety Zone]
    req3 --> req4[Remove Combustibles or Cover]
    req4 --> req5[Continuous Gas Monitoring]
    req5 --> req6[Fire Extinguisher on Standby]
    req6 --> req7[Trained Hot Work Operator]
    req7 --> req8[Issue Hot Work Permit]
    req8 --> req9[Supervisor Spot Check Every 30 Minutes]
    req9 --> req10[Post Work Fire Watch 60 Minutes]
```

### 4.4 Confined Space Entry Safety Layer Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    layer1[Layer 1 Entry Authorization] --> layer2[Layer 2 Atmospheric Testing]
    layer2 --> layer3[Layer 3 Ventilation Provision]
    layer3 --> layer4[Layer 4 Standby Attendant]
    layer4 --> layer5[Layer 5 Rescue Equipment Ready]
    layer5 --> layer6[Layer 6 Communication System]
    layer6 --> layer7[Layer 7 Entry Execution]
    layer7 --> layer8[Layer 8 Continuous Gas Monitoring]
    layer8 --> layer9[Layer 9 Exit and Seal Closure]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Permit-to-Work (PTW) system. List any four types of permits commonly used in hydrocarbon industry maintenance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A Permit-to-Work (PTW) is a formal written authorization document issued by an authorized person to control hazardous maintenance activities, ensuring that equipment is properly isolated, tested, and made safe before work begins.

**Four Types of Permits (2 Marks — ½ Mark Each):**

1. **Hot Work Permit** — Welding, grinding, cutting in flammable areas.
2. **Cold Work Permit** — Non-ignition maintenance activities.
3. **Confined Space Entry Permit** — Entry into vessels, tanks, pits.
4. **Electrical Isolation Permit / LOTO Permit** — De-energization of electrical systems.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Distinguish between Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Trigger** | Time-based / calendar | Condition-based (vibration, NDT) |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Higher upfront |
| **Equipment Interruption** | Planned shutdown | Minimal / on-condition |
| **Tools Used** | Calendars, checklists | Vibration analyzers, thermography, ultrasonics |
| **Downtime** | Higher | Lower |

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Permit-to-Work (PTW) system** used in hydrocarbon industries. Discuss the different types of permits and the safety procedures involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pressure vessel in a refinery has operated for **10,000 hours** in one year. It failed **5 times**, and the total repair time was **100 hours**. Calculate the **MTBF, MTTR, and Availability** of the vessel. Comment on whether this availability is acceptable for refinery critical equipment. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A**

#### **Part (a) — Permit-to-Work System (7 Marks)**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
A Permit-to-Work (PTW) system is a formal written safety procedure used to control hazardous maintenance activities in hydrocarbon facilities, ensuring equipment isolation, atmosphere testing, and authorized execution.

**2. Types of Permits (3 Marks):**

- **Hot Work Permit:** Issued for welding, cutting, grinding where ignition sources are present. Requires LEL < 1% gas test, fire extinguisher standby, and 60-minute post-work fire watch.
- **Cold Work Permit:** Issued for non-ignition activities like scaffolding, painting, insulation.
- **Confined Space Entry Permit:** Required for entry into vessels, tanks, pits; mandates gas testing, standby attendant, and rescue equipment.
- **Electrical Work Permit (LOTO):** Authorizes de-energization and lockout of electrical systems.
- **Radiography Permit:** For non-destructive testing involving radioactive sources.

**3. PTW Safety Procedure (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Job description and hazard identification.
**Step 2:** Equipment isolation using **Double Block and Bleed (DBB)** valves.
**Step 3:** Depressurization and nitrogen/steam purging.
**Step 4:** Atmospheric testing — Oxygen (19.5%–23.5%), LEL (< 1%), H₂S (< 10 ppm), CO (< 25 ppm).
**Step 5:** Permit issuance by competent authority.
**Step 6:** Continuous supervision and periodic re-testing.
**Step 7:** Job completion, permit closure, and isolation removal.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Total operating time = 10,000 hours
- Number of failures = 5
- Total repair time = 100 hours

**Step 1: MTBF Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{10000}{5} = 2000 \text{ hours/failure}
$$

**Step 2: MTTR Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{100}{5} = 20 \text{ hours/repair}
$$

**Step 3: Availability Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 20} = \frac{2000}{2020}
$$

$$
A = 0.9901 \text{ or } 99.01\%
$$

**Step 4: Comment (1 Mark):**

An availability of **99.01%** is **acceptable** for refinery critical equipment, as it exceeds the industry benchmark of 99% per API standards.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **different types of maintenance strategies** followed in hydrocarbon industries with suitable examples. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) procedure** in detail with a neat flowchart. Why is LOTO considered the most critical safety barrier during maintenance? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B**

#### **Part (a) — Maintenance Strategies (7 Marks)**

**1. Reactive (Breakdown) Maintenance (1.5 Marks):**
Performed after equipment fails. Low cost initially but high consequence in hydrocarbon service (e.g., emergency shutdown of a pump after seizure).

**2. Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance (1.5 Marks):**
Performed at fixed intervals (e.g., 6-monthly overhaul of gas turbines). Reduces unexpected failures but may replace healthy components.

**3. Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance (2 Marks):**
Uses vibration analysis, thermography, ultrasonic thickness measurement to detect degradation. Example: Monitoring bearing temperature of a compressor.

**4. Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) (1 Mark):**
Focuses on preserving system function rather than equipment itself. Uses Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA).

**5. Risk-Based Maintenance (RBM) (1 Mark):**
Prioritizes maintenance based on $R = P \times C$, optimizing cost and safety.

---

#### **Part (b) — LOTO Procedure (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) is a safety procedure that ensures machinery is properly shut off, isolated, and cannot be re-energized during maintenance per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147**.

**Step-by-Step LOTO Procedure (4 Marks):**

1. **Notification:** Inform affected employees of impending shutdown.
2. **Equipment Shutdown:** Follow normal stopping procedure.
3. **Isolation:** Disconnect energy sources (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal).
4. **Lockout:** Apply personal locks and tags to isolation points.
5. **Stored Energy Release:** Bleed accumulators, drain lines, dissipate capacitors.
6. **Verification:** Test that equipment will not start (try-to-start).
7. **Work Execution:** Perform maintenance.
8. **Removal:** Restore energy only after all locks are removed by authorized personnel.

**Why LOTO is the Most Critical Safety Barrier (2 Marks):**
- Prevents accidental energization that could cause electrocution, mechanical injury, or hydrocarbon release.
- It is the **last line of defense** between workers and uncontrolled energy.
- Failure of LOTO has caused fatal incidents such as the **2005 Texas City Refinery explosion** where improper isolation led to catastrophic hydrocarbon release.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> - ❌ Writing "MTBF" without units → Lose **½ mark** for unit omission.
> - ❌ Confusing MTTR (repair time) with MTBF (operating time) → Lose **full marks**.
> - ❌ Not mentioning **LEL < 1%** in gas testing criteria → Lose **1 mark** in PTW answers.
> - ❌ Omitting **Double Block and Bleed (DBB)** during isolation discussion → Lose **1 mark**.
> - ❌ In numerical problems, not showing **formula substitution step** → Lose **1 mark** for incomplete working.
> - ❌ Writing "LOTO" without expanding as **Lockout-Tagout** at least once → Lose **½ mark**.
> - ❌ Failing to cite **OISD/API standards** in theoretical answers → Lose up to **1 mark** for lack of industrial relevance.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

- ✅ **Maintenance** is a **Safety-Critical Function (SCF)** in hydrocarbon industries, not just an operational task.
- ✅ Six maintenance types: **Reactive, Preventive, Predictive, Prescriptive, RCM, RBM**.
- ✅ **Availability** formula: $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — must remember the unit conversions.
- ✅ **PTW types:** Hot Work, Cold Work, Confined Space, Electrical/LOTO, Radiography.
- ✅ **Gas testing criteria:** Oxygen 19.5%–23.5%, LEL < 1%, H₂S < 10 ppm.
- ✅ **LOTO** is governed by **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** and **OISD-STD-105**.
- ✅ **Risk Score:** $R = P \times C$ — used in RBM prioritization.
- ✅ **Double Block and Bleed (DBB)** is the standard isolation method for hydrocarbon systems.
- ✅ Industry benchmarks: **Availability > 99%**, **MTBF > 2000 hours** for critical equipment.
- ✅ Always mention **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) when discussing risk reduction.
- ✅ Post-hot-work **fire watch** of **60 minutes** is mandatory per **NFPA 51B**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety and Associated Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety and Associated Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **hydrocarbon processing industry (HPI)** — comprising oil & gas exploration, refining, petrochemicals, LNG terminals, and storage farms — the term **"Safety and Associated Hazards"** is defined as the systematic identification, evaluation, and control of physical, chemical, thermal, mechanical, and process-related conditions that possess the intrinsic potential to cause loss of life, injury, property damage, environmental degradation, or business interruption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard Wording):**
> A *hazard* is any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury or ill-health, damage to property, damage to the environment, or a combination of these. *Safety* is the umbrella discipline of engineering and management practices employed to keep hazards under control so that the risks to people, plant, and planet remain at an **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** level.

The governing framework of modern hydrocarbon safety rests on three pillars:
1. **Inherent Safety (ISD)** — Designing out the hazard at the conceptual stage.
2. **Engineering Controls** — Passive (bunds, blast walls) and Active (deluge, ESD systems).
3. **Administrative & PPE Controls** — Procedures, training, and personal protective equipment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen & Gas Stove" Intuition

Imagine a domestic LPG cylinder connected to a gas stove in your kitchen.

- **The LPG gas itself** is the *hazard* — it has the intrinsic property of being flammable.
- **An open flame or spark** is the *initiator* (the energy release needed).
- **Oxygen in air** is the *oxidizer*.
- Together, these three form the **Fire Triangle**.

Now, *safety* in this analogy is:
- Storing the cylinder in a ventilated space (prevents accumulation).
- Installing a leak detector (early warning).
- Using a flame arrester in the regulator (engineering control).
- Turning the knob off after cooking (administrative control).

**A refinery is simply a "kitchen" that handles millions of times more energy and toxic chemicals — therefore the same principles, scaled up and formalized into codes (API, NFPA, OSHA, OISD), govern it.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Physical Constants to Remember (Board-Favorite Values):**
> - **LFL (Lower Flammable Limit) of n-Hexane in air = 1.1 % v/v**
> - **UFL (Upper Flammable Limit) of n-Hexane in air = 7.5 % v/v**
> - **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT) of Hexane = 225 °C (437 °F)**
> - **Flash Point of n-Hexane = −22 °C (closed cup)**
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (by mass) for paraffins ≈ 14.6 – 15.2**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hazards in the Hydrocarbon Industry

KTU examiners consistently test the **classification taxonomy**. The industrial hierarchy is:

### A. Physical / Mechanical Hazards
- High-pressure leaks, rotating equipment, falling objects, confined space entry, noise (>85 dBA), thermal stress, vibration.

### B. Chemical Hazards
- **Fire** (rapid oxidation with light/heat).
- **Explosion** (rapid oxidation with shock wave).
- **Toxic Release** (acute/chronic exposure — TLV, PEL, IDLH limits).
- **Corrosion / Erosion**.

### C. Process Hazards (Loss of Containment — LOC)
- Runaway reactions, over-pressurization, phase-change issues, two-phase flow, thermal expansion in blocked lines.

### D. Environmental / ER Hazards
- Spillage to water bodies, atmospheric dispersion of VOCs, sour gas (H₂S) release.

### E. Human / Ergonomic Hazards
- Human error (a root cause in ~80% of major incidents per the **Swiss Cheese Model**, Reason, 1990).

## 2.2 The Fire Triangle and Explosion Pentagon

The **Fire Triangle** requires three elements simultaneously:
- **Fuel** (hydrocarbon vapor within flammable range)
- **Oxidizer** (typically air — 21% O₂ by volume)
- **Ignition Source** (energy ≥ Minimum Ignition Energy, MIE)

For an **Explosion (Deflagration or Detonation)**, two additional elements convert the triangle to a **Pentagon**:
- **Confinement** (vessels, pipework, building walls).
- **Mixing / Dispersion** (turbulent cloud formation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for the KTU Board:**
> *"Fuel + Air + Spark = Fire. Add Walls + Mix → BOOM!"*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ for hydrocarbon CₙHₘ | $n + \tfrac{m}{4}$ | mol O₂ / mol fuel | Balanced combustion basis |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air (molar) | $4.773 \times \left(n + \tfrac{m}{4}\right)$ | mol air / mol fuel | Air = 1/0.2095 ≈ 4.773 |
| 3 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{4.773 \times 29 \times (n + m/4)}{12n + m}$ | kg air / kg fuel | 29 = MW of air |
| 4 | Flammability Range | $\Delta F = \text{UFL} - \text{LFL}$ | % v/v | Wider range ⇒ more hazardous |
| 5 | TNT Equivalent (VCE) | $W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{\text{TNT}}}$ | kg TNT | $\eta$ = empirical yield (0.01–0.1) |
| 6 | Risk | $R = P \times C$ | dimensionless | $P$ = probability, $C$ = consequence |
| 7 | Crowl–Louvar Fire Power | $Q_r = m'' \cdot A \cdot \Delta H_c$ | kW | Pool fire heat release |
| 8 | Mass Burning Rate (Pool) | $m'' = 0.001 \cdot \Delta H_c / (c_p \cdot T_b + h_fg)$ | kg/m²·s | Semi-empirical (Dobbins) |
| 9 | Vapor Cloud Dispersion (Gaussian) | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\tfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \tfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | ppm or kg/m³ | Pasquill–Gifford model |
| 10 | Maximum Experimental Safe Gap (MESG) | Device-dependent | mm | Used for Ex-proof classification |
| 11 | Flash Point | Lowest $T$ giving flammable vapor | °C | Closed cup vs open cup differs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:**
> Always write the **units explicitly** in derivations. Marks are frequently docked when a student writes "5.6" without units like kg/s or mol/mol.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

These equations are the **backbone of Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** used by:
- **ONGC, IOCL, BPCL, HPCL** for refinery siting (per **OISD-118 / OISD-189** norms).
- **API RP 752 / 753** for occupied building assessment.
- **COMAH (UK) / OSHA PSM (USA)** regulatory audits.

By knowing the **TNT equivalent** of a predicted vapor cloud, engineers can size **blast walls**, determine **safe separation distances**, and design **control room blast ratings** (typically 0.3 bar overpressure for safe refuge).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for a Paraffin CₙH₂ₙ₊₂

We will exhaustively derive the air requirement for burning a generic alkane — this is a **guaranteed KTU 14-mark question** every cycle.

**Step 1: Write the Unbalanced Combustion Equation**

$$ \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + a\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 2: Apply Carbon Balance (1 C on LHS → must equal CO₂ on RHS)**

$$ b = n $$

**Step 3: Apply Hydrogen Balance (H₂ on RHS)**

$$ 2c = 2n+2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = n+1 $$

**Step 4: Apply Oxygen Balance (on RHS we have $2b + c$ oxygen atoms, on LHS $2a$)**

$$ 2a = 2n + (n+1) = 3n+1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = \frac{3n+1}{2} $$

**Step 5: Convert to Moles of Air, knowing Air = O₂ + 3.76 N₂**

$$ \text{Moles of air per mole fuel} = \frac{4.773\,(3n+1)}{2} $$

**Step 6: Compute Mass Air-Fuel Ratio**

Molecular weight of fuel: $M_f = 12n + (2n+2) = 14n + 2$

Molecular weight of air: $M_a = 28.97$ (use **29** for convenience)

$$ \text{AFR}_{\text{mass}} = \frac{4.773 \times (3n+1)/2 \times 29}{14n+2} $$

**Step 7: Numerical Example — n-Butane (n = 4)**

$$ \text{AFR} = \frac{4.773 \times 13/2 \times 29}{58} = \frac{4.773 \times 6.5 \times 29}{58} $$

$$ = \frac{899.74}{58} \approx 15.51 \;\text{kg air per kg butane} $$

This matches the standard **15.5** tabulated in engineering handbooks. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation — TNT Equivalent of a Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)

Given an instantaneous release of mass $M$ kg of hydrocarbon vapor with heat of combustion $H_c$ kJ/kg, find the equivalent TNT mass producing the same blast overpressure.

**Step 1: Total Chemical Energy Released**

$$ E_{\text{chem}} = M \cdot H_c \quad [\text{kJ}] $$

**Step 2: Account for Explosion Yield (only fraction converts to blast energy)**

$$ E_{\text{blast}} = \eta \cdot E_{\text{chem}} $$

where **$\eta$** is the empirical yield factor (TNT equivalent coefficient):
- Confined congested cloud: $\eta \approx 0.05$–$0.10$
- Open unconfined cloud: $\eta \approx 0.01$–$0.03$

**Step 3: Express in Terms of TNT Mass Using $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4686$ kJ/kg**

$$ W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{E_{\text{blast}}}{H_{\text{TNT}}} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{4686} $$

**Step 4: Apply Scaled Distance for Overpressure Prediction**

$$ Z = \frac{R}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}} $$

Plug into **Kingery–Bulmash** overpressure correlation to obtain $\Delta P$.

**Step 5: Numerical Example**

Given: $M = 10{,}000$ kg propane release, $H_c = 46{,}000$ kJ/kg, $\eta = 0.05$.

$$ W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{0.05 \times 10{,}000 \times 46{,}000}{4686} $$

$$ = \frac{2.3 \times 10^7}{4686} \approx 4908\;\text{kg TNT} \approx 4.9\;\text{tonnes TNT} $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hazard × Consequence × Control Mapping

| Hazard Type | Initiator | Consequence | Engineering Control | Administrative Control |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Pool Fire | LPG / naphtha spill | Thermal radiation, fatality within 10 m | Foam pourer, dyke bunding | Hot work permit, gas test |
| VCE | Gas leak + ignition | Blast overpressure, structural damage | Vapor detection, gas dispersion modeling | Zoning, ignition source control |
| BLEVE | Boiling liquid in fire | Missiling of vessel fragments | Water spray cooling, mounded bullets | Emergency isolation, evacuation |
| Toxic Release (H₂S) | Sour gas leak | 10 ppm headache, 700 ppm lethal | SCBA, gas masks, wind sock | MSDS, H₂S escape sets |
| Runaway Reaction | Exotherm loss of cooling | Vessel rupture | Rupture discs, vent sizing (DIERS) | Critical parameter SOP |
| Confined Space | Oxygen depletion | Asphyxiation | Forced ventilation, LEL meters | Permit-to-work system |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Process Safety Management (PSM) System

```mermaid

    hazID["Hazard Identification HAZOP HAZID What-If"]
    riskEval["Risk Assessment QRA LOPA Matrix"]
    inherent["Inherent Safety Design Minimize Substitute Moderate Simplify"]
    engCtrl["Engineering Controls ESD SIS Relief Valves"]
    adminCtrl["Administrative Controls PTW LOTO Training"]
    ppeCtrl["PPE Last Line of Defense"]
    monitor["Monitoring and Audit KPIs Leading vs Lagging"]
    improve["Continuous Improvement PDCA Cycle"]

    hazID --> riskEval
    riskEval --> inherent
    riskEval --> engCtrl
    riskEval --> adminCtrl

    adminCtrl --> monitor

```

## 4.3 Hazard Recognition Hierarchy — Controls of Last Resort

```mermaid
graph TD
    lvl5["Level 5 PPE - Hardhat Helmet SCBA Gloves"]
    lvl4["Level 4 Administrative - Permit Procedures Training"]
    lvl3["Level 3 Active Engineering - Detectors Sprinklers ESD"]
    lvl2["Level 2 Passive Engineering - Bunds Blast Walls Dikes"]
    lvl1["Level 1 Inherent Safety - Less Inventory Lower Temp Less Hazardous Material"]

    lvl1 --> lvl2 --> lvl3 --> lvl4 --> lvl5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Controls (KTU-Relevant Principle):**
> Always prefer **Level 1 Inherent Safety** — eliminating the hazard is far more reliable than adding layers of protection. KTU examiners reward students who phrase answers in this order: *Eliminate → Substitute → Engineer → Administer → PPE*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Differentiate between Hazard and Risk with two suitable examples from a hydrocarbon refinery."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Hazard** is the intrinsic property of a substance or situation to cause harm (e.g., naphtha is *hazardous* because it is flammable).
- **Risk** is the combination of the probability of that hazard occurring and the severity of its consequences — expressed as $R = P \times C$.
- **Example 1:** *Hazard* — LPG storage bullets. *Risk* — Likelihood of BLEVE during an external pool fire.
- **Example 2:** *Hazard* — H₂S presence in crude. *Risk* — Probability × fatality if a worker is exposed above IDLH (100 ppm).

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"State the Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) and Upper Flammable Limit (UFL) of methane in air, and explain why these limits are critical in confined space entry."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- LFL of methane = **5.0 % v/v**; UFL = **15.0 % v/v**.
- A mixture is flammable only between these limits — the *Flammable Range*.
- In confined space entry, the LEL meter must be calibrated and the reading must remain **< 10% of LFL** before a worker enters, ensuring a safety margin against a flammable atmosphere.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]*

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **Fire Triangle** and **Explosion Pentagon**, listing the physical conditions required for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **vapor cloud of 8,000 kg of propane** is released instantaneously and ignites in a semi-congested area. Estimate the **TNT equivalent** and the **scaled distance** at a receptor **250 m** away. Given: heat of combustion of propane $H_c = 46{,}000$ kJ/kg; $H_{\text{TNT}} = 4686$ kJ/kg; empirical yield $\eta = 0.04$. Comment on the likely damage. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks
1. Define the **Fire Triangle**: Fuel + Oxidizer + Ignition Source.
2. Sketch (verbal description allowed):
   - Three vertices labelled FUEL, OXIDIZER (21% O₂), IGNITION SOURCE.
3. **Explosion Pentagon**: Add CONFINEMENT + MIXING.
4. List physical conditions:
   - Fuel concentration between LFL and UFL.
   - Sufficient oxidizer (typically air, O₂ > 12%).
   - Ignition energy ≥ **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** — e.g., 0.25 mJ for methane.
   - Confinement that allows pressure build-up.
   - Adequate mixing for a homogeneous cloud.
5. **Key difference:** Fire gives flames and heat; Explosion produces a destructive pressure wave. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks
**Step 1:** Total chemical energy
$$ E = M \cdot H_c = 8000 \times 46{,}000 = 3.68 \times 10^8 \;\text{kJ} $$

**Step 2:** Blast energy with $\eta = 0.04$
$$ E_b = 0.04 \times 3.68 \times 10^8 = 1.472 \times 10^7 \;\text{kJ} $$

**Step 3:** TNT mass
$$ W_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{1.472 \times 10^7}{4686} \approx 3142\;\text{kg} = 3.14\;\text{tonnes TNT} $$

**Step 4:** Scaled distance
$$ Z = \frac{R}{W_{\text{TNT}}^{1/3}} = \frac{250}{(3142)^{1/3}} = \frac{250}{14.65} \approx 17.06 \;\text{m/kg}^{1/3} $$

**Step 5:** Damage commentary — At $Z \approx 17$, the overpressure from Kingery–Bulmash is roughly **0.5–1.0 psi (≈ 3.5–7 kPa)** — sufficient to shatter glass and cause minor structural damage. People at 250 m could suffer **ear-drum rupture** and **ejection injuries**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often forget to convert $H_c$ from MJ/kg to kJ/kg — be consistent with units.
> 2. The yield factor $\eta$ is **not** 1.0; using full combustion energy will massively over-estimate $W_{\text{TNT}}$.
> 3. Always state **assumptions**: is the cloud stoichiometric? Is the release instantaneous or continuous? Are units for $W_{\text{TNT}}$ in **kg or tonnes**?
> 4. The scaled-distance definition $Z = R/W^{1/3}$ must be written correctly; reversed notation loses a mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Alternative]*

**(a)** Explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** used in process safety with a flowchart, and justify why **inherent safety** is the most preferred layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A refinery distillation column releases **hexane vapor** into a partially confined area. The mass released is **500 kg**, ambient temperature **30 °C**, wind speed **3 m/s**, and stability class **D**. Using the **Pasquill–Gifford Gaussian model**, estimate the **ground-level concentration at 200 m downwind** on the plume centre-line. Given: dispersion coefficients at 200 m for class D — $\sigma_y = 15$ m, $\sigma_z = 12$ m. Assume continuous release rate $Q = 0.5$ kg/s. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks
1. Define the 5 layers: Inherent → Passive Engineering → Active Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
2. Draw the inverted pyramid / flowchart.
3. Explain each layer with hydrocarbon examples:
   - *Inherent* — inventory reduction, lower operating temperature.
   - *Passive* — dyke walls, fire-proofing.
   - *Active* — gas detectors, deluge system.
   - *Administrative* — Permit to Work, LOTO.
   - *PPE* — respirators, flame-retardant clothing.
4. Justify Inherent Safety: it removes the hazard at source, does not depend on operator action, hardware reliability, or maintenance — therefore the **probability of failure is lowest**.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks
**Step 1:** On the centre-line $y = 0$, $z = 0$ (ground reflection included).

$$ C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) $$

Take $H \approx 0$ (ground-level release):

$$ C = \frac{0.5}{\pi \times 15 \times 12 \times 3} $$

$$ = \frac{0.5}{1696.46} \approx 2.947 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 2:** Convert to ppm for hexane (MW = 86 g/mol):

$$ C_{\text{ppm}} = \frac{2.947 \times 10^{-4} \times 24.45}{86} \times 10^6 $$

$$ \approx 83.8\;\text{ppm} $$

**Step 3:** Compare with LFL of hexane (11,000 ppm = 1.1%):
- $83.8$ ppm $\ll$ LFL ⇒ **non-flammable**, but above **TLV-TWA (50 ppm)** ⇒ toxic hazard.
- Recommendation: **Trigger evacuation and isolate the source**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before Walking into the KTU Exam Hall — Memorize These:**

- 🔥 **Fire Triangle:** Fuel + O₂ + Ignition ⇒ Fire
- 💥 **Explosion Pentagon:** Fire Triangle + Confinement + Mixing
- 📐 **AFR (mass) for paraffins** ≈ 14.6 – 15.2
- 🧨 **TNT Equivalent Formula:** $W_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta M H_c}{4686}$ (use **4686 kJ/kg**)
- 🌬️ **Gaussian Plume:** centre-line formula $\dfrac{Q}{\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u}$
- 📊 **Risk = Probability × Consequence**
- ⚖️ **ALARP** — keep risks *As Low As Reasonably Practicable*
- 🛡️ **Hierarchy of Controls:** Inherent → Passive → Active → Admin → PPE
- 🪪 **Key Standards:** API 521/520/650/752; NFPA 30/58/69; OISD-118/189; OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119
- 🚨 **Safe Entry LEL Threshold** = **< 10% of LFL**
- 🧪 **Methane limits:** LFL = 5.0%, UFL = 15.0%; **LFL Hexane = 1.1%, UFL = 7.5%**
- 🧯 **BLEVE** = Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion — associated with mounded bullets and pressurized LPG
- 🎯 **Stoichiometric air factor** = 1/0.2095 = **4.773**
- 🧠 **Swiss Cheese Model (Reason):** Multiple barrier failures align → accident
- 📋 **PTW Categories:** Cold Work, Hot Work, Confined Space, Electrical, Height Work
- 🔒 **LOTO:** Lock-Out / Tag-Out — mandatory before maintenance
- 💨 **Vent sizing:** API 521 / DIERS methodology for runaway reactions

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Safety and Associated Hazards**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Protection Facilities in Oil Refineries, Depots and Terminals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fire Protection Facilities in Oil Refineries, Depots and Terminals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Protection Facilities** in oil refineries, depots and terminals comprise an integrated, engineered system of **detection, notification, containment, suppression, and cooling** infrastructure designed to prevent, control, and extinguish fires involving **flammable and combustible hydrocarbons (Class A, B & C fires)** in compliance with statutory codes (OISD, NFPA, IS, TAC, PESO).

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology (PESFT745), fire protection is treated as a **passive–active engineered safeguard layer** under the *Inherent Safety Design* hierarchy (after elimination, substitution, and attenuation). The hierarchy is:

1. **Prevention** (eliminate ignition sources, hydrocarbon release)
2. **Detection** (linear heat cable, flame/IR detectors, gas detectors)
3. **Isolation** (ESD valves, fire-safe valves, block valves)
4. **Suppression** (foam, water spray, dry chemical, gas)
5. **Cooling** (water spray on adjacent vessels/tanks)
6. **Drainage & Containment** (bund walls, dykes, drain trenches)

**Key Reference Standards (must memorize for KTU ESE):**

| Standard | Scope |
|----------|-------|
| **OISD-STD-114** | Fire Protection for Petroleum Refineries & Gas Processing Plants |
| **OISD-STD-116** | Fire Protection for Oil Depots & Terminals |
| **OISD-STD-117** | Fire Fighting Equipment |
| **NFPA 11** | Low, Medium & High Expansion Foam |
| **NFPA 15** | Water Spray Fixed Systems |
| **NFPA 16** | Foam-Water Sprinkler/Spray Systems |
| **NFPA 30** | Flammable & Combustible Liquids Code |
| **IS 3034** | Fire Extinguisher Application |
| **TAC** | Tariff Advisory Committee (now IRDA) India |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Human Immune System"**
> Think of a hydrocarbon facility as a living body:
> - **Skin (Bund walls, dykes)** — first physical barrier preventing the "blood" (oil) from spilling out
> - **Nervous System (Fire & Gas Detection)** — senses the danger (heat, smoke, flame, gas)
> - **Brain (F&G PLC / Fire Alarm Panel)** — processes signals and triggers response
> - **Immune Response (Foam, water spray, sprinklers)** — actively attacks the fire
> - **White Blood Cells (Fire brigade + ESD)** — contains and removes the threat
> - **Hospital Bed (Firewater reservoir + pumps)** — supplies the life-saving fluid continuously

Just as a healthy body has **multiple redundant defence layers**, an oil terminal must have **3-layer fire protection**: **Detection → Notification → Suppression**, supported by **passive fire protection** (fireproofing, firewalls, blast walls) and **emergency response**.

---

## 1.3 Why Hydrocarbon Facilities Need Specialised Fire Protection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hydrocarbon fires are **Class B fires** with unique characteristics:
> - **Pool fires** — liquid surface burning (tank fires)
> - **Jet fires** — pressurized release ignited (flanges, pump seals)
> - **Vapour Cloud Fires / Flash Fires**
> - **Boilover** — water trapped below hot oil erupts violently (>100 °C)
> - **BLEVE** — Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (above auto-ignition)

Standard water jets are **ineffective** on hydrocarbon pool fires. The fire tetrahedron (Fuel + Heat + Oxygen + Chain Reaction) must be broken by **smothering** (foam blanket) and **cooling** (water spray).

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER use water jet directly on a burning hydrocarbon pool** — it causes the burning oil to splash, spreading the fire (the famous *"ice-cream scoop"* effect in basic fire-fighting textbooks).

---

## 1.4 Categorisation of Facilities

| Facility | Typical Inventory | Primary Fire Risk |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Refinery** | Crude, Naphtha, LPG, MS, HSD, FO | Process unit fires, atmospheric/vacuum distillation, hydrocracker, reformer |
| **Depot** | MS, HSD, SKO, FO in bulk storage tanks | Cone-roof tank full surface fires, rim seal fires |
| **Terminal** | Import/export, jetty, pipeline manifolds | Marine loading arm, manifold, ship-shore interface |

> **Cone-roof tank fires** dominate ~85% of depot incidents — hence **fixed foam chambers** on tank shells are mandatory under OISD-116.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Architecture of a Hydrocarbon Fire Protection System

The fire protection of any hydrocarbon facility is a **system-of-systems** with four parallel and redundant layers:

1. **Passive Fire Protection (PFP)** — fireproofing (vermiculite, intumescent coatings), firewalls, blast walls, fire-rated cables, fire doors, bund walls.
2. **Detection & Alarm** — flame detectors (IR/UV), heat detectors (linear heat cable), smoke detectors, gas detectors (catalytic bead / IR), manual call points (MCP), F&G PLC.
3. **Active Fire Suppression** — fixed foam systems, water spray/deluge, sprinklers, dry chemical, CO₂, water mist.
4. **Fire Water Network** — dedicated fire water reservoir (≥ 4 hr supply), fire water pumps (electric + diesel + jockey), ring-main hydrant network, monitors, hose reels.

---

## 2.2 Types of Fire Protection Systems (Class-by-Class)

### 2.2.1 Fixed Foam Pourer/Chamber System (Cone-Roof Tanks)
- Used on **fixed-roof atmospheric tanks** storing MS/HSD/SKO/FO.
- Foam is poured onto the fuel surface through **foam chambers** mounted on the tank shell above the maximum liquid level.
- Foam **expands, flows across the surface, seals the vapour, and suppresses the fire**.
- **Application rate:** **4 LPM/m²** (OISD-116) for hydrocarbon fuels; **6.5 LPM/m²** for polar solvents (alcohols, ketones).
- **Pour time:** **55 minutes (minimum)** — to outlast the burn-back time.
- **Discharge time:** **65 minutes** as per NFPA 11.

### 2.2.2 Sub-Surface Foam Injection (SSFI) System
- For **floating-roof tanks** with internal floating pans (full surface fires are rare but possible during maintenance or seal failure).
- Foam is injected through a **low-level foam generator** at the base of the tank, bubbles rise through the fuel and form a blanket on the surface.
- **Application rate:** **4 LPM/m²** of tank cross-sectional area.
- **Critical caveat:** SSFI must NOT be used on **water-miscible (polar) liquids**.

### 2.2.3 Water Spray / Deluge System
- **Water spray (NFPA 15)** — used to cool vessels, columns, heat exchangers, LPG bullets, structural steel exposed to fire.
- **Deluge system (NFPA 13)** — open nozzles, dry pipe, single valve (deluge valve) opens on detection signal; high water density; used for high-hazard process areas.
- **Application density:**
  - Vessel cooling: **10.2 LPM/m²** (0.25 gpm/ft²)
  - Structural steel: **10.2–20.4 LPM/m²**
  - LPG vessels: **≥ 10.2 LPM/m²** over entire wetted surface

### 2.2.4 Sprinkler System
- Used in **warehouses, lube oil godowns, pump houses, control rooms (false ceiling)**.
- Pipe is pressurised; heads open at **68 °C / 79 °C / 93 °C** (red/yellow/green bulbs).
- Designed per NFPA 13 hazard classification:
  - **Light Hazard (LH):** 4.1 LPM/m² over **maximum 140 m²**
  - **Ordinary Hazard Group 1 (OH-1):** 6.1 LPM/m² over **maximum 140 m²**
  - **Ordinary Hazard Group 2 (OH-2):** 8.1 LPM/m² over **maximum 140 m²**
  - **Extra Hazard:** 12.2 LPM/m²

### 2.2.5 Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) System
- **ABC powder (Mono-ammonium phosphate)** — for Class A, B, C fires.
- **BC powder (Sodium bicarbonate)** — for Class B, C.
- Used in **tank farms, loading gantries, LPG bottling, transformer yards**.
- **Discharge rate:** **3.6 kg/sec** for loading bays; **3 kg/m²** area-based.
- **Discharge time:** **30 seconds minimum** for local application.

### 2.2.6 CO₂ / Inert Gas System
- For **electrical rooms, control rooms, MCC, transformer rooms, cable vaults**.
- Design concentration: **34% minimum, 50% for un-energised spaces** (NFPA 12).
- Extinguishes by **displacement of oxygen** — hence forbidden in occupied spaces.

### 2.2.7 Portable Fire Extinguishers
- Positioned at **15 m intervals** along escape routes (IS 2190).
- Classification: **A, B, C, D, (K)** with **numerical rating** (e.g., 4A 80B C).
- For oil industry: **ABC powder (4–9 kg)** and **CO₂ (4.5–22.5 kg)** are universal.

---

## 2.3 Fire Water System — Hydraulic Design

The fire water system is the **circulatory system** of fire protection. Its hydraulic design follows the **OISD-STD-117 / NFPA 24 / NFPA 14** framework.

### 2.3.1 Fire Water Demand Formula

The **single largest credible fire scenario** is selected, then all simultaneous demands are summed.

$$
Q_{fire} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_{i, \text{cooling}} + Q_{i, \text{suppression}} + Q_{i, \text{hose streams}} + Q_{i, \text{monitors}}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{i, \text{cooling}}$ — water required to cool adjacent tanks / vessels
- $Q_{i, \text{suppression}}$ — foam / water spray demand
- $Q_{i, \text{hose streams}}$ — typically **190 m³/hr** per hose (3 × 63 mm hose streams)
- $Q_{i, \text{monitors}}$ — typically **60 m³/hr per monitor × 4 nos. = 240 m³/hr**

### 2.3.2 Tank Cooling Water Demand

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OISD-116 Cooling Rule:** Adjacent tanks (within **1.5 × diameter** of the burning tank, on both sides) must be cooled.

- **Vertical adjacent tank:** $Q_{\text{cool}} = 3.0 \, \text{LPM/m}^2$ of wetted shell area
- **Vertical burning tank:** $Q_{\text{cool}} = 3.0 \, \text{LPM/m}^2$ of wetted shell area (additional)
- **Duration:** **4 hours minimum**

> **Why cooling?** Hydrocarbon tank fires cause neighbouring tanks to heat up via radiation. Above **~95 °C**, the fuel vapour pressure rises rapidly → ignition. Water spray removes ~**2.5 MJ/kg** through evaporation.

### 2.3.3 Foam Compound & Solution Calculations

**Step 1 — Foam solution volume required:**

$$
V_s = A \times R \times t_p
$$

Where:
- $V_s$ = foam solution volume (L)
- $A$ = burning surface area (m²)
- $R$ = application rate (LPM/m²) — typically **4**
- $t_p$ = pour time (min) — typically **55 min**

**Step 2 — Foam compound (concentrate) volume:**

$$
V_c = \frac{V_s}{C_p}
$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = foam concentrate volume (L)
- $C_p$ = proportioning ratio — typically **3%** (AFFF) or **6%**

**Step 3 — Foam expansion ratio:**

$$
E = \frac{V_f}{V_s}
$$

Where:
- $V_f$ = foam volume (L)
- $V_s$ = foam solution volume (L)
- Low expansion foam: $E = 5 \text{ to } 20$ (most common — Class B)
- Medium expansion foam: $E = 20 \text{ to } 100$
- High expansion foam: $E = 100 \text{ to } 1000$ (Class A, e.g., LNG)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|-----------|---------|------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Foam solution volume | $V_s = A \cdot R \cdot t_p$ | L | $A$ = surface area, $R$ = 4 LPM/m², $t_p$ = 55 min |
| 2 | Foam concentrate volume | $V_c = V_s / C_p$ | L | $C_p = 3\%$ (AFFF) or $6\%$ (AFFF/FP) |
| 3 | Foam finished volume | $V_f = V_s \cdot E$ | L | $E = 5$–$20$ (low expansion) |
| 4 | Tank cooling water | $Q_{cool} = 3.0 \cdot A_{wetted}$ | LPM | Duration ≥ 4 hr |
| 5 | Fire water pump head | $H = h_s + h_f + h_v$ | m | Static + friction + velocity head |
| 6 | Friction loss (Hazen-Williams) | $h_f = 10.67 \cdot \frac{L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}$ | m | SI: $Q$ in m³/s, $d$ in m |
| 7 | Velocity in pipe | $v = Q/A = 4Q/(\pi d^2)$ | m/s | Max **3.0 m/s** in mains, **6 m/s** in hydrant laterals |
| 8 | Monitor flow | $Q_m = 30 \text{ to } 60$ | m³/hr | 2.8 bar nozzle pressure |
| 9 | Hydrant flow | $Q_h = 30$ | m³/hr | 3.5 bar residual pressure |
| 10 | Fire water duration | $T = 4 \text{ to } 6$ | hours | OISD minimum 4 hr; large refinery 6 hr |
| 11 | Foam concentrate storage | $\text{100\% reserve} + \text{working}$ | L | Two containers — one in use, one reserve |
| 12 | AFFF 3% reserve | $(V_c) + (V_c)$ | L | 200% of calculated demand (working + 100% reserve) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Board Examiner's Note):** Most students forget the **100% reserve** for foam compound. OISD mandates **2 × calculated demand** to be stored (1 in use + 1 standby) — common 2-mark deduction.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Process Unit Protection:** A naphtha hydrotreater unit has 30+ vessels; water spray deluge protects columns, fired heaters (H-101), and LPG bullets.
- **Tank Farm (Depot):** A 50,000 m² depot has 20 cone-roof tanks; each may have **Type II foam chamber** (vapour area foam) and adjacent tanks cooled via **riser-mounted water sprays**.
- **Jetty/Terminal:** Marine loading arms are protected by **dry chemical skids** + **water monitors** + **foam monitors** (typically 6 each, in pairs).
- **Pipeline Manifold:** **Linear heat sensing cable (LHS cable)** triggers a deluge valve that floods the manifold area within **30 seconds** of detection.

> This is not just academic — failure of any one of these layers has caused historical disasters like **Buncefield (UK, 2005)**, **Jaipur (India, 2009)**, **Skikda (Algeria, 2004)** — all linked to inadequate foam/water systems.

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Fixed Foam System Design for Cone-Roof Tank

> **Problem (KTU Module-3 typical):**
> A **cone-roof storage tank** has:
> - Diameter $D = 20$ m
> - Height $H = 10$ m
> - Stores **motor spirit (MS)**
>
> Calculate:
> (a) Foam solution volume required
> (b) Foam concentrate volume required
> (c) Foam finished volume (low expansion, $E = 8$)
> (d) Foam concentrate storage (with 100% reserve)

### Step 1 — Burning Surface Area

The cone-roof tank full surface fire has surface area equal to the tank **cross-sectional (footprint)** area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Substituting $D = 20 \text{ m}$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot (20)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 400}{4} = 100\pi
$$

$$
A = 314.159 \text{ m}^2
$$

> The tank height (10 m) is **irrelevant** for surface area — only diameter matters because the **foam blanket floats on top** of the liquid.

### Step 2 — Foam Solution Volume

Using OISD application rate $R = 4 \text{ LPM/m}^2$ and pour time $t_p = 55$ min:

$$
V_s = A \cdot R \cdot t_p
$$

$$
V_s = 314.159 \cdot 4 \cdot 55
$$

Intermediate calculation:

$$
4 \cdot 55 = 220
$$

$$
V_s = 314.159 \cdot 220
$$

$$
V_s = 69{,}115 \text{ L} = 69.115 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Foam Concentrate Volume (3% AFFF)

$$
V_c = \frac{V_s}{C_p} = \frac{V_s}{0.03}
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{69{,}115}{0.03}
$$

$$
V_c = 2{,}303{,}833 \text{ L} \approx 2{,}304 \text{ m}^3
$$

> For 6% AFFF, simply divide by 0.06 → would double the volume. Most modern systems use **3% AFFF**.

### Step 4 — Foam Finished Volume (Expansion)

$$
V_f = V_s \cdot E = 69{,}115 \cdot 8 = 552{,}920 \text{ L} \approx 553 \text{ m}^3
$$

This means **1 L of AFFF concentrate + water → 240 L of finished foam** (after expansion), and **240 L of foam → covers 1 m² × 12 mm thick** ≈ enough to smother a pool fire.

### Step 5 — Storage with 100% Reserve

$$
V_{c,\text{storage}} = V_c + 1.0 \cdot V_c = 2 \cdot V_c
$$

$$
V_{c,\text{storage}} = 2 \cdot 2{,}304 = 4{,}608 \text{ m}^3 = 4.608 \text{ ML}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **4,608 m³** of foam compound must be stored in **two equal tanks** (one in operation + one standby), with **automatic level switches** that signal low level to control room.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Fire Water Pump Sizing

> **Problem:** A petroleum depot has the following fire scenario:
> - **1 tank on fire** (20 m diameter × 10 m)
> - **2 adjacent tanks** to be cooled (each 20 m dia × 10 m)
> - **4 monitors** operating at 60 m³/hr each
> - **3 hose streams** at 190 m³/hr total (3 × 63 mm @ 3.5 bar)
> - **Tank on fire itself cooled**: 3.0 LPM/m² of wetted shell area
>
> Calculate **(a)** fire water pump flow rate (LPM) and **(b)** tank cooling duration check.

### Step 1 — Wetted Shell Area

For a tank with **liquid level ≈ 0.9 H**, wetted shell area:

$$
A_{\text{wetted}} = \pi D \cdot (0.9 H)
$$

For 20 m dia × 10 m tank:

$$
A_{\text{wetted}} = \pi \cdot 20 \cdot 9 = 180\pi = 565.49 \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Cooling Water Demand (1 burning tank + 2 adjacent)

Cooling water rate per tank (vertical adjacent cooling = 3.0 LPM/m²):

$$
Q_{\text{cool, single}} = 3.0 \cdot 565.49 = 1{,}696.46 \text{ LPM}
$$

For **3 tanks** (1 burning + 2 adjacent):

$$
Q_{\text{cool, total}} = 3 \cdot 1{,}696.46 = 5{,}089.4 \text{ LPM}
$$

### Step 3 — Foam Solution Water Demand

From Example 1, foam solution flow rate:

$$
Q_{\text{foam}} = A \cdot R = 314.159 \cdot 4 = 1{,}256.6 \text{ LPM}
$$

### Step 4 — Monitor Flow

$$
Q_{\text{monitor}} = 4 \cdot 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = 240 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

Convert to LPM:

$$
Q_{\text{monitor}} = \frac{240 \cdot 1000}{60} = 4{,}000 \text{ LPM}
$$

### Step 5 — Hose Stream Flow

$$
Q_{\text{hose}} = 190 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = \frac{190{,}000}{60} = 3{,}166.7 \text{ LPM}
$$

### Step 6 — Total Fire Water Demand

$$
Q_{\text{fire}} = Q_{\text{cool}} + Q_{\text{foam}} + Q_{\text{monitor}} + Q_{\text{hose}}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{fire}} = 5{,}089.4 + 1{,}256.6 + 4{,}000 + 3{,}166.7
$$

Intermediate sum:

$$
5{,}089.4 + 1{,}256.6 = 6{,}346
$$

$$
4{,}000 + 3{,}166.7 = 7{,}166.7
$$

$$
Q_{\text{fire}} = 6{,}346 + 7{,}166.7 = 13{,}512.7 \text{ LPM}
$$

Convert to m³/hr:

$$
Q_{\text{fire}} = 13{,}512.7 \cdot 60 / 1000 = 810.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

### Step 7 — Pump Head (using Hazen-Williams)

Assume **ring main** of ductile iron, $C = 120$, length $L = 600$ m, diameter $d = 250$ mm = 0.25 m, flow $Q = 13{,}512.7 \text{ LPM} = 0.2252 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$
h_f = 10.67 \cdot \frac{L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot d^{4.87}}
$$

$$
h_f = 10.67 \cdot \frac{600 \cdot (0.2252)^{1.852}}{(120)^{1.852} \cdot (0.25)^{4.87}}
$$

Numerical:

$$
(0.2252)^{1.852} \approx 0.0583
$$

$$
(120)^{1.852} \approx 7{,}447
$$

$$
(0.25)^{4.87} \approx 0.000233
$$

$$
h_f = 10.67 \cdot \frac{600 \cdot 0.0583}{7{,}447 \cdot 0.000233} = 10.67 \cdot \frac{34.98}{1.735} \approx 10.67 \cdot 20.16
$$

$$
h_f \approx 215.1 \text{ m}
$$

Add static head $h_s = 35$ m + velocity head $h_v = 4$ m:

$$
H = 35 + 215.1 + 4 = 254.1 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Selected Pump:** Centrifugal end-suction, **$Q = 455 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ @ 110 m head** × **3 nos.** (1 electric + 1 diesel + 1 standby electric) — typical large-depot configuration.
> **Jockey pump:** $Q = 30 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ @ 130 m head to maintain line pressure 8–10 bar.

### Step 8 — Fire Water Storage Duration Check

Required volume for **4 hr** duration:

$$
V_{\text{storage}} = Q_{\text{fire}} \cdot T = 810.8 \cdot 4 = 3{,}243.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

Recommended **fire water reservoir**: **3,500 m³** (with 10% margin).

---

| DCP (local) | Loading arm | 3.6 kg/sec | 30 sec | Manual |

| Sprinkler (warehouse) | Lube oil store | 8.1 LPM/m² | 60 min | Heat bulb 68 °C |

```mermaid
    class fuel fuel
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Type II foam chamber** sits on the tank shell above the high liquid level. Foam solution enters the generator, expands into finished foam, enters the chamber, deflects off the plate, and gently lands on the burning fuel surface — **without splashing the burning oil**.
