# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
*List any **six** essential fire protection facilities required in a petroleum oil depot as per OISD-STD-116.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 6 points × 0.5 each):**

1. **Bund wall / dyke** around tank clusters to contain spill.
2. **Fixed foam pourer / chamber system** on each cone-roof tank.
3. **Fire water ring main** with hydrants and monitors.
4. **Water spray / deluge system** on process vessels and LPG bullets.
5. **Dry chemical powder (DCP)** skids at loading gantries.
6. **Fire detection & alarm system** (F&G detectors + manual call points + FAP).
7. *(Bonus)* Portable extinguishers, fire-resistant PPE, breathing apparatus.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between **Low Expansion Foam** and **High Expansion Foam** with one engineering application each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Low Expansion Foam | High Expansion Foam |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Expansion ratio $E$ | 5 – 20 | 100 – 1000 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 60 – 200 | 0.5 – 5 |
| Application | **Class B pool fires** (MS/HSD tank fires) | **Class A fires** (LNG sub-station, warehouses, mines) |
| Application rate | 4 LPM/m² | Variable (1–5 m³/min/m³ of room vol.) |
| Mechanism | Smothers vapour at liquid surface | Floods 3-D volume, displaces O₂ |

**Application example:** Low expansion — `Cone-roof MS tank fire suppression.`  High expansion — `LNG compressor hall total flooding.`

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Compulsory]**

A petroleum refinery has the following fire protection scenario:

- One **cone-roof tank** of diameter **25 m**, height **12 m** storing **motor spirit (MS)** is on full surface fire.
- **Two adjacent tanks** (each 25 m × 12 m) are within 1.5 × D and need cooling.
- **Four water monitors** of capacity 60 m³/hr each are in operation.
- Three hose streams of 63 mm diameter @ 3.5 bar are pressurised.

Using **OISD-116** standards:

**(a)** Calculate the **foam concentrate volume required** (3% AFFF, 55 min pour time, 4 LPM/m²). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **total fire water demand** (LPM) and **fire water reservoir capacity** for 4-hour duration. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Tank Surface Area (1 mark)**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (25)^2}{4} = \frac{625\pi}{4} = 490.87 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Foam Solution Volume (2 marks)**

$$
V_s = A \cdot R \cdot t_p = 490.87 \cdot 4 \cdot 55
$$

$$
V_s = 490.87 \cdot 220 = 107{,}991 \text{ L} \approx 108 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Foam Concentrate Volume (3% AFFF) (2 marks)**

$$
V_c = \frac{V_s}{C_p} = \frac{107{,}991}{0.03} = 3{,}599{,}700 \text{ L} = 3{,}600 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Storage with 100% Reserve (2 marks)**

$$
V_{c,\text{storage}} = 2 \cdot V_c = 2 \cdot 3{,}600 = 7{,}200 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Foam solution = **108 m³**
- Foam concentrate = **3,600 m³**
- Storage (with reserve) = **7,200 m³** in two tanks of 3,600 m³ each.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Wetted Shell Area (1 mark)**

$$
A_{\text{wetted}} = \pi D \cdot (0.9 H) = \pi \cdot 25 \cdot (0.9 \cdot 12) = \pi \cdot 25 \cdot 10.8 = 848.23 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Cooling Water (3 tanks × 3 LPM/m²) (2 marks)**

$$
Q_{\text{cool}} = 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 848.23 = 7{,}634 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 3 — Foam Solution Demand (1 mark)**

$$
Q_{\text{foam}} = 490.87 \cdot 4 = 1{,}963.5 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Monitors + Hose Streams (2 marks)**

$$
Q_{\text{monitor}} = 4 \cdot 60 \cdot 1000 / 60 = 4{,}000 \text{ LPM}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{hose}} = 190 \cdot 1000 / 60 = 3{,}166.7 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Demand (1 mark)**

$$
Q_{\text{fire}} = 7{,}634 + 1{,}963.5 + 4{,}000 + 3{,}166.7 = 16{,}764.2 \text{ LPM}
$$

**Step 6 — Reservoir Capacity (4 hr) (1 mark)**

$$
V_{\text{storage}} = Q_{\text{fire}} \cdot T / 1000 = 16{,}764.2 \cdot 240 / 1000 = 4{,}023 \text{ m}^3
$$

→ Recommended **4,200 m³ reservoir**.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Paper, Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **fire protection system for an oil refinery process unit** with neat block diagrams. Cover at least **five distinct protection systems** with their design criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **selection, sizing, and layout of fire water pumps** for a hydrocarbon storage terminal as per OISD-117 / NFPA 20. Include a calculation example for a fire water pump. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A refinery process unit (e.g., **Naphtha Hydrotreater** or **Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation Column**) requires:

| # | System | Location | Design Rate | Duration | Trigger |
|---|--------|----------|-------------|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Water spray deluge** | Distillation column shell | 10.2 LPM/m² of wetted area | 30 min | Heat detector + manual |
| 2 | **Water spray on fired heater** | Heater tube & coil | 10.2 LPM/m² | 30 min | LHS cable + flame detector |
| 3 | **DCP system** | Pump manifold area | 3 kg/m² area or 3.6 kg/sec | 30 sec | Manual + Detector |
| 4 | **Foam pourer on slop tank** | Slop oil tank (cone-roof) | 4 LPM/m² | 55 min | Heat detector |
| 5 | **Fire water monitors (manual)** | Unit boundary (every 30 m) | 60 m³/hr each | Continuous | Manual |
| 6 | **CO₂ flooding** | Analyzer room / MCC room | 34% concentration | Hold | Smoke detector |
| 7 | **Hydrant network** | 30 m spacing | 30 m³/hr @ 3.5 bar | 4 hr | Manual |

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.1)** — passive layer + detection → F&G PLC → actuation (deluge valves, foam chambers, DCP) → suppression → cooling.

**Design Justifications:**
- Columns can fail by **pressure rupture** above 12 bar internal — water spray removes heat via evaporation (~2.5 MJ/kg).
- DCP interrupts the **chemical chain reaction** of pool fire; fast knockdown (<10 sec).
- **Foam chamber** on slop tank prevents escalation to bund fire.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Fire water pump selection follows NFPA 20 / OISD-117:**

1. **Jockey Pump (Pressure Maintenance):**
   - Capacity: 3–5% of main pump flow OR **30 m³/hr**
   - Head: **130% of main pump rated head**
   - Maintains ring main pressure at 8–10 bar.

2. **Electric Main Pump:**
   - Capacity: 100% of calculated demand (or as per OISD, 150% of largest single user)
   - Head: 110 m typical (depot)
   - Power: 415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz; DOL starting.

3. **Diesel Standby Pump:**
   - **Mandatory** if supply is from a single grid (≥ 95% reliability required).
   - Independent fuel tank for **6 hr continuous operation**.
   - Battery auto-start within **30 sec** of electric pump failure signal.

4. **Pump House Layout (refer to SECTION 4.4):**
   - Separate building, fire-rated construction.
   - Pump house located **above flood level**, with **redundant suction from reservoir**.

**Sample Calculation:**

> Required fire water flow = **810 m³/hr**, head = **110 m**

→ Select **Kirloskar / Grundfos / Sulzer** make, **end-suction centrifugal**, **450 mm impeller**, **6-pole 1450 rpm motor** rated **110 kW**.

→ Pump curve must be verified at **110% rated flow** (892 m³/hr @ 90 m) — i.e., pump must deliver at least 80% of rated head at 110% flow to be non-overloading.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Foam pour time** — students write **30 min** instead of **55 min**. OISD mandates **55 min** (NFPA = 65 min). [−1 mark]
> 2. **Forget 100% foam compound reserve.** OISD-116 says store 200% of calculated demand. [−2 marks]
> 3. **Confusing LPM and m³/hr.** 1 m³/hr = 16.67 LPM. Always show conversion. [−1 mark]
> 4. **Using tank height H for surface area** in cone-roof tank — incorrect. Only diameter D matters because foam is poured on top. [−2 marks]
> 5. **Missing tank cooling for adjacent tanks** — must include **all tanks within 1.5 × D** on both sides. [−3 marks]
> 6. **Wrong AFFF percentage** — 3% means 3 parts concentrate in 100 parts foam solution, NOT 3% of total. Use $V_c = V_s / 0.03$ not $V_c = 0.03 V_s$. [−2 marks]
> 7. **Writing "diesel pump is optional"** — OISD says diesel pump is **mandatory** if grid reliability < 95%. [−1 mark]

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fire Protection in Hydrocarbon Industry**

- **Three Cs of Hydrocarbon Fire Protection:** **Containment** (bund), **Cooling** (water spray), **Conflagration Prevention** (foam blanket).
- **OISD-STD-114** → Refineries; **OISD-STD-116** → Depots/Terminals; **OISD-STD-117** → Fire Fighting Equipment.
- **Cone-roof tank fixed foam**: Type II chamber, 4 LPM/m², 55 min pour time, 3% AFFF.
- **SSFI**: For floating-roof tanks only; **never** for water-miscible liquids.
- **Water spray**: 10.2 LPM/m² for vessel/column cooling.
- **Deluge**: Open nozzle, single deluge valve, fast discharge (30 sec activation).
- **Sprinkler hazard class**: LH (4.1) → OH-1 (6.1) → OH-2 (8.1) → EH (12.2) LPM/m².
- **DCP**: 3.6 kg/sec (local) OR 3 kg/m² (area), 30 sec discharge.
- **CO₂**: 34% concentration for energised spaces; never in occupied rooms.
- **Hydrant** flow: **30 m³/hr @ 3.5 bar**; spacing **30 m** in process areas, **45 m** in tank farms.
- **Water monitor**: **60 m³/hr**; range ~60 m; mounted at **15–18 m height**.
- **Fire water pumps**: **3 nos.** (1 electric + 1 diesel + 1 standby electric + 1 jockey); reservoir ≥ **4 hr supply**; auto-start within 30 sec.
- **Foam compound storage**: **2 × calculated demand** (one in use + one reserve).
- **Adjacent tank cooling distance**: within **1.5 × D** (both sides).
- **Fire water ring main**: ductile iron, **DN250 typical**, **velocity ≤ 3 m/s** in mains.
- **Detection**: Flame (IR/UV), Heat (LHS cable), Smoke, Gas — all wired to **F&G PLC** with SIL-2 ESD logic.
- **Drainage**: **1% slope** towards oil-water separator; no dead pockets.
- **Ignition sources** must be eliminated in **hazardous area classification** zones: Zone 0, 1, 2 (gas) and Zone 20, 21, 22 (dust).
- **First-aid fire fighting**: ABC powder (4–9 kg) at every 15 m escape interval (IS 2190).
- **Buncefield 2005**: Largest European petroleum depot fire — caused by **inadequate foam system** (under-design) and **unsealed bund overfill** — proves the formula matters.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — Fire Protection Facilities in Oil Refineries, Depots and Terminals**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation of Petroleum and Petrochemical Products (Safety Considerations, Statutory Considerations)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transportation of Petroleum and Petrochemical Products (Safety Considerations, Statutory Considerations)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Transportation of Petroleum and Petrochemical Products** refers to the systematic movement of crude oil, refined petroleum fractions (LPG, gasoline, diesel, kerosene, aviation turbine fuel, naphtha), lubricating oils, and petrochemical feedstock (ethylene, propylene, aromatics) from production/storage locations to processing plants, distribution terminals, and end-users, carried out through five principal modes:

1. **Pipelines (Cross-country/Trunk & Gathering/Distribution)**
2. **Road Tankers (Tank Trucks / Bulk Liquid Carriers)**
3. **Rail Wagons (Tank Wagons)**
4. **Marine Tankers (Crude/Product carriers, Chemical tankers, Gas carriers)**
5. **Aerial (rare; mostly for specialty chemicals & emergencies)**

Each mode operates under a defined **statutory and regulatory framework** governed by Indian statutes (Petroleum Act 1934, Petroleum Rules 2002, Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Indian Railways Act 1989, Explosives Act 1884, Factories Act 1948, Environment Protection Act 1986) and international codes (ADR, IMDG, RID, ICAO-TI, SOLAS, MARPOL).

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Considerations** address the technical, operational, and engineering controls (vessel integrity, pressure/temperature control, static electricity dissipation, fire/explosion prevention, emergency shutdown) that prevent loss of containment during transit.
>
> **Statutory Considerations** address the legal, regulatory, and documentary requirements (licenses, classifications, marking, placarding, trained personnel, environmental compliance) that govern the lawful movement of hazardous cargo.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Circulatory System of the Hydrocarbon Industry"

Think of the hydrocarbon industry as a **living organism**:
- The **refinery** is the **heart** — it pumps out finished products.
- **Pipelines** are the **arteries** (continuous, high-volume, buried).
- **Road tankers** are the **ambulances / delivery vans** (flexible, smaller batches, last-mile).
- **Rail wagons** are the **bulk cargo trains** (medium distance, large volumes).
- **Marine tankers** are the **cargo ships** (intercontinental, massive volumes).
- **Safety systems** are the **immune system** — they detect, contain, and neutralize threats.
- **Statutes/regulations** are the **laws of the body** — non-negotiable rules that keep the organism from poisoning itself or its environment.

Just as a clot in an artery causes a stroke, a **leak, fire, or explosion during transportation** can cause catastrophic loss of life, property, and environmental damage. Hence, the safety and statutory framework is as critical as the product itself.

### 1.3 Why Hydrocarbon Transport Demands Special Treatment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical & Chemical Properties Driving Safety Design:**
> - **Low Flash Point** (Gasoline: **–43 °C**, Naphtha: **< 0 °C**) → always in flammable range at ambient.
> - **High Vapour Pressure** → vapour space in any container is always within explosive limits (**LEL 1.4 %, UEL 7.6 %** for gasoline).
> - **Electrostatic Charge Accumulation** during flow/filtering/sloshing → ignition source.
> - **Toxicity** (Benzene **IARC Group 1 carcinogen**, H₂S **IDLH 100 ppm**).
> - **Reactivity** with incompatible materials.
> - **Buoyancy** (LPG vapour is heavier than air; pools in low areas).

### 1.4 Classification of Products by Hazard

| Class | Product Examples | Primary Hazard | UN Number Series |
|-------|------------------|----------------|------------------|
| Class 3 | Gasoline, Diesel, Kerosene, Naphtha | Flammable Liquid | UN 1202, 1223, 1863 |
| Class 2.1 | LPG, LNG, Methane, Propane | Flammable Gas | UN 1971, 1972 |
| Class 2.3 | Ammonia, Chlorine | Toxic Gas | UN 1005, 1017 |
| Class 5.1 | Hydrogen Peroxide | Oxidizer | UN 2014, 2015 |
| Class 8 | Sulphuric Acid, Caustic Soda | Corrosive | UN 1824, 1823 |
| Class 9 | Misc. Dangerous (Asphalt hot) | Elevated Temp | UN 3257 |

> [!NOTE]
> Every shipment must carry a **UN Number**, **Proper Shipping Name**, **Hazard Class**, and **Packing Group** as mandated by **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods (UN RTDG)** — adopted into Indian law via the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR)** and **Indian Railways Conference Association (IRCA)** rules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mode-Wise Deep Dive

#### A. Pipeline Transportation

Pipelines are the **safest (lowest incidents per tonne-km)** but **highest capital** mode. They are categorized as:

- **Trunk/Main pipelines**: 16"–48" diameter, cross-country, multi-product (batched).
- **Gathering pipelines**: Field to processing plant (4"–16").
- **Distribution pipelines**: 2"–8", urban, single product (e.g., LPG to bottling plant).

**Engineering & Safety Design Basis:**

1. **Material Selection**: API 5L Grade X42, X46, X52, X60, X65, X70 (higher for high-pressure gas lines). Coatings: 3LPE (3-Layer Polyethylene), FBE (Fusion Bonded Epoxy) for external corrosion protection.
2. **Design Code**: **ASME B31.4** (Liquid pipelines), **ASME B31.8** (Gas pipelines).
3. **Pressure Control**: Maximum Allowable Operating Pressure (**MAOP**) = (2 × t × σ × E × F × T) / D (Hoop stress equation).
4. **SCADA-based Leak Detection**: Mass balance, pressure wave, acoustic, fibre-optic DTS/DAS.

**Operational Hazards & Controls:**

| Hazard | Cause | Control |
|--------|-------|---------|
| Third-party damage | Excavation near buried line | ROW fencing, marker posts, "Call Before You Dig", One-Call system |
| Internal corrosion | H₂O, CO₂, H₂S, MIC | Chemical inhibition, pigging, CP system |
| External corrosion | Soil moisture | Cathodic protection (sacrificial anode / impressed current) |
| Over-pressure | Pump trip/valve closure | PSV, surge analysis, SCADA shut-in |
| Leak | Weld defect, external damage | Leak detection (≥ 1 % flow), periodic ILI pigging |
| Static charge | High velocity flow | Velocity limit **≤ 7 m/s** for hydrocarbons; bonding & grounding |

#### B. Road Tanker Transportation

- Governed by **Motor Vehicles Act 1988**, **CMVR 1989**, **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (Tanker Safety)**, **PESO**, and **OISD-159** (Road Tanker Safety).
- Capacity: **10 KL, 12 KL, 16 KL, 20 KL, 24 KL** (general), up to **45 KL** for bitumen.
- Tank shell: Mild steel / SS 304 / aluminium; **elliptical cross-section** to reduce surge.

**Critical Safety Fittings:**

1. **Pressure/Vacuum Relief Valve (PVRV)** — set pressure **+20 to +35 kPag** for gasoline; flame arrestor integrated.
2. **Emergency Shut-off Valve (ESV)** — at top & bottom; thermal fuse at **≥ 70 °C** that melts and closes valve.
3. **Bottom Loading Adaptor** (API 1004 / EN 13079) — for vapor-recovery (Stage-I vapour recovery).
4. **Manhole Cover** with bolted flange and O-ring seal.
5. **Static Discharge Reel / Bonding Cable** — resistance **≤ 10 Ω** (some standards: **≤ 25 Ω**).
6. **Anti-roll Baffles** inside compartments.
7. **Rear Under-run Protection** (RUPD).
8. **Reflective Conspicuity Tape** (HSE-2 grade, 45° chevrons).

#### C. Rail Wagon Transportation

- Governed by **Indian Railways Act 1989**, **RID (COTIF-R)** for international, **IRCA Tank Wagon Rules**, **OISD-156**.
- **BTFL (Bogie Tank Flask Low)** — 42–58 t for petroleum, **BTFG** for gases.
- **Tank code** denotes design pressure, materials, fittings: e.g., **22S** = 2.2 bar test pressure, mild steel, bottom-discharge with three-way cock.

**Hazards Specific to Rail:**

- **Jack-knifing / derailment** due to over-speed at curves.
- **Crude Oil / LPG tankers** on passenger lines = catastrophic BLEVE risk.
- **Shunting damage** — only tank wagons with chevron marking, JPP (Joint Plan of Protection) escort for toxic/Class 2.3.

#### D. Marine Transportation

- Governed by **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea), **MARPOL 73/78** (oil spill prevention), **IMDG Code** (International Maritime Dangerous Goods), **ISGOTT** (International Safety Guide for Oil Tankers & Terminals), **STCW** (crew certification), **OCIMF MEG4** (Mooring Equipment Guidelines).
- Vessel types: **VLCC** (Very Large Crude Carrier, 2–3 lakh DWT), **Suezmax**, **Aframax**, **MR Product Tanker**, **Chemical Tanker (IMO Type 1/2/3)**, **LNG/LPG Carrier (Moss/Spherical, Membrane, IMO Type C)**.

**Key Marine Hazards:**

1. **Fire in pump room** — most common cause of total loss.
2. **Sloshing-induced structural failure** (free-surface effect).
3. **Collision/Allision** in congested waterways.
4. **Oil spill** — double-hull mandatory since **MARPOL Annex I, Reg 19** (post *Erika* & *Prestige*).
5. **Inert gas system (IGS)** — keeps O₂ in cargo tank **< 8 % by volume** to prevent ignition.
6. **Static electricity during tank washing** — controlled wash cycle; max wash water velocity.

#### E. Air Transportation

- Governed by **ICAO-TI** (Technical Instructions) and **IATA DGR** (Dangerous Goods Regulations).
- **Forbidden on passenger aircraft**: Class 2.1 gases except small aerosols, Class 6.1 toxic.
- Cargo-only freighters carry limited quantities under **Operator Variations**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression / Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Hoop stress in pipeline | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | Pipe wall thickness check |
| 2 | Longitudinal stress | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | Axial stress |
| 3 | Barlow's wall thickness | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F}$ | Minimum wall thickness |
| 4 | Hazen-Williams head loss | $h_f = 10.67 \cdot \dfrac{L \cdot Q^{1.852}}{C^{1.852} \cdot D^{4.87}}$ | Water/weak slurry, C ≈ 120–140 for steel |
| 5 | Darcy-Weisbach | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | General fluid flow |
| 6 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime |
| 7 | Static charge generation (Klinkenberg) | $I = K \cdot v^{1.7} \cdot d^{0.5}$ | Current I (pA) in flowing hydrocarbon |
| 8 | Vapour cloud mass | $m_{vap} = V_{pool} \cdot \rho_{liq} \cdot \dfrac{T_{amb}}{P_{amb}} \cdot \dfrac{P_{sat}}{T_{liq}} \cdot MW / R$ | TNT-equivalent explosion |
| 9 | TNT-equivalent mass | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m_{vap} \cdot \Delta H_c}{Q_{TNT}}$ | Explosion modelling |
| 10 | BLEVE overpressure | $P_{BLEVE} = \dfrac{n \cdot R \cdot T}{V}$ | Pressure vessel rupture |
| 11 | Tanker fill ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{filled}}{V_{total}} \leq 0.95$ | Volume allowance for thermal expansion |
| 12 | Thermal expansion volume | $\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ | Used to set fill limit |
| 13 | Centrifugal pump NPSH | $NPSH_a = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{v}}{\rho g} \pm h_s$ | Avoid cavitation |
| 14 | Critical flow velocity (static) | $v_c \leq \dfrac{\sqrt{0.7}}{\sqrt{\rho}}$ m/s | For light hydrocarbons, ≈ **7 m/s** |
| 15 | Bonding resistance | $R \leq 10\ \Omega$ | Earthing of road tanker |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always present **units** explicitly in KTU numerical answers. Examiner deducts marks for missing units even if numerical value is correct.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **PSV / PRV sizing** uses API 520/521 with critical (choked) flow formulae.
- **Quantitative Risk Analysis (QRA)** for pipeline routes uses these equations to estimate **individual risk** and **societal risk** contours, guiding **buffer zone** (right-of-user-way) determination.
- **Oil spill response** planning uses the **Adios2 / GNOME** models derived from fluid mechanics.
- **Insurance premium** for hydrocarbon transporters is directly tied to compliance with these formulae-driven safety standards (OISD-117, OISD-118, OISD-141, OISD-144).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pipeline Wall Thickness (Barlow's Equation)

The pipeline is treated as a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel. The hoop stress at the inner wall must not exceed the allowable stress.

**Step 1**: Consider a longitudinal cut through the pipe of length **L** and diameter **D**. The internal pressure **P** pushes outward on the projected area $A = D \cdot L$.

**Step 2**: This force is resisted by the metal in two cut sections, each of area $t \cdot L$, total resisting area $2 t L$.

$$
\sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t \cdot L = P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

**Step 3**: Simplify to get the basic hoop stress equation.

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
$$

**Step 4**: Introduce the design factor **F** (typically 0.72 for liquid lines, 0.5–0.8 for gas lines per ASME B31.8) and the joint efficiency **E** (1.0 for seamless pipe, 0.85–1.0 for welded).

$$
\sigma_{allow} = S \cdot E \cdot F
$$

**Step 5**: Rearrange for **t**:

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot F}
$$

**Step 6**: Add a corrosion allowance **c** (typically **1.5 mm to 3 mm**) and a mill tolerance (12.5 % for seamless).

$$
t_{nom} = \dfrac{t + c}{1 - \dfrac{mill\_tolerance}{100}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a typical cross-country crude oil trunk line: **D = 36 inch = 914.4 mm**, **P = 70 bar = 7 MPa**, **S = 250 MPa (X52)**, **E = 1.0**, **F = 0.72**.
> Calculate: $t = \dfrac{7 \times 914.4}{2 \times 250 \times 1.0 \times 0.72} = \mathbf{17.78\ mm}$ → nominal **19.1 mm with corrosion allowance.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Static Charge Current (Klinkenberg / Bustin Model)

During turbulent flow of a low-conductivity hydrocarbon through a pipe or filter, charge separates and a streaming current **I** is generated.

**Step 1**: Empirical relation (British Standard 5958):

$$
I = K \cdot v^{1.7} \cdot d^{0.5}
$$

where:
- $I$ = streaming current in **pA (pico-Amperes)**
- $v$ = linear velocity (m/s)
- $d$ = pipe diameter (m)
- $K$ = empirical constant (≈ **1500** for aviation fuel in filters)

**Step 2**: For gasoline ($K \approx 850$), at **v = 5 m/s**, **d = 0.1 m**:

$$
I = 850 \times (5)^{1.7} \times (0.1)^{0.5}
$$

**Step 3**: Evaluate powers.

$$
5^{1.7} = 5^{1} \cdot 5^{0.7} \approx 5 \times 3.09 = 15.45
$$

$$
0.1^{0.5} = 0.3162
$$

**Step 4**: Multiply.

$$
I = 850 \times 15.45 \times 0.3162 \approx 4153\ \text{pA} \approx 4.15\ \text{nA}
$$

**Step 5**: Energy stored in the isolated vessel (capacitance $C \approx 100\ \text{pF}$).

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2
$$

If leakage resistance is high, voltage can rise to **> 10 kV** — well above the **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) of gasoline = 0.25 mJ**. Hence **bonding, grounding, and residence time** are mandatory.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tank-Truck Fill Ratio (Thermal Expansion Safety)

A petroleum road tanker is filled to a volume such that, at the **maximum expected ambient temperature**, the liquid does not overflow.

**Step 1**: Volumetric thermal expansion coefficient $\beta$.

$$
\beta = \dfrac{1}{V} \dfrac{dV}{dT}
$$

Typical values (per °C):
- Gasoline: $\beta \approx 0.00095$
- Diesel: $\beta \approx 0.00084$
- LPG (in liquid): $\beta \approx 0.0018$

**Step 2**: Expanded volume at temperature $T_{max}$.

$$
V_{max} = V_0 \cdot (1 + \beta \cdot \Delta T)
$$

**Step 3**: For an unheated compartment filled at $T_1 = 30\ \text{°C}$ and travelling to $T_{max} = 50\ \text{°C}$:

$$
\Delta V / V_0 = 0.00095 \times 20 = 0.019 = 1.9\ \%
$$

**Step 4**: Therefore the safe fill limit is:

$$
\phi = \dfrac{V_{filled}}{V_{total}} \leq \dfrac{1}{1 + \beta \cdot \Delta T}
$$

$$
\phi \leq \dfrac{1}{1.019} \approx 0.981 = 98.1\ \%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OISD-159** mandates that the fill ratio for **Class A** petroleum (gasoline, naphtha) at **38 °C** reference must not exceed **95 % of shell capacity** to provide a vapour space cushion that also reduces sloshing and ullage fire risk.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hazard × Mode × Control

| Hazard | Pipeline | Road Tanker | Rail Wagon | Marine Tanker |
|--------|----------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| Fire/Explosion | PSV, SCADA shut-in, fire water | ESV, PVRV, dry-break couplers | Inert gas padding, ESV | IGS, foam, CO₂ fixed system |
| Spill/Leak | Leak detection, dual containment | Drip tray, pop-off valve | Bottom valve with shear section | Double hull, SBT, COW |
| Toxic exposure | H₂S monitoring | SCBA on driver, MSDS | Toxic placard, JPP escort | Toxic gas detector, SCBA locker |
| Static charge | Grounding stations at pump | Bonding before loading/unloading | Bonding on filling rack | Relaxation time ≥ 30 min post-loading |
| Overfill | LSV +HH alarm +ESD | Overfill probe + automatic shutoff | Level gauge + overfill alarm | **OOW (Overfill)** alarm + **EDT (Emergency Disconnect)** |
| External impact | Burial depth ≥ 1.2 m, marker | Reflective tape, escort for HAZMAT | Speed governor, route selection | Pilotage, TSS (Traffic Separation Scheme) |

### 3.5 Numerical Example — Tanker Volume Loading

A road tanker of capacity **16,000 L** is to be loaded with gasoline at **30 °C** ambient. Maximum ambient expected during transit: **48 °C**. Density of gasoline = **0.745 kg/L**. Compute the **safe loading volume** and **mass loaded**.

**Step 1**: $\Delta T = 48 - 30 = 18\ \text{°C}$.

**Step 2**: Thermal expansion fraction.

$$
\Delta V / V_0 = 0.00095 \times 18 = 0.0171
$$

**Step 3**: Safe fill ratio (max).

$$
\phi_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1.0171} = 0.9832
$$

**Step 4**: Apply OISD-159 cap of **95 %**.

$$
V_{safe} = \min(0.9832 \times 16000,\ 0.95 \times 16000) = \min(15731,\ 15200) = 15200\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 5**: Mass loaded.

$$
m = 15200 \times 0.745 = 11324\ \text{kg} \approx 11.32\ \text{tonnes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always apply the **more restrictive** of (i) thermal expansion limit and (ii) statutory OISD/PESO limit. Examiner expects students to demonstrate awareness of both.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Petroleum Transport Safety Management

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sourceA[Producer Field Refinery Terminal]
    mode1[Pipelines Trunk Distribution]
    mode2[Road Tankers Bulk Liquid Carriers]
    mode3[Rail Wagons Tank Wagons]
    mode4[Marine Tankers VLCC MR Product Carrier]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* List any **three statutory regulations** governing the road transport of petroleum in India and state the controlling authority for each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Petroleum Act, 1934** & **Petroleum Rules, 2002** → administered by **PESO (Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation)**, Nagpur (formerly CCE).
2. **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** & **Central Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989 (CMVR)** → administered by **Ministry of Road Transport & Highways / State RTO**.
3. **OISD-159** (Standard for Road Tankers) → voluntary but mandatory by MoPNG notification; administered by **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate)** under MoPNG.

*(Plus any one of: Static & Mobile Pressure Vessels Rules, 1981; Central Motor Vehicles Rules; Factory Act 1948 at depots.)*

---

> **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* Define the **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** of a hydrocarbon and state typical MIE values for **gasoline**, **LPG**, and **hydrogen**.

**Model Answer:**
MIE is the minimum energy required to ignite the most easily ignitable mixture of a fuel with air at atmospheric pressure, expressed in **millijoules (mJ)**.

| Fuel | Typical MIE (mJ) |
|------|-------------------|
| Hydrogen | 0.02 |
| LPG (Propane) | 0.25 |
| Gasoline | 0.25 – 0.30 |
| Methane | 0.28 |
| Diesel | 0.50 – 0.80 |

*Reason for limit:* Static charge from flow/filters must be dissipated before its energy exceeds the MIE.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

> **Q3A.** *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]* *(a) Describe in detail the **safety considerations** for the transportation of petroleum by pipelines. Cover material selection, design code, corrosion control, leak detection, and right-of-way (ROW) management. (7 marks)*
> *(b) A 24-inch, 0.625-inch wall thickness, API 5L X52 pipeline transports crude oil at a flow rate of 5,000 m³/day. The maximum operating pressure is 60 bar. Verify if the wall thickness is adequate using Barlow's equation and check the design factor. Assume joint efficiency = 1.0 and corrosion allowance = 1.5 mm. (7 marks)*

#### Model Answer for Q3A(a) — Pipeline Safety

Pipeline transport carries **> 80 %** of crude oil and > 60 % of petroleum products in India. Safety depends on **four pillars**:

1. **Material & Code Compliance:**
   - Pipe: **API 5L** grades **X42 to X70**.
   - Design codes: **ASME B31.4** (liquids), **ASME B31.8** (gas).
   - Allowable stress $S$ for X52 = **52,000 psi ≈ 358 MPa** (SMYS). Derate to $S \cdot E \cdot F$ where $F$ = 0.72 for liquid.

2. **Corrosion Control:**
   - **External:** 3LPE coating + **cathodic protection** (sacrificial Mg/Zn anode or impressed-current Ti anode).
   - **Internal:** Chemical inhibitor injection (H₂S scavenger, corrosion inhibitor), regular **pigging** (utility, cleaning, and intelligent pig).

3. **Leak Detection & SCADA:**
   - **Mass-balance** method, **pressure wave** analysis, **statistical** (Kalyanpur), **acoustic emission**, **fibre-optic DAS/DTS** distributed sensing.
   - Minimum detectable leak: **1 % of flow rate** within **10 minutes**.

4. **ROW & Third-Party Damage Prevention:**
   - Minimum depth: **1.2 m** for class location 1.
   - **Marker posts every 100 m** (urban) / 500 m (rural).
   - **One-call system** (Dial-Before-You-Dig in India).
   - ROW width: **15–30 m** depending on diameter and class.

5. **Pressure Surge & Safety Devices:**
   - **Hydraulic surge analysis** → PSV, surge tank, fast-acting valves.
   - **Mainline block valves (MLBV)** every **12–32 km**.
   - **ESD system** with remote shut-off.

#### Model Answer for Q3A(b) — Numerical Verification

**Given:**
- $D = 24\ \text{inch} = 24 \times 25.4 = 609.6\ \text{mm} = 0.6096\ \text{m}$
- $t = 0.625\ \text{inch} = 15.875\ \text{mm}$
- $P = 60\ \text{bar} = 6.0\ \text{MPa}$
- $E = 1.0$, corrosion allowance $c = 1.5\ \text{mm}$
- API 5L X52: SMYS = **52,000 psi = 358.5 MPa**
- Assume design factor $F = 0.72$ (Class 1 liquid line)

**Step 1: Effective thickness** (after subtracting corrosion allowance).

$$
t_{eff} = t - c = 15.875 - 1.5 = 14.375\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate hoop stress.**

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t_{eff}}
$$

Convert units consistently (MPa and mm):

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{6.0 \times 609.6}{2 \times 14.375} = \dfrac{3657.6}{28.75} = 127.2\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Allowable stress.**

$$
S_{allow} = S \cdot E \cdot F = 358.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.72 = 258.1\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Compare.**

$$
\sigma_h = 127.2\ \text{MPa} \ll S_{allow} = 258.1\ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5: Verdict.**

The wall thickness is **safe**, with a large margin. The actual design factor achieved is:

$$
F_{actual} = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{S} = \dfrac{127.2}{358.5} = 0.355
$$

This is well below 0.72, indicating the design is conservative — acceptable per ASME B31.4. 

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q3A):** Students often:
> - Forget to subtract the **corrosion allowance** before applying Barlow's formula.
> - Mix units (psi vs MPa, inch vs mm). Always convert to a single system first.
> - Use $F = 1.0$ instead of the correct $F = 0.72$ for liquid lines.
> - Forget to mention that the mill tolerance (12.5 %) must also be considered before final verdict.

---

> **Q3B.** *(Alternative Question for Internal Choice)*
> *(a) Discuss the **statutory considerations** for road transport of petroleum in India under the Petroleum Act 1934 and Petroleum Rules 2002. Mention licence types and safety conditions. (7 marks)*
> *(b) A road tanker of capacity **18,000 L** carries gasoline from a depot at **30 °C** to a market where ambient can reach **48 °C**. Compute (i) the safe loading volume using thermal expansion and (ii) verify against OISD cap of 95 %. Take β = 0.00095 /°C for gasoline. (7 marks)*

#### Model Answer for Q3B(a) — Statutory Framework

**Key Statutes:**

1. **Petroleum Act, 1934** — the parent Act defining "petroleum" and authorizing the Central Government to make rules.
2. **Petroleum Rules, 2002** — the principal rule-book consolidating storage, transport, and handling of petroleum.

**Licences under Petroleum Rules 2002:**

| Licence | Form | Authority |
|---------|------|-----------|
| Licence to import | Form IV | PESO |
| Licence to store petroleum in tanks/installations | Form XV | PESO / District Authority |
| Licence for transportation in bulk | Form XII | PESO |
| Licence for petroleum service stations | Form XI / XIII | PESO |
| Licence for Petroleum Refinery | Form VI | PESO |

**Safety Conditions for Road Transport:**

- Tankers must be **pressure-tested** (hydrostatic) every **5 years**.
- **Road tanker driver** must hold a **Hazardous Materials Driver Training Certificate** (HMDTC) from an institute approved by **PESO / MoRTH**.
- Tanker must display **hazardous goods placards** (Class 3 / Class 2.1) and **emergency contact numbers**.
- Carrying of **dangerous goods together** (e.g., LPG and oxidizer) is **prohibited**.
- The route must be approved by the **district transport authority**.
- Smoking / naked flame within **10 m** of the tanker is **prohibited**.
- The vehicle must carry a **portable fire extinguisher** of **9 kg DCP** minimum.

#### Model Answer for Q3B(b) — Safe Loading Volume

**Given:**
- $V_{total} = 18{,}000\ \text{L}$
- $T_1 = 30\ \text{°C}$ (filling temperature)
- $T_{max} = 48\ \text{°C}$
- $\beta = 0.00095\ \text{per °C}$

**Step 1: Temperature rise.**

$$
\Delta T = 48 - 30 = 18\ \text{°C}
$$

**Step 2: Thermal volume expansion fraction.**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \beta \cdot \Delta T = 0.00095 \times 18 = 0.01710 = 1.71\ \%
$$

**Step 3: Maximum fill ratio from thermal expansion.**

$$
\phi_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \beta \cdot \Delta T} = \dfrac{1}{1.01710} = 0.98318
$$

**Step 4: Maximum safe volume from thermal consideration.**

$$
V_{safe,thermal} = 0.98318 \times 18{,}000 = 17{,}697\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 5: Apply OISD cap of 95 %.**

$$
V_{safe,OISD} = 0.95 \times 18{,}000 = 17{,}100\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the more restrictive limit.**

$$
V_{load} = \min(17697,\ 17100) = 17{,}100\ \text{L}
$$

**Result:**
- Load the tanker with **17,100 L** of gasoline.
- Ullage = 18,000 − 17,100 = **900 L** (5 %).
- At 0.745 kg/L, mass loaded = **12,740 kg ≈ 12.74 tonnes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q3B):**
> - Students often pick the larger of the two values, but the law mandates the **smaller, safer** value.
> - Forgetting units; KTU strictly penalises missing units.
> - Students sometimes use **β = 0.0018** (LPG value) for gasoline — wrong fluid, wrong value.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Petroleum transport has **five modes**: Pipeline, Road, Rail, Marine, Air — each with unique hazards.
- ✅ The **Petroleum Act 1934** + **Petroleum Rules 2002** form the bedrock Indian statute; **PESO** (formerly CCE) is the apex regulator.
- ✅ Road tankers must display **UN Number**, **Hazchem code**, **Class label**, **Proper Shipping Name** and **consignor/consignee** details.
- ✅ Pipelines designed per **ASME B31.4 / B31.8** with design factor **F = 0.5–0.8**; corrosion allowance + mill tolerance are mandatory.
- ✅ **Static electricity** — limit velocity **≤ 7 m/s** in light hydrocarbon; provide **bonding (R ≤ 10 Ω) & grounding** at every loading/unloading; allow **≥ 30 s residence time** post-filtration.
- ✅ **Fill limit** must satisfy both thermal expansion and statutory caps (e.g., **95 % for OISD-159** Class A).
- ✅ Marine transport governed by **SOLAS, MARPOL, IMDG, ISGOTT, OCIMF** — double-hull mandatory since **1996**.
- ✅ Rail wagons must have **tank code, JPP escort (toxic gases), and bottom valve with shear section**.
- ✅ **Air transport** is severely restricted — most gases and Class 6.1 toxics are **forbidden on passenger aircraft**.
- ✅ **Emergency response** includes **Mutual Aid Plan, MSDS availability, SCBA, foam, dry chemical, oil spill booms, communication with regulators** within statutory time-frames (e.g., **2 h to DG Shipping**, **immediate to OISD for major incident**).
- ✅ Always follow the **Hierarchy of Controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ **Document Retention**: Incident records, calibration of safety instruments, training records, and statutory licences must be retained for a minimum of **5–7 years** as per PESO and OISD guidelines.
- ✅ **Static charge time** after filtration: allow **≥ 30 seconds relaxation time** before gauging/sampling.
- ✅ **Marking & Labelling** must be in **English + Hindi** for domestic and **English only** for international shipments.
- ✅ In numerical answers, show **all units**, retain **3–4 significant figures**, and state assumptions explicitly.

> **Final Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**  
> **"P-S-M-R-A — Pipe, Ship, Move, Rail, Air → Safe by Statute, Sound by Science."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Construction Requirements for Cross Country Hydrocarbon Pipelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Construction Requirements for Cross Country Hydrocarbon Pipelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cross Country Hydrocarbon Pipeline** is a continuously welded, buried or above-ground pressure piping system used to transport crude oil, refined petroleum products, natural gas, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), or other hydrocarbons over long distances—typically from production fields, refineries, storage terminals, or port facilities to distribution networks. The design, materials, fabrication, installation, testing, and operation of these pipelines are governed by international codes such as **ASME B31.4** (Liquid Petroleum Transportation Piping Systems), **ASME B31.8** (Gas Transmission & Distribution Piping Systems), and **API 1104** (Welding of Pipelines and Related Facilities).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is critical for Module 3 of PESFT745. Students must understand the design basis, route selection, wall-thickness calculation, material specification, construction sequence, and hydrostatic testing philosophy of cross-country pipelines because these form the foundation of industrial hydrocarbon safety.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highway for fluids**. Just as a national highway must be engineered for the *load* (vehicles), *terrain* (mountains, rivers), *climate* (heat, rain, frost), and *safety* (barriers, lighting, traffic rules), a cross-country pipeline must be engineered for the *internal pressure* of the fluid, the *soil/terrain conditions*, the *environmental temperature swings*, and the *population density* of areas it crosses.

- The **pipe wall** is like the *highway pavement thickness* — thicker where traffic (pressure) is high.
- The **Right of Way (RoW)** is the *land corridor* reserved exclusively for the pipeline.
- **Valves and fittings** are the *traffic control signals* — they can shut off flow in emergencies.
- **Cathodic protection** is the *anti-corrosion paint* that prevents the steel from rusting.
- **Hydrostatic testing** is the *load test certificate* — proving the pipeline can handle pressures beyond its normal operation before it is allowed to "open for service."

The pipeline is the **arterial lifeline** of the hydrocarbon industry. A failure is not just an economic loss — it can cause **fire, explosion, ecological disaster, and loss of human life**. Hence, the design and construction are bound by strict, internationally recognized codes.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | g | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Young's Modulus of Carbon Steel | E | **2.07 × 10⁵** | MPa |
| Thermal Expansion Coefficient (Steel) | α | **1.17 × 10⁻⁵** | per °C |
| Poisson's Ratio (Steel) | ν | **0.30** | — |
| Density of Crude Oil (typical) | ρ | **850 – 950** | kg/m³ |
| Density of Natural Gas (line condition) | ρ | **0.7 – 80** | kg/m³ |
| Standard API Design Factor (Class 1) | F | **0.80** | — |
| Standard API Design Factor (Class 2) | F | **0.60** | — |
| Standard API Design Factor (Class 3) | F | **0.50** | — |
| Standard API Design Factor (Class 4) | F | **0.40** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Location Class (as per ASME B31.8):**
> - **Class 1:** Rural, sparsely populated, less than 10 dwellings per mile.
> - **Class 2:** Suburban / fringe areas, 10 to 46 dwellings per mile.
> - **Class 3:** Urban / built-up areas, more than 46 dwellings per mile.
> - **Class 4:** Multi-story buildings, high-density zones (e.g., city centers).
>
> The design factor **F decreases** as the population density **increases** — a direct embodiment of the safety principle of *higher risk → lower stress*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy of Cross-Country Pipelines

The design of a cross-country pipeline is not a single equation — it is a **systematic decision-making process** rooted in the philosophy of **Defence-in-Depth**. The pipeline must remain safe against:

1. **Internal pressure surges** (steady-state, transient, water hammer)
2. **External loads** (soil, traffic, seismic)
3. **Environmental degradation** (corrosion, temperature)
4. **Third-party damage** (excavation, sabotage)
5. **Material/manufacturing flaws** (defects, weld imperfections)

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Design Logic

- **Step 1 — Establish Design Basis:** Determine the fluid type, flow rate (MMCMD for gas, MMTPA for liquids), inlet pressure, delivery pressure, and operating temperature range.
- **Step 2 — Hydraulic Sizing:** Compute the pipeline diameter using the Weymouth, Panhandle, or Darcy-Weisbach equations (for gas) or Hazen-Williams (for liquids). The diameter is selected such that the **pressure drop** between inlet and delivery does not exceed allowable limits.
- **Step 3 — Route Selection:** Identify an optimal route considering topography, geology, environmental sensitivity, population density, and crossings.
- **Step 4 — Classification of Route:** Divide the route into **Location Classes (1 to 4)** based on population density along the RoW.
- **Step 5 — Material Selection:** Select line-pipe material as per **API 5L** specification (e.g., API 5L X42, X52, X60, X65, X70, X80) based on strength and toughness.
- **Step 6 — Wall Thickness Calculation:** Apply the **modified Barlow's formula** as per ASME B31.4 / B31.8.
- **Step 7 — Stress Check:** Verify that the **combined effective stress** does not exceed allowable limits (typically **72% of SMYS** for steel pipe).
- **Step 8 — Safety Additions:** Add corrosion allowance, select coating, design cathodic protection, and specify valving.

### 2.1.2 Why These Steps?

- **Hydraulic sizing first** because the *diameter drives both capex and opex*. A slightly larger diameter reduces pumping cost but increases material cost.
- **Route selection second** because the *safety and integrity of the pipeline depend on what is around it* — a pipeline routed through a city must be designed more conservatively (Class 4) than one routed through a desert.
- **Wall thickness via Barlow** because the **hoop stress** generated by internal pressure is the primary load; Barlow's equation is derived from a thin-walled pressure vessel assumption which is valid for D/t > 30 (true for most cross-country lines).
- **Combined stress check** because in real life, the pipe experiences **axial** (Poisson effect, thermal, bending) AND **hoop** stress simultaneously — a failure criterion like von Mises captures this.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam ready**. Memorize the variable definitions, units, and code-references in parentheses.

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot F \cdot E \cdot T}$ | t = nominal wall thickness (mm); P = design pressure (MPa); D = outside diameter (mm); S = SMYS (MPa); F = design factor; E = longitudinal joint factor (0.6 – 1.0); T = temperature derating factor (≤ 1.0) | ASME B31.4 / B31.8 (Barlow) |
| 2 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | σ_h = hoop stress (MPa) | Thin-walled cylinder |
| 3 | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t} = \nu \cdot \sigma_h$ | σ_L = longitudinal stress due to internal pressure (MPa); ν = Poisson's ratio | Closed-end cylinder |
| 4 | $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | σ_T = thermal stress (MPa); E = Young's modulus (MPa); α = thermal expansion coefficient (per °C); ΔT = temperature differential (°C) | Thermal loading |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_L + \sigma_T + \sigma_B$ | σ_B = bending stress due to soil/load (MPa) | Combined loading |
| 6 | $\sigma_e = \sqrt{\sigma_h^{\,2} + \sigma_L^{\,2} + \sigma_T^{\,2} - \sigma_h\sigma_L - \sigma_L\sigma_T - \sigma_h\sigma_T}$ | σ_e = equivalent (von Mises) stress (MPa) | Combined stress check |
| 7 | $\sigma_e \leq 0.72 \cdot S$ | Allowable combined stress limit (72% of SMYS) | ASME B31.4 |
| 8 | $P_{\text{test}} = 1.25 \cdot P_{\text{design}}$ (min) | Hydrostatic test pressure multiplier | ASME B31.4 |
| 9 | $P_{\text{test}} \leq \dfrac{2 \cdot S \cdot t}{D} \cdot F_{\text{test}}$ | Maximum test pressure; F_test = 1.0 (test factor) | ASME B31.4 |
| 10 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | ΔP = pressure drop; f = friction factor; L = length; v = velocity; ρ = density | Darcy-Weisbach |

> [!NOTE]
> **Longitudinal Joint Factor (E):** E = 1.00 for seamless, ERW, DSAW; E = 0.80 for lap-welded (legacy). Modern cross-country lines always use E = 1.00.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Sector:** The very backbone of upstream-to-downstream transport. Examples: **Jamnagar–Loni pipeline** (India's longest crude pipeline, ~2,500 km), **Hazira–Vijaipur–Jagdishpur (HVJ) gas pipeline**, **Turkstream** (Russia–Turkey), **Nord Stream** (Russia–Germany).
- **Refinery Feed:** Crude pipelines feed refineries; product pipelines deliver petrol/diesel to terminals.
- **City Gas Distribution (CGD):** Branching networks from the cross-country main line to households and industries.
- **Defence & Strategic:** Used for jet fuel transport to airbases; subject to special security protocols.

The design and construction standards used in these pipelines ensure a typical **service life of 30 to 50 years**, with integrity preserved by **In-Line Inspection (ILI)** tools, **Cathodic Protection (CP)**, and periodic **hydrostatic revalidation**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Modified Barlow's Formula for Wall Thickness

The classical **Barlow's equation** is derived from the equilibrium of a longitudinal strip of a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel. Consider a pipe of **inside diameter D_i** and **outside diameter D_o**, subjected to an internal gauge pressure **P**. The pipe wall is thin (D/t > 30).

### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of a Half-Cylinder

Imagine the pipe cut along a longitudinal axis. The two halves are pushed apart by the internal pressure acting on the projected area. The longitudinal section area resisting this force is the **wall cross-section** in the axial direction.

$$
\text{Projected Area} = D_o \cdot L
$$

$$
\text{Resisting Force} = 2 \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sigma_h
$$

### Step 2 — Force Equilibrium

The bursting force is balanced by the hoop stress acting on the two wall cross-sections.

$$
P \cdot D_o \cdot L = 2 \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sigma_h
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify to Obtain Hoop Stress

Cancelling L from both sides:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D_o}{2 \cdot t}
$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Allowable Stress and Safety Factors

The pipe should never operate at the yield point. Hence, the allowable stress is the SMYS (S) divided by a safety factor. In ASME B31.4 / B31.8, this safety factor is expressed indirectly as a **Design Factor (F)**, and additional derating is applied for **joint efficiency (E)** and **temperature (T)**.

$$
\sigma_h \leq F \cdot E \cdot T \cdot S
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Wall Thickness

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
\frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} = F \cdot E \cdot T \cdot S
$$

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot F \cdot E \cdot T}
$$

This is the **Modified Barlow's Formula**. A **corrosion allowance (CA)** of typically **1.5 mm to 3 mm** is added to the calculated t to obtain the **nominal design thickness**, and the next higher standard schedule is chosen for procurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why F decreases with population density?**
> Because the consequence of failure in a Class 4 (urban) area is catastrophic — many lives, properties, and critical infrastructure are at risk. A lower design factor means a thicker wall, hence a lower operating stress and a higher safety margin.

## 3.2 Derivation of Longitudinal Stress (Poisson Effect in Closed-End Pipe)

For a **closed-end** pipe (which all cross-country pipelines are, since they are sealed by valves and end flanges), the internal pressure also produces a **longitudinal (axial) tensile stress**. This is derived from force balance on a transverse cross-section of the pipe.

### Step 1 — Identify the Forces

The internal pressure acting on the **end cap** area is balanced by the longitudinal stress in the **pipe wall annulus**.

$$
\text{Pressure Force on End Cap} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_i^{\,2}
$$

$$
\text{Resisting Wall Force} = \sigma_L \cdot \pi \cdot D_i \cdot t
$$

(For thin wall, the wall area = mean circumference × thickness.)

### Step 2 — Force Balance

$$
P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_i^{\,2} = \sigma_L \cdot \pi \cdot D_i \cdot t
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$
\sigma_L = \frac{P \cdot D_i}{4 \cdot t}
$$

### Step 4 — Relate to Hoop Stress

For thin walls, $D_i \approx D_o = D$, and for an **elastic material**, the axial strain from the longitudinal stress causes a **Poisson contraction** that, when constrained by the closed ends, results in the well-known relationship:

$$
\sigma_L = \nu \cdot \sigma_h = 0.30 \cdot \sigma_h
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in KTU exams?** The von Mises equivalent stress expression includes both σ_h and σ_L. Students often forget to subtract the σ_h·σ_L cross term. Marks are awarded for **identifying the Poisson effect** explicitly.

## 3.3 Derivation of Thermal Stress (σ_T)

When a pipeline is laid at an **ambient temperature T_amb** and later operates at a **different temperature T_op** (e.g., hot crude at 60 °C, or cryogenic LNG at −160 °C), the steel tries to expand or contract. If the pipe is **fully restrained** (e.g., by soil friction, anchors, or end fixed points), this thermal strain is converted into **stress** (Hooke's Law).

### Step 1 — Free Thermal Strain (If Unrestrained)

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Step 2 — Equivalent Stress Under Full Restraint

Since strain is prevented, the equivalent stress is:

$$
\sigma_T = E \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example (Standard Values)

For carbon steel with E = **2.07 × 10⁵ MPa**, α = **1.17 × 10⁻⁵ per °C**, and ΔT = **50 °C**:

$$
\sigma_T = (2.07 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.17 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (50)
$$

$$
\sigma_T = 2.07 \cdot 1.17 \cdot 50 \approx 121.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This thermal stress (~121 MPa) is **comparable to the operating hoop stress**! It is the reason pipelines operating at elevated temperatures require **expansion loops**, **bends**, or **axial stress analysis**. KTU often tests this numerical value.

## 3.4 Equivalent Stress (von Mises Criterion)

For a pipe under combined **hoop, longitudinal, and thermal** stresses, the **maximum shear strain energy** (von Mises) criterion is used. For a 2D stress state (ignoring radial stress in thin walls):

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{\sigma_h^{\,2} + \sigma_L^{\,2} + \sigma_T^{\,2} - \sigma_h\sigma_L - \sigma_L\sigma_T - \sigma_h\sigma_T}
$$

The acceptance criterion per ASME B31.4:

$$
\sigma_e \leq 0.72 \cdot S
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU 2024):** Many students write the **principal stress formula** ($\sigma_e = \sqrt{\sigma_h^{\,2} + \sigma_L^{\,2}}$) instead of the **full von Mises** expression. The cross terms must be retained.

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example

**Problem:** A cross-country crude oil pipeline has:
- Outside Diameter, D = 610 mm (24 inch)
- SMYS, S = 358 MPa (API 5L X52)
- Design Pressure, P = 9.0 MPa
- Location Class = 2 → F = 0.60
- E = 1.00 (ERW pipe)
- T = 1.00 (Temperature < 120 °C)
- Operating Temperature differential ΔT = 40 °C

### Step 1 — Calculate Wall Thickness (Barlow)

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot F \cdot E \cdot T} = \frac{9.0 \times 610}{2 \times 358 \times 0.60 \times 1.00 \times 1.00}
$$

$$
t = \frac{5490}{429.6} \approx 12.78 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Add Corrosion Allowance (CA = 2 mm)

$$
t_{\text{design}} = 12.78 + 2.0 = 14.78 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt the next higher standard thickness, e.g., **t = 15.09 mm** (standard schedule).

### Step 3 — Calculate Hoop Stress at Design Pressure

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{9.0 \times 610}{2 \times 15.09} = \frac{5490}{30.18} \approx 181.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Longitudinal Pressure Stress

$$
\sigma_L = \nu \cdot \sigma_h = 0.30 \times 181.9 = 54.57 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Thermal Stress

$$
\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = (2.07 \times 10^5)(1.17 \times 10^{-5})(40) = 96.88 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 6 — Calculate Equivalent (von Mises) Stress

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{181.9^2 + 54.57^2 + 96.88^2 - (181.9)(54.57) - (54.57)(96.88) - (181.9)(96.88)}
$$

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{33088 + 2978 + 9386 - 9927 - 5286 - 17623}
$$

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{12616} \approx 112.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

### Step 7 — Check Allowable Limit

$$
0.72 \cdot S = 0.72 \times 358 = 257.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $\sigma_e = 112.3 \text{ MPa} \leq 257.8 \text{ MPa}$ ✅, the design is **safe**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** A high equivalent stress of 112 MPa is still well within 72% SMYS. The factor 0.72 (i.e., 72%) is a code-mandated upper limit, not a working stress — actual operating stress is often only 40 to 50% of SMYS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Pipeline Construction Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation & Feasibility Study] --> B[Route Survey and Selection]
    B --> C[Hydraulic Sizing and Diameter Selection]
    C --> D[Design Basis Definition and Location Classification]
    D --> E[Material Selection API 5L X42 to X80]
    E --> F[Wall Thickness Calculation via Modified Barlow]
    F --> G[Combined Stress Check von Mises]
    G --> H{Pass Safety Check?}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Procurement of Line Pipes and Coatings]
    I --> J[RoW Clearing and Grading]
    J --> K[Trenching]
    K --> L[Pipe Stringing along RoW]
    L --> M[Field Bending and Fit-Up]
    M --> N[Welding as per API 1104]
    N --> O[Non-Destructive Testing RT and UT]
    O --> P[Field Joint Coating and Wrapping]
    P --> Q[Lowering In using Sidebooms]
    Q --> R[Backfilling and Tamping]
    R --> S[Hydrostatic Testing at 1.25 to 1.4 times Design Pressure]
    S --> T[Test Pass?]
    T -- No --> M
    T -- Yes --> U[Cathodic Protection Installation]
    U --> V[Pre-Commissioning and Commissioning]
    V --> W[Line Pack and Gas In or Oil In]
    W --> X[Operation Handover to Pipeline Operator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a sequential construction phase. The diamond decision blocks (`H` and `T`) introduce **iteration loops** — if a hydrostatic test fails, the operator must locate the leak, cut out the defective segment, re-weld, and re-test.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Wall-Thickness and Stress Check Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Design Pressure P] --> P2[Multiply by OD D]
    D1[SMYS S] --> D2[Multiply by F x E x T]
    P2 --> Calc
    D2 --> Calc[Barlow Equation]
    Calc --> t[Wall Thickness t]
    t --> C1[Add Corrosion Allowance]
    C1 --> tNom[Nominal Thickness]
    tNom --> Stress1[Calculate Hoop Stress]
    Stress1 --> Stress2[Calculate Longitudinal Stress]
    Stress2 --> Stress3[Add Thermal Stress]
    Stress3 --> V1[Compute von Mises]
    V1 --> Limit{sigma_e less than 0.72 S?}
    Limit -- Yes --> OK[Design Accepted]
    Limit -- No --> F1[Increase t or Reduce P]
    F1 --> tNom
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Pipeline Cross-Section (Schematic Functional Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sub1[Cross Section of a Buried Cross Country Pipeline]
        Top1[Top Soil Layer] --> Top2[Grade and Tamping]
        Top2 --> Top3[Warning Tape at 0.5 m depth]
        Top3 --> Top4[Backfill Sand Bed]
        Top4 --> Top5[Anti Corrosion Coating]
        Top5 --> Pipe1[Carrier Pipe API 5L X65]
        Pipe1 --> Coat1[3LPE or FBE Coating]
        Coat1 --> Casing[Sleeve for Road or Rail Crossing]
        Casing --> CP[Cathodic Protection Cable]
        CP --> Bed[Compacted Sand Bedding]
        Bed --> Subsoil[Virgin Subsoil]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** The warning tape placed 0.5 m above the pipe is a critical safety feature — it alerts any future excavator **before** they reach the pipeline, giving them a chance to stop and contact the operator. This simple plastic tape has prevented countless third-party damage incidents globally.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Style Note:** Part A questions test **recall and understanding** (Bloom Level 1 & 2). Keep answers concise — 3 to 4 lines per question. Define first, then explain.

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]`

**Define a cross-country hydrocarbon pipeline and state any THREE international design codes that govern its engineering.**

**Model Answer:**
A cross-country hydrocarbon pipeline is a long-distance, continuously welded, buried pressure piping system used to transport crude oil, refined products, natural gas, LPG, or other hydrocarbons between production, processing, storage, and distribution facilities.
The three primary international design codes are:
1. **ASME B31.4** — *Pipeline Transportation Systems for Liquid Hydrocarbons and Other Liquids.*
2. **ASME B31.8** — *Gas Transmission and Distribution Piping Systems.*
3. **API 1104** — *Welding of Pipelines and Related Facilities.*

Additionally, **API 5L** specifies line-pipe material grades, and **ASME B31.3** covers process piping within terminals.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]`

**What is a Location Class in pipeline design, and how does the design factor (F) vary with location class as per ASME B31.8?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Location Class** is a classification of the pipeline route based on the **population density and building occupancy** along the right-of-way. ASME B31.8 defines four classes:
- **Class 1** — Rural, < 10 dwellings per mile → **F = 0.80**
- **Class 2** — Suburban, 10–46 dwellings per mile → **F = 0.60**
- **Class 3** — Urban, > 46 dwellings per mile → **F = 0.50**
- **Class 4** — Multi-story buildings / CBD → **F = 0.40**

The design factor **decreases** as the population density increases, ensuring **higher safety margins** in areas where a failure would have catastrophic human consequences.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. Students must answer EITHER Question A **OR** Question B in full (sub-parts a and b, 7 marks each).

### ✅ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q8(a)]`

**A 24-inch (OD = 610 mm) cross-country crude oil pipeline is to be designed as per ASME B31.4. The design pressure is 9.5 MPa and the design temperature is 50 °C. The line pipe material is API 5L X60 with SMYS = 415 MPa. The route passes through a Class 2 location. The longitudinal joint factor is 1.00 and temperature derating factor is 0.967. Required corrosion allowance is 1.5 mm.**

**(a)** Calculate the **nominal wall thickness** of the line pipe using the modified Barlow's formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the operating temperature differential is ΔT = 45 °C, calculate the **hoop stress**, **longitudinal stress**, **thermal stress**, and the **equivalent von Mises stress**. Verify the design against the 72% SMYS limit. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Wall Thickness

Given:
- D = 610 mm, P = 9.5 MPa, S = 415 MPa, F = 0.60 (Class 2)
- E = 1.00, T = 0.967, CA = 1.5 mm

Step 1 — Apply modified Barlow's equation:

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot F \cdot E \cdot T} = \frac{9.5 \times 610}{2 \times 415 \times 0.60 \times 1.00 \times 0.967}
$$

Step 2 — Numerator:

$$
9.5 \times 610 = 5795
$$

Step 3 — Denominator:

$$
2 \times 415 \times 0.60 \times 0.967 = 415 \times 1.1604 = 481.6
$$

Step 4 — Compute t:

$$
t = \frac{5795}{481.6} \approx 12.03 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 5 — Add corrosion allowance:

$$
t_{\text{design}} = 12.03 + 1.5 = 13.53 \text{ mm}
$$

**Adopt next standard thickness, e.g., t = 14.27 mm.**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Stress Check

Step 1 — Hoop stress at design pressure:

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{9.5 \times 610}{2 \times 14.27} = \frac{5795}{28.54} \approx 203.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

Step 2 — Longitudinal stress (Poisson effect):

$$
\sigma_L = \nu \cdot \sigma_h = 0.30 \times 203.1 \approx 60.9 \text{ MPa}
$$

Step 3 — Thermal stress:

$$
\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = (2.07 \times 10^5)(1.17 \times 10^{-5})(45) \approx 108.99 \text{ MPa}
$$

Step 4 — Equivalent von Mises stress:

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{\sigma_h^{\,2} + \sigma_L^{\,2} + \sigma_T^{\,2} - \sigma_h\sigma_L - \sigma_L\sigma_T - \sigma_h\sigma_T}
$$

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{203.1^2 + 60.9^2 + 108.99^2 - (203.1)(60.9) - (60.9)(108.99) - (203.1)(108.99)}
$$

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{41250 + 3709 + 11879 - 12369 - 6638 - 22136}
$$

$$
\sigma_e = \sqrt{15695} \approx 125.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

Step 5 — Allowable limit:

$$
0.72 \cdot S = 0.72 \times 415 = 298.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $125.3 \text{ MPa} \ll 298.8 \text{ MPa}$ → **Design is SAFE.** ✅

---

### ✅ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Q8(b) – Internal Choice]`

**(a)** List and briefly explain **FIVE key design parameters** that govern the engineering of a cross-country hydrocarbon pipeline. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe, with a neat flow, the **step-by-step construction sequence** of a buried cross-country pipeline from route survey to commissioning. Highlight the role of hydrostatic testing. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Five Key Design Parameters

1. **Design Pressure (P):** The maximum sustained internal pressure the pipeline is expected to experience during operation, including surge allowance. It is the primary load case for wall-thickness design.

2. **Design Temperature:** The maximum (or minimum, for cryogenic) metal temperature the pipe will see. It governs material selection (charpy impact requirements), coating selection, and the temperature derating factor T.

3. **Outside Diameter (D):** Determined by hydraulic analysis (flow rate × pressure drop). Common sizes range from 6 inches (small gathering lines) to 56 inches (major transmission trunks).

4. **Material Grade (SMYS, S):** Specified by API 5L (X42, X52, X60, X65, X70, X80). Higher grades enable **thinner walls, lower material tonnage, and higher operating pressures** — but at higher cost per ton and stricter welding requirements.

5. **Location Class / Design Factor (F):** As discussed in Question 2 of Part A, F captures the **societal risk** of the route. It ranges from 0.80 (Class 1) to 0.40 (Class 4). It is the most important *safety-policy* parameter.

6. *(Bonus – acceptable)* **Corrosion Allowance (CA):** Extra wall thickness reserved to compensate for expected internal/external corrosion over the design life. Typical: 1.5 to 3 mm.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Construction Sequence

| Step | Activity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Route Survey** | Aerial, GPS, and ground survey to finalise the alignment, identify crossings and constraints. |
| 2 | **RoW Clearing & Grading** | Remove vegetation, level the ground to enable vehicle movement. |
| 3 | **Trenching** | Excavate to depth ≥ 1.0 m (more in rocky/agricultural land) using trenchers. |
| 4 | **Stringing** | Lay individual pipe joints along the trench route. |
| 5 | **Field Bending** | Cold-bend pipes to follow route curvature using hydraulic bending machines. |
| 6 | **Welding** | Join pipes using SMAW / FCAW / GMAW as per API 1104 qualified procedures. |
| 7 | **NDT (RT/UT)** | 100% radiographic or ultrasonic testing of every weld to detect defects. |
| 8 | **Joint Coating** | Field-applied epoxy + shrink sleeve to protect girth welds. |
| 9 | **Lowering-In** | Use side-boom tractors to gently lift and lower the welded string into the trench. |
| 10 | **Backfilling** | Place padding sand around pipe, then native soil, with warning tape at 0.5 m above. |
| 11 | **Hydrostatic Testing** | Fill with water, pressurise to 1.25–1.4 × design pressure, hold for 8–24 hours. Confirms strength and leak-tightness. |
| 12 | **Dewatering, Drying, Pigging** | Remove test water, dry the line (for gas), and run cleaning pigs. |
| 13 | **Cathodic Protection & Commissioning** | Energise CP system; introduce product gradually; hand over to operations. |

**Role of Hydrostatic Testing:** It is the **final integrity gate** before the pipeline enters service. The test is conducted at a pressure **above the design pressure** to verify the **strength of the pipe, girth welds, and fittings** and to **reveal any leak paths** under stress. ASME B31.4 mandates a minimum hold of 8 hours (or 24 hours for gas lines) at the test pressure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance** when computing the final adopted thickness. Marks are split — half for calculated t, half for design t.
> 2. **Mixing up σ_L formulas** — using σ_L = PD/4t AND σ_L = ν·σ_h interchangeably without checking the assumption (closed end vs free end).
> 3. **Omitting cross-terms in von Mises** — the full three-stress expression with cross products is required, not the principal stress approximation.
> 4. **Wrong units** — SMYS is given in MPa, not kgf/cm². 1 kgf/cm² = 0.0981 MPa. Frequent conversion error.
> 5. **Not commenting on design safety** — the final step must explicitly state σ_e vs 0.72·S and conclude SAFE / UNSAFE.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this before the exam!)**

- ⭐ **Modified Barlow's Formula:** $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot F \cdot E \cdot T}$ — memorize all six variables and their units.
- ⭐ **Design Factor (F):** Class 1 → 0.80, Class 2 → 0.60, Class 3 → 0.50, Class 4 → 0.40 (ASME B31.8).
- ⭐ **Hoop Stress:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ — the dominant stress in pressurised pipes.
- ⭐ **Longitudinal Stress:** $\sigma_L = \nu \cdot \sigma_h$ for closed-end pipes (Poisson effect).
- ⭐ **Thermal Stress:** $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ — significant for hot crude, hot gas, or cryogenic LNG.
- ⭐ **Allowable Combined Stress:** $\sigma_e \leq 0.72 \cdot S$ (ASME B31.4).
- ⭐ **Hydrostatic Test Pressure:** minimum $1.25 \cdot P_{\text{design}}$, hold time ≥ 8 h.
- ⭐ **Corrosion Allowance:** Add 1.5 to 3 mm to calculated t.
- ⭐ **Joint Factor (E):** 1.00 for modern ERW / seamless / DSAW pipes.
- ⭐ **Line Pipe Material:** API 5L grades X42, X52, X60, X65, X70, X80 — higher grade = higher SMYS.
- ⭐ **Welding Standard:** API 1104 governs procedure qualification and welder qualification.
- ⭐ **Construction Sequence (10 steps):** Survey → Trench → String → Bend → Weld → NDT → Coat → Lower → Backfill → Hydrotest → Commission.
- ⭐ **Crossings:** Special protection for road, rail, and river crossings — casings, encasement pipes, or HDD (Horizontal Directional Drilling).
- ⭐ **Right of Way (RoW):** Statutory land corridor; typical width 10 to 30 m depending on diameter and class.
- ⭐ **Cathodic Protection:** Impressed Current or Sacrificial Anode — to mitigate external corrosion.
- ⭐ **Inspection Tools:** Intelligent pigs (MFL, UT, geometry) for in-line inspection every 5 to 7 years.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) Bottling Plant Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) Bottling Plant Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) Bottling Plant** is an integrated hydrocarbon handling facility designed for the **bulk receipt, storage, transfer, and filling of liquefied petroleum gas into portable pressure vessels (cylinders)** of varying capacities (typically **14.2 kg domestic, 19 kg commercial, 47.5 kg industrial/bulk**, and composite cylinders), followed by **quality testing, storage, and dispatch** of the filled cylinders to downstream consumers, conforming to **OISD-144**, **SMPV (Static and Mobile Pressure Vessels) Rules**, and **PESO (Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation)** statutory norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (PESFT745 — Module 3):**
> The student must articulate the *end-to-end operational chain* of an LPG bottling plant — from terminal receipt of bulk LPG via tankers/rail wagons → bulk storage → pumping → cylinder filling → testing → storage → dispatch — and integrate the **safety interlocks, statutory codes, fire protection philosophy, and HAZOP/QRA-based risk controls** that govern each stage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a giant, super-chilled, pressurized **water cooler bottle** that supplies many small thermos flasks. The big bottle (called a *Horton sphere* or *mounded vessel*) holds the LPG as a liquid under pressure. When you fill a small domestic gas cylinder, you are essentially **"siphoning" a measured quantity** of this pressurized liquid into a smaller portable container. The entire bottling plant is a **metered transfer station** — like a petrol pump — but operating with a much more dangerous fuel that is *heavier than air*, *odourless in its pure form*, and *stored as a liquid at moderate pressure*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Physical Property Anchor:**
> - **LPG** = mixture of **Propane (C₃H₈)** and **Butane (C₄H₁₀)** in ratios typically **60:40, 50:50, or 30:70** depending on climate (winter/summer grade).
> - Stored as **liquid under pressure ≈ 0.7–1.7 MPa** at ambient temperature.
> - **Vapour density ≈ 1.5–2.0 times that of air** → settles in low-lying areas → prime asphyxiation and explosion hazard.
> - **Auto-Ignition Temperature ≈ 410–550 °C**.
> - **Flammability range in air: 2.1 % – 9.5 %** by volume.
> - **Boiling point (propane) = −42 °C**; **(butane) = −0.5 °C**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Philosophy of an LPG Bottling Plant

The plant is engineered around **four cardinal safety principles**:

1. **Containment Integrity** — All pressurized equipment designed, fabricated, and tested as per ASME Sec VIII / IS 2825.
2. **Leak Minimization** — Welded pipelines preferred; flanged joints minimized; double-block-and-bleed valves at critical isolation points.
3. **Detection & Isolation Speed** — Gas detection, flame detection, and ESD-1/ESD-2/ESD-3 systems trigger within seconds of a credible upset.
4. **Segregation of Hazards** — Bulk storage isolated from filling shed; filled-cylinder shed isolated from dispatch; statutory inter-distance norms (OISD-118) enforced.

### 2.2 Unit Operations Block Decomposition

An LPG bottling plant comprises the following **eight functional blocks**:

| # | Functional Block | Equipment / Asset | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bulk Receipt (Tanker / Wagon Unloading) | Compressed arms, vapor return lines, earthing reels, gas detectors | OISD-144, OISD-150 |
| 2 | Bulk Storage | Horton spheres / mounded bullets / above-ground bullets | IS 2825, ASME Sec VIII, OISD-144 |
| 3 | Transfer Pumping | LPG centrifugal / reciprocating pumps, vaporisers | API 2510, OISD-144 |
| 4 | Cylinder Filling | Carousel / in-line filling machines, electronic scales | IS 6044, OISD-144 |
| 5 | Cylinder Testing & Maintenance | Hydrostatic test rig, valve replacement bench | Gas Cylinder Rules (GCR 2016), IS 3196 |
| 6 | Storage of Filled Cylinders | Open-ventilated sheds with firewalls | OISD-144, OISD-118 |
| 7 | Dispatch | Cylinder inspection gate, loading bays | OISD-118 |
| 8 | Safety & Utility Systems | Fire water ring main, foam pourers, water spray, ESD, deluge, PA system | TAC norms, IS 15105 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All values in SI Units. KTU examiners expect unit awareness in every numerical answer.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Filling Density } (\rho_f) = \dfrac{\text{Mass of LPG in cylinder}}{\text{Water capacity of cylinder}}}
$$

- Typical value for **pure propane: $\rho_f = 0.42 \,\text{kg/L}$**
- Typical value for **pure butane: $\rho_f = 0.51 \,\text{kg/L}$**
- **For commercial LPG mixtures: $0.42 \le \rho_f \le 0.51 \,\text{kg/L}$**

$$
\boxed{\log_{10}(P) = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}}
$$

**Antoine Equation** for vapor pressure of LPG components (P in mmHg, T in °C).

$$
\boxed{\text{Hydrostatic Test Pressure } (P_h) = 1.5 \times P_{design}}
$$

For domestic LPG cylinders: $P_{design} = 2.0 \,\text{MPa} \Rightarrow P_h = 3.0 \,\text{MPa}$.

$$
\boxed{\text{Tare Weight Check: } (W_{gross} - W_{tare}) \in [W_{nominal} \pm 1\%]}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Time to Reach LFL} = \dfrac{V_{room}}{Q_{leak} \cdot \dfrac{C_{LFL}}{100}} \;\text{seconds}}
$$

Where $Q_{leak}$ = volumetric leak rate (m³/s); $V_{room}$ = enclosure volume; $C_{LFL}$ = Lower Flammability Limit (≈ **2.1 %** for propane).

$$
\boxed{\text{BLEVE Energy } (E) = \Delta H_{vap} \cdot m}
$$

Where $\Delta H_{vap}$ (propane) ≈ **425 kJ/kg**; $\Delta H_{vap}$ (butane) ≈ **385 kJ/kg**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Fire Water Demand: } Q_{FW} = \sum (q_i \cdot A_i)}
$$

Where $q_i$ = design water application density (L/min/m²); $A_i$ = exposed surface area (m²). For LPG vessels, **q = 10.2 L/min/m² (OISD-117)**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cascade filling logic** (used in 14.2 kg cylinder filling) leverages the pressure differential between bulk storage and cylinder — when the cylinder is at lower pressure, LPG flows in as liquid, then partial vaporizes.
- **Net weight verification** at the filling carousel uses **load-cell-based electronic scales** with automatic shut-off at the set-point, eliminating overfilling (a primary root cause of cylinder BLEVE incidents).
- **Gas Cylinder Rules 2016** mandate periodic hydrostatic testing of all domestic cylinders **every 5 years** — making the testing rig an indispensable asset.
- **ESD hierarchy (ESD-0 → ESD-1 → ESD-2 → ESD-3)** ensures phased shutdown — ESD-3 (local) → ESD-2 (filling shed) → ESD-1 (plant) → ESD-0 (terminal-wide).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Filling Density Constraint for a 14.2 kg Domestic LPG Cylinder

> **KTU 2024 Typical Exam Pattern:** "A 14.2 kg LPG cylinder has a water capacity of 33.0 litres. Determine the filling density and comment on its compliance with statutory norms."

**Given:**
- Cylinder water capacity: $V_w = 33.0 \,\text{L}$
- Nominal LPG mass: $m_{LPG} = 14.2 \,\text{kg}$
- LPG mixture: 60% Propane + 40% Butane (winter grade)
