**Step 1:** Apply the filling density formula directly.

$$
\rho_f = \dfrac{m_{LPG}}{V_w}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
\rho_f = \dfrac{14.2 \,\text{kg}}{33.0 \,\text{L}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerical result.

$$
\rho_f = 0.4303 \,\text{kg/L}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify against statutory envelope.

The applicable rule is **Gas Cylinder Rules, 2016 / IS 6044 (Part 1)** which stipulates:

$$
0.42 \le \rho_f \le 0.51 \,\text{kg/L}
$$

Since $0.42 \le 0.4303 \le 0.51$, **the cylinder is in compliance**.

**Step 5:** Cross-check via mixture rule (pure-component blending).

$$
\rho_{f,\text{mixture}} = x_P \cdot \rho_{f,P} + x_B \cdot \rho_{f,B}
$$

$$
\rho_{f,\text{mixture}} = (0.60)(0.42) + (0.40)(0.51)
$$

$$
\rho_{f,\text{mixture}} = 0.252 + 0.204 = 0.456 \,\text{kg/L}
$$

**Step 6:** Reconcile.

The mixture's intrinsic $\rho_f$ of 0.456 kg/L is the **upper permissible loading limit**, while 0.4303 kg/L is the **actual loaded value** — confirming **~6 % safety margin** is preserved against liquid-phase thermal expansion in the unlikely event the cylinder is exposed to 55 °C ambient.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: BLEVE Threshold Energy for a Filled Domestic Cylinder

> **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectation:** "Compute the BLEVE release energy if a fully loaded 14.2 kg domestic LPG cylinder suffers catastrophic failure."

**Given:**
- LPG mass: $m = 14.2 \,\text{kg}$
- LPG composition (assume pure propane for worst case): $\Delta H_{vap} = 425 \,\text{kJ/kg}$

**Step 1:** Define BLEVE released energy.

$$
E_{BLEVE} = m \cdot \Delta H_{vap}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
E_{BLEVE} = 14.2 \times 425
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate.

$$
E_{BLEVE} = 6035 \,\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to TNT-equivalent for blast radius estimation.

$$
M_{TNT} = \dfrac{E_{BLEVE}}{4500 \,\text{kJ/kg}}
$$

$$
M_{TNT} = \dfrac{6035}{4500} = 1.34 \,\text{kg TNT equivalent}
$$

**Step 5:** Estimate blast overpressure zone using the **Cube-Root Law**.

$$
R = k \cdot \sqrt[3]{M_{TNT}}
$$

For 2 psi overpressure (window-shattering threshold), $k \approx 18$ (Hopkins-Cube Root).

$$
R = 18 \cdot \sqrt[3]{1.34}
$$

$$
R = 18 \cdot 1.1005 = 19.81 \,\text{m}
$$

**Interpretation:** A single domestic LPG cylinder BLEVE can shatter windows and cause masonry damage up to **~20 m radius** — justifying OISD-118 prescribed inter-distance of **15 m** from the bulk storage to the nearest boundary wall.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: HAZOP Nodes in LPG Bottling Plant

| Node | Intent | Deviations | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk LPG unloading | Transfer liquid LPG from road tanker to bullet | No/Low flow | Blocked valve, pump trip | Hose rupture, gas cloud | Excess flow valve, ESD, gas detector |
| Cylinder filling carousel | Fill 14.2 kg into each cylinder | Over-fill | Scale drift, valve seat leak | Hydraulic over-pressure, BLEVE on exposure to fire | Over-fill cut-off (load cell interlock), flame arrester, water spray |
| Filled cylinder storage | Hold filled cylinders in shed | Fire / explosion | External ignition source | Pool fire → BLEVE cascade | Sprinkler deluge, fire wall, vented shed roof, OISD inter-distance |
| Cylinder dispatch bay | Load cylinders onto delivery trucks | Mechanical impact | Forklift collision | Valve damage, leak | Speed governors, anti-collision sensors, PPE |
| Bulk storage sphere | Hold 500–3000 MT LPG | Loss of containment | Corrosion, weld defect | Major pool fire, vapor cloud explosion | Hydrostatic test every 5 yrs, NDT, cathodic protection, ESD |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Process Flow of an LPG Bottling Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TANKER UNLOADING BAY] --> B[EARTHING + VAPOUR RETURN]
    B --> C[BULK LPG BULLET STORAGE]
    C --> D[LPG TRANSFER PUMP]
    D --> E[VAPOURISER HEAT EXCHANGER]
    E --> F[CYLINDER FILLING CAROUSEL]
    F --> G[LEAK TEST + TARE CHECK]
    G --> H[FILLED CYLINDER STORAGE SHED]
    H --> I[CYLINDER INSPECTION GATE]
    I --> J[DISPATCH BAY + LOADING]
    C --> K[SAFETY SYSTEMS BLOCK]
    F --> K
    H --> K
    K --> K1[ESD HIERARCHY]
    K --> K2[GAS DETECTION]
    K --> K3[FIRE WATER RING MAIN]
    K --> K4[FOAM POURERS]
```

**Reading the Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture):**

- **Left-to-Right Material Flow:** Bulk LPG enters at the tanker bay, passes through earthing and vapour return manifolds (preventing static-induced ignition during transfer), then enters the bullet sphere.
- **Pump-Vaporiser-Carousel Sub-Loop:** Liquid LPG is pressurized and vaporised to controlled pressure before entering the filling carousel, where **load-cell-based electronic scales** govern individual cylinder cut-off.
- **Top-Right Safety Cluster:** Three parallel safety branches — *ESD Hierarchy*, *Gas Detection*, and *Fire Water + Foam* — are wired into every primary asset. Any credible upset triggers an integrated shutdown.

---

### 4.2 ESD Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[OPERATOR LOCAL PANEL] --> L2[ESD 3 LOCAL ISOLATION]
    L2 --> L3[ESD 2 SHED LEVEL SHUTDOWN]
    L3 --> L4[ESD 1 PLANT WIDE SHUTDOWN]
    L4 --> L5[ESD 0 TERMINAL WIDE BLACK START]
    L2 --> S1[CAROUSEL STOP]
    L2 --> S2[PUMP TRIP]
    L3 --> S3[STORAGE VALVE CLOSE]
    L3 --> S4[VENT VALVE OPEN]
    L4 --> S5[FIRE WATER PUMP START]
    L4 --> S6[FOOM CHARGING VALVE OPEN]
```

**Functional Logic of the Hierarchy:**

- **ESD-3 (Local):** Operator push-button at the carousel — *immediate* isolation of the cylinder under fill.
- **ESD-2 (Shed Level):** Triggered by gas detection within the filling shed → stops carousel, closes block valves on storage feed lines.
- **ESD-1 (Plant Level):** Triggered by hydrocarbon cloud spreading beyond the shed → activates foam pourers, fire water pumps, and starts emergency evacuation.
- **ESD-0 (Terminal Black Start):** All power de-energized except intrinsically safe instrumentation — last-resort manual containment.

---

### 4.3 Cylinder Filling Carousel — Sequential Logic

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as OPERATOR
    participant Cyl as EMPTY CYLINDER
    participant Sc as ELECTRONIC SCALE
    participant Vl as FILL VALVE
    participant Pump as LPG SUPPLY PUMP
    Op->>Cyl: Place cylinder on carousel
    Cyl->>Sc: Tare weight registration
    Sc-->>Op: Weight confirmed within tolerance
    Op->>Vl: OPEN fill valve command
    Vl->>Pump: Request flow
    Pump-->>Vl: LPG flows at controlled rate
    Vl->>Sc: Continuous weight feedback
    Sc-->>Vl: Trigger CLOSE at set point
    Vl-->>Op: AUTO SHUT OFF at 14.2 kg net
    Op->>Cyl: Cylinder removed for leak test
```

**Operational Highlights:**
- The **load-cell-to-valve interlock** is the single most critical control loop — failure here is the most cited root cause in historical cylinder BLEVE accident reports.
- A **dual-path shutdown** (mechanical excess-flow valve + electronic load cell) provides defence-in-depth.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Pattern]**

**Q1.** Define **LPG Bottling Plant** as per OISD-144 and list **four** statutory codes that govern its design and operation.

**Model Answer:**
An LPG Bottling Plant is a hydrocarbon handling facility designed for bulk receipt, storage, and filling of LPG into portable pressure cylinders, conforming to OISD-144. The four governing codes are:
1. **OISD-144** — Design and Layout of LPG Bottling Plants
2. **SMPV Rules** — Static and Mobile Pressure Vessels (Unfired)
3. **Gas Cylinder Rules 2016** — Periodic testing and certification of cylinders
4. **OISD-118** — Layout criteria for oil and gas installations

**[3 Marks Distribution: 1 definition + 4 codes = correct and complete]**

---

**Q2.** Why is **statutory inter-distance** prescribed between the bulk storage vessel and the nearest property boundary in an LPG plant? What minimum distance does OISD recommend for a 1000 m³ Horton sphere?

**Model Answer:**
Inter-distance limits the **thermal radiation exposure** and **blast overpressure** on surrounding populations in the event of a BLEVE or pool fire from the bulk storage. For a 1000 m³ Horton sphere, OISD-118 prescribes a minimum inter-distance of **~30 m** from the nearest property boundary (exact value depends on sphere design pressure and population density of the surrounding zone).
**[3 Marks: 2 marks rationale + 1 mark specific value with caveat]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat process flow block diagram, describe the **end-to-end operations** of an LPG Bottling Plant starting from bulk receipt to dispatch of filled cylinders. Highlight the **safety interlocks** at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A domestic LPG cylinder has a water capacity of **33.0 litres** and is filled with **14.2 kg** of LPG (60% propane + 40% butane). Verify whether the filling density complies with statutory norms and determine the maximum permissible loading mass. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A**

**(a) End-to-End Operations Description:**

1. **Bulk Receipt:** LPG tankers are received at the unloading bay. Earth-reels are connected before hose-coupling to prevent static discharge. Vapour return lines equalize pressure.
2. **Bulk Storage:** Liquid LPG is transferred to Horton spheres (typical 500–3000 MT capacity) at ~0.7–1.7 MPa.
3. **Pumping:** Centrifugal or vane pumps move LPG through the vaporiser to the filling carousel.
4. **Cylinder Filling:** Empty cylinders are placed on the carousel. Load-cell based electronic scales dispense LPG to within ±1 % of 14.2 kg net. Auto-shut-off valves engage at the set-point.
5. **Leak Test & Tare Check:** Each cylinder is checked for valve leakage using a soap-bubble or detector fluid.
6. **Storage of Filled Cylinders:** Cylinders are stored in open, well-ventilated sheds with firewall separation from bulk storage.
7. **Dispatch:** Cylinders undergo final inspection (seal, date stamp, valve cap) before loading onto delivery trucks.

**Safety interlocks at each stage:**
- Earth-reel continuity check at unloading
- Excess-flow valves at storage outlet
- Load-cell-based overfill cut-off at carousel
- Hydrostatic test every 5 years
- Gas detectors with auto-isolation
- Fire water ring main with auto-deluge

**(b) Numerical Verification:**

Filling density:

$$
\rho_f = \dfrac{m}{V_w} = \dfrac{14.2}{33.0} = 0.4303 \,\text{kg/L}
$$

Maximum permissible loading using the lower bound $\rho_f = 0.42 \,\text{kg/L}$:

$$
m_{max} = 0.42 \times 33.0 = 13.86 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Observation:** The cylinder is **loaded beyond the lower bound** (which corresponds to a worst-case pure propane composition), but **below the upper bound** (0.51 kg/L for pure butane). Since the mixture rule yields:

$$
\rho_{f,\text{mixture}} = (0.60)(0.42) + (0.40)(0.51) = 0.456 \,\text{kg/L}
$$

and $0.4303 < 0.456$, **the cylinder is in compliance** with the mixture-specific limit. Maximum safe loading ≈ **15.05 kg** (rounded per plant SOP).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **HAZOP methodology** with reference to two critical nodes in an LPG Bottling Plant. Identify at least **four guide words** with deviations, causes, and safeguards for each node. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1000 m³ Horton sphere contains 70% propane and 30% butane by mass at 25 °C. Compute the **vapor pressure** of the mixture using Raoult's Law and comment on the safety implications if the sphere suffers a fire exposure. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B**

**(a) HAZOP Methodology (Brief):** HAZOP is a structured, team-based risk-assessment technique that uses **guide words** (No/Not, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than) to interrogate each node (equipment + section) of a process flow. For LPG plants, critical nodes include *Bulk Storage*, *Pumping*, and *Filling Carousel*.

**Node 1: Bulk Storage Sphere**
- *Less Flow* — Causes: Pump trip / blocked valve. Consequences: Pressure surge → relief valve lift. Safeguards: Burst disc + pressure safety valve (PSV).
- *More Pressure* — Causes: Sun exposure / vaporiser failure. Safeguards: PSV set at 1.5 MPa, water spray cooling.
- *As Well As* (contaminants): Water in LPG → hydrate formation. Safeguards: Drier unit, drain valves.
- *Reverse Flow*: Vapour return backflow → wrong product mix. Safeguards: Check valves, slug catchers.

**Node 2: Filling Carousel**
- *More Flow* — Overfill due to scale drift → BLEVE risk. Safeguards: Dual cut-off (load cell + mechanical excess-flow valve).
- *No Flow* — Empty cylinder retained in dispatch → customer complaint. Safeguards: Cycle counter interlock.
- *Other Than* — Cylinder of wrong size on carousel. Safeguards: RFID / barcode scanning before fill.
- *Part Of* — Valve seat partially open → slow leak. Safeguards: Daily valve torque audit.

**(b) Vapor Pressure via Raoult's Law:**

Given:
- Sphere volume: $V = 1000 \,\text{m}^3$
- Mass fractions: $w_P = 0.70$, $w_B = 0.30$
- T = 25 °C

**Vapor pressures of pure components at 25 °C:**
- $P_P^{sat} = 0.957 \,\text{MPa}$ (propane)
- $P_B^{sat} = 0.244 \,\text{MPa}$ (butane)

**Molecular weights:** $M_P = 44.1 \,\text{g/mol}$, $M_B = 58.1 \,\text{g/mol}$.

**Mole fractions:**

$$
x_P = \dfrac{w_P/M_P}{w_P/M_P + w_B/M_B}
$$

$$
x_P = \dfrac{0.70/44.1}{0.70/44.1 + 0.30/58.1} = \dfrac{0.01587}{0.01587 + 0.00516}
$$

$$
x_P = \dfrac{0.01587}{0.02103} = 0.7546
$$

$$
x_B = 1 - 0.7546 = 0.2454
$$

**Total vapor pressure:**

$$
P_{total} = x_P \cdot P_P^{sat} + x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}
$$

$$
P_{total} = (0.7546)(0.957) + (0.2454)(0.244)
$$

$$
P_{total} = 0.7222 + 0.0599 = 0.7821 \,\text{MPa}
$$

**Safety Implication under Fire Exposure:**

If the sphere is engulfed in pool fire, wall temperature rises to ~600 °C in 5–10 minutes. With vapor pressure scaling roughly **exponentially with temperature (Antoine form)**, the vessel pressure will rapidly exceed the PSV set point of ~1.5 MPa, leading to:

1. **PSV lift-off** → vapor cloud generation → flash fire risk.
2. If the fire is sustained without water spray cooling, the **shell metal** loses yield strength beyond 550 °C → **catastrophic BLEVE**.
3. The calculated 0.7821 MPa is the *cold* saturation pressure — under fire exposure, this multiplies by **2–3× within minutes**, demanding immediate deluge activation (water spray density 10.2 L/min/m² per OISD-117).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mole-to-mass conversion** in Raoult's Law — students often use mass fractions directly, losing ~2 marks.
> 2. **Ignoring the statutory lower-bound** of $\rho_f = 0.42 \,\text{kg/L}$ when verifying filling density — KTU examiners specifically look for the **dual-bound check** (lower + upper).
> 3. **Confusing OISD-117** (fire protection) **with OISD-144** (bottling plant design) — citations matter.
> 4. **Skipping the unit declaration** in final numerical answers — always write "$\rho_f = 0.4303 \,\text{kg/L}$" not just "0.4303".
> 5. In HAZOP questions, students often describe *consequences* but **omit the safeguard column** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks systematically.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** LPG Bottling Plant = Bulk receipt + Storage + Filling + Testing + Storage + Dispatch of LPG into portable cylinders (OISD-144).
- **LPG Composition:** Propane (C₃H₈) + Butane (C₄H₁₀); winter grade richer in propane, summer grade richer in butane.
- **Critical Physical Constants:** Auto-ignition 410–550 °C; LFL 2.1 %; UFL 9.5 %; vapor density ≈ 1.5–2.0 × air; BP of propane −42 °C.
- **Filling Density Envelope:** $0.42 \le \rho_f \le 0.51 \,\text{kg/L}$ (statutory).
- **Cylinder Test Pressure:** Hydrostatic = **3.0 MPa** for domestic cylinders (1.5 × design).
- **Storage Capacity Range:** Horton sphere 500–3000 MT; mounded bullets gaining preference (OISD-144).
- **ESD Hierarchy:** ESD-3 → ESD-2 → ESD-1 → ESD-0.
- **Safety Systems:** Fire water ring main, foam pourers, water spray deluge, gas detectors, flame detectors, ESD.
- **Statutory Acts:** Gas Cylinder Rules 2016, SMPV Rules, Petroleum Rules 2002, Factories Act 1948, OISD-118, OISD-144, OISD-117, OISD-150.
- **HAZOP Guide Words:** No, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than.
- **BLEVE Energy:** $E = m \cdot \Delta H_{vap}$; 14.2 kg cylinder ≈ **6035 kJ** ≈ **1.34 kg TNT-eq**.
- **Fire Water Demand:** 10.2 L/min/m² for LPG vessels (OISD-117).
- **Five-Year Rule:** Domestic cylinders hydrostatically tested every 5 years.
- **Inter-Distance:** OISD-118 prescribes 15–30 m between bulk storage and boundary.
- **PESO:** Apex regulator for design, fabrication, and inspection of all pressure vessels in India.
- **Cylinder Carousel Safety:** Dual cut-off (electronic load cell + mechanical excess-flow valve) is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Philosophies
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Philosophies

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Design Philosophies

In the context of safety engineering for hydrocarbon processing industries, a **Design Philosophy** is a formally documented, top-down strategic framework that governs the conceptualization, engineering, and operational lifecycle of a facility such that risks to personnel, environment, assets, and the public are reduced to a level deemed **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** under the **HSE (Health, Safety & Environment)** mandate.

A design philosophy is not a single engineering calculation; rather, it is a *governing document* that specifies **how** the process plant, storage terminals, pipelines, and offshore platforms shall be conceived, which **standards** shall govern them (e.g., API, ASME, NFPA, IS, OISD), and what **layers of protection** shall be built into the design to manage the inherent hazards of flammable hydrocarbons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 3):**
> Students must understand that a "Design Philosophy" is the *first* deliverable produced during the Conceptual / Front-End Engineering Design (FEED) stage of any hydrocarbon project. It governs everything that follows — P&ID development, equipment sizing, layout plot plans, fire protection, and emergency shutdown logic.

The four classical pillars universally accepted in hydrocarbon industries are:
1. **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)**
2. **Risk-Based Design (RBD)**
3. **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)**
4. **Performance-Based / Prescriptive (Codes-Based) Design**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swiss-Cheese Layered Cake"

Imagine you are driving a high-performance sports car on a busy Indian national highway. You know the road has blind corners, stray cattle, and unpredictable truck drivers. To protect yourself, you do **not** rely on a single safeguard:

- You wear a **seatbelt** (primary safeguard)
- The car has **airbags** (secondary safeguard)
- The car has **anti-lock brakes** (tertiary safeguard)
- You drive **defensively** (operational safeguard)
- The highway has **median dividers** (engineering safeguard)
- The hospital is nearby with a **trauma center** (emergency response safeguard)

Each safeguard, like a slice of Swiss cheese, has *holes* (failures). But because you have **multiple independent layers**, the chance of a hazard penetrating all layers simultaneously is astronomically low. This is the philosophical foundation of **Defense-in-Depth**, and it is *exactly* how hydrocarbon plant designers think about containing a release of LPG or crude oil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — ALARP:** "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" is the legal and ethical cornerstone of process safety design, originating from the UK *Edwards v. National Coal Board* (1948) case, and adopted by KTU-referenced standards such as **OISD-STD-118** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119**.

---

## 1.3 Why Design Philosophies Exist in Hydrocarbon Industries

Hydrocarbons (methane, propane, butane, naphtha, gasoline, diesel, crude oil, etc.) possess severe intrinsic hazards:
- **Flammability** (lower flammability limit of propane ≈ **2.1% vol**)
- **Explosivity** (deflagration index for gasoline ≈ **>300 bar·m/s**)
- **Toxicity** (H₂S in sour crude — **IDLH = 100 ppm**)
- **Cryogenic nature** (LNG at **−162 °C**)
- **High-pressure storage** (LPG bullets up to **~17 bar**, ethylene spheres up to **~20 bar**)

Because the *inventory* of energy stored in such facilities can catastrophically release, a structured design philosophy is the only way to ensure consistent, auditable, and legally defensible safety decisions across thousands of engineering deliverables.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Major Design Philosophies

| # | Philosophy | Core Tenet | Best Application |
|---|-----------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** | Eliminate or reduce the hazard *itself*, not its consequences | Greenfield projects, new process selection |
| 2 | **Risk-Based Design (RBD)** | Quantify risk; design to a tolerable risk target (e.g., 10⁻⁵/yr fatality) | Offshore platforms, dense urban refineries |
| 3 | **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** | Multiple, independent protective layers (IPL) | Refineries, petrochemical complexes, pipeline terminals |
| 4 | **Prescriptive / Codes-Based** | Strict adherence to published standards (OISD, NFPA, IS) | Brownfield expansions, regulatory-driven designs |
| 5 | **Performance-Based Design** | Meet defined safety objectives; freedom of engineering solution | Novel processes, FPSOs, LNG carriers |
| 6 | **ALARP-based Design** | Reduce risk until "gross disproportion" test fails | UK / EU / Indian regulatory framework |

> [!TIP]
> In real projects, these philosophies are **not mutually exclusive**. A modern KTU-industry-aligned engineer blends **ISD + DiD + ALARP** into one cohesive design basis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Philosophy 1 — Inherently Safer Design (ISD)

ISD was popularized by **Trevor Kletz** (a KTU-referenced authority) in his landmark 1978 paper *"What You Don't Have, Can't Leak."* It contains four pillars:

### 2.1.1 The Four Pillars of ISD (Kletz's Hierarchy)

1. **Minimize (Inventory Reduction):**
   - Use smaller vessels, fewer intermediates, smaller pipe inventories.
   - *Mathematical basis:* the leak rate $Q$ from a hole of area $A_h$ is:
   $$Q = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\rho}}$$
   - Smaller inventory → smaller $V$ released → lower consequence.

2. **Substitute:**
   - Replace a hazardous material with a less hazardous one.
   - Example: Use aqueous ammonia instead of anhydrous; use methanol instead of HF as alkylation catalyst.

3. **Moderate (Attenuation):**
   - Operate at lower temperature and pressure.
   - Use refrigerated storage instead of pressurized ambient LPG.

4. **Simplify:**
   - Reduce complexity. Fewer fittings → fewer leak paths.
   - "Friendlier" plant layout → easier operator intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of ISD:** *Elimination > Prevention > Mitigation > Control.* You cannot have a leak of a substance that does not exist in your plant.

## 2.2 Philosophy 2 — Risk-Based Design (RBD)

Risk is defined formally as the **triple product**:

$$R = f \times C \times E$$

Where:
- $f$ = **Frequency** of the hazardous event (per year)
- $C$ = **Consequence** magnitude (fatalities, financial loss, environmental damage)
- $E$ = **Exposure** factor (number of people exposed, area affected)

The internationally accepted individual fatality risk (IR) targets in hydrocarbon industries are:

| Risk Band | Frequency (per year) | Action Required |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Negligible** | $< 10^{-7}$ | Accept — no action |
| **ALARP Region** | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-5}$ | Reduce if reasonably practicable |
| **Tolerable upper limit** | $10^{-5}$ | Mandatory reduction or shutdown |
| **Intolerable** | $> 10^{-5}$ | Operation prohibited |

### 2.2.1 Societal Risk — F-N Curve

For multiple fatalities, risk is plotted on an **F-N curve**:

$$F \cdot N^a = C$$

Where:
- $F$ = cumulative frequency of $N$ or more fatalities
- $a$ = aversion factor (typically **a = 1** for neutral, **a = 2** for highly averse)
- $C$ = constant (position of the line)

## 2.3 Philosophy 3 — Defense-in-Depth (DiD) — The Onion Model

DiD is the IEC-61511 / API-RP-754 concept of **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)**. Each IPL must have:
- **Specificity:** reduces risk from one specific cause
- **Independence:** does not share components with other layers
- **Dependability:** Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) is known and bounded

| IPL Layer | Function | Typical PFD |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Layer 1** — Process Design | Prevent upset by robust design | (Passive) |
| **Layer 2** — Basic Control System (BPCS) | Maintain within operating window | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| **Layer 3** — Critical Alarms + Operator | Operator intervention | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| **Layer 4** — Automatic SIS / ESD | Independent shutdown system | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| **Layer 5** — Mechanical Devices (PSV, Rupture Disc) | Physical relief | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| **Layer 6** — Fire & Gas Detection + Deluge | Active mitigation | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| **Layer 7** — Emergency Response, Bunds, Dikes | Post-release containment | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PFD — Probability of Failure on Demand:** When a hazardous demand occurs, what is the probability that this layer *fails* to perform its function? Lower PFD = stronger layer.

The total mitigated frequency $f_{mitigated}$ for a sequence with $n$ IPLs in series is:

$$f_{mitigated} = f_{initiating} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$

## 2.4 Philosophy 4 — ALARP

The **"Reasonably Practicable"** test (UK HSE / Indian OISD framework):

$$\text{Cost of further risk reduction} \stackrel{?}{\leq} \text{Gross disproportion to the risk being avoided}$$

Or mathematically stated:

$$\frac{\text{Cost of risk reduction (in monetary units)}}{\text{Risk reduction achieved (in statistical lives saved)}} \leq k$$

where $k$ is a societal "disutility of money" factor (commonly **k = 1 × 10⁶ £ per statistical life saved** for low risk, decreasing as risk increases).

## 2.5 Philosophy 5 — Prescriptive (Codes-Based) Design

This is the *traditional* engineering approach. The designer follows **prescriptive clauses** of standards such as:
- **API 650** — Welded tanks for oil storage
- **API 2510** — Design and Construction of LPG Installations
- **OISD-STD-117** — Fire Protection Facilities for Petroleum Refineries and Oil/Gas Processing Plants
- **OISD-STD-118** — Layouts for Oil and Gas Installations
- **NFPA 30** — Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code
- **IS 4379** — Identification of the contents of industrial gas cylinders

> [!WARNING]
> A *major KTU pitfall:* students often treat "design philosophy" as merely "follow the codes." **Following the codes alone is NOT a design philosophy** — it is only one tool used to *implement* a philosophy.

## 2.6 Philosophy 6 — Performance-Based Design

The designer specifies **what outcome** must be achieved (e.g., "the pool fire shall not reach neighbouring equipment within 30 minutes"), and is free to choose the engineering solution. Common in offshore and LNG facilities where prescriptive codes are silent.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| Leak rate (liquid) | $Q = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}$ | $C_d$ ≈ 0.61 (discharge coeff.), $A_h$ in m², $\Delta P$ in Pa, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $Q$ in m³/s |
| Leak rate (gas) | $Q = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot P \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma}{\rho RT} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$ | $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats |
| Risk | $R = f \times C$ | dimensionless product |
| Mitigated frequency | $f_{mit} = f_{init} \cdot \prod PFD_i$ | per year |
| F-N Curve | $F \cdot N^a = C$ | $a$ = 1 (neutral) to 2 (averse) |
| ALARP test | $\frac{\Delta C_{risk}}{N_{saved}} \leq k$ | monetary / statistical life |
| Pool fire radius | $r = \sqrt{\frac{\dot{m}}{\pi \cdot m_b}}$ | $m_b$ ≈ 0.05 kg/m²/s for gasoline |
| Flash fraction | $f_v = \frac{C_p (T_{liq} - T_{bp})}{H_v}$ | dimensionless, 0 ≤ $f_v$ ≤ 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention for KTU:** Use **SI units throughout**. Pressure in Pa (or bar with explicit conversion: 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa). Temperature in Kelvin for gas equations, Celsius for storage discussions.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Dominant Philosophy |
|----------------|-----------------------------|
| Onshore Refinery (India) | Prescriptive (OISD) + DiD + ALARP |
| Offshore Platform (Mumbai High) | Risk-Based + Performance-Based |
| LNG Terminal (Dahej, Kochi) | Performance-Based + ISD + DiD |
| Cross-Country Pipeline (GAIL, IOCL) | Prescriptive (PNGRB) + DiD |
| Petrochemical Complex (Jamnagar) | Risk-Based + ISD + Prescriptive |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bernoulli-Based Liquid Leak Rate

Consider a pressurized hydrocarbon liquid vessel at gauge pressure $P_g$ discharging through a sharp-edged orifice of diameter $d$ into the atmosphere.

**Step 1 — Apply steady-state mechanical energy balance (Bernoulli) between the vessel interior (point 1) and the leak exit (point 2):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that the liquid surface velocity $v_1 \approx 0$ (large vessel), and assume a horizontal leak ($z_1 = z_2$):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$

**Step 3 — Express the head loss $h_L$ as a fraction of the velocity head using a coefficient of discharge $C_d$:**

$$h_L = \frac{v_2^2}{2g} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{C_d^2} - 1\right)$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$v_2 = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert velocity to volumetric flow rate using the orifice area $A_h = \pi d^2 / 4$:**

$$Q = v_2 \cdot A_h = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

This is the standard **liquid leak rate equation** used in OISD-GDN-206 consequence analysis worksheets.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gas Leak Rate (Critical / Choked Flow)

For a gas under high pressure discharging to atmosphere, the maximum mass flow rate occurs at **choked (sonic) conditions** at the orifice throat.

**Step 1 — Write the isentropic compressible flow equation (compressible Bernoulli):**

$$\frac{v^2}{2} + \int \frac{dP}{\rho} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2 — Use isentropic relation $P / \rho^\gamma = \text{const}$ to integrate:**

$$\int \frac{dP}{\rho} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{P}{\rho}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the choked-flow criterion (Mach number M = 1 at the throat):**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{2}{\gamma + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Combine to get the **choked gas mass flow rate**:**

$$\dot{m}_{choked} = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R T_1} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> For methane ($\gamma$ = 1.31, $R$ = 518 J/kg·K), this gives the constant $\approx 0.83$. For propane ($\gamma$ = 1.13), the constant $\approx 0.69$. KTU examiners love testing these constants.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mitigated Event Frequency

Consider a process unit with one initiating cause of frequency $f_{init}$ and **three** independent protection layers (IPL-1, IPL-2, IPL-3) downstream.

**Step 1 — Define the event tree branches:**

$$f_{demands} = f_{init}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of failure of each layer in sequence:**

The probability that the hazard propagates past Layer 1 is $PFD_1$.
Past Layers 1 AND 2: $PFD_1 \times PFD_2$.
Past Layers 1 AND 2 AND 3: $PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3$.

**Step 3 — Multiply by initial frequency:**

$$f_{mitigated} = f_{init} \cdot PFD_1 \cdot PFD_2 \cdot PFD_3$$

**Step 4 — Generalized form for $n$ IPLs:**

$$f_{mitigated} = f_{init} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$

**Numerical KTU Example:**
Let $f_{init}$ = $1 \times 10^{-1}$ /yr (heat exchanger tube rupture once every 10 years). Three IPLs with PFDs of $10^{-2}$, $10^{-2}$, $10^{-3}$ respectively:

$$f_{mitigated} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-8} \text{ /yr}$$

This is in the **negligible** band (< 10⁻⁷) → **ALARP demonstrated.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ALARP Cost-Benefit Test

Suppose a refinery proposes a $₹$ 50 crore upgrade to a flare system that reduces individual risk by $1 \times 10^{-6}$ per year for 50 exposed workers.

**Step 1 — Calculate total statistical lives saved over the plant life (assume 25-year plant life):**

$$\Delta N_{lives} = \Delta IR \times N_{exposed} \times T_{life} = (10^{-6}) \times 50 \times 25 = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ lives}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate cost per statistical life saved:**

$$\text{Cost} / \text{life} = \frac{₹ 50 \text{ Cr}}{1.25 \times 10^{-3}} = ₹ 4 \times 10^{10} \text{ per life} = ₹ 400 \text{ Crore per life}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the gross disproportion test:**

For an IR already at $10^{-5}$/yr, the "disproportion factor" $k \approx 3$ (per UK HSE R2P2 framework). A cost per statistical life of ₹400 Crore *vastly exceeds* 3× the statistical value of a life (commonly ~ ₹5 Crore in Indian regulatory practice).

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$\frac{₹ 4 \times 10^{10}}{₹ 5 \times 10^7} = 800 \gg 3$$

Therefore, the proposed upgrade **fails the ALARP cost-benefit test** and is **NOT reasonably practicable** to implement — the existing design is acceptable.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Philosophies

| Dimension | ISD | RBD | DiD | Prescriptive | Performance-Based |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|--------------|-------------------|
| **Cost** | Lowest (lifecycle) | Moderate | High | Lowest (capex) | Moderate |
| **Flexibility** | Low (process-locked) | High | Moderate | Low (code-locked) | Highest |
| **Auditability** | Medium | Highest | High | Highest | Lowest |
| **Innovation** | Encouraged | Encouraged | Neutral | Discouraged | Encouraged |
| **Best Stage** | Conceptual | FEED / Detailed | Detailed | All stages | FEED |
| **Failure Mode if Misapplied** | May oversimplify | Numerical gaming | Layer proliferation | Brittle to new hazards | Subjective acceptance |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem — F-N Curve Point

A hydrocarbon pipeline rupture has a frequency of $5 \times 10^{-5}$/yr. Each event has a probability 0.3 of killing 1 person and 0.05 of killing 5 people.

**Step 1 — Compute frequency for N ≥ 1:**

$$F(1) = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times (0.3 + 0.05) = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.35 = 1.75 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2 — Compute frequency for N ≥ 5:**

$$F(5) = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.05 = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 3 — Verify against tolerable limit:**

Both points lie *above* the standard $10^{-5}$/yr line for **N = 1** → ALARP demonstration required via additional IPL (e.g., thicker pipe wall, better isolation valves).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Defense-in-Depth "Onion" Model (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hazard[Hazard Source eg LPG Pool]
    L1[Layer 1 Process Design Inherently Safer]
    L2[Layer 2 Basic Control System BPCS]
    L3[Layer 3 Operator Alarm Intervention]
    L4[Layer 4 Safety Instrumented System SIS ESD]
    L5[Layer 5 Mechanical Relief PSV Rupture Disc]
    L6[Layer 6 Fire and Gas Detection Deluge]
    L7[Layer 7 Emergency Response Bunds Dikes]

    hazard --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7 --> outcome[Residual Risk Acceptable]
```

> Each block represents an **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)**. The hazard must penetrate *all* layers simultaneously for an uncontrolled outcome — the probability of which is the product of all PFDs.

---

## 4.2 Risk-Based Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Hazard] --> B[Estimate Frequency f per yr]
    B --> C[Estimate Consequence C fatalities damage]
    C --> D[Compute Risk R f times C]
    D --> E{R Greater than 10 power minus 5}
    E -- Yes --> F[STOP Intolerable Redesign Required]
    E -- No --> G{R between 10 power minus 7 and 10 power minus 5}
    G -- Yes --> H[Enter ALARP Region Apply Cost Benefit Test]
    G -- No --> I[Negligible Risk Accept Document]
    H --> J{ALARP Test Passed}
    J -- Yes --> K[Implement Risk Reduction Measure]
    J -- No --> I
```

---

## 4.3 Inherently Safer Design (Kletz) Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Eliminate Hazard Most Effective] --> B[Substitute Less Hazardous Material]
    B --> C[Minimize Reduce Inventory]
    C --> D[Moderate Lower T and P]
    D --> E[Simplify Reduce Complexity Least Effective of ISD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrow direction indicates the **order of preference**, with **Elimination at the top of the hierarchy**. Always attempt higher-tier ISD options before falling to Simplify.

---

## 4.4 ALARP Cost-Benefit Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Risk Reduction Option Proposed] --> q1[Estimate Cost of Implementation C impl]
    q1 --> q2[Estimate Reduction in Risk Delta R]
    q2 --> q3[Compute Cost per Statistical Life Saved CSLS]
    q3 --> test{CSLS less than Disutility Factor k}
    test -- Yes --> yes[Implement ALARP Demonstrated]
    test -- No --> no[Reject Option Existing Risk Tolerable]
```

---

## 4.5 Overall Design Philosophy Integration Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ISD[Inherently Safer Design] --> FEED[FEED Stage Decision]
    RBD[Risk Based Design] --> FEED
    Perf[Performance Based Design] --> FEED
    FEED --> DiD[Defense in Depth IPL Allocation]
    DiD --> Codes[Prescriptive Codes Verification]
    Codes --> ALARP[ALARP Demonstration]
    ALARP --> Construct[Detailed Engineering and Construction]
    Construct --> Operate[Operations and Maintenance]
    Operate --> Modify[Management of Change MOC]
    Modify --> ISD
```

> This is the **lifecycle integration** of all philosophies. Note how ISD re-enters at the *Management of Change* stage — every plant modification must revisit the original design philosophy.
