# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define Inherently Safer Design (ISD). List its four pillars in order of priority.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Inherently Safer Design, as conceptualized by Trevor Kletz, is a design philosophy that seeks to **eliminate or reduce hazards at the source** rather than add protective equipment to control them. It is based on the principle that *"what you don't have, can't leak."*

The four pillars in priority order are:
1. **Minimize** — Reduce inventory of hazardous materials
2. **Substitute** — Replace hazardous substances with less hazardous ones
3. **Moderate** — Use less severe operating conditions (lower T, P)
4. **Simplify** — Reduce plant complexity and human error opportunities

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correct ordered listing.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is ALARP? State its significance in hydrocarbon facility design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ALARP stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Practicable."** It is a risk management principle requiring operators to reduce risks until the **cost of further reduction becomes grossly disproportionate** to the safety benefit achieved.

**Significance:**
- Provides a **legal and engineering balance** between safety and economic feasibility
- Mandated under UK HSE, Indian OISD-STD-118, and offshore safety case regulations
- Justifies design decisions during regulatory audits
- Ensures continuous improvement via the **gross disproportion test**

*(Award: 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Options (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** philosophy in detail with reference to hydrocarbon storage tanks. Draw the layered protection model and explain the concept of **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** with **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal LPG bullet of volume **100 m³** is filled to **80%** capacity with propane at **17 bar (g)** and **30 °C**. A **25 mm** diameter leak develops at the bottom flange. Using the discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.61$ and propane density $\rho = 493$ kg/m³:
1. Calculate the mass leak rate in kg/s.
2. If a deluge system activates with **20-second** delay and a single isolation valve has PFD = **0.05**, compute the **total mass released** assuming the isolation valve fails.
3. Comment on whether this is acceptable under ALARP. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Defense-in-Depth:**

The Defense-in-Depth philosophy applies **multiple, independent, and diverse** safety layers so that failure of any single layer does not result in an uncontrolled release. For an LPG storage bullet:

- **Layer 1 — Inherent Process Design:** Spherical geometry (lowest surface/volume ratio → minimum wall steel, lowest leak probability), refrigerated storage option
- **Layer 2 — BPCS:** Level transmitters, pressure controllers, temperature indicators on the bullet
- **Layer 3 — Operator + Critical Alarms:** High-high level alarm at 90%, emergency response procedure
- **Layer 4 — SIS / ESD:** Independent PLC-based shutdown, redundant level switches (SIL-2 certified, PFD ≈ 10⁻²)
- **Layer 5 — Mechanical Relief:** Two full-bore pressure safety valves (PSVs) set at 18.5 bar with PFD ≈ 10⁻⁴
- **Layer 6 — Fire & Gas + Deluge:** Flame detectors, heat detectors, water spray nozzles (PFD ≈ 10⁻²)
- **Layer 7 — Bunds + Emergency Response:** Bund wall of 110% tank volume, foam pourers, firewater pumps

**PFD Concept:** Each IPL is characterized by a Probability of Failure on Demand. For a hydrocarbon facility, the mitigated event frequency is:

$$f_{mitigated} = f_{init} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks layered model, 2 marks IPL concept, 2 marks PFD table, 1 mark formula.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert pressure to Pa:**

$$\Delta P = 17 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.7 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute hole area:**

$$A_h = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply liquid leak rate equation:**

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.61 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 493 \times 1.7 \times 10^6}$$

$$\dot{m} = 2.994 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{1.676 \times 10^9}$$

$$\dot{m} = 2.994 \times 10^{-4} \times 4.094 \times 10^4 = 12.26 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Total mass released before deluge activation (20 seconds):**

$$m_{1} = 12.26 \times 20 = 245.2 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Isolation valve fails (PFD = 0.05) → 95% chance of continued leak:**

**Step 6 — Additional mass until emergency isolation (assume another 60 s before manual shutdown):**

$$m_{2} = 12.26 \times 60 = 735.6 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7 — Total mass released:**

$$m_{total} = 245.2 + 735.6 = 980.8 \text{ kg} \approx 981 \text{ kg of propane}$$

**Step 8 — ALARP commentary:**
A release of nearly **1 tonne of propane** from a single flange leak is **unacceptable** under ALARP. Recommendation: install **two redundant isolation valves** (each PFD = 0.05) to bring combined PFD to $0.05^2 = 0.0025$, and consider a **flange guard** or **welded connection** (ISD-Simplify principle).

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks mass flow, 2 marks total mass, 1 mark deluge delay calc, 2 marks ALARP discussion.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Risk-Based Design (RBD)** with **Prescriptive (Codes-Based) Design**. Discuss their relative merits, demerits, and at least two real-world hydrocarbon industry scenarios where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sour crude processing unit has the following hazards identified during HAZOP:

| Scenario | Initiating Frequency (per year) | IPL-1 PFD | IPL-2 PFD | IPL-3 PFD |
|----------|--------------------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Overpressure of distillation column | $5 \times 10^{-2}$ | $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-2}$ | $10^{-3}$ |
| H₂S release from sample cooler | $1 \times 10^{-1}$ | $10^{-2}$ | $10^{-2}$ | — |

Compute:
1. Mitigated frequency for **both** scenarios.
2. Plot the points on the F-N curve (assume N = 1 fatality each).
3. State whether each scenario lies in the **ALARP region, negligible region, or intolerable region**, with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — RBD vs Prescriptive Comparison:**

**Risk-Based Design (RBD)** starts from a quantified risk target (e.g., individual fatality risk ≤ 10⁻⁵/yr) and works backward to derive the engineering specifications. It allows trade-offs and innovation but requires extensive **QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment)** capability.

**Prescriptive Design** starts from standards (API, OISD, NFPA, IS) and applies their mandated clauses verbatim. It is highly auditable but rigid and may oversize or undersize for novel hazards.

| Aspect | RBD | Prescriptive |
|--------|-----|--------------|
| Basis | Numerical risk | Code clauses |
| Innovation | Encouraged | Discouraged |
| Cost | Optimized | Conservative |
| Skill needed | High (QRA, statistics) | Medium (code familiarity) |
| Audit | Difficult | Easy |
| Best for | Offshore, LNG, novel | Brownfield, regulatory |

**Real-world scenarios:**
- **RBD preferred:** Mumbai High offshore platform (no prescriptive code for deepwater HP/HT wells), LNG floating storage (no specific IS code)
- **Prescriptive preferred:** IOCL refinery expansion in Gujarat (OISD-STD-117 directly applicable), LPG bottling plant (IS 14461 governs)

*(Award 7 marks: 3 marks comparison, 2 marks real scenarios, 2 marks merits/demerits.)*

---

**Part (b) — Mitigated Frequency Calculations:**

**Scenario 1 — Distillation Column Overpressure:**

$$f_{mit,1} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ /yr}$$

Since $5 \times 10^{-8} < 10^{-7}$ → **NEGLIGIBLE REGION. ACCEPT. No further action required.**

**Scenario 2 — H₂S Release:**

$$f_{mit,2} = 1 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /yr}$$

Since $f_{mit,2} = 10^{-5}$ → exactly at the **TOLERABLE UPPER BOUNDARY / ALARP LIMIT.** Must demonstrate ALARP via additional safeguard or accept with justification.

**F-N Curve Plot (textual):**

```
Frequency F (per yr)
  |
  |  *
  |   *   <- Scenario 1 (5×10⁻⁸, N=1)  -> NEGLIGIBLE
  |
  |     *  <- Scenario 2 (1×10⁻⁵, N=1)  -> ALARP limit
  |
  +------------------- Number of Fatalities N
                1
```

**Justification:** Scenario 1 passes the IR test. Scenario 2 requires **additional risk reduction measures** (e.g., add a 4th IPL — local H₂S detector with automatic isolation, PFD = 10⁻², bringing mitigated frequency to $10^{-7}$/yr, into the ALARP region with margin).

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks scenario 1 calc, 2 marks scenario 2 calc, 1 mark F-N plot, 2 marks region classification + recommendation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deduction in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong formula for liquid leak rate:** Using gas choked-flow equation for liquids → loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Missing the word "Independent"** when describing IPLs → loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing ALARP with ALARA** (different frameworks; ALARA = radiation protection).
> 4. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers → 0.5 mark deduction per occurrence.
> 5. **Not stating PFD values** in DiD answer → loses 2 marks (examiners specifically expect a numerical table).
> 6. **Treating codes as a philosophy** rather than as tools to *implement* a philosophy → conceptual error, up to 3 marks lost.
> 7. **In HAZOP-QRA problems, not interpreting the F-N position** (just calculating without classifying the region) → 2 marks lost.
> 8. **In ALARP problems, not performing the "gross disproportion" test** → 1–2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Design Philosophies — Module 3):**
> - **ISD** has 4 pillars: **Minimize → Substitute → Moderate → Simplify** (in order of preference).
> - **ALARP** = "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" — governed by the gross disproportion cost-benefit test.
> - **DiD** = layered protection (typically **7 layers** for hydrocarbon plants) — each layer is an **IPL**.
> - **PFD** = Probability of Failure on Demand — lower is better (10⁻² typical for SIS).
> - **Mitigated frequency** $f_{mit} = f_{init} \times \prod PFD_i$.
> - **Risk tolerability bands:** $< 10^{-7}$ negligible, $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-5}$ ALARP, $> 10^{-5}$ intolerable.
> - **Liquid leak rate (Bernoulli):** $Q = C_d A_h \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$, $C_d \approx 0.61$.
> - **Gas choked flow:** uses $\gamma$ and stagnation conditions; choked at $P/P_{atm} \geq$ critical pressure ratio.
> - **Prescriptive design** = follow codes (OISD, NFPA, API, IS); NOT a philosophy by itself.
> - **Performance-based** = define target outcome, engineer the means.
> - **F-N curve** plots $F$ (cumulative frequency) vs $N$ (fatalities) on log-log axes.
> - **Trevor Kletz** is the founding authority on ISD (KTU-favourite name).
> - **UK HSE Edwards v. National Coal Board (1948)** is the legal origin of ALARP.
> - **MOC (Management of Change)** re-triggers the design philosophy for every modification.
> - **Major OISD standards to recall:** OISD-STD-117 (fire protection), OISD-STD-118 (layouts).
> - **API standards to recall:** API 650 (tanks), API 2510 (LPG), API RP 14C (offshore safety).
> - **Modular arithmetic of risks** is multiplicative across IPLs, not additive.
> - **Engineering ethics:** A "design philosophy" is a *living* document; it must be revisited at every project phase.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Practices - Safety and Fire Protection in Bottling Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operating Practices - Safety and Fire Protection in Bottling Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bottling Plant (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *Bottling Plant* is an industrial facility designed for the **safe receipt, storage, transfer, and filling of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), compressed natural gas (CNG), or other hydrocarbon fluids into portable pressure vessels (cylinders)** for distribution to end consumers. Operating practices encompass the codified procedures, engineering controls, and statutory safeguards (per **OISD-144**, **OISD-150**, **IS 6044**, and the **Static and Mobile Pressure Vessels (SMPV) Rules**) that govern cylinder movement, filling, leak testing, evacuation, and dispatch.

A *Bottling Plant* typically handles hydrocarbons in their **two-phase (liquid + vapor)** state, where the liquid phase is filled under controlled temperature and pressure into cylinders fitted with pressure-relief devices.

### 1.2 Key Hydrocarbon Fluids Handled

| Fluid | Phase at Filling | Typical Filling Pressure | Critical Hazard Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| LPG (Propane/Butane mix) | Liquid (saturated vapor) | **6.5 – 17 bar** | Class 2.1 Flammable Gas |
| CNG | Compressed Gas | **200 – 250 bar** | Class 2.1 Flammable Gas |
| LNG | Cryogenic Liquid | **–162 °C, ~1 bar** | Class 2.1 + Cryogenic Burn |
| Propylene / Butadiene | Liquid | **5 – 10 bar** | Class 3 + Flammable |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Kitchen Refill Station at Industrial Scale":** Imagine a tea kettle filled with pressurized boiling water and steam (that is what LPG looks like inside a tank). A bottling plant is essentially a **"giant refill station"** — like the small shop that refills your domestic LPG cylinder — but operating at industrial throughput (often **2,000 – 5,000 cylinders/day**). The challenge is the same as in your kitchen: control the *liquid*, control the *vapor*, prevent *leaks*, and never let an ignition source meet the vapor. The only difference is that in a domestic refill, you manage one cylinder; in a bottling plant, you manage hundreds simultaneously with **carousels, conveyors, and rotary filling machines**.

### 1.4 Standard Operating Practices (KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlights)

The **Operating Practices** syllabus block emphasizes six cardinal pillars:

1. **Cylinder Reception & Visual Inspection** (corrosion, dents, valve condition)
2. **De-gassing / Evacuation** of residual LPG using vacuum pumps or compressors
3. **Weighing & Tare Verification** before filling
4. **Filling on Carousel / Rotary Filling Machine** (mass-flow filling)
5. **Leak Testing at Test Pressure** (typically **1.0 × design pressure**)
6. **Stamping, Capping, Sealing & Dispatch** with documented QA

### 1.5 Fundamental Physical Constants

| Constant / Metric | Value | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Filling Density of LPG** | **0.53 kg/L (max)** | Per IS 6044 / SMPV |
| **Hydrostatic Test Pressure (LPG cylinders)** | **30 bar** | Every 5 years |
| **Design Pressure (14.2 kg cyl.)** | **16.7 bar** | Per IS 3196 |
| **Vapor Pressure of Propane @ 40 °C** | **~14 bar** | Filling ceiling |
| **LPG Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** | **2.1 % (v/v)** | Detection alarm |
| **LPG Upper Flammability Limit (UFL)** | **9.5 % (v/v)** | Explosion envelope |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operating Workflow — Structured Logic Breakdown

A bottling plant operating procedure is governed by a **closed-loop, fail-safe sequence**. Every step is designed to ensure that **mass balance, pressure integrity, and leak-tightness** are preserved.

**Step 1 — Cylinder Reception (Inbound Gate)**
- Why: Eliminate damaged cylinders before they enter the hazardous zone.
- How: 100 % visual inspection + pneumatic valve test at **1.0 bar air pressure**.
- Engineering Control: Sparking-free tools, brass/copper-beryllium valve sockets.

**Step 2 — Tare Weighing & Barcode Tagging**
- Why: Mass-based filling requires precise tare (typically **14.2 kg, 19.0 kg, 47.5 kg** cylinders per IS 3196).
- How: Electronic load cells with **± 50 g accuracy**.

**Step 3 — Residual Gas Evacuation (Degassing)**
- Why: Residual LPG + air = explosive mixture inside the cylinder.
- How: Vacuum pump pulls cylinder to **≤ –0.6 bar gauge**, OR displacement compressor pushes vapor to storage.
- Critical Equation: Residual mass < **5 g per cylinder** (per OISD-144).

**Step 4 — Pre-fill Leak Test**
- Pressure: **1.0 × test pressure** (≈ 16.7 bar for domestic cylinder).
- Hold Time: **60 seconds minimum**.
- Acceptance: Pressure decay < **0.2 bar/hour** (no bubble test on soap solution).

**Step 5 — Filling on Carousel (The Heart of the Plant)**
- Why: Carousels allow simultaneous filling of **12 – 36 cylinders** per rotation.
- How: Mass-flow meters (Coriolis type preferred) dispense LPG into the cylinder until the target mass (e.g., **14.2 kg + tare**) is reached.
- Engineering Control: **Filling Density Limit = 0.53 kg/L** — never exceed, or liquid expansion on heating will hydrostatically rupture the cylinder.

**Step 6 — Post-fill Leak Test (Bubble Test)**
- Why: Detect valve-seat leaks before cylinder leaves the plant.
- How: Snoop solution or leak-detection fluid at the valve; reject any cylinder showing bubble growth > **3 mm/s**.

**Step 7 — Stamping, Capping, and Dispatch**
- Why: Statutory requirement under SMPV Rules.
- How: Permanent stamp on shoulder, tamper-evident seal on valve cap.

### 2.2 Fire Protection System — Architectural Logic

Fire protection in a bottling plant is built on **three concentric layers** (per **OISD-117** and **TAC Tariff Advisory Committee** guidelines):

- **Layer 1 — Prevention:** Elimination of ignition sources (EX-rated electrical, intrinsically safe instrumentation, bonding/grounding).
- **Layer 2 — Detection:** Combustible gas detectors (LEL meters) + flame/heat detectors + closed-circuit television.
- **Layer 3 — Suppression:** Water spray systems (deluge), foam pourers for tank farm, dry chemical powder (DCP) for cylinder fires, CO₂ for electrical fires.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and conform to OISD/IS standards taught in KTU PESFT745 Module 3.

| Equation / Metric | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Boundary / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Filling Density** | $\rho_f = \dfrac{m_{\text{LPG}}}{V_{\text{water capacity of cyl.}}}$ | $m$ = LPG mass (kg); $V$ = cylinder volume (L) | $\rho_f \leq \mathbf{0.53 \text{ kg/L}}$ |
| **Vapor Pressure (Antoine, Propane)** | $\log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | $P$ = vapor pressure (bar); $T$ = °C | Valid: –50 °C to +50 °C |
| **Liquid Volume Expansion** | $\Delta V = V_0 \, \beta \, \Delta T$ | $\beta$ = volumetric expansion coeff. (Propane ≈ **0.00345 K⁻¹**) | $\beta_{\text{propane}} \gg \beta_{\text{water}}$ |
| **Required Filling Temp Margin** | $T_{\text{fill}} + \Delta T_{\text{sun}} < T_{\text{critical of cyl.}}$ | Sun gain ≈ **+55 °C** above ambient | Cylinder hydro-test = **30 bar** |
| **Fire Water Demand (per TAC)** | $Q = K \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{P}$ | $K$ = discharge coeff. (~29); $A$ = orifice area (cm²); $P$ = nozzle pressure (bar) | Minimum **3.5 bar** at hydraulically remote nozzle |
| **Hydrant Spacing** | $S_{\text{spacing}} \leq 45 \text{ m}$ (high hazard) | Per Tariff Advisory Committee | Max horizontal throw **≥ 15 m** |
| **Foam Solution Rate** | $R_{\text{foam}} = 4.0 \text{ L/min·m}^2$ | For LPG tank fires (per OISD-117) | Application time ≥ **65 min** |
| **Water Spray Rate (LPG vessels)** | $R_{\text{water spray}} = 10.2 \text{ L/min·m}^2$ | Deluge cooling | Application ≥ **30 min** |
| **Gas Detection Set Points** | Alarm-1 = **20 % LEL**; Alarm-2 = **40 % LEL**; Trip = **60 % LEL** | Per OISD-150 | All detectors must be ATEX-certified |
| **Bonding Resistance** | $R_{\text{bond}} \leq 10 \, \Omega$ | Static discharge control | Measured annually |
| **Safety Distance (Cyl. Storage)** | $D = f(Q_{\text{total}}, W_{\text{radiation}})$ | API RP 521 / IS 1448 | Based on **thermal radiation ≤ 6.31 kW/m²** |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real Plants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The **Filling Density equation** is the single most-asked concept in KTU examinations — it directly determines whether a cylinder can survive a tropical summer (where ambient + solar gain may push contents above **55 °C**).
> - The **Water Spray formula** determines pump-head and reservoir capacity — a 10,000 m² tank farm can require **6,000 m³ of fire-water reserve**.
> - **Gas detection set points** are the first line of defense in DCS-controlled shutdown — a student must be able to articulate why *Alarm at 20 % LEL* is chosen (it provides time for evacuation before reaching the **100 % LEL** "Lower Explosion Limit" threshold).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Filling Mass for a Domestic 14.2 kg LPG Cylinder

**Given (per IS 3196):**
- Cylinder water capacity: $V = 33.3 \text{ L}$
- Maximum allowable filling density: $\rho_f^{\max} = 0.53 \text{ kg/L}$
- Tare mass (empty cylinder + valve): $T = 14.2 \text{ kg}$

**Find:** Maximum LPG mass that may be charged into the cylinder.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1: Define the Filling Density Equation.

$$\rho_f = \dfrac{m_{\text{LPG}}}{V_{\text{water capacity}}}$$

Step 2: Rearrange for the LPG mass.

$$m_{\text{LPG}} = \rho_f \times V_{\text{water capacity}}$$

Step 3: Substitute the maximum allowed filling density and the cylinder's water capacity.

$$m_{\text{LPG}} = 0.53 \text{ kg/L} \times 33.3 \text{ L}$$

Step 4: Evaluate numerically.

$$m_{\text{LPG}} = 17.649 \text{ kg}$$

Step 5: Cap to the standard domestic fill of 14.2 kg (per Indian practice). Since **17.649 kg > 14.2 kg**, the operating limit is **14.2 kg**, ensuring a generous vapor-head space (typically **20 % minimum**).

$$m_{\text{LPG, actual}} = \mathbf{14.2 \text{ kg}} \quad (\text{per IS 3196})$$

Step 6: Sanity check — verify the resulting density.

$$\rho_f^{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{14.2 \text{ kg}}{33.3 \text{ L}} = 0.4264 \text{ kg/L}$$

This is **less than 0.53 kg/L**, confirming the cylinder has a sufficient vapor space of:

$$V_{\text{vapor}} = 33.3 - \dfrac{14.2}{0.493} \approx 4.49 \text{ L}$$

This vapor space accommodates thermal expansion without rupturing the cylinder wall.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fire Water Demand for an LPG Tank Farm (Hydraulic Calculation)

**Given:**
- Tank farm area exposed to fire: $A = 2{,}500 \text{ m}^2$
- Water spray rate per OISD-117: $R = 10.2 \text{ L/min·m}^2$
- Application duration required: $t = 30 \text{ min}$ (minimum)

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1: Express total volumetric flow.

$$Q_{\text{total}} = R \times A$$

Step 2: Substitute values.

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 10.2 \text{ L/min·m}^2 \times 2{,}500 \text{ m}^2$$

Step 3: Evaluate.

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 25{,}500 \text{ L/min}$$

Step 4: Convert to cubic meters per minute.

$$Q_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{25{,}500}{1{,}000} = 25.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

Step 5: Compute total water volume required for 30 min application.

$$V_{\text{water}} = Q_{\text{total}} \times t$$

$$V_{\text{water}} = 25.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \times 30 \text{ min}$$

$$V_{\text{water}} = \mathbf{765 \text{ m}^3}$$

Step 6: Add 4-hour reserve for hose-stream operations.

$$V_{\text{reserve}} = Q_{\text{hydrant}} \times 4 \text{ h} = 2{,}860 \text{ L/min} \times 240 \text{ min} = 686.4 \text{ m}^3$$

Step 7: Total effective reservoir.

$$V_{\text{total reservoir}} = 765 + 686.4 = \mathbf{1{,}451.4 \text{ m}^3}$$

Step 8: Design pump capacity.

$$P_{\text{pump}} = Q_{\text{total}} \times H_{\text{head}} \times \rho \times g / \eta$$

Where head $H = 70 \text{ m}$, $\eta = 0.65$ (pump efficiency).

$$P_{\text{pump}} = \dfrac{25.5 \times 70 \times 1000 \times 9.81}{0.65 \times 1000 \times 60}$$

$$P_{\text{pump}} \approx \mathbf{448.5 \text{ kW}}$$

Thus the plant requires a dedicated fire-water pump of approximately **450 kW** and a reservoir of **1,450 m³**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bonding Resistance Criterion for Static Control

**Given:**
- Maximum acceptable bonding resistance per IS/BS 5958: $R \leq 10 \, \Omega$.

**Derivation logic:**

Step 1: Static charge generation rate in LPG transfer ≈ $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-6} \text{ C/m}^3$.

Step 2: Ignition energy of LPG-air mixture (stoichiometric propane) ≈ **0.25 mJ**.

Step 3: A sphere of radius $r$ carrying charge $Q$ has breakdown voltage given by:

$$V_{\text{breakdown}} = \dfrac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}$$

Step 4: For an LPG hose of $r = 5 \text{ mm}$, the breakdown charge is:

$$Q_{\text{critical}} = 4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r \cdot V_{\text{breakdown}}$$

With $V_{\text{breakdown}} \approx 4 \text{ kV}$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$.

$$Q_{\text{critical}} = 4 \pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.005 \times 4000$$

$$Q_{\text{critical}} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C}$$

Step 5: If cylinder and pipeline are not bonded, a potential difference accumulates. To ensure no spark energy > 0.25 mJ, the resistance must be:

$$R \leq \dfrac{V^2 \cdot t}{2 \cdot E_{\text{ignition}}}$$

Empirically validated as **10 Ω** in standards (IS 7689, NFPA 77).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for Bottling Plant Hazards

| Hazard | Source | Consequence | Primary Safeguard | Secondary Safeguard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cylinder BLEVE** | External fire impingement | Catastrophic rupture | Water spray cooling | Minimum 30 m separation |
| **Pool fire (LPG spill)** | Liquid release | Radiation 30 kW/m² | Foam pourers (4 L/min·m²) | Dyke bund wall |
| **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** | Unconfined vapor release | Overpressure ~7 bar | Gas detectors (LEL) | Vapor recovery system |
| **Static spark** | Liquid flow at high $u$ | Ignition at < 0.25 mJ | Bonding + grounding ($R < 10 \Omega$) | Anti-static additives |
| **Overfill** | Mass-flow meter failure | Liquid-full cylinder | Density interlock | Mechanical stop-fill at 80 % |
| **Cylinder fatigue** | Long service life | Crack propagation | Hydrostatic test every 5 yr | Visual + eddy current |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bottling Plant Operating Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[inbound truck] --> B[cylinder reception yard]
    B --> C{visual inspection}
    C -->|reject| D[scrap yard]
    C -->|accept| E[tare weighing]
    E --> F[residual gas evacuation]
    F --> G[pre fill leak test]
    G -->|fail| D
    G -->|pass| H[rotary carousel filling]
    H --> I{mass flow check}
    I -->|under fill| H
    I -->|within tolerance| J[post fill leak test]
    J -->|fail| D
    J -->|pass| K[stamping and sealing]
    K --> L[finished goods warehouse]
    L --> M[dispatch bay]
    H --> N[fixed gas detection network]
    N -->|20 percent LEL| O[alarm stage 1]
    N -->|40 percent LEL| P[alarm stage 2]
    N -->|60 percent LEL| Q[emergency shutdown ESD]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Fire Protection System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[fire water reservoir] --> B[electric driven fire pump]
    A --> C[diesel driven jockey pump]
    A --> D[diesel driven standby pump]
    B --> E[deluge valve manifold]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[water spray nozzles over tank farm]
    E --> G[foam pourers at bund area]
    E --> H[hydrant ring main]
    H --> I[monitor nozzles]
    H --> J[fire hose reels]
    K[gas detector LEL] -->|signal| L[fire alarm control panel]
    L -->|trip| E
    L -->|alert| M[occupant warning]
    L -->|signal| N[plant fire brigade]
    L -->|signal| O[external fire service]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Three-Layer Safety Defense Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P[Layer 1 Prevention]
        P1[EX rated electrical fittings]
        P2[intrinsically safe instruments]
        P3[bonding and grounding system]
        P4[hot work permit system]
    end
    subgraph D[Layer 2 Detection]
        D1[LEL combustible gas detector]
        D2[IR flame detector]
        D3[linear heat detection cable]
        D4[CCTV with analytics]
    end
    subgraph S[Layer 3 Suppression]
        S1[water spray deluge system]
        S2[foam pourer system]
        S3[DCP trolley units]
        S4[CO2 flooding for electrical room]
    end
    P --> D
    D --> S
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Cylinder Filling Density Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph O[Over filled cylinder danger]
        O1[liquid fill 95 percent]
        O2[thermal expansion on heating]
        O3[hydrostatic rupture]
        O1 --> O2 --> O3
    end
    subgraph C[Correctly filled cylinder]
        C1[liquid fill 80 percent]
        C2[vapor space 20 percent]
        C3[safe thermal expansion]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3
    end
    subgraph U[Under filled cylinder waste]
        U1[liquid fill 50 percent]
        U2[excess vapor space]
        U3[lower energy content]
        U1 --> U2 --> U3
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Filling Density" of LPG and state its maximum permissible value as per IS 6044. Why is this limit critical for safety?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Filling Density** is defined as the ratio of the mass of liquefied gas charged into a cylinder to the water capacity of that cylinder.

$$\rho_f = \dfrac{m_{\text{LPG}}}{V_{\text{water capacity}}}$$

Per IS 6044, the maximum permissible filling density for commercial LPG is **0.53 kg/L**. This limit is critical because LPG expands approximately **260 times** when vaporized, and its volumetric coefficient of thermal expansion ($\beta \approx 0.00345 \text{ K}^{-1}$) is **far greater than water** ($\beta_{\text{water}} \approx 0.000214 \text{ K}^{-1}$). If a cylinder were overfilled, even a modest temperature rise (e.g., from 27 °C ambient to 55 °C with solar gain) would cause the liquid to fill the entire vessel, leading to **hydrostatic rupture** since liquids are virtually incompressible.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three layers of fire protection provided in an LPG bottling plant with one example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Layer 1 — Prevention:** Use of explosion-proof (EX-rated) electrical fittings in hazardous areas (Zone 1/Zone 2) to eliminate ignition sources.
2. **Layer 2 — Detection:** Combustible gas detectors calibrated to LPG (set points: **Alarm-1 at 20 % LEL**, **Alarm-2 at 40 % LEL**, **Trip at 60 % LEL**) provide early warning.
3. **Layer 3 — Suppression:** Water spray deluge system over the tank farm operating at a discharge density of **10.2 L/min·m²** for a minimum of **30 minutes** to cool vessels and prevent BLEVE.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose **3–4 marks per sub-part** on this topic for these mistakes:
> - Writing *"filling density = 0.53 kg/m³"* (wrong unit — should be **kg/L**).
> - Forgetting to mention **vapor space** (typically 20 % minimum) in any filling density explanation.
> - Stating *"water spray rate = 10.2 L/s"* instead of **L/min·m²** — the unit kills the mark.
> - Omitting the **time duration** (30 min water spray, 65 min foam) in fire-system answers.
> - Missing the reference to **OISD-117 / OISD-144 / OISD-150** in safety practice answers.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *With the aid of a labeled flowchart, describe the **seven standard operating procedures** followed in a modern LPG bottling plant, from cylinder reception to dispatch.* **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *Explain the **three concentric layers of fire protection** (Prevention, Detection, Suppression) implemented at an LPG bottling plant. For each layer, provide at least two engineering controls with their design specifications.* **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Seven Operating Procedures **[7 Marks]**

1. **Cylinder Reception & Visual Inspection (1 mark):** All incoming cylinders are visually inspected for dents, corrosion, valve damage, expired test dates, and unauthorized markings. Per OISD-144, **100 % inspection** is mandatory. Spark-resistant (non-ferrous) tools are used.

2. **Tare Weighing (1 mark):** Empty cylinders are weighed on electronic load cells (accuracy ± 50 g) and tagged with barcodes. Standard tare weights are **14.2 kg, 19.0 kg, or 47.5 kg** per IS 3196.

3. **Residual Gas Evacuation (1 mark):** Cylinders are connected to a vacuum pump or displacement compressor. Vacuum is held at **≤ –0.6 bar gauge** for at least **30 seconds**. Residual LPG must be **< 5 g** to prevent flammable air-LPG mixtures in subsequent leak tests.

4. **Pre-fill Leak Test (1 mark):** Cylinders are pressurized to **1.0 × test pressure (≈ 16.7 bar)** with dry air or nitrogen and held for **60 seconds**. Pressure decay must be **< 0.2 bar/hour** and there must be no soap-bubble formation.

5. **Carousel / Rotary Filling (1 mark):** Cylinders are loaded on a 12 to 36-station carousel. **Coriolis mass-flow meters** dispense LPG to target mass. Filling is automatically terminated at **14.2 kg + tare**. The filling density must not exceed **0.53 kg/L**.

6. **Post-fill Leak Test (1 mark):** Each filled cylinder is tested at the valve-stem using **Snoop solution** or a calibrated electronic sniffer. Reject if bubble growth rate **> 3 mm/s**.

7. **Stamping, Capping & Dispatch (1 mark):** The cylinder shoulder is stamped with **date, batch code, and inspector code**. A tamper-evident seal is applied. Cylinders are then moved to the finished-goods warehouse and dispatched via truck.

#### Part (b) — Three Layers of Fire Protection **[7 Marks]**

**Layer 1 — Prevention (2 marks):** Engineering controls that eliminate ignition sources.
- All electrical equipment in hazardous zones must be **flameproof (Ex-d)** or **intrinsically safe (Ex-i)** per IS 5571.
- Bonding and grounding resistance must be **≤ 10 Ω** measured annually to dissipate static electricity.
- Hot-work permits are mandatory; smoking is prohibited within **15 m** of LPG storage.

**Layer 2 — Detection (2 marks):** Continuous monitoring of the hydrocarbon atmosphere.
- **Combustible gas detectors** (catalytic bead or IR type) at every filling bay, with set points **20 % LEL (alarm), 40 % LEL (caution), 60 % LEL (trip)**.
- **IR flame detectors** at the tank farm with spectral response **4.4 μm** (specific to hydrocarbon flames), detection range up to **30 m**.
- **Linear heat detection cable** along conveyor belts with alarm at **68 °C**.

**Layer 3 — Suppression (3 marks):** Active systems to control and extinguish fires.
- **Water spray deluge system** over LPG vessels: discharge rate **10.2 L/min·m²** for minimum **30 minutes**.
- **AFFF foam pourers** at bund areas: application rate **4.0 L/min·m²** for **65 minutes**, foam expansion ratio **6:1 to 8:1**.
- **DCP (Dry Chemical Powder) trolleys** of **150 kg capacity** at every filling bay for cylinder fires.
- **Hydrant ring main** with spacing **≤ 45 m** and minimum nozzle pressure **3.5 bar**.
- **Fire water reservoir** of dedicated capacity, typically **4 to 6 hours of fire demand**, fed by **electric + diesel + jockey pumps** (any two must meet full demand).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of **Filling Density** and **Vapor Space** in LPG cylinders. Derive the maximum permissible LPG mass for a domestic 14.2 kg cylinder with a water capacity of 33.3 litres, and discuss the role of **vapor pressure** and **thermal expansion** in determining these limits.* **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** *An LPG tank farm has a total exposed surface area of **2,500 m²** requiring water spray cooling. Compute the **(i)** total fire-water flow rate, **(ii)** total fire-water volume required for a 30-minute application, and **(iii)** the recommended pump capacity assuming a head of 70 m and pump efficiency of 65 %.* **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Filling Density & Vapor Space **[7 Marks]**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Filling Density** ($\rho_f$) is the mass of liquefied petroleum gas per unit water capacity of the cylinder, expressed in **kg/L**.
- **Vapor Space** is the unfilled volume inside the cylinder above the liquid level, available for vapor accumulation and pressure equalization.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Given: $V_{\text{water}} = 33.3 \text{ L}$ and $\rho_f^{\max} = 0.53 \text{ kg/L}$.

$$m_{\text{LPG, max}} = \rho_f^{\max} \times V_{\text{water}}$$

$$m_{\text{LPG, max}} = 0.53 \times 33.3 = 17.65 \text{ kg}$$

Since the standard fill is **14.2 kg**, the actual filling density is:

$$\rho_f^{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{14.2}{33.3} = 0.426 \text{ kg/L}$$

The vapor space is:

$$V_{\text{vapor}} = V_{\text{water}} - \dfrac{m_{\text{LPG}}}{\rho_{\text{liquid at 40 °C}}}$$

With $\rho_{\text{propane, liquid at 40 °C}} = 0.493 \text{ kg/L}$:

$$V_{\text{vapor}} = 33.3 - \dfrac{14.2}{0.493} = 33.3 - 28.80 = 4.5 \text{ L} \approx \mathbf{20 \% vapor space}$$

**Role of Vapor Pressure & Thermal Expansion (2 marks):**
- Propane's vapor pressure at **40 °C** is **~14 bar**, which is below the cylinder's hydrostatic test pressure of **30 bar**, providing a margin.
- With a thermal expansion coefficient of $\beta \approx 0.00345 \text{ K}^{-1}$, a 28.8 L liquid column expands by approximately **99 mL per °C rise**.
- The 4.5 L vapor space absorbs this expansion safely without hydrostatic rupture.

#### Part (b) — Fire Water Computation **[7 Marks]**

**Given:** $A = 2{,}500 \text{ m}^2$, $R = 10.2 \text{ L/min·m}^2$, $t = 30 \text{ min}$, $H = 70 \text{ m}$, $\eta = 0.65$.

**(i) Total flow rate (2 marks):**

$$Q = R \times A = 10.2 \times 2{,}500 = 25{,}500 \text{ L/min}$$

Converting: $Q = 25.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$.

**(ii) Total water volume (2 marks):**

$$V = Q \times t = 25.5 \times 30 = 765 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iii) Pump capacity (3 marks):**

Hydraulic power is:

$$P_{\text{hydraulic}} = \rho g Q H = 1{,}000 \times 9.81 \times \dfrac{25.5}{60} \times 70$$

$$P_{\text{hydraulic}} = 291{,}600 \text{ W} = 291.6 \text{ kW}$$

With pump efficiency $\eta = 0.65$:

$$P_{\text{brake}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{hydraulic}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{291.6}{0.65} \approx 448.6 \text{ kW}$$

Thus the plant requires a dedicated fire-water pump of approximately **450 kW** (≈ 600 HP) at a head of **70 m**, supplied by a fire-water reservoir of **at least 765 m³ for the spray system**, plus 4-hour hydrant reserve.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Operating Practices in Bottling Plants**

- 🔹 **Filling Density** is the single most critical operating parameter — capped at **0.53 kg/L** for LPG per IS 6044.
- 🔹 Every cylinder must have **20 % minimum vapor space** to absorb thermal expansion.
- 🔹 Standard domestic LPG fill in India is **14.2 kg** with a water capacity of **33.3 L**.
- 🔹 Pre-fill leak test is at **1.0 × test pressure (16.7 bar) for 60 seconds**; decay < 0.2 bar/hr.
- 🔹 **Three Layers of Fire Protection**: Prevention → Detection → Suppression.
- 🔹 **Gas Detector Set Points**: 20 % LEL (alarm), 40 % LEL (caution), 60 % LEL (trip).
- 🔹 **Water Spray Rate**: **10.2 L/min·m²** for **30 min** on LPG vessels.
- 🔹 **Foam Application Rate**: **4.0 L/min·m²** for **65 min** with **AFFF 6 %**.
- 🔹 **Bonding resistance** must be **≤ 10 Ω**; checked annually.
- 🔹 **Hydrostatic test** for LPG cylinders: **30 bar every 5 years**.
- 🔹 **Vapor Pressure of Propane** at 40 °C ≈ **14 bar** — well below cylinder design pressure.
- 🔹 **Filling carousel** typically carries 12 to 36 cylinders simultaneously.
- 🔹 **DCP trolleys (150 kg)** are placed at every filling bay.
- 🔹 Always cite **OISD-117** (fire protection), **OISD-144** (LPG bottling), and **OISD-150** (LPG storage) in examination answers.
- 🔹 **Cylinder resi­dual gas** must be **< 5 g** before filling to prevent explosive air-LPG mixtures.
- 🔹 **Fire water reservoir** must hold at least **4 to 6 hours of total fire demand**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation of Bulk Petroleum Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transportation of Bulk Petroleum Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transportation of Bulk Petroleum Products** is defined as the integrated multimodal logistics operation involving the safe physical transfer of large volumes of crude oil, intermediate refinery streams, and finished petroleum products (LPG, Naphtha, MS, HSD, SKO, ATF, FO/LSHS, Bitumen) from **production wells → gathering stations → refineries → bulk storage terminals → secondary distribution depots → end-user retail outlets**, employing four principal transport modalities — **Pipelines, Marine Tankers, Rail Tank Wagons, and Road Tank Trucks** — each governed by statutory codes such as OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate), IMDG (International Maritime Dangerous Goods) Code, ADR (Accord Dangereux Routier), RID (Rail), and ASME B31.4/B31.3 piping standards.

In the context of **Process Safety Management (PSM)** under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum, transportation is treated as the *third-highest consequence domain* (after refining and bulk storage) because the inventory is dispersed, transient, geographically unbounded, and exposed to public domain risk.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Circulatory System of the Oil Industry":** Think of the hydrocarbon value chain as a human body. **Pipelines = Aorta & arteries** (continuous, high-volume, fixed-path flow from heart to organs). **Marine tankers = Red blood cells in major vessels** (carry huge payloads between continents). **Rail tank wagons = WBCs in capillaries** (medium-volume, scheduled, point-to-point). **Road tankers = Lymphatic system & last-mile capillaries** (smaller volume, flexible routing, door-to-door). Just as a clot in any vessel can be fatal, a single leak/spark/explosion in any mode can cause catastrophic loss of life, property, and environment.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **API Gravity Standard Reference Temperature:** **60 °F (15.56 °C)**
- **Standard Barrel:** **1 bbl = 0.158987 m³ = 42 US gallons**
- **DWT (Deadweight Tonnage) standard for tankers:** **1 DWT = 1 long ton = 1016.05 kg**
- **1 Tanker Wagons (Indian Railways):** **~58–70 m³ capacity each**
- **Statutory Flash Point Boundary (Petroleum Class A):** **FP < 23 °C** (per OISD-118)
- **Bonding Resistance Limit (Road tanker earthing):** **R ≤ 10 Ω** (per IS 10987 / API RP 2003)
- **Static Relaxation Time of Hydrocarbons (τ):** **0.1 – 4.0 seconds** depending on conductivity

### 1.4 Modes of Transport — Snapshot Table

| Mode | Typical Capacity | Typical Distance | KTU-Relevant Statutory Code |
|------|------------------|------------------|-----------------------------|
| Cross-country Pipeline | 5–80 MMTPA | 100–3000 km | ASME B31.4 / OISD-141 |
| Marine Tanker (VLCC) | 2,00,000 – 3,20,000 DWT | Trans-oceanic | SOLAS / MARPOL / IMDG |
| Marine Tanker (Product Carrier) | 30,000 – 80,000 DWT | Coastal/short-sea | SOLAS / MARPOL |
| Rail Tank Wagon | 20–70 m³ per wagon | 200–2500 km | RID / IRCM Ch. VI |
| Road Tanker (Truck) | 10–24 kL | 50–1500 km | ADR / OISD-156 / CMV Rules |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Multi-Modal Transport? — The Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single mode cannot economically or operationally serve the entire supply chain. **Pipelines** are capital-intensive but opex-cheap for >5 MMTPA on fixed corridors. **Marine** is the only viable mode for trans-oceanic bulk. **Rail** balances cost vs. flexibility for inland trunk routes. **Road** dominates the last-mile (≤500 km) distribution to retail outlets because it provides door-to-door service.

The decision matrix for mode selection depends on:
1. **Quantity** (volume per trip)
2. **Distance & geography**
3. **Product hazard class** (FP, toxicity, vapor pressure)
4. **Delivery urgency** (continuous vs. batch)
5. **CAPEX vs. OPEX trade-off**
6. **Regulatory & environmental constraints**

---

### 2.2 Petroleum Product Classification (OISD / IMDG Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> For safety design, petroleum products are classified by **Flash Point (FP)** under OISD-118 and by **Hazard Class** under IMDG (Class 3 — Flammable Liquids) and Class 2 (Gases).

| Class (Indian Petroleum Act 1934) | Flash Point (°C) | Examples | Transport Hazard |
|----------------------------------|------------------|----------|------------------|
| **Class A** | FP < 23 °C | MS (Petrol), Naphtha, Benzene | Extremely flammable; vapour at all ambient T |
| **Class B** | 23 °C ≤ FP < 65 °C | HSD (Diesel), Kerosene, ATF | Flammable; vapour above 35 °C |
| **Class C** | 65 °C ≤ FP < 93 °C | LDFO, LSHS, Light Lube stocks | Combustible; hot-work only |
| **Non-petroleum (Class D)** | FP ≥ 93 °C | Bitumen, heavy lubes | Low volatility hazard |

---

### 2.3 Road Transportation — Deep Theory

#### 2.3.1 Road Tanker Anatomy & Safety Fittings

A modern petroleum road tanker (per IS 10987 / OISD-156) is essentially a **horizontal cylindrical pressure-vessel mounted on a multi-axle chassis**, equipped with:

- **Top-loading API 4″ swing-check valve** (to prevent product spillage on rollover)
- **Bottom-loading API 4″ butterfly valve + API 1004 dry-break coupling**
- **Emergency shut-off (ESD) valve** — fails closed on impact
- **Vapour-recovery line** (Stage I — tanker to terminal) per CPCB norms
- **Static discharge reel + earthing chain** (R ≤ 10 Ω)
- **Rear-mounted, fire-resistant roll-over protection** (FRP bumpers)
- **Rear 9 kg DCP + 6 kg CO₂ portable extinguishers**
- **Spill containment bund (≥110% of largest compartment)**
- **Anti-surge / anti-slosh baffles** (per API 650 §5.9 equivalent for tanker geometry)
- **Spark-arrestor on exhaust** and **flame arrester on PVRV**

#### 2.3.2 Compartment Sizing & Maximum Fill Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Fill Rule (OISD-156 §6.4):** Each compartment shall be filled to **not more than 95% of its water capacity** (97% for bitumen), to allow thermal expansion at +55 °C ambient per IS 5842 ambient design temperature.

Let **V_w** = water capacity of compartment (m³), **α** = cubical expansion coefficient of liquid (/°C).

The safe fill volume at loading temperature T_L is:

$$V_{fill} = \frac{0.95 \, V_w}{1 + \alpha \, (T_{max} - T_L)}$$

where **T_max = 55 °C** (Indian summer design).

---

### 2.4 Pipeline Transportation — Deep Theory

#### 2.4.1 Pipeline Categories

| Category | Diameter (NB) | Operating Pressure | ASME Std |
|----------|--------------|--------------------|----------|
| **Trunk / Cross-country** | 16″–48″ | 40–100 bar | ASME B31.4 |
| **Distribution / Spur** | 6″–14″ | 16–40 bar | ASME B31.4 |
| **Plant piping** | 1″–24″ | per design | ASME B31.3 |
| **Gathering lines** | 4″–10″ | 10–25 bar | ASME B31.4 |

#### 2.4.2 Hydraulic Design — Darcy–Weisbach & Hazen-Williams

For turbulent flow of a viscous hydrocarbon in a long pipeline, the **head loss** is:

$$h_f = f \, \frac{L}{D} \, \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

where:
- **h_f** = frictional head loss (m)
- **f** = Darcy friction factor (Moody chart or Colebrook equation)
- **L** = pipe length (m)
- **D** = inside diameter (m)
- **V** = mean velocity (m/s)
- **g** = 9.81 m/s²

For hydrocarbon pipelines (smooth pipe, Re > 4000), an explicit **Chen equation** approximation is:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log\left( \frac{\epsilon/D}{3.7065} - \frac{5.0452}{Re} \log\left( \frac{(\epsilon/D)^{1.1098}}{2.8257} + \left(\frac{7.149}{Re}\right)^{0.8981}\right) \right)$$

Equivalently, in **field units** (psi, ft, s), the **Weymouth equation** (preferred for gas lines) and **Hazen-Williams** (water/light liquids) are:

$$Q = 0.2785 \, C \, D^{2.63} \, S^{0.54}$$

where **C** = Hazen-Williams coefficient (**C = 130–150** for new steel; **C = 100–120** for old).

#### 2.4.3 Surge Pressure (Water-Hammer) Analysis

> [!WARNING]
> Sudden valve closure at the discharge end of a pipeline generates a **Joukowsky surge pressure** that can exceed 2× the static pressure and rupture the line. Always design for surge.

$$\Delta P_{surge} = \rho \, a \, \Delta V$$

with **a** = pressure-wave celerity:

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{K / \rho}{1 + \frac{K \, D}{E \, t} \, \left(1 - \nu^2\right)}}$$

where **K** = bulk modulus of fluid (~1.4 GPa for oil), **E** = Young's modulus of pipe (~2.0×10¹¹ Pa for steel), **t** = wall thickness, **ν** = Poisson's ratio (0.3 for steel).

---

### 2.5 Static Electricity — The Silent Killer in Loading

#### 2.5.1 Charge Generation Mechanism

When a hydrocarbon with low electrical conductivity (e.g., MS, JP-54, Naphtha) flows through a pipe, gets filtered, or is splashed into a tank, **electrostatic charge** accumulates on the bulk liquid. If the **relaxation time** of the liquid exceeds the residence time, charge cannot bleed off and a **bound charge layer** forms at the liquid surface. When a probe (e.g., gauge tape, sample bottle on a metal rod) approaches, a **spark discharge** ignites the vapour space.

$$\tau = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r}{\kappa}$$

- **τ** = relaxation time (s)
- **ε₀** = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m
- **εᵣ** = relative permittivity (≈ 2 for hydrocarbons)
- **κ** = electrical conductivity (S/m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **MS (Petrol), κ ≈ 1 × 10⁻¹² S/m → τ ≈ 18 seconds**. For **HSD, κ ≈ 5 × 10⁻¹¹ S/m → τ ≈ 0.35 s** (self-relaxing). Therefore **MS requires anti-static additive (Stadis-450 @ 1–3 ppm)** to raise conductivity to ≥ 50 pS/m.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Constants |
|---|------------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Maximum fill volume | $V_{fill} = \dfrac{0.95 \, V_w}{1 + \alpha(T_{max}-T_L)}$ | V in m³, α in /°C, T in °C |
| 2 | Darcy-Weisbach head loss | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | h in m, V in m/s, g = 9.81 m/s² |
| 3 | Pressure drop | $\Delta P = \rho \, g \, h_f$ | Pa |
| 4 | Joukowsky surge | $\Delta P_{surge} = \rho \, a \, \Delta V$ | Pa |
| 5 | Wave celerity | $a = \sqrt{\dfrac{K/\rho}{1 + \dfrac{KD}{Et}(1-\nu^2)}}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Electrostatic relaxation time | $\tau = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\kappa}$ | seconds |
| 7 | Hazen-Williams flow | $Q = 0.2785 \, C \, D^{2.63} \, S^{0.54}$ | m³/s, D in m |
| 8 | Bonding resistance | $R \le 10 \, \Omega$ | Ω |
| 9 | API gravity | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60°F}} - 131.5$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Specific gravity @ T | $SG_T = SG_{15.6} \left[1 - \beta(T-15.6)\right]$ | β ≈ 0.00065 /°C |
| 11 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Fire water demand | $Q_f = 3.6 \, A \, q$ | m³/h, A in m², q in L/min·m² |

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline hydraulics** drives the entire optimization of cross-country pipeline diameter selection — a 1″ increase in diameter reduces pumping power by ~30%.
- **Static electricity** is the leading cause of road-tanker loading fires in India; the **OISD-156** mandate of Stadis-450 addition has reduced such incidents by 90% since 2003.
- **Surge analysis** is mandatory for any crude trunk line; failure to design surge relief valves caused the **San Bruno gas-pipeline rupture (2010)**.
- **Mode-selection economics** dictate that the IOCL Paradip-Haldia pipeline (1400 km, 4.5 MMTPA) saves ~₹700 cr/year vs. equivalent rail/road transport.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Maximum Safe Fill Volume for a Road Tanker Compartment

> **Problem:** A Class A petroleum road tanker has a compartment of **water capacity 4500 L**. The product is **motor spirit (α = 9.5 × 10⁻⁴ /°C)**. It is loaded at **T_L = 32 °C**. Maximum ambient during transit can reach **55 °C**. Find the maximum allowable fill volume at loading. (KTU numerical style)

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

$$V_{fill} = \frac{0.95 \, V_w}{1 + \alpha \, (T_{max} - T_L)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$V_{fill} = \frac{0.95 \times 4500}{1 + (9.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (55 - 32)}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the temperature-rise term:**

$$9.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 23 = 2.185 \times 10^{-2} = 0.02185$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$1 + 0.02185 = 1.02185$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator:**

$$0.95 \times 4500 = 4275 \text{ L}$$

**Step 6 — Divide numerator by denominator:**

$$V_{fill} = \frac{4275}{1.02185} = 4183.7 \text{ L} \approx \mathbf{4184 \text{ L}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
The compartment, although of 4500 L water capacity, must be loaded with **only 4184 L of MS** to leave a **5 % ullage (~316 L)** which, combined with thermal expansion (the denominator > 1), keeps the compartment liquid-full at the worst-case 55 °C transit temperature without spillage or compartment damage. Loading more than this amount is a **statutory violation** under OISD-156.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Pressure Drop in a Crude Oil Pipeline

> **Problem:** A 30″ NB (DN 750, ID = 0.762 m) cross-country crude pipeline carries **SG 0.86 oil at 25 °C (μ = 12 cP = 0.012 Pa·s)** at a flow rate of **6000 m³/h**. Pipe roughness ε = 0.045 mm. Pipe length = 120 km. Determine: (a) Reynolds number, (b) Friction factor, (c) Head loss, (d) Pumping power required.

**Step 1 — Compute velocity V:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{6000 / 3600}{\pi (0.762)^2 / 4} = \frac{1.667}{0.4560} = 3.655 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Reynolds number Re:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{860 \times 3.655 \times 0.762}{0.012} = \frac{2396.3}{0.012} = 1.9969 \times 10^5$$

Since **Re ≈ 2 × 10⁵**, flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 3 — Compute relative roughness ε/D:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.045 \times 10^{-3}}{0.762} = 5.91 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Colebrook equation (iterative) or Haaland approximation:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[ \left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{Re} \right]$$

Substitute:

$$\left(\frac{5.91\times10^{-5}}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{1.9969\times10^5}$$
$$= (1.597\times10^{-5})^{1.11} + 3.455\times10^{-5}$$

For x small, x^{1.11} ≈ x (linear approx) → 1.6 × 10⁻⁵

Sum ≈ 5.05 × 10⁻⁵

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}(5.05\times10^{-5}) = -1.8 \times (-4.297) = 7.734$$

$$f = \frac{1}{(7.734)^2} = 0.01673$$

**Step 5 — Compute head loss h_f:**

$$h_f = 0.01673 \times \frac{120000}{0.762} \times \frac{(3.655)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$= 0.01673 \times 157480 \times \frac{13.36}{19.62}$$

$$= 0.01673 \times 157480 \times 0.6809$$

$$= \mathbf{1794 \text{ m of oil}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P = \rho \, g \, h_f = 860 \times 9.81 \times 1794 = 1.513 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 15.13 \text{ MPa} = 151.3 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 7 — Compute pumping power:**

$$P_{pump} = \Delta P \times Q = 1.513 \times 10^7 \times 1.667 = 2.522 \times 10^7 \text{ W} \approx 25.22 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 8 — Add pump + motor efficiency (η = 75 %):**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{25.22}{0.75} \approx 33.6 \text{ MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** 33 MW for a single pipeline translates to **continuous pumping stations every 60–80 km**, each station drawing **~40 MW** of grid power — a major driver for siting crude pipelines near robust power infrastructure.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Joukowsky Surge Pressure

> **Problem:** A 24″ (ID = 0.6 m) petroleum pipeline operates at **steady velocity 2.0 m/s**. A downstream ESD valve closes in **t = 0.8 s**. Pipe wall thickness **t_w = 12 mm**, **E = 2 × 10¹¹ Pa**, **ν = 0.3**, oil bulk modulus **K = 1.4 GPa**, oil density **ρ = 850 kg/m³**. Determine: (a) Wave celerity, (b) Surge pressure.

**Step 1 — Compute the denominator term:**

$$\frac{KD}{E\,t_w}(1-\nu^2) = \frac{1.4\times10^9 \times 0.6}{2\times10^{11}\times0.012}\times(1-0.09)$$

$$= \frac{8.4\times10^8}{2.4\times10^9} \times 0.91 = 0.35 \times 0.91 = 0.3185$$

**Step 2 — Compute K/ρ:**

$$\frac{K}{\rho} = \frac{1.4\times10^9}{850} = 1.647\times10^6 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute wave celerity a:**

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{1.647\times10^6}{1 + 0.3185}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.647\times10^6}{1.3185}}$$

$$= \sqrt{1.2492\times10^6} = 1117.7 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Verify closure is "rapid" (critical time):**

$$t_{critical} = \frac{2L}{a} = \frac{2\times50000}{1117.7} = 89.5 \text{ s}$$

Since **t_close = 0.8 s << 89.5 s**, closure is **rapid** and Joukowsky applies.

**Step 5 — Compute surge pressure:**

$$\Delta P_{surge} = 850 \times 1117.7 \times 2.0 = 1.90 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 19.0 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with operating pressure (say 40 bar):**

$$\frac{\Delta P_{surge}}{P_{op}} = \frac{19.0}{40} = 0.475 = 47.5\,\%$$

This surge pushes the line to **59 bar transient** — within ASME B31.4 allowable (1.39 × 40 = 55.6 bar? actually need 20% margin → design pressure = 55 bar; 59 bar exceeds → **install surge relief or slow-closing valve**).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Mode Selection

| Selection Criterion | Pipeline | Marine | Rail | Road |
|--------------------|----------|--------|------|------|
| **CAPEX per km (Indexed)** | High (1.0) | Very High (port) | Medium | Low |
| **OPEX per ton-km (Indexed)** | Lowest (0.15) | Low (0.25) | Medium (0.45) | High (1.0) |
| **Lead Time** | Continuous | Long (3–7 d port) | Medium (1–3 d) | Short (hours) |
| **Hazmat Suitability** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Limited (small volumes) |
| **Public Risk Exposure** | Low (buried) | Medium (port) | Medium | **HIGH** (population) |
| **Flammable Class A OK?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes, with full compliance |
| **Environmental Risk** | Spill → soil/water | Catastrophic (tanker spill) | Derailment | Roll-over on highway |
| **Indian regulatory code** | OISD-141 | IMDG + MOEF | RID + IR | OISD-156 + ADR |

---

### 3.5 Quantitative Risk Comparison — KTU Reference Data

For **1 MMTPA petroleum transport** over 1000 km, published IOCL risk data (used in KTU case studies) is approximately:

| Mode | Expected fatalities/yr (world avg) | Spill events/yr |
|------|------------------------------------|-----------------|
| Pipeline | 0.005 – 0.05 | 0.3 – 2.0 |
| Marine | 0.01 – 0.10 | 0.5 – 3.0 |
| Rail | 0.05 – 0.20 | 1.0 – 4.0 |
| Road | 0.5 – 2.5 | 5 – 25 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Road transport is **50–100× more fatal** per unit throughput than pipeline, despite carrying ~30% of petroleum in India — the central motivation for **OISD/PNGRB push toward pipeline-ization**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Multimodal Petroleum Transport Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: Well or Refinery] --> B{Volume per Day?}
    B -- Greater than 1 MMTPA --> C[Cross-Country Pipeline]
    B -- 0.1 to 1 MMTPA --> D{Distance?}
    B -- Less than 0.1 MMTPA --> E{Distance?}

    D -- Over 1500 km --> F[Marine Tanker via Port Terminal]
    D -- 500 to 1500 km --> G[Rail Tank Wagon via Siding]
    D -- Less than 500 km --> H[Road Tanker via Loading Gantry]

    E -- Less than 300 km --> H
    E -- Greater than 300 km --> G

    C --> I[Intermediate Pigging Station]
    C --> J[Final Terminal at Refinery or Depot]

    F --> K[SPM or Jetty Unloading]
    G --> L[Rail Loading Gantry]
    H --> M[Top or Bottom Loading Gantry]

    I --> N[Continuous Flow with Surge Protection]
    J --> O[Bulk Storage Tanks]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> P[Secondary Distribution Depots]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Retail Outlet via Last-Mile Road Tanker]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Road Tanker Bottom-Loading System with Vapour Recovery and Static Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RT[Road Tanker Chassis] --> ES[ESD Valve at Rear]
    ES --> BL[Bottom Loading API Coupling]
    BL --> BC[Bonding Cable and Reel]
    BC --> EG[Earthed Loading Gantry - R less than 10 ohm]

    TG[Terminal Metering Skid] --> FF[Strainers and Filters]
    FF --> AS[Anti-Static Additive Injection at 1 to 3 ppm]
    AS --> MFC[Mass Flow Controller]
    MFC --> BL

    RT --> VR[Vapour Recovery Line]
    VR --> VRS[Vapour Recovery System to Terminal Vapour Holder]
    VRS --> VCU[Vapour Combustion Unit or Carbon Bed]

    BL --> LAL[Overfill Alarm at 95 percent]
    LAL --> ESV[Automatic ESDV Shutoff]

    OP[Operator at Gantry] --> PER[Permit-to-Work Check]
    PER --> GD[Grounding Verification]
    GD --> BC
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Cross-Country Pipeline Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Pump Station 1] --> ML[Mainline 30 inch NB]
    ML --> ISV[Intermediate Sectionalising Valve]
    ISV --> ML
    ML --> PS2[Pump Station 2]
    PS2 --> ML
    ML --> PT[Pig Trap Launcher and Receiver Stations]
    ML --> SC[SCADA Telemetry and Leak Detection]
    ML --> SRV[Surge Relief Vessel at Downstream of ESDV]
    SRV --> RT[Receiving Terminal Manifold]
    RT --> ST[Bulk Storage Tanks per API 650]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Marine Tanker Loading Sequence (Terminal Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Berthing and Mooring of Tanker] --> B[Permit-to-Work and Bonding Cable Connection]
    B --> C[Pre-Loading Meeting ISGOTT Check]
    C --> D[Open Inert Gas System and Tank Inspection]
    D --> E[Connect Loading Arms API 4 inch]
    E --> F[Line-up and Leak Test of Loading Header]
    F --> G[Start Loading at Low Rate 1 m/s]
    G --> H[Monitor Flow Rate and Pressure]
    H --> I[Increase to Design Rate after Line Cooled]
    I --> J[Trim and Ullage Monitoring per Load Plan]
    J --> K{Are Tanks at 95 percent?}
    K -- No --> J
    K -- Yes --> L[High-Level Alarm and ESD Shutdown]
    L --> M[Top-up to Stop Level per ISGOTT]
    M --> N[Disconnect Arms and Bonding]
    N --> O[Vapour Return Hose Connection]
    O --> P[Cast Off and Departure]
```
