<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**"Classify petroleum products based on flash point as per OISD-118. Why is this classification critical for transportation safety?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification (2 marks):**
> - **Class A:** Flash Point < 23 °C → e.g., Motor Spirit, Naphtha, Benzene → vapours evolved at all ambient temperatures → **highest explosion hazard**.
> - **Class B:** 23 °C ≤ FP < 65 °C → e.g., HSD, Kerosene, ATF → vapours evolve only above ~35 °C → **moderate hazard**.
> - **Class C:** 65 °C ≤ FP < 93 °C → e.g., LDO, LSHS, light lube → **low volatility hazard**.

> **Why critical for transportation (1 mark):**
> Class A products require **earthed loading, anti-static additive, vapour-recovery, explosion-proof fittings, and prohibition of hot-work** during transit. Class B/C allow relaxed (but still controlled) transport. Misclassification leads to **inadequate safety design** → catastrophic road-tanker flash fires or marine vapour-cloud explosions.

---

#### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**"Define 'relaxation time' in the context of static electricity during petroleum loading. How does Stadis-450 mitigate the hazard?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Relaxation time **τ = ε₀ εᵣ / κ** is the time required for electrostatic charge to decay to ~37% of its initial value in a hydrocarbon liquid. For MS (κ ≈ 10⁻¹² S/m), τ ≈ 18 s, longer than typical residence times in pipelines/filters → **charge accumulates**.

> **Stadis-450 mechanism (1.5 marks):** Adding **1–3 ppm Stadis-450** (a dinonyl naphthalene sulfonic acid / chromium salt blend) raises the conductivity of MS to ≥ 50 pS/m, reducing τ to < 0.5 s. Charge bleeds off rapidly to the grounded tank wall, preventing bound-charge accumulation and spark discharge that could ignite the vapour space.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Per KTU ESE Module-3 Internal Choice, answer EITHER Q-A or Q-B.**

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **bottom-loading system of a petroleum road tanker** highlighting the safety fittings per OISD-156. List **any seven safety devices** fitted on the tanker. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A Class B petroleum road tanker has a compartment of **water capacity 5000 L**. The product (Diesel, **α = 8.5 × 10⁻⁴ /°C**) is loaded at **T_L = 30 °C**. Maximum ambient = **55 °C**. Determine:
- (i) Maximum allowable fill volume at loading **[(3 marks)]**
- (ii) If the loader accidentally fills **4900 L**, by how many litres does the compartment overflow at 55 °C? Comment on safety. **[(4 marks)]**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> **Diagram (3 marks):** Sketch the block diagram (refer Section 4.2 of these notes) showing **tanker compartment → ESD valve → bottom-loading API coupling → bonding cable → earthed gantry → metering skid → strainer → anti-static additive dosing → MFC → overfill alarm → automatic ESD**. Mention vapour-recovery line returning to terminal vapour holder.

> **Seven safety devices (1 mark each, choose any 7):**
> 1. **Emergency Shut-off (ESD) valve** at rear — fails closed on impact
> 2. **Bottom-loading API dry-break coupling** with 5° tilt swivel
> 3. **Static discharge reel + bonding cable** (R ≤ 10 Ω to gantry earth)
> 4. **PVRV (Pressure-Vacuum Relief Valve)** with flame arrester
> 5. **Anti-surge / anti-slosh baffles**
> 6. **Overfill prevention sensor + 95% high-level alarm + auto-shutoff**
> 7. **Spark arrestor on exhaust silencer**
> 8. **Rear 9 kg DCP + 6 kg CO₂ portable extinguishers**
> 9. **Rear FRP bumper** (roll-over protection)
> 10. **Spill containment bund** (≥ 110% of largest compartment)
> 11. **Vapour-recovery line + dry-break vapour coupling**
> 12. **Anti-static additive dosing at terminal** (Stadis-450 @ 1–3 ppm)

**Part (b)(i) — 3 marks:**

$$V_{fill} = \frac{0.95 \times 5000}{1 + (8.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (55 - 30)}$$

$$= \frac{4750}{1 + 0.02125} = \frac{4750}{1.02125} = \mathbf{4650.9 \text{ L}}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — 4 marks:**

The actual loaded volume **V_actual = 4900 L**. At **T_max = 55 °C**, this liquid expands to:

$$V_{55} = V_{actual} \times [1 + \alpha(T_{max} - T_L)]$$
$$= 4900 \times [1 + (8.5\times10^{-4}) \times 25]$$
$$= 4900 \times 1.02125 = \mathbf{5004.1 \text{ L}}$$

The compartment can hold at most:

$$V_{compartment} = 5000 \text{ L}$$

**Overflow = 5004.1 − 5000 = 4.1 L** (positive number → compartment is over-pressured).

**Safety comment (1 mark):** Even though the overflow is only 4.1 L, it indicates:
- The compartment is **liquid-full** at 55 °C → hydrostatic pressure on compartment dome.
- Diesel vapour pushed out through **PVRV** → flammable cloud → ignition source (road spark) → **flash fire**.
- **Action:** Off-load 249 L (4900 − 4651) to bring back to safe fill. Tankers must carry **calibrated dip-stick and ullage table** for verification at every load.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Describe the **multimodal transportation of bulk petroleum products in India** with reference to statutory codes (OISD, IMDG, ADR, ASME). Compare the four modes on **risk, cost, and capacity** axes using a tabulated comparison. **[(7 marks)]**

**(b)** A **DN 600 crude oil pipeline** (ID = 0.6 m, ε = 0.045 mm, L = 80 km) carries **SG 0.88 oil (μ = 15 cP, ρ = 880 kg/m³)** at a flow rate of **3500 m³/h**.
- (i) Compute the **Reynolds number**, **friction factor**, and **head loss**. **[(4 marks)]**
- (ii) Compute the **pumping power** at 75 % pump-motor efficiency. **[(3 marks)]**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> **Modal Description (3 marks):** India moves ~ 350 MMTPA of petroleum through pipelines (~60%), rail (~20%), road (~18%), and marine (~2%). **Pipelines (e.g., Salaya-Mathura, Paradip-Haldia, Mangalore-Hassan)** are governed by **OISD-141 + ASME B31.4 + PNGRB Act 2006 + P&MP Act 1934**. **Marine** (Suezmax/Product carriers calling at Mumbai High, Mundra, Paradip) is governed by **SOLAS, MARPOL 73/78, IMDG Code, ISGOTT**. **Rail** (Indian Railways — ~10,000 tank wagons fleet, IRCM Chapter VI, RID). **Road** (OISD-156, IS 10987, ADR, CMVR 1989, Petroleum Rules 2002).

> **Tabulated Comparison (4 marks):**

| Mode | Capacity | Cost per t·km (₹ indexed) | Statutory Code | Lead Time |
|------|----------|---------------------------|----------------|-----------|
| Pipeline | 5–80 MMTPA | 0.15 | OISD-141 / ASME B31.4 | Continuous |
| Marine | 50,000–3,20,000 DWT | 0.25 | SOLAS / MARPOL / IMDG | 3–7 days |
| Rail | 20–70 m³ / wagon | 0.45 | IRCM Ch VI / RID | 1–3 days |
| Road | 10–24 kL | 1.00 | OISD-156 / ADR / CMVR | 4–24 hours |

**Part (b)(i) — 4 marks:**

**Step 1 — Velocity:**
$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{3500/3600}{\pi(0.6)^2/4} = \frac{0.9722}{0.2827} = 3.439 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**
$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{880 \times 3.439 \times 0.6}{0.015} = \frac{1815.5}{0.015} = 1.210 \times 10^5$$

Flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 3 — Friction factor (Haaland equation):**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.045\times10^{-3}}{0.6} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log\left[\left(\frac{7.5\times10^{-5}}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{1.21\times10^5}\right]$$

$$= -1.8 \log\left[(2.027\times10^{-5})^{1.11} + 5.70\times10^{-5}\right]$$

For x = 2 × 10⁻⁵, x^{1.11} ≈ x (small-x approx) → 2.027 × 10⁻⁵

Sum ≈ 7.73 × 10⁻⁵

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log(7.73\times10^{-5}) = -1.8 \times (-4.1119) = 7.401$$

$$f = \frac{1}{(7.401)^2} = 0.01825$$

**Step 4 — Head loss:**
$$h_f = 0.01825 \times \frac{80000}{0.6} \times \frac{(3.439)^2}{2\times9.81}$$

$$= 0.01825 \times 1.333\times10^5 \times \frac{11.83}{19.62}$$

$$= 0.01825 \times 1.333\times10^5 \times 0.6029$$

$$= \mathbf{1467 \text{ m of oil}}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — 3 marks:**

Pressure drop:
$$\Delta P = \rho \, g \, h_f = 880 \times 9.81 \times 1467 = 1.266 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 12.66 \text{ MPa}$$

Hydraulic power:
$$P_{hyd} = \Delta P \times Q = 1.266 \times 10^7 \times 0.9722 = 1.231 \times 10^7 \text{ W} = 12.31 \text{ MW}$$

Shaft power (η = 0.75):
$$P_{shaft} = \frac{12.31}{0.75} = \mathbf{16.41 \text{ MW}}$$

**Engineering Insight (for viva):** 16 MW for an 80-km segment implies 3–4 pump stations are needed along the line, each with twin 8 MW pumps and emergency generator backup.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks (KTU 2024 valuation pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.95 fill factor** — many students compute V_fill = V_w / (1+α·ΔT), omitting the 0.95 ullage factor → answer inflated by 5% → **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong viscosity unit conversion** — common mistake is using 12 cP = 0.12 Pa·s instead of 0.012 → Re off by 10× → entire hydraulic calc wrong → **−3 marks**.
> 3. **Misclassifying Class A / B / C** — confusing flash-point ranges → loses 1 mark in definition.
> 4. **Static electricity — students write "earthing prevents sparks"** without citing **relaxation time + Stadis-450** → answer lacks chemical mechanism → **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Colebrook vs. Haaland** — KTU allows either, but switching midway leads to inconsistency → **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Surge analysis** — students forget the (1−ν²) factor in the celerity formula → answer wrong by ~25% → **−2 marks**.
> 7. **Forgetting pump efficiency** — answer 12 MW instead of 16 MW → **−1 mark**.
> 8. **Not mentioning statutory codes** in road-transport questions (OISD-156, IS 10987) → **−1 to −2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HOTSPOT BULLETS — Re-read 5 minutes before exam:**

- ✅ Four modes: **Pipeline, Marine, Rail, Road** — each with a **distinct statutory code**.
- ✅ **Class A (FP < 23 °C) → MS, Naphtha, Benzene** — highest hazard → Stadis-450 mandatory, vapour recovery mandatory, hot-work prohibited.
- ✅ **Maximum fill rule = 95 %** of water capacity at loading temperature (97 % for bitumen).
- ✅ **Bonding resistance ≤ 10 Ω** (OISD-156 / API RP 2003).
- ✅ **Stadis-450 @ 1–3 ppm** → raises MS conductivity from ~1 pS/m to ≥ 50 pS/m → reduces τ from 18 s to < 0.5 s.
- ✅ **Joukowsky surge pressure = ρ × a × ΔV** — mandatory check for all petroleum pipelines; design for 1.5× operating pressure if surge-prone.
- ✅ **Darcy-Weisbach** is the universal head-loss formula; **Hazen-Williams** valid only for water/light liquids at moderate temperatures.
- ✅ **Colebrook (iterative)** vs. **Haaland (explicit)** — both accepted in KTU.
- ✅ **Indian design ambient T_max = 55 °C** for tanker transit thermal-expansion design.
- ✅ **Reynolds > 4000** → turbulent; < 2300 → laminar; **2000 < Re < 4000** → transition (avoid).
- ✅ **Road fatality rate per MMTPA-1000 km is 50–100× higher** than pipeline → justifies pipeline-ization push.
- ✅ **OISD-156** is the *Bible* of road-tanker safety in India — memorize its 7 key fittings.
- ✅ **Bottom-loading > top-loading** — eliminates drop height, reduces static, enables vapour recovery.
- ✅ **Pipeline right-of-way (RoW)** — 30 m for trunk lines; **no habitation within RoW**.
- ✅ **Surge relief valves** + **non-slam check valves** + **slow-closing ESDVs** together protect against water-hammer.
- ✅ **ISGOTT** (International Safety Guide for Oil Tankers & Terminals) — 6 chapters covering tanker + terminal safety.
- ✅ **MARPOL Annex I** — prohibits operational discharge of oil at sea; requires **SOPEP** (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan) on every tanker.
- ✅ **Vapour-recovery (Stage I)** mandatory in metros per CPCB for Class A products.
- ✅ **Foam pour rate for tank fires = 6.5 L/min·m²** (per NFPA 11 / IS 636) — used in tanker parking yard design.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage and Handling of Bulk Liquefied Petroleum Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storage and Handling of Bulk Liquefied Petroleum Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)** is a flammable mixture of hydrocarbon gases, principally **propane (C₃H₈)** and **butane (C₄H₁₀)**, with small proportions of propylene, butylene, and isobutane. It is stored and transported in the **liquid phase** at moderate pressures (typically **0.7 MPa to 1.6 MPa gauge** at ambient temperature) or at atmospheric pressure when refrigerated to approximately **-42 °C**.

**Bulk LPG storage** refers to installations exceeding a threshold volume (usually **450 m³ water equivalent** or as per OISD-144 / PESO / IS 6044 norms) that require dedicated tank farms, pressure vessels, fire protection systems, and statutory licensing under the **Indian Explosives Act, 1884** and **SMPV (Static and Mobile Pressure Vessels) Rules, 2016**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Commercial Grades (KTU-Standard Naming):**
> - **Commercial Butane (CB):** Predominantly C₄ hydrocarbons, low gauge pressure (~0.7 bar at 40 °C).
> - **Commercial Propane (CP):** Predominantly C₃ hydrocarbons, higher vapor pressure (~13 bar at 40 °C).
> - **Auto LPG:** Blend matching automotive fuel specifications (HD-5 / IS 14861).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Giant Thermos Flask With a Safety Brain"

Imagine LPG as a **bottle of soda**: dissolved gas is held in liquid form by **pressure** (CO₂ in soda) rather than just by a lid. If you shake it, heat it, or pierce it, the gas escapes rapidly. Now scale this up to a **giant steel thermos flask** holding thousands of liters, with:

- **Double walls and insulation** to keep the temperature (and pressure) stable.
- A **pressure-relief lid** (safety valve) that hisses gently to prevent the bottle from bursting.
- A **fire sprinkler blanket** wrapped around it in case the kitchen (refinery) catches fire.
- A **ground wire** to prevent static sparks during refilling.

This is exactly what a **bulk LPG storage installation** is: a carefully engineered pressure vessel + transfer system + safety network, all governed by codes that account for worst-case human and environmental error.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is a KTU 2024 High-Weightage Pillar

LPG-related **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion)** is historically the **single most catastrophic accident class** in hydrocarbon industry history (e.g., **Mexico City 1984, San Juanico Disaster — 500+ fatalities**). Hence, KTU explicitly dedicates Module 3 to its engineering controls.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Units to Memorize (Board-Exam Favorites):**
> - **Pressure:** 1 bar = 0.1 MPa = 14.5 psi
> - **Volume:** 1 m³ = 35.31 ft³ = 1000 L
> - **Temperature:** T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15
> - **LPG specific gravity (liquid):** 0.51 to 0.58 at 15 °C
> - **LPG specific gravity (vapor):** 1.5 to 2.0 (relative to air)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 LPG Storage — The Three Engineering Pillars

### A. Pressure Storage (Most Common in India — Climate-Driven)

The liquid is kept at saturation pressure corresponding to the maximum design ambient temperature. No refrigeration required. Common vessel types:

| Vessel Type | Geometry | Typical Capacity | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Bullet** | Cylindrical on saddle supports | 10 m³ to 200 m³ | Small/medium depots |
| **Spherical (Horton Sphere)** | True sphere | 500 m³ to 5000 m³ | Large refineries/terminals |
| **Mounded Bullet** | Buried in sand/earth | 50 m³ to 1000 m³ | Urban / high-density sites |
| **Cylindrical Vertical** | Standing cylinder on skirt | 50 m³ to 500 m³ | Process plants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Spheres are Preferred Above 1000 m³:** A sphere has the **lowest surface-area-to-volume ratio**, minimizing heat ingress, weld length, and cost per unit volume.

### B. Refrigerated (Cryogenic) Storage

Used when **volumes > 50,000 m³** are required (large import terminals). LPG is cooled to roughly **-42 °C (propane)** and stored at **near-atmospheric pressure (~0.3 barg)** in double-walled, insulated tanks. The vapor released by heat leak is compressed and re-liquefied.

### C. Semi-Refrigerated / Pressure-Refrigerated Hybrid

Operates at intermediate temperatures (-10 °C to 0 °C) and pressures (3–5 barg). Useful for very large butane storage.

## 2.2 Engineering Design Basis — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Establish the Design Code:** ASME BPVC Section VIII Div 1, IS 2825 (Indian), BS 5500, EN 13445, or PD 5500.
2. **Determine Design Pressure (DP):** The maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP) must exceed the LPG vapor pressure at the **maximum design temperature** (typically 50 °C) with a safety margin.
3. **Select Design Metal Temperature (DMT):** Lowest expected operating temperature to choose notch-tough material.
4. **Calculate Shell & Dish Thickness:** Using pressure vessel formulae.
5. **Apply Corrosion Allowance:** Usually **1.5 mm to 3 mm** depending on service life.
6. **Specify Relief Device Sizing:** API 521 / API 520.
7. **Lay Out the Safety Chain:** ESD, flame detectors, deluge, foam pourers, dyke walls.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Card (Print This!)**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic test pressure | $P_h = 1.3 \times MAWP$ | bar — per ASME Sec VIII |
| 2 | Pneumatic test pressure | $P_t = 1.1 \times MAWP$ | bar — for non-hydraulic testable vessels |
| 3 | Cylindrical shell thickness (internal pressure) | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\eta \cdot E \cdot J - 0.6 P} + C$ | mm |
| 4 | Spherical shell thickness | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2 \eta \cdot E \cdot J - 0.2 P} + C$ | mm |
| 5 | Torispherical head thickness | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D \cdot W}{2 \eta \cdot E \cdot J - 0.2 P} + C$ | mm |
| 6 | Mass of LPG in vessel | $m = V_{liq} \cdot \rho_{liq}$ | kg |
| 7 | Liquid fill ratio (limit) | $\phi \le 0.85$ (at 40 °C) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Vapor volume generated by heat input | $V_v = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{H_v \cdot \rho_{liq}} \cdot V_{liq}$ | m³ |
| 9 | Wetted surface area (cylindrical tank) | $A_w = \pi D L + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² |
| 10 | Wetted surface area (sphere) | $A_w = \pi D^2$ | m² |
| 11 | Required relief capacity (fire case) | $Q = 70{,}600 \cdot A_w^{0.82}$ | kg/h (API 521, LNG/light HC) |
| 12 | BLEVE fireball diameter | $D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot m^{0.325}$ | m |
| 13 | BLEVE fireball duration | $t_{fb} = 0.852 \cdot m^{0.26}$ | s |
| 14 | Radiative heat flux (point source) | $q = \dfrac{2.2 \cdot \eta \cdot m \cdot H_c}{4 \pi R^2 \cdot t_{fb}}$ | kW/m² |
| 15 | Reid vapor pressure relation | $\log P = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$ | mmHg or bar |

**Where:** P = design pressure (MPa), R = inner radius (mm), η = joint efficiency, E = allowable stress (MPa), J = stress factor, C = corrosion allowance (mm), $V_{liq}$ = liquid volume, $A_w$ = wetted area, m = mass of fuel (kg), $H_v$ = latent heat of vaporization, $H_c$ = heat of combustion, η = emissivity factor (0.2–0.4), T = temperature (K).

> [!TIP]
> **Codes Examined by KTU Board Regularly:** OISD-144, OISD-117, IS 6044, IS 6042, IS 2825, ASME Sec VIII, NFPA-58, NFPA-59, SMPV Rules 2016, PESO Regulations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery LPG Mounded Bullets** reduce vapor cloud explosion risk in dense Indian urban perimeters.
- **Horton Spheres** are still the bulk-storage workhorse in IOCL, BPCL, HPCL depots.
- **Refrigerated LPG** is essential for the **Kochi–Mangalore import pipelines** and the **Jamnagar–Loni pipeline corridor**.
- BLEVE predictive formulas are used in **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** mandated by the **MoEFCC** for all hydrocarbon facilities in India.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: BLEVE Fireball Diameter (Hasegawa & Sato, 1977)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks for a **plug-and-derive** BLEVE formula. The original empirical relation is calibrated against historical accident data.

**Statement:** Given the mass of LPG released in a BLEVE event (m in kg), the maximum fireball diameter $D_{fb}$ and duration $t_{fb}$ can be estimated.

**Step 1 — Define the empirical constants from regression data:**

$$
\alpha = 6.48 \quad \text{(dimensionless, scaling coefficient for } D_{fb} \text{)}
$$

$$
\beta = 0.852 \quad \text{(dimensionless, scaling coefficient for } t_{fb} \text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Establish the functional form (mass raised to an empirical exponent):**

For diameter, a power law is fitted:

$$
D_{fb} = \alpha \cdot m^{n_D}
$$

where $n_D = 0.325$ is the experimentally determined exponent.

**Step 3 — Substitute the constants:**

$$
D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot m^{0.325}
$$

**Step 4 — For duration, the corresponding relation is:**

$$
t_{fb} = \beta \cdot m^{n_t}, \quad n_t = 0.26
$$

$$
t_{fb} = 0.852 \cdot m^{0.26}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example worked out for a 10,000 kg release:**

$$
D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot (10{,}000)^{0.325}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(10{,}000) = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10^{0.325 \times 4} = 10^{1.30} = 19.95
$$

$$
D_{fb} = 6.48 \times 19.95 = 129.3 \text{ m}
$$

$$
t_{fb} = 0.852 \cdot (10{,}000)^{0.26}
$$

$$
10^{0.26 \times 4} = 10^{1.04} = 10.96
$$

$$
t_{fb} = 0.852 \times 10.96 = 9.34 \text{ s}
$$

**Result (Worked for Board Answer):** A 10-tonne LPG BLEVE produces a fireball **~129 m in diameter** lasting about **9.3 seconds** — consistent with observed historic events (Mexico City 1984).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Fire-Case Relief Capacity (API 521 Equation)

**Statement:** Determine the minimum relieving capacity (kg/h) of a pressure relief valve (PSV) protecting a propane bullet engulfed in a pool fire.

**Step 1 — Total heat absorbed by the wetted surface under fire condition:**

API 521 specifies the heat input per unit wetted area as an empirical power function of $A_w$:

$$
Q = K \cdot A_w^{n}
$$

For light hydrocarbons (LPG, LNG), the constants are:

$$
K = 70{,}600 \quad n = 0.82
$$

Thus:

$$
Q = 70{,}600 \cdot A_w^{0.82} \quad \text{[W]}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the energy to mass flow of vapor that must be relieved:**

The heat absorbed vaporizes liquid LPG. The latent heat of vaporization $H_v$ for propane is approximately **425 kJ/kg**:

$$
\dot{m}_{relief} = \dfrac{Q}{H_v}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in kg/h:**

$$
\dot{m}_{relief} = \dfrac{70{,}600 \cdot A_w^{0.82}}{425} \times 3600 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{relief} = \dfrac{70{,}600}{425} \times 3.6 \times A_w^{0.82}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{relief} = 598 \cdot A_w^{0.82} \quad \text{[kg/h]}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conversion Pitfall:** Always verify whether the API constant is in **W (Watts)** or **Btu/h**. The W version is canonical in modern Indian OISD documentation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — LPG Storage Hazard Scenarios

| Scenario | Trigger Event | Primary Consequence | Critical Safety Barrier |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Overpressure** | Fire engulfment, blocked outlet | Vessel rupture | PSV, ESD-1 |
| **BLEVE** | Flame impingement on shell above liquid level | Fireball + missiles | Water deluge, mounding |
| **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** | Large leak + delayed ignition | Blast overpressure | Gas detection, isolation |
| **Cold Embrittlement** | Sudden LPG release at high ambient | Brittle fracture at low T | DMT-correct material selection |
| **Static Discharge** | High-rate transfer of non-conductive LPG | Spark during filling | Bonding, earthing, relaxation time |
| **Roll-over** | Density inversion in large refrigerated tanks | Sudden vapor surge | Stratification monitoring |
| **Tube Rupture** | Inner vessel failure (double-wall cryogenic) | LNG/LPG release | Vacuum/pressure monitors |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Cylindrical LPG Bullet Sizing

**Problem:** Design a horizontal cylindrical bullet to store **50 m³** of commercial propane at 40 °C. Determine: (a) shell thickness, (b) PSV relief rate.

**Given:** MAWP = 1.7 MPa (≈ 17 bar), D = 2.5 m, L = 11.0 m, E = 137 MPa, η = 1.0, J = 1.0, C = 2 mm, $H_v$ = 425 kJ/kg, propane ρ = 493 kg/m³.

**Step (a) Shell thickness:**

$$
R = D/2 = 1.25 \text{ m} = 1250 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\eta \cdot E \cdot J - 0.6 P} + C
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{1.7 \times 1250}{1 \times 137 \times 1 - 0.6 \times 1.7} + 2
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{2125}{137 - 1.02} + 2 = \dfrac{2125}{135.98} + 2
$$

$$
t = 15.63 + 2 = 17.63 \text{ mm}
$$

**Adopt standard plate thickness: 18 mm.**

**Step (b) Wetted area & relief rate:**

$$
A_w = \pi D L + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \pi (2.5)(11.0) + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi (2.5)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_w = 86.39 + 9.82 = 96.21 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{relief} = 598 \cdot (96.21)^{0.82}
$$

$$
(96.21)^{0.82} = e^{0.82 \ln 96.21} = e^{0.82 \times 4.567} = e^{3.745} = 42.30
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{relief} = 598 \times 42.30 = 25{,}295 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Result:** Select a PSV with orifice size "**T-orifice or larger**" capable of discharging **> 25,300 kg/h** of propane vapor at the set pressure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Functional Architecture: Bulk LPG Storage Installation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InletRail["INLET FROM RAIL / ROAD TANKER"] --> manifold
    InletPipe["INLET FROM PIPELINE"] --> manifold
    manifold["INLET MANIFOLD WITH BLOCK VALVES AND STRAINER"] --> bullet
    bullet["BULLET VESSEL HORIZONTAL OR SPHERICAL MWP 17 BAR"]
    bullet --> relief["PSV PRESSURE SAFETY VALVE API 520"]
    bullet --> levelG["LEVEL GAUGE RADAR OR MAGNETIC"]
    bullet --> tempG["TEMPERATURE TRANSMITTER PT 100"]
    bullet --> pressG["PRESSURE TRANSMITTER"]
    bullet --> outLiq["LIQUID OUTLET TO PUMPS"]
    bullet --> outVap["VAPOR OUTLET TO COMPRESSOR"]
    outLiq --> pump["LPG TRANSFER PUMP SEALED OR SUBMERSIBLE"]
    pump --> dispatch["DISPATCH MANIFOLD TO BOT CYLINDER FILLING"]
    outVap --> compV["VAPOR RECOVERY COMPRESSOR"]
    compV --> returnLine["VAPOR RETURN LINE TO TANKER"]
    dispatch --> bottling["CAROUSEL BOTTLING PLANT"]
    bottling --> warehouse["CYLINDER STORAGE AND DISPATCH"]
    relief --> vent["VENT STACK HEIGHT AS PER OISD 117"]
    levelG --> ESD1
    tempG --> ESD1
    pressG --> ESD1
    ESD1["ESD 1 EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN SYSTEM"] --> isolation["REMOTE ISOLATION VALVES"]
    ESD1 --> alarms["AUDIBLE VISUAL FIRE GAS ALARMS"]
    ESD1 --> deluge["WATER DELUGE VALVE ACTIVATION"]
    deluge --> bullet
```

## 4.2 Logic Flow: Fire Case Detection → Mitigation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FIRE DETECTOR HEAT OR FLAME"] --> B["FIRE PANEL RECEIVES SIGNAL"]
    B --> C{"CONFIRMED FIRE YES"}
    C -- YES --> D["ACTUATE DELUGE VALVE"]
    C -- YES --> E["ACTUATE ESD 1 BULLET ISOLATION"]
    C -- YES --> F["START FIRE WATER PUMPS"]
    C -- YES --> G["NOTIFY FIRE BRIGADE AND NEIGHBOURS"]
    C -- NO --> H["LOG ALARM FOR INVESTIGATION"]
    D --> I["PSV DISCHARGES EXCESS VAPOUR TO VENT STACK"]
    E --> J["TRANSFER PUMPS TRIP"]
    F --> K["COOL BULLET BELOW BLEVE THRESHOLD"]
    I --> L["PRESSURE STABILISED"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["INCIDENT COMMANDER ASSESSES RECOVERY"]
```

## 4.3 Process Hierarchy: Mounded vs. Above-Ground Bullet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["LPG BULK STORAGE DECISION"] --> B{"SITE LOCATION"}
    B -- "URBAN HIGH DENSITY" --> C["MOUNDED BULLET"]
    B -- "REMOTE RURAL" --> D["ABOVE GROUND HORIZONTAL BULLET"]
    B -- "VERY HIGH VOLUMES" --> E["HORTON SPHERE"]
    B -- "COASTAL MEGA TERMINAL" --> F["REFRIGERATED CYLINDRICAL"]
    C --> C1["BURIAL SAND LAYER 1 M MIN"]
    C1 --> C2["VENT PIPE TO ATMOSPHERE"]
    D --> D1["FIRE WATER RING MAIN"]
    D1 --> D2["DYKE WALL 110 PERCENT OF LARGEST TANK"]
    E --> E1["FOUNDATION PILE TO CARRY MASS"]
    F --> F1["DOUBLE WALL CRYOGENIC TANK"]
    F1 --> F2["VAPOUR RECOVERY RELIQUEFACTION"]
```

## 4.4 ESD Level Hierarchy (Per OISD-117)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SD0["SD 0 PROCESS ABNORMALITY"] --> SD1
    SD1["SD 1 LEAK OR LOCAL FIRE"] --> SD2
    SD2["SD 2 MAJOR FIRE OR VCE"] --> SD3
    SD3["SD 3 FACILITY WIDE EMERGENCY"] --> SD4
    SD4["SD 4 OFFSITE EMERGENCY EVACUATION"]
    SD1 --> action1["ISOLATE AFFECTED SECTION"]
    SD2 --> action2["STOP ALL TRANSFERS AND ISOLATE BULLET"]
    SD3 --> action3["TRIP POWER VENTILATION AND IGNITION SOURCES"]
    SD4 --> action4["ACTIVATE MUTUAL AID AND DISTRICT AUTHORITIES"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer / Definition)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define "BLEVE." State two design measures to prevent it in a bulk LPG storage bullet.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion)** is a catastrophic failure of a pressure vessel containing a superheated liquid, triggered by flame impingement on the shell above the liquid level, resulting in an instantaneous vaporization and explosive expansion that produces a large fireball and high-velocity missile fragments.

**Two design measures to prevent BLEVE:**

1. **Mounding the bullet** with sand/earth so the external surface temperature cannot rise above the critical threshold even under fire exposure.
2. **Water deluge system** that continuously wets the shell, keeping its temperature below the BLEVE limit (~120 °C for LPG steel).
3. (Optional third) **Thermal insulation** with a fire-resistive cladding, e.g., vermiculite-cement or intumescent coating.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for two measures.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between pressure storage and refrigerated storage of LPG. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pressure Storage | Refrigerated Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | 7–17 bar(g) | 0.3–0.5 bar(g) |
| Operating Temperature | Ambient (15–50 °C) | -42 °C (propane) to +0.5 °C (butane) |
| Vessel Wall | Thicker single wall | Double-wall, insulated |
| Cost per m³ Stored | High for large volumes | Low for very large volumes |
| Vapor Loss | Continuous small venting | Recompressed and re-liquefied |
| Example | 1000 m³ Horton sphere at IOCL Bottling Plant | 1,00,000 m³ refrigerated tank at Kochi LNG/LPG terminal |

*(Marks: 1.5 for distinction table + 1.5 for examples.)*

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **constructional features and safety accessories of a horizontal cylindrical LPG bullet** of capacity 100 m³. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A propane bullet of diameter 3.0 m and length 14.0 m is exposed to an external fire. Calculate the **(i)** wetted surface area, **(ii)** required relief capacity in kg/h as per API 521, and **(iii)** minimum fire-water flow rate if 5 L/min per m² of wetted area is recommended by OISD-117. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Constructional Features & Safety Accessories:**

- **Shell & Dished Ends:** SA-516 Gr. 70 carbon steel, welded, post-weld stress-relieved. Thickness calculated as per ASME Sec VIII Div 1.
- **Saddle Supports:** Two-strap type, reinforced with bearing plates and wear plates.
- **Manhole & Handholes:** Top-mounted for internal inspection; sized for personnel access.
- **Nozzles:** Liquid inlet, liquid outlet, vapor outlet, vapor return, relief valve, level instrument, pressure instrument, temperature instrument, drain.
- **Internal:** Baffle plates to dampen sloshing during transport/operation.
- **External accessories:**
  - Two Pressure Safety Valves (PSV) sized for fire case.
  - Excess Flow Valve (EFV) on each liquid line.
  - Magnetic/Radar level gauge + sight glass.
  - Pressure transmitter + dial gauge.
  - Temperature transmitter.
  - Earthing lugs (2 minimum, diametrically opposite).
  - Nameplate, ladders, platforms, davit crane for manhole cover.
  - Water deluge ring with spray nozzles covering 100% of shell.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i) Wetted Surface Area:**

$$
A_w = \pi D L + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_w = \pi \times 3.0 \times 14.0 + 2 \times \dfrac{\pi \times (3.0)^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_w = 131.95 + 14.14 = 146.09 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step (ii) Relief Capacity:**

Using $\dot{m}_{relief} = 598 \cdot A_w^{0.82}$:

$$
A_w^{0.82} = (146.09)^{0.82} = e^{0.82 \cdot \ln 146.09} = e^{0.82 \cdot 4.985} = e^{4.088}
$$

$$
A_w^{0.82} = 59.51
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{relief} = 598 \times 59.51 = 35{,}587 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step (iii) Fire-Water Flow Rate:**

$$
Q_{fw} = 5 \, \text{L/min/m}^2 \times 146.09 \, \text{m}^2 = 730.45 \, \text{L/min} \approx 12.17 \, \text{L/s}
$$

**Final Results:**
- (i) $A_w = 146.09 \text{ m}^2$
- (ii) Relief rate = **35,587 kg/h**
- (iii) Fire-water rate = **730 L/min**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **BLEVE phenomenon** with a labeled diagram. Derive the empirical equations for fireball diameter and duration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refrigerated LPG storage tank of capacity 20,000 tonnes (propane) suffers catastrophic failure. Estimate the **(i) fireball diameter**, **(ii) fireball duration**, and **(iii) thermal radiation intensity at a distance of 300 m**, assuming emissivity factor 0.3 and heat of combustion 46 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) BLEVE Phenomenon:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BLEVE** (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion) occurs when a pressure vessel containing a superheated liquid ruptures catastrophically. The liquid instantly flashes to vapor, expanding to ~250× its original volume, producing:
> 1. A large **fireball** (combustion of vaporized fuel),
> 2. High-velocity **missile fragments** from the vessel shell,
> 3. A destructive **blast overpressure** wave.

**Empirical Equations (Hasegawa & Sato, 1977):**

For a fuel mass m (kg):

$$
D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot m^{0.325} \quad \text{(m)}
$$

$$
t_{fb} = 0.852 \cdot m^{0.26} \quad \text{(s)}
$$

The point-source thermal radiation model gives heat flux at distance R:

$$
q = \dfrac{2.2 \cdot \eta \cdot m \cdot H_c}{4 \pi R^2 \cdot t_{fb}} \quad \text{(kW/m}^2\text{)}
$$

*(Refer to Section 4 for schematic of BLEVE sequence and to Section 3.1 for the derivation.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i) Fireball Diameter:**

$$
m = 20{,}000 \times 1000 = 2.0 \times 10^7 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot (2.0 \times 10^7)^{0.325}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(2.0 \times 10^7) = 7.301
$$

$$
(2.0 \times 10^7)^{0.325} = 10^{0.325 \times 7.301} = 10^{2.373} = 235.96
$$

$$
D_{fb} = 6.48 \times 235.96 = 1529 \text{ m}
$$

**Step (ii) Fireball Duration:**

$$
t_{fb} = 0.852 \cdot (2.0 \times 10^7)^{0.26}
$$

$$
10^{0.26 \times 7.301} = 10^{1.898} = 79.07
$$

$$
t_{fb} = 0.852 \times 79.07 = 67.37 \text{ s} \approx 67.4 \text{ s}
$$

**Step (iii) Thermal Radiation Intensity at R = 300 m:**

$$
q = \dfrac{2.2 \times 0.3 \times 2.0 \times 10^7 \times 46{,}000}{4 \pi (300)^2 \times 67.37}
$$

Numerator: $2.2 \times 0.3 = 0.66$, $\times 2.0 \times 10^7 = 1.32 \times 10^7$, $\times 46{,}000 = 6.072 \times 10^{11}$ kJ units → convert to kJ.

Denominator: $4 \pi (300)^2 = 4 \pi \times 90{,}000 = 1{,}130{,}973$, $\times 67.37 = 7.62 \times 10^7$.

$$
q = \dfrac{6.072 \times 10^{11}}{7.62 \times 10^7} = 7968 \text{ kW/m}^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit sanity check:** The result of ~8 MW/m² is unreasonably high at 300 m. The point-source model is **only valid for R > D_fb/2** (~765 m). For R < 765 m, the radiation must be calculated using the **solid-flame model** (Hymes formula) which gives a much lower and more realistic value. **This is a common KTU examiner trap** — students apply the wrong model region.

> **Correct approach (solid flame model):**
> View factor F = (D/2R)² (small source approximation) → q = F · E · τ, where E is the surface emissive power (≈ 350 kW/m² for propane), τ = atmospheric transmissivity ≈ 0.7.
> q ≈ (765/600)² × 350 × 0.7 ≈ **415 kW/m²** — sufficient to cause **2nd-degree burns in <10 s** and ignite wood at 300 m.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this module:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in PSV sizing:** Failing to convert API 521 heat input from W to kW to kg/h.
> 2. **Wrong fire model zone:** Applying point-source BLEVE radiation at R < 0.5 D_fb.
> 3. **Confusing commercial butane & propane properties** — different vapor pressure curves, different RVP, different DMT.
> 4. **Forgetting corrosion allowance** in the shell thickness derivation.
> 5. **Omitting PESO / SMPV statutory reference** in design-based questions — examiners expect names like **OISD-144, IS 6044, IS 2825, PESO, SMPV Rules 2016** as evidence of industry awareness.
> 6. **Skipping Mermaid sketches / diagrams** in 14-mark answers — a labelled sketch is worth at least 2 marks per question.
> 7. **Not stating codes** (ASME / OISD) alongside formulae — a formula presented without its parent code loses authenticity marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **The "30-Second" Revision Summary (Print & Sticky-Note This):**

- **LPG** = predominantly C₃ + C₄ hydrocarbons; stored as liquid under pressure or refrigeration.
- **Bulk storage vessels:** Bullet (horizontal), Horton Sphere, Mounded Bullet, Refrigerated Cylindrical.
- **Design pressure** = max vapor pressure of LPG at max design temperature + margin.
- **Design codes:** ASME Sec VIII, IS 2825, OISD-144, OISD-117, NFPA-58, SMPV Rules 2016.
- **Shell thickness (cylindrical):** $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\eta E J - 0.6P} + C$
- **Shell thickness (spherical):** $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{2 \eta E J - 0.2P} + C$
- **PSV sizing (API 521 fire case):** $\dot{m}_{relief} = 598 \cdot A_w^{0.82}$ kg/h (LPG).
- **Wetted area:** Cylinder → $\pi D L + 2 \cdot \pi D^2/4$; Sphere → $\pi D^2$.
- **BLEVE diameter:** $D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot m^{0.325}$ m.
- **BLEVE duration:** $t_{fb} = 0.852 \cdot m^{0.26}$ s.
- **Fill limit:** ≤ 85% liquid volume at 40 °C.
- **ESD levels:** SD-0 (process) → SD-1 (local) → SD-2 (major) → SD-3 (facility) → SD-4 (offsite).
- **Safety accessories (must list 5+):** PSV × 2, EFV, level gauge, pressure gauge, temperature gauge, deluge, ESD valve, earthing, flame detector, gas detector.
- **PESO / SMPV:** Statutory licensing authority for storage; non-compliance = criminal liability.
- **Hazard classes covered:** Overpressure, BLEVE, VCE, cold embrittlement, static discharge, roll-over, tube rupture.
- **KTU 2024 skill tested:** Code-aware design, formula derivation, BLEVE QRA arithmetic, ESD logic, statutory awareness.
- **Memorize these constants:** ρ_LPG(liq) ≈ 0.51–0.58, ρ_LPG(vap) ≈ 1.5–2.0, H_v(propane) ≈ 425 kJ/kg, H_c(LPG) ≈ 46 MJ/kg, K_api = 70,600 (W), n = 0.82.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drilling Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Drilling Oil # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Oil Drilling **Oil Drilling** is the engineering process of penetrating the earth's subsurface by creating a wellbore using specialized rotating equipment (drill bit + drill string) to access hydrocarbon reservoirs (oil and natural gas) trapped in porous rock formations deep below the surface. The operation combines mechanical energy, hydraulic power, and geological science to safely establish a conduit between the surface and the producing zone. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Drilling oil is a high-hazard, capital-intensive industrial activity involving the coordinated operation of hoisting, rotating, and circulating systems to construct a wellbore while maintaining well control, environmental integrity, and personnel safety. ### Key Subsystems in Drilling 1. **Hoisting System** — Lifts and lowers the drill string. 2. **Rotating System** — Provides torque to the drill bit. 3. **Circulating System** — Pumps drilling mud down the string and back up the annulus. 4. **Power System** — Supplies energy (diesel generators, AC drives). 5. **Well Control System** — Blowout Preventers (BOPs) and choke manifolds. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "Drilling an Oil Well is like Drilling a Hole in a Wall to Reach a Honeycomb inside a Building"** > > Imagine you want to extract honey trapped inside a building's hollow wall cavity, several floors below ground level. > - **The Drill String** is the long screwdriver rotating to carve the path. > - **Drilling Mud** is the soapy water that cools the screwdriver, carries the dust (cuttings) back to the surface, and **plugs the wall** so honey does not burst out unexpectedly. > - **The Blowout Preventer (BOP)** is the emergency valve you can slam shut if honey starts gushing out (a "kick"). > - **The Derrick/Mast** is the scaffolding tower that suspends and moves the screwdriver up and down. > - **Casing** is the layered steel/PVC pipe you cement in stages to reinforce the tunnel walls so they don't collapse. > > Without proper mud, plugs, valves, and casing, the operation would either **collapse, blow out, catch fire, or flood** — just like real oil drilling accidents. --- ## 1.3 Physical & Operational Constants in Drilling > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Drilling Metrics (Industry Benchmarks):** > - **Mud Weight Range:** **8.5 – 18.0 ppg (pounds per gallon)** > - **Typical Well Depth:** **1,000 m to 9,000+ m** (deepest: ~12,289 m — Kola Superdeep, Russia) > - **Surface Pressure Standard:** **300 – 10,000 psi (pounds per square inch)** > - **Rotary Speed:** **60 – 250 RPM** at the bit > - **Circulation Rate:** **300 – 1,200 gpm (gallons per minute)** > - **API Standard Pipe Sizes:** **4-1/2"**, **5-1/2"**, **7"**, **9-5/8"**, **13-3/8"** --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Phases of the Oil Drilling Process The drilling of an oil well follows a **staged engineering lifecycle** — each phase has distinct safety hazards and procedural controls. ### Phase 1: Site Preparation & Rig Mobilization - Land surveying, access road construction, foundation pad. - Rig-up of mast/derrick, substructure, mud pumps, and power generators. - **Safety Focus:** Lifting operations, dropped objects, electrical isolation. ### Phase 2: Spudding In (Conductor Hole) - Drilling of the large-diameter **conductor hole (26"–36")** to ~30–50 m. - Setting and cementing the **conductor casing**. - **Safety Focus:** Shallow gas detection, shallow water kick. ### Phase 3: Surface Hole & Surface Casing - Drilling the **surface hole (17-1/2")** to ~300–600 m. - Running and cementing **surface casing (13-3/8")** to protect freshwater aquifers. - **Safety Focus:** Underground blowout risk, cement slurry handling. ### Phase 4: Intermediate Hole & Intermediate Casing - Drilling the **intermediate section (12-1/4")** to 1,500–3,000 m. - Setting **intermediate casing (9-5/8")** to isolate unstable formations and abnormal pressure zones. - **Safety Focus:** Kick detection, mud weight optimization. ### Phase 5: Production Hole & Production Casing - Drilling the **production section (8-1/2" or 6")** to total depth (TD). - Setting **production casing (7" or 5-1/2")** and **production liner**. - **Safety Focus:** Reservoir pressure management, H₂S exposure. ### Phase 6: Well Completion - Perforating the casing at the producing zone. - Installing **tubing, packer, Christmas tree (wellhead assembly)**. - **Safety Focus:** High-pressure wellhead testing, ESD systems. --- ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Subsystem > [!NOTE] > - **Why Drilling Mud?** — Provides **hydrostatic pressure** to balance formation pressure, **lubricates** the bit, **carries cuttings** to surface, and **seals permeable formations**. > - **Why BOPs?** — Last mechanical barrier to prevent uncontrolled flow of formation fluids to surface (a **blowout**). > - **Why Multiple Casings?** — Each casing string isolates a different geological zone, providing **redundancy in well integrity**. --- ## 2.3 Major Hazards in Drilling Operations | # | Hazard Category | Specific Risk | Consequence | |---|----------------|---------------|-------------| | 1 | **Mechanical** | Dropped objects from derrick | Fatal impact injuries | | 2 | **Pressure** | Blowout (uncontrolled kick) | Explosion, fire, well loss | | 3 | **Fire/Explosion** | Hydrocarbon release + ignition | Pool fire, jet fire, BLEVE | | 4 | **Toxic Exposure** | H₂S (hydrogen sulfide) release | Fatal at >700 ppm | | 5 | **Confined Space** | Mud pit, cellar, tank entry | Asphyxiation, engulfment | | 6 | **Ergonomic** | Manual handling of pipe | Musculoskeletal disorders | | 7 | **Environmental** | Spill of mud, diesel, crude | Soil/water contamination | | 8 | **Electrical** | Generator faults, lightning | Arc flash, ignition source | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical Formulas for Drilling Safety & Engineering Calculations** | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application | |-------------|----------|-------------------|-------------| | **Hydrostatic Pressure** | $P_{hs} = 0.052 \times \rho_{mud} \times TVD$ | $\rho_{mud}$ in ppg, TVD in ft, P in psi | Bottom-hole pressure control | | **Formation Pressure Gradient** | $G_f = 0.052 \times \rho_{w,eq}$ | $\rho_{w,eq}$ in ppg, G in psi/ft | Kick detection threshold | | **Kick Tolerance (MAASP)** | $MAASP = \dfrac{SF \times G_f \times TVD - P_{sur}}{1 + SF}$ | SF = Safety Factor (typically 0.5–1.0) | Max allowable wellhead shut-in pressure | | **Mud Weight Increase Needed** | $\Delta \rho = \dfrac{\Delta P}{0.052 \times TVD}$ | $\Delta P$ in psi, $\rho$ in ppg | Kill mud weight calculation | | **Stripping / Snubbing Pressure** | $P_{strip} = P_{ann} - P_{fric}$ | $P_{ann}$ = annular, $P_{fric}$ = friction | Well control while moving pipe | | **Reynolds Number (Mud Flow)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | v = velocity, D = pipe ID, μ = viscosity | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) | | **ECD (Equivalent Circulating Density)** | $ECD = \rho_{mud} + \dfrac{\Delta P_{fric}}{0.052 \times TVD}$ | $\Delta P_{fric}$ = frictional loss | Fracture gradient margin check | | **Bleed-off Volume per Stroke** | $V_b = A_p \times L_s$ | $A_p$ = piston area, $L_s$ = stroke length | BOP accumulator capacity design | | **API 16D Accumulator Capacity** | $V_{req} = (V_B \times N_R) + \text{Reserves}$ | $N_R$ = number of ram preventers | BOP stack design per API | | **Heat of Combustion (Methane)** | $\Delta H_c = -890 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Stoichiometric basis | Fire/explosion energy estimation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always include **units** in your final numerical answer. Marks are often deducted for missing or inconsistent units in the formula sheet application. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The drilling industry is the foundation of the **upstream hydrocarbon value chain**. Mastery of drilling safety is essential for: - **Well Control Engineers** designing BOP stacks for HPHT (High-Pressure/High-Temperature) wells. - **Drilling Supervisors** managing kick events on offshore rigs (e.g., Deepwater Horizon, 2010). - **HSE Officers** implementing **API RP 500, 505, 521, 53** safety standards. - **Process Safety Engineers** performing **HAZOP, Bow-Tie, and LOPA** analyses on drilling systems. - **Regulatory Compliance** with **OISD, OSHA 29 CFR 1910, ISO 17776**, and **DNV-OS-E101** standards. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Mud Pressure (The Heart of Well Control) The fundamental equation of well control — **hydrostatic pressure exerted by a column of drilling mud**. ### Statement The hydrostatic pressure at any depth in a static fluid column is given by: $$P_{hs} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$ ### Step-by-Step Conversion to Field Units (ppg, ft, psi) **Step 1:** Start with the SI form. $$P_{hs} \text{ (Pa)} = \rho \text{ (kg/m}^3) \times g \text{ (9.81 m/s}^2) \times h \text{ (m)}$$ **Step 2:** Convert density from $\text{kg/m}^3$ to **ppg (pounds per gallon)**. $$1 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.008345 \text{ ppg}$$ So: $$\rho \text{ (ppg)} = \rho \text{ (kg/m}^3) \times 0.008345$$ **Step 3:** Convert height from meters to **feet**. $$1 \text{ m} = 3.2808 \text{ ft}$$ So: $$h \text{ (ft)} = h \text{ (m)} \times 3.2808$$ **Step 4:** Convert pressure from Pa to **psi**. $$1 \text{ psi} = 6894.76 \text{ Pa}$$ So: $$P_{hs} \text{ (psi)} = \dfrac{P \text{ (Pa)}}{6894.76}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute all conversion factors into the original equation. $$P_{hs} = \rho \text{ (ppg)} \times \dfrac{1}{0.008345} \text{ (kg/m}^3\text{/ppg)} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times h \text{ (ft)} \times \dfrac{1}{3.2808} \text{ (m/ft)} \times \dfrac{1}{6894.76} \text{ (Pa/psi)}$$ **Step 6:** Compute the constant multiplier. $$\text{Constant} = \dfrac{9.81}{0.008345 \times 3.2808 \times 6894.76} = 0.05195 \approx 0.052$$ **Step 7:** The final field-friendly equation. $$\boxed{P_{hs} = 0.052 \times \rho_{mud} \times TVD}$$ Where: - $P_{hs}$ = hydrostatic pressure in **psi** - $\rho_{mud}$ = mud weight in **ppg** - $TVD$ = true vertical depth in **ft** > [!NOTE] > The constant **0.052** is the cornerstone of all drilling hydraulics calculations. Memorize it. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Allowable Annular Surface Pressure (MAASP) When a kick is detected, the well is shut-in, and the wellhead pressure rises. We must limit this pressure to avoid fracturing the open hole at the weakest point (typically just below the previous casing shoe). ### Statement The MAASP is the maximum surface pressure that can be applied to the casing annulus without exceeding the **formation fracture pressure** at the casing shoe. $$MAASP = \dfrac{\left(P_{frac} \times SF\right) - P_{hs,shoe}}{1 - SF}$$ For a typical safety factor approach (used in many industry texts): $$MAASP = \dfrac{SF \times G_f \times TVD - P_{sur}}{1 + SF}$$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Identify the critical pressure point — the casing shoe. $$P_{frac,shoe} = 0.052 \times \rho_{frac} \times TVD_{shoe}$$ **Step 2:** The hydrostatic pressure inside the wellbore at the shoe (with the existing mud) is: $$P_{hs,shoe} = 0.052 \times \rho_{mud} \times TVD_{shoe}$$ **Step 3:** The pressure differential that the shoe can withstand is: $$\Delta P_{max} = P_{frac,shoe} - P_{hs,shoe} = 0.052 \times (\rho_{frac} - \rho_{mud}) \times TVD_{shoe}$$ **Step 4:** In a shut-in well, the total bottom-hole pressure at the shoe equals: $$P_{bh,shoe} = P_{hs,mud,shoe} + P_{wellhead,ann}$$ **Step 5:** Setting $P_{bh,shoe} = P_{frac,shoe}$ and applying a **safety factor SF** (typically 0.5 to 1.0 psi/psi): $$0.052 \times \rho_{mud} \times TVD_{shoe} + MAASP \times SF = 0.052 \times \rho_{frac} \times TVD_{shoe} \times SF$$ **Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate MAASP. $$MAASP = \dfrac{0.052 \times TVD_{shoe} \times (SF \cdot \rho_{frac} - \rho_{mud})}{SF}$$ **Step 7:** Final compact form. $$\boxed{MAASP = SF \times 0.052 \times TVD_{shoe} \times \rho_{frac} - 0.052 \times TVD_{shoe} \times \rho_{mud}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Kick Detection Methods | # | Detection Method | Indicator | Response Time | Reliability | |---|-----------------|-----------|---------------|-------------| | 1 | **Pit Level Increase** | Mud pit volume rises | 1–3 minutes | High | | 2 | **Flow Rate Mismatch** | Outflow > Inflow | Real-time | Very High | | 3 | **ROP (Rate of Penetration) Increase** | Driller notes sudden acceleration | 30 sec | Moderate | | 4 | **Standpipe Pressure Drop** | Pressure decreases unexpectedly | Real-time | Moderate | | 5 | **Gas Chromatograph** | CH₄, C₂H₄, H₂S detected in mud | 1–2 minutes | Very High | | 6 | **Connection Gas** | Pressure pulse on connections | 5–10 minutes | High | | 7 | **Drilling Parameter** | Weight on bit drop, torque variation | Real-time | High | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequent Exam Topic:** "List the primary indicators of a kick and the immediate shut-in procedure." A kick must be **confirmed by at least two independent indicators** before shut-in. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: BOP Stack Components | BOP Component | Function | Pressure Rating | Activation | |---------------|----------|----------------|------------| | **Annular Preventer** | Seals around any shape (drill pipe, kelly, open hole) | 5,000–10,000 psi | Hydraulic | | **Ram Preventer (Blind/Shear)** | Shears drill pipe and seals well | 10,000–20,000 psi | Hydraulic | | **Ram Preventer (Pipe)** | Seals around specific pipe size | 10,000–20,000 psi | Hydraulic | | **Choke Manifold** | Controls back-pressure on kick | 10,000 psi | Manual/Hydraulic | | **Kill Line** | Pumps kill mud downhole | 10,000 psi | Pump-driven | | **Accumulator** | Stores hydraulic energy for BOP closure | 3,000 psi pre-charge | Self-contained | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Drilling Rig Subsystems > [!NOTE] > The following **Mermaid Block Diagram** maps the interactions between the major drilling rig subsystems, focusing on the safety-critical control loops. ```mermaid flowchart TB PowerSource["POWER GENERATION
Diesel Generators 1-4 MW AC"] HoistingSys["HOISTING SYSTEM
Drawworks Crown Block Travelling Block Hook"] RotatingSys["ROTATING SYSTEM
Top Drive Kelly Drill String Rotary Table"] CirculationSys["CIRCULATING SYSTEM
Mud Pumps Standpipe Kelly Hose Mud Pit Shale Shaker"] Wellbore["WELLBORE
Open Hole Annulus Drill Bit Casing Strings"] Formation["FORMATION
Reservoir Pressure Pore Fluid"] BOPStack["WELL CONTROL BOP STACK
Annular Preventer Ram Preventer Choke Manifold Kill Line"] Wellhead["WELLHEAD CHRISTMAS TREE
Master Valve Wing Valves Choke"] MudEng["MUD ENGINEERING UNIT
Mud Weight Mixing Monitoring"] KickDetect["KICK DETECTION SYSTEM
Pit Level Flow Sensor PWD Gas Chromatograph"] HSEControl["HSE CONTROL ROOM
SCADA ESD System H2S Sensors Fire Detection"] Personnel["PERSONNEL PROTECTION
PPE Gas Masks Fall Arrest"] EnvProtect["ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION
Bunding Drainage Spill Kit"] PowerSource --> HoistingSys PowerSource --> RotatingSys PowerSource --> CirculationSys PowerSource --> BOPStack PowerSource --> HSEControl HoistingSys --> RotatingSys RotatingSys --> Wellbore CirculationSys --> Wellbore MudEng --> CirculationSys Wellbore --> BOPStack BOPStack --> Wellhead Formation --> Wellbore Wellbore --> Formation Wellbore --> KickDetect KickDetect --> HSEControl KickDetect --> BOPStack BOPStack --> HSEControl Wellhead --> HSEControl HSEControl --> Personnel HSEControl --> EnvProtect HSEControl --> CirculationSys ``` --- ## 4.2 Kick Detection and Well Control Flow > [!NOTE] > The following flowchart illustrates the **decision tree** for kick detection, confirmation, and shut-in. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Drilling in Progress"]) --> Mon1["Monitor Pit Level
Flow Rate
Standpipe Pressure
ROP"] Mon1 --> Kick1{"Primary Kick
Indicator
Detected"} Kick1 -- "No" --> Mon1 Kick1 -- "Yes" --> Conf{"Confirmed by
Second Indicator"} Conf -- "No" --> Mon1 Conf -- "Yes" --> FlowCheck{"Flow In or
Flow Out
Dominant"} FlowCheck -- "Inflow" --> ShutIn["SUSPEND DRILLING
HOIST OFF BOTTOM
STOP PUMPS
OPEN BOPs"] FlowCheck -- "No Inflow
Steady State" --> Mon1 ShutIn --> CloseAnnul["Close Annular
Preventer"] CloseAnnul --> ReadPress["Read SIDPP
and SICP"] ReadPress --> Strokes["Calculate Strokes
and Pressures
for Kill Sheet"] Strokes --> Circ["Circulate Kick Out
via Drillers Method
or Wait and Weight"] Circ --> Verify{"Pit Level
Stable
Gas Zero"} Verify -- "No" --> Circ Verify -- "Yes" --> Resume(["Resume Normal
Drilling
Increase Mud Weight"]) ``` --- ## 4.3 Bow-Tie Diagram — Blowout Prevention Safety Barrier Model > [!NOTE] > A **Bow-Tie Diagram** is the KTU-preferred safety analysis tool for high-consequence events. Here, the **TOP EVENT** is "Blowout" with **PREVENTION BARRIERS** on the left and **MITIGATION BARRIERS** on the right. ```mermaid flowchart LR Threats["THREATS
1 Formation Overpressure
2 Loss of Mud Weight
3 Swabbing Effect
4 Failure of Cement Job
5 Equipment Failure"] PrevB1["BARRIER 1
Mud Hydrostatic Pressure
Correct Mud Weight"] PrevB2["BARRIER 2
BOP Shear Ram
Annular Preventer"] PrevB3["BARRIER 3
Pressure Relief
Choke Manifold"] PrevB4["BARRIER 4
Well Monitoring
Pit Level Sensors"] TopEvent[/"TOP EVENT
UNCONTROLLED BLOWOUT
Surface or Underground"/] MitB1["MITIGATION 1
Emergency Shutdown ESD"] MitB2["MITIGATION 2
Capping Stack
Hot Tap Operation"] MitB3["MITIGATION 3
Firewater Deluge
Ignition Source Control"] MitB4["MITIGATION 4
H2S Gas Detection
Personnel Evacuation"] MitB5["MITIGATION 5
Oil Spill Response
Booms Skimmers"] Conseq["CONSEQUENCES
Loss of Well
Fatality from Explosion
Environmental Disaster
Asset Destruction"] Threats --> PrevB1 --> TopEvent Threats --> PrevB2 --> TopEvent Threats --> PrevB3 --> TopEvent Threats --> PrevB4 --> TopEvent TopEvent --> MitB1 --> Conseq TopEvent --> MitB2 --> Conseq TopEvent --> MitB3 --> Conseq TopEvent --> MitB4 --> Conseq TopEvent --> MitB5 --> Conseq ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** In a properly engineered well, every prevention barrier should have a **validated test certificate** and a **documented inspection schedule** per **API 53** and **API 16A/16D/16AR**. Loss of any two consecutive barriers constitutes a **Major Accident Hazard (MAH)** under KTU safety assessment criteria. --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a "kick" in oil drilling. List any **four** primary indicators of a kick. **Model Answer:** A **kick** is an unintentional entry of formation fluids (oil, gas, or water) into the wellbore, caused when the **bottom-hole pressure (BHP) drops below the formation pore pressure**. It is the precursor to a blowout if not controlled immediately. The four primary kick indicators are: 1. **Increase in mud pit volume** — formation fluids displace mud out of the well. 2. **Increase in outflow rate over inflow rate** — measured at the flow sensor/shale shaker. 3. **Sudden increase in Rate of Penetration (ROP)** — reduced hydrostatic pressure softens the rock. 4. **Decrease in standpipe pump pressure** — reduced friction loss as the well "swallows" mud. *(Any four valid indicators accepted.)* > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is a Blowout Preventer (BOP)? Name the **three main types** of BOPs used on a drilling rig. **Model Answer:** A **Blowout Preventer (BOP)** is a large mechanical and hydraulic device installed on top of the wellhead, designed to **seal, control, and monitor the wellbore** in case of a kick or uncontrolled formation fluid flow. The three main types of BOPs are: 1. **Annular Preventer** — A rubber sealing element that closes around any shape (drill pipe, kelly, kelly cock, or open hole). Used as the first line of defense. 2. **Ram Preventer (Pipe Ram)** — Seals around a specific drill pipe size using steel rams with elastomer seals. 3. **Ram Preventer (Blind/Shear Ram)** — Has no opening; closes and shears the drill pipe entirely, sealing an open hole. --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** > > **(a)** Explain the complete **oil well drilling process** with neat sketch. Discuss the functions of **drilling mud** in detail. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the **Blowout Preventer (BOP) stack** in detail. List and explain any **five major hazards** in drilling operations. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Drilling Process & Mud Functions **Drilling Process — Step by Step:** 1. **Site Preparation & Rig-up:** The location is cleared, foundation pad is laid, and the drilling rig (derrick/mast, substructure, drawworks, mud pumps, BOPs, and power generators) is assembled. 2. **Conductor Hole:** A large-diameter hole (26"–36") is drilled to ~30–50 m and a **conductor casing** is cemented to stabilize the topsoil and prevent wash-out. 3. **Surface Hole & Casing:** The surface hole (17-1/2") is drilled to ~300–600 m. **Surface casing (13-3/8")** is set and cemented to protect freshwater aquifers and support the BOP stack. 4. **BOP Installation:** The BOP stack is nippled up on the surface casing and pressure-tested. 5. **Intermediate Hole & Casing:** The intermediate section (12-1/4") is drilled through unstable formations and abnormal pressure zones. **Intermediate casing (9-5/8")** is set and cemented. 6. **Production Hole & Casing:** The production section (8-1/2" or 6") is drilled to **Total Depth (TD)**. **Production casing (7" or 5-1/2")** or a liner is set. 7. **Well Completion:** The casing is **perforated** at the producing zone, and **tubing, packer, and Christmas tree** are installed for production. **Functions of Drilling Mud (Seven Functions):** 1. **Hydrostatic Pressure Control:** Provides bottom-hole pressure to balance formation pore pressure, preventing kicks and blowouts. 2. **Cuttings Transport:** Carries rock cuttings from the bit to the surface via the annulus. 3. **Bit Cooling & Lubrication:** Reduces frictional heat and extends bit life. 4. **Formation Stabilization:** The mud cake (filter cake) seals permeable formations and supports the wellbore wall. 5. **Suspension of Cuttings:** When circulation stops, the mud's gel strength suspends cuttings to prevent sag. 6. **Pressure Transmission:** Enables downhole pressure measurements and well control operations. 7. **Corrosion Control:** Inhibits corrosion of drill string and casing. #### Part (b) — BOP Stack and Drilling Hazards **BOP Stack Description:** The BOP stack is a series of large valves installed on the wellhead. From top to bottom, a typical BOP stack contains: - **Annular Preventer** (Top) — Universal seal around any pipe shape. - **Blind/Shear Ram Preventer** — Shears pipe and seals open hole. - **Pipe Ram Preventer** — Seals around a specific pipe size. - **Drilling Spool** — Connects to the choke and kill lines. - **Wellhead Connector** — Casing head flange. The stack is connected to a **hydraulic accumulator** (per API 16D) that supplies fluid for closure, and a **remote control panel** located at least 25 m from the well. **Five Major Drilling Hazards:** 1. **Blowout:** Uncontrolled release of formation fluids due to kick mismanagement. *Mitigation:* BOPs, mud weight control, kick drill practice. 2. **H₂S Exposure:** Hydrogen sulfide is a toxic, flammable gas. At >700 ppm, it can cause instant fatality. *Mitigation:* H₂S monitoring, SCBA, evacuation plans, wellbore neutralizers. 3. **Fire and Explosion:** Hydrocarbon release + ignition source (welding, smoking, hot work). *Mitigation:* Hot work permits, gas testing, firewater deluge system, classified electrical equipment (ATEX/IECEx). 4. **Dropped Objects:** Pipe, tools, or blocks falling from the derrick/mast. *Mitigation:* Dropped object prevention program, tool tethers, restricted access zones, hard hats with chin straps. 5. **Confined Space Hazards:** Entry into mud pits, shale shaker hoppers, or storage tanks. *Mitigation:* Permit-to-work, gas testing, standby rescue team, ventilation. --- > ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** > > **(a)** Explain the **principle of well control** with a clear sketch of hydrostatic pressure balance. Derive the **hydrostatic pressure equation** $P_{hs} = 0.052 \times \rho \times TVD$. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Discuss the **bow-tie analysis** of a drilling blowout. Identify the **prevention barriers** and **mitigation barriers** separately. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Well Control Principle & Derivation **Principle of Well Control:** Well control is the practice of maintaining the **bottom-hole pressure (BHP) ≥ formation pore pressure at all times** to prevent formation fluid entry. The BHP is composed of: $$BHP = P_{hs,mud} + P_{annulus,surface} - P_{friction}$$ In **primary well control**, the mud hydrostatic pressure alone exceeds the formation pressure. In **secondary well control**, the BOP stack is used to shut in the well and circulate the kick out. **Sketch Description (for exam paper):** ``` Surface | Mud ===== (Drilling Mud Column) Col- | | ρ_mud = 10 ppg umn | | TVD = 10,000 ft | | <----- Pore Pressure of Formation -----> | v Bottom Hole P_pore = 0.465 psi/ft × 10,000 = 4650 psi P_hs = 0.052 × 10 × 10,000 = 5200 psi Safety Margin = 550 psi ``` **Derivation of $P_{hs} = 0.052 \times \rho \times TVD$:** *(Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. For brevity, key steps are:)* **Step 1:** Start with $P_{hs} = \rho g h$ in SI units. **Step 2:** Substitute $\rho \text{ (kg/m}^3) = \rho_{ppg} / 0.008345$. **Step 3:** Substitute $h \text{ (m)} = h_{ft} / 3.2808$. **Step 4:** Substitute $P \text{ (Pa)} = P_{psi} \times 6894.76$. **Step 5:** Combine constants: $$K = \dfrac{9.81 \times 1}{0.008345 \times 3.2808 \times 6894.76} = 0.05195 \approx 0.052$$ **Step 6:** Final field equation: $$\boxed{P_{hs} = 0.052 \times \rho_{mud} \times TVD}$$ **Numerical Example (for further clarity):** Given $\rho = 12$ ppg, $TVD = 8,000$ ft: $$P_{hs} = 0.052 \times 12 \times 8,000 = 4,992 \text{ psi}$$ #### Part (b) — Bow-Tie Analysis of Blowout *(Refer to the Mermaid Bow-Tie diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes for the complete model.)* **Threats (Left Side):** 1. Formation overpressure (geological anomaly). 2. Loss of mud column (lost circulation). 3. Swabbing effect (pipe movement). 4. Cement job failure. 5. Equipment failure (BOP leak, seal failure). **Top Event:** Uncontrolled blowout — surface or underground. **Prevention Barriers (between Threats and Top Event):** 1. **Mud Hydrostatic Pressure** — Correct mud weight maintained at all times. 2. **BOP Stack** — Shear/annular preventers ready and tested per API 53. 3. **Pressure Relief System** — Choke manifold operational. 4. **Well Monitoring** — Pit level sensors, flow sensors, gas chromatograph. 5. **Competent Personnel** — IWCF-certified drillers, regular drills. **Mitigation Barriers (between Top Event and Consequences):** 1. **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** — Surface and subsea ESD valves. 2. **Capping Stack / Hot Tap** — Mechanical intervention to seal the well. 3. **Firewater Deluge System** — Cooling and ignition control. 4. **H₂S Detection & Evacuation** — Toxic gas response and muster. 5. **Oil Spill Response** — Booms, skimmers, dispersant application. **Consequences (Right Side):** - Loss of the well (months of redrill cost). - Fatality from explosion, fire, or toxic exposure. - Long-term environmental contamination. - Severe financial loss (>$1 Billion for offshore deepwater incidents). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Drilling Safety Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the units in the hydrostatic equation** — Always write $\rho$ in ppg and $TVD$ in feet. Writing $\rho$ in kg/m³ and $TVD$ in meters will give a wrong answer 100× off. > > 2. **Confusing Primary and Secondary Well Control** — Primary = mud hydrostatic pressure. Secondary = BOP operation. Tertiary = capping/stimulation. This 3-tier classification is a frequent KTU question. > > 3. **Listing "BOP" as one device** — The BOP is a STACK containing multiple preventers. Marks require specific naming of **Annular, Blind/Shear Ram, Pipe Ram**. > > 4. **Missing the role of choke manifold in well control** — The choke is what regulates back-pressure while circulating the kick out. Many students forget this and lose 2–3 marks. > > 5. **Not referencing API Standards** — KTU expects knowledge of **API 53 (BOP systems), API 16A/16D (Equipment), API RP 500/505 (Hazardous area classification)**. Quoting the right code impresses the examiner. > > 6. **Ignoring H₂S in hazard analysis** — A drilling safety question without H₂S mitigation is considered incomplete under KTU 2024 NEP-aligned CO3 (Engineering Safety in Hydrocarbon Operations). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Drilling Oil"** > > ✅ **Drilling is a 6-stage process:** Conductor → Surface → Intermediate → Production → Completion → Production. > > ✅ **Drilling mud has 7 functions:** Hydrostatic balance, cuttings transport, cooling, lubrication, wall stabilization, cuttings suspension, corrosion control. > > ✅ **Constant 0.052** is derived from unit conversion (SI to Field Units: ppg, ft, psi). Use it for $P_{hs} = 0.052 \cdot \rho \cdot TVD$. > > ✅ **Kick** = Formation pressure > Mud hydrostatic pressure. Detection by 7 indicators (pit level, flow, ROP, pressure, gas chromatograph, connection gas, drilling parameters). > > ✅ **BOP Stack** = Annular + Blind/Shear Ram + Pipe Ram + Drilling Spool + Kill/Choke Lines. Hydraulic accumulator per API 16D. > > ✅ **Well Control Methods:** Driller's Method (2 circulations) vs. Wait-and-Weight Method (1 circulation with heavier kill mud). > > ✅ **Five Major Hazards:** Blowout, H₂S exposure, Fire/Explosion, Dropped Objects, Confined Space. > > ✅ **Bow-Tie Model:** Threats → Prevention Barriers → Top Event → Mitigation Barriers → Consequences. > > ✅ **Key Standards:** API 53, API 16A/16D, API RP 500, OISD-GDN-180, ISO 17776, DNV-OS-E101, OSHA 29 CFR 1910. > > ✅ **H₂S Thresholds:** 10 ppm (TLV), 100 ppm (SCBA mandatory), 700 ppm (immediate fatality). > > ✅ **MAASP formula:** Always apply a **safety factor (SF)** to the formation fracture pressure at the casing shoe. > > ✅ **Primary well control** uses mud weight; **Secondary** uses BOPs; **Tertiary** uses capping stacks. > > ✅ **ESD system** must be tested weekly; **BOP stack** pressure-tested every 14 days or after any well event. > > ✅ **PPE for drilling floor:** Hard hat (with chin strap), safety boots, flame-resistant coveralls (FRC), safety glasses, gloves, hearing protection, H₂S personal monitor. > > ✅ **KTU Keyword Mastery:** Whenever the question says "well control" — the answer must involve **MAASP, kill sheet, kill weight, BOP, choke manifold, accumulator**. ---

topics.title: Techniques and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques and Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition: Techniques and Equipment in Hydrocarbon Industry Safety

In the context of the **hydrocarbon industry** (petroleum refining, natural gas processing, petrochemicals, and oil & gas exploration), **Techniques and Equipment** refer to the engineered methodologies, instruments, and physical hardware deployed to **prevent, detect, control, and mitigate** process hazards. These encompass the three pillars of process safety: **prevention** (stopping incidents), **detection** (identifying deviations), and **mitigation** (limiting consequences).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT745 - Module 4):**
> The syllabus frames Techniques and Equipment under the *engineering control* and *mitigation* hierarchy of the **Hierarchy of Controls** (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative → PPE). Module 4 specifically addresses the *Engineering Controls* layer, which is the most reliable line of defense in hydrocarbon facilities.

### 1.2 Classification Overview

The techniques and equipment in a hydrocarbon facility can be broadly grouped into **five functional classes**:

| Class | Primary Function | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Inspection & NDT | Detect material defects before failure | Ultrasonic, Radiography, MPI |
| Process Control & SIS | Maintain safe operating envelope | ESD, PLC, SCADA, DCS |
| Fire & Explosion Protection | Suppress/contain fires and blasts | Sprinklers, Foam systems, BLEVEs control |
| Gas & Hazard Detection | Sense leaks of flammable/toxic substances | Catalytic bead, IR, LEL meters |
| Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) | Protect the human worker | SCBA, flame-retardant suits, gas masks |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automobile Safety Suite"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Think of a refinery as a high-performance race car.**
> - **Inspection (NDT)** is like a pit-crew mechanic checking the engine and brakes *before* the race.
> - **Process Control (SIS/ESD)** is the car's automatic braking system and traction control — it reacts the instant something goes wrong.
> - **Fire Protection** is the fire suppression foam can and roll-cage — designed to contain damage if everything else fails.
> - **Gas Detection** is the smoke and CO₂ sensor inside the cabin — it gives early warning.
> - **PPE** is the driver's helmet, fireproof suit, and harness — the last line of defense protecting the human inside.

In a refinery, all five layers work *in tandem*, not in isolation. A failure in one layer is meant to be caught by the next. This is the **"Defense-in-Depth"** philosophy, codified in standards like **API 780** and **IEC 61511**.

### 1.4 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

- **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)** — the minimum concentration of a gas in air that can ignite. **Standard reference: 100% LEL**.
- **STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit)** — typically a **15-minute** time-weighted average exposure.
- **TWA (Time-Weighted Average)** — usually over an **8-hour** workday.
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** — discrete levels **1, 2, 3, 4** as per **IEC 61508/61511**.
- **NDT Reference Standards**: **ASME Section V**, **API 570**, **ASNT SNT-TC-1A**.
- **Flash Point** of common hydrocarbon solvents: gasoline ≈ **−40 °C**, diesel ≈ **55–65 °C**.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam answers, always quote the **standard name + clause/year** (e.g., *"As per API 670 for machinery protection..."*). Examiners allocate extra marks for citing the correct governing standard.
