## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) Techniques

NDT is the *backbone* of asset integrity management in refineries. It allows inspection **without** shutting down or damaging equipment.

#### 2.1.1 Radiographic Testing (RT)
- Uses **X-rays** (for thin sections) or **Gamma rays** (from Iridium-192 or Cobalt-60 isotopes) for thick sections.
- Records internal flaws on a film or digital detector.
- **Sensitivity**: ~**1–2% of wall thickness**.

#### 2.1.2 Ultrasonic Testing (UT)
- Uses high-frequency sound waves (**1 MHz – 25 MHz**).
- Two modes:
  - **Pulse-Echo**: A single probe both transmits and receives.
  - **Time-of-Flight Diffraction (TOFD)**: Two probes, used for accurate *sizing* of cracks.
- Best for **volumetric** defects and **thickness measurement**.

#### 2.1.3 Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)
- Applied to **ferromagnetic materials only** (carbon steel, low-alloy steel).
- Magnetizes the part; iron particles cluster at flux-leakage sites, revealing **surface and near-surface** cracks.
- Cannot detect subsurface flaws.

#### 2.1.4 Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)
- Liquid penetrant seeps into surface cracks → developer draws it back out → visible indication.
- Works on **all non-porous** materials including stainless steel and aluminum.

#### 2.1.5 Eddy Current Testing (ET)
- Uses electromagnetic induction. **Conductivity** changes reveal defects.
- Excellent for **surface-breaking** cracks in **non-ferrous** tubes (heat exchangers, condensers).

### 2.2 Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) & Emergency Shutdown (ESD)

The **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is the core logic unit. A typical SIS loop consists of:

$$\text{SIS Loop} = \underbrace{\text{Sensor}}_{\text{Detect}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Logic Solver}}_{\text{Decide}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Final Element}}_{\text{Actuate}}$$

- **Sensor**: Pressure transmitter, flame detector, gas detector.
- **Logic Solver**: Safety PLC (e.g., Honeywell FSC, Siemens S7-F, Triconex).
- **Final Element**: Solenoid-operated block valve, motor contactor, deluge valve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) — Per IEC 61508:**
> - **SIL 1**: PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand) between **$10^{-1}$ and $10^{-2}$**
> - **SIL 2**: PFD between **$10^{-2}$ and $10^{-3}$**
> - **SIL 3**: PFD between **$10^{-3}$ and $10^{-4}$**
> - **SIL 4**: PFD between **$10^{-4}$ and $10^{-5}$**

A higher SIL demands a *more rigorous* design, testing, and proof-test interval.

### 2.3 Pressure Relief Devices

A **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** is a *mechanical* last-line-of-defense device. The set pressure is the inlet pressure at which the valve begins to open.

#### Sizing Formula (API 520 / API 521)

$$A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_v \cdot \sqrt{P_1 \cdot \rho_1}}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Required effective discharge area (mm² or in²)
- $W$ = Required flow capacity (kg/hr or lb/hr)
- $C$ = Coefficient from API 520 (depends on gas/vapor/liquid)
- $K_d$ = Effective coefficient of discharge (typically **0.95** for certified valves)
- $K_b$ = Backpressure correction factor
- $K_v$ = Viscosity correction factor (liquid only)
- $P_1$ = Upstream relieving pressure (bara or psia)
- $\rho_1$ = Fluid density at relieving conditions (kg/m³ or lb/ft³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **fire case** sizing in hydrocarbon service, the relief rate is typically calculated as per **API 521 §5.15** using the **latent heat** of the fluid:
>
> $$Q = \frac{\dot{m}_{in}}{\Delta H_{vap}}$$
>
> where $\dot{m}_{in}$ is the mass inflow to the vessel under fire exposure and $\Delta H_{vap}$ is the latent heat of vaporization.

### 2.4 Fire Protection Equipment

| Equipment | Working Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Water Spray / Deluge** | Sprays water at **≥ 10.2 L/min·m²** over vessel surface | Cooling hydrocarbon vessels against fire |
| **Foam System** | Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF) blankets fire | Storage tank fires (Class B) |
| **Dry Chemical (ABC)** | Sodium bicarbonate or monoammonium phosphate powder | Small hydrocarbon pool fires |
| **CO₂ System** | Displaces O₂; concentration **≥ 50%** for flammable liquids | Confined spaces, electrical rooms |
| **Clean Agent (FM-200, Novec 1230)** | Chemical heat absorption | Server rooms, control rooms |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU, remember the **NFPA 11** application rates for foam: **6.5 L/min·m²** for hydrocarbon spills (AFFF) and **4.1 L/min·m²** for application on storage tanks.

### 2.5 Gas Detection — The LEL Triangle

A flammable gas detector reports %LEL. The relationship between LEL and actual concentration:

$$C_{actual} = \left(\frac{\%\text{LEL}}{100}\right) \times C_{LEL}$$

Where $C_{LEL}$ is the absolute Lower Explosive Limit concentration in **% v/v** (e.g., methane LEL = **5% v/v**, propane LEL = **2.1% v/v**).

### 2.6 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) for Hydrocarbons

PPE levels in hydrocarbon facilities (per **NFPA 2112** and **ANSI Z87.1**):

- **Level D**: Normal work clothing, safety boots, hard hat, glasses.
- **Level C (Modified)**: Chemical-resistant coveralls (Tyvek/Saranex), half-face APR (Air-Purifying Respirator).
- **Level B**: Supplied-air respirator (SAR) or SCBA, chemical-resistant suit.
- **Level A**: Fully encapsulated **gas-tight** suit, **positive-pressure SCBA** (highest protection).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Units | Applicable Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PRV Orifice Area | $A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_v \cdot \sqrt{P_1 \rho_1}}$ | mm² | API 520 |
| 2 | Fire-case heat input | $Q = 70{,}630 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$ (wetted) | BTU/hr | API 521 |
| 3 | Gas concentration in %LEL | $C_{actual} = (\%LEL/100) \cdot C_{LEL}$ | %v/v | Industrial hygiene |
| 4 | PFD for SIL | PFD ∈ $[10^{-1}, 10^{-5}]$ | dimensionless | IEC 61508 |
| 5 | Reynolds number for flow | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Fluid dynamics |
| 6 | Hydrostatic test pressure | $P_{test} = 1.5 \cdot P_{design}$ | bar | ASME Sec. VIII |
| 7 | Heat flux in radiation | $\dot{q}'' = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | kW/m² | Stefan-Boltzmann |
| 8 | Oxygen stoichiometry | $C_mH_n + (m+n/4)O_2 \rightarrow mCO_2 + (n/2)H_2O$ | mol | Combustion |
| 9 | Ductile-to-brittle transition | $CVN_{min} \geq 27$ J at minimum design temp | Joules | ASME B31.3 |
| 10 | Toxic dose | $D = C \cdot t$ (Haber's Law) | ppm·min | Industrial toxicology |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Students often confuse **%LEL** with **%v/v**. They are *not* the same. **%LEL** is a fraction of the substance's LEL. Always state both, especially in gas detection problems.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

These techniques and equipment are not academic — they are **life-saving** and **asset-saving**:
- **NDT** prevents catastrophic vessel ruptures (Bhopal, 1984 — partly attributed to poor inspection).
- **SIS/ESD** has prevented major loss-of-containment events in offshore platforms (e.g., **Piper Alpha, 1988** post-incident upgrades).
- **PRV** sizing failures have led to BLEVE incidents (e.g., **Jaipur, 2009**).
- **PPE & gas detection** are mandatory under the **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (PSM standard) and Indian **Factories Act, 1948** with the **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989 (MSIHC)**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — PRV Orifice Sizing for a Vapor (API 520)

**Problem Statement:** A pressure vessel containing propane is exposed to external fire. Determine the **required effective orifice area** of the rupture disc/PRV. Given:
- Required mass flow $\dot{m} = 12{,}500$ kg/hr
- Relieving pressure $P_1 = 18$ bara
- Relieving temperature $T_1 = 60^\circ C = 333$ K
- Molecular weight of propane $M = 44.097$ kg/kmol
- Universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K)
- Compressibility factor $Z = 0.85$
- Coefficient $C = 520 \times K_D \times K_{SH} \times K_N / (P_1 + 1.033)$ — for the certified "API 526" orifice designation
- $K_d = 0.95$, $K_b = 1.0$, $K_v = 1.0$
- Rated coefficient $C = 0.0396$ (for **'L' orifice**, propane, superheated)

**Step 1: Compute the inlet density using the real gas law.**

$$\rho_1 = \frac{P_1 \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T_1}$$

Insert values:

$$\rho_1 = \frac{(18 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}) \times (44.097 \text{ kg/kmol})}{(0.85) \times (8314 \text{ J/(kmol·K)}) \times (333 \text{ K})}$$

Numerator: $18 \times 10^5 \times 44.097 = 7.93746 \times 10^7$
Denominator: $0.85 \times 8314 \times 333 = 2.353 \times 10^6$

$$\rho_1 = \frac{7.93746 \times 10^7}{2.353 \times 10^6} = 33.74 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Convert mass flow to consistent units.**

$$\dot{m} = 12{,}500 \text{ kg/hr} = \frac{12{,}500}{3600} = 3.472 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3: Apply the API 520 vapor service formula.**

$$A = \frac{\dot{m}}{C \cdot K_d \cdot \sqrt{P_1 \cdot \rho_1}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the term $\sqrt{P_1 \cdot \rho_1}$.**

For the formula, the units of $P_1$ must be in **bara** when $C$ is in the proper **API 520 form**, OR a *unit-conversion* constant must be inserted. The clean form:

$$A = \frac{0.006316 \cdot W \cdot \sqrt{T_1 \cdot Z \cdot M}}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1}$$

where $W$ is in **kg/hr**, $P_1$ in **bara**, $A$ in **mm²**. This unit-coherent form of API 520 is more reliable.

**Step 5: Compute the numerator.**

$$\sqrt{T_1 \cdot Z \cdot M} = \sqrt{333 \times 0.85 \times 44.097} = \sqrt{12{,}485.3} = 111.74$$

Numerator = $0.006316 \times 12{,}500 \times 111.74 = 8{,}822.0$

**Step 6: Compute the denominator.**

$$C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 = 0.0396 \times 0.95 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 18 = 0.6772$$

**Step 7: Final area.**

$$A = \frac{8{,}822.0}{0.6772} = 13{,}026 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 8: Select standard orifice from API 526 table.**

The nearest standard orifice designations:
- 'K' orifice ≈ 9{,}855 mm²
- 'L' orifice ≈ 11{,}858 mm²
- 'M' orifice ≈ 16{,}516 mm²
- 'N' orifice ≈ 18{,}387 mm²
- 'P' orifice ≈ 25{,}806 mm²

**Selected: 'P' orifice (25,806 mm²)** — the next size up to ensure adequate capacity. (It is normal practice to *oversize* by one API 526 designation.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why oversize?** The oversizing accounts for **set pressure variation**, **overpressure accumulation** (typically max **21%** for fire case), **backpressure** corrections, and **fouling**. Engineers never size exactly — they round up to the next certified orifice.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Gas Detection Threshold Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A catalytic-bead LEL meter reads **25% LEL** in a confined space. The substance is **n-Hexane** ($C_6H_{14}$). Hexane's LEL is **1.1% v/v**. The TLV-TWA is **50 ppm** and the STEL is **75 ppm**. Determine:
- (a) The actual volumetric concentration in ppm.
- (b) The % above STEL.
- (c) Whether entry is permitted under the **OSHA Confined Space Rule 29 CFR 1910.146**.

**Step 1: Relate %LEL to absolute concentration.**

$$C_{actual} = \left(\frac{\%\text{LEL}}{100}\right) \times C_{LEL}$$

$$C_{actual} = 0.25 \times 1.1\% = 0.275\% \text{ v/v}$$

**Step 2: Convert %v/v to ppm.**

$$1\% \text{ v/v} = 10{,}000 \text{ ppm}$$

$$C_{actual} = 0.275 \times 10{,}000 = 2{,}750 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 3: Compare with STEL.**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{C_{actual}}{STEL} = \frac{2{,}750}{75} = 36.67$$

**Step 4: Express as % above STEL.**

$$\% \text{ above STEL} = (36.67 - 1) \times 100 = 3567\% \text{ above STEL}$$

**Step 5: Compliance verdict.**

> [!WARNING]
> The reading is **36.67× STEL** and is well above the **10% LEL** action level for hot work per **NFPA 326** and **OSHA 1910.252**. The confined space is **immediately dangerous to life and health (IDLH)** for hexane. Entry **is NOT permitted** until the atmosphere is purged, isolated, and re-tested to **< 10% LEL** and **< 1% oxygen deviation** from 20.9%.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — NDT Method Selection

| Defect Type | Material | Best NDT Method | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface crack | Carbon steel | MPI or DPI | Magnetic flux leakage or capillary action reveals surface breaks |
| Subsurface crack | Carbon steel | UT (TOFD) | Sound waves reflect from internal flaw faces |
| Internal porosity | Weld in stainless | RT (X-ray) | Volumetric defect, X-ray shows void density |
| Wall thinning | Pipe (any) | UT thickness gauging | Measures remaining wall directly |
| Crack in copper tube | Copper heat-exchanger tube | ET (Eddy Current) | Non-ferrous, eddy currents induced cleanly |
| Stress-corrosion cracking (SCC) | Austenitic SS | UT phased array + EMAT | SCC is shallow, sub-surface; phased array with high-frequency probes |
| Intergranular corrosion | SS welds | Dye Penetrant + Metallography | IGC is at the HAZ; surface etching works |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU problems where the examiner asks, *"Suggest a suitable NDT method for...,"* always justify with **three reasons**: (1) type of defect, (2) material, (3) accessibility/in-service or out-of-service.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SIL Determination (Risk Graph)

Per **IEC 61511**, the SIL is chosen by mapping four parameters onto a risk graph:

| Parameter | Choices |
|---|---|
| **Consequence (C)** | Minor (C1), Serious (C2), Major (C3), Catastrophic (C4) |
| **Occupancy (F)** | Rare (F1), Frequent (F2) |
| **Possibility of Avoidance (P)** | Avoidable (P1), Not avoidable (P2) |
| **Demand Rate (W)** | ≤ 1/yr (W1), > 1/yr (W2) |

Combining them yields SIL 1, 2, 3, or 4. KTU students should be able to *work through* one example: a high-pressure LNG pump in a manned area, with rare demand, unavoidable consequences → typically **SIL 2 or SIL 3**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In the SIS loop, the *Logic Solver* is the brain, the *Final Element* is the muscle, and the *Sensor* is the sense organ. The "Alarm to Operator" path is **NOT** part of the safety function (per IEC 61511) — it is supplementary. A truly independent SIS has **no operator intervention** in the automatic shutdown path.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2024]**
**Differentiate between Active and Passive Fire Protection systems used in a petroleum refinery. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Fire Protection | Passive Fire Protection |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Systems that **require detection and actuation** to suppress fire | Systems that **inherently contain or limit** fire spread without actuation |
| Trigger | Automatic (heat/smoke detector) or manual | No trigger; always in place |
| Examples (2 each) | (1) Deluge sprinkler system, (2) AFFF foam pourers | (1) Fire-resistant insulation (intumescent coating), (2) Bund walls / firewalls / fire-rated walls |

**Marking Insight (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for active examples, 1 mark for passive examples).**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Internal Assessment]**
**What is the significance of SIL in process industries? List the four SIL levels as per IEC 61508.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

**Significance of SIL:**
SIL (Safety Integrity Level) is a **discrete reliability target** assigned to a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) based on the risk reduction required. It quantifies the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of a safety function and dictates the design, installation, testing, and maintenance rigor. (1½ marks)

**SIL Levels per IEC 61508 (1½ marks):**
- **SIL 1**: PFD between $10^{-1}$ and $10^{-2}$
- **SIL 2**: PFD between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-3}$
- **SIL 3**: PFD between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-4}$
- **SIL 4**: PFD between $10^{-4}$ and $10^{-5}$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle, advantages, and limitations** of the following NDT techniques used in hydrocarbon industry:
    (i) Ultrasonic Testing (UT)
    (ii) Radiographic Testing (RT)
    (iii) Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)
    (iv) Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)
    **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrocarbon storage sphere contains **butane** at a MAWP of **9.5 barg** and a design temperature of **60 °C**. The vessel is exposed to external fire. Calculate the required **PRV orifice area** using the API 520 vapor service equation. Given:
- Required mass flow $W = 9{,}200$ kg/hr
- Set pressure = MAWP = 9.5 barg → Relieving pressure $P_1 = 9.5 + 1.033 = 10.533$ bara
- $T_1 = 333$ K, $M = 58.12$ kg/kmol, $Z = 0.82$
- $C = 0.0294$ (certified value for the chosen orifice)
- $K_d = 0.95$, $K_b = 1.0$, $K_c = 1.0$
    **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks (1.75 marks each technique):**

**(i) Ultrasonic Testing (UT):**
- *Principle:* High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) are transmitted into the material through a piezoelectric transducer. A couplant (gel or water) ensures acoustic contact. The waves reflect from internal defects or the back wall. The Time-of-Flight reveals the depth of the reflector.
- *Advantages:* Detects both surface and sub-surface defects; accurate thickness measurement; works on most metals; suitable for in-service inspection.
- *Limitations:* Requires a skilled operator; couplant needed; rough/corroded surfaces may need grinding; difficult on austenitic welds due to grain scattering.

**(ii) Radiographic Testing (RT):**
- *Principle:* X-rays (for thin sections, e.g., up to 75 mm steel) or Gamma rays (Ir-192 for medium; Co-60 for very thick) pass through the object. Differential absorption creates an image on a film or digital detector. Voids (cracks, porosity) appear darker.
- *Advantages:* Permanent record; excellent for volumetric defects (porosity, slag, lack of fusion); no couplant needed.
- *Limitations:* Radiation hazard — requires controlled area, badge monitoring, RSO; cannot detect planar defects that are parallel to the beam; expensive; slow; can't be used on some materials.

**(iii) Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI):**
- *Principle:* The part is magnetized (using a yoke, prod, or coil). Magnetic flux leaks at the surface where a crack or discontinuity exists, attracting ferromagnetic particles applied as dry powder or wet slurry, creating a visible indication.
- *Advantages:* Quick; inexpensive; very sensitive to surface and near-surface cracks (down to ~0.1 mm); portable.
- *Limitations:* Only works on **ferromagnetic** materials (carbon/low-alloy steel); requires demagnetization post-test; surface preparation (clean, bare metal) needed; doesn't show sub-surface depth.

**(iv) Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI):**
- *Principle:* A liquid penetrant is applied to the cleaned surface, drawn into surface-breaking defects by capillary action. Excess is removed, a developer is applied which draws the penetrant back out, creating a visible indication.
- *Advantages:* Works on all **non-porous** materials (incl. SS, Al, Ti, plastics); low cost; high sensitivity to surface cracks.
- *Limitations:* Only detects **surface-breaking** flaws; requires clean, dry surface; multiple-step process; cannot be used on porous materials (cast iron).

**Part (b) — 7 marks: API 520 PRV Sizing for Butane Vapor**

**Step 1: Apply API 520 vapor formula in unit-consistent form:**

$$A = \frac{0.006316 \cdot W \cdot \sqrt{T_1 \cdot Z \cdot M}}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\sqrt{T_1 \cdot Z \cdot M}$:**

$$\sqrt{333 \times 0.82 \times 58.12} = \sqrt{15{,}873.5} = 126.0$$

**Step 3: Numerator:**

$$0.006316 \times 9{,}200 \times 126.0 = 7{,}321.6$$

**Step 4: Denominator:**

$$0.0294 \times 0.95 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 10.533 = 0.2941$$

**Step 5: Final orifice area:**

$$A = \frac{7{,}321.6}{0.2941} = 24{,}895 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6: Select from API 526 orifice designations** (next size up): 'P' orifice (25,806 mm²).

**Conclusion: A 'P' orifice PRV is required.** (Full 7 marks awarded for proper formula use, unit consistency, numeric accuracy, and proper selection.)

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Module]**

**(a)** Describe the **Fire Protection and Prevention** strategy in a petroleum refinery. Explain the working of the following:
    (i) Foam pourer system for cone-roof tanks
    (ii) Deluge valve system for LPG spheres
    **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A confined-space entry is to be performed in a butadiene storage pit. The catalytic-bead LEL meter reads **18% LEL**, oxygen is **18.5% v/v**, and temperature is **38 °C**. The LEL of butadiene is **2.0% v/v**, TLV-TWA is **10 ppm**, and STEL is **20 ppm**.
    (i) Compute the actual concentration in **ppm**.
    (ii) Determine the **action level** based on NFPA 326.
    (iii) State the **safety requirements** per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146**.
    **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Refinery Fire Protection Strategy:** A refinery uses the **3-Zone Defense**:
1. **Zone 1 — Process Design Prevention**: Inherently safer design, NFPA 30 layout, adequate spacing (≥ 1.5× tank diameter between floating-roof tanks).
2. **Zone 2 — Detection and Active Suppression**: Gas/flame detectors, foam pourers, deluge, dry chemical, CO₂.
3. **Zone 3 — Mitigation & Escape**: Bund walls (110% volume of largest tank), emergency venting, fire escape routes, PPE.

**(i) Foam pourer system for cone-roof tanks (3.5 marks):**
A foam pourer is installed on the rim of a **cone-roof tank** above the liquid level. When a fire is detected (manually or automatically by a heat detector), the foam system is activated: a foam concentrate (AFFF) is mixed with water at a **3% or 6%** ratio, and the foam solution is poured onto the burning liquid surface, forming a **vapor-suppressing blanket**. The foam blanket separates the fuel from oxygen, suffocating the fire. Per **NFPA 11**, the application rate is **6.5 L/min·m²** for hydrocarbon fuels with a **65-minute** discharge duration. A foam dam near the rim prevents foam from sloshing off.

**(ii) Deluge valve system for LPG spheres (3.5 marks):**
A **deluge valve** is a quick-release, hydraulically-actuated valve that **fails open** (a critical safety property). The system is *dry* on the spray side — pipes are empty until activation. A **fuse-link** or **heat detector** at the LPG sphere triggers the release of the latching mechanism, allowing city water to flood the deluge valve, which then opens, and water is discharged at **≥ 10.2 L/min·m²** over the sphere's surface. The water cools the sphere, preventing a **BLEVE** (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion). The system is tested weekly and is always *armed* in hydrocarbon service.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Compute concentration (2 marks):**
$$C_{actual} = 0.18 \times 2.0\% = 0.36\% \text{ v/v} = 3{,}600 \text{ ppm}$$

**(ii) Action level (2 marks):**
NFPA 326 specifies a **10% LEL** action level for hot work. Since the reading is **18% LEL** > 10% LEL, **hot work is strictly prohibited**. The atmosphere is IDLH for butadiene.

**(iii) OSHA 1910.146 Safety Requirements (3 marks):**
- **Atmospheric testing** with a 4-gas monitor (LEL, O₂, CO, H₂S) before entry.
- **Oxygen**: 18.5% is **below 19.5%** (oxygen-deficient) — entry NOT permitted until purged to **20.9% ± 1%**.
- **LEL**: 18% > 10% — entry NOT permitted.
- **Ventilation**: Mechanical ventilation (blower) for at least 15 min before re-entry.
- **Permit**: Confined Space Entry Permit with attendant, rescue plan, communication, and PPE (Level B with SCBA).
- **Rescue**: Tripod and winch with retrieval line, trained rescue team on standby.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PESFT745 Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing %LEL with %v/v.** Always convert and state both. Examiners actively test this conversion.
> 2. **Wrong units in PRV formula.** PRV sizing is unit-sensitive; mismatched units can cause a 14-mark answer to score < 5 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting "oversize" by one API 526 designation** — you will lose 1 mark for not stating this engineering practice.
> 4. **In ESD questions, students describe DCS shutdown but forget IEC 61511's separation rule** — the BPCS (Basic Process Control System) and SIS must be *independent*. If you say "DCS will trip the pump," you are wrong — the **Safety PLC** must do it.
> 5. **In PPE questions, students list gloves and helmets but don't match them to NFPA 2112/ANSI standards.** Examiners expect *standard-clause citations*.
> 6. **Confusing STEL with TLV-TWA** — STEL is **15 min**, TWA is **8 hr**. This is a 1-mark trap.
> 7. **NDT questions: students write the principle but skip limitations.** Limitations carry ~30% of the marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Techniques and Equipment**
>
> - **Five pillars**: NDT, SIS/ESD, Fire Protection, Gas Detection, PPE.
> - **NDT** method choice = function(defect location, material, accessibility). Always give 3 justifications.
> - **SIS loop** = Sensor → Logic Solver → Final Element. *Never* place the operator in the automatic shutdown path.
> - **SIL levels** correspond to **PFD ranges**: SIL 1 ($10^{-1}$–$10^{-2}$), SIL 2 ($10^{-2}$–$10^{-3}$), SIL 3 ($10^{-3}$–$10^{-4}$), SIL 4 ($10^{-4}$–$10^{-5}$).
> - **PRV formula** (API 520 vapor): know the **unit-consistent form**; always oversize to next API 526 orifice.
> - **%LEL ≠ %v/v**; convert using $C_{actual} = (\%LEL/100) \cdot C_{LEL}$, then ×10,000 to get ppm.
> - **Fire foam (AFFF) application rate** = **6.5 L/min·m²** for hydrocarbon; **65-min duration** (NFPA 11).
> - **Deluge water spray** = **≥ 10.2 L/min·m²** for LPG vessel protection.
> - **PPE levels** in hydrocarbon: D → C → B → A; A = gas-tight, positive-pressure SCBA.
> - **Action level** for hot work: **10% LEL** (NFPA 326); **>10% LEL** → hot work prohibited.
> - **Confined space**: O₂ must be **19.5–23.5%**, LEL **< 10%**, CO **< 25 ppm**, H₂S **< 10 ppm** (OSHA 1910.146).
> - **Always cite the standard**: API 520, API 521, API 650, NFPA 11, NFPA 30, NFPA 326, IEC 61508/61511, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119/146, ANSI Z87.1, NFPA 2112.
> - **TWA = 8 hr; STEL = 15 min; IDLH = 30 min** (NIOSH definition).
> - **Combustion stoichiometry** for methane: $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$ — useful for oxygen-balance calculations in inerting.
> - **Oxygen limit for inerting**: hydrocarbon tanks typically blanketed at **< 8% v/v O₂** (per API 2003 / NFPA 69) to prevent combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — PESFT745 Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> For a perfect 14-mark answer, structure as: *Definition (1 mark) → Standard reference (1 mark) → Working principle (3 marks) → Mathematical derivation if any (4 marks) → Practical example or case study (2 marks) → Safety standards / limitations (3 marks)*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: On-Shore and Off-Shore Drilling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** On-Shore and Off-Shore Drilling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 On-Shore Drilling – Formal Definition
**On-shore (land) drilling** is the process of creating a wellbore through the earth's subsurface from a fixed, terrestrial drilling rig to access hydrocarbon reserves located beneath dry land. It encompasses site selection, rig erection, well construction (casing, cementing), drilling fluid circulation, and well control operations governed by standards such as API, OISD, and the Oil Mines Regulations (OMR) of India.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** On-shore drilling is the *classical* drilling domain — safety protocols here form the **baseline** against which off-shore risks (which are typically amplified) are evaluated.

### 1.2 Off-Shore Drilling – Formal Definition
**Off-shore drilling** is the process of creating a wellbore from a marine-based structure — fixed platform, semi-submersible, drillship, or jack-up rig — to access hydrocarbon reserves located beneath the seabed. Operations are governed by API RP 2D, IMO MODU Code, and classification societies (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition – Wellbore:** A wellbore is the physical hole (bore) drilled into the earth to enable the exploration, extraction, or injection of fluids. Its safety integrity determines the entire life-cycle safety of the well.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Straw in a Cup"
Imagine trying to drink a thick milkshake (hydrocarbons + drilling mud) from a sealed cup (the reservoir) using a complex straw (the wellbore + casing string):
- **Drilling fluid** acts like the suction force — keeping the "lid" of the cup sealed (preventing hydrocarbons from gushing out uncontrollably).
- **Blowout Preventer (BOP)** acts like a thumb pressing the top of the straw — a mechanical last line of defense.
- **Casing & Cement** are like a reinforced sleeve inside the straw — preventing the straw from collapsing or leaking sideways.
- **On-shore** = straw held in your hand on a kitchen counter.
- **Off-shore** = straw held in your hand on a rocking boat during a storm — every force is **multiplied** by waves, currents, and wind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (API):** Well pressure is conventionally reported in **psi (lb/in²)** or **ppg (pounds per gallon)** for mud weight. The conversion factor is **0.052 × ppg = psi/ft** of hydrostatic gradient.

### 1.4 Why Safety in Drilling?
According to KTU CO mapping for this module, the student must be able to **identify hazards, evaluate risks, and prescribe control measures** specific to drilling operations. Drilling is statistically the **most hazardous phase** of the upstream hydrocarbon life-cycle — a single uncontrolled release (blowout) can cause fatalities (e.g., Deepwater Horizon, 2010 — 11 deaths), environmental catastrophe, and asset loss measured in **billions of USD**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 On-Shore Drilling – Operational Workflow
The on-shore drilling operation follows a structured sequence:

1. **Site Survey & Geotechnical Investigation** — Soil bearing capacity, slope stability, drainage, proximity to populated areas.
2. **Rig Selection** — Based on depth, terrain, mobility (land rig vs. workover rig vs. desert rig).
3. **Rig Erection & Commissioning** — Mast raising, BOP installation, pressure testing.
4. **Spudding In** — Beginning of the wellbore at the surface.
5. **Drilling the Conductor Hole** — First section, typically 16″–26″ diameter.
6. **Setting Conductor Casing & Cementing** — Surface structural anchor.
7. **Surface Hole Drilling & Surface Casing** — Protects fresh-water aquifers (Regulatory requirement under OMR / Indian EPA).
8. **Intermediate Hole & Casing** — Isolates unstable formations, lost-circulation zones.
9. **Production Hole & Liner** — Final section to target reservoir depth.
10. **Well Completion** — Perforating, gravel packing, running production tubing.
11. **Well Testing & Handover** — To production operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why':** Every casing string is set to **isolate a specific hazard zone** (loss of circulation, gas cap, water table, overpressured shale). Skipping or under-designing any string is the most common root cause of well-control incidents.

### 2.2 On-Shore Drilling Hazards (KTU Board Priority)
- **Blowouts** (formation fluid kicks escalating to surface)
- **H₂S exposure** (sour-service formations, e.g., Assam, Gujarat fields)
- **Falls from height** (rig floor, monkey board, Derrick — leading cause of fatal injury)
- **Caught-in / Caught-between** (rotating equipment, draw-works)
- **Vehicle collisions** (remote site transport)
- **Heat stress & dehydration** (e.g., Rajasthan, Middle-East sites)
- **Electrical hazards** (lightning, generator back-feed)
- **Noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)**

### 2.3 Off-Shore Drilling – Platform Taxonomy
Off-shore drilling structures are classified by mobility and water depth:

| Platform Type | Mobility | Typical Water Depth | Stability Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Platform (Steel Jacket)** | Permanent | 0 – 500 m | Piled to seabed |
| **Compliant Tower** | Permanent | 500 – 900 m | Flexes with waves |
| **Jack-up Rig** | Self-elevating | 10 – 150 m | Legs jacked above water |
| **Semi-Submersible** | Floating | 300 – 3000 m | Ballasted pontoons, moored or DP |
| **Drillship** | Floating | 1500 – 4000 m | Ship-shaped, dynamic positioning |
| **Tension-Leg Platform (TLP)** | Floating, tethered | 300 – 1500 m | Vertical tensioned tendons |
| **SPAR** | Floating, moored | 600 – 3000 m | Deep draft, single cylindrical hull |

> [!NOTE]
> **DP (Dynamic Positioning):** Computer-controlled thrusters maintain a vessel's position and heading without anchors — critical for ultra-deepwater drilling where mooring lines are impractical.

### 2.4 Off-Shore Specific Hazards (KTU Priority)
- **Marine hazards** — vessel collision, helicopter ditching, dropped objects
- **Weather-related** — hurricanes, rogue waves, ice loading (arctic ops)
- **Ballast & Stability failures** — semi-submersible capsize risk
- **Cryogenic / cold-water exposure** — Musculoskeletal, hypothermia
- **Isolation & Evacuation difficulty** — far from onshore medical facilities
- **Hydrocarbon release on water** — fire spreads rapidly across sea surface
- **H₂S over open water** — toxic plume can affect full platform

### 2.5 The Drilling Fluid (Mud) System
Drilling fluid is a **multi-functional safety device**, not just a lubricant. It performs four primary safety functions:

1. **Hydrostatic Pressure Control** — balances formation pressure to prevent kicks.
2. **Cuttings Transport** — lifts drilled rock to surface.
3. **Wellbore Stabilization** — prevents collapse, fracturing.
4. **Formation Evaluation** — logging while drilling (LWD) data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mud Weight Window:** The mud weight (density) must be maintained **above pore pressure** (to prevent influx) AND **below fracture pressure** (to prevent lost circulation). This range is called the **"Operational Mud Weight Window"**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet – Critical Drilling Equations

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{hs} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD$ | Hydrostatic pressure, Mud weight, True vertical depth | psi, ppg, ft | Kick detection, well control |
| 2 | $\Delta P = P_{form} - P_{hs}$ | Underbalance (positive = kick risk) | psi | Monitoring formation balance |
| 3 | $MAASP = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD_{csg} \times SF - P_{fric}$ | Max Allowable Annular Shut-in Pressure | psi | Well kill design |
| 4 | $P_{frac} = 0.052 \times FG \times TVD$ | Formation fracture pressure | psi | Casing shoe integrity test |
| 5 | $FG = 0.052 \times \rho_{frac}$ | Fracture gradient (ppg/ft) | psi/ft | LOT (Leak-Off Test) result |
| 6 | $SG = 0.052 \times MW$ | Static mud gradient | psi/ft | Direct readout on rig |
| 7 | $V_{kill} = \frac{(SIDPP \times 0.052 \times V_{ann})}{MW - MW_{kill}}$ | Kill mud volume (Driller's method) | bbl | Circulating out a kick |
| 8 | $R_{c} = \frac{\rho_w - \rho_{air}}{\rho_{air}}$ | Riser tension / buoyant load ratio | — | Off-shore riser design |
| 9 | $F_{blowout} = P \times A_{orifice}$ | Uncontrolled release thrust | N | API 16A choke sizing |
| 10 | $Kick_{tol} = \frac{(P_{frac} - P_{form})}{0.052 \times MW}$ | Safe influx volume | bbl | Kick tolerance envelope |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard:** The constant **0.052** arises from the conversion: $\frac{1 \text{ ppg} \times 1 \text{ ft}}{144 \text{ in}^2/\text{ft}^2} = 0.052$ psi/ft. Memorize this — it appears in every KTU well-control problem.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility
- **On-shore:** Direct application at sites operated by ONGC (Ankleshwar, Assam), Reliance (KG basin on-shore), Cairn India.
- **Off-shore:** Direct application at Mumbai High (ONGC), KG-D6 (Reliance), KG-DWN-98/3, and globally — Gulf of Mexico, North Sea, Campos Basin, pre-salt Brazil.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hydrostatic Pressure in a Wellbore

**Starting Principle:** A static column of fluid exerts pressure at the bottom equal to the weight of the column per unit area.

Let:
- $\rho$ = density of fluid (slugs/ft³ or ppg)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (32.2 ft/s² or implicit in ppg conversion)
- $h$ = vertical height of fluid column (ft)

**Step 1:** Pressure at depth h in a continuous fluid:

$$P_{hs} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Convert to drilling-engineering units (ppg, ft, psi). Recognize that **1 ppg = 7.4805 lb/ft³** (since water at 8.33 ppg = 62.4 lb/ft³, so 1 ppg = 62.4/8.33 = 7.48 lb/ft³).

**Step 3:** Express the weight of a 1-ft³ column of mud of weight $MW$ ppg:

$$W = MW \times 7.4805 \text{ lb (per ft}^3\text{ of column)}$$

**Step 4:** Distribute this weight over a 1-ft² cross-section of the wellbore. Pressure per square foot = force / area:

$$P_{hs} \text{ per ft of depth} = \frac{MW \times 7.4805 \text{ lb/ft}^2}{\text{per ft depth}}$$

**Step 5:** Convert lb/ft² to lb/in² (psi). Since 1 ft² = 144 in²:

$$P_{hs}/\text{ft} = \frac{MW \times 7.4805}{144} = 0.0519 \times MW \approx 0.052 \times MW \text{ psi/ft}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by True Vertical Depth (TVD) for total hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the fluid column:

$$P_{hs} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD$$

This is the **single most important equation in well-control**, used to detect kicks, design kill sheets, and verify casing integrity.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example – KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem (Model):** A well is being drilled at TVD = 10,000 ft. Current mud weight is 12.0 ppg. Formation pressure gradient is 0.465 psi/ft. A kick of 25 bbl is observed. Calculate:
- (a) Hydrostatic pressure of mud at TD
- (b) Formation pressure at TD
- (c) Underbalance (kick driving force)
- (d) Maximum safe kick volume before reaching fracture pressure of 0.65 psi/ft at the casing shoe (8,000 ft)

**Solution (a): Hydrostatic pressure of mud at TD**

$$P_{hs} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD = 0.052 \times 12.0 \times 10{,}000$$

$$P_{hs} = 6{,}240 \text{ psi}$$

**Solution (b): Formation pressure at TD**

$$P_{form} = 0.465 \times 10{,}000 = 4{,}650 \text{ psi}$$

**Solution (c): Underbalance pressure (kick driver)**

$$\Delta P = P_{form} - P_{hs} = 4{,}650 - 6{,}240 = -1{,}590 \text{ psi}$$

The negative value confirms **overbalanced** condition — no kick should occur if the well is healthy.

**Solution (d): Maximum allowable influx volume (Kick Tolerance):**

Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure at casing shoe with current mud:

$$P_{hs,shoe} = 0.052 \times 12.0 \times 8{,}000 = 4{,}992 \text{ psi}$$

Step 2 — Fracture pressure at casing shoe:

$$P_{frac} = 0.65 \times 8{,}000 = 5{,}200 \text{ psi}$$

Step 3 — Allowable overpressure margin (skin):

$$\Delta P_{allow} = P_{frac} - P_{hs,shoe} = 5{,}200 - 4{,}992 = 208 \text{ psi}$$

Step 4 — Equivalent allowable additional hydrostatic head (i.e., how much *less* mud weight is needed to bring shoe pressure down by 208 psi):

$$MW_{eq} = \frac{208}{0.052 \times 8{,}000} = 0.50 \text{ ppg reduction equivalent}$$

Step 5 — Convert to kick volume using annular capacity. Assume 9-5/8″ casing in 12-1/4″ hole:

$$V_{ann} \approx 0.33 \text{ bbl/ft (from standard capacity table)}$$

Step 6 — Using the kick tolerance approximation:

$$V_{kick,max} = \frac{\Delta P_{allow}}{0.052 \times MW} \times V_{ann} \times h_{influx}$$

Assuming kick is gas (compressible), an inflow of **≤ 10 bbl** is typically the safe tolerance. Anything larger demands **immediate shut-in**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix – "When to Shut-In"

| Observed Condition | Primary Action | Secondary Action |
|---|---|---|
| Pit gain > 5 bbl | Flow check | Sound alarm |
| Flow increase on POOH | Shut-in (Driller's method) | Notify well-site supervisor |
| Connection gas | Slow pump rate | Continue circulation |
| Loss of returns | Pull off bottom, fill annulus | Investigate formation fracture |
| H₂S > 10 ppm | Don SCBA, evacuate unessential | Activate muster plan |
| Severe weather warning (off-shore) | Secure well, suspend ops | Disconnect or hang-off |
| Slow penetration + background gas | Stop drilling, circulate bottoms-up | Take sidewall core |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three C's of Well Control:** **Circulate, Close, Calculate** — these are the foundational decision steps every KTU student must internalize.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – Functional Architecture of an On-Shore Drilling Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Generation System] --> B[Draw-works]
    B --> C[Mast and Substructure]
    C --> D[Rotary Table and Top Drive]
    D --> E[Drill String]
    E --> F[Bit at Bottom Hole]
    G[Mud Pumps] --> H[Mud Treatment System]
    H --> I[Shale Shakers and Desander]
    I --> J[Reserve Pit]
    J --> G
    K[BOP Stack 7 inch 3000 psi] --> E
    L[Choke Manifold] --> K
    M[Choke Line to Mud Gas Separator] --> L
    N[Surface Casing 13.375 inch] --> K
    O[Intermediate Casing 9.625 inch] --> N
    P[Production Liner 7 inch] --> O
    Q[Wellhead and X-mas Tree] --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The mud system is a **closed loop** — mud exits the well (carrying cuttings), is cleaned at the shale shakers, and re-enters via the mud pumps. The **BOP and choke manifold** are the well-control barrier, *not* part of the drilling power path.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Off-Shore Drilling Platform Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hull or Jacket Structure] --> B[Mooring System or DP Thrusters]
    A --> C[Drilling Module]
    C --> D[Derrick and Substructure]
    C --> E[BOP Stack Subsea or Surface]
    C --> F[Marine Riser Tensioner System]
    F --> G[Wellhead at Seabed]
    H[Helideck] --> A
    I[Accommodation Block] --> A
    J[Process and Mud Module] --> A
    K[Cranes and Material Handling] --> A
    L[Emergency Shutdown ESD System] --> A
    M[Power Generation and Distribution] --> A
    N[Ballast and Stability Control] --> A
    O[Fire and Gas Detection F and G System] --> A
    P[Marine Evacuation System MES] --> A
    Q[Lifesaving and Survival Craft] --> P
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – Well Control Barrier Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Primary Barrier: Hydrostatic Head of Drilling Mud] --> B
    B[Secondary Barrier: BOP Annular Preventer] --> C
    C[Tertiary Barrier: BOP Pipe Rams and Blind Shear Rams] --> D
    D[Quaternary Barrier: Kill Fluid and Circulating Path] --> E
    E[Final Barrier: Surface Casing Cement Sheath]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key:** Examiners consistently test the concept that **at least two independent barriers must be maintained at all times**. This is the **"Two-Barrier Rule"** of API Standard 53 and OMR 2017.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram – Kick Detection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Drill Bit in Hole and Circulating] --> B{Pit Level Increasing?}
    B -- No --> C[Continue Normal Operations]
    B -- Yes --> D[Flow Check on POOH}
    D --> E{Well Flowing?}
    E -- No --> F[Monitor and Document]
    E -- Yes --> G[Sound Alarm and Shut-in BOP}
    G --> H[Record SIDPP and SICP]
    H --> I[Determine Kick Type Gas Oil Water}
    I --> J[Calculate Kill Mud Weight]
    J --> K[Drillers Method or Wait and Weight Method]
    K --> L[Bullhead or Circulate Out Kick]
    L --> M[Verify Well is Dead}
    M --> N[Resume Operations with Increased Mud Weight}
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define a "kick" in drilling operations. State the three primary indicators used on a rig to detect a kick.*

**Model Answer:**
A **kick** is an unintended influx of formation fluids (gas, oil, water) into the wellbore, occurring when the hydrostatic pressure exerted by the drilling fluid drops below the formation pore pressure.

The **three primary kick indicators** are:
1. **Increase in pit level / pit gain** — measured continuously by the trip tank and active pit volume sensor.
2. **Flow rate increase on POOH** — when the drill string is pulled out, the well flows instead of remaining static.
3. **Drilling break / increase in ROP** — sudden acceleration of penetration indicates a porous, overpressured zone.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 3 indicators)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between a "jack-up rig" and a "semi-submersible rig" in off-shore drilling. Give one example of operational water depth for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Jack-Up Rig | Semi-Submersible |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Self-elevating; legs jacked above water | Floating; ballasted pontoons below water |
| **Mobility** | Towed between locations; cannot float in operating mode | Self-propelled or towed; operates floating |
| **Water Depth** | 10 – 150 m (shallow) | 300 – 3000 m (deep / ultra-deep) |
| **Stability Principle** | Bottom-supported on seabed | Moored or dynamically positioned |
| **Example** | Shelf drilling in Palk Bay | Deepwater KG-D6 (Reliance) |

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

**Q3A. [14 Marks, KTU ESE Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **blowout preventer (BOP) stack** used in drilling operations. Draw a neat labelled sketch of a subsea BOP stack and describe the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **"Driller's Method"** and **"Wait-and-Weight Method"** of well control. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
A BOP stack is a series of high-pressure valves installed at the wellhead (surface BOP) or on the seabed (subsea BOP) to provide redundant mechanical barriers against uncontrolled flow.

**Components of a subsea BOP stack (from top to bottom):**

1. **Annular Preventer** — A rubber-inserted donut-shaped element that can close around any shape in the wellbore (drill pipe, kelly, wireline, or open hole). Primary use: closing on open hole during tripping.
2. **Pipe Rams** — Two sets; close on drill pipe to seal the annulus while allowing circulation through the drill string. Used during stripping and snubbing.
3. **Blind Shear Rams** — Cut the drill pipe and seal an empty wellbore. This is the **last-resort barrier** before uncontrolled flow reaches the surface.
4. **Connector / Wellhead Connector** — Latches the BOP to the wellhead.
5. **Kill and Choke Lines** — High-pressure lines for circulating kill fluid and controlling back-pressure.
6. **Hydraulic Accumulators** — Store pressurized hydraulic fluid to close preventers within ≤ 30 seconds (API 16D requirement).
7. **Subsea Control Pods** — Redundant (often 2-of-3 voting logic) electronics/hydraulics to operate the stack from the surface.

*(7 marks: 1 labelled sketch + 6 component descriptions; KTU typically awards 2 marks for a clean sketch)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Driller's Method (Two-Circulation Method):**
- **First circulation:** Circulate the kick out using the *original* mud weight at reduced pump rate. The bottom-hole pressure is held constant by carefully adjusting the choke.
- **Second circulation:** After the kick is out, displace the well with *heavier kill mud* to restore overbalance.
- **Advantage:** Simpler, requires less calculation on rig floor. Good for small kicks.
- **Limitation:** Well is underbalanced momentarily between circulations if not executed perfectly.

**Wait-and-Weight Method (Engineer's Method, Single Circulation):**
- **Pre-calculate** the kill mud weight (using SIDPP) and the kill rate.
- **Wait** for the kill mud to be prepared (typically 30 – 90 minutes).
- **Single circulation** displaces kick out and replaces mud with kill weight in one step. Choke is held at constant bottom-hole pressure equal to original hydrostatic.
- **Advantage:** Well never goes underbalanced. Faster total operation; lower cumulative exposure.
- **Limitation:** Requires accurate pre-calculation; longer initial wait time.

*(7 marks: 2.5 for Driller's, 2.5 for W&W, 2 for comparison)*

---

**Q3B. [14 Marks, KTU ESE Model – Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the major **hazards of on-shore drilling operations** with reference to falls, blowouts, and H₂S exposure. For each hazard, prescribe at least two control measures as per OISD/API guidelines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the **emergency response system on an off-shore drilling platform**. Explain the role of the Temporary Refuge (T-Refuge), ESD system, and lifeboats. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
On-shore drilling hazards and controls:

1. **Falls from Height**
   - *Cause:* Working on monkey board, derrick, scaffolding, BOP deck.
   - *Controls:* Full-body harness with shock absorber; guard rails (≥ 42″); safety nets; SCBA-equipped Derrick-man training (API RP 54); 100% tie-off policy.

2. **Blowouts**
   - *Cause:* Underbalanced mud, BOP failure, improper cement bond.
   - *Controls:* Maintain mud weight in operational window; pre-job LOT (Leak-Off Test); regular BOP pressure test (annually + after major repairs); two-barrier philosophy (API 53); well-control drills weekly.

3. **H₂S Exposure**
   - *Cause:* Drilling through sour formations (e.g., Assam, Cambay Basin).
   - *Controls:* Continuous H₂S monitoring (fixed + personal); SCBA on every shift; wind-direction awareness; muster points upwind; H₂S training (API RP 2210); contingency H₂S scavenger in mud; medical standby with O₂ resuscitator.

*(7 marks: ~2.3 per hazard)*

**Model Solution (b):**
The off-shore emergency response system is integrated and tri-redundant.

**Major Blocks:**
1. **Fire and Gas Detection (F&G)** — Detects hydrocarbon gas, H₂S, fire, smoke; signals ESD.
2. **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) System** — Hierarchical (ESD-0, ESD-1, ESD-2, ESD-3) to isolate process, vent, power-down, and abandon.
3. **Temporary Refuge (T-Refuge)** — A fire-rated, blast-rated, positively-pressurized enclosure that serves as the muster point and provides **≥ 2 hours** of breathable atmosphere, drinking water, and communication during an un-evacuable event (e.g., total platform fire).
4. **Life-Saving Equipment** — Lifeboats (totally enclosed, fire-resistant, free-fall type per SOLAS), life-rafts, immersion suits, life-jackets, MES (Marine Evacuation System — slide/chute for platform evacuation to sea).
5. **PPE and Breathing Apparatus** — SCBA (30-min), EEBD (Emergency Escape Breathing Device — 10 min), chemical suits.
6. **Communication** — PA system, tannoy, radio (UHF/VHF), public address to T-Refuge, GMDSS for marine distress.

**Role of T-Refuge:** Acts as a "lifeboat on deck" — occupants can survive a pool fire or major gas release for the time required for marine rescue. Critical in Arctic/North Sea operations where evacuation is weather-dependent.

**Role of ESD:** Hierarchical shutdown prevents escalation. ESD-1 typically isolates the well (closes SCSSV, SSSV); ESD-3 may disconnect the riser and release the rig.

**Role of Lifeboats:** Each platform must have **100% capacity** of personnel in totally-enclosed, motor-propelled, fire-resistant lifeboats (TEMPSC) launched from both sides.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 5 for descriptions)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Causing Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Wrong unit for 0.052 conversion** — Students often write `P = 0.052 × ρ × h` and forget the constant is dimensionless — it is a *unit-conversion factor*. Always state: "**0.052 converts ppg·ft → psi.**"
> 2. **Confusing MAWP and MAASP** — MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure) is for *vessels*; MAASP (Maximum Allowable Annular Surface Pressure) is for *well control*. The two are not interchangeable.
> 3. **Omitting the two-barrier rule** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for explicitly stating the "**at least two independent barriers must be maintained at all times**" rule in any well-control answer.
> 4. **Missing the LOT step in casing design** — Always show the Leak-Off Test interpretation: $\text{LOT equivalent mud weight} = \text{pressure at leak-off} / (0.052 \times \text{TVD at shoe})$.
> 5. **Treating on-shore and off-shore as identical** — Off-shore adds marine, weather, and evacuation risks. The examiner will deduct marks if these are absent.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention OMR 2017** — The Indian Oil Mines Regulations (with 2024 amendments) are the legal framework for Indian on-shore drilling safety — referencing this adds a "law-bonus" mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Drilling** is the most hazardous phase of upstream hydrocarbon operations.
- ✅ The **two-barrier rule** (hydrostatic + mechanical) is non-negotiable per API 53 and OMR.
- ✅ The **golden constant** is **0.052 psi/ft per ppg** — appears in every well-control equation.
- ✅ A **kick** is the first symptom; a **blowout** is the final outcome if a kick is mismanaged.
- ✅ **Mud weight window** = above pore pressure AND below fracture pressure.
- ✅ **BOP stack components** in order: Annular → Pipe Rams → Blind Shear Rams (top to bottom).
- ✅ **Driller's method** = two circulations; **Wait & Weight** = one circulation, slower start.
- ✅ On-shore hazards priority: **Falls, Blowout, H₂S, Vehicle, Noise**.
- ✅ Off-shore hazards priority: **Weather, Stability, Evacuation, Marine traffic, Dropped objects**.
- ✅ Platform types: **Fixed → Jack-up → Semi-sub → Drillship** (increasing water depth capability).
- ✅ **T-Refuge** provides ≥ 2 hours of safe atmosphere during un-evacuable events.
- ✅ **ESD system** is hierarchical; **ESD-0/1/2/3** corresponds to escalating shutdown.
- ✅ **MAASP** (not MAWP) is the key surface-pressure limit in kill sheet design.
- ✅ **Kick tolerance** (bbl) defines the safe influx envelope before casing shoe fractures.
- ✅ Always cite **API Spec 16A/16D, API RP 53, OMR 2017, and IMO MODU Code** in answers for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Wells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Wells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Well Classification** in the hydrocarbon industry is the systematic categorization of oil, gas, and injection wells based on operational parameters (pressure, temperature, flow rate), fluid characteristics (composition, phase behavior, H₂S/CO₂ content), wellbore geometry, intended purpose, and life-cycle stage. This classification forms the foundational framework for designing **well control programs**, **blowout preventer (BOP) stacks**, **wellhead equipment ratings**, and **emergency response procedures** as per **API RP 53**, **API Spec 6A**, and **API Spec 16A** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Well classification is the first step in safety design. Every downstream safety decision — from casing design to ESD valve selection — hinges on knowing the well's class. Misclassification is one of the leading root causes of hydrocarbon incidents (per OGP/IADC safety reports).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital" Analogy

Imagine a large hospital complex. Not every patient room is built the same way:
- The **general ward** (like a low-pressure, sweet oil well) handles mild, predictable cases with standard equipment.
- The **ICU** (like a high-pressure gas well) requires reinforced walls, backup power, and constant monitoring.
- The **isolation ward** (like a sour well with H₂S) needs sealed ventilation, decontamination showers, and SCBA stations.

Similarly, a hydrocarbon well's "threat level" determines its **engineering safeguards**. A sweet, low-pressure vertical oil well in shallow sand needs far less protection than a sour, ultra-HPHT horizontal gas well penetrating an H₂S-bearing carbonate reservoir.

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters — Core Drivers

| Driver | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Equipment Specification** | Select correct BOP, Christmas tree, and choke manifold ratings. |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Adhere to OISD, API, and ONGC/MDN safety standards. |
| **Risk Assessment (HIRA/HAZOP)** | Quantify the probability and consequence of loss of containment. |
| **Emergency Response Planning** | Decide muster points, evacuation radius, SCBA inventory. |
| **Casing & Cementing Program** | Design barriers based on pressure and corrosive fluid exposure. |
| **Personnel Training & Certification** | IWCF/IADC certification level is matched to well class. |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard surface gravity (oil & gas):** **g = 9.81 m/s²** (= 32.174 ft/s²)
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** **Pₐₜₘ = 101.325 kPa** (= 14.7 psi = 1.01325 bar)
- **H₂S sour threshold (NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156):** **> 10 ppm partial pressure > 0.0003 MPa**
- **H₂S sour threshold (OSHA/PEL):** **≥ 20 ppm at any workplace concentration**
- **Standard formation pressure gradients:** Hydrostatic water gradient = **0.433 psi/ft** (= 9.792 kPa/m)
- **Standard temperature gradient:** **~25 °C/km** (geothermal, average continental crust)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the "Sour" Threshold:** The NACE threshold of **10 ppm H₂S in the gas phase at standard conditions** is the globally accepted boundary. Some operators (e.g., Shell, Saudi Aramco) use a more conservative **5 ppm** trigger for enhanced safety protocols.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Well Classification

A well is never classified on a single parameter. Engineers cross-reference **five independent pillars** before issuing a final "Well Class Certificate."

### Pillar 1 — By Pressure Regime

The pressure regime is the most safety-critical parameter. It is determined by the **Maximum Anticipated Surface Pressure (MASP)** or **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** at the wellhead.

| Class | Surface Pressure Range | Typical Reservoir | BOP Rating Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Pressure (LP)** | **< 2,000 psi** (< 13.8 MPa) | Shallow depleted sands | 2,000 – 3,000 psi |
| **Medium Pressure (MP)** | **2,000 – 5,000 psi** (13.8 – 34.5 MPa) | Moderate-depth clastics | 5,000 psi |
| **High Pressure (HP)** | **5,000 – 10,000 psi** (34.5 – 68.9 MPa) | Deep carbonates, gas | 10,000 psi |
| **Ultra High Pressure (UHP)** | **> 10,000 psi** (> 68.9 MPa) | Deep gas, HPHT fields | 15,000 psi |
| **HPHT (Special Class)** | **> 10,000 psi & > 350 °F** (> 177 °C) | HPHT plays (e.g., North Sea Central Graben) | 15,000 psi + high-temp trim |

### Pillar 2 — By Fluid Type (Hydrocarbon Phase)

- **Oil Well** — Produces primarily liquid hydrocarbon (stock-tank oil). API gravity **> 10°** (otherwise classified as bitumen/tar).
- **Gas Well** — Produces primarily gaseous hydrocarbon; **Gas-Oil Ratio (GOR) > 100,000 scf/stb** at surface.
- **Gas-Condensate Well** — Single-phase gas in reservoir, condenses to liquid at surface; produces **condensate + gas**.
- **Dry Gas Well** — Methane-dominant, no condensate drop-out.
- **Water Well (Disposal / Produced Water Re-injection)** — Non-hydrocarbon; governed by separate environmental regulations.

### Pillar 3 — By Function (Lifecycle Purpose)

- **Exploration / Wildcat Well** — Drilled in unproven acreage; high geological risk.
- **Appraisal Well** — Drilled post-discovery to define reservoir extent, fluid contacts, productivity.
- **Development / Producer Well** — Drilled on a regular pattern (5-spot, 9-spot, etc.) for commercial production.
- **Injection Well** — Used for reservoir pressure maintenance (water injection, gas injection, WAG).
- **Disposal Well** — Class II underground injection; for produced water, cuttings, or waste.
- **Observation / Monitoring Well** — Used for reservoir surveillance; equipped with downhole gauges (DHS, PDG).
- **Service Well** — Utility well (water supply, gas lift supply).
- **Workover / Abandonment Well** — Plugged and abandoned (P&A) or worked over for recompletion.

### Pillar 4 — By Wellbore Geometry / Trajectory

- **Vertical Well** — Inclination **< 15°** from vertical; the historical standard.
- **Deviated Well** — Inclination **15° – 60°**; access from a fixed surface location.
- **Horizontal Well** — Inclination **≥ 90°** (commonly 88° – 95°); exposes long reservoir section.
- **Multilateral Well** — Two or more horizontal branches from a single motherbore.
- **Slim / Monobore Well** — Reduced hole size for offshore/remote applications.
- **Extended Reach Drilling (ERD) Well** — Horizontal step-out **> 3,000 m**; high tortuosity.

### Pillar 5 — By Corrosive/Corrosive Service (Safety Pillar)

This is the **safety-critical classification** that drives metallurgy, PPE, and shutdown philosophy.

| Class | H₂S Content | CO₂ Content | Designation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sweet** | **< 10 ppm** | Any (separate limit) | NACE MR0175 "Region 0" |
| **Low-Sour** | **10 – 50 ppm** | < 1000 ppm | NACE Region 1 |
| **Sour** | **50 – 1,000 ppm** | 1000 – 5000 ppm | NACE Region 2 |
| **High-Sour** | **> 1,000 ppm** | > 5000 ppm | NACE Region 3 |
| **Acid Gas** | **> 30 mol%** in total gas | + high CO₂ | Dedicated alloy (e.g., Alloy 825, Inconel) |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the table below for solving KTU numerical problems. Memorize all units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) | Units (Field) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{bh} = P_{wh} + \rho_{f} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Bottom-hole pressure from surface pressure | Pa | psi |
| 2 | $P_{bh} = P_{wh} + 0.052 \cdot MW \cdot TVD$ | Hydrostatic pressure gradient (mud/oil/water) | psi (with TVD in ft) | psi/ft × ft |
| 3 | $G_{p} = \dfrac{P_{bh} - P_{wh}}{TVD}$ | Pressure gradient along wellbore | Pa/m | psi/ft |
| 4 | $MAASP = \dfrac{S}{F}$ | Maximum Allowable Annular Surface Pressure; S = casing burst, F = design factor | Pa | psi |
| 5 | $MAWP = \min(P_{burst},\, P_{collapse}) \cdot F_{design}$ | Maximum Allowable Working Pressure | Pa | psi |
| 6 | $P_{partial}(H_2S) = y_{H_2S} \cdot P_{total}$ | Partial pressure of H₂S for NACE classification | Pa | psi |
| 7 | $PPM = \dfrac{y_{H_2S} \cdot M_{H_2S}}{y_{mix} \cdot M_{mix}} \times 10^{6}$ | ppm of H₂S from mole fraction | — | — |
| 8 | $GOR = \dfrac{Q_{gas,\,scf}}{Q_{oil,\,stb}}$ | Gas-Oil Ratio at surface | — | scf/stb |
| 9 | $WOR = \dfrac{Q_{water}}{Q_{oil}}$ | Water-Oil Ratio | — | bbl/bbl |
| 10 | $P_{kick\_intensity} = P_{bh} - P_{hydrostatic}$ | Kick tolerance / warning threshold | Pa | psi |
| 11 | $\sigma_{v} = \rho_{rock} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Overburden (lithostatic) stress | Pa | psi |
| 12 | $P_{p}/P_{h} = \text{Pore pressure ratio}$ | Used to classify abnormally pressured wells | — | — |

**Where:**
- $P_{bh}$ = bottom-hole pressure, $P_{wh}$ = wellhead pressure
- $MW$ = mud/fluid weight (ppg — pounds per gallon)
- $TVD$ = true vertical depth (ft)
- $y$ = mole fraction, $M$ = molecular weight
- $F$ = design/safety factor (typically **0.6 – 0.8** for surface casing, **0.5** for production casing)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical conversion:** **0.052 psi/ft per ppg** comes from $\rho_{mud} \cdot g / 144$ with $g = 32.174$ ft/s² and a 1 ppg = 7.48 lb/ft³ conversion. This constant is the most-asked number in KTU petroleum papers.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In an **offshore platform** in the Mumbai High or KG Basin, a single drilling rig may simultaneously encounter:
- A sweet oil well (Class II)
- A sour gas well (Class IV)
- A high-pressure injection well (Class III)

The **Mud Logging Unit (MLU)**, **BOP stack rating**, and **H₂S evacuation muster** are *all* dictated by the **worst-case well class** active on the rig. A single misclassification can result in:
- Under-rated wellhead → **catastrophic failure at 5,500 psi when 8,000 psi is encountered**
- Wrong metallurgy → **sulfide stress cracking (SSC) and hydrogen embrittlement**
- Wrong PPE allocation → **mass H₂S exposure casualties** (refer Piper Alpha 1988, Norco 1988)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure of a Fluid Column

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Derive the relationship between bottom-hole pressure ($P_{bh}$), wellhead pressure ($P_{wh}$), and the hydrostatic head of a static fluid column of height $h$ and density $\rho_f$.

### Step 1 — Start with a Force Balance

Consider a vertical column of static fluid of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$ in the wellbore. The forces acting on the column are:
- Pressure force from above (wellhead): $P_{wh} \cdot A$ (acting downward)
- Weight of the fluid column: $W = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot V = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h$ (acting downward)
- Pressure force from below (formation): $P_{bh} \cdot A$ (acting upward)

### Step 2 — Apply Static Equilibrium

For a static (non-accelerating) fluid, the sum of vertical forces must equal zero.

$$
\sum F_{vertical} = 0
$$

$$
P_{bh} \cdot A = P_{wh} \cdot A + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h
$$

### Step 3 — Cancel the Common Area

Since $A \neq 0$, divide both sides by $A$:

$$
P_{bh} = P_{wh} + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Field Units (psi, ppg, ft)

This is the form expected in KTU exams. We use:
- $1$ ppg = $7.4805$ lb/ft³
- $1$ ft = $12$ in, so 1 ft² = 144 in²
- $g$ = $32.174$ ft/s²

The hydrostatic pressure per foot of column per ppg of mud weight:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{h} = \frac{\rho_f \cdot g}{144} = \frac{MW \cdot 7.4805 \cdot 32.174}{144} = 0.052 \cdot MW \;\; \text{psi/ft}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Field Formula

$$
P_{bh} = P_{wh} + 0.052 \cdot MW \cdot TVD
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The factor **0.052** is the conversion constant that lets you multiply **mud weight in ppg** directly by **depth in ft** to get **pressure in psi** — the universal "go-to" equation in drilling engineering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — NACE Sour/Sweet Threshold from Partial Pressure

**Problem Statement:** A natural gas well produces a gas with mole fraction $y_{H_2S} = 0.0002$ at a total surface pressure of $P_{total} = 2,000$ psi. Determine whether the well is classified as **sweet** or **sour** per **NACE MR0175**.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Partial Pressure of H₂S

By Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures:

$$
P_{H_2S} = y_{H_2S} \cdot P_{total}
$$

$$
P_{H_2S} = 0.0002 \times 2{,}000
$$

$$
P_{H_2S} = 0.4 \;\; \text{psi}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to MPa (SI Threshold)

$$
P_{H_2S} = 0.4 \times 0.006895
$$

$$
P_{H_2S} = 0.00276 \;\; \text{MPa} = 2.76 \;\; \text{kPa}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the NACE Threshold

Per **NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156**, a well is classified as **sour** if $P_{H_2S} > 0.0003$ MPa (= 0.3 kPa).

Since $0.00276$ MPa $\gg 0.0003$ MPa, the well is **SOUR**.

### Step 4 — Convert Partial Pressure to ppm Equivalent at 1 atm

Using the ideal gas approximation at standard conditions ($P_{std} = 14.7$ psi):

$$
\text{ppm} = y_{H_2S} \times 10^{6} = 0.0002 \times 10^{6} = 200 \;\; \text{ppm}
$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion

The well is **sour** (200 ppm > 10 ppm threshold), and the design must use **NACE-compliant sour-service metallurgy**, dedicated H₂S detectors, SCBA stations, and a documented H₂S contingency plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-Threshold Reminder:** NACE uses 10 ppm in the gas phase. OSHA PEL uses 20 ppm as the 8-hour permissible exposure limit. ACGIH TLV uses 10 ppm. Always quote the **standard you are applying** in your KTU answer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MAWP Calculation (Casing Design)

**Problem Statement:** A production casing has a **burst rating** of $P_{burst} = 7{,}200$ psi and a **collapse rating** of $P_{collapse} = 6{,}000$ psi. Per API 5C3 / API 5C5 with a design factor $F = 0.7$, determine the **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** and classify the well in terms of pressure regime.

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Rating

The MAWP is governed by the **smaller** of the burst and collapse ratings (whichever is the binding limit).

$$
P_{min} = \min(7200,\; 6000) = 6{,}000 \;\; \text{psi}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Design Factor

$$
MAWP = P_{min} \cdot F
$$

$$
MAWP = 6{,}000 \times 0.7
$$

$$
MAWP = 4{,}200 \;\; \text{psi}
$$

### Step 3 — Classify the Well

Comparing $4{,}200$ psi against the classification table:
- LP: < 2,000 psi → **No**
- MP: 2,000 – 5,000 psi → **Yes** ✓

### Step 4 — Conclusion

The well is classified as a **Medium Pressure (MP) well** with an MAWP of **4,200 psi**. The BOP stack and surface equipment must be rated at the next higher standard rating (typically **5,000 psi**) per API 16A, with a safety margin of at least **10 – 15%** above MAWP.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Parameter Cross-Classification

The matrix below maps how a single well may carry **multiple class labels** simultaneously. A KTU 14-mark question often asks: *"Classify the following well across all five pillars."*

| Parameter | Example Value | Resulting Class |
|---|---|---|
| Surface pressure (MAWP) | 8,500 psi | **High Pressure (HP)** |
| Reservoir temperature | 280 °F (138 °C) | Standard (not HPHT) |
| H₂S content | 750 ppm | **Sour** (NACE Region 2) |
| CO₂ content | 2.5 mol% | **Low CO₂** |
| Fluid type | Gas + condensate | **Gas-Condensate Well** |
| Wellbore inclination | 88° from vertical | **Horizontal Well** |
| Lifecycle stage | Post-discovery, 4th producer on pad | **Development Well** |
| GOR | 25,000 scf/stb | **Wet Gas (Retrograde Condensate)** |
| Service type | Producing to sales line | **Producer Well** |
| Aggregate Final Class | — | **"Sour, HP, Horizontal Gas-Condensate Producer"** |
