# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Define 'Sour Well' as per NACE MR0175 standards. List two metallurgical requirements for sour-service equipment.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
A well is classified as **sour** when the partial pressure of H₂S in the produced gas exceeds **0.0003 MPa (0.3 kPa)** at standard conditions, which corresponds to a gas-phase concentration greater than **10 ppm**. This threshold is defined by **NACE MR0175 / ISO 15156** to prevent **Sulfide Stress Cracking (SSC)** and **Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC)** in carbon-steel components.

Two metallurgical requirements for sour-service equipment:
1. **Hardness limit:** Base materials and welds must have a maximum hardness of **22 HRC (250 HV)** to resist SSC.
2. **SSC-resistant alloys:** Use of **Inconel 718, Alloy 825, or AISI 410SS in H₂S-resistant temper** for downhole and wellhead components, with proper **NACE-qualified welding procedures**.

> **Distribution (KTU marking):** Definition = 1.5 marks, Two metallurgical requirements = 1.5 marks.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between a 'Producer Well' and an 'Injector Well'. Why is the classification of injection wells critical for safety in hydrocarbon fields?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Producer Well | Injector Well |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of flow** | Reservoir → Surface | Surface → Reservoir |
| **Purpose** | Hydrocarbon recovery | Pressure maintenance, disposal, storage |
| **Typical fluid** | Oil, gas, condensate | Treated seawater, gas, CO₂, produced water |
| **Pressure at surface** | Decreases over time | Increases over time |

The classification of **injection wells** is **safety-critical** because they operate at **high pressures in the opposite direction** to natural reservoir flow. Failure of the injection string can lead to:
- **Fracturing the cap rock** and loss of containment seal.
- **Cross-flow into freshwater aquifers** (regulatory violation under EPA UIC Class II).
- **Cap-rock breach** leading to induced seismicity and greenhouse gas release (especially for CO₂ injection).

> **Distribution:** Tabular differentiation = 1.5 marks, Safety justification = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2024 Model Paper]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `CHOICE 1`**

**Classify the following well using all five pillars of classification. Justify each with data and standards.**

A well has the following data:
- **MAWP (Wellhead):** 6,800 psi
- **Reservoir Temperature:** 295 °F
- **Gas Analysis:** H₂S = 350 ppm, CO₂ = 4.0 mol%, CH₄ = 78%
- **Wellbore Inclination:** 92° from vertical
- **Lifecycle Stage:** 5th well on a regular 5-spot pattern
- **Surface Flow:** 1,800 bbl/d oil + 4.5 MMscf/d gas

#### **Part (a) — Classify by Pressure Regime and Fluid Type** `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Pressure Classification:**

The wellhead MAWP = **6,800 psi**. Comparing to the classification table:
- 5,000 – 10,000 psi → **High Pressure (HP)** well.

Since $T = 295°F < 350°F$, it is **not** an HPHT well. The HP class is confirmed.

**Step 2 — Fluid Type Classification:**

The GOR is:

$$
GOR = \frac{Q_{gas,\,Mscf/d} \times 10^{3}}{Q_{oil,\,stb/d}}
$$

$$
GOR = \frac{4{,}500{,}000 \; scf/d}{1{,}800 \; stb/d} = 2{,}500 \; scf/stb
$$

Since $GOR = 2{,}500$ scf/stb (within the typical **500 – 5,000 scf/stb** range), the well produces a **black oil / volatile oil** stream with associated gas. It is classified as an **Oil Well with associated gas** (not a gas-condensate well, since GOR < 100,000 scf/stb).

**Sub-Answer:** **High Pressure (HP) Oil Well** — 7 marks distributed across MAWP comparison (3), GOR calculation (2.5), conclusion (1.5).

#### **Part (b) — Classify by H₂S Service, Geometry, and Lifecycle Stage** `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — H₂S / Corrosive Service Classification:**

Given: $y_{H_2S} = 350$ ppm = $350 \times 10^{-6}$ mole fraction.

Partial pressure at wellhead:

$$
P_{H_2S} = 350 \times 10^{-6} \times 6{,}800 \; psi = 2.38 \; psi = 0.0164 \; MPa
$$

Since $0.0164$ MPa $\gg 0.0003$ MPa (NACE threshold), the well is **SOUR**. Specifically, at 350 ppm, it falls in the **NACE Region 2 (50 – 1,000 ppm)**.

The **CO₂ content of 4.0 mol%** is also above the typical 2% corrosion threshold, contributing to **sweet corrosion** in addition to **sour cracking**. This is a **dual-corrosion** environment requiring **13Cr / L80-13Cr metallurgy or higher**.

**Step 2 — Geometry Classification:**

Inclination 92° ≥ 90° → **Horizontal Well**.

**Step 3 — Lifecycle Classification:**

5th well on a **5-spot pattern** → **Development / Production Well**.

**Final Aggregate Class:**

> **"Sour, High Pressure, Horizontal, Development Oil Producer (with dual H₂S/CO₂ corrosion service)"**

**Sub-Answer:** 7 marks distributed across partial pressure calculation (3), geometry (1.5), lifecycle (1), final aggregate (1.5).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `CHOICE 2`**

**A casing string has a burst rating of 8,000 psi and a collapse rating of 5,500 psi. The design factor is 0.6. The well produces 2 MMscf/d gas with 5 ppm H₂S. The reservoir depth (TVD) is 10,000 ft, and the well is inclined at 75°.**

#### **Part (a) — Calculate the MAWP and classify the well by pressure regime** `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Identify the governing (limiting) casing rating:**

The collapse rating is lower (5,500 psi < 8,000 psi), so collapse is the **binding limit**.

$$
P_{min} = 5{,}500 \; psi
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the design factor:**

$$
MAWP = P_{min} \times F = 5{,}500 \times 0.6 = 3{,}300 \; psi
$$

**Step 3 — Classify the Pressure Regime:**

3,300 psi falls in the 2,000 – 5,000 psi band → **Medium Pressure (MP)**.

**Step 4 — Validate Equipment Selection:**

The next standard BOP rating is **5,000 psi** (API 16A), which provides a safety margin of:

$$
\text{Margin} = \frac{5{,}000 - 3{,}300}{3{,}300} \times 100\% = 51.5\%
$$

This exceeds the API-recommended minimum of **10 – 15%**, so the **5,000 psi BOP stack** is safe.

**Sub-Answer:** 7 marks — Binding rating identification (2), MAWP calculation (2.5), classification (1), margin validation (1.5).

#### **Part (b) — Classify by H₂S service and wellbore geometry. Compute the horizontal displacement.** `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — H₂S Classification:**

At 5 ppm H₂S, the well is **SWEET** (below the NACE threshold of 10 ppm).

$$
P_{H_2S} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 3{,}300 = 0.0165 \; psi = 0.000114 \; MPa
$$

Since $0.000114 \; MPa < 0.0003 \; MPa$, the well is **Sweet — NACE Region 0**.

> However, many operators (e.g., Shell, ONGC) treat wells with H₂S > **1 ppm** as "low-sour" for **enhanced personal H₂S monitoring** and SCBA placement, even if metallurgically classified as sweet.

**Step 2 — Wellbore Geometry Classification:**

Inclination = 75° falls in the range 60° – 89° → **Highly Deviated Well** (sometimes called "slant well").

**Step 3 — Compute Horizontal Displacement (Step-Out):**

Given TVD = 10,000 ft, inclination = 75°:

$$
\text{Horizontal Displacement} = TVD \cdot \tan(\theta) = 10{,}000 \cdot \tan(75°)
$$

$$
\text{Horizontal Displacement} = 10{,}000 \times 3.732 = 37{,}320 \; ft
$$

Converting to meters:

$$
\text{Horizontal Displacement} = 37{,}320 \times 0.3048 = 11{,}375 \; m
$$

**Step 4 — Final Aggregate Class:**

> **"Sweet, Medium Pressure, Highly Deviated Gas Well (12 km step-out)"**

**Sub-Answer:** 7 marks — Sweet calculation (2.5), geometry classification (1.5), step-out derivation (2), final aggregate (1).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams (Dec 2023 / May 2024 patterns observed):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to MPa** when applying the NACE threshold. The threshold is **0.0003 MPa**, not 0.3 kPa directly in psi. Show all unit conversions or lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing MAWP with MASP.** MASP = Maximum **Anticipated** Surface Pressure (worst-case kick scenario). MAWP = Maximum **Allowable** Working Pressure (equipment rating limit). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Using the wrong conversion factor 0.052 vs 0.0525.** The KTU-accepted value is **0.052 psi/ft per ppg**. Do not round to 0.05.
> 4. **Forgetting the dual H₂S/CO₂ corrosion service.** A well can be "sweet" (low H₂S) but still "corrosive" (high CO₂). Classify them **independently**.
> 5. **Not stating the governing standard.** Always write "As per NACE MR0175" or "Per API 16A" — quoting the standard scores 0.5 – 1 bonus mark.
> 6. **Mixing up HPHT definition.** HPHT = **> 10,000 psi AND > 350 °F simultaneously**. Either condition alone is NOT HPHT.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall):**

- ✅ A well is classified on **five independent pillars**: Pressure, Fluid Type, Function, Geometry, Corrosive Service.
- ✅ **Pressure classes:** LP (<2,000 psi) | MP (2,000-5,000) | HP (5,000-10,000) | UHP (>10,000) | **HPHT** (>10,000 psi + >350°F).
- ✅ **NACE Sour threshold:** Partial pressure of H₂S **> 0.0003 MPa** OR concentration **> 10 ppm** in gas phase.
- ✅ **Field formula:** $P_{bh} = P_{wh} + 0.052 \cdot MW \cdot TVD$ — memorize 0.052.
- ✅ **MAWP** is governed by the **smaller** of (burst, collapse) ratings, **multiplied by design factor** (typically 0.5 – 0.8).
- ✅ **BOP rating** must be **at least 10 – 15% above** MAWP per API 16A safety margin.
- ✅ **Geometry classes:** Vertical < 15° | Deviated 15° – 60° | High-Deviation 60° – 89° | **Horizontal ≥ 90°** | **Multilateral ≥ 2 branches**.
- ✅ **Lifecycle classes:** Wildcat → Appraisal → Development/Producer/Injector → Workover → P&A (Plug & Abandonment).
- ✅ **Horizontal displacement** at inclination θ: $D_h = TVD \cdot \tan(\theta)$.
- ✅ **GOR** classifies fluid: < 2,000 scf/stb = Black Oil | 2,000 – 5,000 = Volatile Oil | 5,000 – 100,000 = Retrograde Gas-Condensate | > 100,000 = Wet/Dry Gas.
- ✅ Always **quote the standard** in your answer (NACE MR0175, API 16A, API 6A, OISD-STD-140).
- ✅ **Dual corrosion** (H₂S + CO₂) requires **alloy upgrade** to 13Cr/L80-13Cr/Inconel, even when one species is below threshold.
- ✅ Misclassification → wrong BOP rating, wrong metallurgy, wrong PPE — direct path to **blowout / mass-casualty incident**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drilling Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drilling Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drilling Method** in hydrocarbon industries refers to the engineered, systematic process of creating a borehole (wellbore) through subsurface rock formations to access underground reservoirs of crude oil, natural gas, or other hydrocarbons. It is the foundational upstream activity that enables exploration, appraisal, development, and production phases of the petroleum value chain.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT745) Module 4 syllabus, drilling methods are classified based on the **mechanism of rock penetration**, **directional trajectory**, **rig configuration**, and **fluid circulation strategy**. The principal methods covered are:

1. **Cable Tool Drilling (Percussion Method)** — A historic, impact-based method.
2. **Rotary Drilling Method** — The modern industry standard.
3. **Directional & Horizontal Drilling** — For deviated or extended-reach wells.
4. **Offshore Drilling** — From fixed platforms, jack-ups, semisubmersibles, or drillships.
5. **Specialty Methods** — Coiled Tubing Drilling (CTD), Underbalanced Drilling (UBD), and Slim-Hole Drilling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (For First-Time Learners):**
> Think of drilling an oil well exactly like **drilling a hole through a layered cake using an electric hand drill, but with continuous water spraying to keep the drill bit cool and wash away the crumbs**.
> - The **drill bit** is your drill's tip.
> - The **drilling mud (fluid)** is the sprayed water — it cools, cleans, and prevents the hole from collapsing.
> - The **drill string** is the long shaft connecting the surface to the bit, like a long extension rod.
> - The **derrick** is the tripod holding everything vertically.
> - The **Blowout Preventer (BOP)** is the **emergency safety valve** — just like the pressure-relief valve on a pressure cooker. If the "steam" (high-pressure gas) tries to escape uncontrollably, the BOP shuts the well.

In the hydrocarbon industry, drilling must contend with **enormous subsurface pressures (often > 10,000 psi)**, **toxic gases (H₂S)**, **flammable hydrocarbons**, and **unstable rock formations**. Therefore, **safety engineering** is integrated into every stage of the drilling method.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Standard mud weight of water:** 8.33 ppg (pounds per gallon) or 1.0 SG (Specific Gravity).
- **1 ppg = 0.119 SG** | **1 SG = 8.33 ppg**
- **Conversion factor for hydrostatic pressure (US Field Units):** **0.052**
- **Gradient of fresh water:** 0.433 psi/ft
- **Normal formation pressure gradient:** ~0.465 psi/ft (equivalent to 9.0 ppg mud)
- **Abnormal pressure threshold:** Gradient > 0.465 psi/ft
- **Geothermal gradient (average):** 1 °F per 100 ft (~3 °C per 100 m)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rotary Drilling Method — Operational Logic

The **Rotary Drilling Method** is the dominant drilling technique used in > 95% of modern oil & gas wells. It operates on the principle of **rotary abrasion and hydraulic cleaning**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Power Generation & Transmission**
   - Diesel engines or electric motors power the **drawworks**, **rotary table**, and **mud pumps**.
2. **Rotary Motion Delivery**
   - The **rotary table** turns the **kelly** (square/hexagonal pipe), which transmits torque to the entire **drill string**.
3. **Weight-on-Bit (WOB) Application**
   - The **drawworks** raises/lowers the drill string to apply controlled downward force (WOB) on the **drill bit**.
4. **Rock Cutting (Bit Action)**
   - As the bit rotates under WOB, its **cutting elements** (either steel teeth for soft formations or PDC/tungsten carbide inserts for hard formations) crush and shear the rock.
5. **Cuttings Removal by Mud Circulation**
   - **Drilling mud** is pumped down through the **drill string**, exits at nozzles in the bit, and returns up the **annulus** carrying **cuttings** to the surface.
6. **Surface Processing**
   - Returned mud passes through **shale shakers**, **desanders**, **desilters**, and **degassers** before being recirculated.
7. **Well Control Integration**
   - The **BOP stack** (Annular + Ram Preventers) is installed on the wellhead for emergency well shut-in.

## 2.2 Drill String Components (Surface to Bottom)

| Component | Function | Safety Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Swivel** | Connects rotating kelly to stationary mud hose; supports drill string weight | Prevents hose twisting |
| **Kelly** | Transmits torque from rotary table | High fatigue stress zone |
| **Drill Pipe** | Connects surface to BHA; ~30 ft joints | Weakest tensile link |
| **Drill Collars** | Heavy, thick-walled; provide WOB | Concentrate weight near bit |
| **Heavy-Weight Drill Pipe (HWDP)** | Transitions between drill pipe and collars | Reduces fatigue failures |
| **Drill Bit** | Crushes/shears rock | Primary wear component |
| **BHA (Bottom Hole Assembly)** | Includes jars, stabilizers, reamers | Critical for directional control |

## 2.3 Cable Tool Drilling (Percussion Method)

> [!NOTE]
> A historical, slow method (still used rarely for water wells). A heavy bit is repeatedly raised and dropped to crush rock. A bailer periodically removes slurry. **Very low penetration rate** (5–15 ft/day) but **highly effective in hard, fractured rock**.

| Parameter | Rotary | Cable Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Penetration Rate | 50–500 ft/day | 5–15 ft/day |
| Hole Stability | Excellent | Poor |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Modern Use | ~98% of wells | <2% |

## 2.4 Directional, Horizontal & Offshore Drilling

- **Directional Drilling:** Intentionally deflects the wellbore to reach targets offset from the surface location (e.g., offshore from an onshore pad, or to access multiple reservoirs from one platform slot).
- **Horizontal Drilling:** Wellbore deviates to ≥ 90° from vertical; maximizes reservoir contact for shale/unconventional plays.
- **Offshore Drilling:** Conducted from:
  - **Jack-up rigs** (shallow water, < 400 ft)
  - **Semi-submersibles** (deep water, dynamic positioning)
  - **Drillships** (ultra-deep water)
  - **Fixed platforms** (shallow water)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are the most frequently tested in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure (US Field) | $P_{hs} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD$ | MW in ppg, TVD in ft, P in psi |
| 2 | Hydrostatic Pressure (SI) | $P_{hs} = MW \times 9.81 \times h \times 10^{-3}$ | MW in SG, h in m, P in kPa |
| 3 | Formation Pressure Gradient | $G_f = P_f / TVD$ | psi/ft |
| 4 | Fracture Pressure | $P_{frac} = G_{frac} \times TVD$ | psi/ft gradient |
| 5 | Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD) | $ECD = MW + \dfrac{APL}{0.052 \times TVD}$ | ppg |
| 6 | Max. Allowable Annular Surface Pressure | $MAASP = P_{frac} - P_{hs}$ at weakest casing shoe | psi |
| 7 | Kick Tolerance Volume | $V_{kick} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (D_h^2 - D_p^2) \times h$ | bbls (height × annular capacity) |
| 8 | Specific Gravity from ppg | $SG = MW / 8.33$ | — |
| 9 | Pressure Gradient from Mud Weight | $G = 0.052 \times MW$ | psi/ft |
| 10 | ROP (Maurer Model) | $ROP = K \times (WOB - WOB_t)^a \times N^b$ | ft/hr; K, a, b are constants |

> **Where:**
> - $MW$ = Mud Weight
> - $TVD$ = True Vertical Depth
> - $APL$ = Annular Pressure Loss
> - $D_h, D_p$ = Hole & Pipe diameters
> - $h$ = Kick height
> - $N$ = Rotary speed (rpm)
> - $WOB_t$ = Threshold Weight on Bit

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Well Control Calculations:** Hydrostatic pressure balance is the **#1 cause of blowouts** if miscalculated. The mud weight MUST be heavy enough to suppress formation pressure, yet light enough to avoid fracturing the formation.
- **Casing Design:** MAASP determines the safe shut-in pressure window.
- **Drilling Optimization:** ROP models (Bingham, Maurer) reduce non-productive time (NPT) — a key KPI in modern drilling contracts.
- **Kick Detection:** Understanding pressure relationships allows early detection of formation fluid influx.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure of a Fluid Column

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most critical derivation** in well control and drilling safety. It is tested repeatedly in KTU exams.

### Starting Principle — Pascal's Hydrostatic Law

A static column of fluid exerts a pressure at any depth equal to the weight of the fluid column above that depth, per unit area.

**Step 1: Define the pressure from a fluid column of height $h$.**

$$
P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of fluid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $h$ = vertical height of fluid column (m)

**Step 2: Express density in terms of specific gravity (SG).**

The density of water is $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Any fluid with specific gravity $SG$ has:

$$
\rho = SG \cdot \rho_w = SG \cdot 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the hydrostatic equation.**

$$
P = (SG \cdot 1000) \cdot 9.81 \cdot h
$$

**Step 4: Simplify to obtain SI formula in kPa.**

$$
P = SG \cdot 9810 \cdot h \;\; \text{Pa}
$$

$$
P = SG \cdot 9.81 \cdot h \;\; \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to US Field Units used in petroleum engineering.**

In the US oilfield, mud weight is expressed in **ppg** (pounds per gallon), and depth in **feet**.

The conversion uses the factor **0.052**, which is derived as follows:

1 m = 3.281 ft, so $1 \text{ ppg} = 1 \text{ lb/gal} = 0.1198 \text{ SG}$.

Hydrostatic pressure gradient of any fluid in psi/ft:

$$
\frac{dP}{dh} = 0.052 \times MW \;\; \text{(psi/ft)}
$$

Integrating over a vertical depth $TVD$:

$$
P_{hs} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD \;\; \text{psi}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A well is drilled to a TVD of 10,000 ft with 12.0 ppg mud. Calculate the hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the hole. If formation pressure is 5,200 psi, is the well overbalanced or underbalanced?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the hydrostatic pressure formula.

$$
P_{hs} = 0.052 \times 12.0 \times 10{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply sequentially.

$$
P_{hs} = 0.052 \times 12.0 = 0.624 \text{ psi/ft}
$$

$$
P_{hs} = 0.624 \times 10{,}000 = 6{,}240 \text{ psi}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare to formation pressure.

$$
\Delta P = P_{hs} - P_{form} = 6{,}240 - 5{,}200 = +1{,}040 \text{ psi}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.

Since $P_{hs} > P_{form}$ (positive differential), the well is **overbalanced** by 1,040 psi. The mud column is safely suppressing formation fluids — no kick risk under static conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always state the units explicitly in every step. Marks are deducted for writing "0.052 × 12 × 10000 = 6240" without showing intermediate calculation of the gradient.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD)

When mud is being **circulated** (not static), frictional pressure losses occur in the annulus, increasing the **effective downhole pressure** beyond hydrostatic.

**Step 1: Total downhole pressure while circulating.**

$$
P_{dh} = P_{hs} + P_{annular\;loss}
$$

**Step 2: Express annular pressure loss in terms of equivalent mud weight.**

The pressure at depth $TVD$ from a fluid of density $ECD$ would be:

$$
P_{dh} = 0.052 \times ECD \times TVD
$$

**Step 3: Equate the two expressions.**

$$
0.052 \times ECD \times TVD = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD + APL
$$

**Step 4: Solve for ECD.**

$$
ECD = MW + \frac{APL}{0.052 \times TVD}
$$

### Numerical Example

**Given:** MW = 10.5 ppg, TVD = 8,000 ft, Annular Pressure Loss (APL) = 320 psi during circulation.

**Solution:**

$$
ECD = 10.5 + \frac{320}{0.052 \times 8{,}000}
$$

**Step 1:** Calculate denominator.

$$
0.052 \times 8{,}000 = 416
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate fraction.

$$
\frac{320}{416} = 0.769 \text{ ppg}
$$

**Step 3:** Add to static mud weight.

$$
ECD = 10.5 + 0.769 = 11.27 \text{ ppg}
$$

> The well experiences an **effective mud weight of 11.27 ppg during circulation**, which must remain below the formation fracture pressure gradient to avoid lost circulation.

## 3.3 Derivation: MAASP (Maximum Allowable Annular Shut-in Pressure)

MAASP is the **highest surface pressure** that can be safely applied on the annulus during kick circulation without fracturing the weakest exposed formation (usually the casing shoe).

**Step 1: Pressure at the casing shoe from formation fracture gradient.**

$$
P_{frac,shoe} = G_{frac,shoe} \times TVD_{shoe}
$$

**Step 2: Hydrostatic pressure of mud in the annulus to the shoe.**

$$
P_{hs,shoe} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD_{shoe}
$$

**Step 3: MAASP is the difference (the pressure the surface can safely add).**

$$
MAASP = P_{frac,shoe} - P_{hs,shoe}
$$

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \times (G_{frac,shoe} - MW) \times TVD_{shoe}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget to multiply by 0.052 in Step 3. This is an automatic 2-mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rotary Drilling Rig — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This Mermaid block diagram represents the functional flow of mechanical, hydraulic, and fluid systems in a land-based rotary drilling rig, mapped from surface power generation down to the bit and back.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR[Power Generation System]
        A1[Diesel Engines]
        A2[Electric Generators]
    end

    subgraph TRANS[Power Transmission]
        B1[Driller Console]
        B2[Drawworks]
        B3[Rotary Table]
        B4[Mud Pumps]
    end

    subgraph DERR[Derrick and Hoisting]
        C1[Derrick Mast]
        C2[Crown Block]
        C3[Traveling Block]
        C4[Hook]
    end

    subgraph STR[Drill String Assembly]
        D1[Swivel]
        D2[Kelly]
        D3[Drill Pipe Joints]
        D4[Drill Collars]
        D5[Drill Bit]
    end

    subgraph WC[Well Control and Safety Stack]
        E1[Annular Preventer]
        E2[Ram Preventer Blind]
        E3[Ram Preventer Pipe]
        E4[Choke Manifold]
        E5[Kill Line]
    end

    subgraph FLUID[Mud Circulation Loop]
        F1[Mud Pit]
        F2[Shale Shaker]
        F3[Desander and Desilter]
        F4[Degasser]
        F5[Mud Gun Mixer]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> C4
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> D5
    D5 --> R1[Rock Formation Penetration]
    R1 --> R2[Cuttings Enter Annulus]
    R2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4
    E4 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4
    F4 --> F1
    F1 --> F5
    F5 --> B4
    B4 --> D1
    E4 --> E5
```

## 4.2 Drilling Method Decision Flow (Selection Logic)

This flow diagram guides the engineer in selecting an appropriate drilling method based on reservoir, geological, and economic constraints.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Well Planning Phase] --> q1{Formation Type?}
    q1 -->|Soft to Medium| q2{Well Type Required?}
    q1 -->|Hard Fractured Rock| A1[Cable Tool Percussion]
    q1 -->|Standard Reservoir| A2[Conventional Rotary]
    q1 -->|Unconventional Shale| A3[Horizontal Rotary with PDC Bit]

    q2 -->|Vertical| B1[Vertical Rotary]
    q2 -->|Multiple Targets| B2[Directional Rotary]
    q2 -->|Extended Reach| B3[ERD Rotary]

    A2 --> q3{Environment?}
    q3 -->|Onshore| C1[Land Rig]
    q3 -->|Shallow Water| C2[Jack up Rig]
    q3 -->|Deep Water| C3[Semi submersible]
    q3 -->|Ultra Deep| C4[Drillship]

    A3 --> q4{Fluid Strategy?}
    q4 -->|Conventional Mud| D1[Overbalanced Drilling]
    q4 -->|Light Fluid| D2[Underbalanced Drilling UBD]
    q4 -->|Gas or Foam| D3[Managed Pressure Drilling MPD]

    C1 --> END[Method Selected]
    C2 --> END
    C3 --> END
    C4 --> END
    B1 --> END
    B2 --> END
    B3 --> END
    D1 --> END
    D2 --> END
    D3 --> END
    A1 --> END
```

## 4.3 Mud Circulation Path (Closed-Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mud Pit Reserve] --> B[Suction Line]
    B --> C[Mud Pump]
    C --> D[Standpipe]
    D --> E[Kelly Hose and Swivel]
    E --> F[Drill String Internal]
    F --> G[Drill Bit Nozzles]
    G --> H[Annulus Carries Cuttings Upward]
    H --> I[Flowline at Surface]
    I --> J[Shale Shaker]
    J --> K[Desander Desilter]
    K --> L[Degasser and Mud Cleaner]
    L --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The mud circulation loop is a **closed hydraulic system**. Any leak, loss of circulation (LCA — Lost Circulation Area), or gas cut in the return mud triggers well-control protocols.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Rotary Drilling and Cable Tool Drilling methods in terms of penetration mechanism, rate of penetration, and typical field application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Rotary Drilling | Cable Tool Drilling |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Continuous rotation + hydraulic jetting | Repeated lifting and dropping of heavy bit |
| **Cutting Action** | Shear and crush with cuttings removed by mud | Crushing and impact; cuttings removed by bailer |
| **Rate of Penetration** | 50–500 ft/day | 5–15 ft/day |
| **Hole Stability** | Maintained by mud pressure | Poor; needs frequent casing |
| **Field Application** | Standard for oil and gas wells | Water wells, exploration in hard formations |
| **Modern Use** | ~98% of all hydrocarbon wells | <2% |

> **Conclusion:** Rotary drilling is the industry standard due to its superior penetration rate, ability to maintain wellbore stability through mud hydrostatics, and compatibility with modern downhole tools.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the significance of the conversion factor 0.052 used in drilling fluid pressure calculations.**

**Model Answer:**

The factor **0.052** converts mud weight in **pounds per gallon (ppg)** and depth in **feet** into pressure in **psi**.

**Significance:**
- 1 ppg of mud exerts a hydrostatic pressure gradient of 0.052 psi per foot of vertical depth.
- It is derived from unit conversions: $1 \text{ ppg} = 0.1198 \text{ SG}$ and $1 \text{ ft} = 0.3048 \text{ m}$.
- It is used universally in well control, kick analysis, and casing design in the petroleum industry.
- **Master formula:** $P_{hs} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD$

> **Conclusion:** The 0.052 factor is the bridge between mud weight and downhole pressure — the foundational unit conversion in drilling safety engineering.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks. Both alternatives must be attempted. Below are TWO complete alternatives.

---

### **Question B1 (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Describe the Rotary Drilling Method with a neat block diagram of the rig system. List the major components of the drill string and explain the function of each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
The rotary drilling method is the most widely used technique in modern hydrocarbon extraction. It involves rotating a drill string with a bit at its end to penetrate rock formations, while drilling mud is circulated to clean the hole and stabilize the wellbore.

**Major Drill String Components (Surface to Bottom):**

1. **Swivel** — Allows the kelly to rotate while the mud hose remains stationary. Supports the entire weight of the drill string.
2. **Kelly** — A square or hexagonal pipe that transmits torque from the rotary table to the drill string. Typically 40 ft long.
3. **Drill Pipe** — The main length of the string; comes in ~30 ft joints. Carries mud downward and provides tensile strength.
4. **Drill Collars** — Thick-walled, heavy steel pipes placed just above the bit. Provide Weight on Bit (WOB) and stiffness to the BHA.
5. **Heavy Weight Drill Pipe (HWDP)** — Transitions between drill pipe and drill collars; reduces fatigue failures.
6. **Drill Bit** — The cutting tool. Types include tricone (roller cone) bits and Polycrystalline Diamond Compact (PDC) bits.
7. **BHA (Bottom Hole Assembly)** — Includes stabilizers, jars, and reamers for directional control and hole straightening.

**Block Diagram: Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.**

**Surface Rig Components:**
- Derrick/Mast, Crown Block, Traveling Block, Hook, Drawworks, Rotary Table, Mud Pumps, Mud Pits, Shale Shakers, BOP Stack.

**Safety Note:**
Each component has a **fail-safe design** — for example, the BOP can shut in the well within 30 seconds of a kick detection, preventing a blowout.

---

#### **(b) A well is drilled vertically to a TVD of 12,000 ft using 13.5 ppg mud. The formation pressure gradient is 0.465 psi/ft and the formation fracture gradient is 0.70 psi/ft. Calculate: (i) Hydrostatic pressure at TD, (ii) Formation pressure at TD, (iii) Fracture pressure at TD, and (iv) State whether the well is overbalanced or underbalanced. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $TVD = 12{,}000 \text{ ft}$
- $MW = 13.5 \text{ ppg}$
- $G_{form} = 0.465 \text{ psi/ft}$
- $G_{frac} = 0.70 \text{ psi/ft}$

**(i) Hydrostatic Pressure at TD:**

$$
P_{hs} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD
$$

$$
P_{hs} = 0.052 \times 13.5 \times 12{,}000
$$

$$
P_{hs} = 0.702 \times 12{,}000 = 8{,}424 \text{ psi}
$$

**(ii) Formation Pressure at TD:**

$$
P_{form} = G_{form} \times TVD
$$

$$
P_{form} = 0.465 \times 12{,}000 = 5{,}580 \text{ psi}
$$

**(iii) Fracture Pressure at TD:**

$$
P_{frac} = G_{frac} \times TVD
$$

$$
P_{frac} = 0.70 \times 12{,}000 = 8{,}400 \text{ psi}
$$

**(iv) Overbalanced or Underbalanced?**

$$
\Delta P = P_{hs} - P_{form} = 8{,}424 - 5{,}580 = 2{,}844 \text{ psi}
$$

Since $P_{hs} > P_{form}$, the well is **OVERBALANCED** by 2,844 psi.

**Additional Safety Check:**

$$
\text{Safety Margin to Fracture} = P_{frac} - P_{hs} = 8{,}400 - 8{,}424 = -24 \text{ psi}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **DANGER — Critical Safety Insight:** The hydrostatic pressure **exceeds the fracture pressure by 24 psi**. The well is at risk of **lost circulation** (fracturing the formation). The mud weight MUST be reduced to maintain a safe margin (typically ≥ 200 psi below $P_{frac}$).

**Recommended Mud Weight for Safety:**

$$
MW_{max} = \frac{P_{frac} - 200}{0.052 \times TVD} = \frac{8{,}200}{0.052 \times 12{,}000} = 13.14 \text{ ppg}
$$

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain the function and types of Blowout Preventers (BOPs) used in drilling. Why is the BOP stack considered the most critical safety device on a drilling rig? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Function of the BOP:**
A Blowout Preventer (BOP) is a large, high-pressure valve assembly installed on the wellhead. Its function is to **seal, control, and monitor the wellbore** to prevent the uncontrolled release of formation fluids (oil, gas, water) — an event known as a **blowout**, which can cause catastrophic loss of life, equipment, and environmental damage.

**Types of BOPs:**

1. **Annular Preventer (Bag-type)**
   - Uses a rubber elastomer element that closes around any shape — drill pipe, kelly, or open hole.
   - First line of defense; can seal on irregular shapes.

2. **Ram Preventers**
   - **Blind Ram:** Seals an open hole (no pipe in the well).
   - **Pipe Ram:** Seals around the drill pipe (with pipe in the well).
   - **Shear Ram:** Cuts through the drill pipe and seals the well — used in **emergency disconnection** (e.g., during a pending blowout).
   - **Variable Bore Ram:** Seals on multiple pipe sizes.

3. **Subsea BOP Stack**
   - Used for offshore drilling; remotely operated from the surface vessel via hydraulic pods.
   - Includes redundant systems for reliability.

**BOP Stack Configuration (Typical Land Rig):**
- From top to bottom: Annular Preventer → Pipe Ram → Blind/Shear Ram → Drilling Spool → Casing Head.

**Why BOP is the Most Critical Safety Device:**
- It is the **last line of defense** between high-pressure formation fluids and the surface environment.
- Blowouts are the most catastrophic events in drilling — they can destroy rigs, ignite fires (e.g., Deepwater Horizon, 2010), and cause fatalities.
- BOPs are pressure-tested regularly (typically every 21 days) to API standards.
- They are mandated by regulatory bodies worldwide (API Spec 16A, 16D).

---

#### **(b) With a 10.8 ppg mud in a 12,000 ft well, the annular pressure loss during circulation is 380 psi. The casing shoe is set at 8,000 ft with a formation fracture gradient of 0.65 psi/ft at the shoe. Calculate: (i) Hydrostatic pressure at TD, (ii) ECD at TD, (iii) MAASP at the casing shoe, and (iv) Comment on whether the well is in a safe operating window. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $MW = 10.8 \text{ ppg}$
- $TVD = 12{,}000 \text{ ft}$
- $APL = 380 \text{ psi}$
- $TVD_{shoe} = 8{,}000 \text{ ft}$
- $G_{frac,shoe} = 0.65 \text{ psi/ft}$

**(i) Hydrostatic Pressure at TD (Static):**

$$
P_{hs} = 0.052 \times 10.8 \times 12{,}000
$$

$$
P_{hs} = 0.5616 \times 12{,}000 = 6{,}739.2 \text{ psi}
$$

**(ii) ECD at TD (While Circulating):**

$$
ECD = MW + \frac{APL}{0.052 \times TVD}
$$

$$
ECD = 10.8 + \frac{380}{0.052 \times 12{,}000}
$$

$$
ECD = 10.8 + \frac{380}{624} = 10.8 + 0.609 = 11.41 \text{ ppg}
$$

**Equivalent downhole pressure while circulating:**

$$
P_{dh} = 0.052 \times 11.41 \times 12{,}000 = 7{,}119.8 \text{ psi}
$$

**(iii) MAASP at the Casing Shoe:**

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \times (G_{frac,shoe} - MW) \times TVD_{shoe}
$$

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \times (0.65 - 0.5616) \times 8{,}000
$$

(Note: $0.052 \times MW = 0.052 \times 10.8 = 0.5616 \text{ psi/ft}$)

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \times 0.0884 \times 8{,}000 = 36.77 \text{ psi}
$$

**(iv) Safety Window Analysis:**

The MAASP is only **36.77 psi** — an extremely narrow safety window. During a kick shut-in, surface pressure must be kept below this value, or the formation at the shoe will fracture.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert the **0.052 × MW** term when computing MAASP.
> 2. Confusing **static** hydrostatic pressure with **circulating** ECD pressure.
> 3. Failing to comment on safety implications (narrow window = high risk).
> 4. Not stating units explicitly in the final answer.
>
> **Recommendation:** The well operator must consider **raising the casing shoe to a stronger formation**, **reducing mud weight**, or **using Managed Pressure Drilling (MPD)** to widen the safety window.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Drilling Method**

- [ ] **Definition:** Drilling is the engineered creation of a borehole to access hydrocarbon reservoirs.
- [ ] **Rotary Drilling** is the industry standard (> 95% of wells).
- [ ] **Cable Tool Drilling** is percussion-based; slow; rarely used in modern oil & gas.
- [ ] **Drill String Order (Top to Bottom):** Swivel → Kelly → Drill Pipe → HWDP → Drill Collars → Bit.
- [ ] **Drilling Mud** performs four critical functions: **C**lean, **C**ool, **C**ushion (hydrostatic), and **C**arry cuttings.
- [ ] **Mud Weight Units:** ppg (US Field) and SG (SI). Conversion: $SG = MW / 8.33$.
- [ ] **Master Hydrostatic Equation:** $P_{hs} = 0.052 \times MW \times TVD$ (psi).
- [ ] **ECD Formula:** $ECD = MW + APL / (0.052 \times TVD)$ — accounts for annular friction during circulation.
- [ ] **MAASP Formula:** $MAASP = 0.052 \times (G_{frac,shoe} - MW) \times TVD_{shoe}$ — the safe shut-in pressure limit.
- [ ] **Overbalanced:** $P_{hs} > P_{form}$ (no kick risk statically).
- [ ] **Underbalanced:** $P_{hs} < P_{form}$ (kick risk; used in UBD to avoid formation damage).
- [ ] **BOP Stack** is the LAST line of defense against blowouts; tested every 21 days per API 16A.
- [ ] **BOP Types:** Annular, Pipe Ram, Blind Ram, Shear Ram (cuts pipe in emergencies).
- [ ] **Offshore Rig Types:** Jack-up (shallow), Semi-submersible (deep), Drillship (ultra-deep), Fixed Platform.
- [ ] **Directional Drilling:** Used to reach offset targets; horizontal wells maximize reservoir contact.
- [ ] **ROP (Rate of Penetration)** is governed by **WOB**, **RPM**, and **formation strength** (Maurer Model).
- [ ] **Geothermal Gradient** ~1 °F per 100 ft affects mud density planning and HPHT well design.
- [ ] **Formation Pressure Gradient** (normal) = 0.465 psi/ft ≈ 9.0 ppg equivalent mud.
- [ ] **Fracture Pressure** is the upper limit — exceeding it causes **lost circulation**.
- [ ] **Kick Tolerance** = volume of influx the well can handle before reaching fracture pressure.

> **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** When in doubt, always show the **units at every step**. Examiners allocate partial marks for correct intermediate calculations even if the final answer is wrong.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary Drilling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rotary Drilling

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Rotary Drilling** is the predominant mechanical method employed in the hydrocarbon exploration and production industry for creating deep boreholes into the Earth's subsurface. It operates on the principle of a continuously rotating drill string (comprising drill pipes, drill collars, and a drill bit) that simultaneously applies axial compressive load (Weight on Bit, WOB) and rotational torque to crush, shear, and gouge the rock formation. A specially engineered fluid — **drilling mud** — is pumped down through the hollow drill string, exits at the bit nozzles, and returns up the annulus carrying the drilled rock cuttings to the surface, while also stabilizing the wellbore hydrostatically and geomechanically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PESFT745 / Module 4):** Rotary drilling is treated under the broader learning unit concerning *Drilling Operations & Well Control Safety*. Students must correlate mechanical drilling parameters with **hydrocarbon-specific safety hazards** such as kicks, blowouts, lost circulation, and hydrogen sulfide exposure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine using an electric hand drill to bore a deep hole into a thick wooden plank. As the drill bit spins and pushes downward, it generates fine wood dust (cuttings) and considerable frictional heat. To solve this, you would periodically withdraw the bit and blow away the dust. Now scale this concept up to **kilometers of hard sedimentary rock** under immense subsurface pressures and temperatures.

In rotary drilling, the "bit" is a heavy steel tool studded with hardened cutters, the "drill" is a 3–6 km long steel pipe string suspended from a derrick, and the "blowing away of dust" is replaced by a continuously circulating **thixotropic fluid (mud)** that does six simultaneous jobs: cleans the bit, cools the bit, transports cuttings, stabilizes the wellbore walls, prevents formation fluids from entering the well, and transmits hydraulic horsepower to the bit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Standard Reference:** The API (American Petroleum Institute) Specification 7-1 governs rotary drill bit geometry, while API RP 13B-1/13B-2 governs **drilling fluid testing** procedures. Standard mud weight is expressed in **pounds per gallon (ppg)** or **pounds per cubic foot (pcf)**, and **1 ppg ≈ 0.1198 SG**.

### 1.3 Why Rotary Drilling Dominates the Hydrocarbon Industry

The choice of rotary drilling over older methods (e.g., cable-tool or percussion drilling) stems from four engineering advantages critical to hydrocarbon safety:

| Advantage | Safety/Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| Continuous circulation of mud | Provides immediate **hydrostatic pressure barrier** against formation fluid influx (kick prevention) |
| Faster penetration rate | Reduces personnel exposure time on the rig floor |
| Ability to drill **deviated** and **horizontal** wells | Accesses reservoirs unreachable by vertical wells |
| Continuous wellbore pressure monitoring | Enables real-time **well control** via the BOP stack |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety-Critical Caveat:** Rotary drilling in hydrocarbon-bearing zones is intrinsically hazardous because every meter of penetration exposes the rig to **formation pressures** that may exceed the mud hydrostatic column. Loss of mud circulation or inadequate mud density can trigger an uncontrolled influx of formation fluids — i.e., a **blowout**, the most catastrophic well-control failure in the petroleum industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Rotary Drilling Process

The rotary drilling process can be decomposed into **six concurrent and interdependent subsystems** that must function safely in unison:

**1. Hoisting & Rotating System (Mechanical)**
- **Derrick/Mast:** Lattice steel structure (typically **30–45 m tall**) supporting the entire drill string weight and providing clearance to pull out and run in the string (tripping).
- **Drawworks:** Hoisting winch that raises/lowers the string via the traveling block and crown block.
- **Rotary Table / Top Drive:** Provides rotational torque to the drill string. Top drives have largely superseded rotary tables in modern rigs due to superior **directional drilling** capability.

**2. Drill String (Load-Bearing Column)**
- **Kelly:** Square or hexagonal cross-section pipe (top) that transmits torque from the rotary table.
- **Drill Pipes:** API-standardized lengths (typically **9.1 m / Range 2** or **13.7 m / Range 3**) with tool joints.
- **Drill Collars:** Thick-walled, heavy steel tubes placed directly above the bit to apply WOB and provide **pendulum stiffness** for vertical wells.

**3. Drill Bit (Cutting Element)**
- **Roller Cone Bits (Roller-Bit / Rock Bits):** Three rotating cones with inserted tungsten carbide or milled-teeth cutters; ideal for hard, abrasive formations.
- **Polycrystalline Diamond Compact (PDC) Bits:** Fixed-cutter bits with synthetic diamond cutters; superior in soft-to-medium shale formations.
- **Diamond Impregnated Bits:** Used in extremely hard, non-abrasive formations.

**4. Circulating System (Hydraulic)**
- **Mud Pumps** (typically two triplex or duplex pumps, each rated **750–2200 hp**) pressurize the mud.
- **Standpipe, Rotary Hose, Gooseneck, Swivel:** High-pressure conduit delivering mud downhole.
- **Flow Line, Shale Shakers, Desander/Desilter, Degasser, Mud Cleaner:** Surface equipment that processes returning mud to remove cuttings and entrained gas.

**5. Well Control System (Safety Subsystem)**
- **BOP Stack (Blowout Preventer):** Installed on the wellhead; contains **annular preventer**, **ram preventers** (blind, pipe, shear), and kill line.
- **Choke Manifold:** Controls backpressure on the annulus during well-killing operations.
- **Diverter System:** For surface-hole intervals where shallow gas may be encountered.

**6. Monitoring & Instrumentation**
- **PVT (Pressure While Drilling) / MWD (Measurement While Drilling) / LWD (Logging While Drilling):** Real-time downhole telemetry of weight on bit, torque, RPM, ROP, downhole pressure, and gamma ray.
- **Mud Pit Level Sensors & Flow Sensors:** Early detection of kicks (pit gain) or lost circulation (pit loss).

### 2.2 Why Each Subsystem Matters — The 'Why' Behind the 'How'

| Step / Subsystem | 'Why' It Exists (Engineering Reason) |
|---|---|
| Apply WOB + Rotation | Generate sufficient downhole energy density to overcome rock compressive/shear strength |
| Circulate mud at ≥ **500 gpm** | Lift cuttings upward at **slip velocity < annular velocity** to avoid bed formation |
| Maintain mud density above pore pressure | Hydrostatic overbalance prevents formation fluid migration into the wellbore |
| Maintain mud density below fracture gradient | Prevents induced lost circulation and underground blowouts |
| Install BOP before drilling into pressured zones | Last-line mechanical barrier against catastrophic blowouts |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Reference)

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Units (Field Standard) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_h = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot D$ | Hydrostatic pressure of mud column | $P_h$ in psi, $\rho_m$ in ppg, $D$ (TVD) in ft |
| 2 | $P_h = 0.00981 \cdot \rho_m \cdot D$ | Hydrostatic pressure (SI-mixed: $\rho_m$ in kg/m³, $D$ in m) | $P_h$ in kPa |
| 3 | $\rho_{eq} = \rho_m + \dfrac{\Delta P_{fric}}{0.052 \cdot D}$ | Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD) | ppg |
| 4 | $WOB = W_{DC} \cdot (1 - L_{air})$ | Effective Weight on Bit (corrected for buoyancy) | lbs or kN |
| 5 | $ROP = \dfrac{k \cdot \left(\dfrac{WOB}{d_b}\right) \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}}{1 + c \cdot \dfrac{WOB}{d_b}}$ | Modified Bingham ROP model | ft/hr |
| 6 | $A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_h^2 - D_p^2\right)$ | Cross-sectional area of annular space | in² |
| 7 | $V_{ann} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{ann}}$ | Annular velocity (must exceed cuttings slip velocity by factor of 1.5–2) | ft/min |
| 8 | $P_{kick} = P_{sidpp} + P_h$ | Bottomhole pressure during shut-in (Shut-In Drill Pipe Pressure + Hydrostatic) | psi |
| 9 | $\rho_{kill} = \dfrac{P_{form}}{0.052 \cdot D}$ | Required kill mud weight (Driller's Method / Wait-and-Weight) | ppg |
| 10 | $SG_{mud} = \dfrac{\rho_m \text{ (ppg)}}{8.33}$ | Specific gravity conversion (8.33 ppg = 1.000 SG = water) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in KTU Problems:**
> - Density of water (fresh) = **8.33 ppg** = **62.4 lb/ft³** = **1000 kg/m³**
> - Density of seawater = **8.6 ppg**
> - Normal formation pressure gradient ≈ **0.465 psi/ft** (≈ 10.5 ppg equivalent)
> - Normal compaction pressure gradient ≈ **0.8–1.0 psi/ft**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The rotary drilling principle is the backbone of:
- **Onshore & offshore oil & gas exploration wells**
- **Geothermal energy wells**
- **Water-well drilling**
- **Mineral exploration core holes**
- **CO₂ sequestration injection wells** (an emerging carbon-capture application)

Safety-engineering applications include designing **managed-pressure drilling (MPD)** systems, **underbalanced drilling (UBD)** envelopes, and **well control barrier schematics** that conform to API Standard 53 and ISO 16530.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure Exerted by a Static Mud Column

The single most safety-critical calculation in rotary drilling is the hydrostatic pressure exerted by the drilling fluid column on the bottom of the wellbore. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Pressure definition in a static fluid column**

For a static incompressible fluid, pressure varies linearly with depth according to:

$$P = P_0 + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

where:
- $P_0$ = surface pressure (often atmospheric ≈ 14.7 psi, but typically referenced as 0 psi gauge)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (mass per unit volume)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (32.174 ft/s² in field units)
- $h$ = vertical depth (ft)

**Step 2 — Converting to field-units (ppg × ft → psi)**

The field-units form preferred by petroleum engineers uses $\rho_m$ in **pounds per gallon (ppg)** and depth $D$ in **feet**. We multiply through by a unit-conversion factor.

Because $1 \text{ ppg} = \dfrac{1 \text{ lb}}{1 \text{ gal}}$ and $1 \text{ gal} = 0.13368 \text{ ft}^3$, while $g = 32.174 \text{ ft/s}^2$, the combined unit conversion factor is:

$$0.052 \quad \frac{\text{psi}}{\text{ft} \cdot \text{ppg}}$$

**Step 3 — Final Field Formula**

$$\boxed{P_h = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot D}$$

**Worked Example:**

A well is drilled vertically to a TVD of **10,000 ft** using a mud of density **12.0 ppg**. Compute the hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the hole.

**Solution:**

$$P_h = 0.052 \cdot 12.0 \cdot 10{,}000$$

$$P_h = 0.624 \cdot 10{,}000$$

$$P_h = 6{,}240 \text{ psi}$$

This value must be **greater than** the formation pore pressure (to prevent influx) and **less than** the formation fracture pressure (to prevent lost circulation). The narrow corridor between these two is the **operating window** of safe mud density.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD)

When mud is actively circulating, frictional pressure losses in the annulus ($\Delta P_{fric}$) **add** to the hydrostatic column. Hence the *effective* downhole pressure is higher than the static value. This effective pressure is expressed as the Equivalent Circulating Density (ECD).

**Step 1 — Define the total bottomhole pressure during circulation**

$$P_{bh,c} = P_h + \Delta P_{fric}$$

**Step 2 — Define ECD as the (hypothetical) static mud density that would yield the same $P_{bh,c}$**

$$\rho_{ecd} \cdot 0.052 \cdot D = P_h + \Delta P_{fric}$$

$$\rho_{ecd} \cdot 0.052 \cdot D = \rho_m \cdot 0.052 \cdot D + \Delta P_{fric}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for ECD**

$$\boxed{\rho_{ecd} = \rho_m + \dfrac{\Delta P_{fric}}{0.052 \cdot D}}$$

**Worked Example:**

A vertical well at TVD **12,000 ft** is being drilled with **10.5 ppg** mud. Annular frictional pressure loss is measured at **800 psi**. Compute the ECD.

**Solution:**

$$\rho_{ecd} = 10.5 + \dfrac{800}{0.052 \cdot 12{,}000}$$

$$\rho_{ecd} = 10.5 + \dfrac{800}{624}$$

$$\rho_{ecd} = 10.5 + 1.282$$

$$\rho_{ecd} \approx 11.78 \text{ ppg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Implication:** The ECD (11.78 ppg in this case) — *not* the static mud weight — is what actually balances the formation. If the fracture gradient at 12,000 ft corresponds to a mud weight of **11.50 ppg**, this operation will fracture the formation. The drilling engineer must reduce flow rate, lower mud rheology, or adjust mud density to bring ECD below the fracture limit.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kill Mud Weight (Driller's / Wait-and-Weight Method)

When a kick is detected and the well is shut in, the Shut-In Drill Pipe Pressure (SIDPP) is recorded. The hydrostatic mud pressure is therefore *insufficient* by exactly the SIDPP. To kill the well, additional mud weight must be added.

**Step 1 — Original (under-balanced) condition at bottomhole:**

$$P_{form} = P_h + SIDPP$$

$$\rho_m \cdot 0.052 \cdot D + SIDPP = P_{form}$$

**Step 2 — Desired (balanced) condition with kill mud weight $\rho_{kill}$:**

$$\rho_{kill} \cdot 0.052 \cdot D = P_{form}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two equations:**

$$\rho_{kill} \cdot 0.052 \cdot D - \rho_m \cdot 0.052 \cdot D = SIDPP$$

**Step 4 — Factor and solve:**

$$(\rho_{kill} - \rho_m) \cdot 0.052 \cdot D = SIDPP$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{kill} = \rho_m + \dfrac{SIDPP}{0.052 \cdot D}}$$

**Worked Example:**

During tripping, a kick is observed. The well is shut in; SIDPP reads **450 psi**. Current mud weight is **9.8 ppg**, and TVD at the bit is **8,500 ft**. Compute the required kill mud weight.

**Solution:**

$$\rho_{kill} = 9.8 + \dfrac{450}{0.052 \cdot 8{,}500}$$

$$\rho_{kill} = 9.8 + \dfrac{450}{442}$$

$$\rho_{kill} = 9.8 + 1.018$$

$$\rho_{kill} \approx 10.82 \text{ ppg}$$

The rig must now prepare and circulate a new mud batch at **10.82 ppg** to restore hydrostatic overbalance without fracturing the formation.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Annular Velocity & Cuttings Transport Criterion

For safe cuttings evacuation, the upward mud velocity in the annulus ($V_{ann}$) must exceed the *slip velocity* ($V_{slip}$) of the cuttings — typically by a factor of **1.5 to 2.0**.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the annulus between borehole ($D_h$) and drill pipe ($D_p$):**

$$A_{ann} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_h^2 - D_p^2\right)$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate $Q$ (in gpm) must be converted to ft³/min:**

$$Q_{ft^3/min} = \dfrac{Q_{gpm}}{7.481}$$

**Step 3 — Annular velocity:**

$$\boxed{V_{ann} = \dfrac{24.5 \cdot Q}{D_h^2 - D_p^2} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{ft}}{\text{min}}\right]}$$

where $Q$ is in gpm and $D_h, D_p$ are in inches (the 24.5 factor absorbs all unit conversions: $\frac{144}{\pi \cdot 7.481 \cdot 60} \approx 0.4083$, then $1/0.4083 \approx 2.45$ per minute, and scaled by 10 → the field-standard formula uses **24.5** when $Q$ is in gpm and dimensions in inches).

**Step 4 — Cuttings transport safety criterion:**

$$V_{ann} \geq 1.5 \cdot V_{slip}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Slip Velocities (field estimates):**
> - Sand-sized cuttings: $V_{slip} \approx 30$–$40$ ft/min
> - Shale cuttings: $V_{slip} \approx 15$–$25$ ft/min
> - Coal cuttings: $V_{slip} \approx 10$–$20$ ft/min

**Worked Example:**

A 12¼-in. hole is being drilled with 5-in. drill pipe. Mud flow rate is **600 gpm**. Compute annular velocity and check transport adequacy for shale cuttings ($V_{slip} = 20$ ft/min).

**Solution:**

$$V_{ann} = \dfrac{24.5 \cdot 600}{12.25^2 - 5^2} = \dfrac{14{,}700}{150.06 - 25} = \dfrac{14{,}700}{125.06}$$

$$V_{ann} \approx 117.6 \text{ ft/min}$$

$$1.5 \cdot V_{slip} = 1.5 \cdot 20 = 30 \text{ ft/min}$$

Since $117.6 \gg 30$, transport is **adequate**.

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the interaction between the major subsystems of a rotary drilling rig, focusing on the **functional flow of mechanical energy, hydraulic energy, and safety barriers**. ### 4.2 Functional Flow Description (Read-Through)

1. **Mechanical Path (orange):** Hoisting and rotational torque are delivered from the drawworks and rotary table through the Kelly, drill string, and BHA to the drill bit.
2. **Hydraulic Path (blue):** Mud is drawn from the suction pit by the pumps, pressurized through the standpipe, down the drill string, out of the bit nozzles, up the annulus, and back to surface for cleaning.
3. **Safety Barrier Path (red):** The BOP stack and choke manifold form the *primary* mechanical barrier against a kick; pit-gain sensors provide the *primary* detection mechanism.
4. **Formation Pressure Path (green):** Formation pore pressure is the *threat variable* that the entire mud hydrostatic column must exceed.

### 4.3 Kick and Blowout Sequence (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Normal Drilling: Pmud greater than Ppore] --> S2{Pit Gain detected?}
    S2 -- No --> S1
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Shut in well on BOP - record SIDPP and SICP]
    S3 --> S4{Is Pore Pressure greater than Pmud?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Prepare kill mud at calculated density]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Resume circulation at original density]
    S5 --> S7[Wait and Weight or Drillers Method]
    S7 --> S8[Kick circulated out, well balanced]
    S8 --> S9[Resume drilling operations]

    classDef danger fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safe fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class S2,S3,S4,S7 danger
    class S1,S8,S9 safe
    class S5,S6 action
```
