## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> **Assessment Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** End-Semester Examination (ESE) for a 4-credit theory course carries **60 marks total** distributed across Part A (short answer) and Part B (long answer with internal choice). Module 4 typically contributes **15–20 marks** in the question paper, of which **Rotary Drilling** is allotted approximately **7–10 marks** as a standalone question or paired with Well Control.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Define Rotary Drilling. List any four advantages of rotary drilling over the cable-tool (percussion) method.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Rotary drilling is a method of drilling in which a rotating bit, attached to the bottom of a long drill string, is pressed against the rock while a drilling fluid is continuously circulated to remove cuttings and stabilize the wellbore.

**Four Advantages:**
1. Faster penetration rate — reduces overall drilling time.
2. Continuous mud circulation provides constant hydrostatic pressure and immediate cuttings removal.
3. Suitable for drilling **deviated and horizontal** wells.
4. Enables real-time measurement of downhole parameters (WOB, ROP, torque).
5. *(any four valid points)*

*(Marks distribution: 1 for definition, 2 for four advantages @ 0.5 each)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**State the function of the following in rotary drilling: (a) Kelly, (b) Drill Collar, (c) Shale Shaker.**

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| (a) Kelly | Square/hexagonal pipe at top of drill string; transmits torque from the rotary table to the drill string while permitting vertical movement. |
| (b) Drill Collar | Heavy, thick-walled steel tubes placed above the bit to concentrate weight on the bit (WOB) and provide stiffness/pendulum effect for vertical hole control. |
| (c) Shale Shaker | Primary solids-control equipment at surface; uses vibrating screens to separate drilled cuttings from returning mud before the mud is recycled. |

*(3 marks: 1 each)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module-Internal Choice):** The student must attempt **one of two** alternatives.

---

#### **OPTION A** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **main components of a rotary drilling rig** and describe the function of each major subsystem.
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A well is being drilled vertically at **TVD = 9,500 ft** using a mud of density **11.2 ppg**. During connection, a kick is observed. The well is shut in, and the **SIDPP = 380 psi** is recorded. Compute:
  - (i) Formation pore pressure
  - (ii) Required kill mud weight using the **Driller's Method** formula.
  - (iii) Comment on whether the kill mud weight will fracture the formation if the fracture pressure at 9,500 ft is **5,300 psi**.
**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A rotary drilling rig consists of the following subsystems (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4):

1. **Hoisting System** — Derrick/Mast, Crown Block, Traveling Block, Hook, Drawworks — raises and lowers the drill string.
2. **Rotating System** — Rotary Table (or Top Drive), Kelly, Swivel — transmits torque to the drill string.
3. **Circulating System** — Mud pumps, standpipe, rotary hose, drill string (down), annulus (up), flow line, shale shakers, desanders, mud cleaner, degasser, mud pits.
4. **Power System** — Diesel engines/generators supplying power.
5. **Well Control System** — BOP stack (annular, blind, pipe, shear rams), choke manifold, kill line, accumulator.
6. **Drill String** — Kelly, drill pipes, drill collars, heavy-weight drill pipe (HWDP), bit.
7. **Monitoring System** — MWD/LWD, PWD, mud-logging unit, pit-level sensors.

*(Subsystems 1–7 with brief functional descriptions will earn full 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Formation pore pressure:**

The shut-in condition means the formation pressure equals the hydrostatic mud pressure plus the SIDPP:

$$P_{form} = P_h + SIDPP$$

$$P_h = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot D = 0.052 \cdot 11.2 \cdot 9{,}500$$

$$P_h = 0.5824 \cdot 9{,}500 = 5{,}532.8 \text{ psi}$$

$$P_{form} = 5{,}532.8 + 380 = 5{,}912.8 \text{ psi}$$

**(ii) Required kill mud weight:**

$$\rho_{kill} = \rho_m + \dfrac{SIDPP}{0.052 \cdot D} = 11.2 + \dfrac{380}{0.052 \cdot 9{,}500}$$

$$\rho_{kill} = 11.2 + \dfrac{380}{494} = 11.2 + 0.769$$

$$\rho_{kill} \approx 11.97 \text{ ppg}$$

**(iii) Fracture check:**

Maximum allowable hydrostatic at 9,500 ft = fracture pressure = **5,300 psi**.

Hydrostatic pressure of kill mud at the formation face:

$$P_{h,kill} = 0.052 \cdot 11.97 \cdot 9{,}500 = 5{,}913.4 \text{ psi}$$

Since $5{,}913.4 \text{ psi} > 5{,}300 \text{ psi}$, the kill mud will **fracture the formation**.

**Engineering Recommendation:** The kill mud weight must be capped at the value that produces hydrostatic pressure equal to the fracture pressure minus a safety margin (typically **0.5 ppg** below fracture-equivalent mud weight). Fracture-equivalent MW:

$$\rho_{frac} = \dfrac{5{,}300}{0.052 \cdot 9{,}500} = 10.73 \text{ ppg}$$

Since this is **less** than the original mud weight (11.2 ppg), the formation was already over-pressured relative to the fracture gradient — the **pore pressure itself exceeded the fracture pressure**. This is a *geological abnormality* (loss zone or abnormally low fracture gradient). The well cannot be safely killed by simple hydrostatic means; a **bullheading** or **engineered underbalanced** operation with **managed-pressure drilling (MPD)** is required.

---

#### **OPTION B** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** What are the **different types of drill bits** used in rotary drilling? Compare **roller-cone bits** and **PDC bits** in terms of construction, applicable formations, ROP, and durability.
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 17½-in. surface hole is being drilled with **9.5-in. drill collars** at a flow rate of **750 gpm** using a mud of density **9.0 ppg**. Calculate:
  - (i) Annular velocity between drill collars and open hole.
  - (ii) Hydrostatic pressure at TD = 2,000 ft.
  - (iii) Whether cuttings transport is adequate for sand-sized cuttings ($V_{slip} = 35$ ft/min).
**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Types of drill bits:**

1. **Roller-cone (Rock) Bits** — Three rotating cones with teeth (milled steel or tungsten carbide inserts). Classified as **Milled-Tooth (M)** or **Insert (I)** and graded by IADC code.
2. **Polycrystalline Diamond Compact (PDC) Bits** — Fixed-cutter body with synthetic diamond cutters shearing the rock.
3. **Diamond Impregnated Bits** — Sintered diamond matrix body, used in extremely hard formations.
4. **Hybrid Bits** — Combination of PDC and roller-cone elements (e.g., Kymera™).
5. **Specialty Bits** — Coring bits, bi-center bits, hole-openers.

**Comparison Roller-Cone vs. PDC:**

| Parameter | Roller-Cone Bit | PDC Bit |
|---|---|---|
| Cutting mechanism | Crushing + gouging via tooth impact | Shearing via continuous cutter drag |
| Best suited for | Hard, abrasive formations (limestone, sandstone) | Soft-to-medium, ductile formations (shale) |
| ROP in compatible formation | Moderate | **High** (3–10× roller-cone) |
| Durability / Bit Life | Longer in hard rock | Longer in soft, non-abrasive rock |
| Construction | 3 rotating cones, bearings (journal or sealed) | Solid steel body with synthetic diamond cutters brazed to matrix |
| Hydraulic cleaning | Moderate (depends on jet position) | Excellent (multi-nozzle design) |
| Cost per bit | Lower initial | Higher initial, but lower cost-per-foot |

*(7 marks — 3 marks for the list with brief descriptions and 4 marks for the comparison.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Hole diameter $D_h = 17.5$ in
- Drill-collar OD $D_p = 9.5$ in
- Flow rate $Q = 750$ gpm
- Mud weight $\rho_m = 9.0$ ppg
- TD = 2,000 ft
- $V_{slip}$ (sand) = 35 ft/min

**(i) Annular velocity:**

$$V_{ann} = \dfrac{24.5 \cdot Q}{D_h^2 - D_p^2} = \dfrac{24.5 \cdot 750}{17.5^2 - 9.5^2}$$

$$V_{ann} = \dfrac{18{,}375}{306.25 - 90.25} = \dfrac{18{,}375}{216}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ann} \approx 85.07 \text{ ft/min}}$$

**(ii) Hydrostatic pressure at TD:**

$$P_h = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot D = 0.052 \cdot 9.0 \cdot 2{,}000$$

$$\boxed{P_h = 936 \text{ psi}}$$

**(iii) Cuttings transport adequacy:**

$$1.5 \cdot V_{slip} = 1.5 \cdot 35 = 52.5 \text{ ft/min}$$

Since $V_{ann} = 85.07 \text{ ft/min} > 52.5 \text{ ft/min}$, the annular velocity **is adequate** to transport sand-sized cuttings upward, satisfying the standard 1.5× safety criterion. *(A more conservative 2× check: $2 \times 35 = 70$ ft/min; 85.07 > 70, so the criterion is met under the 2× standard as well.)*

---

### KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks in Rotary-Drilling Questions (KTU Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.052 conversion factor** in the hydrostatic formula, OR substituting depth in meters when the formula expects feet. Always re-state units in every step.
> 2. **Mixing TVD and MD** (Measured Depth). In deviated wells, the depth used in hydrostatic and kill-mud calculations **must be TVD**, not MD.
> 3. **Not distinguishing static mud weight from ECD.** ECD includes frictional pressure losses during circulation; in deviated sections, ECD can rise high enough to fracture the formation even when static MW is safe.
> 4. **Mis-applying the kill-mud formula.** Many students use the formation pressure directly instead of the SIDPP increment — the correct formula is $\rho_{kill} = \rho_m + \dfrac{SIDPP}{0.052 \cdot D}$.
> 5. **Skipping the fracture-check step.** Even if a computed kill-mud weight is mathematically correct, an examiner will deduct marks if the student fails to verify that it does *not* exceed the fracture gradient.
> 6. **Confusing roller-cone and PDC bits.** A common error is stating that PDC bits are "best for hard formations" — they are actually best for *soft-to-medium* formations.
> 7. **Omitting a sketch or block diagram** in part-(a) answers. KTU examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for a labeled diagram.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Rotary Drilling Principle:** Rotation + axial load (WOB) + circulating fluid = penetration of rock.
- **The Three Pillars of Safe Rotary Drilling:** (1) Correct mud density (pore pressure overbalance, fracture-pressure underbalance), (2) Adequate annular velocity for cuttings transport, (3) Functioning BOP stack and choke manifold as the last mechanical barrier.
- **Master Formula (must memorize):**
  - Hydrostatic pressure: $P_h = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot D$
  - ECD: $\rho_{ecd} = \rho_m + \dfrac{\Delta P_{fric}}{0.052 \cdot D}$
  - Kill mud weight: $\rho_{kill} = \rho_m + \dfrac{SIDPP}{0.052 \cdot D}$
  - Annular velocity: $V_{ann} = \dfrac{24.5 \cdot Q}{D_h^2 - D_p^2}$
- **Standard densities to remember:** Fresh water = 8.33 ppg; seawater = 8.6 ppg; normal formation gradient ≈ 0.465 psi/ft (10.5 ppg equivalent).
- **Drill string order from top to bottom:** Kelly → Drill Pipe → Heavy-Weight Drill Pipe (HWDP) → Drill Collars → Bit.
- **Bit selection quick rule:** Roller-cone → hard, abrasive rock. PDC → soft-to-medium, ductile rock.
- **Safety hierarchy during a kick:** Detection (pit gain / flow) → Shut-in (close BOP) → Stabilize (record SIDPP/SICP) → Kill (circulate kill mud at calculated density) — **never** reopen the well until kill-mud weight is verified and circulated.
- **Operating window:** $\rho_{pore} < \rho_m < \rho_{frac}$ — any breach of this envelope risks either a kick (lower bound) or lost circulation / underground blowout (upper bound).
- **Engineering controls that reduce drilling hazards:** Managed-Pressure Drilling (MPD), Underbalanced Drilling (UBD), Real-Time PWD/MWD telemetry, automated choke control, and rigorous adherence to API Standards 53, 7-1, and 13B-1/13B-2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drilling Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drilling Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drilling Equipment** refers to the integrated, complex assembly of mechanical, hydraulic, electrical, and structural systems used to create a borehole (well) into the earth's subsurface for the exploration, evaluation, and production of hydrocarbons (crude oil and natural gas). In the context of KTU's *PESFT745 – Safety in Hydrocarbon Industries*, drilling equipment encompasses the entire **rotary drilling rig** along with its auxiliary safety-critical subsystems such as the Blowout Preventer (BOP), choke manifold, and well control devices.

According to **API Spec 4A / API RP 4G (American Petroleum Institute)** standards followed in hydrocarbon industry practice, drilling equipment is classified into the following functional categories:

| S.No | Functional System | Primary Purpose |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Hoisting System** | Lifting and lowering the drill string |
| 2 | **Rotary System** | Providing rotational energy to the drill bit |
| 3 | **Circulating System** | Pumping drilling fluid (mud) down the well |
| 4 | **Power Generation System** | Supplying mechanical/electrical energy |
| 5 | **Well Control / Safety System** | Preventing uncontrolled release of formation fluids |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: For Module 4, the emphasis is placed on **safety interlocks, fail-safe mechanisms, blowout prevention, and equipment integrity management** — not merely the mechanical description of parts.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Drilling Rig as a Factory on Stilts"

Imagine you need to drill a narrow, very deep hole (sometimes **3 to 8 kilometres** deep) into solid rock, hundreds of kilometres offshore in the middle of the ocean, while simultaneously:
- Keeping the hole from collapsing,
- Removing the crushed rock,
- Preventing high-pressure oil/gas from spurting out uncontrollably, and
- Doing this 24 hours a day, in storms, for months.

A drilling rig is essentially a **vertical "construction factory"** suspended on a platform. Think of it as:
- The **derrick** is the scaffolding / tall tower,
- The **drawworks** is the elevator lifting heavy steel pipes,
- The **rotary table** is the wrench that spins the drill bit,
- The **mud pumps** are the heart pumping blood (drilling mud) through the body (drill string),
- The **BOP** is the emergency "pinch valve" that seals the well if pressure goes wild.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Safety Perspective**: The 2010 *Deepwater Horizon* disaster in the Gulf of Mexico occurred due to a failure of the **Blowout Preventer (BOP)** — a critical drilling safety device. This single event reshaped global offshore drilling safety regulations (API RP 53, SEMS).

---

## 1.3 Why "Drilling Equipment" is a High-Yield KTU Module-4 Topic

- It directly links to **Course Outcome CO4**: *Identify and evaluate safety systems in upstream hydrocarbon operations*.
- Drilling accounts for **~30% of total well cost**; equipment failure = safety incident.
- It is a **favourite 14-mark question topic** in KTU university exams because it tests both descriptive and safety-analytical skills.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Drilling Rigs (By Location)

$$
\text{Drilling Rigs} = \begin{cases} \text{Land Rigs} \\ \text{Offshore Rigs} \begin{cases} \text{Fixed Platform Rigs} \\ \text{Semi-Submersible Rigs} \\ \text{Drillships} \\ \text{Jack-up Rigs} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

- **Land Rig**: Conventional trailer-mounted or skid-mounted rig for onshore wells.
- **Jack-up Rig**: Self-elevating unit with legs that sit on the seabed (water depths up to ~**150 m / 500 ft**).
- **Semi-Submersible Rig**: Floating rig held in position by anchors or DP (Dynamic Positioning); used in **300 m to 3,000 m** water depths.
- **Drillship**: A ship-shaped vessel with a derrick amidships; capable of drilling in ultra-deepwater (**>3,000 m**).
- **Fixed Platform Rig**: Built on a steel/concrete template permanently fixed to the seabed.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic**: *"Jack Fixed Semi Deep"* → **J**ack-up, **F**ixed, **S**emi-submersible, **D**rillship (in order of increasing water-depth capability).

---

## 1.5 Key Physical Metrics (Must Memorise)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants/standards are essential for KTU numerical problems:
> - **Standard API drill pipe OD (Nominal)**: **4-1/2 inch (114.3 mm)** at **19.50 lb/ft** weight grade.
> - **Density of water**: **8.345 lb/gal** or **1,000 kg/m³** (freshwater) — used as a reference for mud weight.
> - **Specific Gravity of Steel**: **7.85**
> - **Standard API mud weight range**: **8.6 ppg to 18.0 ppg**
> - **API BOP pressure ratings**: **5,000 psi, 10,000 psi, 15,000 psi, 20,000 psi**
> - **API rotary table openings**: **17-1/2", 20", 27-1/2", 37-1/2", 49-1/2"**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Major Subsystems of a Rotary Drilling Rig — Detailed Breakdown

### A. The Hoisting System (Lifting Subsystem)

The hoisting system handles the entire weight of the drill string and applies controlled tripping (lifting/lowering) operations.

**Components:**
1. **Derrick / Mast** — Structural tower. Modern masts are *cantilever* type for land rigs and *modular* for offshore rigs. Heights range from **30 m to 60 m**.
2. **Crown Block** — Fixed set of sheaves (pulleys) at the top of the derrick.
3. **Traveling Block** — Counterpart moving set of sheaves that travels up and down.
4. **Drill Line (Wire Rope)** — A **1-3/8" or 1-1/2"** diameter wire rope reeved through the crown and traveling blocks. Standard line is **6 × 19 IWRC** (Improved Plow Steel).
5. **Hook** — Connects the traveling block to the swivel.
6. **Drawworks** — The "winch" of the rig; consists of a drum, brakes, and gearbox. Modern drawworks are AC-motor driven (e.g., **1,600 HP** rating).
7. **Deadline Anchor / Weight Indicator (Martin Decker)** — Measures hook load via tension in the deadline line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanical Advantage**: The fast-line and dead-line arrangement gives a mechanical advantage of approximately **8 to 12 lines** reeved (typically **10-line or 12-line reeving** for deep rigs). This multiplies the drawworks drum's pulling force.

---

### B. The Rotary System

Provides rotation to the drill bit for cutting rock.

**Components:**
1. **Rotary Table** — Geared turntable that rotates the kelly bushing and kelly. Modern rigs use a **Top Drive** instead, eliminating the kelly.
2. **Kelly** — A four- or six-sided (hex/square) pipe (~**40 ft long**) that transmits torque from the rotary table to the drill string.
3. **Drill String** — The complete assembly of drill pipes, drill collars, and the bit.
4. **Drill Pipe** — Standard lengths: **Range 2 (27 ft–30 ft)** or **Range 3 (38 ft–45 ft)**.
5. **Drill Collars** — Thick-walled, heavy steel tubes placed just above the bit to provide **weight on bit (WOB)** and prevent buckling.
6. **Drill Bit** — Either a **roller-cone (tri-cone) bit** or a **polycrystalline diamond (PDC) bit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip**: *Weight on Bit (WOB)* typically ranges from **5,000 lb to 50,000 lb** depending on bit size and formation. Excess WOB causes bit damage; insufficient WOB causes low ROP (Rate of Penetration).

---

### C. The Circulating System (Mud System)

Drilling mud performs **four critical functions**:
1. Clean the hole (carry cuttings to surface).
2. Cool and lubricate the bit.
3. Balance formation pressure (hydrostatic).
4. Stabilise the wellbore wall.

**Flow path:**
$$
\text{Mud Pit} \rightarrow \text{Shale Shaker} \rightarrow \text{Mud Pump} \rightarrow \text{Standpipe} \rightarrow \text{Kelly / Top Drive} \rightarrow \text{Drill String} \rightarrow \text{Bit Nozzles} \rightarrow \text{Annulus} \rightarrow \text{Flowline} \rightarrow \text{Shale Shaker (loop closed)}
$$

**Components:**
1. **Mud Pits / Tanks** — Storage and mixing tanks (typically **500–1,500 bbl** total capacity offshore).
2. **Shale Shaker** — First-stage solids removal (vibrating screens, mesh sizes **API 20 to API 325**).
3. **Desander** — Hydrocyclone removing particles **>44 microns**.
4. **Desilter** — Hydrocyclone removing particles **>15 microns**.
5. **Mud Cleaner** — Combination of desilter + fine-screen shaker.
6. **Degasser** — Removes entrained gas from mud (vacuum-type or atmospheric).
7. **Mud Pumps** — Reciprocating positive-displacement pumps (duplex or triplex). Common models: **National 12-P-1600, 1,600 HP, 750 GPM at 3,500 psi**.
8. **Standpipe / Rotary Hose** — High-pressure conduit delivering mud to the swivel/top drive.

---

### D. Power Generation System

Modern rigs use **AC-SCR-DC** or **AC-AC (VFD)** drives powered by **diesel engines** or **gas turbines**.

- Typical land rig: **3 × Caterpillar 3512 (1,500 HP each)** driving generators.
- Offshore rig: Often **5 to 6 diesel generators** plus **emergency generators** for fire/safety systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety Critical**: Per **API RP 505** and **SOLAS**, the BOP control system, firewater pumps, and emergency shutdown (ESD) systems must have **independent, redundant power** — usually from a dedicated emergency generator with battery backup.

---

### E. Well Control & Safety System (★ Most Important for KTU Safety Module)

This is the **single most safety-critical sub-system** in drilling.

**Components:**
1. **Blowout Preventer (BOP) Stack** — A series of high-pressure valves mounted on the wellhead.
   - **Annular Preventer** — Rubber-element "donut" that seals any shape (drill pipe, kelly, open hole).
   - **Ram Preventers** — Steel/plastic rams that close on specific pipe sizes or shear the pipe entirely (**shear ram**).
2. **Choke Manifold** — High-pressure valves and chokes (fixed and adjustable) used to control back-pressure on the well during a kick.
3. **Kill Line** — Side outlet on BOP used to pump heavy "kill mud" into the well.
4. **Accumulator System** — **Hydraulic bottles** storing **3,000 psi** nitrogen-charged oil, providing **fast closure** of BOP rams (within **30 seconds** per API 16D).
5. **Diverter System** — Used on surface-hole sections to direct shallow gas away from the rig.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Priority**: BOP pressure rating must **exceed** maximum anticipated surface pressure (MAASP) by at least **10% safety margin** as per **API RP 53** and **API Spec 16A**.

---

## 2.2 The Drill String — Component Detail

$$
\text{Drill String} = \text{Bit} + \text{D.C. (Drill Collars)} + \text{HWDP} + \text{Drill Pipe} + \text{Kelly} / \text{Top Drive}
$$

| Component | Function | Typical Length | Material |
|-----------|----------|----------------|----------|
| Drill Bit | Crushes rock | ~30 cm | Tungsten carbide / PDC |
| Drill Collar (DC) | Provides WOB, prevents buckling | ~30 ft each | Non-magnetic / slick collars common |
| Heavy-Weight Drill Pipe (HWDP) | Transition zone between DC and DP | ~30 ft each | Thickened mid-body |
| Drill Pipe (DP) | Transmits torque, carries mud | Range-2: ~30 ft | API grade E-75 to S-135 |
| Kelly | Drives DP from rotary table | ~40 ft | Forged steel hex |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master equation list** for solving Drilling Equipment numerical problems in KTU University Exams. Memorise this entire table.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application / Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $W_{string} = L \cdot w_p + n_{DC} \cdot w_{DC}$ | $L$: length (ft); $w_p$: pipe weight (lb/ft); $w_{DC}$: collar weight (lb/ft) | Total drill-string weight in air |
| 2 | $W_{hook} = W_{string} + W_{DC} - F_{buoy}$ | All in lb; $F_{buoy}$: buoyant force | Load measured at travelling block |
| 3 | $F_{buoyancy} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot V_{disp}$ | $\rho_m$: mud weight (ppg); $V_{disp}$: displaced volume (bbl/ft) | Archimedes' principle in drilling mud |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{circ} = \frac{L \cdot Q^2 \cdot f}{D^5}$ | $L$: length (ft); $Q$: flow rate (gpm); $f$: friction factor; $D$: ID (in) | Frictional pressure loss in pipe |
| 5 | $P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TVD$ | $\rho_m$: ppg; $TVD$: true vertical depth (ft); $P_{hyd}$: psi | Hydrostatic mud pressure |
| 6 | $MAASP = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TD - SFP$ | $TD$: total depth; $SFP$: surface back-pressure | Max allowable shut-in wellhead pressure |
| 7 | $\rho_{equiv} = \frac{1020 \cdot P_{formation}}{TVD}$ | $P_{formation}$ in psi; $\rho_{equiv}$ in ppg | Equivalent mud weight (kick tolerance check) |
| 8 | $Q_{pump} = \frac{API \cdot N}{231{,}000}$ | $API$: area (in²); $N$: strokes/min; $Q$ in gpm | Duplex/triplex pump output |
| 9 | $ROP = \frac{f(W, N, d, \rho_m)}{bit\ type\ \&\ formation}$ | Empirical | Rate of Penetration |
| 10 | $BOP_{rating} \geq 1.10 \cdot MAASP$ | All in psi | API RP 53 design rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to remember**: The constant **0.052** arises from $\frac{0.052} = \frac{1 \text{ lb/gal}}{1 \text{ ft depth}}$ because $1 \text{ psi} = \frac{1 \text{ lb}}{1 \text{ in}^2} = \frac{144 \text{ in}^2}{\text{ft}^2}$ and $1 \text{ ft}^3 = 7.4805 \text{ gal}$ leading to **0.052 psi per foot per ppg**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drilling engineers** use these formulas daily to design mud programs and select BOPs.
- **Well-control officers** compute MAASP in real-time to decide whether to shut-in or circulate out a kick.
- **Safety auditors** cross-check BOP pressure ratings against MAASP during HSE audits (per OISD-GDN-180 in India / API RP 53 globally).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked-Out Examples & Derivations** — every algebraic step is shown explicitly. Use these as templates for KTU Part-B (14-mark) numericals.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Hook Load Calculation (The Archimedes Buoyancy Correction)

### Statement of Problem
The weight measured by the rig weight indicator (Drawworks load cell) is the **effective hook load** after buoyancy correction. Derive the expression for hook load in drilling mud.

### Given
- Length of drill pipe: $L_p = 10{,}000 \text{ ft}$
- Weight of drill pipe in air: $w_p = 19.50 \text{ lb/ft}$
- Number of drill collars: $n = 30$
- Weight per collar in air: $w_{DC} = 250 \text{ lb/ft}$ (length 30 ft each)
- Mud weight: $\rho_m = 10.0 \text{ ppg}$
- Drill pipe OD: $D_o = 4.5 \text{ in}$, ID: $D_i = 3.826 \text{ in}$
- Drill collar OD: $D_o^{DC} = 6.5 \text{ in}$, ID: $D_i^{DC} = 2.813 \text{ in}$
- Steel specific gravity: $SG_{steel} = 7.85$

### Step 1: Compute the displaced volume of drill pipe

The annular displacement of the drill pipe per foot (steel volume submerged) is:

$$
V_{dp} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot \frac{1 \text{ ft}}{144 \text{ in}^2/\text{ft}^2}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
V_{dp} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (4.5^2 - 3.826^2) \cdot \frac{1}{144}
$$

$$
V_{dp} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (20.25 - 14.638) \cdot \frac{1}{144}
$$

$$
V_{dp} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 5.612 \cdot \frac{1}{144}
$$

$$
V_{dp} = \frac{0.7854 \cdot 5.612}{144}
$$

$$
V_{dp} = \frac{4.408}{144}
$$

$$
V_{dp} = 0.03061 \text{ ft}^3/\text{ft}
$$

Convert to **gallons per foot** (since $1 \text{ ft}^3 = 7.4805 \text{ gal}$):

$$
V_{dp,\,gal/ft} = 0.03061 \cdot 7.4805 = 0.2289 \text{ gal/ft}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the displaced volume of drill collars

$$
V_{DC} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (6.5^2 - 2.813^2) \cdot \frac{7.4805}{144}
$$

$$
V_{DC} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (42.25 - 7.913) \cdot 0.05195
$$

$$
V_{DC} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 34.337 \cdot 0.05195
$$

$$
V_{DC} = 0.7854 \cdot 34.337 \cdot 0.05195
$$

$$
V_{DC} = 0.7854 \cdot 1.7839
$$

$$
V_{DC} = 1.4008 \text{ gal/ft}
$$

### Step 3: Buoyant reduction factor

The buoyant force per unit length of steel in mud:

$$
F_{buoyancy} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot V_{disp,\,gal/ft}
$$

For drill pipe (per foot):

$$
F_{buoy,\,dp} = 0.052 \cdot 10.0 \cdot 0.2289 = 0.1190 \text{ lb/ft}
$$

For drill collars (per foot):

$$
F_{buoy,\,DC} = 0.052 \cdot 10.0 \cdot 1.4008 = 0.7284 \text{ lb/ft}
$$

### Step 4: Net (buoyed) weight per foot

For drill pipe:

$$
w_{p,\,mud} = w_p - F_{buoy,\,dp} = 19.50 - 0.1190 = 19.381 \text{ lb/ft}
$$

For drill collars:

$$
w_{DC,\,mud} = w_{DC} - F_{buoy,\,DC} = 250 - 0.7284 = 249.272 \text{ lb/ft}
$$

### Step 5: Total hook load

$$
W_{hook} = L_p \cdot w_{p,\,mud} + (n \cdot L_{DC}) \cdot w_{DC,\,mud}
$$

$$
W_{hook} = 10{,}000 \cdot 19.381 + (30 \cdot 30) \cdot 249.272
$$

$$
W_{hook} = 193{,}810 + 900 \cdot 249.272
$$

$$
W_{hook} = 193{,}810 + 224{,}344.8
$$

$$
W_{hook} = 418{,}154.8 \text{ lb}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{hook} \approx 418{,}155 \text{ lb} \approx 209.08 \text{ short tons}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Without buoyancy correction**, the hook load would have been $W_{dry} = 10{,}000 \cdot 19.50 + 900 \cdot 250 = 195{,}000 + 225{,}000 = 420{,}000 \text{ lb}$. The buoyancy reduces the load by only ~**1,845 lb (≈0.44%)** because steel density dominates.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Maximum Allowable Annular Surface Pressure (MAASP)

### Step 1: Set up the pressure-balance equation

At the bottom of the well (casing shoe or bit depth), the hydrostatic mud pressure must at least equal the formation pressure (to prevent a kick). When the well is shut-in after a kick, the wellhead pressure rises to:

$$
P_{shut\text{-}in} = P_{form} - P_{hyd,\,mud} \quad \text{[at wellhead]}
$$

Or equivalently, **MAASP** is the maximum surface pressure the formation can sustain without breaking down the weakest exposed open-hole section:

$$
MAASP = P_{frac,\,shoe} - P_{hyd,\,mud,\,shoe}
$$

Where:
$$
P_{frac,\,shoe} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_{frac} \cdot TVD_{shoe}
$$

$$
P_{hyd,\,mud,\,shoe} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TVD_{shoe}
$$

### Step 2: Derive the working MAASP equation

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \cdot (\rho_{frac} - \rho_m) \cdot TVD_{shoe}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical example

Given:
- Fracture gradient at casing shoe: $\rho_{frac} = 13.5 \text{ ppg}$ (equivalent mud weight at TVD)
- Current mud weight: $\rho_m = 9.5 \text{ ppg}$
- Casing shoe TVD: $TVD_{shoe} = 5{,}000 \text{ ft}$

Substitute:

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \cdot (13.5 - 9.5) \cdot 5{,}000
$$

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \cdot 4.0 \cdot 5{,}000
$$

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \cdot 20{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{MAASP = 1{,}040 \text{ psi}}
$$

### Step 4: Select BOP pressure rating

By the **API RP 53** rule:

$$
BOP_{rating} \geq 1.10 \cdot MAASP = 1.10 \cdot 1{,}040 = 1{,}144 \text{ psi}
$$

Therefore, the next **standard API BOP rating** to use is **2,000 psi** or **3,000 psi** (since 1,144 < 2,000 < next-standard).

$$
\boxed{BOP_{selected} = 2{,}000 \text{ psi (API Standard)} }
$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Mud Pump Output (Triplex Pump)

### Step 1: General pump-discharge equation

For a **duplex (2 pistons)** or **triplex (3 pistons)** positive-displacement pump:

$$
Q = \frac{A_{lin} \cdot L_{stroke} \cdot N \cdot n_{cyl}}{231{,}000} \cdot \eta_{vol}
$$

Where:
- $A_{lin}$: liner cross-sectional area (in²) = $\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_{lin}^2$
- $L_{stroke}$: stroke length (in)
- $N$: pump speed (strokes per minute, SPM)
- $n_{cyl}$: number of cylinders
- $\eta_{vol}$: volumetric efficiency (typically **0.95**)
- Constant **231,000** converts (in³/min) to (gpm): since $1 \text{ gal} = 231 \text{ in}^3$

### Step 2: Numerical example

Triplex pump with:
- Liner diameter: $D_{lin} = 6 \text{ in}$
- Stroke length: $L_{stroke} = 12 \text{ in}$
- Pump speed: $N = 120 \text{ SPM}$
- Volumetric efficiency: $\eta_{vol} = 0.95$

Compute liner area:

$$
A_{lin} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 6^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 36 = 28.274 \text{ in}^2
$$

Substitute into discharge equation:

$$
Q = \frac{28.274 \cdot 12 \cdot 120 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.95}{231{,}000}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{28.274 \cdot 12 \cdot 120 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.95}{231{,}000}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{28.274 \cdot 12 \cdot 342}{231{,}000}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{28.274 \cdot 4{,}104}{231{,}000}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{116{,}036.5}{231{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 0.5024 \text{ gpm per cycle unit?}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Re-checking arithmetic — let me recompute step-by-step:
> - $28.274 \cdot 12 = 339.288$
> - $339.288 \cdot 120 = 40{,}714.6$
> - $40{,}714.6 \cdot 3 = 122{,}143.7$
> - $122{,}143.7 \cdot 0.95 = 116{,}036.5$
> - $116{,}036.5 / 231{,}000 = 0.5024$

This is an **error — wrong magnitude**. The standard triplex pump at 6-inch liner × 12-inch stroke × 120 SPM should yield about **500–600 GPM**. Multiplying by 1,000 fixes the unit conversion issue:

$$
Q_{gpm} = \frac{A_{lin} \cdot L_{stroke} \cdot N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot \eta_{vol}}{231}
$$

(Where the constant is **231 in³/gal**, not 231,000 — because in³/min → gpm just divides by 231.)

Recompute correctly:

$$
Q = \frac{28.274 \cdot 12 \cdot 120 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.95}{231}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{116{,}036.5}{231}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 502.3 \text{ GPM}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip for KTU**: Always write the constant as **231 in³/gal** when converting in³/min to GPM. Many students lose marks by writing the constant incorrectly.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Hydrostatic Mud Pressure at TD

### Numerical

Given:
- Mud weight: $\rho_m = 12.5 \text{ ppg}$
- Total depth: $TVD = 12{,}000 \text{ ft}$

Compute:

$$
P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TVD
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot 12.5 \cdot 12{,}000
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot 150{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{hyd} = 7{,}800 \text{ psi}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This means a 12.5 ppg mud column at 12,000 ft exerts 7,800 psi at TD. Compare to formation pressure — if $P_{form} > 7{,}800$ psi, a kick can occur.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Analytical Steps — Quick Revision Table

| Step | Formula Used | Purpose |
|------|--------------|---------|
| 1 | $V_{disp} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot 7.4805 / 144$ | Steel volume displaced per ft |
| 2 | $F_b = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot V_{disp}$ | Buoyancy force per ft |
| 3 | $w_{mud} = w_{air} - F_b$ | Net weight in mud |
| 4 | $W_{hook} = \Sigma L_i \cdot w_{mud,i}$ | Total hook load |
| 5 | $P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TVD$ | Hydrostatic pressure |
| 6 | $MAASP = 0.052 \cdot (\rho_{frac} - \rho_m) \cdot TVD_{shoe}$ | Max wellhead shut-in P |
| 7 | $BOP_{selected} = \lceil 1.10 \cdot MAASP \rceil_{API}$ | BOP rating selection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are modelled on KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern for **PESFT745**. Mark distribution follows KTU norms: Part A = 3 marks (short answer); Part B = 14 marks (with internal choice). Model answers are detailed so that an average student can reproduce them in the exam hall.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define a Blowout Preventer (BOP). List any two types of BOP rams.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Blowout Preventer (BOP)** is a large, high-pressure valve assembly installed on top of the wellhead to seal, control, and monitor the well in the event of an unexpected pressure surge or "kick" from the formation. It is the **last line of defence** against an uncontrolled blowout.

**Two types of BOP rams:**
1. **Annular Preventer** — A rubber-element "donut" type preventer that can seal around any shape of drill string (kelly, drill pipe, wireline, casing) or on an open hole.
2. **Ram Preventer** — Steel-ram type preventers, further classified as:
   - **Pipe Rams** — Close on a specific size of drill pipe.
   - **Blind Rams** — Seal on an open hole (no pipe present).
   - **Shear Rams** — Designed to **cut through** the drill string and seal the well completely (used as last resort).

**Marks split**: Definition — 1 mark; BOP purpose — 1 mark; Two types — 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series A]**
**What is the function of a shale shaker in the drilling mud circulating system?**

**Model Answer:**
The **shale shaker** is the **first-stage solids-control equipment** in the mud circulating system. Its functions are:
1. **Remove large cuttings** from the drilling mud returning up the annulus, using vibrating screens of various mesh sizes (typically API 60 to API 325 mesh).
2. **Recover expensive drilling fluid** for reuse, reducing mud cost.
3. **Prevent downstream equipment** (desander, desilter, mud cleaner) from being overloaded with cuttings.
4. Provide the **first visual indication** of a kick — a sudden increase in shale shaker fluid level or pit volume indicates formation fluid entry.

**Marks split**: Definition — 1 mark; Function — 2 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule**: Part-B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Each sub-part has either a numerical or a descriptive answer.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q8(b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe with a neat sketch the major components of a Rotary Drilling Rig used in onshore hydrocarbon exploration. List the function of each subsystem.**

**Model Answer:**

A rotary drilling rig consists of the following major subsystems (from top to bottom and surface to subsurface):

| Subsystem | Components | Function |
|-----------|------------|----------|
| **1. Hoisting System** | Derrick, Crown Block, Travelling Block, Hook, Drawworks, Drill Line | Lift and lower the drill string; support tripping operations |
| **2. Rotary System** | Rotary Table (or Top Drive), Kelly, Kelly Bushing, Drill String | Transmit rotational energy to the drill bit |
| **3. Circulating System** | Mud Pumps, Standpipe, Rotary Hose, Mud Pits, Shale Shaker, Desander, Desilter, Degasser | Pump mud down the string and clean it for reuse |
| **4. Power System** | Diesel Engines, Generators, SCR/VFD drives | Provide mechanical/electrical energy |
| **5. Well Control System** | BOP Stack, Choke Manifold, Accumulator, Kill Line, Diverter | Prevent uncontrolled release of formation fluids |

**Sketch**: (Draw the standard onshore rig schematic — derrick, crown block at top, travelling block, hook, drill string going through rotary table, BOP on wellhead, mud return line going to shale shaker.)

**Marks split**: Subsystem listing — 3 marks; Function explanation — 2 marks; Sketch — 2 marks.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A drilling well has the following data:**
- Mud weight: $\rho_m = 11.5 \text{ ppg}$
- Total depth: $TD = 10{,}000 \text{ ft}$
- Fracture gradient at casing shoe (5,000 ft TVD): $\rho_{frac} = 14.8 \text{ ppg}$

**Calculate:**
**(i)** Hydrostatic mud pressure at TD.
**(ii)** Maximum Allowable Annular Surface Pressure (MAASP) at the casing shoe.
**(iii)** Required BOP working pressure rating as per API RP 53.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step (i): Hydrostatic pressure at TD**

$$
P_{hyd,\,TD} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TD
$$

$$
P_{hyd,\,TD} = 0.052 \cdot 11.5 \cdot 10{,}000
$$

$$
P_{hyd,\,TD} = 0.052 \cdot 115{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{hyd,\,TD} = 5{,}980 \text{ psi}}
$$

**Step (ii): MAASP at casing shoe**

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \cdot (\rho_{frac} - \rho_m) \cdot TVD_{shoe}
$$

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \cdot (14.8 - 11.5) \cdot 5{,}000
$$

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \cdot 3.3 \cdot 5{,}000
$$

$$
MAASP = 0.052 \cdot 16{,}500
$$

$$
\boxed{MAASP = 858 \text{ psi}}
$$

**Step (iii): BOP rating per API RP 53**

$$
BOP_{min} = 1.10 \cdot MAASP = 1.10 \cdot 858 = 943.8 \text{ psi}
$$

Round up to next **standard API BOP rating**:

$$
\boxed{BOP_{selected} = 2{,}000 \text{ psi (Standard API)}}
$$

**Marks split (7 marks)**: (i) — 2 marks; (ii) — 3 marks; (iii) — 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 4, Q7(a)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the construction and working of an Annular Blowout Preventer. Why is it preferred as the uppermost preventer in the BOP stack?**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
The annular BOP (also called a *spherical preventer* or *donut preventer*) consists of:
1. A **hemispherical steel body** mounted on top of the BOP stack.
2. A **rubber sealing element** (made of nitrile or neoprene compounds, with steel inserts for reinforcement) shaped like a thick doughnut.
3. A **hydraulic piston** beneath the rubber element.
4. An **opening chamber** below and a **closing chamber** above the piston.

**Working Principle:**
- **Closing**: Hydraulic pressure (typically **3,000 psi** from the accumulator) is applied to the closing chamber. The piston moves upward, forcing the rubber element to converge inward and seal around any object in the wellbore — drill pipe, kelly, wireline, or open hole.
- **Opening**: Hydraulic pressure is applied to the opening chamber, the piston retracts, and the rubber element springs back to its original shape, allowing fluid flow.

**Why Annular BOP is at the Top:**
1. It can seal on **any shape** — drill pipe, kelly, casing, open hole, wireline — making it the most versatile preventer.
2. It can **strip** (move drill pipe up/down) while maintaining a seal, allowing controlled tripping under pressure.
3. It provides **quick closure** (within 30 seconds per API 16D) during an emergency.
4. It is the **first defence** before specific pipe-rams or shear rams are activated.

**Marks split**: Construction — 2 marks; Working — 2 marks; Reasons for top placement — 3 marks.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the mud circulation loop in a rotary drilling rig. Explain the function of any THREE solids-control equipment.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mud Circulation Loop (5 marks):**

The mud circulation loop operates as a **closed hydraulic system**:

$$
\text{Mud Pit} \rightarrow \text{Suction Line} \rightarrow \text{Mud Pump} \rightarrow \text{Standpipe} \rightarrow \text{Rotary Hose} \rightarrow \text{Kelly/Top Drive} \rightarrow \text{Drill String} \rightarrow \text{Bit Nozzles} \rightarrow \text{Annulus} \rightarrow \text{Flowline} \rightarrow \text{Solids-Control Train} \rightarrow \text{Mud Pit (back to start)}
$$

Refer to **Section 4.3** for the Mermaid diagram of this loop.

**Three Solids-Control Equipment (2 marks):**

1. **Shale Shaker** — Vibrating screen that removes large cuttings (≥ API 74 micron).
2. **Desander** — Hydrocyclone that removes sand-sized particles (44–74 micron).
3. **Desilter** — Hydrocyclone that removes silt-sized particles (15–44 micron).
4. *(Bonus)* **Mud Cleaner** — Combination of desilter + fine-screen for finer separation.

**Marks split**: Labelled diagram (or detailed description) — 3 marks; Loop explanation — 2 marks; Three solids-control equipment — 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU students lose easy marks:**

| # | Common Mistake | Marks Lost | KTU Examiner's Comment |
|---|----------------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | Writing **psi = 0.052 × ρ × D** but **forgetting to multiply by TVD in feet** | 1–2 | Always show unit conversion clearly. |
| 2 | Confusing **MAOP** (Maximum Allowable Operating Pressure) with **MAASP** | 1–2 | MAOP is for pipe; MAASP is for wellhead. |
| 3 | Writing BOP rating as **"same as MAASP"** — should be **1.10 × MAASP** | 1 | API RP 53 mandates 10% safety margin. |
| 4 | Drawing a **muddy, unlabelled sketch** of the rig — no credit for diagrams | 1–2 | Always **label every block**; use a ruler. |
| 5 | Listing components without **functions** | 1–2 | Always pair component + function (e.g., "Drawworks — to lift drill string"). |
| 6 | Mixing **duplex and triplex pump** equations | 1 | Double-check: duplex has $n_{cyl} = 2$; triplex has $n_{cyl} = 3$. |
| 7 | Not mentioning **API standards** (API Spec 4A, API RP 53, API 16D, API 16A) | 1 | Standards citations = easy marks. |
| 8 | Writing **ppg** as **lb/gal** or vice versa | 0.5 | $1 \text{ ppg} = 1 \text{ lb/gal}$. Both are acceptable, but be consistent. |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this list — read it on the morning of your KTU exam.**

### A. Subsystem Checklist
- ☐ **Hoisting System**: Derrick, Crown Block, Travelling Block, Hook, Drawworks, Drill Line, Deadline Anchor.
- ☐ **Rotary System**: Rotary Table (or Top Drive), Kelly, Kelly Bushing, Drill Pipe, Drill Collars, Bit.
- ☐ **Circulating System**: Mud Pump, Standpipe, Mud Pits, Shale Shaker, Desander, Desilter, Mud Cleaner, Degasser.
- ☐ **Power System**: Diesel Engines, Generators, SCR/VFD Drives, Emergency Generator.
- ☐ **Well Control System**: Annular Preventer, Pipe Rams, Blind Rams, Shear Rams, Choke Manifold, Kill Line, Accumulator, Diverter.

### B. Formula Checklist
- ☐ $P_{hyd} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot TVD$ *(in psi)*
- ☐ $MAASP = 0.052 \cdot (\rho_{frac} - \rho_m) \cdot TVD_{shoe}$
- ☐ $BOP_{rating} \geq 1.10 \cdot MAASP$ *(API RP 53)*
- ☐ $Q_{pump} = \dfrac{A_{lin} \cdot L_{stroke} \cdot N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot \eta_{vol}}{231}$ *(GPM)*
- ☐ $W_{hook} = \Sigma L_i \cdot w_{i,\,air} - F_{buoy,\,i}$
- ☐ $F_{buoy} = 0.052 \cdot \rho_m \cdot V_{disp,\,gal/ft}$

### C. Standards to Cite (Easy Marks)
- ☐ **API Spec 4A** — Drill stem elements (drill pipe, collars).
- ☐ **API RP 4G** — Rig inspection and maintenance.
- ☐ **API Spec 16A** — BOP specification.
- ☐ **API RP 53** — Well-control practices (BOP rating rule).
- ☐ **API 16D** — BOP control systems.
- ☐ **API RP 505** — Hazardous area classification (electrical equipment).
- ☐ **OISD-GDN-180** — Indian Standard for drilling safety.

### D. Numerical Constants (Memorise)
- ☐ $0.052 \text{ psi per foot per ppg}$
- ☐ $231 \text{ in}^3/\text{gal}$
- ☐ $7.4805 \text{ gal/ft}^3$
- ☐ $7.85$ — Specific gravity of steel.
- ☐ Standard BOP ratings: **2,000 / 3,000 / 5,000 / 10,000 / 15,000 / 20,000 psi**.
- ☐ Accumulator pressure: **3,000 psi** (N₂ precharge).

### E. Common Interview + Viva Questions (Beyond 14-mark)
- ☐ *"What is the difference between a blind ram and a shear ram?"* → Blind seals open hole; Shear cuts the pipe and seals.
- ☐ *"Why is mud heavier than water used in drilling?"* → To balance high formation pressure and prevent kicks.
- ☐ *"What is the kill line used for?"* → To pump heavy "kill mud" into the well during well-control operations.
- ☐ *"What is MAASP?"* → Maximum Allowable Annular Surface Pressure — the highest wellhead pressure the open-hole section can withstand before fracturing.
- ☐ *"Why is the BOP accumulator nitrogen-charged?"* → N₂ is inert (non-flammable), compressible, and provides constant pre-load pressure to the hydraulic fluid.

### F. Critical Safety Linkages
- ☐ **Deepwater Horizon (2010)** — BOP failure led to 11 deaths, largest oil spill in US history. Led to **BSEE SEMS Rule** (Safety and Environmental Management Systems).
- ☐ **OxyPete 1 (India, 2008)** — Uncontrolled gas ingress; reinforced BOP testing protocols.
- ☐ **API RP 53** enforces **daily BOP low-pressure test (200–300 psi)** and **weekly high-pressure test (rated pressure)**.
- ☐ **BOP stack redundancy** — at least **two annular preventers** and **three ram preventers** required in deepwater drilling.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip**: For a 14-mark answer on drilling equipment, structure your response as:
> 1. **Introduction** (1 mark) — define rotary drilling rig and cite API standards.
> 2. **Subsystem-wise description with sketch** (6–7 marks) — Hoisting, Rotary, Circulating, Power.
> 3. **Well-control / BOP subsystem in detail** (4–5 marks) — This is the **safety focus** of PESFT745.
> 4. **Safety standards and conclusion** (1–2 marks) — API RP 53, OISD-GDN-180, accumulator testing, daily BOP test.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ground and Offshore Structures for Drilling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ground and Offshore Structures for Drilling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition — Ground and Offshore Structures for Drilling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Ground (Land) Drilling Structures** are engineered, load-bearing assemblies installed on terrestrial well sites that provide the necessary elevation, stability, and load-transfer pathway to support the drill string, casing, hoisting system, and rotating equipment during wellbore construction. In contrast, **Offshore Drilling Structures** are marine-deployed platforms — fixed, mobile, or floating — that perform identical drilling functions while being subjected to additional environmental loads such as **wind, waves, currents, and hydrostatic pressure** in an oceanic environment.

A drilling structure is therefore the *skeleton* of any well-construction operation. On land, the structure is essentially a static steel frame embedded into a concrete foundation. Offshore, the structure must simultaneously resist gravity loads (drill string, BOP, casing) **and** dynamic environmental loads (storm waves of 30+ meters), making its engineering a vastly more complex discipline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PESFT745 — Module 4):**
> Students are expected to compare the load paths, safety factors, failure modes, and HSE implications of land rigs vs. offshore rigs. The focus is on **why** the offshore environment demands redundant safety systems, blowout preventers (BOPs), and dynamic positioning.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are hanging a **200 kg chandelier** from the ceiling of your house. The chandelier is heavy, swings a little when you walk, and the ceiling beam must support it for years without cracking.

- **Ground drilling structure** = the ceiling beam in your house. It is fixed, predictable, and only has to deal with the chandelier's weight and small vibrations.
- **Offshore drilling structure** = a chandelier hanging from a **bamboo pole stuck in a swimming pool during a typhoon**. The pole must support the weight, the water pushes sideways, the wind shakes it, and the pole must not snap, bend, or float away.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *"Land rigs fight gravity. Offshore rigs fight gravity AND the ocean."*

This single sentence captures the entire safety-engineering philosophy that differentiates the two domains.

## 1.3 Core Physical & Engineering Metrics

The following standard metrics and physical constants govern the design of both structure classes:

- **API 4F (Specification for Drilling and Well Servicing Structures)** — the governing standard for land rigs.
- **API RP 2A (Recommended Practice for Planning, Designing, and Constructing Fixed Offshore Platforms)** — the governing standard for fixed offshore platforms.
- **API SPEC 2B (Specification for the Fabrication of Structural Steel Pipe)** — for tubular joints.
- **API RP 2D (Operation and Maintenance of Offshore Cranes)**.
- **Modular rigs:** Operating wind speed up to **70 knots (~36 m/s)** for transit, **50 knots** for survival.
- **Water-depth classification:** Shallow (0–500 ft), Deepwater (500–5,000 ft), Ultra-Deepwater (>5,000 ft).
- **Typical land rig hookload capacity:** **250,000 – 2,000,000 lbs (1–9 MN)**.
- **Offshore rig displacement:** Semi-submersibles ≈ **30,000–70,000 tonnes** lightship.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can **quote the correct API standard** for a given scenario. Memorizing API 4F (land) and API 2A (offshore) is non-negotiable for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Land-Based Drilling Structures (Ground Structures)

### 2.1.1 Major Sub-Components

A complete land drilling structure comprises four primary sub-systems, each with a distinct load path:

1. **Substructure** — the base frame that supports the rotary table / top drive and elevates the drill floor above the cellar.
2. **Derrick / Mast** — the vertical tower that supports the crown block, traveling block, and hook.
3. **Crown Block & Traveling Block** — the sheave (pulley) system providing mechanical advantage.
4. **Drawworks & Hoisting System** — the drawworks drum, brake, and motor that raise/lower the drill string.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for safety:** Each sub-system has a distinct **mode of failure** (buckling, fatigue, brake failure). A KTU Part-B question often asks students to map a failure to a sub-system.

### 2.1.2 Load Path on a Land Rig

The downward forces from the drill string follow a **deterministic load path**:

$$\text{Hook} \rightarrow \text{Traveling Block} \rightarrow \text{Crown Block} \rightarrow \text{Derrick Legs} \rightarrow \text{Substructure} \rightarrow \text{Foundations} \rightarrow \text{Soil}$$

Any interruption in this path (e.g., a corroded derrick leg) causes **catastrophic toppling** — historically one of the deadliest rig accident categories.

### 2.1.3 Types of Land Masts

- **Conventional Derrick** — a 4-legged pyramidal structure, fixed or guyed; very high hook-load capacity.
- **Portable Mast** — foldable, designed for fast-mobilization wells.
- **Modular Derrick** — assembled from pre-engineered skid units; the modern industry standard.
- **Cantilever Mast** — pivots horizontally to allow moving the rig without dismantling.

## 2.2 Offshore Drilling Structures

### 2.2.1 Classification by Water Depth & Mobility

| Class | Water Depth | Mooring / Station-Keeping | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Platform (Steel Jacket)** | 0 – 1,500 ft | Piled to seabed | Shallow-water production & drilling |
| **Jack-Up Rig** | 0 – 550 ft | 3–4 legs lowered to seabed | Exploratory drilling in shallow seas |
| **Semi-Submersible** | 500 – 10,000 ft | Moored (8–12 chains) or DP | Deepwater drilling |
| **Drillship** | 5,000 – 12,000+ ft | Dynamic Positioning (DP-3) | Ultra-deepwater exploration |
| **Tension Leg Platform (TLP)** | 1,000 – 5,000 ft | Vertical tendons under tension | Deepwater production |
| **SPAR** | 2,000 – 10,000 ft | Catenary mooring | Deepwater production |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams:** Always specify the **station-keeping method** (anchored vs. DP) when naming an offshore structure. Examiners deduct marks if the student says *"a semi-sub"* without clarifying *moored* or *DP-3*.

### 2.2.2 Dynamic Loads on Offshore Structures

Unlike land rigs, offshore structures experience **six degrees of freedom (6-DOF)** of motion:

- **Surge** (x-translation), **Sway** (y-translation), **Heave** (z-translation)
- **Roll** (x-rotation), **Pitch** (y-rotation), **Yaw** (z-rotation)

Heave is the most critical for drilling because vertical rig motion at the drill floor can exceed the **heave compensation stroke (15–25 ft)** of the motion compensator, causing the drill string to part.

### 2.2.3 The 6-Component Environmental Load Vector

The total environmental load on an offshore structure is the vector sum of:

$$\vec{F}_{env} = \vec{F}_{wind} + \vec{F}_{wave} + \vec{F}_{current} + \vec{F}_{wind\text{-}induced\,current} + \vec{F}_{seismic} + \vec{F}_{ice\ (arctic)}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** These equations appear almost every cycle in KTU Part-A or Part-B. Memorize both the formula **and** the physical meaning of every term.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning of Terms | Standard / Source | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{hook} = n \cdot W_{line} \cdot W_{block}$ | Hook load: $n$ = number of lines, $W_{line}$ = line weight, $W_{block}$ = block weight | API 4F | lbs (lbf) |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F_{hook}}{A_{derrick\,leg}}$ | Allowable stress in derrick leg | AISC / API 4F | psi (lbf/in²) |
| 3 | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Static safety factor (land rigs: **2.0–2.5**, offshore: **1.5–2.0** + dynamic factor) | API 4F | dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^{2}$ | Wind force on exposed area | API 4F / 2A | N (SI) or lbf |
| 5 | $F_{wave} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{water} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^{2}$ | Wave force (Morison's eqn, simplified) | API 2A | N or lbf |
| 6 | $H_{s,max} = f(\text{return period, sea state})$ | Significant wave height (100-yr storm design) | API 2A | m or ft |
| 7 | $V_{heave} = \omega \cdot \zeta_a$ | Heave velocity (sinusoidal sea) | Hydrodynamics | m/s |
| 8 | $\text{Metacentric Height: } \overline{GM} = \dfrac{I}{V} - \overline{GB}$ | Stability of floating rig | Naval Architecture | m |
| 9 | $T_{pretension} = \Delta W$ | TLP tendon pretension = displaced weight | API 2T | kN |
| 10 | $P_{dp} = \dfrac{F_{env}}{\mu \cdot N_{thruster}}$ | DP thruster required power | IMCA M 140 | kW |
| 11 | $R_{air\,gap} = E_{100\,yr\,crest} + R_{subsidence} + C_{margin}$ | Air gap calculation (offshore decks) | API 2A | ft or m |
| 12 | $D_{conductor} = f(\text{BOP stack height, wave, tide})$ | Conductor pipe size selection | API 16A | in |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants used in the formula sheet:**
> - $\rho_{air} = \mathbf{1.225\ kg/m^3}$ (sea-level, 15 °C)
> - $\rho_{water,sea} = \mathbf{1,025\ kg/m^3}$
> - $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
> - $C_d$ (drag coefficient) ≈ **0.6–1.2** for cylindrical members (Reynolds-number dependent)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why the Concept Matters |
|---|---|
| **HPHT (High-Pressure High-Temp) wells** | Land rig hook-load and BOP pressure ratings become limiting factors |
| **Deepwater GOM, North Sea** | Air-gap and 100-year storm crest drive deck elevation |
| **Arctic drilling (Barents Sea)** | Ice load (iceberg impact, ridge-build-up) governs jacket leg design |
| **HP/HT & Sub-salt wells** | Heave compensator stroke must exceed vessel heave amplitude |
| **Unmanned minimum-facility platforms** | Modular masts + battery-backed BOP controls reduce HSE exposure |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Hook-Load Mechanics on a Block-and-Tackle System

A KTU Part-B favourite: *"Derive the relationship between fast-line pull, hook load, and number of lines reeved through the traveling and crown blocks."*

### Step 1 — Define the system

Consider a crown block with $n_c$ sheaves and a traveling block with $n_t$ sheaves. The total number of supporting lines is:

$$N_{lines} = n_c + n_t$$

For a conventional rig with equal sheaves in crown and traveling block:

$$N_{lines} = 2n$$

### Step 2 — Apply mechanical-advantage equilibrium

The hook load $W_{hook}$ is supported by $N_{lines}$ tensioned line segments, each carrying tension $T$. Vertical equilibrium of the traveling block gives:

$$W_{hook} = N_{lines} \cdot T$$

### Step 3 — Fast-line pull (drawworks input)

The **fast line** is the single line leaving the crown block and wrapping the drawworks drum. Its tension equals the line tension $T$ (assuming a frictionless sheave system). Therefore:

$$P_{fast} = T = \dfrac{W_{hook}}{N_{lines}}$$

### Step 4 — Effective fast-line pull with friction

Real sheaves have bearing friction. Define the **line efficiency** $\eta$, where $0.95 \leq \eta \leq 0.98$ for modern roller-bearing sheaves. The actual fast-line pull becomes:

$$P_{fast} = \dfrac{W_{hook}}{N_{lines} \cdot \eta}$$

### Step 5 — Including the weight of the block & lines

If the traveling block weighs $W_{block}$ and the suspended portion of the drill line weighs $W_{line}$, the **gross hook load** (the actual line tension must support these):

$$W_{gross} = W_{hook} + W_{block} + W_{line}$$

$$P_{fast,total} = \dfrac{W_{hook} + W_{block} + W_{line}}{N_{lines} \cdot \eta}$$

### Step 6 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question)

> **Given:** A rig reeves **12 lines** between crown and traveling blocks. The hook supports a drill string of **500,000 lbs**. The traveling block weighs **35,000 lbs**, the suspended drill line weighs **15,000 lbs**, and line efficiency is **0.97**.
> 
> **Find:** Fast-line pull at the drawworks.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{gross} &= 500{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 15{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 550{,}000 \text{ lbs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fast} &= \dfrac{W_{gross}}{N_{lines} \cdot \eta} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{550{,}000}{12 \times 0.97} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{550{,}000}{11.64} \\[4pt]
&= 47{,}250.86 \text{ lbs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P_{fast} \approx 47{,}251 \text{ lbs}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Comment:** A common error is to forget the line efficiency $\eta$ and quote **45,833 lbs** (off by ~3%). The examiner's key awards **full 7 marks only** if $\eta$ is included.

## 3.2 Derivation — Simplified Morison Equation for Wave Force on a Jacket Leg

Used extensively for fixed offshore platforms (API 2A).

### Step 1 — Morison's formulation

For a slender vertical cylinder (jacket leg) of diameter $D$ subjected to wave-induced water-particle velocity $u$ and acceleration $\dot{u}$, the inline force per unit length is:

$$\dfrac{dF}{dz} = \underbrace{C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w D \cdot \vert u \vert u}_{\text{drag term}} + \underbrace{C_M \cdot \rho_w \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dot{u}}_{\text{inertia term}}$$

Where:
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (≈ 0.6 – 1.2)
- $C_M$ = inertia coefficient (≈ 1.5 – 2.0)
- $\rho_w$ = seawater density (1,025 kg/m³)
- $u$, $\dot{u}$ = horizontal water-particle velocity and acceleration

### Step 2 — Integrate over the submerged length $L$

$$
F_{total} = \int_{0}^{L} \left( C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w D \vert u \vert u + C_M \cdot \rho_w \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \dot{u} \right) dz
$$

### Step 3 — Airy-wave substitution (linear wave theory)

For a regular wave of height $H$ and period $T$, the horizontal water-particle velocity at depth $z$ below the mean water level is:

$$u(z,t) = \dfrac{H \omega}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh(k(d+z))}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

Where:
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$
- $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ (wave number)
- $d$ = water depth
- $\lambda$ = wavelength

### Step 4 — Worked example (7-mark sub-question)

> **Given:** A jacket leg, $D = 2\ \text{m}$, submerged over $L = 30\ \text{m}$, $d = 80\ \text{m}$. Wave: $H = 6\ \text{m}$, $T = 9\ \text{s}$. Use $C_D = 0.8$, $C_M = 2.0$, $\rho_w = 1{,}025\ \text{kg/m}^3$.
> 
> **Approximation (suitable for KTU):** Use the maximum drag-only force at the surface ($u_{max}$) and assume a triangular velocity profile with depth.
> 
> $u_{max} = \dfrac{H \omega}{2} = \dfrac{6 \times 2\pi / 9}{2} = \dfrac{6 \times 0.698}{2} \approx 2.09\ \text{m/s}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{drag,max} &\approx C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w D L u_{max}^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 1{,}025 \times 2 \times 30 \times (2.09)^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 0.8 \times 0.5 \times 1{,}025 \times 2 \times 30 \times 4.37 \\[4pt]
&= 107{,}481.6\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{F_{drag,max} \approx 107.5\ \text{kN} \approx 24{,}160\ \text{lbf}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A student who forgets the squared velocity term, or who uses $u_{max}$ instead of $u_{max}^{2}$, loses **3 of 7 marks** immediately. Always double-check the **order of magnitude**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Land vs. Offshore Structures

| Parameter | Land Rig (Ground) | Offshore Rig (Floating) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary load** | Gravity (static) | Gravity + dynamic 6-DOF | Offshore needs motion compensator |
| **Foundation** | Concrete pad / mat | Mooring lines / DP / spud cans | Land foundation is forgiving |
| **Storm survivability** | Shut down, evacuate | Disconnect, move off, or survive-in-place | Offshore has *two* operating modes |
| **BOP configuration** | Land BOP (≤10,000 psi typical) | Subsea BOP (≤20,000 psi, redundant) | Subsea BOP is a $40M+ assembly |
| **Crew transfer** | Trucks, light vehicles | Helicopter, crew boat, gangway | Offshore HSE adds aviation risk |
| **Firefighting** | Onshore fire brigade reachable | Self-contained deluge + firewater pumps | Must be self-sufficient |
| **Evacuation** | Drive/walk off | Lifeboats (free-fall, TEMPSC) | Mandatory per SOLAS/MODU code |
| **Inspection interval** | Annual | Continuous + class society surveys | Offshore has 5-yr special survey |
| **Governing standard** | API 4F | API 2A / 2D / 2T, MODU code | Land is rule-based; offshore is performance-based |
| **HSE regulatory body** | OSHA, local mining inspector | BSEE (US), HSE (UK), NOPSEMA (AU) | Multi-jurisdictional offshore |

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark Part-B answers:** Always structure your comparison along the rows of this matrix. The examiner's key specifically looks for **10+ distinct comparison points**; each missing row is a -2 mark deduction.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Land Rig Load Path

This diagram traces every component the **gravitational load** of the drill string passes through from the bit up to the soil.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Drill Bit & Drill String] --> B[Hook Assembly]
    B --> C[Traveling Block with Multiple Sheaves]
    C --> D[Crown Block with Multiple Sheaves]
    D --> E[Derrick / Mast Legs]
    E --> F[Substructure Frame]
    F --> G[Skid Base or Sub-Base]
    G --> H[Concrete Mat or Cellar Foundation]
    H --> I[Native Soil Bearing Stratum]
    
    J[Fast Line Wire Rope] --> C
    K[Drawworks Drum and Motor] --> J
    L[Brake Band and Cooling System] --> K
    
    M[Crown Block Sheaves] --> D
    N[Catline and Deadline Anchors] --> D
    
    O[Rotary Table or Top Drive] --> F
    P[Drill Floor and Monkey Board] --> F
    Q[BOP Stack on Cellar] --> H
    
    classDef loadbox fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef liftbox fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef fndbox fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000

```

**Reading the diagram:** The red cluster = the **active lifting chain**; blue = the **supporting substructure**; green = the **load-transfer path to the soil**. Failure of any green node is unrecoverable without shutting down the well.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Offshore Semi-Submersible Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENV [Environmental Load Domain]
        EA[Wind on Derricks and Hull] --> EF[Total Environmental Force Vector]
        EB[Wave and Swell] --> EF
        EC[Ocean Current] --> EF
        ED[Tidal Variation] --> EF
    end
    
    subgraph HULL [Hull and Stability System]
        EF --> HS[Heave Motion Sensor]
        EF --> PT[Pitch and Roll Sensor]
        HS --> MC[Active Heave Compensator]
        PT --> MC
        MC --> DSK[Drill Floor Vertical Reference]
        DSK --> DT[Drill String Suspended from Derrick]
    end
    
    subgraph STKEEP [Station Keeping System]
        EF --> MOO[Mooring Line Tension Monitor 8 to 12 chains]
        EF --> DP[DP3 Thruster Controller]
        MOO --> POS[Position Reference GPS and Acoustic]
        DP --> POS
    end
    
    subgraph BOP [Subsea BOP and Well Control]
        DT --> CSG[Wellhead and Conductor]
        CSG --> BOPSTK[BOP Stack with Redundant Rams]
        BOPSTK --> CHOKE[Choke and Kill Lines]
    end
    
    subgraph SAFE [HSE and Survival Layer]
        HULL --> SAFE1[Free Fall Lifeboats TEMPSC]
        HULL --> SAFE2[Firewater Deluge System]
        HULL --> SAFE3[Helideck and Aviation Fuel]
        HULL --> SAFE4[Ballast Control and Bilge Alarms]
    end
    
    classDef envbox fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000

    class EA,EB,EC,ED,EF envbox

    class MOO,DP,POS skbox

```

**Reading the diagram:** Four parallel subsystems — Environmental, Hull/Stability, Station-Keeping, and BOP — feed into a fifth **HSE/Survival layer**. This is exactly the *defense-in-depth* philosophy mandated by the **MODU Code (IMO)** and **API RP 2A**.

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting a Drilling Structure Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Well Planning] --> B{Is the well onshore or offshore?}
    
    B -- Onshore --> C{Land rig capacity needed?}
    C -- Light Workover --> D[Workover Rig]
    C -- Medium Duty --> E[Conventional 1500 HP Rig]
    C -- Heavy Duty Deep Well --> F[AC Modular Rig 2000 HP plus]
    
    B -- Offshore --> G{Water depth?}
    G -- 0 to 550 ft --> H{Is seabed soft or hard?}
    H -- Soft --> I[Jack-Up with Mat-Supported Spud Cans]
    H -- Hard --> J[Jack-Up with Independent Leg Spud Cans]
    G -- 500 to 5000 ft --> K{Production or Exploration?}
    K -- Production --> L[TLP or SPAR]
    K -- Exploration --> M[Moored Semi-Submersible]
    G -- 5000 ft and beyond --> N{Drillship or Semi-Sub?}
    N -- High Mobility --> O[Drillship with DP3]
    N -- High Variable Load --> P[Semi-Sub with DP3]
    
    classDef landbox fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000

```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the standard **structure-selection decision tree** used by drilling engineers. A KTU Part-A question may ask: *"For a 7,000-ft exploration well, which structure would you recommend and why?"* The correct answer: **DP-3 drillship**, with justification based on mobility, transit speed, and 6-DOF heave compensation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between a derrick and a mast as used in land-based drilling structures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Derrick | Mast |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A 4-legged pyramidal or square tower with a horizontal crown supported at the top | A similar tower, but with an integrated crown-block and often telescoping |
| **Configuration** | Fixed or guyed; non-folding | Folding / telescoping for transport |
| **Capacity** | Very high hook-load (1,000,000+ lbs) | Lower to medium hook-load |
| **Use case** | Long-term, high-duty drilling | Quick-mobilization, batch drilling |

> Both perform the **same function** of supporting the crown block and drill string, but differ in transportability and structural configuration. **(3 marks — 1 for definition of each, 1 for distinguishing use case.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Dynamic Positioning (DP) and why is it mandatory for ultra-deepwater drilling rigs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dynamic Positioning (DP) is a **computer-controlled thruster system** that automatically maintains a vessel's position and heading without the use of mooring lines, using reference inputs from **DGPS, acoustic transponders, and/or taut-wire systems**. Three DP classes exist (DP-1, DP-2, DP-3); **DP-3** is the IMO requirement for MODUs in deepwater because it provides *redundancy* — loss of any single compartment cannot cause loss of position. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for class hierarchy, 1 for redundancy argument.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Format:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### QUESTION A — Comprehensive Coverage of Land + Offshore Structures

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *With the help of a clear block diagram, describe the complete load path of a drill string on a conventional land rig, starting from the bit and ending at the soil. Identify the component most prone to fatigue failure and justify your answer.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Drill Bit & Drill String** — Apply WOB; transmits torque from surface. The drill string itself can fail in **tension** (when tripping out) or in **fatigue** (due to cyclic bending in doglegs).
2. **Hook** — Suspends the entire string.
3. **Traveling Block** — Multi-sheave block, carries the heaviest movable load.
4. **Crown Block** — Fixed sheaves at the top of the derrick.
5. **Derrick / Mast Legs** — The four main load-bearing members of the derrick. These are the **second most fatigue-prone** components due to cyclic wind and string-tension variation.
6. **Substructure** — Transfers derrick load to the cellar.
7. **Skid Base & Concrete Mat** — Distributes load to a large bearing area.
8. **Soil Bearing Stratum** — Ultimate load sink.

> **Most fatigue-prone component: the drill string itself (specifically the threaded connections of the tool joints and drill pipe).** Justification: tens of thousands of stress cycles per well due to stick-slip, dogleg bending, and rotation.

**Mark allocation:** 1 mark for each correct step (1×6 = 6 marks) + 1 mark for correctly identifying drill string + 1 for justification = **7 marks**.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *Compare the failure modes of (i) a fixed steel jacket platform and (ii) a semi-submersible drilling rig. Which one is more resilient to a 100-year storm event, and why?*

**Model Solution:**

| Failure Mode | Fixed Jacket | Semi-Submersible |
|---|---|---|
| **Reserve strength** | Low (designed for 100-yr storm) | High (designed for 100-yr storm + can be disconnected) |
| **Foundation failure** | Pile pull-out, scour | Mooring-line failure, anchor drag |
| **Member buckling** | Brace buckling in wave zone | Column buckling of pontoons/columns |
| **Fatigue** | Tubular joint fatigue (splash zone) | Mooring chain fatigue at fairleads |
| **Survival strategy** | Must "ride out" the storm | Can **disconnect** from the well and move off-station |
| **Damage tolerance** | Poor (single failure can be progressive collapse) | Better (compartmentalized hull) |

> **Conclusion:** The **semi-submersible** is *more resilient* to a 100-year storm because (a) it can **disconnect** the marine riser from the subsea BOP and transit to a safe stand-off distance, and (b) its mooring system has explicit **redundant lines** (8–12 chains; only 3 are typically required for station-keeping).

**Mark allocation:** 1 mark per correctly filled row (5 rows × 1 = 5) + 1 mark for naming semi-sub as more resilient + 1 mark for valid justification = **7 marks**.

---

### QUESTION B — Engineering Calculations Focus (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *A land rig reeves 10 lines through crown and traveling blocks. The hook carries a string weighing 400,000 lbs; the traveling block weighs 28,000 lbs; suspended line weighs 12,000 lbs. Line efficiency is 0.96. Calculate the fast-line pull and identify which derrick-leg cross-sectional area is required if the allowable working stress in the steel is 30,000 psi. Assume 4 derrick legs share the load equally.*

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{gross} &= 400{,}000 + 28{,}000 + 12{,}000 = 440{,}000 \text{ lbs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fast} &= \dfrac{W_{gross}}{N_{lines} \cdot \eta} = \dfrac{440{,}000}{10 \times 0.96} = \dfrac{440{,}000}{9.6} = 45{,}833.33 \text{ lbs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derrick leg load per leg:**

$$
F_{leg} = \dfrac{W_{gross} - P_{fast} \text{ (since fast line removes some load)}}{4}
$$

Using gross hook load for conservative design (KTU examiner's convention):

$$
F_{leg} = \dfrac{440{,}000}{4} = 110{,}000 \text{ lbs}
$$

**Required cross-sectional area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{required} &= \dfrac{F_{leg}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{110{,}000}{30{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 3.667 \text{ in}^2 \approx 23.66 \text{ cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> A standard **4 × 4 × ½-inch** square hollow section provides $A = 7.0\ \text{in}^2$, comfortably exceeding the requirement (with a safety factor ≈ 1.91).

**Mark allocation:** 2 marks for gross weight, 2 marks for fast-line pull, 2 marks for leg load, 1 mark for cross-section calculation = **7 marks**.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *For a fixed offshore jacket in 80 m water depth, a single leg (diameter 2.5 m) is exposed to a wave with $H = 8\ \text{m}$, $T = 10\ \text{s}$. Using $C_D = 0.7$, $C_M = 2.0$, and $\rho_w = 1{,}025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, calculate the maximum drag and inertia force components per unit length at the mean water level.*

**Model Solution:**

**Wave angular frequency and linear-theory velocity:**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T} = \dfrac{2\pi}{10} = 0.628\ \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
u_{max} = \dfrac{H \omega}{2} = \dfrac{8 \times 0.628}{2} = 2.514\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Maximum acceleration (linear theory, at mean water level):**

$$
\dot{u}_{max} = \omega \cdot u_{max} = 0.628 \times 2.514 = 1.579\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Drag force per unit length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{dF}{dz}\right)_{drag} &= C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w D \cdot u_{max}^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 1{,}025 \times 2.5 \times (2.514)^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 1{,}025 \times 2.5 \times 6.32 \\[4pt]
&= 5{,}668.0\ \text{N/m} \approx 5.67\ \text{kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inertia force per unit length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{dF}{dz}\right)_{inertia} &= C_M \cdot \rho_w \cdot \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dot{u}_{max} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0 \times 1{,}025 \times \tfrac{\pi (2.5)^{2}}{4} \times 1.579 \\[4pt]
&= 2.0 \times 1{,}025 \times 4.909 \times 1.579 \\[4pt]
&= 15{,}881.6\ \text{N/m} \approx 15.88\ \text{kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\boxed{\text{Drag} \approx 5.67\ \text{kN/m},\quad \text{Inertia} \approx 15.88\ \text{kN/m}}$. Inertia dominates at this water depth, which is typical for **small-diameter, deep-water members**.

**Mark allocation:** 2 marks for ω and $u_{max}$, 1 mark for $\dot{u}_{max}$, 2 marks for drag, 2 marks for inertia = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (per KTU Board Examiner Patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing derrick and mast** — These are NOT synonyms. A *derrick* is typically fixed/guyed; a *mast* folds. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if used interchangeably.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the line efficiency $\eta$** in hook-load problems. Always write $\eta = 0.95$ to $0.98$ explicitly.
> 
> 3. **Using the wrong standard** — API 4F is for *land*; API 2A is for *offshore*. Mixing them up costs **1–2 marks**.
> 
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers. KTU mandates SI units in the answer unless the problem specifies otherwise; missing units = **–1 mark**.
> 
> 5. **Stating "DP" without class** — Always specify **DP-1, DP-2, or DP-3**; only DP-3 is IMO-required for MODUs.
> 
> 6. **Neglecting the modulus sign** in $u_{max}^{2}$ — the squared term is critical in drag calculations; squaring a *negative* velocity is mathematically valid and physically meaningful (energy is always positive).
> 
> 7. **In comparison answers, fewer than 8 distinct points** — KTU Part-B (14 marks) typically requires **8–10 comparison rows** for full credit; each missing row is –2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Ready):**

- **Definition Recap:** A *ground* drilling structure is a static land-based rig; an *offshore* structure adds dynamic 6-DOF environmental loading.
- **Governing Standards:** **API 4F** (land) ; **API 2A / 2D / 2T** (offshore) ; **MODU Code** (IMO) for mobile offshore units.
- **Land rig sub-systems (4):** Substructure → Derrick/Mast → Crown + Traveling Block → Drawworks.
- **Hook load formula (must memorize):** $P_{fast} = \dfrac{W_{hook} + W_{block} + W_{line}}{N_{lines} \cdot \eta}$.
- **Offshore structure classes (6):** Fixed Jacket, Jack-Up, Semi-Submersible, Drillship, TLP, SPAR.
- **Station keeping methods (3):** Piled (jacket), Moored (chains), DP-3 (computer-controlled thrusters).
