<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define Permit-to-Work (PTW). List any FOUR types of permits commonly used in a hydrocarbon refinery.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Permit-to-Work (PTW) is a formal, written, controlled, and time-bound authorization document that ensures potentially hazardous work is performed safely, after prescribed precautions have been verified, by named, competent personnel, in a defined location, at a defined time.

**Four Types (1/2 mark each, 2 marks total):**
1. **Hot Work Permit** — for spark-producing activities (welding, grinding, cutting).
2. **Cold Work Permit** — for non-spark-producing maintenance (painting, scaffolding).
3. **Confined Space Entry Permit** — for entry into vessels, tanks, pits, and columns.
4. **Electrical Isolation / LOTO Permit** — for safe de-energization and lockout-tagout of electrical equipment.

> *Additional accepted permits:* Excavation Permit, Working-at-Height Permit, Vehicle Entry Permit, Radiography Permit.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is Management of Change (MOC) in hydrocarbon operations? Why is it critical for safety?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** MOC is a formal, structured process for evaluating, approving, documenting, and implementing any temporary or permanent change to process materials, equipment, procedures, personnel, or operating conditions — before the change is made.

**Criticality (2 marks):**
- **Hazard re-analysis:** Any change can introduce new failure modes. MOC forces a HAZOP / LOPA re-run.
- **Prevents "drift" creep:** Untracked changes accumulate, eroding the original safety basis.
- **Regulatory compliance:** OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119) and API RP 750 mandate MOC.
- **Pre-startup Safety Review (PSSR):** MOC ensures the change is verified safe before live operation.
- **Real-world example:** The **2005 Texas City Refinery explosion** (15 deaths) was attributed to non-MOC temporary change in raffinate splitter operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and **must** offer an internal choice (either A or B). Marks are split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **Permit-to-Work (PTW) system** in a hydrocarbon refinery. Discuss its importance, types, and the step-by-step lifecycle with a flow diagram.

**(b) [7 marks]** A distillation column has an initiating event frequency for overpressure of **0.5 per year**. It is protected by a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) with PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$ and a SIL 2 SIF with PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$.
- (i) Calculate the **mitigated event frequency**.
- (ii) If the corporate target is $1 \times 10^{-5}$/year, determine whether the current protection is adequate. If not, specify the **additional SIL level** required for the new IPL.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Importance of PTW (2 marks):**
PTW is the **administrative backbone** of operational safety in a hydrocarbon facility. It provides:
- A documented, traceable record of all hazardous work.
- Clear **role accountability** (Originator → Issuer → Receiver → Authorizer).
- Cross-functional verification (Operations + Safety + Maintenance).
- Compliance with OSHA, API RP 2210, Indian Factory Act, OISD standards.

**Types of Permits (2 marks):**
Hot Work, Cold Work, Confined Space Entry, Electrical Isolation (LOTO), Excavation, Working at Height, Vehicle Entry, Radiography, Lift Plan.

**Step-by-Step Lifecycle (3 marks):**
1. **Job Request** by the originator with scope, location, duration, hazards.
2. **Area Classification & Hazard Identification** by area authority.
3. **Permit Type Selection** (Hot / Cold / Vessel Entry / etc.).
4. **Precautions Verification** — Isolation, blinding, gas testing, LOTO.
5. **Permit Issuance** by authorized Permit Issuer with signature.
6. **Work Execution** by competent workers with valid permit displayed.
7. **Periodic Re-Validation** at shift change or every 8 hours.
8. **Permit Closure and Area Handback** with final gas test and sign-off.
9. **Document Archival** for audit, investigation, and learning.

**Flow Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 (Mermaid diagram).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Mitigated Frequency [3 marks]:**

Using LOPA equation:

$$
f_{mitigated} = f_{initiating} \times PFD_{PSV} \times PFD_{SIF}
$$

$$
f_{mitigated} = 0.5 \times 1 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
f_{mitigated} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{per year}
$$

**(ii) Adequacy Check [4 marks]:**

Target frequency = $1 \times 10^{-5}$/year
Current mitigated = $5 \times 10^{-5}$/year

Since $5 \times 10^{-5} > 1 \times 10^{-5}$, the **protection is NOT adequate**. We are 5× above target.

**Required additional PFD:**

$$
1 \times 10^{-5} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \times PFD_{IPL3}
$$

$$
PFD_{IPL3} = 0.2 = 2 \times 10^{-1}
$$

From the SIL table (refer Section 3.3), $PFD = 2 \times 10^{-1}$ falls in the range $10^{-1} \leq PFD < 1$, which corresponds to **SIL 1** (or a non-SIF IPL such as an additional alarm + operator action).

> **Conclusion:** Install an additional **SIL 1 IPL** (e.g., high-pressure alarm with operator intervention procedure, or a redundant pressure transmitter trip).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **Crowl & Louvar Yield-to-TNT model** for vapour cloud explosion analysis. With suitable equations, explain how the explosion energy and TNT equivalent mass are computed. State the cube-root scaling law for overpressure estimation.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **2000 kg** release of cyclohexane occurs in a refinery. The release forms a vapour cloud in which **5% of the mass** participates in a stoichiometric explosion. Given:
- Heat of combustion, $H_c = 43,900$ kJ/kg
- Combustion efficiency, $\eta = 0.15$
- TNT energy, $H_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg
- Distance to inhabited building = 200 m

Calculate: (i) Mass of fuel in cloud, (ii) Explosion energy, (iii) TNT equivalent mass, (iv) Cube-root scaled distance, (v) Approximate peak side-on overpressure at 200 m.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Yield Concept (2 marks):**
Crowl & Louvar define the **fuel yield $Y_{fuel}$** as the ratio of the actual mass of fuel in the cloud to the mass of fuel in a stoichiometric fuel-air mixture. $Y$ ranges from 0 (no fuel) to 1 (stoichiometric).

**Explosion Energy Equation (2 marks):**
$$
E = Y_{fuel} \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot H_c \cdot \eta
$$
where $\eta$ is the combustion efficiency (typically 0.02 to 0.40 for vapour cloud explosions).

**TNT Equivalence (2 marks):**
$$
W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}} = \dfrac{E}{H_{TNT}}
$$
$H_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg is the standard reference value.

**Cube-root scaling (1 mark):**
$$
Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}
$$
where $R$ is the distance from the explosion centre. Overpressure is then read from standard TNT blast curves (function of $Z$).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Mass of fuel in cloud [1 mark]:**
$$
m_{fuel} = 0.05 \times 2000 = 100 \;\text{kg}
$$

**(ii) Explosion energy [2 marks]:**
$$
E = Y \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot H_c \cdot \eta
$$
Assuming $Y = 1$ (stoichiometric cloud as stated):

$$
E = 1.0 \times 100 \times 43{,}900 \times 0.15
$$

$$
E = 658{,}500 \;\text{kJ} = 658.5 \;\text{MJ}
$$

**(iii) TNT equivalent [1 mark]:**
$$
W_{TNT} = \dfrac{E}{H_{TNT}} = \dfrac{658{,}500}{4680} = 140.7 \;\text{kg TNT}
$$

**(iv) Cube-root scaled distance [1 mark]:**
$$
Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \dfrac{200}{(140.7)^{1/3}}
$$

$$
(140.7)^{1/3} = 5.20
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{200}{5.20} = 38.46 \;\text{m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**(v) Overpressure at 200 m [2 marks]:**
From the Crowl-Louvar TNT overpressure chart (Fig. 6-26, 4th ed.) or the empirical correlation (Brasie & Simpson):

For $Z \approx 38.5$ m/kg^(1/3), peak side-on overpressure $\Delta P \approx 35$ kPa (gauge).

This corresponds to:
- **Heavy damage** to brick buildings.
- **Eardrum rupture** in humans.
- **Partial structural collapse** of un-reinforced masonry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $H_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg with $H_{TNT} = 4500$ kJ/kg** (some sources differ). KTU accepts both, but you **must state the value used** in your answer.
> 2. **Always convert m³ to kg** if the problem gives volume — examiners often give volume in m³ as a trap.
> 3. **Cube-root scaling is empirical** — it does not give exact values, only estimates. The KTU answer must end with a **statement of approximation**, not a fake precise number.
> 4. **PTW derivation question**: students often forget the **re-validation step** at shift change. This is a 1-mark deduction in KTU evaluation.
> 5. **LOPA question**: students forget to **multiply PFDs** for multiple IPLs. KTU deducts 2 marks if the multiplicative form is not written.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Operations (Module 4)

#### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] **Operations** = live execution phase of hydrocarbon facility lifecycle; **80% of incidents** occur here.
- [ ] **Swiss Cheese Model** (Reason, 1990) is the underlying theory of operations safety.
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- [ ] **PTW is the single most important administrative control** in any hydrocarbon facility.
- [ ] **MOC** is mandatory for *any* deviation from baseline — design, procedure, material, personnel, operating window.

#### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize
- [ ] **LFL of methane** = **5.0% v/v**; **LFL of propane** = **2.1% v/v**.
- [ ] **H_TNT** = **4680 kJ/kg** (Crowl-Louvar).
- [ ] **Combustion efficiency η** for VCE = **0.02 to 0.40** (typical = 0.20).
- [ ] **Minimum O₂ for combustion** = **10% v/v**.
- [ ] **SIL 1 PFD** = $10^{-1}$; **SIL 2 PFD** = $10^{-2}$; **SIL 3 PFD** = $10^{-3}$.

#### ✅ Key Equations to Memorize
- [ ] **Le Chatelier LFL:** $LFL_{mix} = 100 \Big/ \sum (y_i / LFL_i)$
- [ ] **LOPA Frequency:** $f_{mit} = f_{init} \times \prod PFD_i$
- [ ] **Explosion Energy:** $E = Y \cdot m \cdot H_c \cdot \eta$
- [ ] **TNT Equivalent:** $W_{TNT} = E / H_{TNT}$
- [ ] **Cube-Root Scaling:** $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$

#### ✅ Key Procedures to Describe
- [ ] **PTW Lifecycle**: Request → Classify → Draft → Isolate → Gas Test → Authorize → Execute → Re-validate → Close → Archive.
- [ ] **MOC Lifecycle**: Request → Tech Review → HAZOP/LOPA → Risk Approval → Implement under PTW → PSSR → Document Update → Close.
- [ ] **Operations Lifecycle**: PSSR → Commissioning → Initial Start-up → Normal Operation → (Turnaround / Temporary Op / ESD) → Decommissioning.

#### ✅ Key Regulatory Anchors
- [ ] **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** — Process Safety Management.
- [ ] **API RP 750** — Management of Process Hazards.
- [ ] **API RP 2210** — Flame Arresters in Vapour Cloud Risk.
- [ ] **IEC 61511** — Functional Safety / SIL in process industry.
- [ ] **OISD-STD-105** — Oil Industry Safety Directorate (India) — Permit-to-Work.

#### ✅ Two Highest-Weightage Topics for KTU
1. **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** — guaranteed 7-mark sub-question in every cycle.
2. **Crowl-Louvar Yield-TNT derivation** — favourite 14-mark numerical.

> **Final KTU Advice:** In the ESE, always state the **standard reference** (Crowl & Louvar, API RP 750, OSHA PSM) and the **assumptions** explicitly. This fetches the **bonus 1 mark** KTU examiners are authorized to award for methodological clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport of Crude by Sea
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transport of Crude by Sea

# Transport of Crude by Sea — Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Transport of Crude by Sea** refers to the large-scale maritime movement of unrefined petroleum (crude oil) from production/loading terminals (e.g., the Persian Gulf, West Africa, the North Sea) to refineries located along coastlines, using specialized seagoing vessels known as **crude oil tankers**. It is governed by a robust international regulatory framework comprising **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea), **MARPOL** (Marine Pollution), the **ISM Code**, and the **ISGOTT** (International Safety Guide for Oil Tankers and Terminals).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic belongs to the **Logistics Safety** and **Process Hazard Management** cluster of Module 4, where students are expected to understand tanker engineering, marine terminal operations, and the layered defense against fire, explosion, and marine pollution.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine a city has millions of liters of raw milk that must be sent from rural farms to processing factories daily. You would not use plastic bottles; you would use **specialized stainless-steel insulated tank trucks** that can carry huge volumes, are cleaned in place, and have multiple chambers to prevent sloshing. Crude oil transport by sea is the **ocean-going equivalent** of this concept — but with flammable hydrocarbons instead of milk, and with vastly more catastrophic consequences if something goes wrong.

A crude tanker is essentially a **floating, double-skinned, segregated pipeline**, designed to safely carry anywhere from **30,000 DWT to 550,000 DWT** of crude across oceans, while resisting corrosion, fire, explosion, structural failure, and pollution.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Approximately **60% of the world's crude oil** moves by sea. The 1989 *Exxon Valdez* spill (**37,000 tonnes** of crude) and the 2002 *Prestige* sinking released **63,000 tonnes** of fuel oil into the Atlantic. These disasters reshaped international maritime safety law and are frequently asked in KTU examinations as case-study reasoning questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Crude Oil Tankers

Tankers are categorized by their **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** — the total weight (in metric tonnes) the vessel can carry when fully loaded, including cargo, fuel, water, and supplies.

| Tanker Class | DWT Range | Typical Length | Primary Trade Route |
|--------------|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **General Purpose / Handysize** | 10,000 – 60,000 | 150 – 185 m | Short-sea, regional |
| **Aframax** ("Average Freight Rate Assessment") | 80,000 – 120,000 | 230 – 250 m | Cross-Atlantic, Mediterranean |
| **Suezmax** | 120,000 – 200,000 | 270 – 285 m | Persian Gulf → Europe (via Suez Canal) |
| **VLCC** ("Very Large Crude Carrier") | 200,000 – 320,000 | 320 – 340 m | Long-haul intercontinental |
| **ULCC** ("Ultra Large Crude Carrier") | 320,000 – 550,000 | 380 – 415 m | Persian Gulf → Asia/USA |

> [!NOTE]
> The **DWT formula** is foundational:
> $$\text{DWT} = \text{Displacement}_{\text{loaded}} - \text{Lightweight}$$
> Where the **Lightweight** is the empty ship's displacement. Displacement is computed using Archimedes' principle.

## 2.2 Critical Safety Systems on Modern Tankers

### 2.2.1 Double Hull (Mandatory under MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 19)
- **Outer hull** (skin): 1.5 – 2.5 m from inner hull.
- **Inner hull**: forms the cargo tank boundary.
- **Ballast tanks** sit between the two hulls.
- Reduces probability of oil spill in grounding/collision by **~70%** (per IMO studies post-*Exxon Valdez*).

### 2.2.2 Inert Gas System (IGS)
A critical explosion-prevention system mandated by **SOLAS Chapter II-2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle:** Cargo tanks contain flammable vapors. By replacing the oxygen-rich atmosphere above the cargo with **inert gas (typically flue gas with O₂ < 5% by volume)**, the mixture is pushed below the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)**, making ignition impossible.

The IGS consists of:
1. **Boiler flue gas source**
2. **Scrubber** (removes SO₂)
3. **Blower** (positive pressure delivery)
4. **Distribution piping & non-return valves**
5. **Tank Pressure/Vacuum Breakers**

### 2.2.3 Crude Oil Washing (COW)
- High-pressure jets of crude oil are sprayed against the inner tank walls to dissolve sludge/wax.
- Replaces seawater washing, which previously left oily residues that had to be disposed of at port.
- **Loading rate during COW:** typically **0.6 – 1.0 m/s** nozzle velocity.

### 2.2.4 Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT)
- Dedicated seawater ballast tanks, completely separate from cargo/clean ballast systems.
- Prevents contamination of ballast water with oil.
- Required for tankers > **20,000 DWT** under MARPOL.

### 2.2.5 Fixed Firefighting Systems
- **Foam monitors** on deck.
- **Water spray cooling** for manifold/structure.
- **CO₂ / NOVEC flooding** for pump rooms and engine rooms.
- **Dry chemical powder** for deck fires.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Deadweight Tonnage | $\text{DWT} = \Delta_{\text{loaded}} - \Delta_{\text{light}}$ | Metric tonnes (t) |
| 2 | Cargo Capacity | $V_c = \frac{m_c}{\rho_{\text{crude}}}$ | m³, where ρ ≈ **860–930 kg/m³** |
| 3 | Loading Rate (volumetric) | $Q = A_p \cdot v$ | m³/h |
| 4 | Reynolds Number (pipeline) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless; turbulent if > 4000 |
| 5 | Pressure Drop (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$ | Pa (laminar) |
| 6 | Friction Factor (Blasius) | $f = 0.0791 \cdot Re^{-0.25}$ | Turbulent smooth pipe |
| 7 | Trim correction (draft) | $T_{aft} = T_{fwd} + \Delta T$ | m |
| 8 | LFL of crude vapor | ~ **0.6%** by vol | In air at 25°C |
| 9 | UFL of crude vapor | ~ **8%** by vol | In air at 25°C |
| 10 | O₂ threshold for IGS | $< \mathbf{5\%}$ by vol | Per SOLAS II-2 |
| 11 | Discharge rate (cargo pumps) | $Q_p = \dfrac{V_c}{t_{\text{disch}}}$ | m³/h |
| 12 | Sloshing natural period | $T_n \approx 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{g}}$ | s; tank length L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical safety constants for KTU exams:**
> - API Gravity of light crude: **35 – 45°**
> - API Gravity of heavy crude: **10 – 22°**
> - Auto-Ignition Temperature of crude vapor: **~ 250 °C**
> - Flash Point (typical crude): **< 60 °C** (Class I flammable liquid)

## 2.4 The Loading / Discharge Operation Sequence

A KTU-favorite question concerns the stepwise procedure at a marine terminal:

1. **Pre-Arrival Checks** — Notice of Readiness (NOR), ISGOTT compatibility, inert gas status verified.
2. **Mooring & Bonding** — Insulated flange + earthing cable to prevent static discharge.
3. **Ship/Shore Safety Checklist** — 28 items per ISGOTT.
4. **Ballast Adjustments** — Trim and list corrected to within **0.5°** and **0.3 m draft tolerance**.
5. **Connection of Manifold** — Dry-break couplers, gas-free verification.
6. **Line-up & Gauging** — All valves aligned, ullage measured.
7. **Cargo Transfer** — Pumps ramp up gradually to avoid hydraulic shock.
8. **Crude Oil Washing (COW)** — Concurrent with loading if required.
9. **Topping Off** — Final tanks at reduced rate to prevent overflow.
10. **Tank Inspection & Departure** — Independent gauging, sample retention, NOR tendered.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cargo Loading Time Using Pump Curves

**Problem (KTU-style):** A VLCC of **300,000 DWT** carries crude oil with density **ρ = 870 kg/m³**. The cargo pump delivers a flow that ramps linearly from 0 to maximum in 30 minutes, then holds constant at **12,000 m³/h** until topping-off, where it linearly reduces to zero in the final 30 minutes. Calculate total loading time.

**Step 1 — Compute total cargo volume.**

$$V_c = \frac{m_c}{\rho} = \frac{300{,}000 \times 1000 \text{ kg}}{870 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

$$V_c = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{870} = 344{,}827.6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the loading profile into three phases.**

Let phase 1 = ramp-up (triangular area), phase 2 = constant, phase 3 = ramp-down.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot t_1 \cdot Q_{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.5 \text{ h}) \cdot 12{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \\
    &= 3{,}000 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_3 &= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot t_3 \cdot Q_{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot (0.5 \text{ h}) \cdot 12{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \\
    &= 3{,}000 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for constant-phase volume and duration.**

$$V_2 = V_c - V_1 - V_3 = 344{,}827.6 - 3{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 338{,}827.6 \text{ m}^3$$

$$t_2 = \frac{V_2}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{338{,}827.6}{12{,}000} = 28.236 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Total loading time.**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 0.5 + 28.236 + 0.5 = 29.236 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{total}} \approx 29.24 \text{ hours}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Inert Gas Oxygen Limit Verification

**Problem:** A tanker's IGS must maintain **O₂ < 5% by volume**. Combustion triangle: **Fuel + O₂ + Ignition Source**. Show mathematically why O₂ < 5% prevents combustion of crude vapor (LFL = 0.6%).

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric oxygen requirement for typical hydrocarbon (assumed C₁₀H₂₂, decane).**

$$2\,\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{22} + 31\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 20\,\text{CO}_2 + 22\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — For a stoichiometric fuel-air mixture, the required O₂ fraction is ~ 21% (since air is 21% O₂).**

**Step 3 — Below the LFL (0.6% fuel), even a stoichiometric ratio cannot be reached because there's too little fuel. Above the LFL but with O₂ < 5%, the mixture is fuel-rich but oxygen-starved.**

$$\text{If } O_2 < 5\% \text{ in the gas space, then for any fuel concentration,}$$
$$\text{the reaction is limited by } O_2. \text{ No complete combustion possible.}$$

This is why IGS is a **passive, fail-safe** safeguard — it removes one leg of the fire triangle.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hazard vs. Safeguard

| Hazard | Root Cause | Primary Safeguard | Secondary Safeguard | KTU Weight |
|--------|------------|--------------------|----------------------|------------|
| Fire in cargo tank | Static discharge / hot work | Inert gas (O₂ < 5%) | Bonding, no smoking | High |
| Explosion in pump room | Vapor leak | Vapor detection alarms | Ventilation, CO₂ flooding | High |
| Hull breach (collision) | Navigation error | Double hull, SBT | AIS, radar, pilot | High |
| Overfill | Gauge failure | Level alarms (90/95/98%) | Independent gauging | Medium |
| Sludge buildup | Wax/asphaltenes | Crude Oil Washing | Shore reception | Medium |
| Oil spill at terminal | Manifold leak | Dry-break couplers | Drip trays, boom | Medium |
| Toxic exposure (H₂S) | Sour crude | H₂S sensors (10/15 ppm) | SCBA, decon showers | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tanker Cross-Section (Double Hull + IGS + COW)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Atmosphere["Atmospheric Inlet with P/V Breaker"]
    IGS["Inert Gas Scrubber & Blower"]
    DeckTop["Main Deck - Foamed & Gas Detection"]
    Manifold["Manifold with ESD-1 / ESD-2 Valves"]
    PipeLine["Cargo Pipeline - Stainless"]
    Tank1["Cargo Tank 1 - Double Hull Boundary"]
    Tank2["Cargo Tank 2 - Double Hull Boundary"]
    Tank3["Cargo Tank 3 - Double Hull Boundary"]
    SBT["Segregated Ballast Tank"]
    DoubleHull["Void / Double Hull Space"]
    COWNozzle["Crude Oil Washing Rotating Nozzle"]
    PumpRoom["Pump Room - CO2 Protected"]
    SlopTank["Slop Tank - OWS Effluent"]
    OWS["Oily Water Separator - 15 ppm Limit"]
    EngineRoom["Engine Room - Isolated Ventilation"]

    Atmosphere --> IGS --> DeckTop
    DeckTop --> Manifold --> PipeLine
    PipeLine --> Tank1
    PipeLine --> Tank2
    PipeLine --> Tank3
    Tank1 -. vapor space .-> DeckTop
    Tank2 -. vapor space .-> DeckTop
    Tank3 -. vapor space .-> DeckTop
    Tank1 -. interior wash .-> COWNozzle
    Tank2 -. interior wash .-> COWNozzle
    Tank3 -. interior wash .-> COWNozzle
    Tank1 --- SBT
    Tank2 --- SBT
    Tank3 --- SBT
    Tank1 --- DoubleHull
    Tank2 --- DoubleHull
    Tank3 --- DoubleHull
    PipeLine --> PumpRoom --> SlopTank --> OWS
    PumpRoom --- EngineRoom
```

## 4.2 Marine Loading Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Vessel Arrives] --> A2[Notice of Readiness Tendered]
    A2 --> A3[ISGOTT Ship-Shore Check]
    A3 --> A4{Mooring Lines Passed}
    A4 --> A5[Bonding & Earthing]
    A5 --> A6[Manifold Connection]
    A6 --> A7[Tank Inspection]
    A7 --> A8{Pre-Load Conference}
    A8 --> A9[Cargo Transfer Begins]
    A9 --> A10[Crude Oil Washing Concurrent]
    A10 --> A11[Topping Off at Reduced Rate]
    A11 --> A12[Independent Gauging]
    A12 --> A13[Bill of Lading Signed]
    A13 --> A14[Manifold Disconnected]
    A14 --> A15[Vessel Departs]
```

## 4.3 Fire Triangle & IGS Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Fuel - Crude Vapor] --> Tri{"FIRE TRIANGLE"}
    O1[Oxygen from Air] --> Tri
    I1[Ignition Source - Static / Spark] --> Tri
    Tri --> Hazard[Fire or Explosion in Tank]
    Hazard --> Mitigation1[Safeguard: IGS removes O2 lt 5 percent]
    Hazard --> Mitigation2[Safeguard: Bonding removes ignition]
    Hazard --> Mitigation3[Safeguard: Vapor recovery removes fuel]
    Mitigation1 --> Safe[Inherently Safe Operation]
    Mitigation2 --> Safe
    Mitigation3 --> Safe
```

## 4.4 Regulatory Hierarchy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IMO["International Maritime Organization UN Body"]
    SOLAS["SOLAS Convention Life Safety"]
    MARPOL["MARPOL Annex I Oil Pollution"]
    ISGOTT["ISGOTT Operations Standard"]
    IACS["IACS Class Rules Hull & Machinery"]
    ISM["ISM Code Safety Management"]
    FlagState["Flag State Inspections"]
    PortState["Port State Control Inspections"]
    ClassificationSoc["Classification Society Surveys Annual Special Continuous"]

    IMO --> SOLAS
    IMO --> MARPOL
    IMO --> ISM
    SOLAS --> ISGOTT
    MARPOL --> ISGOTT
    ISGOTT --> FlagState
    ISGOTT --> PortState
    SOLAS --> ClassificationSoc
    MARPOL --> ClassificationSoc
    ISM --> ClassificationSoc
    ClassificationSoc --> IACS
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) of a crude oil tanker. How does it differ from Lightweight and Displacement?**

**Model Answer:**
DWT (Deadweight Tonnage) is the total mass a vessel can carry when fully loaded, expressed in metric tonnes. It includes cargo, fuel oil, diesel oil, lubricating oil, fresh water, ballast water, crew, and provisions.

- **Lightweight (LWT):** The empty ship's displacement (hull + machinery + outfit).
- **Displacement (Δ):** The actual mass of water the ship pushes aside at any given draft.

**Relationship:**
$$\text{DWT} = \Delta_{\text{loaded}} - \Delta_{\text{light}}$$

For a VLCC, DWT ≈ **300,000 tonnes** while Lightweight ≈ **40,000 tonnes**, meaning cargo capacity is roughly **260,000 tonnes**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Why is the Inert Gas System (IGS) mandatory on all new crude oil tankers > 20,000 DWT? State the O₂ threshold.**

**Model Answer:**
The IGS is mandated by **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 62** for tankers above 20,000 DWT carrying crude oil or petroleum products with flash point ≤ 60 °C.

**Reasons:**
1. Cargo tanks contain flammable hydrocarbon vapors (within the LFL–UFL range: 0.6% – 8% by volume).
2. If air ingresses during voyage (due to temperature variation), the tank atmosphere can pass through the **flammable envelope**.
3. IGS maintains **O₂ concentration < 5% by volume**, which keeps the mixture permanently below the **minimum oxygen for combustion (~ 10% for most hydrocarbons)**.
4. This is a **passive, fail-safe** engineering control, independent of human action.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has **internal choice**. Below are TWO independent alternatives (A and B).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the constructional features of a modern **double-hull crude oil tanker**, with a neat sketch showing ballast tanks, cargo tanks, cofferdams, and pump room. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **hazards during ship-shore cargo transfer** and the corresponding **ISGOTT Ship-Shore Safety Checklist** controls. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Constructional Features (7 marks):**

A modern double-hull crude tanker (mandatory post-MARPOL Annex I for tankers contracted after 1993) consists of:

| Component | Function | Typical Dimension |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Outer hull** | Watertight boundary, structural strength | 30–40 mm plate |
| **Inner hull (shell)** | Secondary barrier, forms cargo tank wall | 20–30 mm |
| **Double-bottom ballast tanks** | Provide ballast without contaminating cargo | 1.5–2.5 m height |
| **Wing ballast tanks** | Side ballast, stability | 2.0–3.0 m width |
| **Cargo tanks** | Hold crude oil (slop tanks separate) | 6–8 tanks |
| **Cofferdams** | Gas-tight isolation between cargo/foothold/empty spaces | 600–900 mm |
| **Pump room** | Centrifugal cargo pumps, CO₂ protected | Aft of cargo area |
| **Forepeak** | Collision bulkhead separation | Bow protection |

The double hull provides:
- **Redundancy** if outer hull breaches.
- **Pollution prevention** under normal grounding.
- **Structural stiffness** for larger tankers (VLCC).

**(b) Hazards & ISGOTT Controls (7 marks):**

| Hazard | Cause | ISGOTT Control |
|--------|-------|----------------|
| Fire/explosion at manifold | Static discharge during bonding failure | Verified bonding cable + earthing |
| Overpressure in hose | Pump sudden stoppage | ESD-1 (ship), ESD-2 (shore) valves |
| Spill during connection | Splashing from open flow | Dry-break couplers, drip trays |
| Slips/falls of crew | Wet deck | Non-skid surfaces, lighting |
| Toxic vapor exposure | Crude H₂S release | Personal H₂S monitor, SCSR |
| Collision with terminal | Inadequate mooring | Sufficient mooring lines, tug assist |

The **Ship-Shore Safety Checklist** (28 items, ISGOTT) covers:
1. Mooring adequacy
2. Bonding/earthing verification
3. Inert gas status (O₂ < 5%)
4. Communication (VHF + backup)
5. Fire-fighting equipment status
6. Manifold condition
7. Emergency shutdown functionality
8. No smoking enforcement

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Crude Oil Washing (COW)** process. List its advantages over conventional seawater washing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A VLCC is loading **320,000 tonnes** of crude oil (density **880 kg/m³**). The cargo pumps ramp up linearly to **15,000 m³/h** in 45 minutes, hold constant, then ramp down to zero in the last 45 minutes. Calculate the **total loading time in hours**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Crude Oil Washing (COW) — 7 marks:**

Crude Oil Washing is the process of using **the cargo itself** (instead of seawater) as the cleaning medium for cargo tank interiors during loading.

**Procedure:**
1. Tankers fitted with **portable or fixed washing machines** (Butterworth-type) are mounted inside each cargo tank.
2. As crude is loaded, the machines are operated sequentially, spraying crude at **6–9 kg/cm² pressure** through rotating nozzles.
3. The jet dissolves **wax, asphaltenes, and sludge** deposits on tank walls.
4. The dissolved residue is carried away with the cargo, eliminating shore disposal.

**Advantages over seawater washing:**
- **Eliminates oily sludge disposal problem** (saves ~$50,000–100,000 per voyage).
- **Better cleaning action** — crude has higher solvency for waxes.
- **Reduces corrosion** — no salt deposits.
- **Reduces air pollution** — no oily sludge discharge at sea.
- **Shorter port turnaround time.**

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Cargo volume.**
$$V_c = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{320{,}000 \times 1000}{880} = 363{,}636.4 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Volume during ramp-up.**
$$V_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times t_1 \times Q_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.75 \text{ h} \times 15{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 5{,}625 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Volume during ramp-down.**
$$V_3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.75 \times 15{,}000 = 5{,}625 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Constant-phase volume.**
$$V_2 = V_c - V_1 - V_3 = 363{,}636.4 - 5{,}625 - 5{,}625 = 352{,}386.4 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Constant-phase duration.**
$$t_2 = \frac{V_2}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{352{,}386.4}{15{,}000} = 23.49 \text{ h}$$

**Step 6 — Total loading time.**
$$t_{\text{total}} = 0.75 + 23.49 + 0.75 = \boxed{24.99 \text{ hours} \approx 25 \text{ hours}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DWT with cargo capacity** — Always subtract lightweight.
> 2. **Forgetting the O₂ < 5% threshold in IGS** — This is a one-line answer worth 1 mark; missing it costs you.
> 3. **Using wrong API gravity in density conversion** — Remember: ρ (kg/m³) = 141.5 / (131.5 + °API).
> 4. **Skipping unit conversions** — Density in kg/m³, mass in tonnes (×1000 to kg).
> 5. **Ignoring the ISGOTT Checklist** — KTU examiners specifically look for the 28-item reference.
> 6. **Not mentioning ESD-1 / ESD-2 valves** — These are critical emergency shutdown devices at ship and shore respectively.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Transport of Crude by Sea**

- [x] **Tanker classes:** Handysize < Aframax < Suezmax < VLCC < ULCC
- [x] **DWT formula:** $\text{DWT} = \Delta_{\text{loaded}} - \Delta_{\text{light}}$
- [x] **Double hull** mandatory for tankers > 600 DWT (MARPOL 19)
- [x] **IGS O₂ threshold:** < **5%** by volume (SOLAS II-2)
- [x] **LFL/UFL of crude vapor:** **0.6% / 8%** by volume
- [x] **Flash point of crude:** typically < 60 °C (Class I flammable)
- [x] **Auto-ignition temp of crude:** ~ **250 °C**
- [x] **COW** uses crude itself as washing medium at **6–9 kg/cm²**
- [x] **SBT** required for tankers > 20,000 DWT
- [x] **ESD-1** at ship, **ESD-2** at shore, **ESD-3** at terminal
- [x] **ISGOTT** = 28-item Ship-Shore Safety Checklist
- [x] **MARPOL Annex I** governs oil pollution prevention
- [x] **SOLAS Chapter II-2** governs fire safety
- [x] **OWS** (Oily Water Separator) limit: **15 ppm** overboard discharge
- [x] **Bonding + earthing** before any transfer to dissipate static
- [x] **Trim tolerance:** list ≤ 0.5°, draft tolerance ≤ 0.3 m
- [x] **Crude density range:** **860–930 kg/m³**
- [x] **Critical case studies for KTU:** *Exxon Valdez* (1989), *Prestige* (2002), *Torrey Canyon* (1967)
- [x] **Loading-rate linear ramp** problems require $V = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot t \cdot Q_{\max}$ for triangular phases

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crude Oil Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crude Oil Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crude Oil Hazards** refer to the complete spectrum of physical, chemical, toxicological, reactivity, and environmental dangers inherently associated with the production, transportation, storage, refining, and handling of raw petroleum (crude oil). Crude oil is a naturally occurring, flammable, complex liquid mixture of hydrocarbons (predominantly paraffins, naphthenes, and aromatics), heterocyclic compounds (containing sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen), and trace heavy metals, whose hazards are governed primarily by its **API Gravity**, **Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP)**, **sulfur content (H₂S)**, and **aromatic content (BTEX/PAH)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, crude oil is categorized as a **Class IB Flammable Liquid** under the **NFPA 30 (Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code)** and is regulated under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106** and **OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) Standard 118**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Think of crude oil as a **"volatile chemical cocktail bottled in a pressurized container."** Just like shaking a soda bottle creates dissolved gas that violently escapes when opened, crude oil contains dissolved light hydrocarbon gases (methane, butane, propane) under reservoir pressure. When this pressure is suddenly released at the wellhead, storage tank, or pipeline, the dissolved gases flash out and create an invisible, flammable, and often toxic (H₂S-containing) vapor cloud — exactly the same principle that causes refinery explosions and tank farm fires.

Consider a leaking kitchen LPG cylinder. The danger is not the liquid you see, but the invisible vapor cloud that travels along the ground, finds an ignition source (a stove, a switch), and flashes back. **Crude oil behaves similarly, but on a massive industrial scale, with added toxicity, corrosivity, and chronic health hazards.**

## 1.3 Classification of Crude Oil Hazards (Master Framework)

Crude oil hazards are categorized into **six primary domains**, each with sub-hazards:

| S.No | Hazard Domain | Primary Sub-Hazards |
|------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Fire & Explosion** | Low flash point, flammability envelope, vapor cloud explosion (VCE), Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE), pool fire, jet fire |
| 2 | **Health / Toxicological** | Acute H₂S poisoning, benzene-induced leukemia, PAH carcinogenicity, narcosis from hydrocarbons, dermatitis |
| 3 | **Reactivity / Chemical** | Auto-oxidation, peroxide formation, polymerization, incompatibility with oxidizers, pyrophoric iron sulfide formation |
| 4 | **Physical / Mechanical** | Asphyxiation in confined spaces, slipping on oil films, hot/cold burn from high-RVP crude, static charge accumulation |
| 5 | **Environmental** | Marine oil spills, soil contamination, groundwater pollution, air emissions (VOC, BTEX), firewater runoff |
| 6 | **Corrosion / Material** | Sour crude H₂S cracking (SCC), naphthenic acid corrosion (NAC), microbiologically influenced corrosion (MIC) |

## 1.4 Physical & Chemical Properties Governing Hazard Severity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hazard profile of any crude oil is **NOT fixed** — it changes with temperature, pressure, age, and exposure history. The four governing properties a KTU examiner expects you to identify are:
> 1. **API Gravity** (lightness/heaviness)
> 2. **Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP)** (volatility)
> 3. **Sulfur / H₂S Content** (sour vs. sweet)
> 4. **Aromatic / BTEX / PAH Content** (toxicity)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hazard #1 — Fire and Explosion Hazards

Crude oil is a **multi-component flammable liquid**. Unlike pure substances (e.g., ethanol), it does NOT have a single flash point — it has a **flash point range** because lighter fractions flash first, followed by heavier fractions as temperature rises.

### 2.1.1 Flash Point and Fire Point

- **Flash Point (FP):** The minimum temperature at which a liquid gives off sufficient vapor to form an **ignitable mixture** with air **near the liquid surface** (within a standard test cup). At FP, vapor production is JUST enough to flash but NOT sustain combustion.
- **Fire Point:** The temperature at which the vapor production rate is sufficient to **sustain continuous combustion** for at least **5 seconds** upon ignition. Fire Point is typically **5–10 °C higher** than Flash Point for crude oils.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** "Flash = momentary burn, Fire = continuous burn."

### 2.1.2 Flammability Limits

Every flammable vapor-air mixture has two concentration boundaries:

- **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)** — minimum vapor concentration in air below which the mixture is **too lean** to burn.
- **UEL (Upper Explosive Limit)** — maximum vapor concentration in air above which the mixture is **too rich** (insufficient oxygen) to burn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For crude oil vapors, **LEL ≈ 1% by volume**, **UEL ≈ 8–10% by volume** (varies with composition). This is the *flammability envelope* — any concentration between LEL and UEL is explosive if ignited.

### 2.1.3 Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)

The minimum temperature at which crude oil vapors **self-ignite without an external spark or flame**. For crude oil, **AIT ≈ 230–280 °C (446–536 °F)**. This is critical for hot-work permits and burner management systems.

### 2.1.4 Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)

When a confined crude oil tank is exposed to external fire, the liquid heats, vapor pressure rises exponentially, and the tank ruptures catastrophically. Prevention: **water spray cooling, relief valves, foam blanketing**.

### 2.1.5 Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)

A large release of crude oil vapor (e.g., from a pipeline rupture) forms a drifting cloud. If the cloud reaches an ignition source while still within the **flammability envelope**, it ignites and produces a damaging pressure wave (overpressure). Example: **Hindustan Petroleum Visakh Refinery fire (1997)**.

---

## 2.2 Hazard #2 — Toxicological / Health Hazards

### 2.2.1 Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S) Poisoning — The Silent Killer

> [!WARNING]
> **H₂S is the #1 acute fatality agent in the hydrocarbon industry.** Crude oils containing >50 ppm H₂S in the gas phase are classified as **"Sour Crude"** (per API specs). Sweet crude has <50 ppm H₂S.

**Physiological effects of H₂S (graduated):**

| H₂S Concentration (ppm) | Effect |
|--------------------------|--------|
| 0.02 – 0.5 | Detectable rotten-egg odor |
| 10 – 50 | Olfactory fatigue (nose goes numb — FALSE sense of safety) |
| 100 – 200 | Loss of smell, eye irritation |
| 250 – 500 | Pulmonary edema after 4–8 hours exposure |
| 500 – 700 | Loss of consciousness within 30 minutes |
| 700 – 1000 | Rapid unconsciousness, respiratory paralysis |
| **>1000** | **Instant "knockdown" — single breath can kill** |

**OSHA PEL = 10 ppm (8-hr TWA)**, **STEL = 15 ppm**.

### 2.2.2 BTEX (Benzene, Toluene, Ethylbenzene, Xylenes)

These aromatic hydrocarbons are present in crude oil at varying concentrations and are **chronic toxins / carcinogens**:

- **Benzene**: Group 1 carcinogen (IARC). Causes **Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)**. OSHA PEL = **1 ppm (8-hr TWA)**, STEL = 5 ppm. Listed under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1028**.
- **Toluene**: CNS depressant, causes neurotoxicity on chronic exposure.
- **Ethylbenzene**: Probable carcinogen, liver/kidney damage.
- **Xylenes**: CNS effects, mucosal irritation.

### 2.2.3 Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH)

High-molecular-weight PAHs like **benzo[a]pyrene** in heavy crude fractions (asphalt, bunker fuel) are **potent carcinogens** and **mutagens**. Skin contact causes **chloracne, hyperpigmentation, and skin cancer**.

### 2.2.4 Narcosis and Asphyxiation

Light hydrocarbon vapors (C1–C4) act as **CNS depressants**, causing narcotic effects ("drunkenness," dizziness, loss of coordination). In confined spaces (tanks, pits), they displace oxygen → **simple asphyxiation** (O₂ < 19.5%).

---

## 2.3 Hazard #3 — Reactivity / Chemical Incompatibility

### 2.3.1 Auto-Oxidation and Peroxide Formation

Crude oils containing olefins, conjugated dienes, and certain sulfur compounds undergo **auto-oxidation** when exposed to air, forming **hydroperoxides** and **polyperoxides**. These are:
- Shock-sensitive
- Self-accelerating decomposition (SADT)
- Fire/explosion hazards during storage

### 2.3.2 Pyrophoric Iron Sulfide (FeS)

In sour crude systems, iron reacts with H₂S to form **iron sulfide (FeS)**. When a process unit is opened for maintenance, FeS contacts air and **spontaneously ignites at room temperature** (pyrophoric), causing fires inside tanks and vessels. **Prevention: wet-down maintenance, oxygen-controlled entry.**

### 2.3.3 Incompatibility with Oxidizers

Crude oil + strong oxidizers (concentrated H₂O₂, nitric acid, perchlorates, liquid oxygen) → **violent exothermic reactions**. NFPA 30 prohibits co-storage.

---

## 2.4 Hazard #4 — Physical / Mechanical Hazards

### 2.4.1 Static Electricity Accumulation

Crude oil is a poor conductor (resistivity > 10⁹ Ω·m). Pumping, filtering, and splash-filling generate **electrostatic charges** that accumulate on tank walls. If the charge does not dissipate within the **charge relaxation time** (typically 0.5–2 seconds for crude), a spark discharge can ignite vapor. **Bonding and grounding are mandatory** per NFPA 77.

### 2.4.2 Confined Space Hazards

Tank entry requires:
- **O₂ between 19.5% and 23.5%**
- **LEL < 10%** (entry prohibited above 10%)
- **H₂S < 10 ppm**
- **CO < 25 ppm**

---

## 2.5 Hazard #5 — Environmental Hazards

Crude oil spills cause:
- **Marine ecotoxicity** (mortality of fish, seabirds, plankton)
- **Soil hydrophobicity** (water repellence → agricultural land destruction)
- **Groundwater contamination** (benzene leaches into aquifers)
- **Air pollution** (BTEX, VOC, SO₂ during flaring)

**MARPOL 73/78 Annex I** governs marine discharge. **OPA 90 (Oil Pollution Act, USA)** mandates double-hull tankers.

---

## 2.6 Hazard #6 — Corrosion Hazards

| Type | Mechanism | Consequence |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **SSC (Sulfide Stress Cracking)** | H₂S + steel + stress → atomic H → cracking | Catastrophic pipe rupture |
| **HIC (Hydrogen-Induced Cracking)** | Atomic H diffuses into steel → internal blistering | Pressure vessel failure |
| **NAC (Naphthenic Acid Corrosion)** | High-TAN crude (>0.5 mg KOH/g) + heat → carboxylate attack | Distillation column failure at 220–400 °C |
| **MIC** | Sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) at 30–45 °C | Localized pitting in tanks/pipelines |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all key equations for solving numerical problems on Crude Oil Hazards.** No vertical pipes `|` used inside cells (replaced with `\vert`).

| # | Formula / Property | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|--------------------|----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | API Gravity | $^\circ API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60^\circ F}} - 131.5$ | dimensionless | Higher °API = lighter crude |
| 2 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$ at 60 °F | dimensionless | $SG_{water}$ = 0.999 g/cm³ |
| 3 | Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP) | $RVP = P_{vap} \left(T = 100^\circ F, \dfrac{V}{L} = 4 \right)$ | psi or bar | Higher RVP = higher volatility |
| 4 | True Vapor Pressure (TVP) | $TVP = RVP \cdot e^{C \cdot \Delta T}$ | psi | $C$ ≈ 0.0072 per °F |
| 5 | Vapor Mass Fraction (Raoult's Law) | $y_i = \dfrac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P_{total}}$ | mole fraction | Used for vapor cloud modeling |
| 6 | Flash Point ↔ RVP (API empirical) | $\log_{10} RVP \approx 4.7 - \dfrac{1325}{FP + 460}$ | psi | Used to estimate FP from RVP |
| 7 | Flammability Hazard Index | $FHI = \dfrac{FP_{fuel}}{FP_{ref}}$ | dimensionless | Crude FHI for gasoline > diesel |
| 8 | Lower Flammability Limit | $LEL = \dfrac{100}{n \cdot C_{st}}$ (where n = stoichiometric O₂ ratio) | % vol | Stoichiometric method |
| 9 | Auto-Ignition Temperature | $AIT \approx 230 - 280$ | °C | Crude oil range |
| 10 | Charge Relaxation Time | $\tau = \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 \rho$ | seconds | $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·m) |
| 11 | Heat of Combustion (lower) | $\Delta H_c \approx 10{,}200 \cdot \rho_{oil}$ | kcal/kg | Approximate for crude |
| 12 | Pool Fire Radiation (Thomas) | $q^{\prime\prime}_{max} = 58 \cdot (10^{-0.00823 D})$ | kW/m² | D = pool diameter (m) |
| 13 | BLEVE Overpressure | $\Delta P = k \cdot \dfrac{m_{vapor}}{R^2}$ | bar | k = constant; R = distance |
| 14 | Naphthenic Acid TAN | $TAN = \dfrac{(V_{KOH} \cdot N_{KOH} \cdot 56.1)}{m_{oil}}$ | mg KOH/g | TAN > 0.5 → high NAC risk |
| 15 | H₂S Acute Toxicity Dose | $LCt_{50} = 100 \cdot ppm \cdot min$ | ppm·min | Approx. lethal dose |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: API Gravity and Specific Gravity Inter-Conversion

The American Petroleum Institute (API) defined a gravity scale where **higher °API = lighter (less dense) crude oil**. Crude with °API > 31.1 is "light crude," between 22.3–31.1 is "medium crude," and < 22.3 is "heavy crude."

$$
^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60^\circ F}} - 131.5
$$

**Rearranging for Specific Gravity:**

$$
SG_{60/60^\circ F} = \frac{141.5}{^\circ API + 131.5}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU Typical):**

A crude oil sample has **API Gravity = 32.5°**. Calculate its specific gravity and density at 60 °F in kg/m³.

**Step 1:** Apply the rearranged formula.

$$
SG = \frac{141.5}{32.5 + 131.5}
$$

$$
SG = \frac{141.5}{164.0}
$$

$$
SG = 0.8628
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to density using water density at 60 °F = **999 kg/m³** (often approximated as 1000 kg/m³ in textbooks).

$$
\rho_{oil} = SG \times \rho_{water} = 0.8628 \times 999 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

$$
\rho_{oil} \approx 862.0 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> Since °API = 32.5 > 31.1, this is a **light crude oil**. Light crudes have higher RVP, lower flash point, and **higher fire/explosion hazard**, but are economically more valuable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reid Vapor Pressure and True Vapor Pressure Relation

RVP is measured under standard conditions: **T = 100 °F (37.8 °C)** and **vapor-to-liquid volume ratio (V/L) = 4:1**. In real storage tanks, conditions deviate. The Clausius-Clapeyron / Antoine-type correction gives:

$$
\ln \left( \frac{TVP}{RVP} \right) = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{RVP}} - \frac{1}{T_{tank}} \right)
$$

For crude oil, an empirical linear approximation is widely used:

$$
TVP = RVP \cdot e^{0.0072 \cdot (T_{tank} - 100)} \quad \text{[T in °F]}
$$

**Worked Example:**

A crude has **RVP = 8.0 psi at 100 °F**. Calculate TVP at **120 °F** (summer storage condition).

**Step 1:** Compute temperature difference.

$$
\Delta T = 120 - 100 = 20 \text{ °F}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply empirical equation.

$$
TVP = 8.0 \cdot e^{0.0072 \times 20}
$$

$$
TVP = 8.0 \cdot e^{0.144}
$$

$$
TVP = 8.0 \times 1.1549
$$

$$
TVP \approx 9.24 \text{ psi}
$$

**Step 3:** Safety interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **20 °F rise in storage temperature** increases the vapor pressure by **~15.5%**. This dramatically increases the **flammable vapor inventory** in the tank headspace, raising the probability of reaching LEL and the **VCE risk**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) by Stoichiometric Method

The **stoichiometric method** estimates LEL using the oxygen balance of combustion.

For a hydrocarbon $C_n H_m$:

$$
C_n H_m + \left( n + \dfrac{m}{4} \right) O_2 \rightarrow n CO_2 + \dfrac{m}{2} H_2O
$$

The empirical **Burgess-Wheeler Law** relates LEL to stoichiometric fuel concentration $C_{st}$:

$$
LEL = \frac{C_{st}}{n_{stoich}} \approx \frac{100}{n_{stoich}} \cdot f
$$

where $f$ is an empirical factor (~0.55 for hydrocarbons).

A simpler and widely-used engineering estimate:

$$
LEL \approx \frac{100}{n \cdot C_{st}} \quad (\% \text{ vol})
$$

**Worked Example: Estimate LEL of hexane (C₆H₁₄).**

**Step 1:** Combustion equation.

$$
C_6 H_{14} + \left( 6 + \dfrac{14}{4} \right) O_2 \rightarrow 6 CO_2 + 7 H_2O
$$

$$
C_6 H_{14} + 9.5\, O_2 \rightarrow 6\, CO_2 + 7\, H_2O
$$

**Step 2:** Total moles in stoichiometric mixture.

$$
n_{total} = 1 + 9.5 = 10.5
$$

**Step 3:** Stoichiometric fuel concentration in air (21% O₂).

For 9.5 moles O₂ required → moles of air = $\frac{9.5}{0.21} = 45.24$

$$
C_{st} = \frac{1}{1 + 45.24} \times 100 = \frac{100}{46.24} \approx 2.16 \text{ % vol}
$$

**Step 4:** Empirical LEL estimate.

$$
LEL \approx 0.55 \times C_{st} = 0.55 \times 2.16 \approx 1.19 \text{ % vol}
$$

(Reference value: **LEL of hexane = 1.1 % vol** — close agreement ✓)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Acid Number (TAN) Calculation for Naphthenic Acid Corrosion

**Total Acid Number (TAN)** measures naphthenic acid content, which determines NAC severity.

$$
TAN = \frac{V_{KOH} \times N_{KOH} \times 56.1}{m_{oil}} \quad \text{[mg KOH / g]}
$$

**Worked Example:**

A 5.0 g crude sample is titrated with **0.1 N KOH** solution. Volume consumed = **4.2 mL**. Calculate TAN.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula.

$$
TAN = \frac{4.2 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{5.0}
$$

$$
TAN = \frac{23.562}{5.0}
$$

$$
TAN \approx 4.71 \text{ mg KOH/g}
$$

**Step 2:** Interpret the result.

> [!WARNING]
> TAN = 4.71 mg KOH/g is **VERY HIGH** (threshold for NAC risk is **TAN > 0.5**). This crude is a **naphthenic acid "gobbler"** — at distillation temperatures of 220–400 °C, naphthenic acids aggressively corrode **carbon steel**, requiring **317L / 316L stainless steel** in critical zones like furnace tubes, transfer lines, and column internals.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: H₂S "Lethal Dose" Estimation and Permissible Exposure Comparison

The acute lethality of H₂S is characterized by **Haber's Law** for dose-response:

$$
Dose = C \times t \quad [\text{ppm} \cdot \text{min}]
$$

**Approximate lethal dose:** $LCt_{50} \approx 100 \text{ ppm} \cdot \text{min}$

**Worked Example:**

A worker is exposed to **H₂S = 250 ppm** for **10 minutes** during a tank inspection. Determine if exposure exceeds the lethal threshold.

**Step 1:** Compute dose.

$$
Dose = 250 \times 10 = 2500 \text{ ppm} \cdot \text{min}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with lethal dose.

$$
\frac{2500}{100} = 25 \times \text{lethal dose}
$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exposure is **25 times** the lethal dose. The worker would have suffered **rapid unconsciousness (knockdown)** and likely death within minutes. This incident is a **Catastrophic Toxic Exposure** under OSHA and would trigger the **PSM (Process Safety Management) — 29 CFR 1910.119** Standard. Immediate lessons: continuous H₂S monitoring with audible alarms set at **10 ppm** (action) and **15 ppm** (evacuation), SCBA use, and buddy system in confined spaces.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Hazard Severity vs. Crude Type

| Property / Hazard | Light Crude (API > 31) | Medium Crude (API 22–31) | Heavy Crude (API < 22) |
|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Fire Hazard** | **HIGH** (low FP, high RVP) | Moderate | Lower FP, but hot handling |
| **H₂S Toxicity** | Variable | Often high | Often low |
| **BTEX / Aromatic Toxicity** | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| **Naphthenic Acid (TAN)** | Low | High | Very High (NAC risk) |
| **PAH / Carcinogenicity** | Low | Moderate | **Very High** (skin contact risk) |
| **Asphaltene / Sludge** | Low | Moderate | High (tank cleaning hazard) |
| **Static Electricity** | **Very High** (low conductivity) | High | Moderate |
| **Spill Clean-up Difficulty** | Easy (evaporates) | Moderate | Hard (persistent) |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Hazard Control Hierarchy (Per KTU / OSHA NEP 2024)

| Hierarchy Level | Control Measure | Applied to Crude Oil Hazard |
|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------------------|
| **1. Elimination** | Substitute with safer feedstock | Not feasible — crude oil is the raw material |
| **2. Substitution** | Use stabilized crude (low RVP) when possible | Reduces VCE / BLEVE risk |
| **3. Engineering Control** | Inert gas blanketing (N₂) on tanks, vapor recovery, foam systems, H₂S scrubbers | Primary defense |
| **4. Administrative Control** | Hot-work permits, confined space entry procedures, LOTO, training | Layered defense |
| **5. PPE** | SCBA, chemical-resistant suits, H₂S monitors, FR clothing | Last line of defense |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Crude Oil Hazard Causal Chain

This block diagram maps how a single operational deviation (e.g., a flange leak) propagates through the six hazard domains:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil Source<br>Wellhead / Tanker / Pipeline] --> B[Property Profile<br>API Gravity, RVP, H2S, TAN, BTEX]
    B --> C1[Fire Hazard Domain<br>Low FP, LEL/UEL, AIT]
    B --> C2[Toxicology Domain<br>H2S, BTEX, PAH]
    B --> C3[Reactivity Domain<br>Peroxides, Pyrophoric FeS]
    B --> C4[Physical Domain<br>Static, Asphyxiation]
    B --> C5[Environment Domain<br>Spill, VOC Emission]
    B --> C6[Corrosion Domain<br>SSC, HIC, NAC, MIC]
    C1 --> D[Operational Trigger<br>Leak, Overfill, Hot Work]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E[Initiating Event<br>Loss of Containment]
    E --> F1[Vapor Cloud Explosion]
    E --> F2[Pool Fire / Jet Fire]
    E --> F3[BLEVE]
    E --> F4[Acute Toxic Exposure]
    E --> F5[Chronic Health Effect]
    E --> F6[Environmental Damage]
    F1 --> G[Emergency Response<br>ESD, Foam, Evacuation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    G --> H[Recovery & Lessons<br>Incident Investigation]
    H --> I[Design Improvement<br>Inherently Safer Design]
    I --> A
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Sour Crude Operational Hazard Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Upstream]
        S1A[Wellhead Separator] --> S1B[Sour Crude Stabilizer]
    end
    subgraph S2[Storage]
        S2A[Fixed Roof Tank] --> S2B[Internal Floating Roof]
    end
    subgraph S3[Hazards Detected]
        S3A[H2S Release]
        S3B[BTEX Vapor Release]
        S3C[Static Charge Buildup]
        S3D[Pyrophoric FeS Formation]
    end
    subgraph S4[Controls Deployed]
        S4A[Continuous H2S Monitor]
        S4B[LEL Gas Detector]
        S4C[Bonding and Grounding]
        S4D[Wet Down Maintenance]
        S4E[SCBA PPE]
    end
    S1B --> S2A
    S2A --> S2B
    S2A --> S3A
    S2A --> S3B
    S2B --> S3C
    S2B --> S3D
    S3A --> S4A
    S3A --> S4E
    S3B --> S4B
    S3C --> S4C
    S3D --> S4D
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Hazard Control Hierarchy of Controls

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Level 1: Elimination<br>Not Practically Possible] --> H2[Level 2: Substitution<br>Use Stabilized Low RVP Crude]
    H2 --> H3[Level 3: Engineering Controls<br>N2 Blanketing, Foam, Scrubber, Vapor Recovery]
    H3 --> H4[Level 4: Administrative Controls<br>Permits, Training, LOTO, Confined Space Procedure]
    H4 --> H5[Level 5: Personal Protective Equipment<br>SCBA, Chemical Suit, H2S Monitor, FR Clothing]
    H5 --> H6[Residual Risk<br>Acceptable Level]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — BLEVE Causal Sequence in Crude Oil Storage Tank

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[External Fire Engulfs Tank] --> P2[Wall Heats Above Design Temperature]
    P2 --> P3[Liquid Hydrocarbon Vaporizes Rapidly]
    P3 --> P4[Vapor Pressure Exceeds Relief Setpoint]
    P4 --> P5A[Safety Valve Opens and Vents]
    P4 --> P5B[Vent Capacity Insufficient]
    P5B --> P6[Structural Failure of Tank Shell]
    P6 --> P7[Catastrophic Rupture BLEVE]
    P7 --> P8[Fireball and Missile Projection]
    P8 --> P9[Onsite Casualties and Asset Loss]
    P5A --> P10[Pressure Stabilizes Tank Survives]
```
