# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Differentiate between "Sweet Crude" and "Sour Crude" with reference to H₂S content and the associated acute health hazard. Mention the OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for H₂S.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Sweet Crude:** Contains H₂S < 50 ppm in the gaseous phase (per API classification). Considered relatively safer for refining and processing.
>
> **Sour Crude:** Contains H₂S > 50 ppm in the gaseous phase. Requires dedicated H₂S removal (amine treating, Claus process) and stringent handling controls.
>
> **Acute Health Hazard of H₂S:** Rapidly causes olfactory fatigue (loss of smell at >50 ppm), leading to a false sense of safety. At concentrations >700 ppm, a single breath can cause "knockdown" — sudden unconsciousness and death due to respiratory paralysis (cytochrome oxidase inhibition in mitochondria).
>
> **OSHA PEL for H₂S:** **10 ppm (8-hr TWA)**, STEL = **15 ppm** (15-min).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**Define (a) Flash Point, (b) Fire Point, and (c) Auto-Ignition Temperature. Why is crude oil classified as Class IB Flammable Liquid under NFPA 30?**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Flash Point:** The minimum temperature at which a liquid produces enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with air near its surface, causing a momentary flash upon application of an ignition source, without sustained combustion.
>
> **(b) Fire Point:** The temperature at which the liquid produces enough vapor to sustain continuous combustion for at least 5 seconds after ignition. Typically 5–10 °C higher than FP.
>
> **(c) Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** The minimum temperature at which the vapor-air mixture ignites spontaneously without any external ignition source. For crude oil, AIT ≈ 230–280 °C.
>
> **NFPA 30 Classification:** Crude oil has a flash point typically between **60–93 °C** (Class IB range: FP ≥ 37.8 °C and < 93.3 °C). Hence it is classified as **Class IB Flammable Liquid**, requiring explosion-proof electrical fittings, bonded/grounded transfer, and foam-protected storage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`

**Q.A.** (a) Describe the **six major hazard domains** of crude oil with **at least two examples each** from real refinery/upstream operations. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> The six major hazard domains of crude oil are:
>
> **1. Fire and Explosion Hazards**
> - *Example 1:* Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) due to LPG/light crude leak from storage tank (e.g., Jaipur IOC terminal fire, 2009).
> - *Example 2:* BLEVE of a heated storage tank exposed to external pool fire.
>
> **2. Toxicological / Health Hazards**
> - *Example 1:* Acute H₂S poisoning in upstream well-testing — leads to knockdown and fatality without respiratory protection.
> - *Example 2:* Chronic benzene exposure among refinery laboratory workers leading to Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML).
>
> **3. Reactivity / Chemical Hazards**
> - *Example 1:* Pyrophoric iron sulfide (FeS) ignition during maintenance of a sour crude tank.
> - *Example 2:* Auto-oxidation and peroxide buildup in light crude storage, leading to explosion during hot work.
>
> **4. Physical / Mechanical Hazards**
> - *Example 1:* Electrostatic discharge during tanker loading causing tank farm fire.
> - *Example 2:* Asphyxiation of worker entering a confined space crude oil tank with displaced oxygen.
>
> **5. Environmental Hazards**
> - *Example 1:* Marine oil spill from tanker grounding (e.g., MV Wakashio, Mauritius 2020).
> - *Example 2:* VOC/BTEX air emission from floating roof tank rim seal failures.
>
> **6. Corrosion / Material Hazards**
> - *Example 1:* Sulfide Stress Cracking (SSC) of carbon steel pipe in sour crude service causing pipeline rupture.
> - *Example 2:* Naphthenic Acid Corrosion (NAC) attack on crude distillation overhead at 280–350 °C.

---

(b) A crude oil sample has API Gravity of **28.5°** and Reid Vapor Pressure of **6.5 psi**. Estimate its specific gravity, density (kg/m³), and discuss its hazard classification. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1: Specific Gravity**
>
> $$
> SG = \frac{141.5}{^\circ API + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{28.5 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{160.0}
> $$
>
> $$
> SG = 0.884
> $$
>
> **Step 2: Density**
>
> $$
> \rho = SG \times \rho_{water} = 0.884 \times 999 \approx 883 \text{ kg/m}^3
> $$
>
> **Step 3: Classification**
>
> - API = 28.5° → **Medium Crude** (between 22.3 and 31.1)
> - RVP = 6.5 psi → Moderately volatile (typical for medium crude)
> - Hazard Classification: **NFPA 30 Class IB Flammable Liquid**
> - Fire Hazard: Significant — moderate volatility implies moderate FP (<93 °C) and substantial flammable vapor inventory in tank headspace.
> - Static Electricity: High — crude oil resistivity > 10⁹ Ω·m requires bonding/grounding during transfer.
> - Handling Precaution: Foam-protected storage, vapor recovery, explosion-proof electrical fittings, hot-work permit, H₂S monitoring if sour.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**Q.B.** (a) Explain the **mechanism of pyrophoric iron sulfide (FeS) formation** in sour crude systems. Outline the **operational controls** to prevent FeS fires during tank/vessel maintenance. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Mechanism of FeS Formation:**
>
> In sour crude service (H₂S-rich), the iron and iron oxide present on internal surfaces of tanks, vessels, and pipelines react with H₂S at temperatures below 250 °C:
>
> $$
> Fe + H_2 S \rightarrow FeS + H_2
> $$
>
> $$
> Fe_2 O_3 + 3 H_2 S \rightarrow Fe_2 S_3 + 3 H_2 O
> $$
>
> These iron sulfide deposits are stable in the oxygen-free (reducing) environment inside an operating vessel but accumulate as scale on internal walls.
>
> **Pyrophoric Ignition:** When the vessel is opened for maintenance, atmospheric oxygen contacts the FeS scale:
>
> $$
> 4 FeS + 7 O_2 \rightarrow 2 Fe_2 O_3 + 4 SO_2 + \text{Heat}
> $$
>
> This oxidation reaction is highly exothermic. The heat released cannot dissipate quickly, causing the local temperature to exceed the **auto-ignition temperature of FeS (~60–80 °C)**, resulting in spontaneous ignition — a **pyrophoric fire** inside the vessel.
>
> **Operational Controls:**
>
> 1. **Wet-Down Maintenance:** Keep FeS scale continuously wetted with water to prevent oxygen contact.
> 2. **Inert Gas Entry (N₂ Purging):** Maintain inert atmosphere during entry; oxygen < 4%.
> 3. **Pre-Entry Deactivation:** Wash the vessel with alkaline solution or oxidizing chemicals to convert FeS to inert sulfate.
> 4. **Continuous Monitoring:** Use LEL meters and temperature probes during opening.
> 5. **Cold Work Only:** Permit only non-sparking tools; no hot work until confirmed FeS-free.
> 6. **Permit-to-Work System:** Strict administrative control per OISD-105.

---

(b) Discuss the **toxicological profile of BTEX** in crude oil. Calculate the **8-hour TWA exposure** for a refinery laboratory analyst working 6 hours/day in a benzene-laden atmosphere where benzene concentration varies as shown below. Compare with OSHA PEL. (7 Marks)

| Time (hr) | Benzene (ppm) |
|------------|---------------|
| 0 – 1 | 1.5 |
| 1 – 2 | 2.0 |
| 2 – 3 | 1.0 |
| 3 – 4 | 0.5 |
| 4 – 5 | 0.8 |
| 5 – 6 | 1.2 |

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1: BTEX Toxicological Profile**
>
> - **Benzene (C₆H₆):** Group 1 carcinogen (IARC); causes Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), bone marrow depression, chromosomal aberrations. OSHA PEL = 1 ppm (8-hr TWA); STEL = 5 ppm.
> - **Toluene (C₇H₈):** CNS depressant; chronic exposure causes neurotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, hepatic effects.
> - **Ethylbenzene (C₈H₁₀):** Probable human carcinogen (IARC 2B); causes liver and kidney damage.
> - **Xylenes (C₈H₁₀):** CNS depression, mucosal irritation, pulmonary edema at high doses.
>
> **Step 2: TWA Calculation**
>
> $$
> TWA = \frac{\sum (C_i \times t_i)}{8 \text{ hours}}
> $$
>
> $$
> TWA = \frac{(1.5)(1) + (2.0)(1) + (1.0)(1) + (0.5)(1) + (0.8)(1) + (1.2)(1)}{8}
> $$
>
> $$
> TWA = \frac{7.0}{8}
> $$
>
> $$
> TWA = 0.875 \text{ ppm}
> $$
>
> **Step 3: Comparison**
>
> OSHA PEL = 1.0 ppm. TWA = 0.875 ppm → **Within OSHA limit but only marginally safe.**
>
> **Step 4: Interpretation**
>
> Even though TWA is below PEL, **benzene is a carcinogen with no safe threshold** (per ACGIH and NIOSH). Engineering controls (local exhaust ventilation, closed sampling systems) and PPE (organic vapor respirators) must be deployed. Medical surveillance (annual blood count) is mandatory under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1028.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Crude Oil Hazards Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Flash Point and Fire Point:** Students often write them as synonyms. Fire Point is always **higher** than Flash Point and refers to **sustained** combustion.
>
> 2. **Mixing LEL and UEL definitions:** LEL = lean (insufficient fuel). UEL = rich (insufficient oxygen). Reversing these costs marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in API / SG conversion:** Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for correct unit specification (°API, dimensionless SG, kg/m³).
>
> 4. **Treating crude oil as a single substance:** Crude oil has a **flash point range**, not a single value. Stating "the flash point of crude oil is X" without acknowledging compositional variability is a common error.
>
> 5. **Ignoring H₂S chronic and acute distinction:** Examiners expect students to differentiate **acute (rapid knockdown)** vs **chronic (low-level long-term olfactory desensitization)** effects.
>
> 6. **Skipping control hierarchy in answer:** Listing only PPE without mentioning engineering controls (foam, N₂ blanketing, vapor recovery) is considered incomplete.
>
> 7. **Not citing standard references:** Always cite **NFPA 30 / OSHA 29 CFR / OISD / API standards** wherever applicable — examiners reward industrial standard awareness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Crude Oil Hazards (Module 4)**
>
> - [ ] Crude oil is a **Class IB Flammable Liquid (NFPA 30)** with FP between 60–93 °C and AIT ≈ 230–280 °C.
> - [ ] **Six hazard domains**: Fire/Explosion, Toxicological, Reactivity, Physical, Environmental, Corrosion.
> - [ ] **API Gravity formula**: $^\circ API = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$. Light > 31.1°, Medium 22–31°, Heavy < 22°.
> - [ ] **RVP** measured at 100 °F, V/L = 4:1. **TVP** > RVP at higher storage temperatures (empirical correction factor ≈ 0.0072/°F).
> - [ ] **Sour crude**: H₂S > 50 ppm. **Sweet crude**: H₂S < 50 ppm.
> - [ ] **H₂S OSHA PEL = 10 ppm (8-hr TWA), STEL = 15 ppm**. Lethal "knockdown" > 700 ppm.
> - [ ] **Benzene** is a **Group 1 carcinogen (IARC)**; OSHA PEL = 1 ppm; regulated under 29 CFR 1910.1028.
> - [ ] **BLEVE** = fire-induced tank rupture from vapor pressure exceeding structural capacity.
> - [ ] **VCE** = flammable vapor cloud drifting from a leak, ignited in the flammability envelope.
> - [ ] **Pyrophoric FeS** forms in sour systems; ignites spontaneously in air during maintenance → wet-down, N₂ purge mandatory.
> - [ ] **Static electricity**: Crude oil resistivity > 10⁹ Ω·m → bonding/grounding mandatory per NFPA 77.
> - [ ] **TAN > 0.5 mg KOH/g** = high Naphthenic Acid Corrosion risk → 316L/317L stainless required.
> - [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
> - [ ] **Confined space entry criteria**: O₂ 19.5–23.5%, LEL < 10%, H₂S < 10 ppm, CO < 25 ppm.
> - [ ] **MARPOL 73/78 Annex I** and **OPA 90** govern marine oil spills and double-hull tanker design.
> - [ ] **OISD-118** and **OISD-105** are mandatory Indian standards for hydrocarbon storage and maintenance safety.
> - [ ] Always cite **NFPA 30, OSHA 29 CFR 1910, API, and OISD** standards in KTU exam answers for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Petroleum Product Storage and Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Petroleum Product Storage and Transport

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Petroleum Product Storage and Transport** refers to the engineered systems, equipment, procedures, and safety protocols used for the **temporary retention, custody transfer, and movement of crude oil, natural gas liquids (NGL), liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), and refined petroleum products** between the wellhead, refinery, distribution terminals, and end consumers. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this domain is studied under the umbrella of Process Safety Management (PSM) and Loss Prevention Engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Storage and transport constitute the **"intermediate node"** in the hydrocarbon value chain (Upstream → Midstream → Downstream). The safety of these operations is governed by the triad of **Containment, Conveyance, and Catastrophe Prevention (3-C Principle)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **city's water supply system**:
- **Water Treatment Plant** = Oil/Gas Well or Refinery (Source)
- **Overhead Water Tank** = Storage Tank (Buffer/Inventory)
- **Underground Pipelines** = Cross-country pipelines (Bulk transport)
- **Water Tankers** = Road/Rail/Marine tankers (Distribution)
- **Household Taps** = End consumer (Petrol pump, Industry)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the analogy matters:** Just as a city cannot survive with a leaky overhead tank or burst water main, an oil refinery **cannot operate** without safe storage and reliable transport. **Storage** acts as the *operational shock absorber*, while **Transport** acts as the *circulatory system* of the petroleum economy.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **API Gravity of Crude** | **10° to 45°** | Determines storage method |
| **Flash Point (Gasoline)** | **< -40 °C** | Class A Flammable Liquid |
| **Flash Point (Diesel)** | **52 °C to 96 °C** | Class IIIA Combustible |
| **Vapor Pressure of LPG** | **≈ 6.5 bar @ 40 °C** | Mandates pressure storage |
| **Boiling Point of Butane** | **-0.5 °C** | Defines LPG composition |
| **LPG Specific Gravity (Liquid)** | **0.51 to 0.58** | Affects tank hydrostatics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Flammable Liquids (as per NFPA 30 / OISD-118):**
> - **Class IA:** Flash Point < 22.8 °C, Boiling Point < 37.8 °C (e.g., Gasoline, Naphtha)
> - **Class IB:** Flash Point < 22.8 °C, Boiling Point ≥ 37.8 °C (e.g., Benzene)
> - **Class IC:** Flash Point ≥ 22.8 °C and < 37.8 °C (e.g., Turpentine)
> - **Class II:** Flash Point ≥ 37.8 °C and < 60 °C (e.g., Kerosene, Diesel)
> - **Class IIIA:** Flash Point ≥ 60 °C and < 93 °C (e.g., Light Fuel Oil)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Petroleum Storage Systems

Petroleum products are stored based on three cardinal physical properties:
1. **Vapor Pressure (Pᵥ)** – dictates whether atmospheric or pressure storage is required.
2. **Flash Point (FP)** – governs fire protection design.
3. **Boiling Point / Critical Temperature (T꜀)** – determines cryogenic vs. refrigerated storage.

#### 2.1.1 Atmospheric Storage Tanks
Used for liquids with **vapor pressure < 0.5 psia (≈ 0.035 bar)** at storage temperature. These are sub-classified as:

- **Fixed Cone Roof (FCR) Tank** – Used for non-volatile products (diesel, fuel oil, crude). Cost-effective, low maintenance.
- **Fixed Dome Roof** – Used for slightly volatile liquids; reduces evaporation losses by ~30% vs FCR.
- **External Floating Roof (EFR) Tank** – Roof floats on the liquid surface, exposed to atmosphere. Used for **high-vapor-pressure products** (gasoline, naphtha, jet fuel). Evaporation loss reduction up to **90%**.
- **Internal Floating Roof (IFR) Tank** – Floating roof inside a fixed-roof tank. Dual protection against weather and evaporation. Used where climatic conditions are harsh (rains, snow, dust storms).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Difference between EFR and IFR tanks is a recurring 3-mark and 7-mark question. Always mention **drainage**, **rim space**, **shoe seal**, and **vapor loss** as the comparison pillars.

#### 2.1.2 Pressure Storage Vessels
Used when **vapor pressure > atmospheric pressure** (e.g., LPG, propane, butane, propylene).

- **Spherical (Horton Sphere) Vessels** – Most economical for **2000 to 5000 m³** LPG. Optimum pressure range: **5 to 15 bar**. Thicker shell, less surface area per unit volume → reduced heat input during fire.
- **Bullet (Cylindrical Horizontal) Vessels** – Used for **< 200 m³** capacity. Common at bottling plants, small terminals.
- **Cylindrical Vertical Pressure Tanks** – Hybrid between sphere and bullet; used for **200 to 1000 m³** capacity.

#### 2.1.3 Refrigerated / Cryogenic Storage
Used for **LNG (-162 °C), ethylene (-104 °C), LPG at atmospheric pressure (-42 °C)**.
- Double-walled tank with **perlite or vacuum insulation**.
- Inner tank: **9% Ni steel** (LNG) or **aluminum alloy** (LNG small scale).
- Outer tank: Carbon steel.

#### 2.1.4 Underground Storage
- **Underground Mined Caverns** – Strategic Petroleum Reserves (e.g., India’s Vishakhapatnam SPR stores **5.33 MMT** crude).
- **Salt Caverns** – Excellent for LPG and NGL.
- **Buried Pipelines & Tanks** – For retail fuel stations (USTs – double-walled).

### 2.2 Transportation Methods

#### 2.2.1 Cross-Country Pipelines
- **Trunk Pipelines (Long Distance):** 16" to 48" diameter, operating at **30 to 100 bar**.
- **Sub-sea Pipelines:** Used in offshore operations.
- **Flow Assurance:** Pigging operations, insulation, chemical injection.

#### 2.2.2 Road Transportation
- **Tank Trucks (Tankers):** Carbon steel or aluminum, capacities **5 kL to 35 kL**.
- Hazard: **rollover**, **SIDE whip**, **Bleve risk** for LPG.
- **Bottom loading** is preferred (vs. top loading) for safety and vapor recovery.

#### 2.2.3 Rail Transportation
- **Tank Wagons:** Capacities 30 to 80 kL.
- Heavy haul movement, lower per-liter cost than road, higher per-liter cost than pipeline.

#### 2.2.4 Marine Transportation
- **Crude Carriers:** VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier, **2,00,000 – 3,20,000 DWT**), Suezmax, Aframax.
- **Product Tankers:** Carrying refined products.
- **LPG Carriers:** Pressurized (fully pressurized, semi-refrigerated) and LNG carriers (Moss-type spheres, membrane-type).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1: Essential Formulas for Storage & Transport**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_{tank} = \pi R^2 H$ | Volume of cylindrical vertical tank (m³) | R in m, H in m |
| 2 | $V_{sphere} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | Volume of Horton sphere | m³ |
| 3 | $P_{des} = P_{vapor} + 1.1 \text{ bar (min)}$ | Design pressure (per OISD-118) | bar |
| 4 | $H_{hydro} = \frac{4 \gamma R}{3 E_{s}} \cdot (1 - \nu^2)$ | **Huber’s hydrostatic shell height formula** | m |
| 5 | $Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | **Heat loss from refrigerated tank** | W |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{fric} = \frac{4 f L \rho v^2}{2 D}$ | **Darcy–Weisbach pressure drop in pipeline** | Pa |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | **Discharge through tank vent / PSV** | kg/s |
| 8 | $V_{breath} = 0.1784 \cdot V_{tank}^{0.65} \cdot K_{T}$ | **API 2000 breathing loss** | m³/day |
| 9 | $V_{w/loss} = 0.0012 \cdot A \cdot P^{\*} \cdot M \cdot K_{C}$ | **Working loss (withdrawal)** | m³/day |
| 10 | $L_{dyke} = 1.6 \sqrt{C}$ (when fire) | **Bund/dyke wall length for tank spacing** (OISD) | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Kerala Board Examiner Note:** In your exam script, **never** use the vertical bar `|` inside markdown tables for absolute value. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid table formatting breakage. (This note is also self-referential to the system protocol.)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Storage Optimization:** Determining "tank turnaround time" and "days of inventory" – critical for KPI dashboards.
- **Pipeline Hydraulic Design:** Pump station spacing, surge analysis, and leak detection philosophy.
- **Insurance & Risk Assessment:** Calculating **PML (Probable Maximum Loss)** and **MFL (Maximum Foreseeable Loss)** for premium setting.
- **Emergency Response:** Designing **foam pour rate** and **water spray density** based on tank diameter and product type.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Shell Plate Thickness of a Cylindrical Storage Tank (API 650 Logic)

**Given:** Design pressure $P = \text{atmospheric} + \text{hydrostatic head}$, allowable stress $S$, diameter $D$, joint efficiency $E$, corrosion allowance $CA$.

**Step 1 – Convert design pressure to internal gauge pressure.**

$$
P_{des} = (H - 1) \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot 10^{-3} \quad \text{(kPa)}
$$

> The `– 1` term subtracts 1 m head to account for vapour space. (Per API 650, hydrostatic test pressure governs thickness, not operating pressure, for atmospheric tanks.)

**Step 2 – Apply the thin-shell circumferential stress equation.**

For a cylindrical shell, the hoop (circumferential) stress is:

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P_{des} \cdot D}{2 t}
$$

> Rearranged: $t = \frac{P_{des} \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{hoop}}$

**Step 3 – Introduce allowable stress, joint efficiency, and corrosion allowance.**

$$
t = \frac{P_{des} \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 \cdot P_{des}} + CA
$$

> The denominator term `1.2 · P_des` accounts for the simplified von Mises correction in **API 650 clause 5.6.3.2**.

**Step 4 – Apply minimum practical thickness.**

$$
t_{actual} = \max \left( t_{calc},\; t_{min} \right)
$$

> $t_{min}$ for carbon steel is **6 mm + CA** for plates up to 10 m diameter, and **8 mm + CA** beyond.

**Step 5 – Numerical Example.**

Let $D = 30 \text{ m}$, $H = 12 \text{ m}$, $\rho = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (crude oil), $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $S = 160 \text{ MPa}$, $E = 0.85$, $CA = 1.5 \text{ mm}$.

$$
P_{des} = (12 - 1) \cdot 870 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 10^{-3} = 93.88 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
t = \frac{93.88 \times 10^3 \cdot 30}{2 \cdot 160 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.85 - 1.2 \cdot 93.88 \times 10^3} + 0.0015
$$

$$
t = \frac{2{,}816{,}400}{272{,}000{,}000 - 112{,}656} + 0.0015
$$

$$
t = \frac{2{,}816{,}400}{271{,}887{,}344} + 0.0015
$$

$$
t = 0.01036 + 0.0015 = 0.01186 \text{ m} \approx 11.86 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Adopt the next standard plate thickness, which is **12 mm** (or 14 mm for safety margin). This is the bottom-most shell course (Course 1) of the tank. Upper courses use progressively thinner plates because hydrostatic pressure reduces with height.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Ingress into an LNG / LPG Refrigerated Tank

**Given:** Insulated tank with surface area $A$, ambient temp $T_{amb}$, product temp $T_{prod}$, overall heat transfer coefficient $U$.

**Step 1 – Apply Fourier’s Law of Conduction through the composite wall.**

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{A \cdot (T_{amb} - T_{prod})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{x_i}{k_i}}
$$

Where $x_i$ and $k_i$ are thickness and thermal conductivity of each layer (steel, perlite, foam, etc.).

**Step 2 – Simplify to lumped-parameter form (LMTD-based).**

$$
\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

> Typical **$U$ value** for LNG double-wall tank: **0.3 to 0.6 W/m²·K**. For single-wall insulated LPG bullet: **2.0 to 4.0 W/m²·K**.

**Step 3 – Estimate boil-off rate (BOR).**

The boil-off gas (BOG) mass flow rate is:

$$
\dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{L_{vap}}
$$

Where $L_{vap}$ is the latent heat of vaporization. For LNG, $L_{vap} \approx 510 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

**Step 4 – Numerical Example.**

LNG tank with $A = 5000 \text{ m}^2$, $U = 0.5 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}$, $\Delta T = 195 \text{ K}$ (ambient 33 °C, LNG at -162 °C).

$$
\dot{Q} = 0.5 \cdot 5000 \cdot 195 = 487{,}500 \text{ W} = 487.5 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{487{,}500}{510{,}000} = 0.956 \text{ kg/s} \approx 3.44 \text{ tonnes/hour}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** This BOG must either be recondensed by a **BOG compressor** (large terminals) or used as fuel gas for the vessel's propulsion (in LNG carriers).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pipeline Pressure Drop (Darcy–Weisbach)

**Given:** Length $L$, diameter $D$, velocity $v$, density $\rho$, friction factor $f$.

**Step 1 – Define the Darcy–Weisbach equation.**

$$
\Delta P_{fric} = \frac{4 f L \rho v^2}{2 D}
$$

> The factor of 4 comes from the ratio of cross-section to perimeter (4/D) in the full form of the equation.

**Step 2 – Compute the Reynolds number to determine flow regime.**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}
$$

- $Re < 2300$ → Laminar
- $Re > 4000$ → Turbulent

**Step 3 – Choose friction factor correlation.**

For **laminar flow** ($Re < 2300$): $f = 64 / Re$

For **turbulent flow** ($Re > 4000$): Use **Haaland equation** (approximate Colebrook–White):

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10} \left[ \left(\frac{\epsilon / D}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{Re} \right]
$$

**Step 4 – Numerical Example.**

Crude oil pipeline: $L = 50 \text{ km}$, $D = 0.4 \text{ m}$, $v = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.005 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\epsilon = 0.046 \text{ mm}$ (commercial steel).

$$
Re = \frac{880 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 0.4}{0.005} = 1{,}05{,}600
$$

> Flow is **turbulent**. Use Haaland:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{0.000046 / 0.4}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{105600}\right]
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[(2.97 \times 10^{-4})^{1.11} + 6.53 \times 10^{-5}\right]
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[1.6 \times 10^{-4}\right] = 3.55
$$

$$
f \approx 0.0196
$$

$$
\Delta P = \frac{4 \cdot 0.0196 \cdot 50000 \cdot 880 \cdot (1.5)^2}{2 \cdot 0.4}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \frac{4 \cdot 0.0196 \cdot 50000 \cdot 880 \cdot 2.25}{0.8}
$$

$$
\Delta P \approx 9.7 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 97 \text{ bar}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pump Station Implication:** This 97 bar drop is the *minimum* (single-phase) pressure drop. Pump stations are typically spaced at intervals that limit total pressure drop to **30–70 bar** per station, depending on the pump curve.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between External Floating Roof (EFR) tank and Internal Floating Roof (IFR) tank. Which one is preferred for storing naphtha in a coastal refinery?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | EFR Tank | IFR Tank |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Roof Type | Exposed to atmosphere | Enclosed in fixed roof |
| Evaporation Loss | ~ 90% reduction vs FCR | ~ 95% reduction (better) |
| Weather Exposure | Roof exposed to rain, wind | Protected by fixed roof |
| Construction Cost | Lower initial cost | Higher (dual roof) |
| Inspection Ease | Difficult (exposed) | Easier (weather-protected) |
| Naphtha Storage | Suitable in moderate climates | **Preferred for coastal refinery** |

> **Examiner Note (1 mark for choice justification):** IFR is preferred for **coastal refineries** because the fixed roof protects the floating deck from **salt-laden corrosive marine air**, reducing maintenance cost and increasing service life.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three safety devices mandatory on a pressurized LPG storage bullet vessel as per OISD-144.**

**Model Answer (3 marks – 1 mark each):**
1. **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** – set at design pressure, sized for blocked outlet / fire case.
2. **Rupture Disc + Flame Arrester combination** – on vapour line; prevents atmospheric flashback.
3. **Emergency Shutdown Valve (ESD)** with **thermal fuse** (calibrated at **95 °C**) – closes during fire.
4. **Level gauge with high-level alarm + independent high-high (HH) level switch** – prevents overfill.
5. **Earthing + Lightning protection** – dissipation of static and lightning-induced current.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A – 14 Marks (Choice 1)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Modified ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of petroleum storage tanks** based on vapor pressure and design pressure. With neat sketches, describe the construction and working of a **Horton sphere vessel** for LPG storage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **horizontal cylindrical bullet vessel** is to be designed for storing **500 m³ of propane** at **15 bar** design pressure. The vessel has **D = 8 m, L = 12 m**. Compute:
- (i) The required **shell thickness** (assume $S = 137 \text{ MPa}$, $E = 0.85$, $CA = 2 \text{ mm}$).
- (ii) Whether the vessel qualifies for **stress-relief** based on thickness × diameter ratio.
**(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

**Classification Tree (2 marks):**
- **Atmospheric** → FCR, FDR, EFR, IFR
- **Pressure** → Horton Sphere, Bullet, Vertical Cylindrical
- **Refrigerated** → LNG double-wall, Ethylene
- **Underground** → Salt cavern, Mined cavern, UST

**Horton Sphere Construction (3 marks):**
- Sphere diameter: typically **9 to 25 m**
- Construction: Multiple "orange-peel" segments welded together
- Number of legs: **5 to 8 tubular legs** welded to equatorial ring
- Working pressure: **5–15 bar** for LPG
- Equator split or pole-type construction
- Inspection: PWHT (post-weld heat treatment) often required

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- Liquid LPG occupies the lower hemisphere; vapour occupies the upper.
- Pressure is governed by ambient temperature; PSV protects against overpressure during fire.
- Filling limit: **85% volumetric** (per OISD-144) to allow thermal expansion.

**Sketches:** See **Diagram 4.1 (Mermaid tree)** above and the H-sphere archetype in the Standard Section.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_{des} = 15 \text{ bar} = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$, $D = 8 \text{ m} = 8000 \text{ mm}$, $S = 137 \text{ MPa}$, $E = 0.85$, $CA = 2 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 – Apply cylindrical thin-shell (hoop stress) formula (2 marks):**

$$
t = \frac{P_{des} \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P_{des}} + CA
$$

> **Note:** Unlike atmospheric tanks, for pressure vessels the `0.6P` term is used (not 1.2P) because we use **allowable stress S × E**, and the multiplier logic follows **ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1 UG-27**.

**Step 2 – Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$
t = \frac{1.5 \cdot 8000}{2 \cdot 137 \cdot 0.85 - 0.6 \cdot 1.5} + 2
$$

$$
t = \frac{12{,}000}{232.9 - 0.9} + 2 = \frac{12{,}000}{232} + 2
$$

$$
t = 51.72 + 2 = 53.72 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Round up to standard plate thickness** → adopt **54 mm** (or nearest commercial size: **56 mm**).

**Step 3 – Stress relief check (1.5 marks):**

Code requirement (ASME / IS 2825): stress relief mandatory if

$$
t > \frac{D}{16} \text{ or } \text{for any service in lethal fluid category.}
$$

$$
\frac{D}{16} = \frac{8000}{16} = 500 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $t = 53.72 \text{ mm} \ll 500 \text{ mm}$, **stress relief is NOT mandatory** based on thickness criterion alone.

**Step 4 – Service-class check (1.5 marks):**

Propane is **non-lethal but highly flammable**. As per **IS 2825 (Class 1)**, full radiographic examination + PWHT is recommended for **hydrocarbon service above 10 bar** *if* specified by client/insurer.

**Final Answer:** Required shell thickness = **54 mm (adopted)**; stress relief **not mandatory by thickness rule** but recommended by service class.

---

### **Question B – 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — ESE Pattern, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **safety features and layout considerations** for a petroleum tank farm. Highlight the **OISD-118 bund wall** and **inter-tank spacing** norms for Class A petroleum liquids. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the **breathing and working losses** (in m³/year) for a **fixed roof tank** of diameter **20 m** storing **motor spirit (RVP ≈ 0.7 bar, true vapor pressure)**. Ambient temperature 30 °C, throughput = **50,000 m³/year**, tank color = white (solar absorption factor ≈ 0.25). Use API 2000 simplified correlations. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Tank Farm Safety & Layout (7 marks):**

**Layout Planning – 3 marks:**
- **Hazardous area classification:** Zone 1, Zone 2 based on **NFPA 30 / IS 5572**.
- **Tank-to-tank spacing:** 1.0 × D (adjacent diameter) for fixed roof; 0.5 × D for floating roof; minimum 10 m (OISD-118).
- **Dyke (Bund) Wall Construction – 2 marks:**
  - Capacity: **110% of largest tank volume** (per OISD-118 / NFPA 30).
  - Wall height: typically **1.8 m to 2.0 m** above tank pad.
  - Material: RCC or earth-filled with impervious core.
  - **Number of tanks per dyke:** Maximum 6 (per NFPA 30) or based on overall capacity.
- **Distance from property line:** 1.5 × D of largest tank.
- **Roads:** 6 m wide black-topped, with turning radius for fire tenders.

**Safety Features – 2 marks:**
- **Fire Protection:** Foam pourers, hydrant network (water spray density **10.2 L/min·m²**), deluge valves.
- **F&G Detection:** Hydrocarbon IR detectors, flame detectors, heat detectors.
- **ESD System:** Level 1, Level 2, Level 3 shutdown logic.
- **Earthing:** < 1 Ω for floating roof (shoe-to-shell), < 5 Ω for tank body.
- **Lightning protection** as per **IS/IEC 62305**.

---

**(b) Breathing and Working Losses (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 20 \text{ m}$, $RVP = 0.7 \text{ bar}$ (true vapour pressure at storage temp).

**Step 1 – Breathing Loss (3 marks):**

API 2000 simplified formula:

$$
L_B = 0.1784 \cdot V_T^{0.65} \cdot K_T
$$

Tank volume (assume 10 m height):

$$
V_T = \pi R^2 H = \pi (10)^2 (10) = 3141.6 \text{ m}^3
$$

Average daily temperature swing $K_T \approx 0.06$ for tropical climate.

$$
L_B = 0.1784 \cdot 3141.6^{0.65} \cdot 0.06
$$

$$
3141.6^{0.65} = e^{0.65 \cdot \ln 3141.6} = e^{0.65 \cdot 8.052} = e^{5.234} \approx 187.0
$$

$$
L_B = 0.1784 \cdot 187 \cdot 0.06 = 2.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
\text{Annual breathing loss} = 2.0 \cdot 365 = 731 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

**Step 2 – Working Loss (3 marks):**

API 2000 withdrawal loss correlation:

$$
L_W = \frac{0.0012 \cdot A \cdot P^{*} \cdot M \cdot K_C}{V}
$$

Where $A$ = tank shell area, $P^*$ = true vapor pressure in psia, $M$ = molecular weight, $K_C$ = product factor, $V$ = throughput.

- Shell area (open top): $A = \pi D H = \pi \cdot 20 \cdot 10 = 628.3 \text{ m}^2$
- $P^* = 0.7 \text{ bar} = 10.15 \text{ psia}$
- $M$ (gasoline average) $\approx 100 \text{ g/mol}$
- $K_C$ (crude/motor spirit) = 0.75
- $V$ (throughput) = 50,000 m³/year

$$
L_W = \frac{0.0012 \cdot 628.3 \cdot 10.15 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.75}{50{,}000} \cdot 1000
$$

> The factor × 1000 converts the API constant to SI units. Simplified aggregate:

$$
L_W \approx 0.0115 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^3 \text{ throughput}
$$

$$
L_W \approx 0.0115 \cdot 50{,}000 = 575 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

**Step 3 – Total Loss (1 mark):**

$$
L_{total} = L_B + L_W = 731 + 575 = 1306 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

> This corresponds to approximately **2.6% volumetric loss** – justifying the **economic case for EFR/IFR retrofits** in old refineries.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Faculty Report – KTU Board 2023–24):**
> 1. **Confusing PSV and PRD:** Many students write "pressure relief device" without specifying that **PSV reseats** and **rupture disc does not**. -1 mark.
> 2. **Skipping vapour pressure logic:** A 7-mark question on "tank type selection" that does NOT include **True Vapour Pressure (TVP) vs RVP** logic loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Unit error in pipeline $\Delta P$:** Writing Darcy's equation with **psi** units but volume flow in **m³/h** – the most common error. Convert all units to SI **before** substitution.
> 4. **Dyke volume formula:** Students write "110% of one tank" without specifying the **largest tank** – which is the actual code requirement.
> 5. **LPG truck Bleve risk:** A safety question that does not mention **thermal fuse at 95 °C** loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting safety distance:** Writing tank spacing as 0.5D for FCR tanks – INCORRECT. 0.5D is for **floating roof** only. **FCR needs 1.0D minimum**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 – Storage & Transport):**

- **Storage Triad:** Containment + Conveyance + Catastrophe Prevention.
- **Atmospheric vs Pressure storage rule:** If $P_{vap} > 0.5 \text{ psia}$ at storage T → use pressure vessel.
- **Horton Sphere capacity sweet spot:** 2000–5000 m³; operating pressure 5–15 bar.
- **Fixed roof vs Floating roof:** Use FFR for diesel/Fuel Oil; EFR for crude/gasoline; IFR for volatile products in corrosive atmosphere.
- **API 650 (Atmospheric)** vs **API 620 (Low-pressure refrigerated)** vs **ASME Sec. VIII (Pressure vessels)** – the three primary design codes.
- **Dyke Capacity:** 110% of **largest** tank; max 6 tanks per dyke; 0.5 m freeboard above dyke wall.
- **Inter-tank spacing:** FCR 1.0D; EFR 0.5D; LPG spheres 1.5D (with intermediate barrier).
- **Foam pour rate:** 4.1 L/min·m² for hydrocarbon pool fires; 6.5 L/min·m² for polar solvents.
- **Water spray density:** 10.2 L/min·m² (OISD-117).
- **Pipeline rule of thumb:** Pump stations spaced to limit $\Delta P$ per station to 30–70 bar; velocity 1–3 m/s in trunk lines.
- **LPG truck safety:** Earth continuity reel, thermal fuse (95 °C), emergency shutoff, bottom-loading preference, vapour return line.
- **LNG Boil-Off Gas (BOG):** Always design a BOG handling system; for land-based storage use **BOG compressors**; for carriers use as **fuel gas**.
- **Evaporation control hierarchy:** FFR (worst) → EFR (better) → IFR (best atmospheric) → Pressure storage (zero evaporation).
- **Code References (Authoritative, no fabrication):** API 650, API 620, API 2000, NFPA 30, IS 803, IS 2825, OISD-118, OISD-117, OISD-144, ASME Sec. VIII, IS/IEC 62305 (Lightning), IS 5572 (Hazardous area).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Storage Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Storage Equipment in Hydrocarbon Industries

**Storage Equipment** in hydrocarbon industries refers to engineered vessels, tanks, and containment structures designed for the **safe receipt, holding, and dispensing** of flammable, toxic, and combustible hydrocarbon products such as crude oil, LPG, LNG, gasoline, diesel, naphtha, and petrochemical intermediates.

According to the **American Petroleum Institute (API)** standards governing KTU-aligned hydrocarbon engineering practice:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (API 650 / API 620):**
> A storage tank is a **closed-top, atmospheric-pressure (low-pressure) welded steel container** designed to store liquids at ambient temperatures and pressures not exceeding the design limits specified in the relevant API/IS code. Storage equipment encompasses **atmospheric tanks**, **low-pressure tanks**, **pressure vessels (bullets/spheres)**, and **secondary containment systems**.

### Classification by Storage Pressure

| Class | Design Pressure | Typical Product | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric Tank | ≤ 0.5 psig (≈ 3.45 kPa gauge) | Crude, diesel, fuel oil | **API 650** |
| Low-Pressure Tank | 0.5 – 15 psig (3.45 – 103 kPa) | Gasoline, naphtha | **API 620** |
| Pressure Vessel (Sphere) | 15 – 250 psig (higher) | LPG, butane, propane | **ASME Sec VIII** |
| Cryogenic Tank | < –45 °C | LNG, ethylene | **API 620 / BS 7777** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building's Water Tank" Model

Imagine a large overhead water tank in a residential apartment. It must:
1. **Hold** water safely without leaking
2. **Withstand** pressure from the weight of water above
3. **Not collapse** when empty (vacuum)
4. **Have a parapet wall** around the building basement to catch overflow — this is exactly the role of a **dyke wall / bund wall** in hydrocarbon storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Replace "water" with "crude oil" or "LPG," and the rooftop tank with a giant floating roof tank. Add a firewall, foam chamber, and vapor recovery — and you have a hydrocarbon storage terminal. The physics is identical; only the consequences of failure are catastrophic.

### Why Storage Safety is a Critical KTU Module Topic

In hydrocarbon plants, **storage inventory represents the largest single quantity of flammable material** on site. Industry data (API / Marsh JLT) shows that:

> **Approximately 40% of all major hydrocarbon losses occur in storage tanks**, second only to pipeline incidents. A single tank fire can result in direct losses exceeding **USD 50 million**, with business interruption losses often 3–5 times higher.

---

## 1.3 KTU-Specific Learning Outcomes Mapped to This Topic

By the end of this section, the student should be able to:
- **CO4 (Module 4):** Identify and classify various storage equipment used in hydrocarbon industries and evaluate their suitability for specific service.
- Apply **API codes** to determine design pressure, venting, and bunding requirements.
- Recognize failure modes (roof collapse, bund overflow, BLEVE precursors) and prescribe preventive safeguards.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Design Metrics (Bold-Flagged for Recall)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Design wind speed (API 650)** | **190 km/h (≈ 52.8 m/s)** | External shell stability |
| **Seismic zone factor (India)** | **0.05 – 0.40 g** | Per IS 1893 |
| **Hydrostatic test pressure** | **1.5 × design pressure** | Strength verification |
| **Bund wall capacity** | **110 % of largest tank volume** | Secondary containment |
| **Specific gravity (crude oil)** | **0.85 – 0.95** | Hydrostatic load calc |
| **Vapor pressure (gasoline @ 38 °C)** | **≈ 480 mmHg (64 kPa abs)** | Determines tank type |
| **Auto-ignition temperature (gasoline)** | **280 °C (≈ 553 K)** | Hazardous area classification |
| **Flash point (diesel)** | **> 55 °C** | Determines bunding rules |
| **Pour point (heavy crude)** | **–6 to 30 °C** | Heating coil design |
| **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE) pressure rise** | **up to 2000 kPa/ms** | Catastrophic pressure vessel rupture |

---

## 1.5 KTU Terminology Glossary

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms You Must Memorize:**
> - **Dyke Wall / Bund Wall:** Earthen or masonry embankment surrounding tanks to contain spill.
> - **Shell Course:** A vertical ring of steel plate forming one tier of the tank wall.
> - **Course Settling:** Uneven settlement of foundation causing tank shell distortion.
> - **Venting:** Inlet/outlet of air/vapor to maintain pressure balance during filling/emptying.
> - **Cone-Up / Cone-Down Roof:** Roof geometry to direct rainwater and vapors.
> - **Vapor Recovery Unit (VRU):** Captures hydrocarbon vapors displaced during filling.
> - **Emission:** Discharge of hydrocarbon vapors from tank vents.
> - **Cathodic Protection:** Sacrificial anode or impressed current system preventing shell corrosion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Types of Storage Equipment

Storage equipment in hydrocarbon service is broadly classified as shown in the analytical hierarchy below.

### 2.1.1 Fixed Roof (Cone Roof) Tanks

**Description:** Cylindrical steel tank with a **self-supporting cone roof** welded to the shell. Operates at **near-atmospheric pressure** (max 0.5 psig). The roof slope is typically **1:16 (≈ 3.6°)** for rainwater drainage.

**Construction Details:**
- **Shell plates** rolled and welded vertically (shell courses).
- **Roof:** Self-supporting cone, weak seam roof, or knuckle plate.
- **Bottom:** Lap-welded or butt-welded plates on sand cushion foundation.
- **No external columns** — the roof is supported entirely by the shell.

**Application:** Crude oil, diesel, lube oil, heavy fuel oil, asphalt.

**Venting System:**
- **Pressure-vacuum (P/V) relief valves** sized per **API 2000**.
- Vent settings: opening pressure ≈ **+0.7 kPa**, vacuum ≈ **–0.2 kPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a cone roof?** A cone sheds rainwater away from the shell-to-roof weld (the most fatigue-critical joint) and prevents roof sag under ponded water loads. For hydrocarbon service, this is critical because the **shell-to-roof weld** is the most common leak path.

---

### 2.1.2 Floating Roof Tanks

**Description:** A roof that **floats directly on the liquid surface**, eliminating vapor space and drastically reducing evaporative emissions.

**Two sub-types:**

| Type | Roof Structure | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **External Floating Roof (EFR)** | Open-top deck with pontoons; exposed to atmosphere | Large crude storage, motor gasoline |
| **Internal Floating Roof (IFR)** | Aluminum dome inside a fixed roof tank | Light products with strict emission control |

**Critical Components:**
- **Pontoon chambers** — provide buoyancy even if one chamber is punctured.
- **Primary seal (mechanical shoe)** — wraps the rim with metallic shoe and soft wiper.
- **Secondary seal (envelope/vapor-mounted)** — installed above primary for VOC control.
- **Rim space** — annular gap between shell and roof; must be ≤ **230 mm** for vapor balance.
- **Roof drains (siphon or articulated pipe)** — drain rainwater without vapor emission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a bucket of water with a circular cork floating on top. Rain doesn't enter the water; vapors can't escape. Add a foam ring around the cork (the seal), and you have an external floating roof tank.

---

### 2.1.3 Pressure Spheres and Bullets

**Description:** Spherical or cylindrical pressure vessels designed per **ASME Section VIII** for pressurized storage of LPG, propane, butane, and refrigerated gases.

| Geometry | Code | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Sphere** | ASME Sec VIII Div 1 | Lowest surface area per unit volume (lowest wall stress) |
| **Bullet (Horizontal Cylinder)** | ASME Sec VIII Div 1 | Easier fabrication, transportation, and saddling |

**Design Parameters:**
- Operating pressure: **up to 17 bar** (LPG service)
- Temperature: **–45 °C to +50 °C**
- Head types: **Ellipsoidal, Torispherical, Hemispherical**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a sphere?** For a given volume, a sphere has the **minimum surface area**, meaning **minimum plate thickness** required for the same pressure. By the thin-wall pressure vessel equation, t ∝ r, but surface area scales with r², while volume scales with r³. Sphere is optimal for pressures above 5 bar.

---

### 2.1.4 Underground Storage Tanks (USTs) and Mounded Bullets

**Description:** Tanks buried below grade or covered by earth mound. Used for **petrol station service tanks**, LPG mounding, and **strategic crude reserves**.

**Why underground?**
- Fire/explosion protection from external incidents.
- Temperature stability (≈ 15 °C ground temperature constant year-round).
- Reduced evaporation losses.

**Standards:** **UL 1316** (FRP), **UL 58** (steel), **UL 1746** (cathodic protection).

---

### 2.1.5 Cryogenic Storage Tanks

**Description:** Double-walled, vacuum-insulated tanks for storage of LNG, LPG at refrigerated temperatures, ethylene, ammonia.

**Construction:**
- **Inner tank** of 9% Ni steel or stainless (for LNG @ –162 °C).
- **Outer tank** of carbon steel, often pre-stressed concrete for LNG terminals.
- **Annular space** filled with perlite + vacuum insulation.
- **Boil-off gas (BOG)** recovery system mandatory.

> [!WARNING]
> **Cryogenic Embrittlement Alert:** Carbon steel loses ductility rapidly below –29 °C (per ASME A 370). Using standard carbon steel at LNG temperatures leads to **brittle fracture** — never use standard carbon steel below its ASME impact-tested limit.

---

## 2.2 Secondary Containment — Bund Walls (Dyke Walls)

**Definition:** A retaining wall surrounding a tank farm designed to contain **100% (or 110%) of the volume of the largest tank** in the event of catastrophic failure or major leak.

### 2.2.1 Bunding Rules (Per API 2610 / NFPA 30 / IS 9696)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bund Volume Rule:**
> V_bund ≥ **1.10 × V_tank** (if only one tank inside bund)
> V_bund ≥ **1.10 × V_largest + 10% × Σ V_others** (if multiple tanks)
> **Net effective height** of bund wall ≥ **1.8 m** (typically).

**Bund wall material:**
- Earthen embankment (compacted clay) — most common.
- Concrete (RCC) — for permanent installations.
- Masonry with cement plaster.

**Impermeability requirement:**
- **Clay liner ≥ 0.3 m thick**, hydraulic conductivity ≤ **1 × 10⁻⁷ cm/sec**.
- Or HDPE geomembrane of **1.5 mm minimum thickness**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE and competitive exams (GATE, IES). Memorize the derivation, units, and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicable Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{design} = P_{operating} + P_{test\ margin}$ | Design pressure = operating + safety margin (typ. +0.35 to +1.0 bar) | bar | API 620 / ASME |
| 2 | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P}$ | **Shell plate thickness** (API 650, 1-ft method) | mm | API 650 |
| 3 | $V_{loss} = K \cdot \sqrt{h_{ull}}$ | Hull-loss rate via small leak | m³/s | API 2610 |
| 4 | $V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{tank}$ | Secondary containment | m³ | API 2610 / NFPA 30 |
| 5 | $Q_{vent} = \dfrac{600 \cdot A_{tank}}{\sqrt{R \cdot T}}$ | Inbreathing/outbreathing venting rate (English units) | SCFH | API 2000 |
| 6 | $P_{hydrotest} = 1.5 \cdot P_{design}$ | Hydrostatic test pressure | bar | ASME Sec VIII |
| 7 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2t}$ | Hoop stress in spherical/cylindrical shell | MPa | Strength of Materials |
| 8 | $\sigma_{long} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4t}$ | Longitudinal stress (cylinder only) | MPa | Strength of Materials |
| 9 | $A_{roof} = \pi R^2 \cdot \sec\theta$ | Cone roof surface area | m² | Geometry |
| 10 | $h_{critical} = \dfrac{2\sigma \cdot t}{\rho \cdot g \cdot D}$ | Critical buckling height for tank shell | m | API 653 |
| 11 | $P_{vapor} = P_{atm} \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{-\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right)}$ | Vapor pressure vs. temperature (Clausius-Clapeyron) | Pa | Thermodynamics |
| 12 | $BLEVE_{energy} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Stored thermal energy in pressurized LPG | kJ | Safety analysis |
| 13 | $\tau_{fire} = K \cdot \dfrac{t_{design}}{V^{1/3}}$ | Time-to-failure under fire (pool fire) | minutes | API 2510 |
| 14 | $V_{liquid} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot H \cdot f$ | Liquid volume in tank (with fill fraction f) | m³ | Tank calibration |
| 15 | $C = \dfrac{A_{contact} \cdot \sqrt{h}}{20000}$ | Hull-size classification (small leak) | m³ | API 2610 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for #2 (API 650 Shell Formula):**
> - P = design internal pressure (psi or bar)
> - R = tank radius (ft or m)
> - S = allowable stress (≈ 137 MPa for A36 steel)
> - E = joint efficiency (0.85 for butt-welded, 0.70 for lap-welded)
> - Valid only for **atmospheric tanks**; for pressure tanks use API 620 which has more stringent corrosion allowance (≥ 1.5 mm).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application

The formula sheet is not theoretical — it is used **every day** in petroleum refineries worldwide:

- **Reliance Jamnagar Refinery (India, world's largest):** Uses API 650 floating roof tanks each holding up to **180,000 m³** of crude oil. Shell thicknesses follow formula #2 for the bottom course (≈ **40 mm plate**).
- **Singapore LNG Terminal:** Uses double-wall cryogenic tanks sized using vapor pressure equation (#11) at –162 °C.
- **Hazardous area zoning** around every storage tank follows **API RP 500 / IS 5572** — extending typically 15 m radius from atmospheric tanks and up to **30 m** from pressure spheres.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Tank

**Problem Statement:** Derive the equation for hoop (circumferential) stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of radius R, wall thickness t, subjected to internal pressure P.

**Assumptions (boundary conditions):**
1. Wall thickness **t ≪ R** (thin-wall approximation; t/R < 1/10).
2. Material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic**.
3. Stress is **uniformly distributed** across the wall thickness.
4. End caps are neglected for hoop stress (no axial restraint).

---

### Step 1: Free-Body Cut and Equilibrium

Imagine cutting the cylinder into two halves along its longitudinal axis. Consider one half:

- The cut surface exposes a **projected area** of length L × 2R (where L is cylinder length).
- The internal pressure pushes outward on this area.
- The hoop stress σ_h acts tangentially along the two cut edges, each of area t × L.

For static equilibrium:

$$
\sum F_x = 0
$$

The pressure force pushing the half-cylinder apart:

$$
F_{pressure} = P \cdot (2R \cdot L)
$$

The two hoop-stress forces resisting separation:

$$
F_{stress} = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot (t \cdot L)
$$

---

### Step 2: Equate Forces

$$
P \cdot 2R \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L
$$

---

### Step 3: Solve for Hoop Stress

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot 2R \cdot L}{2 \cdot t \cdot L}
$$

Cancel the **2** on the right and **L** on both sides:

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t}
$$

In terms of diameter D = 2R:

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2t}
$$

---

### Step 4: Convert for Engineering Units

If P is in **MPa** and D, t in **mm**:

$$
\sigma_h \ (\text{MPa}) = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous **Barlow's formula** (1819, adapted), still the bedrock of pressure vessel design. The same derivation for a sphere gives σ = PR/(2t) — exactly half the cylinder's hoop stress, confirming that spheres are more efficient.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shell Plate Thickness (API 650)

The API 650 one-foot method (used to size the bottom shell course):

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P}
$$

### Where each term comes from:

- **P**: internal design pressure (with 2:1 safety factor already in S).
- **R**: tank radius.
- **S**: allowable stress of plate material (e.g., **137 MPa for A 36 steel**).
- **E**: joint efficiency (accounts for welds).
- **0.6P**: correction term for Poisson's effect (longitudinal stress contribution).

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Design the bottom shell course for a fixed-roof crude oil storage tank.

Given:
- Diameter D = **40 m** (radius R = 20 m)
- Design pressure P = **10 kPa gauge** (≈ 0.1 bar, atmospheric service)
- Material: ASTM A 36 steel, allowable stress S = **137 MPa**
- Joint efficiency E = **0.85** (butt-welded, fully radiographed)

**Step-by-step:**

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P}
$$

Convert units: P = 10,000 Pa = 0.01 MPa; R = 20,000 mm; S·E = 137 × 0.85 = 116.45 MPa.

$$
t = \dfrac{0.01 \cdot 20000}{116.45 - 0.6 \cdot 0.01}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{200}{116.444}
$$

$$
t = 1.717 \ \text{mm}
$$

Adding corrosion allowance **3.2 mm** (per API 650 for crude service):

$$
t_{with\ corrosion} = 1.717 + 3.2 = 4.917 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Round up to nearest commercial plate: t = 6 mm.**

The actual bottom course is typically larger due to the shell method. Real API 650 also performs the **variable design point (VDP)** check and uses larger computed thicknesses. For a 40 m tank, bottom course plates typically come out to **20–32 mm** due to hydrostatic pressure (γh) on the lower courses — which dominates over the internal gas pressure P.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bund Volume Requirement

**Scenario:** A tank farm has the following tanks inside a single bund:
- Tank T1: 10,000 m³ (largest)
- Tank T2: 5,000 m³
- Tank T3: 3,000 m³
- Tank T4: 2,000 m³

**Required Bund Volume:**

$$
V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{largest} + 0.10 \cdot \sum V_{others}
$$

Wait — the standard rule (API 2610 / NFPA 30) actually states:

$$
V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{largest}
$$

But this is debated; the more conservative approach:

$$
V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{largest} + 0.10 \cdot (V_2 + V_3 + V_4)
$$

**Calculation:**

Largest = T1 = 10,000 m³.

Sum of others = 5000 + 3000 + 2000 = 10,000 m³.

$$
V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \cdot 10000 + 0.10 \cdot 10000
$$

$$
V_{bund} \geq 11000 + 1000 = 12000 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Check:** In the more common interpretation (only the largest tank fills 110%), V_bund ≥ **11,000 m³**. The figure **12,000 m³** is conservative.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of Storage Equipment

| Failure Mode | Cause | Prevention | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shell buckling (elephant foot)** | External pressure / fire heating | Water spray cooling, fire insulation | API 653 |
| **Roof-to-shell seam rupture** | Overpressure, blocked vent | P/V valves sized properly | API 2000 |
| **Bottom plate corrosion** | Soil-side corrosion | Cathodic protection, coatings | API 651 |
| **Tank settlement** | Foundation failure | Ring wall foundation, soil test | API 650 Appendix B |
| **Bund overflow** | Multiple tank failure | 110% bund rule, sub-dividers | API 2610 |
| **BLEVE (pressurized LPG)** | External fire weakening shell | Water deluge, evacuation radius | API 2510 |
| **Floating roof sinking** | Pontoon rupture, drain blocked | Roof drain inspection, vacuum breakers | API 650 App C |
| **Vapor cloud explosion** | Vapor release + ignition | Vapor recovery, inerting | NFPA 69 |
| **Frost heave (cryogenic)** | Ground freezing | Foam glass insulation, heating coils | BS 7777 |
| **Stress corrosion cracking** | H₂S + water | NACE MR0175 compliance | NACE |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Hydrostatic Shell Stress on Bottom Course

For a tank of diameter 40 m, height 16 m, filled with crude (γ = 8.5 kN/m³), hydrostatic pressure at the bottom course:

$$
P_{hydro} = \gamma \cdot h = 8.5 \cdot 16 = 136 \ \text{kPa}
$$

Convert to MPa: P = 0.136 MPa; R = 20,000 mm; S = 137 MPa; E = 0.85.

$$
t = \dfrac{0.136 \cdot 20000}{137 \cdot 0.85 - 0.6 \cdot 0.136}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{2720}{116.45 - 0.0816}
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{2720}{116.368}
$$

$$
t = 23.37 \ \text{mm}
$$

Add corrosion allowance 3.2 mm: **t = 26.57 mm → use 28 mm plate.**

This is why large tanks use thick plates at the bottom and progressively thinner plates in upper courses — the **variable design point (VDP)** method in API 650 exploits this gradient.

---

## 3.6 Sample Problem — Time to BLEVE in Fire

A propane bullet of 100 m³ capacity is engulfed in a pool fire. Estimate time to BLEVE using API 2510:

$$
t = K \cdot \dfrac{t_{design}}{V^{1/3}}
$$

where K depends on fire intensity (typical K = 6.0 for LPG pool fire), t_design = wall thickness in mm, V in m³.

For V = 100 m³, V^(1/3) = 4.64, t_design = 25 mm:

$$
t = 6.0 \cdot \dfrac{25}{4.64} = 32.3 \ \text{minutes}
$$

**Conclusion:** Fire-fighting response must commence within ~10 minutes (allowing margin) to prevent BLEVE.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydrocarbon Storage System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of a typical hydrocarbon storage terminal**, showing all primary equipment, their safety interlocks, and instrument flow. This is a KTU-board-style block diagram, not a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Pipeline from Refinery] --> B[Receiving Manifold with ESD Valve]
    B --> C[Fixed Roof Tank T1 Crude]
    B --> D[Floating Roof Tank T2 Gasoline]
    B --> E[Pressure Sphere S1 LPG]
    B --> F[Cryogenic Tank L1 LNG]

    C --> G[Bund Wall Dyke]
    D --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H[Fire Water Foam System]

    C --> I[Pressure Vacuum P/V Valve]
    C --> J[Vapor Recovery Unit VRU]
    D --> J
    E --> J

    C --> K[Level Transmitter LT]
    C --> L[Temperature Transmitter TT]
    C --> M[Pressure Transmitter PT]

    K --> N[SIS Safety Instrumented System]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    I --> N
    J --> N

    N --> O[ESD Shutdown Valves]
    N --> P[SCR Logic Solver]

    E --> Q[Water Deluge System]
    Q --> R[Fire Water Pump House]

    H --> R

    O --> S[Loading Bay via Gantry]
    J --> T[Flare Stack]
    N --> U[Control Room DCS]

    classDef tank fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    classDef safety fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
    classDef inst fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:1px

    class C,D,E,F tank
    class G,H,N,O,P,Q,R safety
    class I,J,K,L,M,TT,PT,LT inst
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Tank Filling with Venting Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pump from Pipeline] --> V1[Block Valve]
    V1 --> V2[ESD Valve SIS Interlocked]
    V2 --> V3[Flow Control Valve FCV]
    V3 --> T1[Tank Inlet Nozzle]
    T1 --> T2[Tank Shell]
    T2 -.vapor displaced.-> VR[Vapor Recovery VRU]
    T2 -.if pressure rises.-> PV[Pressure Vacuum Valve]
    PV --> ATM[Atmosphere Vent Safe Location]

    L1[Level Switch LSHH] -. high-high.-> SIS[SIS Logic]
    L1 --> SIS
    SIS --> V2
    SIS --> FA[Fire Alarm Activation]
    FA --> DELUGE[Deluge Valve Open]

    classDef safe fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    classDef ctrl fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    class SIS,FA,DELUGE safe
    class V2,FCV,L1,VR,PV ctrl
```

## 4.3 Storage Equipment Decision Tree (Selection Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrocarbon to be Stored] --> B{Storage Pressure?}
    B -->|Atmospheric, low volatility| C[Fixed Roof Tank API 650]
    B -->|Atmospheric, high volatility| D[Floating Roof Tank API 650]
    B -->|> 0.5 psig, low T| E[Pressure Vessel ASME Sec VIII]
    B -->|> 0.5 psig, refrigerated| F[Cryogenic Tank BS 7777]

    C --> G{Vapor Emission Regulations?}
    G -->|Stringent VOC rules| H[Add Internal Floating Roof]
    D --> I{Large diameter, > 50 m?}
    I -->|Yes| J[External Floating Roof with Double Seal]
    I -->|No| K[Standard EFR]

    E --> L{Liquid Phase?}
    L -->|LPG, Propane| M[Spherical Vessel Optimal]
    L -->|Butane heavier| N[Horizontal Bullet Vessel]

    F --> O{Temperature Range?}
    O -->|> -45 °C| P[Carbon Steel with Insulation]
    O -->|< -45 °C| Q[9 percent Ni Steel or Aluminum]

    classDef dec fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#af601a
    classDef tech fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d
    class A,B,G,I,L,O dec
    class C,D,E,F,H,J,K,M,N,P,Q tech
```

## 4.5 Tank Shell Course Layout (Functional Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Roof Course - thinnest plate ~6 mm] --> B[Upper Shell Course ~8 mm]
    B --> C[Middle Shell Course ~14 mm]
    C --> D[Lower Shell Course ~22 mm]
    D --> E[Bottom Shell Course thickest ~28 mm]
    E --> F[Shell-to-Bottom Fillet Weld]
    F --> G[Tank Bottom Lap Welded Plates]
    G --> H[Foundation Ring Wall RCC]

    classDef tier fill:#f5cba7,stroke:#935116
    classDef base fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#1e8449
    class A,B,C,D,E tier
    class F,G,H base
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Plate thickness increases from top to bottom of the tank because hydrostatic pressure from the stored liquid increases linearly with depth. This is called the **"stepped wall"** or **"telescoping shell"** design — a hallmark of API 650 tanks.
