## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define tank cleaning. List any four situations when tank cleaning becomes mandatory in a hydrocarbon storage facility.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Tank cleaning is the planned sequence of operations — isolating, emptying, gas-freeing, ventilating, washing, desludging, drying, and certifying — a petroleum storage tank to render it safe for human entry, hot work, or product changeover, while ensuring compliance with OISD, IS 1593, and NFPA 30 standards.

**Four Situations (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Periodic internal inspection** as per API 653 (typically every 5–10 years for fixed roof tanks).
2. **Product changeover** when switching between incompatible hydrocarbons (e.g., kerosene → aviation fuel).
3. **Hot work execution** such as welding on tank shell/roof — LEL must be reduced below 1%.
4. **Repair and maintenance** of corroded floor plates, shell, or roof structure.
5. *Bonus:* Re-calibration of volumetric measurement gauges.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is LEL? Why is the safe entry threshold set at <1% of LEL and not at the LEL itself?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**LEL Definition (1 mark):**
LEL (Lower Explosive Limit) is the minimum concentration of a hydrocarbon vapor in air below which the mixture is too lean to ignite. Below LEL, there is insufficient fuel to propagate a flame.

**Why <1% LEL and not 100% LEL? (2 marks):**
1. **Safety Margin / Safety Factor:** Tank atmosphere is non-homogeneous — pockets of higher concentration may exist near sludge or in corners. Setting the threshold at <1% LEL ensures a 100× safety margin against localized rich zones reaching flammable range.
2. **Instrument Error:** Combustible gas indicators have a typical accuracy of ±5–10%. A reading of "1% LEL" could be instrument noise; the 1% threshold reduces false negatives.
3. **Sludge Vapor Release:** Agitation during cleaning can release trapped vapors from sludge, suddenly raising local LEL above 10%. A pre-set <1% baseline accommodates this transient excursion.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** OISD-118 and IS 1593 explicitly mandate <1% LEL for human entry into hydrocarbon tanks.

---

### 📕 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 Choice A]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for tank cleaning** of a fixed roof crude oil storage tank, including the role of each stage, the responsible person, and the permits required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical floating roof tank of diameter **30 m** and shell height **14 m** is to be gas-freed after draining. The ullage volume is **15% of the total tank volume**. If the initial vapor concentration is **30% of LEL** and the target is **1% of LEL**, calculate the **minimum number of air changes** required assuming mixing efficiency of **0.65**. Also determine the **blower capacity in m³/hr** if 8 air changes per hour are to be achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

The 10-stage procedure for cleaning a fixed roof crude oil tank is as follows:

**Stage 1 — Pre-Planning and HAZOP (0.5 mark):**
A multidisciplinary team (Safety Officer, Tank Engineer, Operations, Maintenance Contractor) conducts a HAZOP study to identify all hazards — flammable atmosphere, H₂S, pyrophoric FeS, oxygen deficiency, falls, heat stress. Job Safety Analysis (JSA) is prepared.

**Stage 2 — Isolation (0.5 mark):**
All inlet and outlet piping is physically disconnected or **blind flanged**. Drain lines are blinded. The tank is electrically isolated (LOTO — Lock Out Tag Out). Steam coils, heating tubes, and mixers are also isolated.

**Stage 3 — Product Removal (0.5 mark):**
Maximum possible product is pumped out to a slop tank or another compatible tank using the existing transfer pump. The level is reduced to the lowest suction point.

**Stage 4 — Sludge Extraction (0.5 mark):**
Bottom sludge (containing water, iron sulfide, sand, asphaltenes) is pumped out using a sludge pump or vacuum truck. The tank may be **flooded with water** to keep FeS submerged.

**Stage 5 — Initial Gas-Freeing (1 mark):**
A forced-draft ex-proof blower introduces fresh air at the tank top while displaced vapor exits through the bottom manhole. This continues for **12–24 hours** (10–15 air changes). For sour crude, **N₂ purging** may precede air dilution to reduce H₂S release into atmosphere.

**Stage 6 — Atmospheric Testing (0.5 mark):**
A trained Safety Officer with a calibrated 4-gas meter measures **LEL, O₂, H₂S, CO**. Each manhole is tested. Atmosphere is declared "safe for entry" only when **all parameters are within limits**:
- LEL < 1%
- O₂ between 19.5% and 23.5%
- H₂S < 10 ppm
- CO < 25 ppm

**Stage 7 — Confined Space Entry Permit (0.5 mark):**
A written **Confined Space Entry Permit** (OISD-105 format) is issued by an authorized person, valid for **maximum 8 hours**, listing all workers, the standby person, rescue plan, and emergency contacts.

**Stage 8 — Mechanical Cleaning (1 mark):**
Trained cleaners enter wearing **SCBA (or supplied air), full body harness with lifeline, chemical-resistant coveralls**. They use **high-pressure water jetting (1500–3000 psi)** to remove residual sludge. The standby person remains at the manhole at all times. Atmosphere is re-tested **every 15 minutes**.

**Stage 9 — Final Wash, Rinse, and Drying (0.5 mark):**
A final freshwater rinse removes detergent/chemical residues. The tank is mopped dry and ventilated to remove moisture.

**Stage 10 — Certification and Hand-Over (1 mark):**
The Safety Officer issues a **Certificate of Fitness for Entry/Hot Work**. The tank is now ready for inspection, repair, or product changeover. A permit closure document is signed by all stakeholders.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- Tank diameter: $D = 30$ m
- Shell height: $H = 14$ m
- Ullage fraction: $f = 0.15$
- Initial vapor concentration: $C_0/C_{LEL} = 0.30$
- Target concentration: $C_n/C_{LEL} = 0.01$
- Mixing efficiency: $\eta = 0.65$
- Air change rate: $ACH = 8$ hr⁻¹

**Step 1: Total Tank Volume (1 mark)**

$$V_{total} = \pi r^2 H = \pi \times 15^2 \times 14 = \pi \times 225 \times 14 = 3150\pi$$

$$V_{total} = 3150 \times 3.1416 = 9896.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Ullage Volume (1 mark)**

$$V_{ullage} = 0.15 \times 9896.0 = 1484.4 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Number of Air Changes (n) for LEL Reduction (3 marks)**

Using the gas-freeing equation:

$$\frac{C_n}{C_0} = e^{-\eta \cdot n}$$

$$\frac{0.01}{0.30} = e^{-0.65 \cdot n}$$

$$0.0333 = e^{-0.65n}$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$\ln(0.0333) = -0.65n$$

$$-3.401 = -0.65n$$

$$n = \frac{3.401}{0.65} = 5.23 \approx 6 \text{ air changes (rounded up)}}$$

**Step 4: Blower Capacity (2 marks)**

$$Q_{blower} = ACH \times V_{ullage} = 8 \times 1484.4$$

$$\boxed{Q_{blower} = 11,875.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} \approx 11,875 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

**Conversion to CFM:**

$$Q = \frac{11875.2}{60} = 197.92 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 197.92 \times 35.31 = 6988 \text{ CFM}$$

**Engineering Selection:** A **7,000 CFM flameproof blower** with ducting should be deployed at top and bottom manholes for cross-ventilation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4 Choice B]`

**(a)** Discuss the **hazards associated with tank cleaning operations** in the hydrocarbon industry. For each hazard, specify the corresponding **engineering and PPE control measures**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **pyrophoric iron sulfide**? Explain its formation chemistry, the danger it poses during tank cleaning, and the **four control measures** used to prevent pyrophoric fires. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

The four principal hazards of tank cleaning are:

**1. Fire and Explosion Hazard (2 marks):**
- **Cause:** Residual hydrocarbon vapors in the ullage remain within the flammable range (between LEL and UEL). A single spark from non-spark tools, static electricity, or hot work can trigger a vapor cloud explosion (VCE).
- **Engineering Controls:** Forced ventilation to reduce LEL to <1%; nitrogen purging for highly toxic products; use of intrinsically safe (IS) electrical equipment; bonding and grounding to dissipate static; elimination of ignition sources through hot work permits.
- **PPE Controls:** Flame-resistant (FR) coveralls, anti-static footwear, and intrinsically safe radios.

**2. Oxygen Deficiency / Enrichment Hazard (1.5 marks):**
- **Cause:** Displacement of air by hydrocarbon vapors or N₂ purging reduces O₂ below 19.5% (deficiency). Conversely, leaking oxygen lines can enrich O₂ above 23.5% (enrichment), making any material easily ignitable.
- **Engineering Controls:** Continuous O₂ monitoring with alarms at 19.5% and 23.5%; forced ventilation at 6–10 air changes/hr; lockout of oxygen lines during tank cleaning.
- **PPE Controls:** SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus) when O₂ <19.5%; supplied-air respirator with escape bottle as backup.

**3. Toxic Exposure Hazard (2 marks):**
- **Cause:** Release of H₂S (sour crude), Benzene (gasoline), n-Hexane (naphtha). H₂S causes olfactory paralysis at >100 ppm and death at >700 ppm. Benzene causes leukemia on chronic exposure.
- **Engineering Controls:** N₂ purging before air dilution; chemical scrubbing of vented vapors; continuous toxic gas monitoring (H₂S sensors at 10 ppm alarm); wind direction monitoring to position standby upwind.
- **PPE Controls:** Chemical-resistant coveralls (Tyvek/Saranex), full-face respirator with H₂S cartridges (only for low concentrations <10 ppm); SCBA for high concentrations; H₂S personal monitor with audible alarm.

**4. Physical and Mechanical Hazards (1.5 marks):**
- **Cause:** Falls from height (tanks up to 20 m), slip on wet surfaces, drowning from rain ingress, collapse of floating roof pontoons, heat stress from PPE in hot weather.
- **Engineering Controls:** Guardrails at roof edges; fixed ladders with cages; lifelines anchored to structural members; rain water drainage plugs removed only under controlled conditions; floating roof drained and grounded.
- **PPE Controls:** Full body harness with shock-absorbing lanyard, hard hat, non-slip gumboots, cooling vests for heat stress.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Pyrophoric iron sulfide (FeS) is a black, fine-grained compound that **spontaneously ignites upon contact with air** at room temperature. It is commonly found as sludge in sour crude oil storage tanks.

**Formation Chemistry (2 marks):**

Iron sulfide forms via two primary reactions in the tank:

**Reaction 1 — Direct reaction of mild steel with H₂S:**

$$Fe_{(s)} + H_2S_{(g)} \rightarrow FeS_{(s)} + H_2{_{(g)}}$$

**Reaction 2 — Reaction of rust (iron oxide) with H₂S:**

$$Fe_2O_3{_{(s)}} + 3H_2S_{(g)} \rightarrow 2FeS_{(s)} + 3H_2O_{(l)} + S_{(s)}$$

These reactions occur over months/years of sour crude storage, with FeS depositing in the sludge layer at the tank bottom.

**Danger During Tank Cleaning (2 marks):**

When the tank is opened for cleaning and FeS contacts atmospheric oxygen, the following exothermic oxidation occurs:

$$4FeS + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2Fe_2O_3 + 4S + \text{Heat (exothermic)}$$

The heat released can raise local temperature **above 200°C**, igniting surrounding hydrocarbon residues and causing a **tank fire with no external ignition source** — known as **spontaneous combustion of pyrophorics**. The fire can spread rapidly through dry sludge, endangering cleaners inside the tank.

**Four Control Measures (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Keep Tank Flooded with Water:** Maintain a water layer above the sludge during cleaning to prevent FeS contact with air. This is the simplest and most effective method.

2. **Apply Chemical Neutralizers:** Spray sludge with alkaline solutions (e.g., sodium hydroxide NaOH) or commercial rust converters that oxidize FeS to non-pyrophoric Fe₂O₃ before exposure.

3. **Wet Cleaning Only:** Use high-pressure water jetting (1500–3000 psi) to keep the sludge wet at all times. **Never** allow sludge to dry inside the tank.

4. **Continuous Water Spray / Misting:** During entry, maintain a fine water mist in the tank atmosphere to suppress any incipient FeS ignition. Some facilities install fixed water spray nozzles that activate automatically on temperature rise.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor:** When calculating air changes, ALWAYS multiply the theoretical value by 2× to account for non-uniform mixing. A 6-change theoretical answer becomes 12 actual changes.
> 
> 2. **Unit confusion in blower capacity:** The KTU exam expects answer in **m³/hr** first, then optional conversion to CFM. Writing CFM only is considered incomplete.
> 
> 3. **Mixing the LEL definitions:** Students often confuse LEL (lower) with UEL (upper) in tables. The LEL is the **lean** limit (insufficient fuel), UEL is the **rich** limit (insufficient oxygen).
> 
> 4. **Missing pyrophoric FeS topic:** This is a **favorite KTU question**. Not mentioning FeS in any "tank cleaning hazards" answer is an automatic 2-mark loss.
> 
> 5. **Confined Space Permit Validity:** KTU expects you to state the permit is **valid for 8 hours maximum** and must be **re-issued** for extended work. Writing "valid for the job" is incomplete.
> 
> 6. **SCBA vs. Air Purifying Respirator:** SCBA is mandatory for IDLH atmospheres. Writing "use a respirator" generically loses 0.5 mark — the examiner wants the **type** specified.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Last-Minute Revision — Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Definition:** Tank cleaning = isolating + emptying + gas-freeing + ventilating + washing + certifying.

- ✅ **Why clean tanks?** Inspection, hot work, product changeover, repair, re-certification.

- ✅ **Four hazards:** Fire/Explosion, O₂ deficiency/enrichment, Toxic exposure (H₂S, Benzene), Physical (falls, heat stress).

- ✅ **LEL safe entry threshold:** **< 1% of LEL** (not the LEL itself — 100× safety margin).

- ✅ **O₂ safe range:** **19.5% to 23.5%** — outside this range is dangerous.

- ✅ **H₂S limits:** TWA = 10 ppm; IDLH = 100 ppm; olfactory fatigue at >100 ppm (smell disappears but toxicity increases).

- ✅ **Benzene TWA:** **0.5 ppm** (OSHA PEL — KTU loves this number).

- ✅ **Ventilation rate:** **6–10 air changes per hour** minimum.

- ✅ **Pyrophoric FeS:** Spontaneously ignites in air. Formed from Fe + H₂S. **Keep tank flooded** during cleaning.

- ✅ **Permit types:** Confined Space Entry Permit, Hot Work Permit, Line Breaking Permit — all from OISD-105.

- ✅ **Atmospheric testing sequence:** LEL → O₂ → H₂S → CO → Benzene (PID).

- ✅ **Continuous monitoring interval:** **Every 15 minutes** during work.

- ✅ **Standby person:** MUST remain outside the manhole with lifeline and communication.

- ✅ **PPE ensemble:** SCBA, FR coveralls, full body harness, lifeline, 4-gas personal monitor, hard hat, anti-static boots.

- ✅ **Gas-freeing equation:** $\dfrac{C_n}{C_0} = e^{-\eta n}$; with safety factor = 2× the theoretical value.

- ✅ **Blower capacity formula:** $Q = ACH \times V_{ullage}$ (in m³/hr).

- ✅ **Tank volume formula:** $V = \pi r^2 H$ (cylindrical) + cone roof correction.

- ✅ **10-stage procedure:** HAZOP → Isolation → Emptying → Sludge Removal → Gas-Freeing → Testing → Permit → Cleaning → Final Wash → Certification.

- ✅ **Nitrogen purging** is used for toxic chemicals (before air dilution) to prevent toxic vapor release to atmosphere.

- ✅ **Regulatory standards to mention:** OISD-118, OISD-105, API 653, IS 1593, NFPA 30, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 (Confined Space).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Rescue and Fire Fighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT745 - SAFETY IN HYDROCARBON INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Methods of Rescue and Fire Fighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Methods of Rescue and Fire Fighting** in the hydrocarbon industry refer to the systematic, coordinated set of operational procedures, equipment deployment protocols, and tactical response strategies employed to:
1. **Locate, access, stabilize, and evacuate** personnel who are incapacitated, trapped, or exposed to hazardous atmospheres during an emergency in oil, gas, petrochemical, or refinery facilities.
2. **Detect, contain, control, and extinguish** fires involving flammable hydrocarbons (Class B fires), electrical hazards (Class C), and metal fires (Class D), using engineered suppression systems and trained emergency response teams.

These methods are governed by standards such as **NFPA 1500 (Standard on Fire Department Occupational Safety)**, **NFPA 600 (Standard on Industrial Fire Brigades)**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.156 (Fire Brigades)**, and **API RP 2001 (Fire Protection in Refineries)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the hydrocarbon industry, fire-fighting strategy is dictated by the **Fire Tetrahedron** (Fuel, Oxidizer, Heat, Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction) rather than the classical Fire Triangle, because hydrocarbon fires sustain themselves through free-radical chain reactions in the vapor phase.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at home and a small kitchen fire breaks out due to cooking oil catching flame (a Class K / Class F fire analogous to a hydrocarbon fire). What do you instinctively do?
- You **don't throw water** (water + burning oil = explosive eruption of flaming oil).
- You **cover the pan with a lid** to cut off oxygen (smothering — analogous to foam blanketing).
- You might use a **Class K wet chemical extinguisher** (analogous to AFFF foam in refineries).

In a refinery, the same principle applies but at a **massive industrial scale**:
- A pool fire in a tank farm cannot be smothered with a lid; instead, **Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF)** is applied through monitors and pourers to float a vapor-suppressing blanket over the burning liquid.
- A vessel fire at a flare stack requires **water spray (deluge) systems** to cool adjacent vessels — just as you would cool neighboring utensils in your kitchen.
- A trapped worker in a confined space cannot be "pulled out by hand"; they require a **trained confined-space rescue team with SCBA, retrieval tripods, and gas monitors** — analogous to firefighters entering a smoke-filled room with breathing apparatus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The hydrocarbon industry treats every emergency as a *chain of linked events* — rescue cannot begin until the fire/explosion risk is controlled, and fire-fighting cannot be effective until the rescue team is protected. This is the essence of the **Incident Command System (ICS)**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **SCBA** | Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus — provides clean air in toxic/smoke-filled environments |
| **AFFF** | Aqueous Film Forming Foam — suppresses hydrocarbon fires by forming a vapor-blocking film |
| **Deluge System** | A fixed water-spray system where all nozzles are open and water is released simultaneously upon activation |
| **Monitors** | High-capacity, ground- or platform-mounted water/foam cannons used in tank-farm fire protection |
| **Confined Space** | An enclosure with limited entry/exit, not designed for continuous occupancy (tanks, vessels, pits) |
| **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)** | Catastrophic failure of a pressurized liquid vessel when subjected to external fire |
| **Fire Tetrahedron** | Modern fire model with four elements: Fuel + Oxidizer + Heat + Uninhibited Chain Reaction |
| **Incident Command System (ICS)** | Standardized emergency management structure for coordinating multi-agency response |
| **Hot Work** | Operations involving open flames or sparks (welding, cutting, grinding) — a major ignition source |
| **Standby Man** | A dedicated rescuer positioned outside a confined space ready to summon help and operate retrieval gear |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Minimum SCBA air supply:** **30 minutes** (for escape) to **60 minutes** (for entry/rescue) at **4500 psi / 300 bar** cylinder pressure.
- **Pool fire extinction time for foam:** Typically **3–5 minutes** of gentle foam application (Type II application) on a hydrocarbon pool.
- **AFFF application rate (NFPA 11):** **6.5 L/min/m² (0.16 gpm/ft²)** for hydrocarbon pool fires.
- **Water spray cooling rate (deluge):** **10.2 L/min/m² (0.25 gpm/ft²)** for vessel/structure exposure protection.
- **Minimum safe approach distance for LPG vessel fires:** **100 m (330 ft)** from the vessel shell until cooling is established.
- **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) of gasoline:** **1.4%** by volume in air.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature of diesel:** **210 °C (410 °F)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fire Tetrahedron & Principles of Extinction

Classical fire science recognized three elements (Fire Triangle): **Fuel, Oxygen, Heat**. However, modern hydrocarbon fire science has expanded this to the **Fire Tetrahedron**, which adds the **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** in the gas phase. Hydrocarbon fires sustain themselves because free radicals (H•, OH•, O•) propagate combustion in a self-sustaining chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Extinction Strategy** = Remove **any ONE** leg of the tetrahedron:
> 1. **Fuel Removal** → Shut off block valves, drain tanks, transfer inventory.
> 2. **Oxidizer Removal** → Smother with foam, inert with nitrogen/CO₂.
> 3. **Heat Removal (Cooling)** → Apply water spray/deluge to absorb heat.
> 4. **Chain Reaction Inhibition** → Use dry chemical powder (ABC powder) to interrupt radical propagation.

## 2.2 Classification of Fires (NFPA 10 / IS 2546)

| Class | Fuel Type | Examples in Hydrocarbon Industry | Extinguishing Medium |
|-------|-----------|----------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, PPE, scaffolding | Water, foam |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids/gases | Crude oil, gasoline, diesel, LPG, naphtha | Foam, dry chemical, CO₂ |
| **Class C** | Energized electrical | Control panels, motors, switchgear | CO₂, dry chemical (non-conductive) |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, aluminum, titanium (in catalysts) | Special dry powder (e.g., Met-L-X, NaCl) |
| **Class K** | Cooking oils/fats | Lubricant kitchens, cafeterias | Wet chemical |

## 2.3 Methods of Rescue — Detailed Classification

### A. Confined Space Rescue
Performed when a worker is incapacitated inside a vessel, tank, pit, or column. Requires:
- **Atmospheric monitoring** (LEL, O₂, H₂S, CO)
- **Entry Permit & Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)**
- **SCBA or supplied-air breathing apparatus (SABA)**
- **Retrieval system** — tripod + winch + full-body harness
- **Standby person** — never enters alone; operates retrieval line
- **Communication** — radio, whistle, or rope-tug signals

### B. High-Angle / Vertical Rescue
For workers on tall structures (flare stacks, distillation columns, cooling towers at **>30 m height**). Uses:
- **Two-rope system** (working line + safety line)
- **Rope-access trained rescuers (SPRAT/IRATA certified)**
- **Anchor points rated for ≥22.2 kN (5000 lbf)** per rescuer

### C. Fire Rescue
Performed under active fire conditions:
- **Protected approach** using hose-line advance
- **Victim search patterns** (left-hand/right-hand search, VES — Vent-Enter-Search)
- **Rapid Extraction** using rescue litter/stokes basket
- **Buddy system** — never operate alone

### D. HazMat Rescue
Involves toxic gas releases, chemical spills, or radiation exposure. Requires **Level A HAZMAT suits** (fully encapsulating, SCBA inside) and **decontamination corridor** setup.

### E. Water Rescue
For cooling water reservoir, marine terminal, or off-shore platform man-overboard. Uses **life rings, rescue boats, throw-bag lines, and cold-water immersion PPE**.

## 2.4 Methods of Fire Fighting

### A. Water-Based Methods
- **Hydrant Network** — Class I (2½" hose), Class II (1½" hose), Class III (combined) standpipes.
- **Water Spray (Deluge) System** — Fixed nozzles on vessel skirts, structural steel, tank shells.
- **Water Spray Fixed System for LPG vessels** — Designed to limit shell temperature rise to ≤ **65 °C (150 °F)** above ambient (per API 2510).
- **Water Curtain** — Applied at process junctions to absorb radiant heat.
- **Water Monitor** — Ground/elevated cannons with flows of **1900–9500 L/min (500–2500 gpm)**.

### B. Foam-Based Methods
- **Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF)** — Fluorinated surfactant that spreads on hydrocarbon surface and forms an aqueous film; reduces surface tension below **~20 dynes/cm**.
- **Alcohol-Resistant AFFF (AR-AFFF)** — For polar solvents (methanol, ethanol, MTBE).
- **High-Expansion Foam** — Expansion ratio **>200:1**, used in LNG pits and cable vaults.
- **Medium-Expansion Foam** — Expansion ratio **50:1 to 200:1**.
- **Low-Expansion Foam** — Expansion ratio **<50:1**, used in tank fires.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foam Application Techniques:**
> - **Type I (Gentle):** Foam poured onto the surface; preferred for hydrocarbon pool fires — minimum application rate **6.5 L/min/m²**.
> - **Type II (Violent/Splash):** Foam thrown against a vertical surface to deflect onto the pool; higher rate **~10 L/min/m²**.
> - **Type III (Bank-down / Rain):** Foam rained down through the tank rim seal — used in cone-roof tank fires.

### C. Dry Chemical Powder
- **ABC Powder (Monoammonium Phosphate, 75%)** — Versatile, effective on Class A, B, C fires by interrupting chain reaction.
- **BC Powder (Sodium Bicarbonate / Potassium Bicarbonate)** — Pure Class B/C.
- **Class D Powder (Sodium Chloride, Graphite, Copper)** — For metal fires.
- Application range typically **3–5 m (10–16 ft)** for portable extinguishers.

### D. Gas-Based Suppression
- **CO₂** — Displaces oxygen; used in electrical rooms, control rooms; minimum concentration **34%** for Class B fires.
- **Inert Gas Systems (IG-541, IG-01, IG-100, IG-55)** — Reduce O₂ to **<12%** (below the **17%** threshold required for combustion of most hydrocarbons).
- **Clean Agents (FM-200, Novec 1230)** — Electrically non-conductive, no residue; for computer/control rooms.

### E. Specialized Methods
- **Dry Ice (CO₂) Pellets** — For LNG, ammonia, and cryogenic spills.
- **Vapor Suppression Foam** — For non-ignited spills to prevent vapor release.
- **Vapor Mitigation** — Water spray, water fog, and vapor recovery systems.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| $Q_{water} = 10.2 \times A$ | Water demand for cooling exposed vessels (API 2510) | L/min, where A = wetted area in m² |
| $Q_{foam} = 6.5 \times A_{pool}$ | Foam solution demand for hydrocarbon pool (NFPA 11) | L/min, A in m² |
| $t_{SCBA} = \frac{V_{cyl} \times P_{cyl}}{V_{breath} \times P_{breath}}$ | SCBA duration = cylinder volume × pressure / consumption rate | minutes |
| $C_{O_2}^{CO_2} \geq 34\%$ | Minimum CO₂ concentration for Class B fire extinction | volume % |
| $C_{O_2}^{inert} \leq 12\%$ | Maximum tolerable O₂ in inert gas flooding | volume % |
| $T_{shell}^{max} \leq T_{amb} + 65$ | Maximum allowable LPG vessel shell temperature rise | °C |
| $A_{cooling} = \pi D H_{wetted}$ | Wetted surface area of a vertical cylindrical vessel | m² |
| $A_{pool} = \pi r^2$ | Circular pool fire area | m² |
| $F_{anchor} \geq 22.2$ | Minimum anchor strength for rope rescue | kN per rescuer |
| $Q_{monitor} = 1900$ to $9500$ | Typical water monitor discharge capacity | L/min |
| $t_{chain}^{hydrocarbon} = 0.1$ to $1$ | Hydrocarbon combustion chain-reaction time | ms |
| $LFL_{gasoline} = 1.4\%$ | Lower Flammable Limit of gasoline vapor in air | % by volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by Safety Engineers and Fire Protection Engineers in **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**, **Fire Water Network (FWN) design**, and **EERA (Emergency Escape and Rescue Analysis)** for KTU-aligned process safety audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Foam Solution Demand for a Tank Fire

**Given:** A floating-roof crude oil tank catches fire in the rim seal. The tank diameter is **D = 40 m**, the rim seal area (annular) is approximately equivalent to a **1.5 m wide** band around the circumference (typical for welded or mechanical shoe seals).

**Step 1: Calculate the burning rim-seal area.**

The circumference of the tank is:
$$C = \pi D = \pi \times 40 = 125.66 \text{ m}$$

The rim-seal area is:
$$A_{pool} = C \times w = 125.66 \times 1.5 = 188.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply NFPA 11 minimum application rate for hydrocarbon.**

For a Type I (gentle) foam application on a hydrocarbon pool:
$$q_{foam} = 6.5 \text{ L/min/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate total foam solution demand.**
$$Q_{foam} = q_{foam} \times A_{pool} = 6.5 \times 188.5 = 1225.25 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 4: Convert to gallons per minute (for cross-checking with US standards).**

Using **1 L = 0.2642 gal**:
$$Q_{foam}^{gpm} = 1225.25 \times 0.2642 = 323.7 \text{ gpm}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the foam concentrate (AFFF 3%) demand.**

Since AFFF is typically a **3% concentrate** mixed with **97% water**:
$$Q_{concentrate} = Q_{foam} \times 0.03 = 1225.25 \times 0.03 = 36.76 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 6: Add safety margin (typically 30%) for extended discharge and reliability.**
$$Q_{design} = Q_{foam} \times 1.30 = 1225.25 \times 1.30 = 1592.8 \text{ L/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The fire-water pump house and foam proportioning system must be designed to deliver at least **1593 L/min (421 gpm)** of foam solution to extinguish this rim-seal fire, with a corresponding AFFF concentrate stock of **47.8 L/min** sustained for a minimum of **65 minutes** (per NFPA 11 discharge duration).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Fire Suppression Method vs. Hydrocarbon Scenario

| Scenario | Recommended Primary Method | Secondary Method | Critical Don'ts |
|----------|---------------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Diesel pool fire (small spill)** | AFFF foam (6.5 L/min/m²) | Dry chemical | No water jet (causes boil-over) |
| **LPG vessel fire (jet fire)** | Water spray (deluge, 10.2 L/min/m²) + isolate fuel | Dry chemical on impinging flames | Don't extinguish before fuel cut-off (BLEVE risk) |
| **Transformer oil fire (Class B + energized)** | CO₂ or dry chemical | Water mist (after de-energization) | No water jet on energized equipment |
| **Pump house fire (enclosed, ventilated)** | High-expansion foam | CO₂ flooding | Ventilate before re-entry (smoke layer) |
| **Catalyst metal fire (Class D)** | Special Class D powder (NaCl or graphite) | Dry sand | **NO WATER** (releases H₂, can explode) |
| **LNG vapor cloud (un-ignited)** | Water spray (vapor dispersion) | High-expansion foam blanket | No ignition sources within 1600 m |
| **Gasoline tank full surface fire** | Type III foam (bank-down) + chamber foam pourer | AFFF monitors | Watch for boil-over at water boil temp |
| **H₂S release in confined space** | SCBA-equipped rescue team, fresh-air ventilation | SCBA rescue, no entry without 4-gas monitor | **Never enter without H₂S monitor** |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Confined Space Rescue Decision Logic

**Step 1: Initiate the rescue protocol.**
A worker is reported unconscious inside a sour crude storage tank (H₂S-laden atmosphere).

**Step 2: Atmospheric testing is mandatory BEFORE entry.**
Four-gas monitor readings:
- $\text{O}_2 = 18\%$ (normal: 19.5–23.5%)
- $\text{LEL} = 8\%$ (below 10% LEL = safe to enter)
- $\text{H}_2\text{S} = 35 \text{ ppm}$ (above 10 ppm IDLH threshold — **entry is forbidden without SCBA**)
- $\text{CO} = 5 \text{ ppm}$ (acceptable)

**Step 3: Decision — entry is allowed only with full SCBA + retrieval line.**

**Step 4: Calculate SCBA duration required.**

Cylinder volume $V_{cyl}$ = **6.8 L** at **300 bar** (typical 4500 psi).
$$V_{air} = V_{cyl} \times P_{cyl} = 6.8 \times 300 = 2040 \text{ L of free air}$$

Average breathing rate during heavy work: **40 L/min** (ALARMP — 30 L/min at rest, 40–60 during exertion).

$$t_{SCBA} = \frac{2040}{40} = 51 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 5: Decision — with 51 min of air, allow 10 min for approach, 5 min for retrieval, and 36 min buffer.** Entry is feasible.

**Step 6: Implement retrieval system.**
- Tripod positioned over manhole.
- Winch with 50 m of kernmantle rope.
- Worker pre-attached to full-body harness with dorsal D-ring.
- Communication: radio (handheld, intrinsically safe).

**Step 7: Execute extraction.**
- Standby man maintains constant visual or rope-tug contact.
- Rescuer clips in with shock-absorbing lanyard.
- Worker hoisted out using mechanical advantage (2:1 or 3:1 pulley system).

**Step 8: Decontamination & medical handover.**
- Wash victim (eyes, skin) if H₂S exposure.
- Administer **100% oxygen via mask** (H₂S antidote — supportive, not specific).
- Hand over to on-site medic/ambulance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Error:** Students often forget that the **entrant must be attached to a retrieval line BEFORE entry**, not after. OSHA 1910.146 mandates this for vertical entries deeper than **1.5 m (5 ft)**.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Heat Absorption Calculation for Water Spray Cooling

**Given:** An LPG bullet vessel of diameter **D = 3 m** and length **L = 12 m** is exposed to an adjacent pool fire. The wetted area is the upper **2/3** of the vessel shell (the lower 1/3 is shielded by ground/sumps).

**Step 1: Calculate total vessel surface area.**
$$A_{total} = \pi D L + 2 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \pi \times 3 \times 12 + 2 \times \frac{\pi \times 9}{4}$$
$$A_{total} = 113.1 + 14.14 = 127.24 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate wetted area (upper 2/3 of shell).**
$$A_{shell} = \pi D L = 113.1 \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_{wetted} = 113.1 \times \frac{2}{3} = 75.4 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply API 2510 design water rate for exposure protection.**
$$q_{water} = 10.2 \text{ L/min/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Total cooling water demand.**
$$Q_{water} = 10.2 \times 75.4 = 769.1 \text{ L/min} \approx 203 \text{ gpm}$$

**Step 5: Apply continuity to size the deluge valve.**

With a residual pressure of **5 bar (72.5 psi)** at the most remote nozzle, the deluge valve and riser must be sized for at least **770 L/min** + friction losses (~15%) = **885 L/min** actual hydraulic demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This calculation is the basis of the **Fire Water Hydraulic Network Analysis** done during the design phase of any refinery, LNG terminal, or offshore platform.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: BLEVE Prevention Logic

**Given:** A propane bullet of **100 m³** capacity is exposed to a jet fire from a flange leak.

**Step 1: Recognize the BLEVE precursor conditions.**
- Vessel is **>70% full** of LPG (high liquid fill).
- External fire impinges on the **vapor space** of the shell.
- Shell temperature rises to **≥ 200 °C (392 °F)** — approaching the steel's critical temperature (creep failure).

**Step 2: Apply water cooling immediately.**
At minimum **10.2 L/min/m²**, maintain shell temperature **≤ 65 °C above ambient**.

**Step 3: Reduce inventory if possible.**
Depressurize via vent valve to flare, transferring LPG to a remote vessel. Target fill level **<30%** (so most of the volume is vapor, not liquid — no liquid expansion BLEVE risk).

**Step 4: Withdraw personnel to safe distance.**
**Minimum 300 m (1000 ft)** from a propane BLEVE per API 2510 — the blast and fireball can extend this far.

**Step 5: Monitor continuously.**
- Shell temperature RTDs (resistance temperature detectors) at **minimum 4 locations** (top, bottom, sides).
- Vibration sensors to detect shell bulging.
- Acoustic emission sensors for crack initiation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Many students assume that a BLEVE can be prevented by *extinguishing* the impinging fire. **WRONG.** If the LPG vessel has been heated to near-rupture temperature, extinguishing the external fire does **not** stop BLEVE — it must be **cooled continuously** until the vessel is depressurized.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fire Tetrahedron — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FUEL<br>Hydrocarbon vapor] --> FIRE[FIRE EVENT<br>Sustained Combustion]
    B1[OXYGEN<br>Air at 21 percent] --> FIRE
    C1[HEAT<br>Ignition above AIT] --> FIRE
    D1[CHAIN REACTION<br>Free radicals H OH O] --> FIRE
    FIRE --> E1[REMOVE FUEL<br>Shut block valves]
    FIRE --> E2[SMOTHER<br>Foam blanketing]
    FIRE --> E3[COOL<br>Water deluge spray]
    FIRE --> E4[INHIBIT CHAIN<br>Dry chemical powder]
    E1 --> EXT[EXTINCTION]
    E2 --> EXT
    E3 --> EXT
    E4 --> EXT
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Incident Reported] --> P2[Activate Rescue Plan]
```

## 4.4 Fire Extinguishing Method Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Fire Detected] --> Q2{What is Burning?}
    Q2 -- Hydrocarbon Liquid --> Q3[Class B Pool Fire]
    Q2 -- Hydrocarbon Gas --> Q4[Class B Jet Fire]
    Q2 -- Energized Equipment --> Q5[Class C Electrical]
    Q2 -- Combustible Metal --> Q6[Class D Metal]
    Q3 --> R1[Apply AFFF Foam Type I]
    Q4 --> R2[Cool Vessel with Deluge<br>Do Not Extinguish Yet]
    Q5 --> R3[Use CO2 or Dry Chemical]
    Q6 --> R4[Apply Class D Powder<br>No Water]

    R3 --> S1
    R4 --> S3[Bury Powder and Isolate]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between the Fire Triangle and the Fire Tetrahedron. Why is the Fire Tetrahedron more relevant to hydrocarbon fires?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Fire Triangle** includes three elements: Fuel, Oxygen, and Heat (1 mark).
- **Fire Tetrahedron** adds a fourth element: **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** in the gas phase (1 mark).
- **Relevance to hydrocarbon fires:** Hydrocarbon combustion propagates via free radicals (H•, OH•, O•). Dry chemical powder extinguishes hydrocarbon fires by interfering with this chain reaction, which the Fire Triangle cannot explain. Hence, the tetrahedron is the correct model for Class B hydrocarbon fires (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Q: List any three types of fire extinguishers used in hydrocarbon industries and the fire class each is suitable for.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **AFFF Foam extinguisher** — for Class B hydrocarbon liquid fires (1 mark).
2. **CO₂ extinguisher** — for Class C electrical/energized equipment fires (1 mark).
3. **Class D dry powder extinguisher (Met-L-X or NaCl)** — for combustible metal fires such as magnesium or aluminum (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 internal choice provides two alternatives. Both are presented below.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) Describe in detail the various methods of rescue used in the hydrocarbon industry. Include confined space, high-angle, fire, and HazMat rescue procedures. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Confined Space Rescue (2 marks):**
- Mandatory atmospheric testing (O₂ 19.5–23.5%, LEL <10%, H₂S <10 ppm, CO <25 ppm) using a calibrated 4-gas monitor.
- Use of SCBA or supplied-air breathing apparatus.
- Tripod + winch retrieval system with full-body harness attached **before** entry.
- Standby man stationed outside; communication via radio.
- Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) and Entry Permit System.

**2. High-Angle / Vertical Rescue (2 marks):**
- Required for workers on flare stacks, columns, or towers above 30 m.
- Two-rope system: **working line** (load-bearing) + **safety line** (backup).
- Rope-access trained rescuers (SPRAT/IRATA certified).
- Anchor points rated for ≥22.2 kN per rescuer.
- Mechanical advantage pulley systems (2:1 or 3:1) for hoisting victims.

**3. Fire Rescue (2 marks):**
- Performed under active fire using charged hose lines for protection.
- Search patterns: left-hand/right-hand search, VES (Vent-Enter-Search).
- Rapid victim extraction using Stokes basket/litter.
- Buddy system — no solo operations.

**4. HazMat Rescue (1 mark):**
- Level A fully encapsulating suit + SCBA.
- Decontamination corridor (dry line, wet line, support zone).
- Toxic gas detection, area zoning (hot/warm/cold zones).

**(b) Explain the principle and application of AFFF foam in fighting hydrocarbon pool fires. Discuss the types of foam application techniques and the design application rate. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
AFFF (Aqueous Film Forming Foam) consists of **fluorinated surfactants + hydrocarbon surfactants + water**. When applied to a hydrocarbon pool:
- The foam blanket **smothers** the fuel (cuts off oxygen supply — second leg of fire tetrahedron).
- The aqueous film (with surface tension **~20 dynes/cm**, lower than hydrocarbon's ~25 dynes/cm) floats on the fuel and **prevents vapor release**.
- Heat is removed by the water content (cooling — third leg).

**Application Techniques (3 marks):**

| Type | Description | Application Rate (NFPA 11) |
|------|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Type I (Gentle)** | Foam poured onto the surface | 6.5 L/min/m² |
| **Type II (Violent/Splash)** | Foam thrown against a vertical surface and deflected | 10.2 L/min/m² |
| **Type III (Bank-down/Rain)** | Foam rained down through tank rim | 6.5 L/min/m² |

**Design Application Rate (2 marks):**
- **6.5 L/min/m² (0.16 gpm/ft²)** for hydrocarbon pool fires using Type I or Type III.
- Minimum discharge duration: **65 minutes**.
- Foam concentrate: typically **3% AFFF** mixed with **97% water**.
- For polar solvents (methanol, ethanol), use **Alcohol-Resistant AFFF (AR-AFFF)** at **6.5 L/min/m²** with 3–6% concentrate.

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the Incident Command System (ICS) used in hydrocarbon industry emergencies. List the key positions in the ICS structure. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**ICS Definition (1 mark):**
The Incident Command System is a standardized, on-scene, all-hazards incident management concept that allows for integration of facilities, equipment, personnel, procedures, and communications operating within a common organizational structure.

**Key Positions in ICS (3 marks):**
1. **Incident Commander (IC)** — Has overall authority and responsibility for the incident. Sets objectives and approves action plan.
2. **Safety Officer** — Monitors safety conditions and has authority to stop unsafe operations.
3. **Liaison Officer** — Coordinates with outside agencies (fire department, police, regulatory).
4. **Operations Section Chief** — Manages tactical operations (fire-fighting, rescue, HazMat).
5. **Planning Section Chief** — Collects and analyzes incident data, prepares action plan.
6. **Logistics Section Chief** — Provides facilities, services, and materials.
7. **Finance/Admin Section Chief** — Tracks costs and compensation.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IC[Incident Commander] --> SO[Safety Officer]
    IC --> LO[Liaison Officer]
    IC --> IO[Information Officer]
    IC --> OSC[Operations Section Chief]
    IC --> PSC[Planning Section Chief]
    IC --> LSC[Logistics Section Chief]
    IC --> FSC[Finance Admin Section Chief]
    OSC --> F1[Fire Attack Team]
    OSC --> R1[Rescue Team]
    OSC --> H1[HazMat Team]
    OSC --> M1[Medical Team]
```

**(b) Discuss in detail the different classes of fires (NFPA classification) and the appropriate extinguishing media for each. Give two examples of hydrocarbon-industry fire scenarios for each class. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Fire Classes (NFPA 10) — 5 marks total:**

| Class | Fuel | Extinguishing Medium | Industry Examples (2 marks) |
|-------|------|----------------------|-----------------------------|
| **A** | Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth) | Water, foam | Office fire in control room; PPE store fire |
| **B** | Flammable liquids/gases | AFFF foam, dry chemical, CO₂ | Crude oil tank fire; LPG bullet jet fire |
| **C** | Energized electrical equipment | CO₂, dry chemical (non-conductive) | Switchgear fire in substation; motor control center fire |
| **D** | Combustible metals | Special Class D powder (NaCl, Cu, graphite) | Magnesium alloy fire in catalyst; aluminum powder explosion |
| **K** | Cooking oils/fats | Wet chemical (potassium acetate) | Lubricant kitchen fire in canteen; deep-fryer fire |

**Critical Notes (2 marks):**
- Water is **NEVER** used on Class B hydrocarbon pool fires (causes boil-over and slop-over).
- Water is **NEVER** used on Class D metal fires (reacts violently, releasing H₂).
- CO₂ is preferred for Class C fires as it leaves no residue and is non-conductive.
- ABC powder (MAP) is the most versatile, working on Class A, B, and C fires by interrupting the chain reaction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Class K with Class B** — Class K is specifically for cooking oils above **flash point 315 °C (600 °F)** (deep-fryer temperatures). It's a sub-class of Class B but requires wet chemical (not foam).
> 2. **Forgetting the chain-reaction aspect** — Many students define the fire triangle incorrectly as having **heat, fuel, oxygen** in a triangular layout. It should be a **tetrahedron** with **chain reaction** as the fourth vertex.
> 3. **Application rate confusion** — Foam application rate is **6.5 L/min/m²** (not 65, not 0.65). A factor-of-ten error is the most common mistake.
> 4. **Confined space entry without SCBA** — Stating atmospheric testing alone is "sufficient" loses 1 mark; SCBA is mandatory for any hydrocarbon confined space, even with safe readings (defense-in-depth).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Methods of Rescue and Fire Fighting | KTU 2024 One-Page Summary**

- **Fire Tetrahedron** (NOT triangle) = Fuel + Oxygen + Heat + Uninhibited Chain Reaction. Removing **any one** leg extinguishes the fire.
- **Fire Classes (NFPA 10):** A (solid), B (liquid/gas), C (electrical), D (metal), K (cooking oil). Hydrocarbon industry = predominantly **Class B and C**.
- **Foam (AFFF) Application Rate:** **6.5 L/min/m²** for Type I/III on hydrocarbon pools; **discharge duration ≥ 65 min**.
- **Water Spray (Deluge) Cooling Rate:** **10.2 L/min/m²** for vessel exposure protection; keep LPG shell **≤ 65 °C above ambient**.
- **CO₂ Minimum Concentration for Class B:** **≥ 34%** by volume; **Inert Gas O₂ target:** **≤ 12%**.
- **SCBA air duration formula:** $t = (V_{cyl} \times P_{cyl}) / \text{breathing rate}$ — typical 30–60 min duration.
- **Anchor Strength for Rope Rescue:** **≥ 22.2 kN (5000 lbf)** per attached rescuer.
- **Confined Space Rescue Mandates:** Atmospheric test + Entry Permit + LOTO + SCBA + Retrieval Line (attached BEFORE entry) + Standby Man.
- **High-Angle Rescue:** Two-rope system (working + safety) + SPRAT/IRATA certified team + 2:1 or 3:1 mechanical advantage.
- **BLEVE Prevention:** Cool the vessel continuously (don't extinguish external fire before isolating fuel); withdraw to **300 m (1000 ft)** safe distance for propane.
- **Class D Fires:** Use ONLY Class D powder (Met-L-X, NaCl) — **WATER IS STRICTLY FORBIDDEN** (H₂ explosion risk).
- **Boil-over Risk:** Crude oil with water content may boil-over when heated oil reaches **100 °C + water bottom** — this occurs typically **5–30 minutes** after ignition.
- **Incident Command System (ICS):** Standardized structure with **IC, Safety Officer, Liaison, Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance** sections.
- **Hot Work Permit:** Mandatory for any welding/cutting within **15 m (50 ft)** of flammable storage.
- **Critical References:** NFPA 10, NFPA 11, NFPA 600, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 & 1910.156, API 2510, IS 2546.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Methods of Rescue and Fire Fighting**
